opts.rat: LAST - adjuncts that don't reverse the immediate threat but reduce symptom duration and biphasic reaction risk. Allergy documentation prevents future re-exposure - one of the most important nursing safety actions.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After IM epinephrine, fluid resuscitation, and adjunct therapy, classify each finding over the next 6-24 hours.

opts.text: BP 118/72, HR 86, SpO2 97% on room air, lungs clear, voice normal
opts.rat: Hemodynamic and respiratory recovery within 30-60 minutes of treatment is the expected response to a single dose of IM epinephrine in mild-moderate anaphylaxis.

opts.text: Urticaria fading; lip edema reduced; no new lesions developing
opts.rat: Cutaneous resolution typically lags airway and circulatory recovery by minutes to hours. Continued resolution without new lesions confirms degranulation is settling.

opts.text: Lactate down from 3.6 to 1.2 mmol/L; ABG normalizing
opts.rat: Lactate clearance reflects restored tissue perfusion - the most reliable end-organ marker that resuscitation succeeded.

opts.text: 4 hours after apparent recovery, sudden return of stridor, hypotension, and urticaria with no re-exposure
opts.rat: BIPHASIC REACTION - 5-20% of anaphylaxis cases. Treat as new anaphylaxis: IM epinephrine, fluids, adjuncts. This is exactly why guidelines mandate 4-6+ hour observation post-recovery.

opts.text: New persistent wheeze with worsening dyspnea and oxygen requirement increasing despite resolved skin findings
opts.rat: Protracted anaphylaxis with refractory bronchospasm - escalate to IV epinephrine infusion, continuous albuterol, and consider intubation. May represent ongoing mediator release.

opts.text: Mild tachycardia (HR 102), tremor, and headache after epinephrine administration
opts.rat: Expected and benign side effects of epinephrine - reassure patient, no intervention needed. Often confused with worsening reaction; differentiated by absence of skin/airway/GI symptoms.

opts.text: Allergy alert updated in EHR to 'Ceftriaxone - ANAPHYLAXIS'; patient educated on avoidance of all cephalosporins; epinephrine auto-injector prescribed and demonstration completed
opts.rat: Critical nursing-led safety outcomes. Documentation prevents re-exposure; auto-injector education prevents fatal community recurrence. Without these steps the patient leaves at risk regardless of acute recovery.

opts.text: Tryptase result returns at 24 hours: 32 ng/mL (drawn during event)
opts.rat: Confirms mast cell degranulation occurred but is a retrospective finding - it does not change current management. Useful for documentation, future allergy evaluation, and outpatient referral.

title: Acute Suicidal Ideation with Plan
subtitle: 29 y/o female, severe MDD, presented voluntarily after 911 call by partner
nursesNote: 0930 - 29 y/o female, brought to psychiatric ED by partner after partner found a written note and called 911. Patient endorses two weeks of worsening depressed mood, anhedonia, hypersomnia, 12-pound weight loss, severe hopelessness, and active suicidal ideation with a specific plan and timeline she had set for tomorrow. PMH: MDD diagnosed at 19, two prior hospitalizations (last 4 years ago), one prior attempt at age 22. Currently prescribed sertraline 100 mg daily; reports stopped taking it 6 weeks ago because 'it wasn't helping anymore.' No EtOH, no substances. Estranged from family of origin; partner is sole support. Patient cooperative, tearful, makes intermittent eye contact, states 'I don't want to feel like this anymore but I'm scared.' Agreed to voluntary admission. Belongings searched and contraband removed per protocol. Placed on 1:1 continuous observation. Psychiatry, social work, and partner notified.

patient.admitDate: Today, 0930 - voluntary admission
patient.room: Behavioral Health Unit, Room 4 (1:1 observation)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish HIGH acute suicide risk in this client.

opts.text: Active suicidal ideation with a specific plan and a defined timeline (tomorrow)
opts.rat: Risk stratification key factors per Columbia Protocol: ideation + PLAN + INTENT + TIMELINE = high acute risk. The progression from ideation to plan with means and timing is the strongest single predictor of imminent action.

opts.text: Prior suicide attempt at age 22 with two prior psychiatric hospitalizations
opts.rat: A prior attempt is the single strongest historical risk factor for completed suicide - increases lifetime risk 30-40 fold. Multiple prior hospitalizations indicate chronic, severe illness with episodic acuity (APA suicide risk guidelines).

opts.text: Discontinued sertraline 6 weeks ago without provider guidance, with worsening symptoms over the past two weeks
opts.rat: Untreated MDD with antidepressant discontinuation is a major modifiable risk factor. Symptom acceleration combined with hopelessness and active planning marks the high-risk window where intervention saves lives.

opts.text: Stable vital signs (HR 78, BP 112/68)
opts.rat: Vital signs are reassuring physiologically but do NOT reflect suicide risk. A common error - calm vitals can coexist with severe psychiatric crisis. The risk lives in the mental status exam and history.

opts.text: Negative drug screen and ethanol
opts.rat: Reassuring (rules out toxic ingestion as cause of presentation) but absence of substances does not lower suicide risk. Sober suicidal ideation can be just as lethal.

opts.text: Cooperative, tearful, makes intermittent eye contact
opts.rat: Engagement with the interview is helpful for therapeutic alliance but is NOT a low-risk indicator. Many people who die by suicide appear calm or cooperative in the days before. Do not let demeanor reduce vigilance.

opts.text: Voluntary admission status
opts.rat: Voluntary admission supports therapeutic alliance but does not lower clinical risk. Patients can rescind voluntary status; safety planning and observation level are based on clinical risk, not admission status.

opts.text: TSH within normal limits
opts.rat: Rules out a medical contributor (hypothyroidism mimicking depression) - important workup but not a current risk cue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying clinical reasoning.

opts.text: Hopelessness is the strongest psychological predictor of suicide - more predictive than depression severity itself - and demands direct assessment and active intervention
opts.rat: Beck's research established hopelessness as the key cognitive feature distinguishing those who attempt from those who don't. Targeted by safety planning, behavioral activation, and CBT - not just antidepressants.

opts.text: Means restriction (removing access to the planned method and other lethal means) is one of the most evidence-based suicide prevention interventions - it reduces deaths even without changing underlying suicidal thoughts
opts.rat: Means restriction works because suicidal crises are often time-limited (minutes to hours). Reducing access during the crisis window prevents impulsive lethal action. NIMH and CDC class IA recommendation.

opts.text: Therapeutic communication uses open-ended questions, validation, and active listening rather than reassurance, false promises, or 'why' questions - the goal is to keep the patient talking and feeling heard, not to fix the feeling
opts.rat: Foundational psychiatric nursing principle. 'Why did you try to kill yourself?' is non-therapeutic; 'Tell me what was happening for you that day' invites dialogue. Reassurance ('You'll feel better tomorrow') minimizes the patient's experience.

opts.text: The nurse should make a 'no-suicide contract' with the patient as the first-line safety intervention
opts.rat: OUTDATED and POTENTIALLY HARMFUL practice. No-suicide contracts have NO evidence of preventing suicide and may give false reassurance to staff. Replaced by collaborative safety PLANNING (Stanley-Brown Safety Plan) which identifies warning signs, coping skills, supports, and means restriction.

opts.text: Asking directly about suicidal thoughts implants the idea and increases risk
opts.rat: MYTH. Direct, calm assessment about suicidal ideation does NOT increase risk and is essential to accurate risk stratification (multiple meta-analyses). Avoiding the topic reinforces stigma and isolation.

opts.text: Antidepressants like sertraline rapidly reverse suicidal thoughts within 24-48 hours of restart
opts.rat: SSRIs take 4-6 weeks to reach full antidepressant effect. The first 1-2 weeks may carry slightly increased suicide risk in young adults due to early activation effects (FDA black-box warning). This is why safety supervision and close follow-up matter most early in treatment.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the diagnoses/contributors from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Major Depressive Disorder, severe, recurrent, with active suicidal ideation and plan
opts.rat: Meets MDD criteria (>=5 SIGECAPS for >2 weeks), severe (functional impairment + active SI with plan), recurrent (multiple prior episodes). Primary diagnosis.

opts.text: Antidepressant discontinuation contributing to symptom acceleration
opts.rat: Stopping SSRI 6 weeks ago aligns with the 2-week worsening - relapse versus discontinuation effects (especially with SSRIs, dizziness, flu-like symptoms, mood worsening) overlap. Modifiable contributor.

opts.text: Adjustment disorder with depressed mood
opts.rat: Could be considered but the recurrent severe pattern, prior attempt, and biological signs (weight loss, hypersomnia) point well beyond adjustment-level pathology.

opts.text: Bipolar depression (depressive episode of bipolar disorder)
opts.rat: Important to assess - missing a bipolar diagnosis means missing the need for mood stabilizer rather than just antidepressant. Screen for prior hypomanic/manic episodes; not strongly suggested by current history.

opts.text: Substance-induced mood disorder
opts.rat: Negative drug screen and no substance use history make this very unlikely. Always screened but ruled out here.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical, evidence-based interventions for this client right now.

opts.text: Maintain 1:1 continuous observation by trained staff with clear sight lines at all times, including during bathroom use
opts.rat: 1:1 observation is the standard for high acute risk and has the strongest safety evidence. Bathrooms and shift changes are higher-risk moments - sight lines must be uninterrupted. Per Joint Commission patient safety standards.

opts.text: Conduct a thorough environmental safety check of the room and complete contraband search of belongings
opts.rat: Removing potential means is a class IA suicide prevention intervention. Inventory and document every removed item. Behavioral health units use ligature-resistant fixtures, breakaway curtain rods, and tamper-resistant electrical outlets.

opts.text: Use therapeutic communication: validate her feelings, ask open-ended questions, allow silence, and convey that recovery is possible without minimizing her pain
opts.rat: Therapeutic alliance is itself an evidence-based intervention. Patients who feel heard are more likely to disclose worsening ideation, accept treatment, and remain in care. The nurse-patient relationship is medicine.

opts.text: Tell the patient 'Everything is going to be okay' and 'Don't worry, this will pass'
opts.rat: NON-THERAPEUTIC. False reassurance minimizes the patient's experience and can damage trust. Acknowledge what she said: 'It sounds like you've been carrying something incredibly heavy. I'd like to understand more.'

opts.text: Ask the patient 'Why would you want to do that to yourself? Think about how your partner would feel.'
opts.rat: NON-THERAPEUTIC and harmful. 'Why' questions induce defensiveness and shame. Invoking guilt about loved ones can deepen hopelessness ('I'm a burden, they'd be better off') - a cognitive distortion frequently present in suicidal depression.

opts.text: Ask her to sign a 'no-suicide contract' as a condition of treatment
opts.rat: Outdated practice with no evidence base. Replaced by collaborative Safety Planning (Stanley-Brown), which is evidence-based and teaches usable skills.

opts.text: Initiate a collaborative Stanley-Brown Safety Plan: warning signs, internal coping, social distractions, people to contact, professional resources, and means restriction
opts.rat: Wait - this is also strongly evidence-based and class I recommended. It would replace one of the others if the question allowed four. The TOP three for the immediate moment are observation, environmental safety, and therapeutic communication; safety planning happens once she is settled and engaged.

opts.text: Order PRN haloperidol IM for any expression of distress
opts.rat: Inappropriate. The patient is calm, cooperative, and not agitated. Routine chemical sedation in a non-agitated patient violates least-restrictive principles and damages therapeutic alliance.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Confirm 1:1 observation is in place with trained staff present and sight lines clear; verify environmental safety of the room
opts.rat: FIRST - patient safety is the top priority. Continuous observation and environmental control prevent immediate harm and create the conditions for everything else.

opts.text: Complete the suicide risk assessment using a standardized tool (e.g., Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale) and document baseline; communicate level of risk to the team
opts.rat: SECOND - structured risk stratification guides intensity of monitoring, medication decisions, and length of stay. Standardized tools reduce assessment variability across providers.

opts.text: Sit with the patient using therapeutic communication; explore what brought her in, what she finds meaningful, what has helped her through past episodes
opts.rat: THIRD - therapeutic alliance is the foundation of recovery. Time spent listening is not 'doing nothing' - it is the core nursing intervention in psychiatric care.

opts.text: Collaborate on a Stanley-Brown Safety Plan; coordinate with psychiatry on restarting/adjusting medications and consider scheduling intensive follow-up
opts.rat: FOURTH - once therapeutic alliance is established, safety planning becomes a productive collaboration rather than a checkbox. Patient buy-in is essential to plan effectiveness.

opts.text: Coordinate with social work and the partner regarding home environment safety, lethal means counseling, and post-discharge follow-up
opts.rat: LAST in the immediate sequence but critical for transition. The post-discharge window is high-risk; concrete follow-up appointments within 7 days, partner education, and means restriction at home substantially reduce post-discharge suicide rates.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 72 hours of inpatient care, classify each finding.

opts.text: Patient denies active suicidal ideation, describes one reason for living, and engaged in group therapy twice today
opts.rat: Reduction in active SI plus emergence of protective factors (reasons for living, treatment engagement) marks meaningful clinical improvement. Continue current plan; do not rush discharge.

opts.text: Sleep improved from 14 to 8 hours nightly; ate full meals today; participated in unit milieu
opts.rat: Restoration of basic biological functioning (sleep, appetite) and behavioral activation precede mood improvement on antidepressants. Concrete recovery markers.

opts.text: Patient completed a Stanley-Brown Safety Plan with the team and identified 3 personal coping strategies, 2 social contacts, and the crisis line as resources
opts.rat: An internalized, personalized safety plan reduces post-discharge risk. The plan only works if the patient helped build it - readiness for safety planning indicates readiness for the next level of care.

opts.text: Patient stating 'I'm fine, I want to go home today' while declining to discuss safety plan or follow-up
opts.rat: Sudden 'flight into health' with refusal to engage in safety planning is a RED FLAG, not improvement. May indicate hidden plan to attempt at home. Maintain observation, engage psychiatry, do NOT expedite discharge.

opts.text: On day 2, patient asks staff if she can keep her shoelaces and asks repeated detailed questions about discharge timing
opts.rat: Probing for access to means and discharge timing can signal planning. Reassess SI directly, increase observation, document, communicate with the team.

opts.text: Mild nausea and headache started after sertraline restarted at 50 mg
opts.rat: Common, time-limited SSRI side effects. Reassure, hydrate, take with food. Not a reason to stop; resolves typically within 1-2 weeks. Educate about FDA black-box warning and that it does not indicate worsening illness.

opts.text: Tearful at times during therapy but able to articulate emotions and identify triggers
opts.rat: Tearfulness is not deterioration when accompanied by emotional engagement - it indicates the patient is processing rather than avoiding. Healthy grief work is part of recovery.

opts.text: Discharge plan includes follow-up appointment with outpatient psychiatry in 5 days, therapy in 7 days, partner trained on safety plan, and home means restriction completed
opts.rat: Concrete, scheduled, post-discharge follow-up within 7 days is associated with substantially lower post-discharge suicide rates. This is the most important nursing-led discharge outcome.

title: Acute Kidney Injury with Indications for Dialysis
subtitle: 67 y/o male, oliguria and rising creatinine 5 days post-CT angiogram
nursesNote: 1530 - 67 y/o male admitted to telemetry 5 days ago for chest pain workup. Underwent CT pulmonary angiogram with IV iodinated contrast on hospital day 1 (PE ruled out). PMH: HTN, type 2 diabetes (A1C 8.4%), CKD stage 3 (baseline Cr 1.6), recent ibuprofen use for back pain. Home meds include lisinopril, metformin, hydrochlorothiazide, and a recent course of vancomycin started yesterday for cellulitis. Today reports 4 lbs weight gain over 24 hours, mild dyspnea, decreased urine output (less than 100 mL over the last 8 hours via Foley), nausea, generalized fatigue, and one episode of vomiting. On exam: bilateral lower extremity edema 2+, fine crackles in posterior bases, mild pericardial friction rub auscultated. Mentation slightly clouded compared to baseline per family. ECG shows peaked T-waves in precordial leads. Nephrology consulted; emergent labs drawn; vascular access for possible hemodialysis being considered.

patient.name: F. Yusupov
patient.admitDate: Hospital Day 5
patient.room: Tele-4, Bed 12

labs.v: 4.8 mg/dL
labs.r: 0.7-1.3 mg/dL (baseline 1.6)

labs.v: 84 mg/dL
labs.r: 7-20 mg/dL

labs.v: 6.4 mEq/L
16 mEq/L
labs.r: 22-26 mEq/L

labs.v: 7.8 mg/dL
9.2 g/dL

labs.r: <1% pre-renal; >2% intrinsic

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish indications for emergent hemodialysis (AEIOU criteria) in this client.

opts.text: Severe hyperkalemia (K+ 6.4) with peaked T-waves on ECG and bradycardia (HR dropping to 62)
opts.rat: The 'E' in AEIOU - Electrolyte derangement, specifically hyperkalemia refractory to medical management or with ECG changes. Peaked T-waves with new bradycardia in a patient who cannot excrete potassium = imminent cardiac arrest risk. Class I dialysis indication.

opts.text: Severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.24, HCO3 16) refractory to bicarbonate therapy
opts.rat: The 'A' in AEIOU - Acidosis. pH <7.2 with inability to compensate (kidneys can't excrete acid) is a class I dialysis indication. Sodium bicarb is a temporizing measure but cannot replace renal function.

opts.text: Volume overload with pulmonary edema (crackles, dyspnea, SpO2 90%, 4-lb weight gain in 24 hr) in an oliguric patient
opts.rat: The 'O' in AEIOU - Overload (fluid). Oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/hr means diuretics often fail. Pulmonary edema unresponsive to diuretics in an anuric/oliguric patient is a class I dialysis indication.

opts.text: BUN 84 mg/dL
opts.rat: The 'U' in AEIOU is UREMIC SYMPTOMS (pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding) - not the BUN number alone. Many patients tolerate BUN 100+ without symptoms. The pericardial rub IS a uremic symptom, but the BUN number itself is not a cue for dialysis.

opts.text: Hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL
opts.rat: Anemia of CKD - chronic and not a dialysis-emergent cue. Treated with EPO and iron, not acute dialysis.

opts.text: FENa 2.8%
opts.rat: FENa >2% supports INTRINSIC AKI (likely ATN here - contrast + NSAIDs + vancomycin trifecta) rather than pre-renal. Important for diagnosis but not an indication for dialysis.

opts.text: Phosphorus 7.8 mg/dL
opts.rat: Hyperphosphatemia is common in AKI and chronic in CKD - chronic problem managed with phosphate binders. Not an emergent dialysis criterion alone.
Volume overload is contributing, but BP itself is not part of the AEIOU criteria. Will improve with volume removal during dialysis.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: This patient has multiple stacked nephrotoxic insults: IV iodinated contrast, NSAIDs (ibuprofen), ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), and vancomycin - the result is acute tubular necrosis superimposed on baseline CKD
opts.rat: Classic 'nephrotoxic stew.' Each of these is a known kidney insult; in combination, especially in a CKD patient with diabetes, ATN is highly likely. FENa >2% supports intrinsic kidney injury rather than pre-renal.

opts.text: Hyperkalemia in AKI is dangerous because the failed kidney cannot excrete potassium AND metabolic acidosis drives K+ out of cells into the bloodstream - the two mechanisms compound each other
opts.rat: Acidosis-hyperkalemia coupling: every 0.1 drop in pH raises serum K+ by ~0.6 mEq/L. Treating acidosis with bicarbonate also helps lower K+. Why both must be addressed concurrently.

opts.text: A pericardial friction rub in this clinical context represents uremic pericarditis - a serious uremic complication and an indication for urgent dialysis to prevent tamponade
opts.rat: Uremic pericarditis is one of the 'U' (uremic symptoms) class I dialysis indications. Untreated, it can progress to pericardial effusion and tamponade. A nursing assessment finding (the rub) directly drives the treatment decision.

opts.text: Metformin should be continued in AKI because it improves glycemic control and reduces cardiovascular risk
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Metformin must be HELD in AKI - accumulation causes lactic acidosis (high mortality). One of the highest-yield NCLEX 'meds to hold' concepts. Resume only after renal recovery.

opts.text: ACE inhibitors are renal-protective and should be uptitrated during AKI
opts.rat: OPPOSITE. ACE inhibitors are renal-protective in CHRONIC CKD/diabetes but must be HELD in acute AKI - they reduce glomerular filtration pressure and worsen the injury. Restart cautiously after recovery.

opts.text: Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) is preferred over intermittent hemodialysis only when the patient is hemodynamically unstable
opts.rat: While true that CRRT is preferred in unstable patients (gentler fluid removal), this patient is currently hypertensive and hemodynamically stable. The acid/electrolyte derangements are immediate threats - intermittent hemodialysis is appropriate. The choice depends on stability, not renal failure severity alone.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the etiologies of this AKI from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Acute tubular necrosis (intrinsic AKI) from cumulative nephrotoxins (contrast, NSAIDs, vancomycin) on baseline CKD
opts.rat: FENa >2%, multiple stacked nephrotoxic exposures, CKD baseline, and time course (peaks 3-5 days post-contrast) all fit. Most likely cause.

opts.text: Pre-renal AKI from volume depletion or hemodynamic compromise
opts.rat: Diuretic and ACE inhibitor use raise possibility, but FENa >2% and volume OVERLOAD findings argue against. Common AKI cause overall.

opts.text: Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) from medication (vancomycin)
opts.rat: Vancomycin can cause AIN. Classic findings (eosinophilia, eosinophiluria, rash) not described but should be sought. Distinguishable on biopsy if needed.

opts.text: Post-renal AKI from urinary obstruction
opts.rat: Foley draining (excludes outlet obstruction), but BPH-related obstruction or stones still possible. Renal ultrasound would rule out hydronephrosis - standard workup in AKI.

opts.text: Hepatorenal syndrome
opts.rat: Requires advanced liver disease, which is not described. Unlikely.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 30 minutes.

opts.text: Administer IV calcium gluconate 1 g over 2-3 min for cardiac membrane stabilization given peaked T-waves and bradycardia
opts.rat: FIRST hyperkalemia treatment when ECG changes are present. Calcium does NOT lower potassium but stabilizes the cardiac membrane within minutes, buying time for K-lowering interventions. Class I emergency intervention.

opts.text: Administer IV regular insulin 10 units with D50W 25-50 g, plus albuterol nebulizer 10-20 mg
opts.rat: Insulin shifts K+ intracellularly within 15-30 min; D50W prevents hypoglycemia (a common error to forget). Albuterol provides additional 0.5-1 mEq/L K+ reduction via beta-2 mechanism. Both temporize while dialysis is set up.

opts.text: Mobilize nephrology and vascular access teams for emergent hemodialysis (likely temporary catheter placement); HOLD metformin, lisinopril, ibuprofen, and HCTZ; renal-dose vancomycin
opts.rat: Definitive treatment is dialysis - temporizing measures only buy hours. Holding nephrotoxic and renally-cleared drugs prevents further injury and toxicity. Vancomycin must be redosed by levels in AKI, not interval.

opts.text: Administer IV furosemide 80 mg push to induce diuresis
opts.rat: Largely INEFFECTIVE in established oliguric AKI (the kidney can't respond). May be tried but is not a primary intervention here. Does not lower K+ acutely. Volume removal in this patient requires dialysis.

opts.text: Administer 1 L 0.9% NS bolus to improve renal perfusion
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Patient is fluid-OVERLOADED with pulmonary edema. Adding more volume worsens the lung picture. This is a cue students miss - 'AKI = give fluids' is wrong when overload is the primary problem.

opts.text: Continue ibuprofen for back pain and lisinopril for HTN
opts.rat: Both must be HELD - they are direct nephrotoxins in this scenario. Use acetaminophen for pain (no kidney effect) and a non-nephrotoxic antihypertensive (e.g., amlodipine) if needed.

opts.text: Increase IV ibuprofen to manage pain and reduce inflammation
opts.rat: Distractor reinforcing the wrong instinct. NSAIDs are absolutely contraindicated in AKI.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Place on continuous cardiac monitor with defibrillator at bedside; obtain stat 12-lead ECG; ensure patent IV access
opts.rat: FIRST - hyperkalemic arrest is the most immediate threat. Monitoring and defib readiness are pre-intervention safety steps.

opts.text: Administer IV calcium gluconate per protocol for cardiac membrane stabilization
opts.rat: SECOND - immediately address the cardiac threat from peaked T-waves and bradycardia. Onset is minutes.

opts.text: Administer insulin + dextrose and nebulized albuterol to shift K+ intracellularly; recheck K+ in 30 min
opts.rat: THIRD - actually lower the K+ (calcium did not). Both interventions begin temporizing the hyperkalemia while dialysis is arranged. Glucose monitoring is essential afterward.

opts.text: Apply high-flow oxygen, sit patient upright, hold all nephrotoxic medications, notify nephrology that hemodialysis is needed urgently
opts.rat: FOURTH - address the pulmonary edema with positioning and oxygen while removing ongoing kidney insults. Nephrology determines vascular access (temporary HD catheter vs existing access) and timing.

opts.text: Educate patient and family on the AKI cause, dialysis plan, dietary restrictions (low K+, low phosphorus, fluid restriction), and post-recovery monitoring
opts.rat: LAST in this immediate sequence - critical for outcome but follows medical stabilization. Family understanding improves adherence to follow-up and dietary changes that can preserve renal function.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After emergent hemodialysis and 24 hours of supportive care, classify each finding.

opts.text: K+ post-dialysis 4.2 mEq/L; ECG with normal T-wave morphology; HR 78 in normal sinus rhythm
opts.rat: Successful dialytic correction of life-threatening hyperkalemia. ECG normalization is the most reliable bedside marker of cardiac safety.

opts.text: pH 7.36, HCO3 22; respiratory effort comfortable; SpO2 96% on 2 L NC after 2 L of fluid removal during dialysis
opts.rat: Acidosis corrected and pulmonary edema substantially improved with ultrafiltration during HD. The combined acid/volume problems are why dialysis is uniquely effective here.

opts.text: Pericardial friction rub no longer audible; patient denies chest pain; bedside echo shows no pericardial effusion
opts.rat: Resolution of uremic pericarditis with effective dialysis. Continued surveillance because effusion can develop later in the course.

opts.text: Sudden hypotension to BP 76/44 during the second hour of dialysis with cramping and nausea
opts.rat: Dialysis-related hypotension - common (10-20% of sessions) but can worsen ischemic injury to recovering kidneys. Slow ultrafiltration rate, give saline bolus through dialysis circuit, recheck. Repeated episodes indicate need for CRRT instead of intermittent HD.

opts.text: Phosphorus 8.4 mg/dL despite dialysis; calcium-phosphorus product elevated
opts.rat: Phosphorus is dialyzed slowly - often rebounds. Initiate phosphate binder with meals (calcium acetate or sevelamer), low-phosphorus diet. Persistent elevation contributes to vascular calcification long-term.

opts.text: Hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL, stable from 9.2 yesterday
opts.rat: Anemia of CKD is a chronic feature. Stable hemoglobin without active bleeding is reassuring; long-term EPO and iron supplementation will be needed if dialysis becomes ongoing.

opts.text: Urine output 8 mL/hr at 24 hours post-dialysis; creatinine still 4.6 mg/dL
opts.rat: Most ATN takes days to weeks to recover - oliguria can persist or pass through anuric, oliguric, and diuretic phases before normal output returns. This is expected, not failure.

opts.text: Patient and family verbalize understanding of medication holds, follow-up nephrology appointment, low-potassium and low-phosphorus diet, and signs requiring return to ED
opts.rat: Discharge readiness criteria for AKI patients. Adherence to dietary restrictions and avoidance of further nephrotoxins is the most important determinant of long-term renal outcome.

title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)
subtitle: 78 y/o female with T2DM, found confused after 4 days of vomiting from UTI
nursesNote: 1815 - 78 y/o female brought to ED by daughter who found her confused, lethargic, and unable to stand at home. Per daughter, mother had nausea/vomiting and dysuria for 4 days, ate and drank very little, and stopped taking her metformin and glipizide because she 'wasn't eating.' PMH: T2DM x 22 years (last A1C 8.6%), HTN, CKD stage 2, CAD with prior MI 6 years ago. Lives alone; daughter visits weekly. On exam: skin tenting, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, tachycardia, no Kussmaul respirations, breath without fruity odor, mild bibasilar crackles, suprapubic tenderness, urine cloudy. Foley placed - 80 mL dark concentrated urine. Patient oriented to person only, drowsy but arousable, follows simple commands inconsistently. Glucose check on arrival = 'HI' (>600 on glucometer). Two large-bore IVs placed; aggressive fluid resuscitation started; ICU admission pending; sepsis workup in progress.

patient.name: M. Greenfield
patient.admitDate: Today, 1815
patient.room: ED-Resus 4

labs.v: 942 mg/dL
362 mOsm/kg
labs.r: 275-295 mOsm/kg

labs.v: 128 mEq/L (corrected ~143)
5.2 mEq/L
62 mg/dL
labs.r: 7-20 mg/dL

labs.v: 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.2)
20 mEq/L
labs.r: 22-26 mEq/L

labs.v: 0.6 mmol/L
labs.r: <0.6 mmol/L

labs.v: Large LE, +nitrites, >50 WBC
labs.r: Negative

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish HHS rather than DKA in this client.

opts.text: Glucose 942 mg/dL with serum osmolality 362 mOsm/kg and altered mental status
opts.rat: ADA HHS criteria: glucose >600, osmolality >320, AMS - all present. HHS glucose runs MUCH higher than DKA (typically 250-600). Osmolality drives the neurologic findings - the higher the osm, the worse the mentation.

opts.text: Minimal acidosis (pH 7.34, HCO3 20) with NEGATIVE ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate 0.6)
opts.rat: THE distinguishing lab feature. HHS patients still produce some insulin - enough to suppress ketogenesis but not enough to control glucose. DKA = pH <7.30, HCO3 <18, large ketones; HHS = mild acidosis, minimal/no ketones (ADA 2009 consensus).

opts.text: Severe dehydration in an older T2DM patient with infection precipitant (UTI), no Kussmaul respirations, no fruity breath
opts.rat: Classic HHS demographic and trigger. HHS develops over DAYS-WEEKS in older T2DM patients with infection or stopped meds, vs DKA which develops over HOURS in younger T1DM patients. The absence of Kussmaul/fruity breath rules out significant ketoacidosis.

opts.text: Heart rate 124 with BP 94/56
opts.rat: Reflects volume depletion - present in BOTH HHS and DKA. Doesn't distinguish between them.

opts.text: Potassium 5.2 mEq/L
opts.rat: Mild hyperkalemia from acidosis-driven cellular shift occurs in BOTH conditions. Total body K+ is depleted in both. Not a distinguishing feature.

opts.text: Hyponatremia (Na+ 128)
opts.rat: Pseudohyponatremia from hyperglycemia (glucose draws water into vasculature, diluting sodium). Corrects to ~143 with the correction formula. Occurs in both HHS and DKA - not distinguishing.

opts.text: Cloudy urine with positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites
opts.rat: Identifies the PRECIPITANT (UTI) - extremely important clinically but doesn't distinguish HHS from DKA. Both conditions can be triggered by infection.

opts.text: Stopped metformin and glipizide due to poor PO intake
opts.rat: Common HHS trigger ('sick day' med non-adherence) but also a common DKA trigger. Not distinguishing.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link HHS pathophysiology and management.

opts.text: HHS patients still produce small amounts of endogenous insulin - enough to suppress lipolysis and ketogenesis, but not enough to drive glucose into cells - which is why hyperglycemia is extreme but ketosis is absent or minimal
opts.rat: Core HHS mechanism distinguishing it from DKA. The 'just enough insulin to prevent ketosis' state is why HHS labs look so different despite higher glucose. NCLEX classic concept (Lewis Med-Surg 11e).

opts.text: Profound osmotic diuresis from glucosuria causes massive volume losses (typically 8-12 L deficit in HHS vs 6-8 L in DKA) - aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation is the FIRST priority, before insulin
opts.rat: HHS volume deficits are LARGER than DKA. ADA recommends 1-1.5 L of 0.9% NS in the first hour, followed by 0.45% NS based on corrected sodium. Insulin without fluids first can cause cardiovascular collapse - fluids restore perfusion.

opts.text: Insulin in HHS should be started ONLY AFTER fluid resuscitation has begun and serum potassium is confirmed >=3.3 mEq/L - and at LOWER doses than DKA because HHS responds dramatically to fluids alone
opts.rat: Critical safety point shared with DKA. HHS glucose often drops 75-100 mg/dL/hr from fluid alone. Insulin doses in HHS are typically 0.05-0.1 U/kg/hr (lower than DKA) to avoid rapid osmotic shifts and cerebral edema.

opts.text: Glucose should be lowered as rapidly as possible to <100 mg/dL within the first 2-4 hours
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Rapid osmotic correction risks cerebral edema (more common in younger DKA patients but still concerning in HHS) and hemodynamic collapse. Target glucose decrease of 50-75 mg/dL/hr; transition to D5 in fluids when glucose hits 250-300 to allow continued insulin without hypoglycemia.

opts.text: HHS patients should be NPO and given no oral fluids until full mental status returns
opts.rat: Once airway is protected and mentation improves, oral fluids supplement IV resuscitation. The point is volume restoration, not arbitrary NPO status. Aspiration risk dictates positioning, not absolute NPO.

opts.text: HHS has lower mortality than DKA due to its slower onset and milder acidosis
opts.rat: OPPOSITE. HHS mortality (~15-20%) is HIGHER than DKA (~1-5%) because HHS patients are older with more comorbidities, deeper volume deficits, and the precipitating infection is often the proximate cause of death. Common NCLEX trap.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the diagnoses and contributors from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State precipitated by UTI in a T2DM patient with medication non-adherence
opts.rat: Glucose 942, osm 362, AMS, no ketosis, classic demographic and trigger. Diagnostic.

opts.text: Urosepsis with associated AKI
opts.rat: Cloudy urine + positive UA + dehydration + AKI (Cr doubled from baseline) + hypotension - highly likely concurrent process. Treating the infection is essential to HHS recovery.

opts.text: DKA from missed insulin and infection
opts.rat: Symptoms overlap but ketones are negative, acidosis is mild, and patient has T2DM (rare to develop true DKA). Worth ruling out, ruled out by labs.

opts.text: Acute stroke causing the altered mental status
opts.rat: Always considered with new AMS in elderly. Hyperosmolality fully explains AMS here, but neuro exam and possible CT during workup are appropriate.

opts.text: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock
opts.rat: Prior MI history makes this worth considering. Volume status and HHS labs explain the picture. ECG and troponin are part of the workup.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the first hour.

opts.text: Initiate aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation: 0.9% NS 1-1.5 L over the first hour, then reassess and transition based on corrected sodium and hemodynamics
opts.rat: Volume restoration is FIRST in HHS - more so than DKA. Fluids alone often drop glucose 75-100 mg/dL/hr without insulin. ADA HHS protocol class I.

opts.text: Obtain blood and urine cultures, then administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within the first hour for the precipitating UTI/possible urosepsis
opts.rat: The HHS does not resolve until the precipitant is treated. Sepsis bundle applies - cultures before antibiotics, antibiotics within 1 hour. Untreated infection = persistent HHS = mortality.

opts.text: Verify K+ >=3.3 mEq/L, then start IV regular insulin infusion at 0.05-0.1 U/kg/hr; recheck glucose hourly with goal decrease 50-75 mg/dL/hr
opts.rat: Insulin AFTER fluids and AFTER K+ check. Lower starting dose than DKA reflects HHS pathophysiology. Hourly monitoring and gradual correction prevent osmotic complications.

opts.text: Administer IV regular insulin 10-unit bolus immediately upon arrival to drop glucose rapidly
opts.rat: DANGEROUS in HHS. Insulin without prior fluids causes intravascular volume to shift further (water follows glucose into cells), worsening hypotension and tissue hypoperfusion. ADA explicitly recommends against bolus insulin in HHS.

opts.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate IV for the metabolic acidosis
opts.rat: Acidosis is mild (pH 7.34) and not driven by significant ketones. Bicarbonate is reserved for pH <7.0 in DKA and is rarely indicated in HHS. Address with fluids and insulin.

opts.text: Restart oral metformin and glipizide once IV access is in place
opts.rat: Both must be HELD acutely. Metformin is contraindicated with AKI (lactic acidosis risk). Sulfonylureas can cause prolonged hypoglycemia in renal impairment. Resume after recovery and renal function normalization.

opts.text: Transfuse 2 units PRBCs prophylactically
opts.rat: No evidence of bleeding or anemia. Inappropriate use of blood products.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Place on continuous cardiac monitor, ensure two large-bore IVs patent, initiate 0.9% NS bolus 1 L wide open, position with airway protection given AMS
opts.rat: FIRST - circulation, IV access, and airway safety. Volume resuscitation is the immediate priority and addresses both shock and HHS pathophysiology simultaneously.

opts.text: Draw labs (CBC, BMP, ABG, beta-hydroxybutyrate, lactate, blood cultures x2, magnesium, phosphorus); obtain urine culture; place Foley for strict I&O
opts.rat: SECOND - diagnostic confirmation and tracking response. Cultures BEFORE antibiotics to preserve diagnostic yield. Hourly UO is the bedside marker of resuscitation success.

opts.text: Administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for urosepsis (e.g., ceftriaxone or piperacillin-tazobactam per local antibiogram)
opts.rat: THIRD - within 1 hour of recognition. Treating the precipitant is essential; HHS persists until infection is controlled.

opts.text: Verify K+ >=3.3 mEq/L; start IV insulin infusion at 0.05-0.1 U/kg/hr; recheck glucose, K+, and Na+ hourly; transition fluids to 0.45% NS based on corrected sodium; add D5 to IVF when glucose <300
opts.rat: FOURTH - insulin only after fluids underway and K+ confirmed safe. Hourly recheck protocol prevents complications. D5 addition prevents rebound hypoglycemia while still treating any residual hyperosmolality.

opts.text: Coordinate ICU admission; SBAR to receiving team; update daughter on diagnosis, plan, and prognosis; arrange diabetes education for post-stabilization
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - higher level of care needed for ongoing monitoring. Family education and diabetes self-management support are essential to prevent recurrence (HHS recurrence rate is high in elderly, isolated patients).

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 12 hours of fluid resuscitation, insulin infusion, and antibiotic therapy, classify each finding.

opts.text: Glucose decreased from 942 to 412 mg/dL at a rate of ~50-60 mg/dL/hr; osmolality 332 mOsm/kg
opts.rat: Controlled, gradual osmotic correction is the goal - rapid drops cause cerebral edema. This trajectory is exactly what's wanted.

opts.text: Mental status returning toward baseline: oriented x3, following complex commands, asking about the dog at home
opts.rat: Mental status improvement closely tracks osmolality correction. Concrete recovery marker - and engagement with family/home concerns suggests cortical function is restoring.

opts.text: BP 128/76, HR 88, urine output 65 mL/hr, lactate cleared from 3.2 to 1.4 mmol/L
opts.rat: Hemodynamic and tissue perfusion recovery confirms successful volume resuscitation. UO >0.5 mL/kg/hr is the renal marker; lactate clearance is the tissue marker.

opts.text: Glucose dropped to 38 mg/dL with diaphoresis and confusion; insulin still infusing at 0.1 U/kg/hr
opts.rat: Iatrogenic hypoglycemia - dangerous, especially in elderly. Stop insulin infusion, give D50 IV bolus, recheck in 15 min, then resume insulin at lower dose with D10 fluids. Common error: not adding dextrose when glucose hits 250-300.

opts.text: K+ dropped to 2.8 mEq/L with new ST depression and frequent PVCs on monitor
opts.rat: Severe hypokalemia from insulin-driven cellular shift - the same mechanism as DKA. Hold insulin until K+ replaced, give IV KCl, continuous cardiac monitoring. Hypokalemia is the most common AVOIDABLE complication of treatment.

opts.text: BUN 58, Creatinine 1.6 (down from 2.4); urine output trending up
opts.rat: Pre-renal AKI improving with volume restoration - the kidney was ischemic, not damaged. Continue holding nephrotoxic meds; monitor for further recovery toward baseline 1.2.

opts.text: Pretibial edema 1+ noted, vesicular breath sounds with mild bibasilar crackles unchanged
opts.rat: Mild crackles persist as fluid redistributes after aggressive volume resuscitation. Concerning if worsening (would suggest pulmonary edema in this elderly patient with prior MI) but stable here. Monitor closely; consider gentle diuresis once HHS resolved if volume overload develops.

opts.text: Daughter and patient verbalize understanding of sick-day rules: don't stop insulin/oral agents when ill, check glucose more frequently, push fluids, call provider for persistent vomiting or glucose >300
opts.rat: Sick-day education is the single most important nursing intervention to prevent HHS recurrence. Elderly diabetic patients living alone need clear written instructions and a check-in plan.

title: COPD Exacerbation - Acute Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure
subtitle: 71 y/o male, severe COPD, increasing dyspnea and confusion x 3 days
nursesNote: 0420 - 71 y/o male brought to ED by spouse with worsening dyspnea, productive cough with green sputum, increased home oxygen requirement (baseline 2 L NC, now using 4 L), and progressive lethargy over 3 days. PMH: GOLD stage IV COPD (FEV1 28% predicted), 50 pack-year smoker (quit 6 years ago), home oxygen, two prior intubations for COPD exacerbations, HTN, prior NSTEMI. Home meds: tiotropium, fluticasone-salmeterol, prn albuterol, lisinopril, atorvastatin. Spouse reports patient slept most of yesterday, became confused overnight, this morning was barely arousable. Arrived in tripod position, pursed-lip breathing, accessory muscle use, intercostal retractions, audible wheezing and rhonchi bilaterally, prolonged expiratory phase. Lethargic, oriented to person only, somnolent between questions, asterixis present. Initial RA SpO2 78%; placed on 2 L NC with rise to 89%. EMS report noted SpO2 had transiently been placed on 6 L NC in field with rise to 96%, after which patient became more somnolent. ABG drawn; chest X-ray pending; pulmonary on the way.

patient.name: G. Costanza

patient.admitDate: Today, 0420

labs.r: 35-45 mmHg

labs.v: 58 mmHg (on 2L NC)
labs.r: 80-100 mmHg

labs.v: 32 mEq/L
labs.r: 22-26 mEq/L

labs.v: 1.8 ng/mL
labs.r: <0.5 ng/mL

labs.v: 184 pg/mL
labs.r: <100 pg/mL

labs.v: 0.04 ng/mL
labs.r: <0.04 ng/mL

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (Type II) requiring NIV or intubation.

opts.text: ABG showing acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis: pH 7.21, PaCO2 78, HCO3 32
opts.rat: The pH defines the acuity. Chronic CO2 retainers compensate to a near-normal pH; pH 7.21 means CO2 has risen FAST enough that bicarbonate compensation can't keep up. Acute-on-chronic respiratory failure - dialysis-level priority finding.

opts.text: Lethargy, somnolence, asterixis, oriented to person only - signs of CO2 narcosis
opts.rat: Hypercapnic encephalopathy: CO2 freely crosses the blood-brain barrier and causes acidosis in the CSF. Asterixis (the 'flap') is the classic neurologic sign of metabolic encephalopathy. AMS in a hypercapnic patient signals impending respiratory arrest.

opts.text: Worsening somnolence after EMS placed patient on 6 L NC despite SpO2 climb to 96%
opts.rat: Classic hypoxic-drive concept (still NCLEX-tested though oversimplified in real ICU practice). The bigger phenomenon: high FiO2 worsens V/Q matching and increases CO2 retention via the Haldane effect. Patient got 'better' oxygen number but worse ventilation. Targeted SpO2 88-92% in COPD - not the higher the better.

opts.text: WBC 16.2 with procalcitonin 1.8 and green sputum
opts.rat: Establishes the bacterial PRECIPITANT (likely pneumonia or bacterial bronchitis) - very important for treatment selection. But these don't establish the respiratory failure itself. Distinguishing the trigger from the failure is a useful NCLEX skill.

opts.text: BNP 184 pg/mL
opts.rat: Mildly elevated - could reflect right heart strain (cor pulmonale) common in advanced COPD, or mild fluid overload. Not diagnostic of acute respiratory failure; troponin is normal so cardiac is unlikely primary driver.
Reflects sympathetic surge from work of breathing and hypercapnia - present in many critical illnesses. Not specific for hypercapnic failure.

opts.text: Tripod positioning with pursed-lip breathing
opts.rat: Classic COPD signs of increased work of breathing - support the COPD diagnosis but don't define the acuity. Many stable COPD patients use these techniques baseline.

opts.text: Two prior intubations for COPD exacerbations
opts.rat: Important historical risk factor for failure of NIV and need for intubation - but not a current cue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Severe COPD destroys alveolar walls (emphysema) and obstructs small airways (chronic bronchitis), causing air trapping, hyperinflation, and a flattened diaphragm that has lost its mechanical advantage - all increasing the work of breathing
opts.rat: The structural pathology distinguishes COPD from reversible asthma. Hyperinflation and flat diaphragm are why COPD patients tire so quickly during exacerbations - their respiratory pump has limited reserve (Lewis Med-Surg 11e, p. 522).

opts.text: Bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) reduces work of breathing, recruits alveoli, and improves CO2 clearance - it is FIRST-LINE for COPD exacerbations with hypercapnic acidosis (pH 7.25-7.35) and reduces intubation rates by ~50%
opts.rat: Class IA recommendation (GOLD guidelines). NIV attempted before intubation in cooperative, awake patients reduces ventilator-associated complications. This patient's borderline mentation makes NIV risky but appropriate as a trial - close monitoring required.

opts.text: Targeted oxygen saturation in COPD is 88-92% (not >=94% as in other patients) because over-oxygenation can suppress hypoxic respiratory drive AND worsens V/Q matching, paradoxically increasing CO2 retention via the Haldane effect
opts.rat: Critical NCLEX concept and real clinical practice. Use a Venturi mask or low-flow NC to titrate to 88-92%. The Haldane effect (oxygenated hemoglobin holds less CO2) is the more important mechanism than hypoxic drive suppression.

opts.text: All COPD patients should receive 100% oxygen via non-rebreather to maximize PaO2
opts.rat: DANGEROUS - and exactly the error EMS made. Worsens hypercapnia in chronic CO2 retainers. The instinct of 'more oxygen is better' is wrong in COPD. Titrate to 88-92%.

opts.text: Pursed-lip breathing should be discouraged because it reduces tidal volume
opts.rat: OPPOSITE. Pursed-lip breathing is a beneficial adaptive technique that creates back-pressure to splint open small airways and prevent collapse during expiration. Encouraged in stable COPD; not relevant during severe distress when patients can't sustain it.

opts.text: Beta-blockers must always be held in COPD patients to avoid bronchospasm
opts.rat: Outdated thinking. Cardioselective beta-blockers (metoprolol, bisoprolol) are SAFE in COPD and beneficial for cardiovascular comorbidity. Continue unless severe bronchospasm. Common 'safety net' answer that's actually wrong.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the diagnoses and contributors from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Acute COPD exacerbation with bacterial precipitant (likely pneumonia) causing acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure
opts.rat: Increased dyspnea, sputum purulence, sputum volume - classic Anthonisen criteria for COPD exacerbation. Elevated WBC and procalcitonin support bacterial trigger. Diagnostic.

opts.text: Acute decompensated cor pulmonale (right heart failure from chronic pulmonary hypertension)
opts.rat: BNP elevation suggests possible right heart strain. Common in advanced COPD - chronic hypoxia leads to pulmonary HTN and RV failure. Often coexists with exacerbation, requires gentle diuresis.

opts.text: Pulmonary embolism (PE) precipitating exacerbation
opts.rat: Important to consider - PE is more common in COPD. Sudden worsening, especially without sputum changes, warrants D-dimer and possible CTPA. Less likely here given clear infection picture.

opts.text: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic component
opts.rat: Worth considering given CAD history, but troponin is normal and presentation is clearly respiratory. Continue serial troponins as part of workup.

opts.text: Pneumothorax (spontaneous in emphysematous lung)
opts.rat: Bullous emphysema raises pneumothorax risk - sudden severe dyspnea is classic. CXR rules out. Less likely given gradual 3-day progression.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 30 minutes.

opts.text: Initiate BiPAP (e.g., IPAP 12 / EPAP 5 / FiO2 30%) targeting SpO2 88-92% with continuous reassessment for response within 1-2 hours
opts.rat: First-line for hypercapnic COPD exacerbation per GOLD guidelines. Reduces work of breathing and improves CO2 clearance. Borderline mentation makes this a calculated trial - if no improvement in 1-2 hours, intubate.

opts.text: Administer nebulized albuterol + ipratropium (combination DuoNeb), IV/PO methylprednisolone 40 mg, and IV antibiotic (e.g., ceftriaxone + azithromycin or per local antibiogram)
opts.rat: Triple therapy for COPD exacerbation: bronchodilators (combination beats either alone), systemic steroid (reduces inflammation, shortens recovery), and antibiotics (Anthonisen criteria support bacterial trigger). All three are class I.

opts.text: Reduce supplemental oxygen to titrate SpO2 to 88-92% (NOT higher); use a Venturi mask or low-flow NC; have intubation equipment at bedside
opts.rat: Avoid the EMS error. Targeted oxygen prevents worsening hypercapnia. Intubation readiness is essential because NIV may fail given altered mentation - you don't want to be hunting for equipment when the patient codes.

opts.text: Bolus 2 L 0.9% NS to support BP and increase intravascular volume
opts.rat: Patient is hypertensive (BP 162/94) and may have right heart strain (BNP 184). Aggressive fluids could worsen pulmonary edema component. No volume depletion rationale here.

opts.text: Administer IV morphine 4 mg for the patient's distress and air hunger
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Opioids depress respiratory drive in a patient already in hypercapnic failure - can precipitate respiratory arrest. Reserved for end-of-life comfort care, not active exacerbation management.

opts.text: Administer 100% oxygen via non-rebreather to maximize oxygenation
opts.rat: Repeats the EMS error. Worsens hypercapnia by reversing hypoxic vasoconstriction (V/Q mismatch worsens) and Haldane effect. Will improve SpO2 number while patient deteriorates clinically.

opts.text: Place a chest tube prophylactically given pneumothorax risk in emphysematous lungs
opts.rat: Not indicated without confirmed pneumothorax on imaging. Invasive procedure with real complications - never prophylactic.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Reduce O2 to titrate SpO2 88-92%, place on continuous SpO2/cardiac monitoring, position upright (high Fowler's), have BVM and intubation kit at bedside
opts.rat: FIRST - immediate corrections to monitoring and oxygen. Position maximizes diaphragmatic excursion. Equipment readiness is anticipatory rather than reactive.

opts.text: Initiate BiPAP at IPAP 12/EPAP 5/FiO2 30%; coach patient on synchrony; reassess response over 30-60 minutes (mental status, RR, ABG)
opts.rat: SECOND - the central intervention for hypercapnic failure. Patient cooperation determines NIV success; coaching matters. Reassess at 1-2 hours - if no improvement (especially in pH and mentation), intubation is indicated.

opts.text: Administer DuoNeb (albuterol + ipratropium), IV methylprednisolone, draw blood cultures, administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotic
opts.rat: THIRD - disease-specific therapy. Bronchodilators reduce airflow obstruction; steroid addresses inflammation; antibiotics address the bacterial trigger. Cultures BEFORE antibiotics.

opts.text: Obtain stat chest X-ray and ECG; review prior advance directive with patient/spouse if possible
opts.rat: FOURTH - imaging confirms infiltrate vs pneumothorax vs pulmonary edema. Advance directive review is essential because patient's directive specifies 'no prolonged ventilation' - clarifies goals of care if NIV fails.

opts.text: Coordinate ICU admission, SBAR to receiving team, contact pulmonology, update spouse on treatment plan and contingencies (intubation vs comfort focus per directive)
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - escalation of care and family communication. The advance directive component is crucial - this patient may want NIV as a ceiling of care rather than a bridge to intubation. Honoring patient wishes is part of the nursing role.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 4 hours of BiPAP, bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics, and ICU admission, classify each finding.

opts.text: Repeat ABG: pH 7.34, PaCO2 56, PaO2 72, HCO3 30 on BiPAP
opts.rat: pH normalizing, CO2 trending down - the goal of NIV in hypercapnic failure. Successful response to BiPAP and the strongest predictor of avoiding intubation. CO2 doesn't normalize fully (patient is a chronic retainer); the goal is restoration of his baseline.

opts.text: Mental status improved: oriented x3, conversational, asterixis resolved, asking about spouse
opts.rat: Resolution of CO2 narcosis tracks pH and CO2 normalization. Concrete bedside marker of NIV success. Engagement (asking about spouse) reflects cortical function recovery.

opts.text: Decreased work of breathing: RR 18, no accessory muscle use, no retractions, tolerating BiPAP without anxiety
opts.rat: Successful unloading of respiratory muscles. The diaphragm and accessory muscles are recovering from fatigue - this is exactly the metric BiPAP targets.

opts.text: Sudden severe right-sided chest pain with dyspnea, decreased breath sounds R side, tracheal deviation to left
opts.rat: Tension pneumothorax - feared complication of positive pressure ventilation in bullous emphysema. Stop BiPAP, prepare for emergent needle decompression and chest tube. Advanced COPD with positive pressure = always a possibility.

opts.text: After 2 hours on BiPAP: pH 7.18, PaCO2 88, more somnolent, mask intolerance
opts.rat: NIV FAILURE. Worsening pH and CO2 with declining mentation = intubation indicated. NIV failure rates in severe acidosis (pH <7.25) are 40-60%. Clear, unambiguous criteria for escalation - and this is when his advance directive becomes the most important conversation.

opts.text: K+ 3.2 mEq/L, HR 124, frequent PVCs on monitor
opts.rat: Hypokalemia from beta-agonist therapy (same mechanism as the asthma case) plus steroid effect. Replace potassium IV, continuous cardiac monitoring, recheck in 2 hours.

opts.text: CXR shows new RLL infiltrate confirming bacterial pneumonia; sputum culture growing pansensitive Streptococcus pneumoniae
opts.rat: Identification of organism allows antibiotic narrowing (de-escalation from broad-spectrum) - good antibiotic stewardship and reduces side effects. The 'improving' here is diagnostic clarity, not patient status alone.

opts.text: Spouse and patient reviewed advance directive with team and reaffirmed: NIV is acceptable; intubation is NOT desired; transition to comfort focus if NIV fails
opts.rat: Goals of care alignment is a major nursing-facilitated outcome. Patient autonomy is honored; team avoids non-beneficial interventions; family is prepared for outcomes. This is what good critical care nursing looks like, regardless of the medical trajectory.

title: Severe Alcohol Withdrawal with Delirium Tremens
subtitle: 54 y/o male, hospital day 3 post-op, sudden agitation and hallucinations
nursesNote: 0245 - 54 y/o male, post-op day 3 from open cholecystectomy, called out by night nurse for acute change in behavior. PMH: HTN, hyperlipidemia, history of heavy alcohol use (per family, 'a fifth of vodka daily for 20 years'); patient initially denied use on admission and was not started on a withdrawal protocol. POD#1 he was tremulous and diaphoretic but attributed to post-op pain; POD#2 mildly anxious and slept poorly. Tonight: pulled out his IV, screaming about 'spiders crawling on the wall,' striking out at staff, oriented only to person, drenched in sweat, severe whole-body tremor. Last drink approximately 72 hours ago per family. Wife at bedside, frightened. CIWA-Ar score on initial nurse assessment: 28 (severe). Rapid response activated. ICU bed requested. New IV access being established; safety measures in place; chemistry, magnesium, and CBC drawn.

patient.name: B. McAllister
patient.admitDate: POD#3 cholecystectomy
patient.room: Surgical floor 4-East, transferring to ICU

labs.v: 1.2 mg/dL
1.6 mg/dL
3.1 mEq/L
78 mg/dL
94 U/L
42 mcg/dL
labs.r: 15-45 mcg/dL

labs.v: Negative
labs.r: Negative

labs.v: Negative
labs.r: Negative

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: Identify delirium tremens vs other delirium
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish delirium tremens (DTs) rather than other causes of post-op delirium.

opts.text: Onset 72 hours after last drink with progressive autonomic hyperactivity (HR 102 -> 138, BP 148/92 -> 184/108) and tremor
opts.rat: DT timeline is diagnostic. Withdrawal symptoms 6-12 hr; seizures peak 24-48 hr; DTs peak 48-96 hr. The 72-hour mark + escalating autonomic instability fits perfectly. Other deliriums (UTI, sepsis, hypoxia) don't follow this pattern.

opts.text: Visual hallucinations (spiders on walls), severe agitation with combativeness, disorientation, and severe diaphoresis - all key components of DTs
opts.rat: DSM-5 DT criteria: alcohol withdrawal + delirium (disturbance in attention/awareness) + cognitive disturbance + perceptual disturbances. Visual hallucinations are classic; tactile hallucinations (formication - feeling bugs crawling) also common. CIWA-Ar 28 = severe.

opts.text: Documented heavy chronic alcohol use (~750 mL vodka/day for 20 years) with last drink 72 hours ago, NO ethanol on labs, no other substances
opts.rat: Confirms the trigger. Not all post-op delirium is DTs - the history is critical. Negative ethanol confirms he hasn't been drinking; negative drug screen rules out other withdrawal/intoxication. Pattern locked in.

opts.text: Magnesium 1.2 and phosphorus 1.6
opts.rat: Common in chronic alcoholism (poor nutrition + renal wasting) and contribute to seizure risk - but they don't ESTABLISH DTs. Important to correct as part of treatment, not a diagnostic cue.

opts.text: AST 186 / ALT 94 with AST:ALT ratio >2
opts.rat: AST:ALT >2 is supportive of alcoholic liver disease (the classic 'AST is greater than ALT in Alcoholism' mnemonic) - confirms history of heavy use but doesn't establish acute DTs.

opts.text: Glucose 78 mg/dL
opts.rat: Borderline-low but not hypoglycemic. Normal finding; rules OUT hypoglycemia as cause of AMS, but isn't itself a DT cue.

opts.text: Recent abdominal surgery
opts.rat: Risk factor for post-op delirium broadly - but doesn't distinguish DTs from other post-op deliriums (anesthesia, infection, hypoxia, electrolytes). The history of alcohol use is the differentiator.

opts.text: Ammonia 42 mcg/dL
opts.rat: Normal - rules OUT hepatic encephalopathy as the AMS cause. Useful to exclude alternatives but not a DT cue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Chronic alcohol use causes neuroadaptation: GABA receptor downregulation and NMDA receptor upregulation. When alcohol is suddenly removed, the brain has reduced inhibition AND amplified excitation - causing autonomic surge, seizures, and DTs
opts.rat: Core neurochemistry of withdrawal. Explains why benzodiazepines (GABA agonists) are first-line treatment - they replace the inhibitory tone that alcohol was providing while the brain re-equilibrates.

opts.text: Thiamine MUST be administered BEFORE any glucose-containing fluids in chronic alcoholics to prevent precipitating Wernicke's encephalopathy or Korsakoff syndrome
opts.rat: The 'banana bag' rule. Alcoholics are thiamine-depleted; carbohydrate metabolism uses thiamine; giving glucose first depletes the last reserves and triggers Wernicke's (ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, confusion - which can become permanent Korsakoff amnesia). Standard order: thiamine 100 mg IV, then folate, then glucose.

opts.text: Symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing using CIWA-Ar (e.g., lorazepam 2-4 mg IV q15min until calmed) is preferred over fixed-dose schedules - it treats the patient's actual state, uses less total medication, and shortens treatment duration
opts.rat: Evidence-based standard. Front-loading with CIWA-Ar guidance reduces total benzo exposure, length of stay, and complications vs fixed q4-q6 dosing. Lorazepam preferred in liver disease (no active metabolites). Diazepam is alternative with longer half-life.

opts.text: Magnesium and phosphorus replacement are unnecessary unless levels drop below 1.0 mg/dL
opts.rat: WRONG. Both should be replaced aggressively in alcohol withdrawal. Hypomagnesemia lowers seizure threshold and causes refractory arrhythmias; hypophosphatemia causes muscle weakness and rhabdomyolysis. Replace early.

opts.text: Haloperidol is the first-line treatment for the agitation and hallucinations of DTs
opts.rat: WRONG and DANGEROUS. Antipsychotics LOWER seizure threshold in withdrawal and don't treat the underlying GABAergic deficiency. Reserved for psychotic features unresponsive to adequate benzodiazepine - and even then second-generation agents are preferred. NCLEX trap.

opts.text: Beta-blockers control the autonomic symptoms (tachycardia, hypertension) and treat DTs definitively
opts.rat: WRONG. Beta-blockers MASK autonomic warning signs without treating underlying excitatory toxicity. They can give false reassurance while seizures still develop. Not appropriate as primary or sole therapy.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the diagnoses and contributors from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Delirium tremens (severe alcohol withdrawal) on POD#3 in an unrecognized chronic heavy drinker
opts.rat: Timeline, autonomic surge, hallucinations, agitation, CIWA 28, history of heavy use, negative substances. Clinically diagnostic.

opts.text: ICU/post-op delirium with multiple contributors (sleep deprivation, pain medications, electrolyte derangements)
opts.rat: Important differential - all post-op delirium causes can coexist. The alcohol history makes DTs primary; other contributors should still be addressed.

opts.text: Post-operative sepsis or pneumonia causing delirium
opts.rat: Worth ruling out - infection is a leading cause of post-op delirium. The classic DT pattern argues against sepsis as primary, but cultures and CXR are part of the workup.

opts.text: Wernicke's encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency)
opts.rat: Possible coexisting condition in any alcoholic - classic triad of confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia. Lower probability acutely but treat empirically with thiamine because consequences of missing it are severe (permanent Korsakoff).

opts.text: Hepatic encephalopathy from alcoholic liver disease
opts.rat: Ammonia is normal - rules out. Reasonable to consider given LFT pattern but unlikely to be the current driver.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 30 minutes.

opts.text: Administer IV lorazepam 2-4 mg q15min titrated to CIWA-Ar score until patient is calm but arousable; transfer to ICU for continuous monitoring
opts.rat: Front-loading benzodiazepine is the intervention that prevents seizures and treats the underlying excitotoxicity. ICU monitoring needed for severe DTs (CIWA >20). Some patients need 100+ mg lorazepam in first 24 hr - dose to symptoms, not predetermined ceiling.

opts.text: Administer IV thiamine 100 mg BEFORE any dextrose-containing fluids; follow with multivitamin (folate, B-complex) and magnesium 2 g IV
opts.rat: The 'thiamine-before-glucose' rule prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy precipitation. Magnesium replacement reduces seizure risk and is often profoundly low in alcoholics. Standard 'banana bag' care.

opts.text: Implement safety measures: 1:1 sit-with-patient observation, padded side rails up, bed in lowest position, remove tubing/cords from reach, ensure suction and oxygen at bedside, prepare for possible seizure
opts.rat: DTs carry 5-15% mortality, often from injury during agitation, aspiration during seizure, or autonomic cardiovascular collapse. 1:1 observation is the standard - chemical sedation alone without environmental safety has caused deaths.

opts.text: Administer IV haloperidol 5 mg for the hallucinations and agitation
opts.rat: Lowers seizure threshold and doesn't treat underlying GABA deficiency. Common error to use it for the agitation/hallucinations - benzodiazepines treat both AND prevent seizures.

opts.text: Apply 4-point physical restraints as the primary intervention for combativeness
opts.rat: Restraints are LAST resort, not first-line. Increase agitation, autonomic activation, rhabdomyolysis risk, and aspiration. Adequate chemical sedation usually obviates need; if used, must be with continuous observation and frequent assessment per CMS standards.

opts.text: Initiate IV beta-blocker (metoprolol) to control HR 138 and BP 184/108
opts.rat: Masks autonomic warning signs without treating cause. The HR and BP will normalize as benzo loading achieves control. Beta-blockers can lead to false reassurance and undertreatment.

opts.text: Administer IV phenytoin loading dose to prevent withdrawal seizures
opts.rat: Phenytoin does NOT prevent alcohol withdrawal seizures - those are GABA-mediated, not focal. Benzodiazepines prevent withdrawal seizures. Phenytoin only useful if patient has independent seizure disorder.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Establish 1:1 observation, secure new IV access (large bore), apply continuous cardiac and SpO2 monitoring, ensure suction and oxygen at bedside, side rails padded
opts.rat: FIRST - safety setup before any medication. Patient already pulled out previous IV; new access is required for any treatment. Environmental safety prevents injury during agitation and seizure.

opts.text: Administer IV thiamine 100 mg, then magnesium sulfate 2 g IV, then begin lorazepam 2-4 mg IV with q15min CIWA-Ar reassessment
opts.rat: SECOND - thiamine FIRST (the classic NCLEX safety order), then electrolyte replacement, then benzodiazepine titration. Sequence matters: glucose-containing fluids should not precede thiamine.

opts.text: Reassess CIWA-Ar, vital signs, mental status every 15-30 minutes; adjust lorazepam dose accordingly; replace electrolytes (potassium, phosphorus) per protocol
opts.rat: THIRD - active titration is the core nursing role. Patient may need many doses of lorazepam over hours. Electrolyte replacement happens concurrently. Documentation of CIWA-Ar trend is critical for handoff.

opts.text: Coordinate ICU transfer with bed control, SBAR to receiving team, transport with cardiac monitoring and benzodiazepine available en route
opts.rat: FOURTH - patient needs ICU level care for severe DTs. Transport itself is high-risk - autonomic instability and possible seizure during the move. Bring lorazepam.

opts.text: Update wife on diagnosis, treatment plan, expected course, and offer her presence at bedside (when safe); arrange addiction medicine and social work consults for post-stabilization
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - family communication and transition planning. Wife's presence often calms the patient and helps with reorientation. Addiction medicine consult during admission improves long-term sobriety rates significantly.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 24 hours of ICU benzodiazepine titration, electrolyte replacement, and supportive care, classify each finding.

opts.text: CIWA-Ar score decreased from 28 to 9; oriented x3; no longer hallucinating; calm and conversational
opts.rat: Major clinical improvement. CIWA <10 generally indicates the patient can be transitioned to lower acuity care. Resolution of hallucinations and orientation are the strongest signs of DT recovery.

opts.text: BP 134/82, HR 88, no diaphoresis, mild residual tremor on outstretched hands
opts.rat: Autonomic surge resolving as GABA tone restored by benzodiazepines. Residual fine tremor is common and resolves over days to weeks.

opts.text: Magnesium replaced to 2.0 mg/dL; potassium 4.1; phosphorus 3.0; eating soft diet; tolerating PO benzodiazepine taper
opts.rat: Electrolyte normalization plus successful PO transition signals readiness to step down from ICU. PO taper schedule (typically over 5-7 days) helps prevent rebound symptoms.

opts.text: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure x 2 minutes despite lorazepam 16 mg total; brief post-ictal period followed by re-emergence of hallucinations
opts.rat: Inadequate benzodiazepine loading - need to escalate aggressively. Some severe DTs require IV phenobarbital or even propofol/dexmedetomidine when benzodiazepines fail (resistant alcohol withdrawal). Continuous EEG may be needed.

opts.text: New rapid atrial fibrillation with rate 152, BP dropping to 88/52, lactate rising to 4.6 mmol/L
opts.rat: DT-induced cardiac complication - 'holiday heart' arrhythmia plus catecholamine cardiomyopathy. Rate control, magnesium repletion, and aggressive benzodiazepine to suppress catecholamine surge. Mortality from cardiovascular collapse in untreated DTs is real.

opts.text: Patient verbalizing intent to stop drinking, asking about AA meetings and outpatient detox, accepting referral to addiction medicine
opts.rat: Ambivalence-to-readiness transition is a meaningful psychosocial outcome. The hospitalization itself may be a 'reachable moment' for change. Concrete referrals during admission dramatically improve long-term sobriety vs verbal advice alone.

opts.text: Surgical incision site clean, dry, intact; abdomen soft; bowel sounds present; pain controlled with PO acetaminophen
opts.rat: Surgical recovery proceeding normally despite the DT episode. Post-op care continues alongside withdrawal treatment - both must be addressed concurrently.

opts.text: Family at bedside, wife verbalizing understanding of the diagnosis, the role of alcohol in the post-op course, the importance of disclosure to providers in the future, and the need for outpatient support
opts.rat: Family education is critical. Many DT cases happen because alcohol use was not disclosed at admission - prevention requires destigmatizing disclosure and routine screening. Family becomes part of the recovery support system.

title: Traumatic Brain Injury with Increased ICP
subtitle: 26 y/o male, MVC with severe closed head injury, ICP rising despite sedation
nursesNote: 1830 - 26 y/o male unrestrained driver, single-vehicle MVC into a tree at ~50 mph approximately 4 hours ago. Brought to trauma bay with GCS 6 (E1 V1 M4), intubated in field. CT head showed right frontotemporal contusion with 8 mm midline shift, small subdural hematoma, and diffuse cerebral edema; no surgical evacuation indicated per neurosurgery (treating medically). Right frontal external ventricular drain (EVD) placed in OR; transferred to Neuro ICU 90 min ago. Currently sedated with propofol infusion, fentanyl for analgesia, mechanically ventilated (AC mode, FiO2 40%, PEEP 5, RR 14, TV 450). HOB at 30 degrees, head midline, neck without compression from ETT ties. ICP was 16 on arrival to NICU; over the past hour ICP has trended 16 -> 22 -> 28 -> 32, with corresponding CPP drop. Pupils currently 3 mm bilaterally, sluggishly reactive. Family at bedside, devastated; mother is healthcare proxy. Neurosurgery and intensivist en route.

patient.name: C. Mendoza
patient.admitDate: Today, ED 1430 - NICU 1700
patient.room: Neuro ICU 8

labs.v: 32 mmHg
labs.r: 5-15 mmHg

labs.v: 55 mmHg
labs.r: 60-70 mmHg

labs.v: 138 mEq/L
292 mOsm/kg
labs.r: 275-295 mOsm/kg

labs.v: 168 mg/dL
38 mmHg
labs.r: 35-45 mmHg

labs.v: 188 mmHg (on 40% FiO2)
labs.r: 80-100 mmHg

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: Identify rising ICP and impending herniation
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish dangerous intracranial hypertension requiring emergent intervention.

opts.text: ICP trending 16 -> 22 -> 28 -> 32 mmHg over 1 hour with calculated CPP of 55 mmHg
opts.rat: ICP >22 mmHg sustained increases mortality (Brain Trauma Foundation 2017). CPP <60 mmHg means inadequate cerebral perfusion. Both thresholds breached - active brain injury occurring with every minute. CPP = MAP - ICP; reverse the ICP rise to restore CPP.

opts.text: Cushing's triad emerging: bradycardia (HR 88 -> 78 -> 62), widening pulse pressure (138/72 -> 176/88), and irregular respirations (over-breathing the vent at 10/min)
opts.rat: The classic LATE sign of dangerously elevated ICP and brainstem compression. Bradycardia + hypertension with widening pulse pressure + abnormal respiratory pattern = brainstem ischemia from herniation pressure. Class I emergency.

opts.text: Sluggishly reactive pupils with sedated GCS 6 baseline TBI on EVD monitoring
opts.rat: Pupil sluggishness signals brainstem dysfunction - a step toward unilateral or bilateral fixation. Combined with the ICP trend, this patient is heading toward uncal herniation. Frequent pupil exams are critical neuro nursing assessments.

opts.text: Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL
opts.rat: Mild anemia, possibly trauma-related. Affects oxygen delivery but not a primary cue of ICP. Important to maintain Hgb >7-9 in TBI for oxygen delivery, but not the ICP cue.

opts.text: Glucose 168 mg/dL
opts.rat: Stress hyperglycemia common in TBI - associated with worse outcomes if severe but not a cue of acute ICP rise. Tight glucose control (140-180) is supportive care.

opts.text: PaCO2 38 mmHg (normocapnia)
opts.rat: Currently NORMAL and appropriate. CO2 is a powerful cerebral vasodilator; the ventilator is set to maintain normocapnia (35-40). Hyperventilation to PaCO2 30-35 is reserved for impending herniation as a rescue, not routine. Normal value here is reassuring, not a cue.

opts.text: Sodium 138 and serum osmolality 292
opts.rat: Both normal but at the LOW end of the target range for TBI patients (target Na 145-155, osm 300-320 with hyperosmolar therapy). Important to track for treatment, not a current acute cue.

opts.text: Family at bedside, mother is healthcare proxy
opts.rat: Important psychosocial context for upcoming decisions but not a clinical cue of ICP.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology and management.

opts.text: The Monro-Kellie doctrine states the cranial vault is a fixed volume containing brain (80%), CSF (10%), and blood (10%); when one component grows (cerebral edema) the others must decrease or ICP rises - and once compensation fails, small volume changes cause large pressure changes
