opts.rat: FOURTH - definitive treatment. Send the right fluid studies the FIRST time - obtaining additional samples later requires repeat procedure. Cytology yield is highest with first drainage.

opts.text: Post-drainage management: repeat echo to confirm resolution and assess for re-accumulation; admit to cardiac ICU for 24-48 hours; oncology consult for malignant effusion (sclerotherapy, intrapericardial chemotherapy, or definitive surgical pericardial window for recurrence prevention); family meeting and goals-of-care discussion given prognostic implications of malignant tamponade (median survival 2-4 months); CT angiography of chest if dissection has not been clearly excluded
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - prevention of recurrence and addressing underlying cause. Family meeting is critical - malignant tamponade is often a life-limiting event and patient-centered goals of care directly affect treatment decisions.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After fluid resuscitation and pericardiocentesis, classify each finding over the next several hours and days.

opts.text: Within 5 minutes of pericardiocentesis (800 mL serosanguineous fluid drained): BP 122/76 from 86/64, HR 92 from 124, JVD resolved, pulsus paradoxus 4 mmHg from 18 mmHg, lactate normalizing over 2 hours, mental status clear and conversational - dramatic hemodynamic recovery characteristic of tamponade relief
opts.rat: The hallmark response of tamponade to drainage. Hemodynamics improve within seconds to minutes because the steep pericardial pressure-volume curve means small volume removal produces large pressure drops. This dramatic improvement essentially confirms the diagnosis retrospectively.

opts.text: Pericardial fluid cytology positive for adenocarcinoma cells consistent with NSCLC primary; oncology coordinates definitive treatment with surgical pericardial window plus intrapericardial bleomycin sclerotherapy to prevent recurrence; resumed second-line systemic chemotherapy with palliative intent
opts.rat: Confirmed malignant etiology guides targeted recurrence prevention. Pericardial window with sclerotherapy reduces recurrence rates substantially compared to drainage alone. Median survival with malignant tamponade is 2-4 months - definitive procedure improves quality of remaining time.

opts.text: Family meeting completed at bedside on hospital day 2; goals of care clarified including palliative care consultation; patient discharged on hospital day 5 with home palliative services, oncology follow-up, and clear instructions on warning signs of re-accumulation
opts.rat: Critical patient-centered outcome. Malignant tamponade is often a sentinel event prognostically. Aligning treatment with patient values prevents inappropriately aggressive interventions and supports quality of remaining life.

opts.text: Mid-procedure: sudden bradycardia to HR 38 and BP 60/30, then PEA arrest; pericardiocentesis aspirate is bright red blood instead of expected straw-colored serosanguineous fluid - team initiates ACLS and emergent transfer to OR
opts.rat: PROCEDURAL COMPLICATION - possible right ventricular puncture, coronary artery laceration, or unmasking of underlying aortic dissection with hemopericardium (which is why dissection must be considered before pericardiocentesis). Bright red blood that clots suggests cardiac chamber puncture. Emergent sternotomy and surgical repair required. Mortality high.

opts.text: Day 2 post-drainage: re-accumulation of pericardial effusion to moderate size on repeat bedside echo with return of pulsus paradoxus 14 mmHg, JVD recurring, mild hypotension; repeat drainage performed and decision made for definitive surgical pericardial window
opts.rat: RECURRENT TAMPONADE - common in malignant effusions (recurrence rate up to 50% within weeks if drainage alone). Pericardial window allows continuous drainage into pleural space and reduces recurrence dramatically. May also be performed via subxiphoid pericardial fenestration for less invasive option.

opts.text: Day 4: new fever 38.7, leukocytosis 18.4, purulent drainage from pericardial drain site, growing pericardial effusion on repeat echo with fibrinous stranding suggesting infection - team initiates broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin + cefepime), removes and replaces drain at fresh site, surgical washout consultation
opts.rat: IATROGENIC PURULENT PERICARDITIS - rare but serious complication of indwelling pericardial drain. Mortality high without aggressive intervention. Removal of contaminated hardware, antimicrobial coverage for skin flora and gram-negatives, and surgical drainage often needed. Drain duration should be limited (typically 48-72 hours).

opts.text: Mild residual fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and decreased exercise tolerance over 1-2 weeks - improving gradually; no new pulsus paradoxus, no JVD, stable echo without re-accumulation
opts.rat: Expected recovery course in setting of underlying metastatic malignancy and recovery from procedure. Stable hemodynamics and stable echo confirm no recurrent tamponade. Continued vigilance for re-accumulation appropriate but no acute intervention needed.

opts.text: Mild subxiphoid tenderness and small bruise at pericardiocentesis access site, resolving over 5-7 days; no expanding hematoma, normal chest auscultation, no SpO2 decline, no signs of pneumothorax on follow-up imaging
opts.rat: Expected post-procedural recovery. Concerning findings would be expanding hematoma, new chest pain or dyspnea (suggesting pneumothorax or hemothorax), new murmur (coronary injury), or hemodynamic instability.

title: Postpartum Hemorrhage with Hypovolemic Shock
subtitle: Obstetric Emergency & Class III Hemorrhagic Shock

patient.name: M. Vasquez
patient.admitDate: Today, 0345 (admitted in active labor)
patient.room: L&D Recovery, Bed 4

labs.v: 8.2 g/dL
162 K/µL
168 mg/dL
labs.r: Postpartum: 350–650 mg/dL (<200 critical in OB)

labs.v: 34 sec
3.4 mmol/L
O Positive
labs.r: n/a
Vaginal delivery PPH: >1,000 mL (ACOG 2017)

nursesNote: 0730 — Client is a 32 y/o G4P4 female, 1.5 hours s/p spontaneous vaginal delivery of viable male infant, 4,250 g (macrosomic). Labor course: prolonged second stage (3.5 hours), oxytocin augmentation x 8 hours, midline episiotomy with 2nd-degree perineal laceration, repaired by provider with apparent good hemostasis at delivery. Placenta delivered intact at 0610 with three-vessel cord. Initial fundal check at 0610: firm, midline, at umbilicus. Routine postpartum oxytocin 10 units IM administered per protocol. Called to room by partner for 'a lot of bleeding.' On assessment: client pale, diaphoretic, anxious, c/o lightheadedness and feeling 'really weak.' Fundal exam reveals boggy uterus, palpated two fingerbreadths above umbilicus, deviated to the right. Heavy lochia rubra with large clots expressed with fundal pressure. Perineal pad saturated to weight measure within last 15 minutes. Bladder appears distended on palpation; client has not voided since delivery. Perineal repair site intact without hematoma, no expanding mass. Skin cool and clammy, cap refill 4 sec, peripheral pulses thready 1+. Lung sounds clear. Client oriented x4 but increasingly drowsy. Single 18g IV right antecubital infusing LR at 125 mL/hr. Oxytocin 20 units/1,000 mL LR ordered post-delivery has been infusing at 125 mL/hr.
providerOrders: Continuous maternal vital signs q5min
Continuous pulse oximetry and cardiac monitoring
Strict I&O — weigh all pads (QBL protocol)
Establish second large-bore IV (16-gauge preferred)
Increase IV oxytocin to 40 units/1,000 mL LR at 200 mL/hr
Methylergonovine (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM x1 — VERIFY BP <140/90 first
Carboprost (Hemabate) 250 mcg IM q15min PRN, max 8 doses — VERIFY no asthma hx
Misoprostol 800 mcg per rectum x1
Tranexamic acid (TXA) 1 g IV over 10 min within 3 hours of delivery
Type & crossmatch 4 units PRBCs
Activate Massive Transfusion Protocol if QBL >1,500 mL or hemodynamic instability persists
STAT bedside straight catheterization if unable to void
Notify OB provider and Anesthesia immediately
Activate CMQCC Stage 2 OB Hemorrhage Bundle

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Boggy uterine fundus above the umbilicus, deviated to the right
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Circulation (ABCs). A boggy, displaced fundus signals uterine atony, the most common cause of early postpartum hemorrhage (~70–80% of cases). The rightward displacement strongly suggests bladder distention, which mechanically prevents the uterus from contracting against itself to compress placental-site vessels.

opts.text: HR rise from 88 → 122 bpm with BP drop from 118/72 → 88/54 over 90 minutes
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Circulation. Tachycardia precedes hypotension in young, healthy obstetric clients who compensate well, so a falling BP indicates Class III hypovolemic shock (>30% blood loss). Pregnant clients can lose up to 1,500 mL before BP drops — by the time BP falls, the client is already significantly hypovolemic.

opts.text: Quantitative blood loss (QBL) of 1,250 mL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Per ACOG 2017 reVITALize definitions, PPH is now defined as cumulative blood loss ≥1,000 mL OR blood loss accompanied by signs/symptoms of hypovolemia within 24 hours of birth, regardless of delivery route. QBL replaces the historically unreliable visual EBL.

opts.text: Fibrinogen 168 mg/dL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-yield NGN concept. In pregnancy, fibrinogen normally rises to 350–650 mg/dL. A postpartum fibrinogen below 200 mg/dL is the single strongest laboratory predictor of severe PPH and developing DIC. This is critical even though the value looks 'normal' on a standard reference range.

opts.text: Skin cool and clammy, cap refill 4 sec, thready 1+ pulses
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Circulation. Signs of peripheral vasoconstriction and poor perfusion as the body shunts blood centrally. Confirms hemodynamic compromise beyond what BP alone suggests.

opts.text: Distended bladder; client has not voided since delivery
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — directly contributing to atony. A full bladder prevents uterine contraction. Emptying the bladder is a first-line, low-cost intervention that often produces immediate improvement.

opts.text: Lactate 3.4 mmol/L
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Elevated lactate reflects tissue hypoperfusion and anaerobic metabolism — confirms shock at the cellular level.

opts.text: Temperature 99.0°F
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue at this point. Mild temperature elevation is common in the immediate postpartum period (transient endogenous pyrogens, mild dehydration). Becomes concerning if persistent or >100.4°F or accompanied by uterine tenderness suggesting endometritis.

opts.text: Mild perineal soreness at episiotomy repair site
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue. Expected finding after midline episiotomy with 2nd-degree laceration repair. Pain would warrant comfort measures but is not a hemorrhage-related warning sign unless accompanied by an expanding hematoma.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology. Apply the 4 T's framework (Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin).

opts.text: Uterine atony (Tone — the T) is the most likely primary cause given boggy fundus + macrosomia + prolonged labor + oxytocin augmentation
opts.rat: CORRECT. All three of these are well-established atony risk factors. Prolonged oxytocin exposure causes oxytocin-receptor desensitization, blunting endogenous response after delivery. Macrosomia and multiparity (G4P4) overdistend the myometrium, impairing contraction. Atony accounts for ~70–80% of all PPH.

opts.text: Bladder distention is mechanically preventing the uterus from contracting and compressing the placental-site vessels
opts.rat: CORRECT. The uterus must contract against itself for myometrial muscle fibers to act as 'living ligatures' clamping spiral arteries. A full bladder physically displaces the uterus and prevents this. Emptying the bladder alone can resolve mild atony.

opts.text: The client is in Class III hypovolemic shock with blood loss of ~1,500–2,000 mL (30–40% of blood volume)
opts.rat: CORRECT. Pregnancy increases blood volume by 40–50%, so healthy gravidas compensate longer. The combination of HR >120, hypotension, narrowed pulse pressure, cool/clammy skin, and altered mentation places this client in Class III shock — meaning true blood loss likely exceeds the visually measured QBL.

opts.text: Low fibrinogen with prolonged PT suggests early consumptive coagulopathy (DIC) developing alongside ongoing hemorrhage
opts.rat: CORRECT — and high-yield. PPH is a leading obstetric cause of DIC because the gravid uterus releases tissue factor and ongoing blood loss consumes clotting factors. Once fibrinogen falls below 200 mg/dL in OB, hemorrhage becomes self-perpetuating without factor replacement (cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate).

opts.text: The 2nd-degree perineal laceration repair is the primary bleeding source
opts.rat: INCORRECT. A repaired 2nd-degree laceration does not produce 1,000+ mL bleeding unless there is an expanding hematoma or unidentified higher tear (cervical/vaginal). The boggy fundus localizes the source to the uterus. Trauma (the second T) should still be reassessed if atony management fails.

opts.text: These findings represent normal physiologic involution and lochia rubra
opts.rat: INCORRECT and dangerous. Normal lochia rubra does not saturate a pad in <15 minutes, the fundus should be firm and midline at U or U-1, and there should be no hemodynamic compromise. This framing reflects 'normalizing the abnormal,' a documented contributor to maternal mortality.

opts.text: Hgb dropped from 12.4 → 8.2; the 4.2 g/dL drop is consistent with ~1.5–2 L acute blood loss
opts.rat: CORRECT. As a rough rule, each 1 g/dL Hgb drop corresponds to ~250–500 mL blood loss in an adult. Acute hemoglobin lags behind true loss until fluid resuscitation hemodilutes, so current Hgb may still underestimate severity.

opts.text: Retained placental tissue (Tissue — the third T) has been ruled out because the placenta was delivered 'intact'
opts.rat: INCORRECT. 'Apparently intact' on delivery does not rule out retained cotyledons or membrane fragments, especially with persistent bleeding despite atony management. If uterotonics fail, manual exploration or bedside ultrasound is indicated.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST). Use ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy.

opts.text: Deficient Fluid Volume r/t acute uterine blood loss
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Circulation (ABCs). Active hemorrhage with shock physiology. Without immediate volume restoration and source control, progresses to cardiovascular collapse and death. Maternal mortality from PPH is largely time-dependent — every minute of uncontrolled bleeding worsens outcomes.

opts.text: Risk for Ineffective Tissue Perfusion r/t hypovolemia and hypofibrinogenemia
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Directly downstream of bleeding. Lactate 3.4 + cool extremities + altered mentation confirm tissue hypoperfusion. Early fibrinogen drop predicts coagulopathy that will perpetuate bleeding if not replaced.

opts.text: Risk for Infection r/t multiple vaginal examinations, prolonged ROM, and instrumentation
opts.rat: #3 — Important but not immediate. Endometritis risk is real with the labor course, but infection develops over hours-to-days. Address after hemorrhage controlled.

opts.text: Anxiety r/t unexpected complication and fear of dying
opts.rat: #4 — Maslow's safety/love-and-belonging level. Real and valid; calm communication and partner involvement matter. Addressed concurrently with physical interventions but doesn't outrank physiologic priorities.

opts.text: Impaired Parent-Infant Attachment r/t maternal-newborn separation during resuscitation
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this acute moment. Genuine concern for the postpartum period but is addressed once the client is stable. Skin-to-skin and breastfeeding initiation can resume after hemorrhage controlled.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment. Consider safety contraindications.

opts.text: Vigorous bimanual fundal massage and expression of clots
opts.rat: FIRST-LINE for atony. Mechanical stimulation promotes myometrial contraction. Expressing retained clots allows the uterus to clamp down on its own vessels.

opts.text: Empty the bladder (straight catheter or indwelling Foley)
opts.rat: Essential and immediate. A full bladder prevents uterine contraction. Foley also enables accurate hourly UOP monitoring (goal >30 mL/hr) as a resuscitation endpoint.

opts.text: Establish a second large-bore IV (16- or 18-gauge)
opts.rat: Per CMQCC bundle. One IV is inadequate for rapid volume/blood product delivery in active hemorrhage. Two large-bore IVs allow simultaneous crystalloid and blood product administration.

opts.text: Increase IV oxytocin to 40 units/1,000 mL LR per protocol
opts.rat: First-line uterotonic. Continuous infusion only — IV push oxytocin causes severe hypotension and is contraindicated. Already on board; titrating up is appropriate.

opts.text: Methylergonovine (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM
opts.rat: Second-line uterotonic — appropriate here. Client's current BP is 88/54, not hypertensive. ⚠️ CRITICAL SAFETY: Methergine is CONTRAINDICATED in clients with hypertension, preeclampsia, or cardiovascular disease because of severe vasoconstriction. Always check BP before administering.

opts.text: Carboprost (Hemabate) 250 mcg IM after confirming no asthma history
opts.rat: Third-line uterotonic (a prostaglandin F2α analog). ⚠️ CRITICAL SAFETY: Hemabate is CONTRAINDICATED in clients with asthma due to bronchospasm risk. Common side effects: diarrhea, fever, nausea — pre-medicate accordingly.

opts.text: Tranexamic acid (TXA) 1 g IV over 10 minutes
opts.rat: WOMAN trial (2017) established TXA reduces death from PPH by ~30% when given within 3 hours of delivery onset of bleeding. Now standard in PPH bundles.

opts.text: Type & crossmatch 4 units PRBCs and prepare for transfusion
opts.rat: Hgb 8.2 with ongoing loss + Class III shock requires blood. If QBL exceeds 1,500 mL or hemodynamic instability persists, activate Massive Transfusion Protocol with 1:1:1 ratio of PRBC:FFP:platelets.

opts.text: Administer IV furosemide (Lasix) 20 mg to support kidney perfusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ HARMFUL. The client is hypovolemic — diuresis would worsen shock. Low UOP here is appropriate physiologic response to hypovolemia, not a kidney problem to fix with diuretics. Restore volume; UOP will follow.

opts.text: Place client in Trendelenburg position
opts.rat: Outdated practice. No evidence of benefit and may impair diaphragmatic excursion, increase ICP, and worsen aspiration risk. Modified supine with legs elevated is acceptable but not Trendelenburg.

opts.text: Encourage breastfeeding immediately to release endogenous oxytocin
opts.rat: Not in the acute resuscitation phase. While nipple stimulation does release oxytocin, this is inadequate as primary therapy for active hemorrhage, and the client is hemodynamically unstable. Once stabilized, breastfeeding supports involution and is encouraged.

opts.text: Withhold all IV fluids until provider arrives to avoid 'overloading' the client
opts.rat: ⚠️ Dangerously incorrect. Active hemorrhage requires aggressive volume resuscitation. Crystalloid is a temporizing measure until blood products arrive. Hesitation costs lives.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST. Use the CMQCC OB Hemorrhage Bundle and ABCs.

opts.text: Call for help — notify OB provider, charge nurse, and activate the CMQCC Stage 2 OB Hemorrhage response (rapid response team)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. You cannot manage Class III hemorrhagic shock alone. Calling for help is the single most important first action. The CMQCC bundle is California's standardized maternal hemorrhage protocol; activating Stage 2 mobilizes blood bank, anesthesia, and additional staff immediately.

opts.text: Begin firm fundal massage and have the client (or assistant) empty the bladder via straight catheter
opts.rat: #2 — Immediate, simultaneous with calling for help. These two interventions reverse the most common mechanical cause (atony + bladder distention) without delay and require no orders to initiate.

opts.text: Establish a second 16- or 18-gauge IV and increase oxytocin to 40 units/L at 200 mL/hr; begin LR bolus through second IV
opts.rat: #3 — Volume access is the foundation of resuscitation. The second IV enables blood products and second-line uterotonics to run concurrently with crystalloid.

opts.text: Administer next-line uterotonics per provider order: Methergine 0.2 mg IM (BP-permitting) or Hemabate 250 mcg IM (asthma-permitting), plus TXA 1 g IV
opts.rat: #4 — Pharmacologic escalation when massage and oxytocin are insufficient. Each agent has a safety check that must occur before administration — never skip the contraindication verification even under pressure.

opts.text: Send STAT labs (CBC, fibrinogen, PT/PTT, type & crossmatch) and prepare for blood transfusion; activate MTP if QBL exceeds 1,500 mL or instability persists
opts.rat: #5 — Anticipatory action while pharmacologic management is underway. Blood products take time to release and warm; ordering early ensures availability when needed. MTP delivers PRBC:FFP:platelets in 1:1:1 ratio to prevent dilutional coagulopathy.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Two hours after interventions, the client is reassessed. For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Fundus firm, midline, at U-1; lochia rubra moderate without large clots; pad weight 80 mL over last hour
opts.rat: TEXTBOOK RESPONSE. The uterus is contracted and clamping its own vessels. Moderate lochia is expected in early postpartum even after hemorrhage resolution. Pad weight <100 mL/hr is acceptable.

opts.text: HR 92, BP 112/68, capillary refill 2 sec, warm extremities, lactate trending down to 1.8 mmol/L
opts.rat: Excellent perfusion markers. Resolution of compensatory tachycardia + normalizing lactate + warm peripheries = end-organ perfusion restored. These are the goals of hemorrhagic shock resuscitation.

opts.text: Hgb 8.6 g/dL after 2 units PRBCs; client alert, oriented, asking to see her baby
opts.rat: Expected post-transfusion response (~1 g/dL rise per unit PRBC). Cognitive recovery confirms cerebral perfusion restored. Bonding cue is also a positive psychological recovery marker.

opts.text: Urine output 65 mL over last hour via Foley; clear yellow
opts.rat: UOP >30 mL/hr is the resuscitation target indicating restored renal perfusion. Clear color suggests adequate dilution, not concentrated hypovolemic urine.

opts.text: Continued saturation of pads with QBL now totaling 2,400 mL; HR 138, BP 76/42; fundus remains boggy despite massage and full uterotonic ladder
opts.rat: REFRACTORY HEMORRHAGE — activate Stage 3 CMQCC response immediately. When the full uterotonic ladder fails, suspect non-atony causes: retained tissue, occult trauma (cervical/vaginal lacerations, uterine rupture, broad ligament hematoma), or coagulopathy. Definitive intervention may require uterine balloon tamponade (Bakri), B-Lynch suture, uterine artery embolization, or hysterectomy.

opts.text: Fibrinogen drops to 132 mg/dL, INR 1.8, PTT 48 sec; oozing from IV sites and around Foley
opts.rat: DIC — disseminated intravascular coagulopathy. Bleeding from multiple sites without local trauma is the hallmark. Requires cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate (target fibrinogen >200 mg/dL), FFP, platelets per MTP. The 'death spiral' of hemorrhage-coagulopathy-hypothermia-acidosis is now active and requires reversal of all four components.

opts.text: Client reports a sudden severe headache, then has a witnessed tonic-clonic seizure; BP now 178/108
opts.rat: RED FLAG — concerning for postpartum eclampsia, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), or stroke. Of note: methylergonovine causes severe vasoconstriction and can precipitate hypertensive emergency, stroke, or MI — always verify BP <140/90 before each dose. Immediate magnesium sulfate loading dose, seizure precautions, and neuroimaging indicated.

opts.text: Mild postpartum cramping with breastfeeding initiation; report of moderate perineal soreness at episiotomy site
opts.rat: EXPECTED postpartum findings. Afterpains (cramping with breastfeeding) reflect oxytocin-mediated uterine contraction and are actually desired. Perineal soreness at the repair site is normal and managed with ice, sitz baths, and PRN analgesia.

opts.text: Temperature 99.4°F, no fundal tenderness, lochia without foul odor
opts.rat: Low-grade temp without other endometritis signs (fundal tenderness, foul lochia, persistent tachycardia, leukocytosis) is common in the immediate postpartum and not actionable. Reassess in 4 hours.

title: Tension Pneumothorax with Chest Tube Management
subtitle: Trauma-Related Obstructive Shock & Chest Tube Management

patient.name: D. Nguyen
patient.admitDate: Today, 0215 (admitted from ED post-MVC)
patient.room: Trauma StepDown, Bed 7

labs.v: 224 K/µL
52 mmHg
labs.r: 35–45 mmHg

labs.v: 58 mmHg
labs.r: 80–100 mmHg

labs.v: 23 mEq/L
labs.r: 22–26 mEq/L

labs.v: 4.1 mmol/L
0.04 ng/mL
labs.r: <0.04 ng/mL

labs.v: Right rib fractures 4–6, small (~15%) right apical pneumothorax, no flail segment, no widened mediastinum
labs.r: n/a

nursesNote: 0600 — Client is a 28 y/o male, restrained driver in T-bone MVC at 0145, with right-sided impact. Admitted from ED to trauma stepdown 90 min ago with right rib fractures 4–6 and a small (~15%) right apical pneumothorax on initial CXR. Plan was observation with serial CXR, 4L NC supplemental O2, IV opioid analgesia, and pulmonary toileting. Called to room by tech for 'patient can't catch his breath.' On assessment: client tripoding at side of bed, severe respiratory distress, accessory muscle use, speaking in 2–3 word sentences, c/o sudden worsening of right chest pain and 'I can't breathe.' Trachea deviated to the LEFT on inspection and palpation. Right chest: absent breath sounds throughout entire right lung field, hyperresonance to percussion. Left chest: diminished breath sounds, but present. JVD prominent at 45° HOB. Skin pale, diaphoretic, peripheral cyanosis to nail beds. Cap refill 5 sec, pulses thready 1+. Cardiac monitor: sinus tachycardia 138 with low voltage QRS. Mental status: anxious, agitated, oriented x3 but slow to respond. No new bleeding observed. Subcutaneous emphysema palpable over right chest wall extending to neck.
providerOrders: Activate Rapid Response Team / trauma surgery STAT
Continuous cardiac monitoring and continuous pulse oximetry
100% O2 via non-rebreather mask
STAT portable upright CXR (do NOT delay decompression for imaging if classic clinical signs)
Needle decompression of right hemithorax: 14g angiocath at 5th ICS midaxillary line (ATLS 2018) or 2nd ICS midclavicular line
Right chest tube placement: 28–32 Fr at 5th ICS midaxillary line
Connect chest tube to closed water-seal drainage system at –20 cm H2O suction
Repeat CXR post-procedure to confirm lung re-expansion and tube position
Establish second large-bore IV access
ABG, lactate, type & screen, CBC
Pain management: fentanyl IV (avoid respiratory depressants until tube placed and stable)
Do NOT initiate non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) — will worsen tension physiology

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Tracheal deviation to the LEFT (away from the affected right side)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pathognomonic for tension pneumothorax. Intrathoracic pressure on the right has built up enough to physically shift mediastinal structures (trachea, heart, great vessels) to the contralateral side. This is a LATE sign — clinical decompensation is imminent or already underway.

opts.text: Absent breath sounds and hyperresonance to percussion throughout right lung field
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Breathing (ABCs). Trapped air in the pleural space completely collapses the right lung and produces a drum-like resonant note on percussion (vs the dull note of hemothorax or consolidation). This pattern with trauma history is tension pneumo until proven otherwise.

opts.text: JVD at 45° HOB with hypotension (82/48) and tachycardia (138)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Circulation. Obstructive shock physiology. Rising intrathoracic pressure compresses the SVC/IVC and kinks the great vessels, dropping venous return → preload collapse → cardiac output crashes. JVD + hypotension + decreased breath sounds is the tension pneumo equivalent of Beck's triad.

opts.text: SpO2 84% on 4L NC, ABG showing PaO2 58 mmHg and SaO2 86%
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Breathing. Severe hypoxemia despite supplemental O2 indicates the affected lung is contributing no gas exchange and the contralateral lung is also being compromised by mediastinal shift. Requires immediate FiO2 escalation to 100% NRB.

opts.text: Subcutaneous emphysema palpable over right chest wall extending to neck
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Air dissecting into subcutaneous tissue confirms ongoing air leak from the lung parenchyma. Crepitus extending toward the neck raises concern for tracheobronchial injury, which may require operative repair beyond chest tube alone.

opts.text: ABG pH 7.28 with PaCO2 52 mmHg (acute respiratory acidosis)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Acute uncompensated respiratory acidosis from inadequate ventilation. The collapsed lung cannot eliminate CO2 and the client is fatiguing despite tachypnea. Bicarb is still normal because there has been no time for renal compensation.

opts.text: Lactate 4.1 mmol/L
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Tissue hypoperfusion from obstructive shock plus impaired oxygen delivery from hypoxemia. Confirms shock at the cellular level — this is decompensating, not just struggling.

opts.text: Right rib fractures 4–6 on admission CXR
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE in context — explains the source. Rib fractures from the MVC produced the initial pneumothorax; a fragment likely lacerated visceral pleura creating the ongoing air leak that progressed to tension physiology.

opts.text: Anxiety and agitation; speaking in 2–3 word sentences
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neurologic manifestation of hypoxemia and impending respiratory failure. Agitation in trauma is hypoxia until proven otherwise. Inability to complete sentences is a clinical marker of respiratory failure severity.

opts.text: Temperature 98.8°F
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue. Normothermic. Fever would suggest pneumonia or empyema as alternate or additional process; absence does not change the immediate plan.

opts.text: Troponin I 0.04 ng/mL (at upper limit of normal)
opts.rat: NOT immediately actionable. Mild troponin elevation can occur from chest trauma (cardiac contusion) or demand ischemia from shock. Does not change the priority action of decompressing the chest. Worth trending after stabilization.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: A one-way valve mechanism is trapping air in the right pleural space with each breath, progressively increasing intrathoracic pressure
opts.rat: CORRECT — the defining mechanism of tension pneumothorax. Air enters the pleural space during inspiration through a parenchymal tear but cannot escape during exhalation. Each breath worsens the problem. This is why positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP, ambu-bag, mechanical ventilation) without a chest tube can rapidly convert a simple pneumothorax into a tension.

opts.text: Rising intrathoracic pressure is compressing the SVC/IVC, dropping preload, and causing obstructive shock
opts.rat: CORRECT. This is the circulatory mechanism. Unlike cardiogenic shock (pump failure) or hypovolemic shock (volume loss), the heart and volume are intact — they just cannot get blood through the chest. Fluid boluses help temporarily but the only definitive fix is decompression.

opts.text: Mediastinal shift toward the contralateral side is also compromising the unaffected lung
opts.rat: CORRECT. The trachea, heart, and uninjured left lung are being pushed leftward. This kinks the great vessels (worsening shock) and limits expansion of the only functional lung (worsening hypoxemia).

opts.text: Subcutaneous emphysema confirms ongoing air leak and raises concern for tracheobronchial injury
opts.rat: CORRECT. Air dissecting from the pleural space into the soft tissues indicates active leak. Rapid spread (face, neck, chest wall) suggests proximal airway injury — bronchoscopy and possible surgical repair may be needed beyond chest tube.

opts.text: Hypoxemia in this client is primarily from V/Q mismatch with shunt physiology in the collapsed lung
opts.rat: CORRECT. Blood continues to perfuse the collapsed right lung but no ventilation reaches alveoli (shunt). Supplemental O2 only partially corrects shunt-physiology hypoxemia — definitive fix requires lung re-expansion.

opts.text: Findings indicate cardiogenic shock from possible cardiac contusion given the trauma mechanism
opts.rat: INCORRECT in this client. Cardiogenic shock would not produce absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, or tracheal deviation. Troponin is barely elevated. The pattern localizes to a thoracic compartment, not a cardiac pump problem. Cardiac contusion remains differential but is not the priority diagnosis.

opts.text: Diagnosis of tension pneumothorax should be confirmed by CXR before any intervention
opts.rat: INCORRECT — and a high-yield NCLEX trap. Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Decompression must NOT be delayed waiting for imaging if classic signs are present (tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, JVD, hypotension, trauma). Delay for CXR has killed patients.

opts.text: BiPAP would be a good non-invasive option to support oxygenation while waiting for chest tube
opts.rat: INCORRECT — DANGEROUS. Any positive-pressure ventilation through an unsealed lung tear forces more air into the pleural space and accelerates tension physiology. BiPAP is contraindicated until the chest is decompressed.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Impaired Gas Exchange r/t lung collapse and V/Q mismatch
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Breathing (ABCs). PaO2 of 58 with SaO2 of 86% is critical hypoxemia. Without immediate decompression and re-expansion, anoxic injury and cardiac arrest follow within minutes. Oxygen delivery to tissues is the single most time-sensitive priority.

opts.text: Decreased Cardiac Output r/t impaired venous return from intrathoracic pressure
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Circulation (ABCs). Obstructive shock is mechanically driven and will not respond to typical shock management (fluids, pressors) without removing the obstruction. Linked tightly to #1 — both resolve with decompression.

opts.text: Acute Pain r/t rib fractures and pleural injury
opts.rat: #3 — Important and influences pulmonary mechanics. Splinting from pain worsens atelectasis and CO2 retention, but management must avoid respiratory depressants until airway/breathing stabilized.

opts.text: Anxiety r/t acute respiratory distress and trauma response
opts.rat: #4 — Real and contributes to oxygen demand and respiratory rate, but downstream of the physiologic emergency. Calm communication and presence are addressed concurrently.

opts.text: Risk for Infection r/t open thoracic procedures and chest tube insertion
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this moment. Becomes relevant after the tube is in. Sterile technique during insertion and clean occlusive dressing maintenance address it then.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask
opts.rat: Maximum non-invasive O2 delivery while preparing decompression. Higher FiO2 also speeds reabsorption of small pneumothoraces by displacing nitrogen — but this is a tension, so decompression cannot wait.

opts.text: Needle decompression at 5th ICS midaxillary line (or 2nd ICS midclavicular line) with 14g angiocath
opts.rat: First-line, time-critical intervention. ATLS 2018 updated the preferred adult site from 2nd ICS MCL to 5th ICS MAL because chest wall thickness at 2nd ICS often exceeds standard catheter length (≥5 cm). Either site is acceptable; the priority is decompression now.

opts.text: Chest tube placement at 5th ICS midaxillary line (28–32 Fr) connected to closed water-seal drainage with –20 cm H2O suction
opts.rat: Definitive treatment. Needle decompression converts a tension to a simple pneumothorax; chest tube provides ongoing evacuation until the lung re-expands and the air leak seals. The 'triangle of safety' (anterior border of latissimus dorsi, lateral border of pectoralis major, line across nipple) defines the safe insertion zone.

opts.text: Establish second large-bore IV and prepare for crystalloid bolus
opts.rat: Supports preload while preparing decompression. Will not fix obstructive shock alone but bridges hemodynamics during the procedure.

opts.text: Maintain water-seal chamber filled to prescribed level and keep drainage system below chest level
opts.rat: Core chest tube nursing care. Water seal acts as a one-way valve — air can leave the pleural space but cannot re-enter. Keeping the system below the chest prevents drainage from flowing back into the pleural space by gravity.

opts.text: Initiate BiPAP at 12/5 cm H2O to support oxygenation while awaiting chest tube
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS in undrained pneumothorax. Positive airway pressure forces additional air into the pleural space through the parenchymal tear and accelerates tension physiology. Absolute contraindication until chest tube is in and functioning.

opts.text: Clamp the chest tube briefly when transporting the client off the unit
opts.rat: ⚠️ HIGH-YIELD NCLEX TRAP. Clamping a chest tube risks re-creating tension pneumothorax if there is an ongoing air leak. Chest tubes should remain unclamped during transport — keep the drainage system upright and below chest level. Clamping is reserved for very specific clinical decisions (assessing for tube removal, replacing the drainage system) and is always brief and supervised.

opts.text: Strip or milk the chest tube routinely to maintain patency
opts.rat: Outdated and discouraged. Stripping generates large negative intrathoracic pressure swings (up to –400 cm H2O) that can injure lung tissue. Modern practice: do not routinely strip; only gently 'fluff' or reposition the tubing if a dependent loop is collecting fluid. Follow institutional policy.

opts.text: If the drainage system becomes disconnected from the chest tube, immediately submerge the tube end in a bottle of sterile water (or saline) to maintain a water seal
opts.rat: CORRECT emergency action. Submerging the end creates a temporary water seal preventing air from entering the pleural space until a new drainage system is connected. Do NOT clamp — clamping risks re-tension.

opts.text: Reinforce the chest tube insertion site dressing with additional petroleum gauze and tape on all four sides if the tube becomes dislodged from the chest wall
opts.rat: TRAP. If the tube comes out of the chest, the correct action is to cover the insertion site with a sterile gauze taped on THREE sides only (creating a one-way flutter valve so air can escape but not re-enter). Taping all four sides converts the wound into another tension pneumothorax risk. Then notify provider immediately.

opts.text: Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber should be reported to the provider as an air leak
opts.rat: CORRECT. Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber indicates ongoing air leak from either the lung (expected initially, should resolve) or from a system leak (tubing connection loose, tube partially out, chamber cracked). Investigate by briefly cross-clamping toward the patient — if bubbling stops, leak is at or distal to the clamp; if it continues, leak is from the patient/lung.

opts.text: Absence of tidaling (fluctuation with respiration) in the water-seal chamber always indicates the lung has fully re-expanded
opts.rat: TRAP — half-truth. Absence of tidaling has TWO possible meanings: (1) lung re-expanded (good — confirm with CXR), OR (2) tubing is obstructed/kinked/clamped (bad — could lead to re-tension). Always investigate — assess the patient, check for kinks, and obtain CXR.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST. Use ABCs.

opts.text: Call rapid response / trauma surgery STAT; do not leave the client; have a colleague mobilize the team
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The client needs procedural intervention you cannot perform alone (or, in many states, at all under RN scope). Mobilizing the team is the single most impactful action. Concurrent: never leave a patient in this state — delegate the call.

opts.text: Switch O2 to 100% non-rebreather and reposition client upright if tolerated to maximize lung expansion
opts.rat: #2 — Immediate, simultaneous with team activation. Maximizes oxygen delivery and uses gravity to assist contralateral lung expansion. Requires no orders.

opts.text: Prepare for emergent needle decompression: set up 14g angiocath, antiseptic, and position client; assist provider with procedure
opts.rat: #3 — Definitive bridging procedure. Decompression converts tension physiology to a simple pneumothorax within seconds. Listen for the characteristic 'rush of air' confirming successful entry into the pleural space.

opts.text: Set up closed chest drainage system (fill water seal to prescribed level, connect to wall suction at –20 cm H2O); assist with chest tube insertion and secure dressing
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive treatment immediately following needle decompression. The needle is a temporary fix; tube is permanent. Pre-prime the system so it's ready when the provider needs it — every second of fumbling delays drainage.

opts.text: Obtain post-procedure CXR, send ABG, document hemodynamic response, and titrate O2 to maintain SpO2 ≥92%
opts.rat: #5 — Verification and ongoing management. CXR confirms tube position, lung re-expansion, and absence of complications. ABG quantifies physiologic improvement. Document baseline drainage output and air leak status as starting reference.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Following needle decompression and chest tube placement, the client is reassessed at various intervals. For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Immediately after needle decompression: rush of air heard, HR 138→112, BP 82/48→104/68, SpO2 84→93% on NRB, JVD resolved, trachea midline
opts.rat: TEXTBOOK successful decompression. Resolution of obstructive physiology with restored venous return. These are the bedside markers used to confirm the decompression worked even before imaging.

opts.text: Two hours post chest tube: lung sounds present bilaterally on right, SpO2 96% on 2L NC, ABG pH 7.38/PaCO2 41/PaO2 84, 220 mL serosanguinous drainage, intermittent tidaling with respiration
opts.rat: Lung re-expanded, gas exchange restored, expected drainage volume for first hours after chest tube. Tidaling (fluctuation of fluid in water-seal chamber with respiration) confirms tube is patent and the lung is still partially mobile. Acceptable trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1 post-procedure: client ambulating with assistance, drainage 40 mL/8 hr, intermittent bubbling in water-seal chamber only with cough
opts.rat: Decreasing drainage and an air leak only with forced expiration (cough) indicates the parenchymal tear is sealing. Continuous bubbling at rest would be concerning; intermittent with cough is expected and reassuring. Mobilization is also positive — reduces atelectasis and DVT risk.

opts.text: 30 minutes post chest tube: SpO2 falls to 88%, HR climbs to 130, BP 88/52, trachea deviating left again, no tidaling in water-seal chamber, no drainage in tubing
opts.rat: RE-TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX — assume tube malfunction. Most likely causes: kinked tubing, dislodged tube, clamped tubing, occluded by clot. Check the system from patient to drainage chamber for any kink, clamp, or disconnection. If no obvious cause, the tube has likely moved out of the pleural space. Notify provider immediately for reinsertion or replacement.

opts.text: Drainage system tips over on the floor; sterile water level in water-seal chamber spills out
opts.rat: Loss of water seal compromises the one-way valve. Upright the system immediately, refill water-seal chamber to prescribed level with sterile water (or saline), and notify provider. Monitor for signs of air re-entering pleural space. Document the event.

opts.text: Day 2 post-procedure: brisk continuous bubbling resumes in water-seal chamber, increased subcutaneous emphysema, fever 101.4°F, sputum now purulent, growing infiltrate on CXR adjacent to tube
opts.rat: Combination of persistent air leak + infection signs raises concern for bronchopleural fistula and/or empyema. Bronchoscopy, surgical consult, and broad-spectrum antibiotics may be needed. Persistent air leak beyond 5–7 days often requires operative intervention (VATS, pleurodesis).

opts.text: Day 3: lung sounds clear bilaterally, drainage <50 mL/24 hr serous, no tidaling, no bubbling even with cough, CXR shows fully expanded lung with tube in place
opts.rat: Criteria for chest tube removal preparation: minimal drainage, no air leak, lung fully expanded. Typical next step is a brief water-seal trial (no suction) followed by repeat CXR to confirm lung remains expanded, then tube removal under Valsalva maneuver.

opts.text: Mild incisional discomfort at tube insertion site responding to scheduled acetaminophen and PRN low-dose oxycodone; client maintaining incentive spirometry goals
opts.rat: Expected post-procedural course. Incision discomfort and the need for analgesia is normal. Adequate pain control enables incentive spirometry and ambulation, which prevent atelectasis and accelerate recovery.

opts.text: Sudden severe chest pain on the LEFT side, new tracheal deviation toward the right, decreased breath sounds on the left, SpO2 drops to 86%
opts.rat: CONTRALATERAL TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX — a rare but described complication of mechanical ventilation or barotrauma. Same emergency, opposite side. Activate rapid response, prepare for left-sided decompression and tube placement. Underlying cause may be ventilator-related barotrauma, ruptured bleb, or trauma-related occult injury that has now manifested.

title: Septic Shock from Urosepsis
subtitle: Distributive Shock & Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 Bundle

patient.name: J. Patel
patient.admitDate: POD#3 s/p transurethral resection of prostate (TURP)
patient.room: Surgical Stepdown, Bed 12

labs.v: 98 K/µL
4.8 mmol/L
labs.r: 0.5–2.2 mmol/L (>4 = severe)

labs.v: 12.4 ng/mL
labs.r: <0.5 ng/mL (>2 = bacterial sepsis)

labs.v: 38 mg/dL
labs.r: 7–20 mg/dL

labs.v: 2.1 mg/dL
labs.r: 0.7–1.3 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)

labs.v: 268 mg/dL
24 mmHg
labs.r: 35–45 mmHg

labs.v: 16 mEq/L
labs.r: 22–26 mEq/L

labs.v: WBC >50/hpf, nitrites +, leukocyte esterase +, gram-negative rods on gram stain
labs.r: WBC <5/hpf, nitrites neg

labs.v: Pending (drawn pre-antibiotics)
labs.r: No growth

nursesNote: 0600 — 67 y/o male, post-operative day 3 from transurethral resection of prostate (TURP) for benign prostatic hyperplasia. PMH: T2DM (HbA1c 8.2 last month), HTN, BPH. Indwelling Foley catheter placed intraoperatively remains in place per surgical plan; continuous bladder irrigation discontinued POD#1. Called to bedside by night shift for 'patient confused and breathing fast.' Client awake but confused — oriented to self only, unable to state day or location, attempting to climb out of bed, speech slurred. Baseline mental status per family and chart: alert and oriented x4. Skin warm and flushed across face/upper chest but mottled and cool at knees and feet; capillary refill 5 seconds; peripheral pulses thready 1+. Foley draining cloudy, dark amber, foul-smelling urine — output last hour 18 mL (down from 60–70 mL/hr earlier in shift). Catheter site without purulence at meatus but encrustation visible on tubing. TURP surgical access points (suprapubic and perineal) without erythema, drainage, or fluctuance. Lungs clear bilaterally. No chest pain. New onset shaking chills observed at 0530 prior to my arrival per night nurse handoff. Family at bedside, distressed.
providerOrders: Activate Code Sepsis / Hour-1 Bundle
ICU transfer pending bed availability
Continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry
STAT blood cultures x2 from separate peripheral sites — BEFORE antibiotics
STAT urinalysis with culture and sensitivity (collect from new sterile catheter after Foley exchange)
Lactate now and repeat q2h until normalized
CBC with differential, CMP, coagulation panel, ABG, procalcitonin
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV q6h — administer within 1 hour of recognition
Vancomycin 25 mg/kg IV load — administer within 1 hour of recognition
Lactated Ringers 30 mL/kg IV bolus (estimated 2.1 L for ~70 kg client) over 30 minutes
Norepinephrine drip prepared at bedside; titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg if hypotension persists after fluid bolus
Source control: remove indwelling Foley after specimen collected; reinsert new sterile catheter
Establish second large-bore IV access (16- or 18-gauge)
Strict I&O, hourly UOP via Foley
O2 titrated to SpO2 ≥92%
Bedside glucose q1h; insulin infusion per ICU protocol — target 140–180 mg/dL
Hourly mental status and neurologic checks
Notify rapid response, urology, and ICU intensivist

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: MAP of ~60 mmHg with BP 84/48 and HR 124
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Circulation. MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP-DBP) = 48 + 12 = 60, below the 65 threshold that defines septic shock per Sepsis-3 criteria. Hypotension is one of the two defining features of septic shock (the other is lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation).

opts.text: Lactate 4.8 mmol/L
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines tissue hypoperfusion. Lactate >2 mmol/L confirms shock at the cellular level; lactate >4 mmol/L is associated with significantly increased mortality and triggers the most aggressive resuscitation pathway in the Surviving Sepsis Campaign bundle.

opts.text: Acute new confusion — baseline alert and oriented x4, now oriented to self only with slurred speech
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Acute change in mental status is a sensitive marker of organ dysfunction in sepsis. Septic encephalopathy occurs in up to 70% of septic clients and is one of the qSOFA criteria (altered mentation, RR ≥22, SBP ≤100). This client meets all three qSOFA criteria — high mortality risk.

opts.text: Fever 102.8°F with shaking chills observed at 0530
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Rigors classically precede bacteremia by 30–60 minutes as bacteria release into the bloodstream. Combined with the temperature spike, this is a strong sign of active infection with bloodstream invasion.

opts.text: Mottled cool extremities with capillary refill 5 seconds and thready 1+ pulses
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — late ('cold') septic shock physiology. Initially clients are vasodilated and warm ('warm shock'). As shock progresses, compensatory peripheral vasoconstriction shunts blood to vital organs, producing mottling and prolonged capillary refill. This client is transitioning into cold shock.

opts.text: Cloudy, foul-smelling urine; UA with WBC >50/hpf, positive nitrites, positive leukocyte esterase, gram-negative rods
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — identifies the source. The infection source is the urinary tract, almost certainly from the indwelling Foley (catheter-associated urinary tract infection, CAUTI). Gram-negative rods on UA gram stain suggests E. coli, Klebsiella, or Proteus as likely pathogens — guides empiric antibiotic selection.

opts.text: UOP 18 mL in last hour (down from 60–70 mL/hr)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — early acute kidney injury. UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr meets KDIGO criteria for AKI. Combined with the creatinine rise from 0.9 baseline to 2.1, this confirms sepsis-induced AKI from hypoperfusion plus inflammatory tubular injury.

opts.text: WBC 18.4 with 18% bands (left shift)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Bandemia >10% is highly suggestive of bacterial infection — the bone marrow is releasing immature neutrophils to meet demand. Procalcitonin 12.4 (well above the 2 ng/mL threshold) further supports bacterial sepsis vs viral or non-infectious inflammation.

opts.text: Indwelling Foley catheter still in place POD#3 with encrustation visible
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the infectious nidus. Biofilm forms on catheter surfaces within 24–72 hours, harboring bacteria that resist antibiotic penetration. Catheter must be REMOVED for definitive source control — antibiotics alone cannot eradicate biofilm-associated infection.

opts.text: Glucose 268 mg/dL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — stress hyperglycemia worsens outcomes in sepsis. Hyperglycemia impairs neutrophil function and promotes inflammation. Target 140–180 mg/dL per Surviving Sepsis (NICE-SUGAR trial showed harm with tighter targets).

opts.text: TURP surgical access points without erythema, drainage, or fluctuance
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue — this REASSURING finding helps rule out surgical site infection as a competing source. Note it during assessment but no action needed.

opts.text: Last bowel movement yesterday, normal
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue. Normal bowel function helps rule out occult GI source (e.g., perforation, C. difficile) but is not actionable in itself.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: This is distributive shock — systemic vasodilation from inflammatory mediators causing relative hypovolemia despite intact intravascular volume
opts.rat: CORRECT. Cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) and nitric oxide produce profound vasodilation. The 'tank' has expanded faster than the 'volume' can fill it. Initial treatment is volume expansion to fill the expanded tank, then vasoconstrictors if filling alone is inadequate.

opts.text: The infection source is a catheter-associated UTI that ascended to pyelonephritis and bacteremia
opts.rat: CORRECT. Indwelling catheters bypass normal urethral defenses. Biofilm on the catheter surface harbors bacteria that ascend retrograde, causing cystitis → pyelonephritis. Bloodstream invasion (urosepsis) follows. CAUTI is the #1 healthcare-associated infection.

opts.text: Lactate elevation reflects anaerobic metabolism in tissues that aren't getting enough oxygen — this is type A hyperlactatemia
opts.rat: CORRECT. When oxygen delivery falls below cellular demand, pyruvate is shunted to lactate instead of entering the Krebs cycle. Type A hyperlactatemia = tissue hypoperfusion (sepsis, shock); type B = metabolic causes (medications, liver dysfunction). Trending lactate is a resuscitation endpoint.

opts.text: Sepsis-induced capillary leak shifts fluid from intravascular into interstitial space, worsening the hypovolemia
opts.rat: CORRECT. Endothelial dysfunction increases capillary permeability — protein-rich fluid leaks into the interstitium (third spacing). This is why septic clients often require 3–5+ liters of crystalloid for initial resuscitation despite no overt blood loss.

opts.text: The metabolic acidosis (pH 7.28, HCO3 16) is being partially compensated by hyperventilation driving PaCO2 down to 24
opts.rat: CORRECT. The respiratory rate of 28 isn't just from sepsis-induced inflammation — the client is blowing off CO2 to compensate for lactic acidosis. Winters formula confirms appropriate compensation: expected PaCO2 = 1.5(16) + 8 ± 2 = 30–34. Actual is 24, suggesting a concurrent respiratory alkalosis from sepsis-driven tachypnea.

opts.text: AKI is from a combination of pre-renal hypoperfusion AND direct sepsis-induced acute tubular injury from inflammatory mediators
opts.rat: CORRECT. Septic AKI is a mixed mechanism — not pure pre-renal. Fluid resuscitation helps but may not fully reverse it. Some clients progress to require CRRT. Avoiding nephrotoxic agents (aminoglycosides, contrast, NSAIDs) is critical going forward.

opts.text: Findings look like cardiogenic shock — start dobutamine to support cardiac output
opts.rat: INCORRECT. No evidence of pump failure (no JVD, no pulmonary edema, no rales, no S3, no chest pain, no ST changes). This is distributive shock. Inotropes are reserved for septic clients with documented myocardial dysfunction on echo or persistent hypoperfusion despite adequate MAP — a small subset.

opts.text: The lactate elevation is from the surgery itself three days ago and is not concerning
opts.rat: INCORRECT — and dangerous. Postoperative lactate elevation resolves within 24 hours in uncomplicated cases. Lactate 4.8 on POD#3 is a sepsis marker, not surgical. Attributing it to surgery would delay life-saving treatment.

opts.text: Antibiotics alone will eradicate the infection — the catheter does not need to be removed
opts.rat: INCORRECT — high-yield NCLEX trap. Biofilms on indwelling devices are largely impervious to systemic antibiotics. Source control (removing the catheter) is just as important as antimicrobial therapy. Failure to remove an infected device is a frequent cause of treatment failure.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Ineffective Tissue Perfusion r/t septic vasodilation, capillary leak, and hypovolemia
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Circulation (ABCs). MAP 60, lactate 4.8, mottled extremities, oliguria, and confusion all confirm that oxygen delivery to vital organs is critically inadequate. Every hour of inadequate perfusion increases mortality by ~7% in septic shock.

opts.text: Risk for Multi-Organ Dysfunction r/t prolonged hypoperfusion and systemic inflammation
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Already showing AKI (Cr 2.1), encephalopathy (confusion), early coagulopathy (INR 1.4, platelets 98), and respiratory involvement (RR 28, hypoxia). Without aggressive resuscitation, progression to full MODS and death follows within hours to days.

opts.text: Risk for Continued Bacteremia r/t infected indwelling urinary catheter
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The infectious source is still in place. Until removed, the client is continuously seeding bacteria into the bloodstream and antibiotics are fighting an unwinnable battle against a biofilm reservoir.

opts.text: Acute Confusion r/t septic encephalopathy and metabolic derangement
opts.rat: #4 — Important and concerning, but downstream of perfusion. As MAP and oxygen delivery improve, sensorium typically clears. Address with frequent reorientation, safety measures (bed alarm), and family presence.

opts.text: Risk for Falls r/t altered mental status, attempting to climb out of bed
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this acute moment, but real. Bed alarm, sitter or family at bedside, and side rails up address it. Becomes higher priority once hemodynamically stable and the physiologic emergency is managed.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Draw blood cultures x2 from separate peripheral sites BEFORE administering antibiotics
opts.rat: CORE Hour-1 bundle. Pre-antibiotic cultures preserve the ability to identify the pathogen and tailor therapy. CAVEAT: do not delay antibiotics more than 45 minutes for cultures — if access is difficult, antibiotics take priority over the second culture set.

opts.text: Administer piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
opts.rat: CORE Hour-1 bundle. Each hour of delay increases mortality by approximately 7%. Broad-spectrum empiric coverage targets likely organisms (gram-negative urinary pathogens + MRSA coverage given hospital exposure) until culture data narrows therapy.

opts.text: Lactated Ringers 30 mL/kg IV bolus over the first 3 hours
opts.rat: CORE Hour-1 bundle. Crystalloid resuscitation 30 mL/kg is the Surviving Sepsis standard for septic shock or lactate >4. LR is preferred over NS in many institutions because large volumes of NS cause hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, potentially worsening AKI.

opts.text: Remove the indwelling Foley after specimen collected; reinsert new sterile catheter
opts.rat: Source control — equal in importance to antibiotics. The infected catheter must come out. Reinserting a new catheter (rather than leaving the bladder undrained) allows monitoring of UOP as a resuscitation endpoint and prevents urinary retention.

opts.text: Start norepinephrine titrated to MAP ≥65 mmHg if hypotension persists after 30 mL/kg fluid bolus
opts.rat: First-line vasopressor in septic shock per Surviving Sepsis guidelines. Norepinephrine is preferred over dopamine because it produces less tachycardia and arrhythmia, and the SOAP II trial showed lower mortality with norepi than dopamine.

opts.text: Repeat lactate at 2-hour intervals to track resuscitation response
opts.rat: Lactate clearance is a validated resuscitation endpoint. A 10% reduction in lactate within 2 hours is associated with improved survival. Failure to clear lactate despite adequate MAP suggests ongoing hypoperfusion and may warrant further intervention.

opts.text: Insulin infusion to maintain glucose 140–180 mg/dL
opts.rat: Stress hyperglycemia worsens infection outcomes, but tight control (80–110) showed increased mortality in NICE-SUGAR. The 140–180 target balances benefit and hypoglycemia risk.

opts.text: Start dopamine as the first-line vasopressor
opts.rat: ⚠️ HIGH-YIELD NCLEX TRAP. The SOAP II trial (2010) showed dopamine causes more arrhythmias and trends toward higher mortality in septic shock vs norepinephrine. Norepi is first-line. Dopamine is reserved for selected cases (e.g., concurrent symptomatic bradycardia).

opts.text: Administer hydroxyethyl starch (HES) colloid for volume expansion
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED in sepsis. The 6S, CHEST, and CRYSTMAS trials showed HES increases AKI and mortality in septic clients. Crystalloid (LR or NS) is the standard. Albumin may be used as an adjunct after substantial crystalloid, but not synthetic colloids.

opts.text: Initiate IV bicarbonate to correct the metabolic acidosis (pH 7.28)
opts.rat: Not routinely indicated unless pH <7.15 (or <7.20 with hemodynamic instability) per Surviving Sepsis. Bicarbonate doesn't improve outcomes for moderate acidosis and may worsen intracellular acidosis. Treat the cause — restore perfusion.

opts.text: Routine hydrocortisone 200 mg/day for all septic shock clients
opts.rat: Not routine. Reserved for refractory septic shock requiring escalating doses of vasopressors (typically norepi >0.25 mcg/kg/min). The ADRENAL trial showed modest benefit only in this subgroup. This client just started resuscitation — too early.

opts.text: Delay antibiotics for 4–6 hours until blood culture results return
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS. Each hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality. Empiric broad-spectrum coverage is started immediately after cultures are drawn, then narrowed when sensitivities return 48–72 hours later. Waiting for cultures = preventable deaths.

opts.text: Insert a second large-bore peripheral IV (16- or 18-gauge)
opts.rat: Essential for volume resuscitation, simultaneous antibiotic administration, and vasopressor backup access. Two IVs minimum for any shock state. Central line is often placed for prolonged vasopressor support but should not delay initial resuscitation.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST. Use the Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 Bundle.

opts.text: Activate Code Sepsis / call rapid response; mobilize ICU intensivist and a second nurse; do not leave the client alone
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Septic shock requires team-based care, multiple simultaneous interventions, and ICU disposition. Calling for help mobilizes the resources needed and is the single highest-yield first action.

opts.text: Establish second large-bore IV; draw blood cultures x2 from separate peripheral sites; send lactate, CBC, CMP, coags, procalcitonin
opts.rat: #2 — Immediate. Two IVs enable simultaneous fluids and antibiotics. Cultures drawn pre-antibiotic preserves diagnostic value. Doing this step quickly avoids delaying antibiotics beyond the 1-hour window.

opts.text: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin) within the 1-hour window
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Every hour of delay raises mortality. Antibiotics should follow cultures as closely as possible but never wait if culture access is difficult. Vancomycin requires longer infusion time — start it running concurrent with the pip-tazo IV push.

opts.text: Initiate Lactated Ringers 30 mL/kg IV bolus (~2.1 L for ~70 kg client) over 30 minutes
opts.rat: #4 — Volume resuscitation runs concurrently with antibiotics. The bolus refills the dilated 'tank' of distributive shock. Reassess MAP, mental status, lactate, and UOP after the bolus completes.

opts.text: Reassess MAP after fluid bolus; if MAP <65, start norepinephrine titrated to MAP ≥65; remove and replace Foley catheter for source control
opts.rat: #5 — Final step in the Hour-1 sequence. Vasopressors target the dilated vasculature after volume has been restored. Source control (Foley exchange) happens once resuscitation is underway — must not delay initial life-saving steps but must not be forgotten.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After implementing the Hour-1 bundle, the client is reassessed at various intervals. For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 2 hours post-resuscitation: lactate drops from 4.8 → 2.4 mmol/L; MAP 72 sustained on norepi 0.08 mcg/kg/min; UOP 45 mL/hr; mental status improving to A&Ox3
opts.rat: TEXTBOOK response to bundle. >10% lactate clearance at 2 hours predicts improved survival. Restoration of MAP, UOP, and sensorium all indicate end-organ perfusion is being restored. Continue current management and trend off pressor as MAP allows.

opts.text: 6 hours post-resuscitation: norepinephrine weaned to off, lactate normalized to 1.4 mmol/L, blood cultures growing E. coli pan-sensitive, antibiotics narrowed to ceftriaxone
opts.rat: Excellent trajectory. De-escalation of antibiotics based on culture sensitivities is a key sepsis principle (antimicrobial stewardship). Cultures growing the expected organism confirms the source was correctly identified. Continued stability allows transfer out of ICU.

opts.text: Day 2: extremities warm, cap refill 2 sec, UOP 60–80 mL/hr, creatinine improving (2.1 → 1.4), eating breakfast, ambulating with assistance
opts.rat: Resolution of organ dysfunction across multiple systems. AKI recovery, normalized hemodynamics, and return to functional baseline all signal successful sepsis treatment. New Foley already discontinued; voiding spontaneously.

opts.text: After 30 mL/kg fluids and antibiotics: MAP still 56, lactate climbed to 6.2 mmol/L, requires norepinephrine 0.4 mcg/kg/min + vasopressin add-on; INR 2.4, platelets 48; oozing from IV sites
opts.rat: REFRACTORY SEPTIC SHOCK with developing DIC. Multiple high-dose vasopressors, rising lactate, and consumptive coagulopathy indicate the client is not responding to standard therapy. Consider hydrocortisone 200 mg/day, evaluate for occult source of infection, and recheck for incorrect or missed pathogen coverage.

opts.text: Day 1: develops bilateral diffuse pulmonary infiltrates, PaO2/FiO2 ratio 140, requires intubation and lung-protective ventilation
opts.rat: ARDS — sepsis is the #1 cause of ARDS. PaO2/FiO2 <300 = mild ARDS, <200 = moderate, <100 = severe. Lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg ideal body weight tidal volumes, plateau pressure <30, permissive hypercapnia) is the standard. Mortality remains high.

opts.text: Day 2: anuric for 6 hours, creatinine 4.2, K+ 6.4, BUN 84 — nephrology consulted for CRRT
opts.rat: Progression to severe AKI requiring renal replacement therapy. Septic AKI has a worse prognosis than pre-renal AKI alone because of direct tubular injury. CRRT is preferred over intermittent HD in hemodynamically unstable septic clients.

opts.text: Day 1: new ST-segment changes on monitor, troponin rises to 0.84, echo shows EF 35% (baseline 60%) with global hypokinesis
opts.rat: Sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy — occurs in ~40% of septic shock clients. Typically reversible if the client survives sepsis. Dobutamine may be added if vasopressors are adequate but cardiac output is inadequate. Distinguish from primary cardiac event.

opts.text: Day 3: mild residual fatigue and decreased appetite; physical therapy beginning bedside exercises; family at bedside
opts.rat: Expected post-sepsis recovery course. Post-sepsis syndrome — fatigue, weakness, cognitive changes, and PTSD — can persist for months. Early mobilization and family engagement reduce ICU-acquired weakness and improve long-term functional outcomes.

opts.text: Hospital day 4: glucose still mildly elevated at 165 mg/dL on insulin infusion at low rates, trending down
opts.rat: Acceptable. Target is 140–180 mg/dL while critically ill. Mild hyperglycemia within range as sepsis resolves and insulin requirements decrease is expected. Transition to subcutaneous insulin as eating resumes.

title: Acute Aortic Dissection (Stanford Type A)
subtitle: Cardiothoracic Emergency & Rate-Before-Pressure Hemodynamics

patient.name: K. Anderson
patient.admitDate: Today, 0445 (admitted from ED, symptom onset ~0415)
patient.room: ED Resus Bay 2, awaiting ICU/OR

labs.v: 198 K/µL
8,400 ng/mL
labs.r: <500 ng/mL (>1,200 suggests dissection)

labs.v: 0.08 ng/mL
labs.r: <0.04 ng/mL

labs.v: 142 pg/mL
labs.r: <100 pg/mL

labs.v: 22 mg/dL
labs.r: 7–20 mg/dL

labs.v: 1.4 mg/dL
labs.r: 0.7–1.3 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)

labs.v: 2.6 mmol/L
Sinus tachycardia 110, nonspecific ST-T changes inferolaterally, no ST elevation, no Q waves
labs.r: Sinus rhythm without ischemic changes

labs.v: Widened mediastinum (8.4 cm), small left pleural effusion, normal cardiac silhouette
labs.r: Mediastinum <8 cm, no effusion

labs.v: Pending — STAT order placed
labs.r: n/a

nursesNote: 0630 — 58 y/o male presented to ED at 0445 via private vehicle with sudden-onset severe 'tearing' chest pain that began at approximately 0415 while shoveling snow. Pain initially 10/10, radiated through the interscapular region of the back, then migrated downward to the lower back over 20 minutes. Client describes the pain as 'unlike anything I've ever felt — like something is ripping inside.' PMH: longstanding hypertension (poorly controlled, lapsed lisinopril prescription 6 weeks ago per family), 30-pack-year smoking history, hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin. Family hx significant: paternal grandfather died in his 60s of 'a torn artery in his chest.' On assessment: client diaphoretic, anxious, restless, pale. Right arm BP 192/106; left arm BP 148/82 — 44 mmHg differential. Left radial pulse weak (1+) compared to right radial (2+). New diastolic decrescendo murmur best heard at the right upper sternal border, not present on prior physical exam per outpatient records. Decreased breath sounds at the left lower lung base. Heart sounds otherwise without S3 or gallop, no rub. Neurologic: alert, oriented x4, no focal motor or sensory deficits at this moment. Lower extremities: pulses palpable but weaker on the left. CXR shows widened mediastinum (8.4 cm) and small left pleural effusion. ECG without ST elevation. Dissection protocol activated; CT angiogram pending. Family at bedside, very distressed.
providerOrders: Activate aortic dissection protocol
STAT cardiothoracic surgery consult — Type A is a surgical emergency
ICU transfer pending; OR/anesthesia notified
Continuous cardiac monitoring; bilateral arm BP monitoring initially, then arterial line in higher-reading (right) arm
STAT CT angiogram of chest, abdomen, and pelvis
Two large-bore peripheral IVs (16- or 18-gauge)
Type and crossmatch 6 units PRBC; have FFP and platelets available
Esmolol IV drip: start 0.05 mg/kg/min, titrate to HR 55–65 bpm — RATE CONTROL FIRST
AFTER HR target achieved: nitroprusside IV drip titrated to SBP 100–120 mmHg
Fentanyl 50 mcg IV q15min PRN pain — reduces sympathetic surge
Maintain strict NPO status
Indwelling Foley catheter to monitor UOP
DO NOT administer aspirin, anticoagulants, or thrombolytics
DO NOT use hydralazine (causes reflex tachycardia)
Do not start vasodilator before rate control is achieved

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Sudden-onset 'tearing' chest pain radiating to the interscapular back, then migrating to the lower back
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pathognomonic for aortic dissection. The triad of 'sudden, severe, ripping/tearing' pain with migration as the dissection extends distally is the single most specific historical feature. MI pain is more typically substernal pressure radiating to left arm/jaw, not migratory.

opts.text: BP differential >20 mmHg between right arm (192/106) and left arm (148/82)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — classic for dissection involving the branch vessels. A 44 mmHg differential indicates compromise of the left subclavian artery (the false lumen is obstructing flow). Always use the HIGHER reading for therapeutic targeting because that arm has the unobstructed flow representing aortic root pressure.

opts.text: Weak left radial pulse (1+) compared to right radial (2+) — pulse deficit
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Pulse deficits are present in ~30% of dissection clients and correlate with branch vessel involvement. The deficit's location helps localize the dissection extent. Loss of carotid pulses would indicate extension into the arch and is a stroke risk.

opts.text: New diastolic decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. This is acute aortic regurgitation — the dissection has extended retrograde into the aortic root, distorting valve coaptation. AR occurs in ~50% of Type A dissections and is one of the indications for emergent surgical repair with a valve-sparing root replacement.

opts.text: Widened mediastinum (8.4 cm) and left pleural effusion on chest x-ray
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — classic imaging finding. Mediastinum >8 cm on an upright PA film is suggestive. The pleural effusion likely represents blood leaking from the false lumen — an ominous sign of impending or contained rupture.

opts.text: D-dimer 8,400 ng/mL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. D-dimer rises from fibrin breakdown in the false lumen clot. While not specific (also elevated in PE, infection, malignancy), values >1,200 ng/mL have very high sensitivity for dissection. A normal D-dimer essentially rules out dissection.

opts.text: Family history of grandfather dying from 'a torn artery'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Genetic connective tissue disorders (Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz, Ehlers-Danlos vascular type) predispose to early aortic dissection. A family history at this strength changes risk assessment and may inform genetic counseling for family members.

opts.text: Lapsed antihypertensive prescription for 6 weeks with poorly controlled HTN
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — identifies the proximate trigger. Uncontrolled HTN is the most common modifiable risk factor for dissection. Heavy exertion (shoveling snow) acutely raised the BP further, providing the final shear force that initiated the intimal tear.

opts.text: ECG with sinus tachycardia and nonspecific ST-T changes — NO ST elevation, NO Q waves
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — distinguishes from STEMI. The absence of acute ST elevation makes STEMI unlikely. A dissection that extends into a coronary ostium (most commonly the right) CAN mimic STEMI on ECG — anchoring on STEMI in this clinical picture and giving heparin/ASA would be catastrophic. Always integrate history.

opts.text: Troponin I 0.08 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — but a DISTRACTOR. Mild troponin elevation can occur in dissection from coronary involvement or demand ischemia. Do NOT let this drive a STEMI treatment pathway (ASA, heparin, cath lab). The full clinical picture (tearing pain, BP differential, AR murmur, widened mediastinum) wins.

opts.text: Temperature 99.4°F
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue. Mild temperature elevation can occur from stress/inflammatory response. Not actionable in this context.

opts.text: BNP 142 pg/mL
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue. Mildly elevated BNP can occur in many conditions; this value doesn't indicate heart failure as the primary process and doesn't change immediate management.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: An intimal tear is allowing blood to dissect between the layers of the aortic wall, creating a false lumen parallel to the true lumen
opts.rat: CORRECT — the defining mechanism. With each cardiac cycle, the false lumen propagates further along the aorta, threatening branch vessels and risking complete rupture through the adventitia.

opts.text: This is a Stanford Type A dissection (involving the ascending aorta) — a true surgical emergency with mortality of approximately 1% per hour without repair
opts.rat: CORRECT. The AR murmur (root involvement) and the pattern of pain confirm ascending aortic involvement. Type A is surgical. Type B (descending aorta only, distal to left subclavian) is managed medically unless complicated by malperfusion, rupture, or refractory pain.

opts.text: The hemodynamic principle is to reduce dP/dt — the rate of pressure change in the aorta — by lowering BOTH heart rate AND blood pressure, with heart rate controlled FIRST
opts.rat: CORRECT — the cornerstone of dissection management. dP/dt represents the shear force on the aortic wall with each pulse. Reducing dP/dt slows propagation of the dissection. Beta blockade (esmolol, labetalol) reduces HR and contractility; vasodilator (nitroprusside) is added after HR control to fine-tune BP.

opts.text: The AR murmur is from retrograde extension of the dissection into the aortic root, distorting leaflet coaptation and causing aortic insufficiency
opts.rat: CORRECT. The dissection plane separates the aortic root from the valve attachments, leaving the leaflets unable to coapt properly during diastole. This can rapidly progress to severe AR with acute heart failure.

opts.text: The BP differential and pulse deficit indicate the false lumen is compressing the left subclavian artery, reducing flow to the left upper extremity
opts.rat: CORRECT. Branch vessel involvement is called 'malperfusion syndrome' and can also affect carotid (stroke), coronary (MI), renal (AKI), mesenteric (gut ischemia), and spinal (paraplegia) arteries depending on dissection extent.
