nursesNote: 0930 — 38 y/o previously healthy male transferred from outside ED for higher-level neurology and ICU care. Two and a half weeks ago he had a self-limited 'food poisoning' episode (3 days of profuse non-bloody diarrhea after a backyard chicken cookout — never sought care). One week ago developed bilateral foot tingling and 'rubbery' legs. Three days ago noted difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a chair. Two days ago could not walk and was brought to outside ED — weakness had ascended to hands and proximal arms; lumbar puncture showed protein 142 with only 2 WBC (albumino-cytologic dissociation); GBS suspected and IVIG 0.4 g/kg started yesterday (dose 1 of 5). Overnight: weakness progressed further with new bilateral facial weakness (cannot fully close eyes, weak smile), dysphagia, and softening voice. On arrival here: profound flaccid quadriparesis (1/5 lower extremities distally and proximally; 2/5 upper extremities; 3/5 neck flexion); bilateral facial diplegia; diminished gag; AREFLEXIA throughout (biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, Achilles all absent bilaterally); sensation grossly preserved with mild distal paresthesias; pupils 3 mm and reactive; cognition intact and clearly distressed; no bowel or bladder involvement yet (denies retention; voided 350 mL spontaneously 2 hr ago). Respiratory mechanics on arrival NIF −24 cm H2O, FVC 1.2 L (~17 mL/kg ideal body weight) with downward trend over 36 hr; ABG on 4 L NC shows pH 7.31 / PaCO2 52 / PaO2 68 — type II respiratory failure. During a routine left-lateral repositioning at 0925, client experienced a sudden hemodynamic swing: HR dropped from 112 to 44 bpm with a 3-second sinus pause on telemetry, BP 168/96 → 82/48, mild diaphoresis, no chest pain or loss of consciousness. Atropine 0.5 mg IV drawn at bedside but NOT yet given; HR returned to 96 within 90 seconds with reverse Trendelenburg and 250 mL fluid bolus. This is autonomic dysfunction — classic for GBS. Campylobacter jejuni IgM positive on serology, confirming the most common antecedent infection. AChR-Ab and anti-GQ1b antibodies negative (rules out MG and Miller Fisher variant). MRI shows faint cauda equina nerve root enhancement supporting demyelinating polyneuropathy. Continuing IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day (currently dose 2 of 5). Family at bedside, wife present and quietly tearful but composed. Clinical picture: ACUTE INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY (classic GBS / AIDP) post-Campylobacter, with rapidly evolving respiratory failure AND autonomic instability — both potentially fatal complications. Anticipated intubation within hours; cardiology aware for possible pacing pads.
providerOrders: ICU admission with continuous cardiac monitoring and continuous pulse oximetry; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP monitoring during autonomic swings
Strict NPO until formal speech therapy swallow eval (bulbar weakness with diminished gag)
Bedside NIF and FVC q4h (more frequently if trending down); rapid response criteria if FVC <15 mL/kg, NIF less negative than −25, or any bulbar incompetence with poor secretion clearance

Continue IVIG 0.4 g/kg IV daily x 5 days total (currently dose 2 of 5); pre-medicate with acetaminophen 650 mg PO and diphenhydramine 25 mg IV; slow infusion rate over 6–8 hr; monitor for aseptic meningitis, AKI, anaphylaxis (especially IgA-deficient), and thromboembolism
DO NOT GIVE CORTICOSTEROIDS — clinical trials have shown NO benefit in GBS (and possible harm); this is OPPOSITE of MG treatment

Anticipated intubation: use REDUCED-DOSE rocuronium (sensitivity unpredictable in neuromuscular disease); AVOID succinylcholine (risk of severe hyperkalemia from upregulated denervation-pattern receptors after first ~5 days of weakness); sugammadex available for reversal
Atropine 0.5 mg IV pre-drawn at bedside for symptomatic bradycardia; transcutaneous pacing pads ON the client; cardiology aware

Treat autonomic hypertension with SHORT-acting agents only (e.g., labetalol 5 mg IV titrated, or nicardipine drip); AVOID long-acting antihypertensives — autonomic swings can crash the BP minutes later
DVT prophylaxis MANDATORY: enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily plus sequential compression devices on both legs (high VTE risk with immobility and autonomic dysfunction)
Pain management for neuropathic dysesthesias: gabapentin 300 mg PO TID titrated up; avoid opioids if possible (autonomic dysfunction + ileus risk)
Stool softener (docusate) and bowel regimen; bladder scan q6h with straight cath if retention >300 mL (autonomic bladder dysfunction common)
Eye care q2h with artificial tears and tape eyelids closed during sleep (facial diplegia → incomplete eye closure → corneal abrasion risk)
Pressure injury prevention: turn q2h, heel float pillows, specialty mattress; meticulous skin assessment with each turn
Communication board / yes-no eye-blink system established BEFORE intubation while client can still nod and speak
Social work, chaplaincy if desired by client/family; daily family meeting; physical and occupational therapy consult for early ROM and positioning

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Symmetric ASCENDING flaccid weakness over ~7 days with AREFLEXIA on exam (biceps, triceps, patellar, Achilles all absent bilaterally)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the bedside fingerprint of GBS. The combination of ascending symmetric flaccid weakness with AREFLEXIA distinguishes GBS from MG (reflexes preserved), from stroke (asymmetric, hyperreflexic over time), and from spinal cord syndromes (sensory level, bowel/bladder involvement early). Areflexia + ascending weakness = AIDP until proven otherwise.

opts.text: NIF trending from −60 → −38 → −24 cm H2O over 36 hours, FVC 1.2 L (~17 mL/kg)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the TREND is what matters, not just the absolute number. A clinically deteriorating respiratory mechanic profile in GBS means intubation should be PLANNED, not reactive. The '20-30-40 rule' (FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than −30, MEP <40) signals imminent failure. This client has crossed all three thresholds.

opts.text: ABG: pH 7.31, PaCO2 52, PaO2 68 on 4 L NC — type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. PaCO2 elevation in neuromuscular disease is a LATE sign of diaphragmatic fatigue. By the time CO2 rises, alveolar ventilation is failing. Like in MG crisis, the bedside mechanics (NIF/FVC) detect failure HOURS before the ABG; do not wait for the ABG to be 'bad enough.'

opts.text: Sudden HR 44 with 3-second sinus pause + BP swing from 168/96 to 82/48 during routine repositioning
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — AUTONOMIC DYSFUNCTION. Up to 70% of severe GBS clients develop autonomic instability: labile BP, tachycardia or bradycardia, sinus pauses, arrhythmias, ileus, urinary retention, abnormal sweating. Sudden bradycardia/asystole can occur with vagal stimulation (suctioning, NG placement, turning, defecation). Have atropine and pacing pads ready.

opts.text: Bilateral facial diplegia with diminished gag and dysphagia to thin liquids
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — bulbar involvement. Cannot fully close eyes (corneal abrasion risk), cannot protect airway (aspiration risk). Strict NPO, aspiration precautions, eye care q2h with taping during sleep. Bulbar involvement is a strong indication to intubate EARLY in GBS, often before NIF/FVC thresholds are crossed.

opts.text: Albumino-cytologic dissociation on CSF: protein 142 mg/dL with only 2 WBC/µL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic. Elevated protein with normal cell count is the classic CSF pattern of GBS (typically appears after day 7 — earlier LP can be normal). Rules out bacterial meningitis (high WBC) and many other mimics. Combined with the clinical picture and NCS, confirms the diagnosis.

opts.text: Campylobacter jejuni IgM positive on serology with recent diarrheal illness 2.5 weeks ago
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — identifies the antecedent trigger. Campylobacter is the most common preceding infection in GBS (~30% of cases) and is associated with the axonal AMAN variant and worse prognosis. Other triggers: CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, HIV, hepatitis E, Zika, COVID-19, and rarely vaccinations. Identifying the trigger does not change acute treatment but informs prognosis and counseling.

opts.text: Nerve conduction studies: prolonged distal motor latencies, conduction block, slowed velocities, absent F waves — consistent with AIDP (demyelinating)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms demyelinating subtype (AIDP) vs axonal (AMAN/AMSAN) vs Miller Fisher (ataxia, areflexia, ophthalmoplegia). AIDP is the most common Western subtype, generally with better recovery prognosis than axonal variants. Treatment (plasmapheresis or IVIG) is the same across subtypes.

opts.text: Negative AChR-Ab and negative anti-GQ1b antibodies
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pertinent negatives that REFINE the differential. Negative AChR-Ab makes MG unlikely (no MG, no concurrent disease). Negative anti-GQ1b rules out Miller Fisher variant (which presents with the classic triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia). Documenting these clinches the AIDP diagnosis.

opts.text: Severe immobility with rapidly progressive paralysis (1/5 in legs, 2/5 in arms) and autonomic dysfunction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — HIGH VTE risk. Immobility plus autonomic dysfunction (which can cause venous pooling and stasis) plus inflammatory state plus possible mechanical ventilation later equals one of the highest-risk VTE profiles in medicine. Pharmacologic AND mechanical prophylaxis are mandatory unless contraindicated.

opts.text: Cognition intact; client is alert, oriented, clearly distressed and able to communicate concern about 'what is happening to me'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial and ethical. GBS preserves cognition while progressively paralyzing the body — an experience patients describe as terrifying. Establish a communication system (eye blinks, communication board, letter board) BEFORE intubation while the client can still nod. Frequent reassurance, presence, explanation, and family involvement are not optional in GBS — they are clinical care.

opts.text: Mild distal paresthesias ('tingling') in feet and fingers
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue in isolation. Sensory symptoms are common but generally mild in classic AIDP — motor involvement dominates. Treat associated neuropathic pain with gabapentin, document distribution, but no immediate intervention needed beyond comfort measures.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Molecular mimicry: antibodies generated against Campylobacter jejuni lipo-oligosaccharide cross-react with PERIPHERAL NERVE myelin (specifically gangliosides), producing immune-mediated segmental demyelination of nerve roots and peripheral nerves
opts.rat: CORRECT — the central mechanism. The 'mimicry' between bacterial surface molecules and host nerve antigens explains the typical 1–3 week interval between infection and onset of GBS. Demyelination slows conduction (slow velocities) and blocks action potentials at affected segments (conduction block) — producing the bedside flaccid weakness and AREFLEXIA. This is the OPPOSITE of MG, where the lesion is post-synaptic at the neuromuscular JUNCTION.

opts.text: AREFLEXIA in GBS reflects the loss of the peripheral afferent and efferent limbs of the reflex arc due to demyelination — distinguishing it sharply from upper motor neuron lesions (hyperreflexia) and from MG (intact reflexes)
opts.rat: CORRECT and high-yield. The reflex arc requires intact peripheral nerves (both directions). Demyelinate them and the reflex disappears. This is one of the strongest single bedside features distinguishing GBS from any central process or from MG. The presence of brisk reflexes essentially rules out classic GBS.

opts.text: Plasmapheresis (5 exchanges over ~1–2 weeks) removes pathogenic antibodies; IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) modulates immune response — BOTH are equally effective in GBS; either is acceptable, NOT both together
opts.rat: CORRECT. Multiple RCTs have shown equivalent efficacy. Either is started within 2–4 weeks of symptom onset for maximum benefit (no added value beyond 4 weeks in most patients). Combining PE + IVIG provides NO added benefit and increases risk and cost. CHOOSE ONE based on availability, vascular access, comorbidities (renal disease favors PE; cardiac instability favors IVIG; IgA deficiency requires special IVIG products).

opts.text: CORTICOSTEROIDS are NOT indicated in GBS — multiple trials have shown they do not improve outcomes and may modestly worsen them. This is OPPOSITE to MG, where steroids are a cornerstone of long-term treatment.
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield distinction. The Plasma Exchange/Sandoglobulin GBS Trial and Cochrane reviews show NO benefit of steroids (alone or combined with IVIG/PE) in GBS. Mechanism may be that steroids interfere with macrophage-mediated clearance of damaged myelin debris, slowing recovery. KEY teaching point — students conflate GBS and MG and assume steroids should be used in both.

opts.text: Autonomic dysfunction in GBS reflects involvement of the autonomic nervous system fibers (which are also peripheral nerves) — producing labile BP, paradoxical bradycardia/tachycardia, sinus pauses, ileus, urinary retention, and abnormal sweating. Up to 70% of severe cases.
opts.rat: CORRECT. The same peripheral nerve demyelination affects autonomic fibers. Manifestations are unpredictable and labile — short-acting antihypertensives only; atropine and pacing for symptomatic bradycardia. Suctioning, NG placement, turning, and Valsalva (defecation) can trigger sudden cardiovascular swings. Continuous monitoring is essential.

opts.text: Succinylcholine is CONTRAINDICATED for intubation in GBS after the first ~5 days of weakness due to risk of severe hyperkalemia from upregulated extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors (denervation-pattern receptor proliferation)
opts.rat: CORRECT — life-threatening pharmacology pearl. In denervation states (GBS, spinal cord injury after day 5, prolonged immobility, burns, crush injury), extrajunctional ACh receptors proliferate. Succinylcholine depolarizes ALL of them, releasing massive amounts of intracellular potassium — fatal hyperkalemia (K >8) has been reported. Use REDUCED-dose rocuronium with sugammadex available. The same caution applies in MG (different mechanism, similar bottom line: avoid succinylcholine).

opts.text: The albumino-cytologic dissociation (high CSF protein with normal cell count) reflects breakdown of the blood-nerve barrier at affected nerve roots, allowing protein leakage into the CSF without an associated inflammatory cellular infiltrate
opts.rat: CORRECT. Typically appears after day 7 (early LP can be falsely normal). The pattern distinguishes GBS from infectious causes of weakness (e.g., bacterial meningitis would show pleocytosis with high WBC). MRI nerve-root enhancement reflects the same disrupted blood-nerve barrier.

opts.text: GBS is identical to myasthenic crisis and should be treated with pyridostigmine and immunosuppression with steroids plus IVIG
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — and the most important conceptual error. GBS and MG share some features (neuromuscular respiratory failure, NIF/FVC monitoring, plasmapheresis/IVIG as immunotherapy) but are FUNDAMENTALLY different: GBS = peripheral nerve demyelination with AREFLEXIA; MG = post-synaptic NMJ antibody with intact reflexes. Pyridostigmine does NOT work in GBS (the problem is upstream of the NMJ). Steroids HELP in MG but DO NOT HELP in GBS.

opts.text: All hypertension in GBS should be treated aggressively with long-acting agents (e.g., amlodipine, lisinopril) to maintain stable BP
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — dangerous. Autonomic swings in GBS mean a hypertensive client can become severely hypotensive minutes later. Long-acting agents trap the BP low when the autonomic surge subsides. Use SHORT-acting agents (labetalol IV titrated, nicardipine drip) for severe symptomatic hypertension only, and titrate cautiously.

opts.text: DVT prophylaxis is optional in GBS because immobility is short-lived (1–2 days)
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT. GBS clients are profoundly immobile for WEEKS to MONTHS, with autonomic dysfunction that promotes venous stasis. Pulmonary embolism is a leading cause of death in GBS. Combined pharmacologic (enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily or heparin 5,000 units SubQ q8h) AND mechanical (SCDs) prophylaxis is mandatory unless contraindicated, throughout the immobility phase.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Impaired Spontaneous Ventilation r/t peripheral nerve demyelination with diaphragmatic and bulbar weakness (NIF −24, FVC 17 mL/kg, PaCO2 52, paradoxical breathing)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Breathing (ABCs). The client has crossed all three '20-30-40' criteria AND has bulbar involvement. Intubation should be planned in the next hour or two, in a controlled setting with experienced operator and reduced-dose rocuronium (NOT succinylcholine).

opts.text: Risk for Cardiac Arrhythmia / Hemodynamic Instability r/t autonomic dysfunction (witnessed sinus pause, BP swing 168/96 → 82/48)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Circulation. The autonomic swing demonstrated during repositioning shows this is an unstable autonomic state. Sudden asystole during routine bedside maneuvers has been described. Arterial line, continuous cardiac monitoring, atropine and pacing pads on the client, and avoidance of vagal triggers (rapid NG placement, deep suctioning without pre-oxygenation, Valsalva).

opts.text: Risk for Aspiration r/t bulbar weakness with diminished gag, facial diplegia, and dysphagia
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Airway protection. Strict NPO, HOB 30°, oral suction at bedside. The combination of bulbar weakness AND respiratory weakness is a strong indication for early intubation. Without airway protection, even small aspiration events in a client with weak cough produce devastating pneumonia.

opts.text: Risk for Venous Thromboembolism r/t profound immobility plus autonomic dysfunction plus inflammatory state
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. PE is a leading cause of death in GBS, but the timeline is days to weeks (vs minutes for the respiratory/cardiac risks). Begin enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily PLUS bilateral SCDs immediately and continue throughout the immobility phase. Monitor for unexplained tachycardia, dyspnea, or hypoxia that could herald PE.

opts.text: Disturbed Body Image / Severe Anxiety r/t rapid loss of motor function with preserved cognition ('locked-in' experience)
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in the acute ranking but ESSENTIAL to address concurrently. GBS preserves cognition while paralyzing the body — patients consistently describe the experience as terrifying. Establish a communication system (eye blinks, board) BEFORE intubation, narrate every nursing intervention, involve family, consider psychiatric/chaplain support, and reassess emotional state with each shift. This is not lower priority because it doesn't matter — it is lower because the airway will kill the client first if neglected.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Continue IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for the planned 5-day course (currently dose 2 of 5); pre-medicate and infuse slowly; monitor for headache, aseptic meningitis, AKI, anaphylaxis, thromboembolism
opts.rat: First-line GBS treatment. Completing the full 5-day course gives the best chance of halting progression and accelerating recovery. Common side effects: headache, low-grade fever, fluid overload. Rare but serious: aseptic meningitis, AKI (especially older sucrose-stabilized products in elderly), anaphylaxis (especially IgA-deficient — check IgA before first dose if possible), thromboembolism (slow infusion, hydrate).

opts.text: Plan anticipated intubation: pre-oxygenate, experienced operator, REDUCED-dose rocuronium (NOT succinylcholine), sugammadex available, atropine drawn, pacing pads on
opts.rat: Controlled intubation BEFORE respiratory arrest is the standard of care. Rocuronium at reduced dose because of unpredictable sensitivity in neuromuscular disease (and sugammadex reverses it quickly if needed). Succinylcholine is CONTRAINDICATED after day 5 of weakness because of denervation-pattern receptor proliferation → severe hyperkalemia.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring, arterial line for beat-to-beat BP, atropine 0.5 mg IV at bedside, transcutaneous pacing pads on the client
opts.rat: Autonomic dysfunction surveillance and rescue. Already demonstrated a 3-second pause during a benign turn — the next event could be longer. Pacing pads ON the client (not just in the room) so the response is seconds not minutes. Notify cardiology for backup.

opts.text: DVT prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily PLUS bilateral sequential compression devices
opts.rat: Mandatory in GBS. Combined pharmacologic and mechanical prophylaxis from admission through the immobility phase. PE is a leading cause of death; missed prophylaxis is a sentinel event. Hold enoxaparin if active bleeding or imminent procedure with bleeding risk.

opts.text: Eye care q2h with artificial tears; tape eyelids closed during sleep and unconsciousness
opts.rat: Bilateral facial diplegia → incomplete eye closure → corneal drying and abrasion. Artificial tears, ointment at bedtime, and light tape across the lids prevent permanent corneal damage. Easy to overlook in the setting of more dramatic concerns but causes lasting morbidity.

opts.text: Communication board, letter board, or eye-blink yes/no system established BEFORE intubation while the client can still nod and speak
opts.rat: Critical. Once intubated and fully paralyzed, communication becomes very limited. Setting up a system in advance — with the client and family — preserves autonomy, reduces anxiety, and is associated with better psychiatric outcomes after recovery. This is nursing leadership.

opts.text: Pressure injury prevention: turn q2h, heel float pillows, specialty mattress; eye care, skin assessment, and joint ROM with each turn
opts.rat: Profound immobility lasting weeks to months puts every bony prominence at risk. Skin breakdown is a major source of morbidity AND of nursing-sensitive quality metrics. Bundle care: turn + skin assessment + ROM + eye care + family update during each repositioning to minimize autonomic triggers.

opts.text: Methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily x 5 days to reduce nerve inflammation
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — and the highest-yield distinction from MG. Multiple RCTs and Cochrane reviews show steroids do NOT improve GBS outcomes and may slightly worsen them (possibly by interfering with macrophage-mediated clearance of damaged myelin debris). This is OPPOSITE of MG, where steroids are a mainstay. Avoid steroids unless the client has a separate steroid-responsive indication (e.g., adrenal insufficiency).

opts.text: Pyridostigmine 60 mg PO every 4 hours to improve muscle strength
opts.rat: Wrong drug for wrong disease. Pyridostigmine treats MG (post-synaptic NMJ pathology). In GBS, the lesion is UPSTREAM at the demyelinated peripheral nerve — there is no ACh delivered to the NMJ to amplify. No benefit; risks SLUDGE side effects, bradycardia, and bronchospasm in an already-vulnerable client.

opts.text: Plasmapheresis 5 exchanges over 7–10 days ADDED to the current IVIG course for combination therapy
opts.rat: Combining PE + IVIG provides NO added benefit over either alone and increases risk and cost (PSGBS trial). PICK ONE based on availability, vascular access, comorbidities. Switching is reasonable if the chosen treatment fails or is contraindicated, but routine combination is not standard.

opts.text: Succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg IV for rapid sequence intubation
opts.rat: ⚠️ DIRECTLY CONTRAINDICATED in GBS after ~5 days of weakness. Denervation-pattern proliferation of extrajunctional ACh receptors → severe hyperkalemia (K rises 5–10 mEq/L in minutes) → cardiac arrest. Use REDUCED-dose rocuronium with sugammadex available.

opts.text: Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily for the hypertension noted during the autonomic swing
opts.rat: Wrong drug class for autonomic instability. Long-acting agents traps the BP low when the next autonomic swing crashes the pressure. Use SHORT-acting agents (labetalol IV titrated, nicardipine drip) for severe symptomatic hypertension only. Treat persistent severe hypertension; tolerate mild fluctuations.

opts.text: Gabapentin 300 mg PO TID titrated up for neuropathic pain from the dysesthesias
opts.rat: Appropriate first-line for the neuropathic pain that frequently accompanies GBS, especially during the early demyelinating phase. Titrate to effect; renally dose. Preferred over opioids because of autonomic-dysfunction risks (ileus, urinary retention, respiratory depression on top of NM weakness).

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Strict NPO; HOB 30°; oral suction at bedside; continuous cardiac monitoring with atropine drawn and pacing pads ON the client; arterial line placement coordinated
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate safety: airway protection (NPO + positioning), arrhythmia rescue setup (atropine + pads at hand, not in a drawer), and continuous hemodynamic surveillance. None of these require additional orders or delay — they are bedside nursing decisions executed in minutes.

opts.text: Call MICU + anesthesia for anticipated intubation; pre-oxygenate the client; have REDUCED-dose rocuronium and sugammadex available; AVOID succinylcholine
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Anticipated intubation in a controlled setting, with experienced operator and the right drugs, is vastly safer than crash intubation after arrest in a client with already-failing airway protection. Reduced rocuronium prevents prolonged paralysis; sugammadex reverses it quickly if a difficult airway is encountered.

opts.text: Establish a communication system (eye-blink yes/no, communication board) WITH the client and family BEFORE intubation; explain what will happen step by step
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical and emotionally essential. Once intubated and paralyzed, the client cannot easily communicate. Establishing the system NOW, while the client can still nod and speak, preserves autonomy and reduces post-recovery PTSD. This is high-impact nursing care that students often defer; experienced ICU nurses prioritize it.

opts.text: Continue IVIG dose 2 of 5; ensure DVT prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily + bilateral SCDs); gabapentin 300 mg PO TID for neuropathic dysesthesias; HOLD pyridostigmine and HOLD any corticosteroids ordered by error
opts.rat: #4 — Ongoing disease-modifying and supportive treatment. IVIG is the definitive treatment; DVT prophylaxis prevents the leading cause of death; gabapentin addresses neuropathic pain. The HOLD orders on pyridostigmine and steroids are essential safety steps — students commonly conflate GBS with MG and assume both drugs are appropriate.

opts.text: Bundle of routine cares: eye care q2h with tape during sleep, turn q2h with skin assessment, ROM exercises, bladder scan q6h with straight cath PRN, family meeting; coordinate PT/OT for early ROM
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Recovery from GBS takes weeks to months; the cumulative impact of consistent bundled nursing care (skin integrity, joint ROM, eye care, bladder management, family support) determines long-term function. Build the daily routine now; reassess and adjust as autonomic stability improves.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 7 after completing IVIG: NIF −48 cm H2O, FVC 2.8 L; able to lift legs 1 inch off the bed; faint return of patellar reflex on the right; bulbar function still impaired but no further descent
opts.rat: Classic recovery trajectory. GBS clients often reach a nadir 2–4 weeks after onset, then begin slow recovery over months. Even subtle return of reflexes and tiny improvements in strength are reliable early signs of remyelination. Respiratory mechanics improving means weaning trials can begin. Manage expectations: full recovery can take 6–12 months and is incomplete in ~20%.

opts.text: Day 30: extubated, tolerating soft diet with thickened liquids; transferred to acute rehab; ambulating 50 feet with two-person assist; communication intact; gabapentin tapered as neuropathic pain resolves
opts.rat: Successful disposition trajectory. Rehabilitation phase is long but progressive. Family teaching on home setup, equipment, ongoing PT/OT, return-to-work timeline, and 'recovery is months, not days' counseling. Vaccination history should be discussed (rare association with some vaccines; routine vaccines are NOT contraindicated long-term).

opts.text: Day 12: client clearly grateful, smiling weakly when nurse explains progress; communication board used effectively; family rotates 24/7 presence with nurse-coordinated rest periods
opts.rat: Psychosocial recovery as important as motor recovery. Patient autonomy preserved through communication system; family integrated into care; nurse coordinates rest to prevent caregiver burnout. Long-term mental health outcomes are improved by these interventions. Continue social work involvement and consider routine psychiatric screening once stable.

opts.text: Hour 4 of admission: during turning for skin assessment, asystole on monitor for 6 sec with loss of consciousness; atropine 0.5 mg IV pushed, immediate chest compressions x 30 seconds, sinus rhythm restored; transcutaneous pacing pads activated and ready
opts.rat: Severe autonomic instability — close call. The pacing pads on the client meant pacing could be activated in seconds. Increase monitoring intensity; consider transvenous pacing if recurrent; minimize vagal triggers (gentle suctioning with pre-oxygenation, no rapid NG manipulation, no Valsalva). Cardiology to bedside; document each event in detail.

opts.text: Day 10: develops a new fever of 102.8°F, leukocytosis 18 K, purulent endotracheal secretions, new right lower lobe consolidation on CXR
opts.rat: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). Obtain endotracheal aspirate culture; start empiric coverage (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin) with appropriate de-escalation per cultures. AVOID aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones if possible (they impair neuromuscular transmission and could worsen residual weakness). Aggressive pulmonary toilet, head elevation, oral care.

opts.text: Day 5: client develops sudden severe pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, tachycardia to 130, hypoxia despite ventilator support; CT pulmonary angiogram shows bilateral segmental pulmonary emboli
opts.rat: Pulmonary embolism — a leading cause of death in GBS. Therapeutic anticoagulation (heparin drip with PTT goal or LMWH at treatment dose); supportive care; hemodynamic monitoring. Even with diligent prophylaxis, PE can occur. Underscores the importance of vigilance for unexplained tachycardia/dyspnea throughout the immobility phase.

opts.text: Day 14: weakness has plateaued at quadriparesis and bulbar paralysis with no signs of recovery; speech therapy and neurology recommend tracheostomy and PEG tube for prolonged ventilator and nutritional support
opts.rat: Plateau phase — neither improving nor worsening. About 25–30% of severe GBS clients require prolonged ventilation (>2 weeks), triggering tracheostomy + PEG decisions. This is a major care milestone — multidisciplinary family meeting, social work involvement, discussion of long-term recovery expectations, and connection with GBS survivor groups (GBS/CIDP Foundation) help orient family expectations realistically.

opts.text: On day 8 the bedside nurse offers prednisone 60 mg PO daily 'just in case it helps with the inflammation'
opts.rat: Knowledge deficit — staff teaching opportunity. Steroids are NOT indicated in GBS and may modestly worsen outcomes. Clarify with the bedside team, review the evidence (PSGBS trial, Cochrane), and ensure the chart explicitly states 'no steroids — GBS.' Consider an education huddle with the unit. This is one of the most common errors driven by intuitive (but wrong) parallels with MG.

opts.text: Day 21: persistent moderate bilateral foot drop and weak finger extension despite otherwise good recovery; rehab team has fitted ankle-foot orthoses and is working on fine motor function
opts.rat: Expected residual deficit in some GBS clients. Approximately 20% have persistent neurologic deficits at 1 year — most commonly distal lower extremity weakness with foot drop. Orthoses, gait training, and ongoing PT/OT maximize function. Realistic expectation-setting and connection to long-term resources (rehab medicine, GBS support groups) are nursing responsibilities.

opts.text: Persistent absent reflexes on day 30 despite return of strength to 4/5 in proximal muscles
opts.rat: Expected. Reflex return lags behind strength return in GBS and may be permanently diminished or absent in some patients. Not an indication of failed recovery. Document and reassure; clinical status (strength, function, respiratory mechanics) is the meaningful measure.

title: Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage with Cerebral Vasospasm
subtitle: Delayed Cerebral Ischemia, Permissive Hypertension, and the CSW vs SIADH Pivot

patient.name: K. Nakamura
patient.admitDate: Hospital Day 7 (admitted from OSH on Day 1 of bleed)
patient.room: Neuro ICU Bed 12 (single room with EVD setup)

vitals.time: Day 1 — ED arrival at OSH (BEFORE aneurysm secured)
Day 2 — POD#1 after endovascular coiling (aneurysm SECURED)
Day 7, 0600 (CURRENT — new neuro deficit at start of shift)
vitals.bp: 142/86 (on minimal nicardipine; about to be transitioned to induced HTN)

labs.n: Transcranial Doppler (TCD) — right MCA mean flow velocity
labs.v: 195 cm/s (rising trend: 110 → 158 → 195 over 72 hr); Lindegaard ratio 4.2
labs.r: <120 cm/s normal; 120–200 mild–moderate vasospasm; >200 SEVERE; Lindegaard ratio >3 confirms vasospasm vs hyperemia

labs.v: Severe focal narrowing of right M1 segment of MCA (~70% lumen reduction); coiled right MCA bifurcation aneurysm remains occluded with no residual filling
labs.r: Normal vessel caliber; secured aneurysm without residual filling

labs.v: Resolving subarachnoid blood in basal cisterns; small hypodensity in right frontal lobe concerning for early infarct; ventricles stable; no rebleed or new hemorrhage
labs.r: Normal brain parenchyma

labs.v: Diffuse subarachnoid blood filling basal cisterns and bilateral Sylvian fissures with thick clot in right Sylvian fissure (modified Fisher grade 3 → HIGH vasospasm risk)
labs.r: No blood in subarachnoid space

labs.v: III (drowsy with mild focal deficit)
labs.r: I lowest severity → V highest

labs.v: 128 mEq/L (trending: 138 → 132 → 128 over 48 hr)
265 mOsm/kg
labs.r: 275–295 mOsm/kg

labs.v: 180 mEq/L (HIGH); urine output 4.2 L over 24 hr
labs.r: Helps distinguish CSW (high urine Na + high UOP + volume DEPLETION) from SIADH (high urine Na + LOW UOP + EUvolemia)

labs.v: Dry mucous membranes, CVP 2 mmHg (low), orthostatic BP drop on prior nursing assessment, weight down 2.4 kg from admission, negative I&O balance of −2.8 L over 48 hr
labs.r: Euvolemic CVP 8–12; balanced I&O

labs.v: K 3.6 / Cl 96 / HCO3 24 / BUN 22 / Cr 0.9 / Glucose 142 / Mg 2.0
labs.r: WNL except mild BUN/Cr elevation and stress hyperglycemia

labs.n: External ventricular drain (EVD) output and ICP
labs.v: EVD leveled at tragus, draining 10–15 mL/hr light pink CSF; ICP 14 mmHg with normal waveform
labs.r: ICP <22 mmHg; CSF clearing over time post-SAH

labs.v: 11.4 g/dL / 192 K / 1.0 (no anticoagulation given coiled aneurysm)
Background mild generalized slowing without epileptiform activity; no electrographic seizures
labs.r: Normal awake background

labs.v: Drowsy but arousable to voice; oriented x2 (knows name and self, fuzzy on date and place); pupils 3 mm bilateral, reactive; LEFT pronator drift; LEFT arm 3/5 (down from 5/5 yesterday); LEFT leg 4/5; mild left facial droop; mild dysarthria; NIHSS now 6 (was 1 yesterday)
labs.r: Baseline NIHSS 1; normal mentation and full strength

nursesNote: 0600 — Hospital Day 7 in this 56 y/o female with aneurysmal SAH (modified Fisher grade 3, Hunt-Hess III on admission) from a ruptured right MCA bifurcation aneurysm that was endovascularly coiled on Day 1 at the OSH and transferred here Day 2 for higher-level neurocritical care. She has been doing well through the early hospital course: aneurysm secured, EVD placed Day 1 for mild hydrocephalus with ICP managed appropriately, nimodipine 60 mg PO q4h started Day 1 and on schedule through dose #36 this morning, daily TCDs being trended, and yesterday morning her exam was at her baseline (NIHSS 1, only mild residual photophobia and headache). At the start of this shift change-of-shift assessment (0600), I noted CLEAR change from baseline: she is now drowsy and arousable only to voice (previously alert), oriented only x2, has new LEFT pronator drift with LEFT arm strength 3/5 (was 5/5 yesterday), LEFT leg 4/5, mild left facial droop, and mild dysarthria. NIHSS has climbed from 1 → 6 over this single shift. Pupils are 3 mm and reactive. EVD continues to drain 10–15 mL/hr light pink CSF with ICP 14 mmHg and normal waveform — no acute hydrocephalus. Stat CT non-contrast confirms NO rebleed and no new hemorrhage (coil mass remains intact) but shows a small new hypodensity in the right frontal lobe concerning for early infarct. CTA shows severe focal narrowing of the right M1 segment (~70% lumen reduction). TCD this morning shows right MCA mean flow velocity of 195 cm/s (rising trend over 72 hr: 110 → 158 → 195) with Lindegaard ratio 4.2 — confirming SEVERE cerebral vasospasm rather than hyperemia. The clinical picture is DELAYED CEREBRAL ISCHEMIA (DCI) from vasospasm — peak risk window Day 4–14 post-SAH, peaking days 7–10, exactly when this patient is presenting. ALSO concerning: serum Na has trended from 138 → 132 → 128 over 48 hr with urine Na 180 mEq/L, urine output 4.2 L/24 hr, dry mucous membranes, CVP 2, weight down 2.4 kg, and negative I&O balance −2.8 L — picture consistent with CEREBRAL SALT WASTING (CSW), NOT SIADH. The distinction matters greatly: CSW is treated with SALT and VOLUME (hypertonic saline + isotonic IVF), whereas SIADH would be treated with FLUID RESTRICTION (which would worsen vasospasm). Neurosurgery, neurology, and neuro-IR are at bedside. Plan: induce hypertension with norepinephrine titrated to SBP 180–220; continue nimodipine; bolus 3% hypertonic saline 250 mL for the symptomatic hyponatremia AND the elevated DCI risk; if no improvement, neuro-IR will pursue intra-arterial vasodilator (verapamil or nicardipine) and possible balloon angioplasty. Husband at bedside, anxious but tracking; daughter en route from out of state.
providerOrders: STOP nicardipine drip; initiate INDUCED HYPERTENSION: norepinephrine starting 0.05 mcg/kg/min, titrate up to MAP-driven SBP target 180–220 mmHg WHILE the aneurysm is secured by coil (DO NOT do this with an unsecured aneurysm — rebleeding risk)
Continue nimodipine 60 mg PO/NG q4h x 21-day course total — DO NOT give IV (causes severe hypotension); HOLD if SBP drops below 100
Hypertonic 3% saline 250 mL IV bolus over 30 minutes for symptomatic hyponatremia (Na 128 with neuro changes); recheck Na in 2 hr; AVOID OVERCORRECTION (>8–10 mEq/L per 24 hr → osmotic demyelination)
Replace ongoing urinary sodium losses: 0.9% NS at 150 mL/hr maintenance; goal euvolemia (NOT triple-H); reassess CVP/I&O/weight q6h
Strict I&O hourly; daily weights; sodium q4h until stable, then q6h
Continuous EVD monitoring: ICP q1h, drainage volume q1h, leveled at tragus, do NOT lower drain too low (over-drainage risk); CSF for cell count/glucose/protein/culture q72h or with clinical change
Neuro exam q1h x 24 hr (was q2h) — track NIHSS and pupillary response; immediately call neurosurgery for any worsening
Continuous EEG monitoring continued — vasospasm window has elevated seizure risk
Continue levetiracetam 1,000 mg IV BID (7-day post-SAH seizure prophylaxis — guidelines variable; continue per neurosurgery team)
DVT prophylaxis: bilateral sequential compression devices BOTH legs; HOLD pharmacologic anticoagulation while EVD in place and re-evaluate after removal
If no neurologic improvement within 30–60 min of induced HTN: neuro-IR for intra-arterial therapy (verapamil or nicardipine, possible balloon angioplasty of the right M1 segment)
Repeat TCDs daily (or BID if rising); CTA/CT perfusion if clinical change
Pain control with acetaminophen 1 g PO q6h scheduled; AVOID oversedation (need accurate neuro exam); reserve short-acting opioids for breakthrough headache
HOB 30°, neutral neck alignment to optimize cerebral venous drainage
NPO for now pending swallow eval; once stable, advance per speech therapy

Family meeting with neurosurgery, neurology, and ICU team this afternoon; ensure husband and daughter understand vasospasm window and the rationale for INDUCED hypertension (counterintuitive to most lay understanding)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: NEW left-sided weakness (LEFT arm 3/5 from 5/5 yesterday, LEFT leg 4/5, LEFT facial droop, dysarthria) with NIHSS climbing from 1 → 6 over this shift
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — DELAYED CEREBRAL ISCHEMIA (DCI) until proven otherwise. Any new focal neurologic deficit in the Day 4–14 post-SAH window must be assumed to be vasospasm-driven DCI and treated immediately. The shift-to-shift change is exactly when bedside nurses catch DCI — astute serial neuro checks save brain.

opts.text: TCD right MCA mean flow velocity 195 cm/s (rising: 110 → 158 → 195) with Lindegaard ratio 4.2
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms SEVERE cerebral vasospasm rather than hyperemia. TCD detects vasospasm by measuring increased flow velocity through narrowed vessels (same flow through smaller lumen = higher velocity). Lindegaard ratio (MCA velocity / ipsilateral extracranial ICA velocity) >3 distinguishes vasospasm from hyperemia. >6 = severe.

opts.text: CTA showing ~70% narrowing of right M1 segment; small new hypodensity in right frontal lobe on CT (early infarct)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — radiologic confirmation of vasospasm AND evidence of established ischemia. The CT hypodensity means brain tissue is already dying. Aggressive intervention now (induced HTN, intra-arterial therapy if needed) is aimed at salvaging the penumbra — tissue that is hypoperfused but not yet infarcted.

opts.text: Modified Fisher grade 3 on admission CT (thick subarachnoid clot in right Sylvian fissure)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — predicts vasospasm risk. Modified Fisher grade reflects amount and distribution of subarachnoid blood. Grade 3 (thick blood without IVH) and Grade 4 (any IVH or parenchymal blood) carry HIGH vasospasm risk. This is why daily TCDs and aggressive monitoring through Day 14 are standard.

opts.text: Aneurysm has been SECURED by endovascular coiling on Day 1 — coil mass intact on today's imaging
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — enables INDUCED HYPERTENSION. Once an aneurysm is secured (coiled or clipped), the rebleeding risk plummets. ONLY in this secured state can we safely raise SBP to 180–220 to drive cerebral perfusion through narrowed vessels. With an UNSECURED aneurysm this BP target would be CATASTROPHIC — instant rebleed risk.

opts.text: Serum Na trending 138 → 132 → 128 over 48 hr with urine Na 180, UOP 4.2 L/24 hr, weight down 2.4 kg, CVP 2, dry mucous membranes, negative I&O −2.8 L
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — CEREBRAL SALT WASTING (CSW), NOT SIADH. Both produce hyponatremia + high urine sodium in SAH patients but the VOLUME STATUS is opposite: CSW is volume DEPLETED (high UOP, weight loss, low CVP, negative balance); SIADH is EUvolemic. The distinction determines treatment: CSW needs SALT + VOLUME (hypertonic saline + IVF); SIADH needs FLUID RESTRICTION. Restricting fluid in CSW during vasospasm window = brain infarction.

opts.text: Nimodipine 60 mg PO q4h has been given on schedule (dose 36 of planned 21-day course)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — guideline-directed therapy. Nimodipine 60 mg PO q4h for 21 days is the only pharmacologic agent proven to improve neurologic outcomes after SAH. Mechanism is NOT primarily vasodilation in cerebral vessels — likely neuroprotection. Always PO/NG (IV nimodipine causes severe hypotension and is NOT used). HOLD if SBP <100 — split into 30 mg q2h to maintain dosing if BP-sensitive.

opts.text: EVD draining 10–15 mL/hr pink CSF at tragus level; ICP 14 mmHg; no acute hydrocephalus on CT
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — EVD is well-managed but constant vigilance required. Acute hydrocephalus is a common complication of SAH (blood blocks CSF reabsorption at arachnoid villi). Sudden change in drainage, waveform, or ICP requires immediate evaluation. NEVER lower the EVD below the prescribed level (over-drainage risk → herniation or rebleed).

opts.text: Heart rate 96 with mild fever 99.6°F
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE in context. Fever in SAH worsens outcomes (cerebral metabolic demand rises with each degree). Could also indicate non-infectious 'central fever' from blood in the subarachnoid space, ventilator-associated pneumonia, EVD-related ventriculitis, line infection, or other source. Workup AND aggressive temperature control with acetaminophen and cooling measures regardless of source.

opts.text: Pupils 3 mm bilateral and briskly reactive
opts.rat: Reassuring (no acute herniation, no oculomotor compression from posterior communicating artery aneurysm regrowth/rebleed). Document but no intervention triggered. Pupillary changes would dramatically escalate concern.

opts.text: Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL
opts.rat: Mildly low but acceptable. Anemia worse than ~8–9 g/dL is associated with worse outcomes in SAH (reduced oxygen delivery to brain), but 11.4 is fine. Document and monitor; transfuse only with clear evidence of inadequate oxygen delivery.

opts.text: Continuous EEG showing mild generalized slowing without epileptiform activity
opts.rat: Mild generalized slowing is common after SAH and not actionable in isolation. The IMPORTANT finding is the ABSENCE of subclinical seizures — non-convulsive status epilepticus is a known cause of unexplained neurologic decline after SAH, so continuous EEG is the right monitoring tool to rule it out.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Blood degradation products (hemoglobin breakdown, endothelin, free radicals) released into the subarachnoid space cause SMOOTH MUSCLE CONTRACTION and inflammation of cerebral arteries, producing focal narrowing (vasospasm) typically Day 4–14 post-SAH with peak Day 7–10 — exactly when this client is presenting
opts.rat: CORRECT — the central mechanism. The amount of subarachnoid blood (modified Fisher grade) predicts the risk. Vasospasm reduces cerebral perfusion through the affected vessels and, if severe and prolonged, produces DELAYED CEREBRAL ISCHEMIA (DCI) → infarction. About 30% of SAH survivors who escape the initial bleed develop clinically significant vasospasm.

opts.text: Once the aneurysm is SECURED (coiled or clipped), induced hypertension (SBP target 180–220) drives blood flow through narrowed vessels using the residual pressure gradient — a deliberate trade-off accepting cardiac stress to perfuse hypoperfused but viable brain tissue (the ischemic penumbra)
opts.rat: CORRECT and high-yield. The 'cerebral perfusion pressure = MAP − ICP' equation: when ICP is stable and vessels are narrowed, raising MAP increases perfusion. Modern guidelines focus on INDUCED HYPERTENSION with EUVOLEMIA (rather than classic Triple-H = HTN + Hypervolemia + Hemodilution) because the hemodilution and hypervolemia components carried risks without proven benefit.

opts.text: Cerebral salt wasting (CSW): brain natriuretic peptide and disturbed sympathetic outflow drive renal sodium loss out of proportion to water, producing hypovolemia with hyponatremia — treated with SALT and VOLUME (hypertonic saline + isotonic IVF)
opts.rat: CORRECT. CSW is common after SAH, brain injury, and tumors. The key bedside discriminator is VOLUME STATUS (low — dry mucous membranes, low CVP, weight loss, negative balance, high UOP). Treatment: replace the urinary sodium losses with normal saline AND hypertonic saline for symptomatic hyponatremia. Restricting fluid would worsen the vasospasm-induced ischemia.

opts.text: SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate ADH): brain injury triggers excess ADH release → water retention → hyponatremia with EUVOLEMIA (not hypovolemia) — treated with FLUID RESTRICTION. The clinical distinction from CSW depends on volume status assessment.
opts.rat: CORRECT — this is what CSW must be distinguished from. Same Na 128, same high urine Na — but OPPOSITE volume status and OPPOSITE treatment. Restricting fluid in a CSW patient during vasospasm window risks brain infarction. Giving extra salt + volume to an SIADH patient could worsen the hyponatremia. Volume status is the discriminator: dry/low CVP/weight loss → CSW; euvolemic/normal CVP → SIADH.

opts.text: Nimodipine improves neurologic outcomes after SAH but probably NOT primarily through vasodilation of cerebral arteries — likely neuroprotection. Must be given PO/NG (not IV — severe hypotension); held if BP-sensitive; planned 21-day course total.
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield nuance. Trials show outcome improvement without strong correlation to TCD-measured vasospasm reduction. Suggests an effect on the calcium-dependent neuronal injury cascade rather than the vessel diameter itself. The dose is fixed (60 mg q4h), the duration is fixed (21 days), and the route is PO/NG only.

opts.text: Hypertonic 3% saline simultaneously CORRECTS the hyponatremia AND lowers ICP (if elevated) by osmotic shift of water out of brain tissue — a useful 'two birds, one stone' agent in SAH with concurrent hyponatremia and ICP concerns
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield clinical pearl. The reason hypertonic saline is increasingly preferred over mannitol in neurocritical care: hypertonic saline raises intravascular volume (good in hypovolemic CSW), raises serum Na (treats hyponatremia), and reduces brain water (lowers ICP). Mannitol can drop intravascular volume — sometimes counterproductive.

opts.text: The same blood pressure management used for ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE (permissive hypertension EARLY then tight control after tPA) applies to SAH — keep BP <180/110 throughout
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — and the most important conceptual trap. Acute ischemic stroke and SAH have OPPOSITE BP management goals during their respective active phases. SAH pre-securing: BP <140 to prevent rebleeding. SAH post-securing with vasospasm: INDUCED HTN with SBP 180–220 to perfuse narrowed vessels. Ischemic stroke pre-tPA: permissive HTN <185/110. Ischemic stroke post-tPA: tight <180/105. Confusing these can be fatal.

opts.text: Fluid restriction is the correct treatment for the hyponatremia of Na 128 because the urine sodium is high
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — and a potentially fatal error in this patient. The high urine sodium alone does NOT distinguish CSW from SIADH. Volume status does. This patient has clear hypovolemia (CVP 2, weight loss, negative balance, dry mucous membranes) — this is CSW. Restricting fluid during the vasospasm window would worsen brain ischemia. Treat with hypertonic saline + isotonic IVF replacement.

opts.text: Nimodipine should be given IV at the same dose for faster onset since the client has new symptomatic vasospasm
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and DANGEROUS. IV nimodipine causes severe systemic hypotension that can WORSEN cerebral ischemia. The ONLY approved route is PO or via NG/OG tube. If the client is NPO, use the liquid formulation through the NG/OG tube. Hold and call provider if SBP drops below 100, possibly splitting dose to 30 mg q2h.

opts.text: If induced hypertension and continued nimodipine fail to improve the neurologic deficit, intra-arterial therapy (intra-arterial verapamil or nicardipine, possible balloon angioplasty) is the next escalation step
opts.rat: CORRECT — the rescue strategy. When medical management fails for symptomatic vasospasm, neuro-interventional radiology can deliver vasodilators directly into the affected arteries (transient effect, may need repeating) and/or perform balloon angioplasty for proximal segment narrowing. Reserved for medically refractory cases because of procedural risks (vessel rupture, embolic stroke).

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Ineffective Cerebral Tissue Perfusion r/t severe cerebral vasospasm with delayed cerebral ischemia (DCI) — new focal deficit, NIHSS 1 → 6, CT showing early infarct
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Brain is dying NOW. Induced hypertension within minutes after confirming the aneurysm is secured; continue nimodipine; if no improvement, neuro-IR for intra-arterial therapy. Each hour of vasospasm-driven hypoperfusion expands the infarct. This is a stroke equivalent — time = brain.

opts.text: Risk for Worsening Neurologic Status r/t symptomatic hyponatremia from cerebral salt wasting (Na 128 with neuro changes) — superimposed on the vasospasm picture
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Hyponatremia of 128 with neurologic symptoms is its own dangerous problem (cerebral edema, seizure risk) AND is compounding the vasospasm-driven encephalopathy. Hypertonic 3% saline 250 mL IV bolus addresses BOTH simultaneously. Critical to AVOID overcorrection (>8–10 mEq/L per 24 hr → osmotic demyelination).

opts.text: Risk for Aneurysm Rebleeding r/t the underlying ruptured aneurysm (secured by coil but ongoing surveillance needed)
opts.rat: #3 — Significant but managed by securement. Once a coil is in place and confirmed intact on imaging, rebleeding risk falls dramatically (from ~20% in the first 24 hr unsecured to <2% after securement). This is what makes induced hypertension safe NOW. Continuous monitoring for rebleed (sudden severe headache, new neurologic decline, pupillary change, ICP spike, herniation signs) must continue.

opts.text: Risk for Hydrocephalus r/t blood blocking CSF reabsorption at arachnoid villi (EVD is currently in place and draining)
opts.rat: #4 — Actively managed by the EVD. Acute hydrocephalus is common after SAH (subarachnoid blood blocks CSF reabsorption). The EVD diverts CSF and monitors ICP. Constant vigilance for changes in drainage, ICP, or waveform. Sudden cessation of drainage with rising ICP → urgent neurosurgical evaluation (catheter blockage, mispositioning, intraventricular clot).

opts.text: Anxiety / Family Distress r/t critical illness and complex counterintuitive treatment plan (induced hypertension, EVD, vasospasm course)
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in acute ranking but a high-impact opportunity. The vasospasm window's induced hypertension is counterintuitive to lay understanding ('why are you DELIBERATELY raising her BP?'). A 5-minute focused conversation at the bedside — diagram, simple language, expected course (Day 14 vasospasm window ends), what to watch for — builds trust and reduces family distress. This is leadership-level bedside nursing.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: STOP nicardipine and INDUCE hypertension: norepinephrine drip 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to SBP 180–220 (aneurysm is secured by coil)
opts.rat: Core intervention for symptomatic vasospasm with secured aneurysm. Driving the MAP raises cerebral perfusion through narrowed vessels (CPP = MAP − ICP). The aneurysm securement makes this SAFE that wouldn't be otherwise. Goal-direct to neurologic improvement or until SBP target reached or cardiac strain develops; arterial line for beat-to-beat monitoring.

opts.text: Continue nimodipine 60 mg PO/NG q4h on schedule for the full 21-day course; HOLD only if SBP <100 (then resume or split dose)
opts.rat: Guideline-directed therapy — the only drug proven to improve neurologic outcomes after SAH. PO/NG ONLY (IV causes severe hypotension and is not approved). If BP-sensitive, split to 30 mg q2h. Never extend beyond 21 days without specific reason.

opts.text: Hypertonic 3% saline 250 mL IV bolus over 30 minutes for symptomatic hyponatremia (Na 128 with new neurologic changes); recheck Na in 2 hr
opts.rat: Two birds, one stone: corrects symptomatic hyponatremia AND supports cerebral perfusion by raising intravascular volume. Limit correction to ≤8–10 mEq/L per 24 hr to avoid osmotic demyelination. The 250 mL of 3% NaCl typically raises serum Na by ~2–4 mEq/L.

opts.text: Replace ongoing urinary sodium losses: 0.9% NS at 150 mL/hr maintenance; goal EUVOLEMIA (NOT triple-H)
opts.rat: Treats the volume-deplete component of CSW. Modern guidelines favor EUVOLEMIA + INDUCED HYPERTENSION over classic 'Triple-H' (HTN + Hypervolemia + Hemodilution) because the hypervolemia component carried risks (pulmonary edema, cardiac strain) without clear benefit. Aim to keep CVP 5–8 and net I&O slightly positive while tracking the natriuresis.

opts.text: Q1H neuro checks with NIHSS calculation; immediate provider notification for any further decline
opts.rat: The bedside nurse IS the vasospasm surveillance system in the post-secured SAH patient. Vasospasm can evolve in minutes to hours. Subtle changes — drowsiness, fine motor change, mild aphasia — are caught only by intentional repeat exams documented on a flow sheet, not by passive observation. Q1H during the active phase, then liberalized as the window closes.

opts.text: Continuous EEG monitoring (already in place); consider levetiracetam continuation per neurosurgery
opts.rat: Subclinical (non-convulsive) seizures are a known cause of unexplained neurologic decline in SAH and could be confused with vasospasm. cEEG catches them. Levetiracetam for post-SAH seizure prophylaxis is variable practice — guidelines suggest 3–7 days; some centers continue per neurosurgery. Continue per local protocol.

opts.text: If induced HTN fails to improve neurologic status within 30–60 min: escalate to neuro-IR for intra-arterial verapamil/nicardipine and/or balloon angioplasty of the right M1 segment
opts.rat: Standard rescue pathway. Intra-arterial vasodilators provide rapid focal vasodilation; angioplasty mechanically dilates the proximal segment (durable effect but limited to proximal vessels). Reserved for medically refractory symptomatic vasospasm because of procedural risks (vessel rupture, embolic stroke).

opts.text: Aggressive fluid restriction to <800 mL/day for the hyponatremia of Na 128
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — this is the SIADH treatment, not CSW. The volume status here (CVP 2, weight loss, negative balance, dry mucous membranes) is hypovolemic = CSW. Restricting fluid during vasospasm window would WORSEN cerebral ischemia. Volume status determines the hyponatremia subtype and the treatment direction.

opts.text: Resume the nicardipine drip and target SBP <140 to prevent further bleeding
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT for this stage of SAH. Once the aneurysm is secured, the BP rationale FLIPS: instead of preventing rebleed by LOWERING BP, we now want to drive cerebral perfusion through narrowed vessels by RAISING BP. Lowering SBP into the 140s during active symptomatic vasospasm would worsen ischemia. (Pre-securing, the answer is the opposite.)

opts.text: Nimodipine 60 mg IV push for faster onset
opts.rat: ⚠️ DIRECTLY CONTRAINDICATED. IV nimodipine causes severe systemic hypotension that can WORSEN cerebral ischemia (lowering CPP). ONLY PO or via NG/OG tube is approved. If NPO, use the liquid formulation through an NG tube. The order set should never permit IV nimodipine.

opts.text: Enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily for DVT prophylaxis
opts.rat: Currently held while EVD is in place (bleed risk at catheter site). Mechanical prophylaxis (bilateral SCDs) alone is the standard while EVD is in place. Reassess pharmacologic prophylaxis 24+ hr after EVD removal in coordination with neurosurgery. (Pharmacologic prophylaxis IS generally appropriate after that point — VTE risk in immobile SAH patients is high.)

opts.text: Bilateral sequential compression devices on both legs; mobilize within bed and out of bed as tolerated when neurologic status permits
opts.rat: Mechanical DVT prophylaxis is appropriate even while pharmacologic is held. Mobilization will progress over days as stability allows; meanwhile, in-bed positioning, passive ROM by PT, and SCDs minimize VTE risk. PT and OT consult once the acute vasospasm window is past.

opts.text: Family meeting today with neurosurgery, neurology, and ICU team to explain the vasospasm window, the rationale for induced hypertension, expected course, and what to watch for
opts.rat: Essential nursing leadership. The vasospasm-window logic is counterintuitive to family ('Why are you raising her BP after a brain bleed?'). A focused multidisciplinary meeting with diagrams, timeline, and 'what to watch for' empowers family as part of the surveillance team and reduces psychological burden. Coordinate, attend, and reinforce daily.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Notify neurosurgery and the rapid response criteria team STAT for new focal deficit; verify aneurysm is secured on today's imaging; place arterial line if not present
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Recognition + escalation. The bedside RN's new-deficit recognition is the trigger for the entire pathway. Verifying aneurysm securement is the safety gate before induced HTN. The arterial line is essential for beat-to-beat BP titration of norepinephrine.

opts.text: STOP nicardipine; START norepinephrine drip at 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to SBP 180–220; continue nimodipine 60 mg PO/NG on schedule
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Induced hypertension within minutes of confirming securement is the highest-yield intervention to drive cerebral perfusion through narrowed vessels. Continue nimodipine (held only if SBP <100). Track neurologic exam continuously; goal is symptom resolution, not just a number on the monitor.

opts.text: Administer hypertonic 3% saline 250 mL IV bolus over 30 minutes for Na 128 with neurologic symptoms; start 0.9% NS at 150 mL/hr; recheck Na in 2 hr
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Treats the CSW hypovolemic hyponatremia AND supports cerebral perfusion. Limits Na correction to ≤8–10 mEq/L per 24 hr (no more than 4–6 mEq/L in the first 4 hours). Recheck guides ongoing correction rate.

opts.text: If symptoms NOT improving within 30–60 minutes of induced HTN: prepare client for emergent neuro-IR for intra-arterial vasodilator and/or balloon angioplasty of right M1
opts.rat: #4 — Escalation gate. Have neuro-IR aware from the start; transport set up, consent considerations addressed, EVD secured for transport. Each hour of refractory vasospasm extends infarct burden — escalate decisively.

opts.text: Family meeting with neurosurgery + ICU team; explain vasospasm window, induced HTN rationale, expected course, and how family can help with surveillance (notify nurse for any change in mentation, weakness, headache)
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Once the acute escalation is in motion, the family meeting is the high-impact nursing leadership task — it integrates them as partners in surveillance and reduces distress. Plan it for later in the shift, when the acute decisions have been made.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 60 min after induced HTN (SBP 198, MAP 118): LEFT arm strength back to 5/5, facial droop resolved, NIHSS 6 → 1; speech clear; pupils 3 mm reactive
opts.rat: Excellent response — exactly what induced HTN is supposed to do in vasospasm with viable penumbra. Continue norepinephrine drip; gradually wean over hours-to-days as the vasospasm window closes (Day 14 mark). Confirms the deficit was reversible ischemia, not infarction.

opts.text: 2 hr after 3% saline bolus: Na 131 (up from 128), repeat osmolality 275; UOP unchanged at 150 mL/hr; mental status improved
opts.rat: Appropriate rate of Na correction (3 mEq/L in 2 hr is within the safe range; full 24-hr correction should stay ≤8–10 mEq/L). Continue isotonic IVF and monitor q4h Na. Avoid the temptation to give another large hypertonic bolus — overcorrection is a permanent neurologic injury.

opts.text: Day 14 (vasospasm window closing): norepinephrine weaned off, SBP back to 130s without intervention; ambulating with PT, mild residual headache only; EVD weaned and removed; transfer to step-down planned
opts.rat: Successful trajectory through the vasospasm window. After Day 14, vasospasm risk drops dramatically. Step-down to a neuro intermediate unit with continued NIHSS monitoring and gradual rehab. Family meeting on long-term recovery timeline — most clients have ongoing fatigue, mild cognitive changes, and headaches for weeks to months.

opts.text: 30 min after induced HTN: no improvement, left arm now 2/5, becoming more drowsy; pupil exam unchanged
opts.rat: Medically refractory vasospasm — escalate immediately to neuro-IR for intra-arterial vasodilators and/or balloon angioplasty of the right M1. Each additional 30 min of hypoperfusion extends the infarct. Have neuro-IR aware from the start so transport is not delayed.

opts.text: Hour 6: sudden severe headache, BP spikes to 220/120, pupil right 6 mm fixed, GCS drops from 14 to 6, ICP spikes from 14 to 42 mmHg
opts.rat: ANEURYSM REBLEED or new bleed elsewhere — neurosurgical emergency. STAT CT, neurosurgery to bedside, secure airway (intubate), aggressive ICP control with hypertonic saline and head elevation, possible osmotic therapy with mannitol, urgent OR for re-securement if rebleed confirmed. Mortality of rebleed is ~50%.

opts.text: Hour 8: Na drops from 131 back to 127 with UOP 250 mL/hr; weight down another 1.2 kg; CVP 2
opts.rat: Ongoing CSW losses outpacing replacement. Escalate IVF (NS 200–250 mL/hr or higher), add salt tabs PO/NG (1–2 g q4–6h) if tolerating, consider fludrocortisone 0.2 mg PO BID to enhance renal sodium retention. Reassess volume status frequently. NOT a reason to fluid restrict.

opts.text: Hour 24: Na has risen rapidly from 128 to 144 (correction of 16 mEq/L in 24 hr)
opts.rat: ⚠️ OVERCORRECTION risk for osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine and extrapontine myelinolysis — devastating, often permanent). STOP hypertonic saline, slow the IVF, consider DDAVP and/or 5% dextrose in water to RE-LOWER the sodium back into the safe range. Goal: keep correction to ≤8–10 mEq/L per 24 hr. Neuro and nephrology consultations.

opts.text: Day 9: temperature 102.6°F with neck stiffness and rising WBC; CSF from EVD shows pleocytosis WBC 350, low glucose, high protein
opts.rat: EVD-associated ventriculitis — a feared complication. Send CSF for culture; start empiric broad-spectrum coverage (vancomycin + an antipseudomonal cephalosporin like cefepime, or meropenem if extensive prior antibiotics); consider exchanging the EVD. Mortality and morbidity high — strict sterile technique with EVD manipulation is the prevention.

opts.text: Day 10: TCD velocity falling from 195 → 145 → 115 over 72 hr; client at neurologic baseline with no new deficits; norepinephrine being weaned
opts.rat: Vasospasm resolving as expected. TCD trend is the bedside biomarker. Continue cautious wean of induced HTN, continue nimodipine for the full 21-day course, anticipate transfer out of ICU within days. Recovery from SAH is months — schedule outpatient neurology, neuro rehab, and cognitive testing.

opts.text: Day 21 outpatient follow-up: client has completed nimodipine course; reports persistent fatigue, intermittent headache, and some mild short-term memory complaints; otherwise fully ambulatory and back to part-time work
opts.rat: Expected long-tail recovery profile after aneurysmal SAH. Fatigue, headaches, mild cognitive changes (especially executive function and memory) persist for weeks to months in a substantial fraction of survivors. Cognitive rehab referral, sleep optimization, and 'pace yourself' counseling are nursing roles. Reassure that this is the expected course, not a new complication.

title: Acute Bacterial Meningitis (Community-Acquired Pneumococcal)
subtitle: Dexamethasone-Before-Antibiotic, Sepsis, and the SIADH Mirror of SAH-CSW

patient.name: D. Reyes
patient.allergies: NKDA (denies penicillin allergy on direct questioning)
patient.admitDate: Today, 0345 (ED arrival via EMS — found obtunded by wife)
patient.room: ED Resus Bay 1 → MICU pending bed

vitals.time: Primary care clinic — 6 weeks ago (baseline, T2DM follow-up)
0445 (CURRENT — after 1 L NS, BP still borderline)

labs.v: 32 cm H2O
labs.r: <20 cm H2O (elevated reflects intracranial inflammation/edema)

labs.v: 2,800 cells/µL with 90% neutrophils (PMNs)
labs.r: <5 cells/µL, lymphocyte predominant

labs.v: 320 mg/dL
labs.r: 15–45 mg/dL

labs.v: 28 mg/dL with serum glucose 248 (CSF:serum ratio 0.11)
labs.r: CSF glucose 40–70 mg/dL with CSF:serum ratio >0.6; ratio <0.4 strongly suggests bacterial

labs.v: Gram-positive diplococci (consistent with Streptococcus pneumoniae)
labs.r: Negative for organisms

labs.v: 8.5 mmol/L
labs.r: <3.5 mmol/L; CSF lactate >4 strongly suggests bacterial vs viral

labs.v: No mass lesion, no midline shift, no acute hemorrhage, no obstructive hydrocephalus; mild basal cistern effacement consistent with diffuse cerebral edema; ventricles normal — SAFE to LP
labs.r: Normal CT before LP — rules out the contraindications to LP (mass, midline shift, posterior fossa lesion)

labs.v: 22.4 K/µL with 18% bands (left shift)
8.2 ng/mL
labs.r: <0.25 ng/mL low; >2 ng/mL strongly suggests bacterial infection

labs.v: 4.2 mmol/L
131 mEq/L (trending downward; 138 at admission 1 hr ago)
248 mg/dL
labs.r: 70–140 mg/dL (poorly controlled T2DM — HbA1c 8.9% last clinic visit)

labs.v: INR 1.4; PT 16; PTT 38; platelets 142 (trending down from 218 on admission); fibrinogen 180 mg/dL (low); D-dimer 5.2 (high)
labs.r: INR <1.1; platelets 150–400; fibrinogen 200–400; D-dimer <0.5 — picture suggestive of EARLY DIC

labs.n: Blood cultures x2 (peripheral, drawn before antibiotics)
labs.v: Pending; preliminary gram stain on 1 bottle: gram-positive diplococci
labs.r: Negative

labs.v: Sparse non-blanching petechial spots over lower extremities and trunk (<10 lesions total); no large purpuric patches
labs.r: No petechiae or purpura

labs.v: Brudzinski POSITIVE (passive neck flexion → involuntary hip/knee flexion); Kernig POSITIVE bilaterally (resistance to knee extension with hip flexed); nuchal rigidity prominent
labs.r: Negative meningeal signs

labs.v: GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6): opens eyes to voice, confused speech, follows commands; no focal motor deficits; pupils 4 mm bilateral, reactive; no papilledema on fundoscopy
labs.r: GCS 15; alert and fully oriented

nursesNote: 0445 — 64 y/o male with PMH of poorly controlled T2DM (last HbA1c 8.9%) and HTN on metformin/lisinopril, brought to ED by EMS at 0345 after wife found him obtunded on the couch. Wife reports a clear deterioration over the past 36 hr: started with what 'seemed like the flu' — congestion, cough, mild headache — 4 days ago, then yesterday morning developed a severe progressive headache with photophobia and neck pain; took acetaminophen with minimal relief. By yesterday evening was complaining of 'the worst headache of my life,' refusing meals, and dozing in the chair. This morning wife could not arouse him beyond mumbled responses and called 911. EMS noted a temperature of 103.1°F and brought him directly to our ED. On arrival: lethargic, opens eyes only to voice, confused speech, follows commands but slowly; GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6). Pupils 4 mm bilateral, reactive; no focal motor deficits. Markedly stiff neck — passive flexion produced clear involuntary hip/knee flexion (Brudzinski POSITIVE) — and bilateral Kernig positive. Sparse non-blanching petechiae noted on lower extremities and trunk (<10 lesions, no large purpuric patches). Sepsis-like physiology with T 103.2°F, HR 132, BP 88/52 (MAP 64), lactate 4.2, procalcitonin 8.2, WBC 22.4 with 18% bands. CT head before LP shows mild basal cistern effacement consistent with diffuse cerebral edema but no mass or midline shift — safe to LP. STAT LP returns at 0420: opening pressure 32 cm H2O; CSF cloudy with 2,800 WBC (90% PMN), protein 320, glucose 28 (CSF:serum ratio 0.11), lactate 8.5, gram stain showing gram-positive diplococci (presumed Streptococcus pneumoniae). Picture is CRITICAL: acute bacterial meningitis (likely pneumococcal) with concurrent sepsis/septic shock and EARLY DIC (platelet drop, INR up, low fibrinogen, high D-dimer). The petechiae could reflect early DIC OR raise concern for meningococcemia until N. meningitidis is conclusively ruled out — DROPLET PRECAUTIONS placed immediately on arrival. Time-critical treatment trio for adult pneumococcal meningitis: DEXAMETHASONE 10 mg IV given 15 minutes BEFORE the first antibiotic dose (reduces mortality and hearing loss in pneumococcal); then EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTICS within 1 hour — ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h PLUS vancomycin (for cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus) PLUS ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (covers Listeria monocytogenes given age >50). Sepsis bundle running concurrently: 30 mL/kg crystalloid (currently at 1 L of planned 2.3 L), repeat lactate, blood cultures already drawn before first antibiotic. Norepinephrine ready to initiate if MAP <65 after fluid resuscitation. Wife at bedside, very distressed; daughter en route. Public health notification will follow once N. meningitidis is excluded vs confirmed. Anticipated MICU admission and likely intubation if GCS drops or shock worsens.
providerOrders: DROPLET PRECAUTIONS — single room, surgical mask within 6 feet of client until N. meningitidis is ruled OUT or 24 hours after effective antibiotic therapy if confirmed

DEXAMETHASONE 10 mg IV push NOW — 15 minutes BEFORE the first antibiotic dose (reduces mortality and hearing loss in adult pneumococcal meningitis); then 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days; DISCONTINUE if CSF/blood cultures grow an organism other than pneumococcus (benefit only proven for pneumococcal)

Empiric antibiotic trio (start within 1 hr of ED arrival, immediately after dexamethasone): ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h + vancomycin 25 mg/kg IV load then 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h (goal trough 15–20) + ampicillin 2 g IV q4h
Add acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h if HSV encephalitis still in differential pending CSF HSV PCR; discontinue when ruled out
Blood cultures x2 (drawn before antibiotics — already done); urine culture; respiratory viral panel; HIV screen

Send CSF for: cell count and differential, glucose, protein, Gram stain (done), bacterial culture and sensitivities, CSF lactate, multiplex bacterial/viral PCR panel (covers S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, H. influenzae, Listeria, HSV-1/2, enterovirus, etc.), India ink and cryptococcal antigen if immunocompromised

Sepsis bundle: 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid (lactated Ringer's or NS) within 3 hr; repeat lactate; norepinephrine drip starting 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP ≥65 if hypotension persists after fluids
Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, end-tidal CO2 if intubated; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP if on vasopressors
Seizure precautions; have IV lorazepam 4 mg available; levetiracetam 1,000 mg IV loading if seizures occur
Q1H neuro checks with GCS and pupil assessment; immediate provider notification for any decline (GCS drop ≥2 points, new focal deficit, pupil change, posturing)
Head of bed 30°; minimize stimulation; group cares to reduce ICP fluctuations
Strict I&O hourly; daily weights; serum sodium and osmolality q6h (monitor for SIADH — common after bacterial meningitis)
Glucose management: hold metformin (acute illness + lactic acidosis risk); start insulin sliding scale per protocol with goal 140–180 mg/dL; recheck fingerstick q1h until stable

Identify close contacts in past 7 days: household members, intimate partners, anyone with direct exposure to oral secretions; chemoprophylaxis with rifampin OR ciprofloxacin OR ceftriaxone IM ONLY if N. meningitidis or H. influenzae type b is confirmed (NOT for pneumococcal — not transmitted that way)
Notify Public Health Department within 24 hr of suspected meningococcal disease per state mandatory reporting (Penal Code 17920.7 in CA reporting requirements)
Ophthalmology for fundoscopic exam if papilledema concern; audiology follow-up post-discharge (sensorineural hearing loss is a major complication of pneumococcal meningitis)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Classic triad: fever 103.2°F + nuchal rigidity + altered mental status (GCS 12) with positive Brudzinski and Kernig signs bilaterally
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the bedside fingerprint of meningitis. The classic triad (fever, neck stiffness, AMS) is present in ~45% of bacterial meningitis cases; nearly all cases have at least 2 of the 4 (add headache). Brudzinski (passive neck flexion → involuntary hip/knee flexion) and Kernig (resistance to knee extension with hip flexed) reflect meningeal irritation. Their PRESENCE is highly specific; their absence does NOT rule out meningitis.

opts.text: CSF findings: opening pressure 32 (high), WBC 2,800 with 90% neutrophils, protein 320, glucose 28 (CSF:serum 0.11), lactate 8.5, Gram-positive diplococci on Gram stain
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic. The bacterial CSF pattern: 'high pressure, lots of neutrophils, high protein, low glucose (low ratio), high lactate, organisms on Gram stain.' Gram-positive diplococci strongly suggest Streptococcus pneumoniae (the most common cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults). This is opposite to the GBS CSF pattern (albumino-cytologic dissociation — high protein, normal cells, normal glucose).

opts.text: Sparse non-blanching petechiae on lower extremities and trunk
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — could reflect early DIC from sepsis OR meningococcemia until proven otherwise. Maintain DROPLET PRECAUTIONS until N. meningitidis is ruled out (PCR / culture results take time). Even if pneumococcal is confirmed, the petechiae here likely reflect the early DIC also present (platelet drop, low fibrinogen, high D-dimer).

opts.text: Sepsis/septic shock physiology: BP 88/52 (MAP 64), HR 132, lactate 4.2, procalcitonin 8.2, WBC 22.4 with 18% bands, fluid-refractory hypotension after 1 L
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — concurrent septic shock. Bacterial meningitis with shock has very high mortality (~30%). Sepsis bundle elements (cultures BEFORE antibiotics, lactate, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, antibiotics within 1 hr, vasopressors if MAP <65 after fluids) must run concurrently with the meningitis-specific therapy. The two pathways are not sequential.

opts.text: CT head BEFORE LP — clear of mass, midline shift, or posterior fossa lesion (LP is safe)
