opts.rat: CORRECT. The timing is a balance: severe preeclampsia/HELLP <34 weeks is generally managed with betamethasone course and delivery soon after if mother stable; HELLP itself often demands earlier delivery. ANY deterioration (uncontrolled HTN, seizure, abruption, hepatic decline, DIC, non-reassuring fetal monitoring, pulmonary edema, hepatic rupture) → expedite delivery regardless of betamethasone status.

opts.text: Aggressive crystalloid bolus 30 mL/kg should be given to support BP and renal perfusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and DANGEROUS in severe preeclampsia/HELLP. Endothelial leak makes these patients exquisitely vulnerable to pulmonary edema — fluid bolus can precipitate flash pulmonary edema. Limit total fluid to ~80–125 mL/hr; resist the sepsis-style reflex to give aggressive volume. The BP problem is fixed with antihypertensives, not fluids.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Eclamptic Seizure r/t severe preeclampsia with HELLP and impending-eclampsia symptoms (severe HA, scotomata, hyperreflexia 4+ with clonus)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The CNS irritability warning signs all point to imminent seizure. Eclamptic seizures cause maternal hypoxia, aspiration, abruption, and fetal bradycardia. Mag sulfate prophylaxis already loading; lorazepam at bedside for breakthrough; quiet environment to minimize cortical stimulation.

opts.text: Risk for Maternal Stroke and Abruption r/t persistent severe-range hypertension (162/104 despite 20 mg labetalol)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Severe-range HTN must be controlled within 30–60 min. Each additional labetalol/hydralazine dose is a step; if the BP doesn't respond to escalating labetalol (40, 80 mg), switch to hydralazine or nicardipine drip. Stroke and placental abruption are the feared maternal complications of persistent severe HTN.

opts.text: Risk for Fetal Compromise r/t chronic uteroplacental insufficiency, IUGR, oligohydramnios, and ongoing maternal disease (minimal variability + variable decelerations)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The fetus shows chronic compromise (IUGR, oligohydramnios) AND acute concerning patterns (minimal variability, variable decelerations). Optimization measures: left lateral position, maternal O2 if non-reassuring patterns persist, IV fluid bolus carefully limited. New LATE decelerations or sustained bradycardia → expedite delivery.

opts.text: Risk for Hepatic Hematoma/Rupture r/t HELLP with severe RUQ pain (subcapsular hematoma is a feared complication)
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Severe persistent RUQ pain is a warning sign for subcapsular hepatic hematoma; sudden severe pain with hemodynamic change suggests hepatic rupture (catastrophic). Bedside US or CT abdomen if RUQ pain worsens or new signs appear. Treatment is emergent surgical repair / packing; mortality is high.

opts.text: Risk for Pulmonary Edema r/t endothelial leak, hypoalbuminemia, and possible iatrogenic fluid overload
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in the acute ranking but a real risk during management. Severe preeclampsia/HELLP has endothelial leak; aggressive fluid resuscitation or excessive maintenance fluids can precipitate flash pulmonary edema. Limit total fluids to 80–125 mL/hr; monitor SpO2 and lung exam; furosemide 20 mg IV if pulmonary edema develops.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4 g IV load over 20 min, then 2 g/hr continuous infusion for seizure prophylaxis; continue x 24 hr postpartum
opts.rat: Standard. Magnesium reduces eclamptic seizures by ~50%. Continue throughout labor/delivery and for 24 hours postpartum (about 50% of eclamptic seizures occur postpartum). Have calcium gluconate at bedside as antidote.

opts.text: Magnesium toxicity surveillance: DTRs q1h, UOP q1h, RR q1h, SpO2 continuous; serum Mg q4h initially; HOLD mag and notify provider for DTRs absent, RR <12, UOP <30 mL/hr, SpO2 <94%, or Mg >8 mg/dL
opts.rat: Core bedside nursing surveillance. Mag is renally cleared and the patient has new AKI (Cr 1.2) — toxicity risk is elevated. DTR loss is the earliest clinical sign. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV at the bedside as the antidote.

opts.text: Repeat LABETALOL 40 mg IV bolus now; if no response in 10 min give 80 mg IV; escalate as needed up to cumulative max 300 mg in 24 hr — TARGET SBP 140–155 / DBP 90–105
opts.rat: First-line severe-range HTN treatment. Labetalol is preferred (clean beta+alpha blockade, predictable effect). Target preserves placental perfusion. If no response to labetalol max, switch to hydralazine 5–10 mg IV q20–40 min or initiate nicardipine drip.

opts.text: BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM now (dose 1 of 2); repeat 12 mg IM in 24 hr for fetal lung maturity at 33+5 weeks
opts.rat: Standard antenatal corticosteroid for delivery between 24–34 weeks (and 34–37 weeks if not previously given). Reduces neonatal RDS, IVH, NEC, and mortality. Single-dose IM is ideal; rescue course possible if more than 14 days has elapsed and the patient is still <34 weeks at risk.

opts.text: Continuous fetal monitoring, left lateral position to optimize uteroplacental flow; maternal O2 if non-reassuring patterns persist
opts.rat: Real-time fetal surveillance is critical. Left lateral position takes the gravid uterus off the IVC, improves venous return and cardiac output, and improves uteroplacental flow. Supplemental O2 traditionally given for non-reassuring tracings (evidence is mixed but it's standard practice as a temporizing measure).

opts.text: Seizure precautions: padded side rails, oxygen and bag-mask at bedside, IV lorazepam 4 mg AT THE BEDSIDE for breakthrough seizure despite mag
opts.rat: Layered safety. Mag prophylaxis reduces but does not eliminate seizure risk; about 10–15% of eclamptic seizures occur despite therapeutic mag. Lorazepam is the breakthrough rescue. Quiet environment, dim lights, and minimal stimulation reduce cortical excitation.

opts.text: Quiet environment, dim lights, minimize stimulation (visual/auditory triggers can precipitate seizure)
opts.rat: Often overlooked nursing intervention. Photophobia and scotomata reflect cortical hyperexcitability — bright lights, loud noises, and excessive stimulation can lower seizure threshold. Cluster nursing tasks, dim the lights, post a sign on the door for staff.

opts.text: Aggressive IV fluid bolus 30 mL/kg of LR for the new AKI and possible volume support
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS in severe preeclampsia/HELLP. Endothelial leak makes these patients exquisitely vulnerable to flash pulmonary edema. Limit total fluid to 80–125 mL/hr. The AKI improves with delivery and BP control, NOT with fluids. The BP problem is fixed with antihypertensives, not volume.

opts.text: Lisinopril 10 mg PO for chronic BP control before discharge
opts.rat: ⚠️ ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy across all trimesters (renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, fetal hypotension/renal failure). Postpartum AND not breastfeeding, an ACE inhibitor can be considered. For pregnancy and breastfeeding, options include: labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa, hydralazine.

opts.text: Nitroprusside drip for rapid BP control
opts.rat: Avoid in pregnancy — cyanide accumulation can cause fetal toxicity. Acceptable pregnancy IV options: labetalol, hydralazine, nicardipine. Nitroprusside is reserved for refractory hypertensive emergencies under careful monitoring and even then time-limited.

opts.text: Coordinate delivery plan with MFM, anesthesia, and NICU; deliver after 24–48 hr betamethasone window if mother stable; deliver SOONER if maternal/fetal status deteriorates
opts.rat: Definitive treatment. The interdisciplinary delivery plan balances betamethasone benefit against maternal/fetal deterioration risk. Modes: vaginal delivery is preferred if cervix favorable and time allows; cesarean for non-reassuring fetal status, failed induction, or other obstetric indication. Regional anesthesia is preferred IF platelets >70–80 K (this patient is borderline).

opts.text: Postpartum: continue magnesium for 24 hr; BP monitoring per protocol; home BP cuff at discharge with 6-week follow-up; ACOG-standard patient education on POSTPARTUM warning signs (severe headache, vision changes, RUQ pain, swelling, decreased UOP, shortness of breath)
opts.rat: High-yield. ~50% of eclamptic seizures and most HELLP-related morbidity occur POSTPARTUM. Many patients are sent home thinking they are 'cured' by delivery and miss warning signs. Mandatory teaching with written instructions, home BP cuff, and a clear plan for returning to the ED. Long-term cardiovascular risk counseling (preeclampsia 2x ASCVD risk lifelong).

opts.text: Calcium gluconate 1 g IV (10 mL of 10%) drawn and AT THE BEDSIDE — the magnesium-toxicity antidote
opts.rat: Standard of care whenever mag is infusing. Calcium gluconate displaces magnesium from neuromuscular and cardiac binding sites, reversing respiratory and cardiac depression. Push slowly (over 3 min) for respiratory or cardiac toxicity. The bedside availability is a quality-of-care metric — do NOT page pharmacy at the moment of crisis.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Place client in LEFT LATERAL position; continuous fetal monitoring; continuous maternal cardiac monitoring; SpO2; Foley catheter for strict hourly UOP; calcium gluconate 1 g IV drawn at bedside
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate safety bundle. Left lateral optimizes uteroplacental flow; Foley enables strict UOP tracking (essential for mag safety with new AKI); calcium gluconate at bedside is a quality-of-care imperative whenever mag is infusing.

opts.text: Start MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4 g IV load over 20 min, then 2 g/hr maintenance infusion (already in progress); begin q1h DTR/UOP/RR/SpO2 surveillance
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Mag prophylaxis is the primary intervention to reduce seizure progression in a patient with impending-eclampsia warning signs. Bedside surveillance for toxicity is concurrent with the infusion — DTR loss is the earliest clinical sign, generally precedes respiratory depression.

opts.text: Repeat LABETALOL 40 mg IV bolus NOW for persistent severe-range HTN (162/104); recheck BP in 10 min and escalate to 80 mg IV if needed; target SBP 140–155 and DBP 90–105
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical (within 30–60 min of severe-range BP). The first 20 mg labetalol was inadequate; escalate per protocol. Goal preserves placental perfusion — not <120/80. If labetalol max ineffective, switch to hydralazine or nicardipine drip.

opts.text: Administer BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM (already given); MFM, anesthesia, and NICU consults for delivery planning; type and screen for crossmatch; STAT repeat CBC, CMP, AST/ALT, LDH, INR/PTT/fibrinogen, peripheral smear q6h to detect HELLP progression and DIC
opts.rat: #4 — Coordination of definitive treatment. Betamethasone gives the fetus a fighting chance for the next 24+ hours; the team coordinates the delivery plan; serial labs detect HELLP progression and DIC. If labs worsen or new severe symptoms develop, expedite delivery regardless of betamethasone status.

opts.text: Quiet environment, dim lights, minimize stimulation, family at bedside for emotional support; explain plan in plain language (esp. that mag is for SEIZURES, not BP, and that DELIVERY is the definitive treatment)
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management and patient-centered care. Cortical hyperexcitability is real — environmental noise/light can lower seizure threshold. Family understanding is essential for support. The plain-language explanation of magnesium and the role of delivery prevents the most common patient/family confusion.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 4: 2 doses of labetalol effective with BP now 144/92, persistent therapeutic mag, headache and scotomata RESOLVED with mag, DTRs now 2+ without clonus, fetal HR baseline 145 with moderate variability after left lateral repositioning
opts.rat: Excellent response. The headache/scotomata resolution AND DTR normalization reflect mag's CNS-stabilizing effect. The fetal pattern improvement after positioning suggests reversible uteroplacental cause. Continue current plan toward betamethasone-completion and delivery.

opts.text: Hour 28: betamethasone course complete; cervix favorable (Bishop 7); induction of labor with oxytocin; vaginal delivery of 1,920 g female infant with Apgars 6/8 transferred to Level 3 NICU; mag continuing for 24-hr postpartum window
opts.rat: Successful delivery trajectory. Vaginal delivery preferred when cervix favorable and time allows. Infant transferred to NICU for prematurity care — Apgar 6/8 is acceptable given gestational age. Mag continues x 24 hr postpartum because most postpartum eclamptic seizures occur in this window.

opts.text: Day 5 postpartum follow-up call: BP 132/82 on home cuff, no symptoms, infant doing well in NICU, mother understands and verbalizes postpartum warning signs (severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, swelling, decreased UOP); plans for 6-week follow-up; primary care referral for long-term cardiovascular risk counseling
opts.rat: Successful disposition with critical postpartum teaching captured. Preeclampsia is a marker of LIFETIME increased cardiovascular risk (about 2x ASCVD over the next 10–20 years) — primary care follow-up for BP, lipid, and lifestyle counseling. Future-pregnancy plan includes ASA 81 mg starting at ~12 weeks for recurrence prevention.

opts.text: Hour 2: generalized tonic-clonic seizure for 90 seconds; left lateral with airway protection; lorazepam 4 mg IV given; mag rebolus 2 g IV; fetal HR drops to 80 for 4 min then recovers; bedside US shows possible early abruption
opts.rat: ECLAMPSIA + possible abruption — emergent. Stabilize airway, complete seizure control, mag rebolus, BP control, EMERGENT CESAREAN DELIVERY for non-reassuring fetal status + possible abruption. Maternal hypoxia and acidosis from prolonged seizure also harm the fetus. Call OB and anesthesia STAT.

opts.text: Hour 6: DTRs ABSENT bilaterally, RR drops from 18 to 10, SpO2 90%, UOP 25 mL/hr last hour; mag level 9.8 mg/dL
opts.rat: MAGNESIUM TOXICITY. STOP mag infusion immediately, give CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV slow push over 3 min, supportive ventilation/intubation if respiratory failure progresses, recheck mag level in 30 min and clinical exam. Volume status reassessment for AKI worsening. Lessons learned: closer mag level monitoring needed given baseline AKI.

opts.text: Hour 12: HELLP labs WORSENING: platelets dropped from 78 → 38 K, LDH 1,500, AST 680, ALT 540, fibrinogen 90, D-dimer >20 — full DIC; sudden vaginal bleeding with tense, painful, tetanically contracting uterus
opts.rat: Two emergencies converging: HELLP with DIC + placental abruption. EMERGENT CESAREAN DELIVERY; massive transfusion protocol (PRBC, FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets); type-specific blood; correct coagulopathy aggressively; preserve airway/access; family update. ICU bed post-delivery.

opts.text: Hour 18: new severe right shoulder pain (referred from hepatic capsule) + sudden hemodynamic decompensation BP 70/40 HR 140, abdominal distension; bedside FAST positive for free fluid; emergent CT or laparotomy reveals subcapsular hepatic hematoma rupture
opts.rat: Catastrophic — hepatic rupture is one of the deadliest complications of HELLP. Emergent surgery (hepatic packing, possible lobectomy), massive transfusion, surgical and ICU intensivist involvement. Mortality is high. This is why severe persistent RUQ pain is taken seriously in HELLP and why bedside imaging is liberal.

opts.text: Day 4 postpartum: new severe headache and BP 168/108 at home; readmitted; mag restarted, labetalol resumed, full preeclampsia/HELLP workup; platelets 145 → 95 again
opts.rat: POSTPARTUM HELLP recurrence — the most-missed teaching point. About 50% of eclamptic seizures occur postpartum and HELLP can present new or worsen for up to 7 days (occasionally longer) after delivery. This is why home BP cuff monitoring x 6 weeks and clear written postpartum warning-sign teaching are mandatory before discharge.

opts.text: Day 1 postpartum: BP gradually trending down to 145/92 on labetalol PO; mag infusion completed and discontinued at 24-hr mark; bowel function returning; mother breastfeeding pumped milk for NICU baby; labs improving (plt 95 → 132, AST 245 → 110)
opts.rat: Expected post-delivery trajectory — many HELLP/preeclampsia clients show rapid normalization after delivery. Continue antihypertensive (labetalol or nifedipine — both breastfeeding compatible), monitor for postpartum-onset HELLP/eclampsia in first 7 days, encourage lactation support given separation from NICU infant. Home BP cuff at discharge.

opts.text: Persistent residual urine protein on dip 1+ at the 6-week postpartum visit
opts.rat: Expected — proteinuria can take 6–12 weeks to fully resolve after delivery. As long as BP normalizes and the patient is asymptomatic, persistent low-level proteinuria is not concerning. If proteinuria persists beyond 12 weeks or is heavy (>500 mg/24 hr), nephrology workup for underlying glomerular disease (some patients have unmasked chronic kidney disease).

title: Acute Tumor Lysis Syndrome After Induction Chemotherapy
subtitle: The Hyperkalemia Ladder, Rasburicase, and Why IV Calcium Is a Trap

patient.name: K. Okonkwo
patient.admitDate: Hospital Day 3 (Day 1 of induction chemotherapy was yesterday)
patient.room: Heme-Onc Unit Bed 14 → MICU pending transfer for CRRT

vitals.time: Hospital Day 2 — 12 hr after starting induction

labs.v: 6.8 mEq/L (rising trend: 4.2 → 5.4 → 6.8 over 28 hr)
labs.r: 3.5–5.0 mEq/L; ≥6.0 with EKG changes is a medical emergency

labs.v: 9.4 mg/dL (rising trend: 3.8 → 6.6 → 9.4 over 28 hr)
14.2 mg/dL (rising trend: 7.9 → 11.4 → 14.2 over 28 hr — partly mitigated by rasburicase dose ~3 hr ago)
labs.r: Female <6 mg/dL; >8 mg/dL is a TLS criterion

labs.v: 0.78 mmol/L (≈ corrected total 6.2 mg/dL; precipitated by the high phosphate)
labs.r: Ionized 1.12–1.30 mmol/L; total 8.5–10.5 mg/dL

labs.v: 2.8 mg/dL (rising trend: 0.9 → 1.6 → 2.8 over 28 hr — AKI Stage 2 by KDIGO)
Falling from 80 mL/hr → 60 → 40 → 22 mL/hr (oliguric trend despite aggressive IV hydration)
labs.r: >0.5 mL/kg/hr; oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/hr

labs.v: 4,200 U/L (rising from pre-chemo 2,100 → 3,400 → 4,200)
labs.r: <280 U/L; massively elevated reflects high cell turnover

labs.v: Pre-chemo 250 K/µL; now 45 K/µL (massive cell lysis from chemotherapy effective on bulk disease)
labs.r: WNL 4.5–11.0 K/µL; this trajectory reflects effective induction with massive nucleic acid/cation release

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 108 with PEAKED T waves V3–V6, PR interval 220 ms, intermittent unifocal PVCs; QRS still narrow at 100 ms
labs.r: Sinus rhythm without hyperkalemic changes; PR <200; QRS <120

labs.v: Glucose 118 / Na 138 / Mg 1.8 / Cl 110 / HCO3 18 (mild non-anion-gap acidosis from saline + AKI)
pH 7.31 / PaCO2 32 / PaO2 86 / HCO3 18 (metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation)
NORMAL — confirmed before rasburicase given
labs.r: Normal G6PD activity allows safe rasburicase use

labs.v: Chvostek POSITIVE on the right (facial twitch with tap on facial nerve); Trousseau equivocal; mild perioral numbness reported by client; no overt tetany or seizures yet
labs.r: Negative

labs.v: Preserved LV function (EF ~55%); no pericardial effusion; no chamber enlargement
labs.r: Normal

labs.v: Lab TLS criteria met (K, phos, uric acid, Ca all ≥25% change AND beyond thresholds); CLINICAL TLS (AKI stage ≥2 + cardiac arrhythmia with PVCs)
labs.r: Lab TLS = ≥2 lab abnormalities; Clinical TLS = lab TLS + organ dysfunction (AKI, arrhythmia, seizure)

nursesNote: 1430 — 58 y/o female admitted 3 days ago for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with hyperleukocytosis (WBC 250 K on admission), bulky lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and elevated baseline LDH 2,100 — high-risk profile for tumor lysis syndrome (TLS). Started '7+3' induction chemotherapy (cytarabine + idarubicin) yesterday. TLS PROPHYLAXIS was started 24 hr BEFORE chemo per protocol: aggressive IV hydration with isotonic NS at 250 mL/hr (~3 L/m²/day), rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV daily x 3 days planned (G6PD activity normal — verified before first dose), and strict q4h electrolyte and renal function monitoring. Through Day 1 of chemo she remained clinically well with mild rise in K (4.2 → 5.4), phos (3.8 → 6.6), and uric acid (7.9 → 11.4). Today at the 0700 lab draw new findings are concerning: K 6.8, phos 9.4, uric acid 14.2, ionized Ca 0.78 (corrected total Ca 6.2), Cr 2.8 (up from 0.9), oliguria with UOP trending 80 → 22 mL/hr last 4 hr despite continued NS at 250 mL/hr. LDH up to 4,200 (from 2,100 baseline — massive cell turnover). She reports new perioral numbness and mild muscle cramping in the hands; Chvostek's POSITIVE on right facial nerve tap; mild Trousseau response equivocal. ECG at 1415 shows sinus tachycardia at 108 with PEAKED T waves V3–V6, PR 220 ms, intermittent unifocal PVCs (QRS still narrow at 100 ms — but the prolonging PR and peaked T's are EARLY warning of progression). The patient now meets CAIRO-BISHOP CLINICAL TLS criteria (lab TLS PLUS AKI stage 2 PLUS cardiac arrhythmia). She has rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg dose given at 1130 (~3 hr ago) — uric acid expected to drop further over the next few hours as rasburicase converts existing uric acid to allantoin. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV is drawn and at the bedside — to be given IMMEDIATELY for cardiac membrane stabilization if ECG progresses (sine wave, wide QRS, brady-arrest pattern). KEY decision points being managed: (1) hyperkalemia ladder is being escalated — insulin 10 units regular IV + D50 25 g IV bolus given at 1420, albuterol 10 mg nebulizer running; (2) calcium gluconate is reserved for actual cardiac membrane instability rather than routine hypocalcemia treatment because of the calcium-phosphate precipitation risk with phos 9.4 — even though the client is mildly symptomatic with Chvostek+ and perioral numbness; (3) sevelamer 1,600 mg PO with each meal as a phosphate binder is started; (4) nephrology and ICU at bedside for likely transition to CRRT given oliguric AKI with severe electrolyte derangements unresponsive to medical management. Husband at bedside, very distressed. Oncology and nephrology have agreed: transfer to MICU for CRRT initiation within the hour; chemotherapy paused until TLS is controlled.
providerOrders: HOLD next dose of chemotherapy until TLS is controlled and renal function stabilizes
Continuous cardiac monitoring with TELEMETRY review every 15 min until K and ionized Ca normalize; document ECG strips with every K result

CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV (10 mL of 10%) over 3–5 min IMMEDIATELY at bedside for: (1) ECG progression to wide QRS, sine wave, or any malignant arrhythmia, OR (2) tetany / seizure from symptomatic hypocalcemia — REPEATABLE q5min until ECG stabilizes; calcium gluconate IS the membrane stabilizer (does NOT lower K) and IS the antidote for severe symptomatic hypocalcemia in TLS, but routine 'asymptomatic' calcium replacement is AVOIDED because of calcium-phosphate precipitation with phos 9.4
INSULIN regular 10 units IV PLUS dextrose 50% 25 g (50 mL) IV bolus over 5 min (already given at 1420); recheck glucose at 30 min and q1h x 6 hr (significant hypoglycemia risk especially with AKI)
ALBUTEROL nebulizer 10 mg in 4 mL NS (high dose — 5–10x asthma dose) over 10 min; may repeat q30min x 3 doses
SODIUM BICARBONATE 50 mEq IV bolus over 5 min ONLY if pH <7.20 — limited benefit and not first-line; the historic role has shrunk
PATIROMER 8.4 g PO daily OR sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma) 10 g PO TID as ongoing K-binder; AVOID Kayexalate (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) — risk of colonic necrosis
Continue RASBURICASE 0.2 mg/kg IV daily x up to 5 days; uric acid will fall to <1 within hours (rasburicase converts uric acid → allantoin which is freely soluble and renally cleared)
Verify G6PD activity NORMAL before EACH rasburicase dose (rasburicase causes severe hemolysis in G6PD deficiency); rasburicase is also CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy and breastfeeding

DISCONTINUE allopurinol if previously running (rasburicase makes allopurinol redundant; both can interfere with uric acid measurement — collect uric acid samples on ICE and process within 4 hr to avoid falsely low results)
SEVELAMER 1,600 mg PO with each meal as phosphate binder; AVOID calcium-based binders (Tums, calcium acetate) in TLS because of calcium-phosphate precipitation risk

Aggressive IV HYDRATION isotonic saline at 250 mL/hr targeting UOP >100 mL/hr or 2–3 mL/kg/hr; once UOP drops despite adequate hydration → escalation to CRRT (NOT to high-dose furosemide which can fail and worsens calcium-phosphate precipitation by alkalinizing urine)
Strict I&O hourly; daily weights; recheck K, phos, Ca, Mg, uric acid, BUN, Cr q2–4h initially during active TLS

DO NOT routinely alkalinize urine with sodium bicarbonate — historical practice; CURRENT GUIDELINES discourage routine alkalinization because while it improves uric acid solubility, it WORSENS calcium-phosphate precipitation in renal tubules. Saline-only hydration is the modern standard.

Nephrology consultation NOW; ICU transfer for CRRT given oliguric AKI + severe hyperK + severe hyperphos refractory to medical management — CRRT removes K, phos, and uric acid simultaneously and is preferred over intermittent HD in hemodynamically tenuous patients
Neutropenic precautions remain in effect; review all infection sources; monitor for tumor lysis-associated DIC (high LDH + cytokine release may trigger consumptive coagulopathy)
Glucose surveillance: fingerstick q1h x 6 hr after insulin/D50 bolus (delayed hypoglycemia is common with AKI because insulin clearance is reduced)

Patient and family education in plain language about TLS — that this is an expected reaction to EFFECTIVE chemotherapy (the cancer is actually responding) and the management is to support kidneys and electrolytes through the rapid cell breakdown phase

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: K 6.8 mEq/L with ECG showing PEAKED T waves V3–V6, PR interval 220 ms, and intermittent PVCs
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — life-threatening hyperkalemia with cardiac membrane instability. The peaked T waves and PR prolongation are EARLY hyperkalemic ECG findings; without prompt intervention, the next stages are wide QRS, sine-wave morphology, and asystole or VF. Calcium gluconate stabilizes the membrane; insulin/D50 + albuterol shift K intracellularly; binders or dialysis remove K from the body.

opts.text: Phosphorus 9.4 mg/dL (rapid rise from 3.8 over 28 hr)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severe hyperphosphatemia from massive cell lysis (DNA and intracellular phosphate release). The phos drives calcium-phosphate precipitation in tissues including renal tubules (worsening AKI), heart, and joints. This is also WHY 'routine' IV calcium replacement is risky — adding calcium with phos 9.4 promotes precipitation.

opts.text: Uric Acid 14.2 mg/dL with Cr rising 0.9 → 2.8 and UOP falling to 22 mL/hr (acute urate nephropathy)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — uric acid crystallization in the renal tubules is one of the principal mechanisms of TLS-AKI. Rasburicase converts existing uric acid to allantoin (highly soluble) — single dose works within 4 hours. Allopurinol BLOCKS new uric acid production but does NOT break down existing uric acid; rasburicase is preferred when uric acid is already high.

opts.text: Ionized Ca 0.78 mmol/L (corrected total 6.2 mg/dL) with positive Chvostek and perioral numbness
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — symptomatic hypocalcemia (calcium-phosphate precipitation consumes free Ca). Sym mild here (Chvostek+, perioral numbness, mild cramping). The DILEMMA: severe symptoms (tetany, seizure, arrhythmia) require IV calcium for safety; mild symptoms generally do NOT — adding IV calcium with phos 9.4 worsens tissue precipitation. The fix is lowering the PHOSPHATE (sevelamer + dialysis), which then allows ionized calcium to rise.

opts.text: Cr 2.8 (from baseline 0.9) with UOP trending 80 → 22 mL/hr despite NS at 250 mL/hr (Stage 2 AKI worsening to oliguric)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — established AKI is failing to respond to aggressive hydration. The mechanisms include uric acid nephropathy, calcium-phosphate precipitation, and ATN from cytokine release. When TLS-AKI reaches oliguria despite hydration → escalate to CRRT — the kidneys cannot keep up with the electrolyte and waste removal demands.

opts.text: Cairo-Bishop CLINICAL TLS criteria met (lab TLS PLUS AKI stage 2 PLUS cardiac arrhythmia)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — formal diagnosis with prognostic implications. Lab TLS = ≥2 of [K >6 OR ↑25%, phos >4.5 OR ↑25%, uric acid >8 OR ↑25%, Ca <7 OR ↓25%]; CLINICAL TLS = lab TLS + organ dysfunction (AKI, arrhythmia, seizure). Clinical TLS triggers ICU transfer, urgent CRRT consideration, and chemotherapy hold.

opts.text: LDH 4,200 (massive elevation reflecting high cell turnover from effective chemotherapy)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — marker of CELL TURNOVER. The high LDH and the falling WBC (250 K → 45 K) both confirm the chemotherapy IS WORKING — and the TLS is the consequence of effective therapy. Risk stratification BEFORE chemo uses LDH and disease bulk to predict TLS — high LDH = high TLS risk = mandatory prophylaxis.

opts.text: G6PD activity NORMAL — verified BEFORE rasburicase dose
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — safety screen. RASBURICASE IS CONTRAINDICATED IN G6PD DEFICIENCY because it generates hydrogen peroxide during uric acid conversion, which causes severe hemolysis in G6PD-deficient red cells. G6PD screening is mandatory before rasburicase — especially in patients of Mediterranean, African, or Southeast Asian ancestry.

opts.text: Prior 24 hr of aggressive IV hydration with isotonic NS at 250 mL/hr and rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg dose 3 hr ago
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the prophylaxis was correctly given and is working partially. The expected trajectory: uric acid should drop sharply over the next 4–6 hr post-rasburicase; the K and phos require additional intervention. Knowing what's ALREADY been given prevents redundant dosing and informs the next step.

opts.text: WBC dropping from 250 K → 45 K within 28 hours
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms effective chemotherapy AND explains TLS magnitude. About 200,000 cells/µL × 5 L blood × ~3 L tissue distribution = massive cation release (K, phos, nucleic acid → uric acid). The faster the cell kill, the worse the TLS. Disease bulk + chemo sensitivity = TLS risk.

opts.text: Glucose 118 mg/dL
opts.rat: Within normal range. However, after the just-given insulin 10 units + D50 25 g, watch for delayed hypoglycemia over the next few hours — particularly because AKI prolongs insulin half-life. Recheck glucose q1h x 6 hr.

opts.text: Mild non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (pH 7.31, HCO3 18) likely from saline loading and AKI
opts.rat: Mild and expected; not a priority for independent treatment. Routine sodium bicarbonate is NOT recommended in TLS (worsens calcium-phosphate precipitation). Only consider bicarbonate if pH <7.20 with hemodynamic concern. Acidosis will improve with CRRT once initiated.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Rapid lysis of tumor cells releases intracellular contents (K, phosphate, nucleic acids) into the circulation; phosphate precipitates with ionized calcium (→ hypocalcemia); nucleic acids are metabolized to UR ic acid which crystallizes in renal tubules (→ AKI); the resulting AKI further impairs K, phos, and uric acid clearance — creating a positive feedback loop
opts.rat: CORRECT — the central mechanism. Each finding (hyperK, hyperphos, hyperuricemia, hypocalcemia, AKI) is a different consequence of the SAME mass cell breakdown. Treatment is parallel: shift/remove K, bind/remove phos, break down/remove uric acid, support renal output — and if any pathway fails, escalate to CRRT which handles them all simultaneously.

opts.text: Hyperkalemia management follows a stepped LADDER: (1) CALCIUM GLUCONATE stabilizes the cardiac membrane (does NOT lower K); (2) INSULIN + D50 shifts K intracellularly (~30–60 min); (3) ALBUTEROL nebulizer shifts K intracellularly (~30 min); (4) BICARBONATE (if acidotic — limited role); (5) BINDERS (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) remove K from the body (hours); (6) DIALYSIS for refractory or severe cases (minutes-to-hours of true removal)
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield ladder. STABILIZE → SHIFT → REMOVE. Calcium gluconate is FIRST because it's the only step that protects the heart in real time; everything else is to actually lower K. Onset times: calcium (minutes), insulin/D50 (~30 min), albuterol (~30 min), binders (hours), dialysis (minutes for K removal). Use them in parallel, not strictly sequential.

opts.text: RASBURICASE (recombinant urate oxidase) converts uric acid to allantoin, which is freely soluble and renally cleared; onset within hours; CONTRAINDICATED in G6PD deficiency (causes severe hemolysis). ALLOPURINOL (xanthine oxidase inhibitor) BLOCKS new uric acid production but does NOT lower existing uric acid; slow onset (days). Rasburicase is preferred when uric acid is already high or rising rapidly.
opts.rat: CORRECT and CRITICAL distinction. The mechanism difference drives the choice. For low-risk patients: allopurinol prophylaxis is sufficient. For high-risk patients (high WBC, bulky disease, high LDH, high baseline uric acid, AKI): rasburicase is preferred. NEVER use both routinely (allopurinol blocks the xanthine pathway and reduces rasburicase substrate; clinical benefit not improved).

opts.text: ROUTINE IV CALCIUM REPLACEMENT FOR ASYMPTOMATIC HYPOCALCEMIA IS AVOIDED in TLS because the high phosphate (9.4 here) drives calcium-phosphate precipitation in renal tubules, soft tissue, and the heart. Reserve IV calcium for: (1) ECG progression / arrhythmia from severe hyperkalemia (membrane stabilization), OR (2) symptomatic hypocalcemia with tetany or seizure. The definitive fix for the calcium is to LOWER the PHOSPHATE (sevelamer + dialysis).
opts.rat: CORRECT — counterintuitive and high-yield. Students reflexively want to 'replace what's low' (Ca 6.2 → give calcium) — but in TLS this worsens tissue precipitation. The right move is to address the PHOSPHATE; the ionized calcium will rise on its own as phosphate falls. Exception: life-threatening symptoms require IV calcium regardless.

opts.text: ROUTINE URINARY ALKALINIZATION with sodium bicarbonate is NO LONGER recommended in TLS — historic practice was to alkalinize the urine to improve uric acid solubility, but it WORSENS calcium-phosphate precipitation in renal tubules (Ca-P solubility falls with rising pH). Modern guidelines use SALINE-ONLY aggressive hydration
opts.rat: CORRECT — the practice change. Bicarbonate is reserved for pH <7.20 with hemodynamic concern. Routine alkalinization is OBSOLETE despite being in older textbooks. NCLEX questions written from older sources may still test the older answer — but the modern correct answer is saline-only.

opts.text: When oliguric AKI develops in TLS despite aggressive hydration AND medical management of electrolytes — CRRT (or intermittent hemodialysis in stable patients) is the definitive intervention; CRRT removes K, phos, uric acid, and excess fluid simultaneously and is preferred over HD in hemodynamically tenuous patients
opts.rat: CORRECT. The point of escalation is when medical management cannot keep up with the rate of cation/anion release. CRRT (continuous renal replacement therapy) runs 24/h with gentler fluid shifts than intermittent HD — better tolerated in unstable critically-ill patients. Intermittent HD is faster per session but causes larger osmotic shifts and may not be well tolerated.

opts.text: TLS only occurs in solid tumors, not hematologic malignancies
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — REVERSED. TLS is most common in HEMATOLOGIC malignancies (Burkitt lymphoma, ALL, AML with hyperleukocytosis, large diffuse B-cell lymphoma) because of their rapid proliferation and chemosensitivity. Solid tumors can have TLS (especially with newer agents like venetoclax in CLL) but the risk is lower. Pre-chemo risk stratification is based on tumor type, bulk, LDH, uric acid, and baseline renal function.

opts.text: Kayexalate (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) is the safest first-line K binder
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — Kayexalate has fallen out of favor due to colonic necrosis risk, especially when given with sorbitol or in postoperative/ileus patients. Modern alternatives (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate/Lokelma) have better safety profiles. Many institutions have removed Kayexalate from formularies.

opts.text: Loop diuretics (furosemide) should be given aggressively to force urine output and clear uric acid
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT for TLS. Furosemide can fail in oliguric AKI, contributes to volume depletion, and alkalinizes urine — WORSENING calcium-phosphate precipitation. Aggressive saline hydration is preferred; if UOP cannot be sustained, escalate to CRRT rather than chasing UOP with diuretics.

opts.text: TLS is the result of INEFFECTIVE chemotherapy — the tumor is not responding
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — reversed. TLS is the result of HIGHLY EFFECTIVE chemotherapy producing rapid cell kill. Patient education should frame TLS as 'the cancer IS responding — we have to support your kidneys through the rapid breakdown phase.' This shifts family perception from 'something is going wrong' to 'this is expected and we are managing it.'

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Lethal Dysrhythmia r/t hyperkalemia (K 6.8) with cardiac membrane instability (peaked T waves, PR prolongation, PVCs)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Cardiac. The ECG changes are progressing; the next stage is wide QRS → sine wave → asystole or VF. Calcium gluconate at the bedside drawn and ready; full hyperkalemia ladder running in parallel (insulin/D50, albuterol, binders); CRRT being arranged. Continuous monitoring with q15min strip review.

opts.text: Risk for Worsening AKI / Need for CRRT r/t oliguric tumor lysis nephropathy (Cr 2.8, UOP 22 mL/hr)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. The kidneys are failing to keep up with the cation/anion load. CRRT escalation now (within the hour). CRRT removes K, phos, uric acid, and excess fluid simultaneously — the only intervention that can fully break the TLS feedback loop.

opts.text: Risk for Tetany / Seizure r/t symptomatic hypocalcemia (Chvostek+, perioral numbness, ionized Ca 0.78)
opts.rat: #3 — Significant. Currently MILD symptoms; routine IV calcium is AVOIDED because of the calcium-phosphate precipitation risk at phos 9.4. The cure is to lower the PHOSPHATE (sevelamer + CRRT). If symptoms PROGRESS to overt tetany, seizure, or hemodynamic compromise → cautious IV calcium gluconate is given for safety.

opts.text: Risk for Continued Cell Lysis / Worsening Electrolytes r/t ongoing chemotherapy effect with high tumor burden
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. The next dose of chemotherapy is HELD until TLS is controlled. The current TLS will progress for ~24–72 hours as the existing cell lysis continues; supportive care (CRRT, hydration, binders, rasburicase) is the bridge. Resume chemotherapy once stable on a tighter prophylaxis plan.

opts.text: Anxiety / Family Distress r/t critical illness, ICU transfer, and complex counterintuitive treatment plan (calcium NOT being replaced, chemotherapy held)
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in acute ranking but high-impact nursing leadership. The plan IS counterintuitive — 'why is my Ca 6 and you won't give calcium?' — and 'TLS = chemo is working' is initially confusing. A 5-minute conversation with the family using simple language and a small drawing builds trust and reduces distress.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Have CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV (10 mL of 10%) drawn at the bedside; give immediately at first sign of ECG progression (wide QRS, sine wave) or for overt tetany/seizure
opts.rat: Standard. Calcium gluconate is the membrane stabilizer in life-threatening hyperkalemia and the rescue for severe symptomatic hypocalcemia. Repeatable q5min for ECG progression. Time-to-administration is what saves life — drawn AT THE BEDSIDE, not in the pharmacy.

opts.text: INSULIN 10 units regular IV + D50 25 g IV bolus over 5 min (already given) — shifts K intracellularly within ~30 min; recheck glucose q1h x 6 hr due to AKI prolonging insulin half-life
opts.rat: Core hyperkalemia treatment. The most reliable K-shifting maneuver. Hypoglycemia is the main side effect — significant risk over the following hours, particularly with AKI. Hourly fingerstick checks; standing D5 infusion may be needed if glucose drops.

opts.text: ALBUTEROL 10 mg nebulizer (5–10x asthma dose) over 10 min; may repeat q30min x 3 doses
opts.rat: Adjunctive hyperkalemia treatment. High-dose albuterol shifts K intracellularly via beta-2 receptor activation; effect at ~30 min and lasts a few hours. Side effects: tremor, mild tachycardia. Adds to insulin/D50 effect; the combination is more effective than either alone.

opts.text: Continue RASBURICASE 0.2 mg/kg IV daily x up to 5 days; G6PD verified normal before each dose
opts.rat: Definitive uric acid management. Rasburicase converts existing uric acid to allantoin (highly soluble, renally cleared). Effect within hours. G6PD screening before each dose is essential — hemolysis from rasburicase in G6PD deficiency is severe. Single-dose protocols (0.15 mg/kg) are also acceptable in many centers.

opts.text: SEVELAMER 1,600 mg PO with each meal as phosphate binder; AVOID calcium-based phosphate binders (Tums, calcium acetate) due to calcium-phosphate precipitation risk
opts.rat: Lowering the phosphate raises the ionized calcium AND reduces calcium-phosphate precipitation in tissues. Sevelamer binds phosphate in the gut. Calcium-based binders are appropriate in chronic CKD but NOT in acute TLS with hyperphosphatemia.

opts.text: Aggressive IV HYDRATION with isotonic NS at 250 mL/hr targeting UOP 100 mL/hr (2–3 mL/kg/hr)
opts.rat: Cornerstone of TLS management — both prophylaxis and treatment. Dilutes the cation/anion load; supports glomerular filtration; flushes uric acid through the tubules. Watch volume status — TLS-AKI can produce fluid retention; transition to CRRT when UOP fails to respond.

opts.text: Nephrology consultation NOW and ICU transfer for CRRT given oliguric AKI + severe hyperK/hyperphos refractory to medical management
opts.rat: Escalation. CRRT removes K, phos, uric acid, and fluid simultaneously and is well-tolerated in tenuous patients. Triggers for CRRT in TLS: severe oliguric AKI not responding to hydration; refractory hyperkalemia; severe hyperphosphatemia; fluid overload; symptomatic hypocalcemia from extreme hyperphosphatemia.

opts.text: Routine IV calcium gluconate 2 g q4h to 'replace' the low ionized calcium (0.78) regardless of symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ COUNTERINTUITIVE BUT TRUE — Asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic hypocalcemia in TLS is NOT routinely 'replaced' with IV calcium because of the calcium-phosphate precipitation risk at phos 9.4. The fix is to lower the phosphate (sevelamer + CRRT); ionized calcium rises as phos falls. Reserve IV calcium for severe symptoms (overt tetany, seizure, arrhythmia).

opts.text: Sodium bicarbonate 100 mEq IV bolus to alkalinize urine and improve uric acid solubility
opts.rat: ⚠️ OBSOLETE PRACTICE. Alkalinization improves uric acid solubility but WORSENS calcium-phosphate precipitation (Ca-P solubility falls with rising pH). Modern guidelines recommend saline-only hydration. Reserve sodium bicarbonate for severe metabolic acidosis pH <7.20 with hemodynamic concern.

opts.text: Furosemide 80 mg IV bolus to increase urine output
opts.rat: Not first-line in oliguric AKI TLS. Loop diuretics may fail in oliguric AKI; volume depletion worsens uric acid precipitation; urine alkalinization with diuretics promotes calcium-phosphate precipitation. Aggressive saline → CRRT pathway is preferred.

opts.text: Kayexalate (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) 30 g PO for K removal
opts.rat: Largely abandoned due to colonic necrosis risk (especially with sorbitol or in postoperative/ileus patients). Modern alternatives: patiromer 8.4 g daily, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma) 10 g TID. CRRT is the definitive removal in severe cases.

opts.text: Glucose surveillance: fingerstick q1h x 6 hr after insulin/D50; standing D5 infusion if glucose <100 to avoid delayed hypoglycemia from prolonged insulin clearance in AKI
opts.rat: Critical safety follow-through. Insulin causes hypoglycemia; AKI further prolongs insulin half-life. Delayed hypoglycemia at 2–6 hours after the bolus is a known complication. Standing q1h fingersticks and ready D5 prevent harm.

opts.text: Family/patient teaching framed as 'TLS = effective chemotherapy with expected metabolic effects' — the cancer IS responding; the management is to support the kidneys and electrolytes through the rapid cell breakdown phase
opts.rat: Reduces distress. The instinctive interpretation (labs are bad → something is going wrong) is reversed in TLS (labs are bad → the chemo is working). A 5-minute conversation reframes the experience and supports cooperation with the complex care plan.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Place client on continuous cardiac monitoring with q15min strip review; have CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV drawn AT THE BEDSIDE; verify two large-bore IVs patent; alert ICU for transfer
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety infrastructure. The bedside calcium gluconate is non-negotiable for hyperkalemia with ECG changes. IV access for the cascade of medications. ICU/CRRT pathway started early so the bed and team are ready when needed.

opts.text: Administer INSULIN 10 units regular IV + D50 25 g IV bolus over 5 min; start ALBUTEROL 10 mg nebulizer; verify SEVELAMER first dose given with meal; recheck K at 30 and 60 min
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. K-shifting bundle. Insulin/D50 + albuterol working in parallel shifts more K than either alone. Sevelamer addresses phos in the gut. Recheck K shortly to confirm response and inform next escalation.

opts.text: Continue aggressive isotonic NS at 250 mL/hr; strict hourly I&O; have rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg dose 1 confirmed in (or due next morning); verify G6PD result documented
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Hydration and uric acid management. NS supports glomerular flow and dilutes the cation/anion load. Rasburicase converts existing uric acid within hours. G6PD verification is a safety check.

opts.text: Call nephrology and prepare for CRRT initiation; arrange ICU transfer; coordinate with oncology to HOLD next chemotherapy dose until TLS resolves
opts.rat: #4 — Escalation. CRRT is the definitive intervention when medical management cannot keep up. The HOLD on chemotherapy stops adding to the cell lysis until kidneys can handle it. Multidisciplinary coordination is the nurse's leadership domain.

opts.text: Family meeting with oncology + nephrology + ICU team; explain TLS in plain language as 'expected response to effective chemotherapy'; review ICU transfer and the rationale for not replacing calcium routinely; arrange chaplaincy / social work as desired
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Once the acute pathway is in motion, the counterintuitive elements (chemo held even though it's working; calcium low but not replaced) must be explained to family. Reduces psychological burden and supports cooperation with the complex plan.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 6 (after CRRT initiated 4 hr ago): K 4.8, phos 5.6, uric acid <1.0, ionized Ca 1.04 (rising), Cr 2.4 (down from 2.8); ECG: peaked T waves resolved, no PVCs
opts.rat: Excellent response to combined medical + CRRT. Rasburicase has cleared uric acid (<1 is typical post-rasburicase); CRRT has cleared K and phos; with phos lower, ionized calcium has risen on its own (no IV calcium needed). The cardiac membrane is stable. Continue CRRT, recheck labs q4h, prepare to resume chemotherapy soon.

opts.text: Day 4: CRRT discontinued, urine output 80 mL/hr without diuretic, K 4.1, phos 3.8, Cr 1.4 (improving from peak 3.6); chemotherapy resumed with tighter TLS prophylaxis and intensive monitoring
opts.rat: Successful trajectory through TLS. Renal function recovering; electrolytes normalized; chemo can resume safely with rasburicase and intensive hydration on a tighter monitoring schedule. Key teaching point: most TLS-AKI recovers fully if managed promptly. Long-term outcome depends on the underlying malignancy's response to chemo, not the TLS itself.

opts.text: Day 10: WBC nadir 0.3 K (expected post-induction); ANC 0; remains in protective isolation; afebrile; no infections; awaiting count recovery (typically 14–21 days post-induction); patient and family report 'understanding what TLS was and why the plan was what it was — it makes sense now'
opts.rat: Expected trajectory. Post-induction neutropenia is universal and expected. Patient and family teaching post-TLS is high-impact — they will go through multiple cycles of chemo and may experience TLS again. Their understanding empowers them to flag warning signs and cooperate with prophylaxis.

opts.text: Hour 1.5 (before CRRT started): K rises from 6.8 to 7.4; ECG progresses to WIDE QRS (140 ms); BP drops to 84/52; patient becomes lethargic
opts.rat: PRE-ARREST hyperkalemia. Immediate calcium gluconate 1 g IV slow push (repeat in 5 min if ECG persists), repeat insulin/D50, urgent CRRT initiation (skip intermittent HD if hemodynamically unstable), consider sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV given the acidosis. Be prepared for code; intubation if needed; transcutaneous pacing pads on.

opts.text: Hour 3: generalized tonic-clonic seizure from severe symptomatic hypocalcemia (ionized Ca 0.62, Chvostek 4+, sustained carpopedal spasm)
opts.rat: Severe symptomatic hypocalcemia overrides the 'avoid IV calcium' rule. Calcium gluconate 1–2 g IV slow push given for seizure control; continue CRRT to lower phosphate (which will then allow durable calcium correction). Magnesium 2 g IV is reasonable adjunct (mag deficiency worsens hypocalcemia). Lorazepam 4 mg IV for ongoing seizure activity.

opts.text: Hour 4: glucose drops to 48 mg/dL with diaphoresis and confusion (delayed hypoglycemia after insulin/D50 bolus, AKI prolonging clearance)
opts.rat: Predictable complication. D50 25 g IV repeat push, start continuous D10 infusion at 50–100 mL/hr, fingerstick q15min until stable, then q1h x 6 hr. Adjust the standing D5 infusion to D10 if AKI is severe. THIS IS WHY q1h glucose checks after insulin/D50 in AKI are mandatory.

opts.text: Day 2: new petechiae, gum oozing, bleeding from line site; labs show INR 2.4, fibrinogen 80, D-dimer >20, platelets 18 K — concurrent DIC complicating the TLS
opts.rat: DIC superimposed on TLS — known complication especially in acute leukemias. Replace consumed factors (cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100, FFP for INR >1.5–2.0 with bleeding, platelets for <20 K or <50 K with bleeding). Source control = control the leukemia. Hematology guidance for transfusion thresholds. Monitor for ICH (highest-mortality DIC complication).

opts.text: Day 14: febrile neutropenia (T 102.4°F, ANC 0.2) requiring empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (cefepime); afebrile within 48 hr on antibiotics; blood cultures negative
opts.rat: Expected complication of induction chemotherapy. Febrile neutropenia is a separate emergency requiring antibiotics within 1 hr (cefepime + vancomycin if line infection suspected). Most resolve with empiric coverage. This is not a TLS issue but a post-induction supportive care issue — many such patients have multiple complications during their inpatient course.

opts.text: Hour 8: ionized Ca remains at 0.82 with no new symptoms; provider considers giving IV calcium gluconate but team decides to continue CRRT and sevelamer to lower phos first; Ca rises to 1.04 over the next 6 hr without supplementation
opts.rat: The right call confirmed. Asymptomatic mild hypocalcemia in TLS resolves on its own as the phosphate falls. Confirming the principle: address phos first; ionized Ca rises secondarily. IV calcium is reserved for severe symptoms or membrane-stabilization indications.

opts.text: Pre-discharge teaching with patient and family covers: TLS recognition signs (oliguria, muscle cramps, palpitations) for future chemo cycles, hydration during chemo cycles, the rasburicase/allopurinol plan, and a clear call-back plan for any concerning symptoms
opts.rat: High-leverage discharge teaching. Patients with high-risk hematologic malignancies will face many future chemotherapy cycles and need to know what TLS feels like, what hydration goals to maintain, and when to seek care. Family education multiplies the surveillance capacity. Discharge with a written plan and a 24-hr clinic number.

title: Anaphylaxis to IV Cefazolin in Pre-Op Holding
subtitle: IM Epinephrine First-Line, Biphasic Reactions, and the Beta-Blocker Trap

patient.name: J. Marchetti
patient.allergies: Penicillin (rash as a child only — no anaphylaxis or angioedema documented) per intake; otherwise NKDA
patient.admitDate: Today, 0815 (pre-op for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy)
patient.room: Pre-Op Bay 7 → MICU pending bed

vitals.time: 0830 — pre-op baseline (after IV started, before any meds)
0915 — CURRENT — 10 min after FIRST IM epinephrine + 1 L NS bolus

labs.v: Diffuse URTICARIA over chest/abdomen/arms; bilateral lip and tongue ANGIOEDEMA; periorbital edema; flushing; itching
labs.r: Clear skin without urticaria/angioedema

labs.v: Inspiratory STRIDOR audible without stethoscope; bilateral diffuse wheeze on auscultation; accessory muscle use; SpO2 88% on RA → 94% on 10 L NRB after epi
labs.r: Clear bilateral breath sounds; no stridor or wheeze

labs.v: Hypotension SBP 78 → 102 after epi + fluid bolus; tachycardia HR 132 → 116; cap refill 4 sec initially → 2 sec after epi
labs.r: Stable BP and perfusion

labs.v: Nausea, one episode of emesis (clear stomach contents); diffuse abdominal cramping
labs.r: No GI symptoms

labs.v: Reports SENSE OF IMPENDING DOOM, dizziness, near-syncope at onset; now alert and tracking after epi
labs.r: No anxiety or LOC concerns

labs.n: Diagnostic criteria — NIAID/FAAN consensus (any ONE of three)
labs.v: CRITERION 2 MET: 2+ organ systems after likely allergen exposure (skin + respiratory + CV + GI). Also CRITERION 3 MET: hypotension after known allergen exposure. Diagnosis = anaphylaxis.
labs.r: Diagnosis confirmed when any one of the three criteria is met

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 116; no ST changes; no QT prolongation; normal intervals
labs.r: Normal sinus rhythm

labs.v: Pending; expected to be elevated (peak 60–90 min post-onset; can be drawn within 3 hours of symptom start to support diagnosis)
labs.r: <11 ng/mL normal; elevation supports mast cell degranulation (anaphylaxis)

labs.v: All WNL — Cr 0.8, Glucose 92, K 4.1, Na 138, Hgb 13.4, Plt 245, WBC 7.2
labs.r: WNL

labs.v: Metoprolol succinate 50 mg PO daily for chronic stable HTN (NEW since last surgery 3 yr ago); levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily; multivitamin
labs.r: n/a

labs.n: Penicillin allergy history (specifics on directed questioning)
labs.v: Mild generalized rash on Day 3 of amoxicillin at age 7 for an ear infection. No hives, no swelling, no breathing difficulty, no anaphylaxis at the time. No further beta-lactam exposures in adult life. Family did not pursue formal allergy testing.
labs.r: Critical detail — most childhood 'penicillin allergy' labels are NOT true IgE-mediated allergy; formal de-labeling is increasingly recommended

labs.n: Pertinent negatives that ruled out the differential
labs.v: ECG without acute ischemia/STEMI (rules out MI/coronary spasm); no chest pain or RV strain (rules out PE in this acute setting); no inspiratory stridor BEFORE drug (rules out pre-existing airway obstruction); not vasovagal pattern (HR 132, not bradycardic; widespread urticaria not seen in vasovagal)
labs.r: Documented to support the anaphylaxis diagnosis and exclude mimics

nursesNote: 0915 — 42 y/o female brought to pre-op holding at 0815 for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. PMH significant for chronic stable HTN on metoprolol succinate 50 mg PO daily (took her morning dose at 0600), hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily, and a remote penicillin 'allergy' noted on intake (childhood rash with amoxicillin at age 7 — no hives, swelling, or breathing difficulty per patient on directed questioning, never further tested). Vitals on arrival reassuring (128/78, 76, RA 98%). Pre-op IV started, 18 g antecubital fossa, normal saline at TKO. Anesthesia briefed and signed; surgical site marked. CEFAZOLIN 2 g IV piggyback started over 5 min at 0900 (provider felt 1st-generation cephalosporin was safe given the remote, NON-IgE-mediated childhood penicillin reaction). Approximately 4 minutes into the cefazolin infusion (0904), client called out feeling 'really weird — like something bad is about to happen' and complained of itching on her chest. Within the next 90 seconds: diffuse urticaria spread to chest/abdomen/arms; lips and tongue began visibly swelling; she developed coughing, then audible inspiratory STRIDOR; BP dropped from 128/78 to 78/44; HR rose to 132; SpO2 fell to 88% on RA; she became diaphoretic, then vomited once, then complained of severe abdominal cramping. CEFAZOLIN INFUSION IMMEDIATELY STOPPED. EPINEPHRINE 0.3 mg (0.3 mL of 1 mg/mL [1:1000]) given IM into the LEFT LATERAL THIGH (vastus lateralis) at 0907. The patient was placed SUPINE with legs elevated. NS 1 L IV WIDE OPEN over the first 10 min for distributive shock support. Oxygen via 10 L non-rebreather mask. Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV, and methylprednisolone 125 mg IV given. Albuterol 2.5 mg nebulizer for the wheeze. Anesthesia and the OR team at bedside; the surgery is CANCELLED. By 0915 (10 min after IM epi): BP up to 102/64, HR 116, stridor markedly diminished, SpO2 94% on NRB, mental status now clear; the angioedema is no longer progressing but lip/tongue swelling remains. Patient is being prepared for MICU transfer for observation given (1) the severity of the initial presentation including airway involvement, (2) the risk of biphasic reaction (up to 23% of cases, median onset 6–8 hr later, occasionally up to 72 hr), and (3) the BETA-BLOCKER COMPLICATION — her chronic metoprolol can blunt the response to additional epinephrine doses; glucagon 1–5 mg IV is drawn at the bedside as the rescue if epi-refractory hypotension recurs. Tryptase level drawn at 0920 to support the diagnosis. Husband contacted, en route. Allergy/immunology consult placed. Critical incident review and chart update to upgrade the 'penicillin allergy' label to 'cefazolin: confirmed anaphylaxis with angioedema and respiratory compromise — avoid all beta-lactams pending formal testing.' Anesthesia agrees to re-evaluate the surgical antibiotic plan for any future procedure. Discharge teaching plan (when stable): EpiPen 0.3 mg auto-injector x 2 (always carry two due to biphasic reaction risk), MedicAlert bracelet, written anaphylaxis action plan, allergist referral for formal beta-lactam testing.
providerOrders: STOP the cefazolin infusion IMMEDIATELY (already done); remove the bag from the IV pole and SAVE it for incident review

EPINEPHRINE 0.3 mg (0.3 mL of 1:1000 / 1 mg/mL) IM in the LATERAL THIGH (vastus lateralis); MAY REPEAT every 5–15 min for ongoing symptoms (up to 3 doses before escalating to IV) — this is the first-line, life-saving intervention; do NOT delay for an IV access or for adjunctive medications

Position SUPINE with legs elevated (for hypotension); if vomiting or dyspnea position upright or in left-lateral; AVOID having the client sit up or stand suddenly — empty inferior vena cava syndrome can cause sudden death in anaphylaxis

100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask 10–15 L/min; prepare for INTUBATION (difficult airway anticipated due to angioedema) — anesthesia at bedside, awake fiberoptic intubation may be needed if airway progression
0.9% normal saline 1–2 L IV bolus rapidly for distributive shock; reassess after each bolus; total fluid often 2–4 L in severe cases
DIPHENHYDRAMINE (H1 blocker) 25–50 mg IV/IM — for cutaneous symptoms; does NOT treat the airway or circulation; SECOND-LINE
FAMOTIDINE (H2 blocker) 20 mg IV — adjunctive for cutaneous symptoms; combined H1+H2 better than either alone for itching/hives
METHYLPREDNISOLONE 125 mg IV (or hydrocortisone 200 mg IV) — onset 4–6 hr; may attenuate biphasic reaction risk; THIRD-LINE; NEVER substitute for epinephrine
ALBUTEROL 2.5 mg nebulizer for bronchospasm; may repeat continuously if needed (standard asthma dose — NOT the high-dose 10 mg used for hyperkalemia)
Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, end-tidal CO2 if intubated; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP if vasopressor-dependent

GLUCAGON 1–5 mg IV (slow over 5 min) AT THE BEDSIDE for any refractory hypotension — needed because the patient is on metoprolol (chronic beta-blocker), which can blunt response to additional epinephrine doses; glucagon bypasses the beta receptors via direct cAMP elevation

If hypotension refractory to repeated IM epi + glucagon + fluids: start IV EPINEPHRINE INFUSION 0.05–0.1 mcg/kg/min titrated; norepinephrine or vasopressin as additional vasopressor support; AVOID IV epinephrine BOLUS (risk of hypertensive crisis, intracranial hemorrhage, dysrhythmia)
TRYPTASE level drawn within 3 hr of symptom onset to support the diagnosis (already done at 0920); repeat at 24 hr for baseline comparison

Cancel today's surgery; reschedule after allergy workup completed (likely 4–6 weeks);  chart update to upgrade allergy label from 'penicillin: rash' to 'CEFAZOLIN: ANAPHYLAXIS with angioedema and respiratory compromise — avoid all beta-lactams pending formal allergy testing'

MICU transfer for OBSERVATION for AT LEAST 4–6 hours after symptom resolution (longer if: severe initial presentation, required >1 epi dose, history of biphasic reactions, comorbidities); some experts recommend 24-hour observation for severe initial presentation with airway involvement
Allergy/Immunology consultation for outpatient testing (beta-lactam skin testing, possible graded oral challenge, advice on safe future antibiotic choices)

DISCHARGE PLAN once stable: prescription for TWO EpiPen 0.3 mg auto-injectors (or equivalent — Auvi-Q), MedicAlert bracelet, written anaphylaxis action plan, family training in EpiPen administration, written list of beta-lactams (penicillins AND cephalosporins) to avoid until formal testing complete

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Diffuse urticaria + bilateral lip/tongue angioedema appearing within 4 min of cefazolin infusion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — skin and mucosal involvement after KNOWN allergen exposure. Urticaria + angioedema by themselves are not enough for anaphylaxis (anaphylaxis requires multi-system involvement or hypotension) but combined with the respiratory and CV findings the diagnosis is clear. Mucosal angioedema is a particular warning for airway progression.

opts.text: Audible inspiratory stridor with diffuse wheeze, SpO2 88% on room air
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — UPPER airway involvement (stridor) + LOWER airway involvement (wheeze). Stridor is a particularly ominous sign because the angioedema may progress to complete obstruction within minutes. Threshold for early intubation should be LOW; awake fiberoptic intubation may be needed to preserve a difficult airway.

opts.text: BP 78/44 with HR 132 minutes after cefazolin
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — distributive shock. Massive mast cell degranulation releases histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins, producing systemic vasodilation, capillary leak, and intravascular volume depletion. The reflex tachycardia (unlike vasovagal bradycardia) helps distinguish anaphylaxis from a syncopal reaction.

opts.text: GI symptoms (one emesis episode, diffuse abdominal cramping) added to the picture
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — GI is one of the 4 systems used to meet the diagnostic criteria (skin/mucosal, respiratory, cardiovascular, GI). Two or more system involvement after likely allergen exposure = anaphylaxis. GI symptoms also represent smooth muscle effects of histamine/leukotrienes.

opts.text: 'Sense of impending doom' reported by the patient at onset
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a documented anaphylaxis warning sign. Has both physiologic basis (early hypotension/hypoxia causes anxiety) and psychological component. Patients describe it consistently; experienced clinicians treat it as a strong predictor of impending cardiovascular collapse. Listen to the patient.

opts.text: Cefazolin given despite a documented 'penicillin allergy' (childhood rash with amoxicillin)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — context for the event and the systems-level learning. Penicillin-cephalosporin cross-reactivity is LOWER than historically taught (~1–2% for 3rd/4th gen cephs; 1st gen like cefazolin slightly higher due to side-chain similarity). The decision to give was defensible — but the OUTCOME mandates a chart update and formal allergy workup.

opts.text: Patient is on METOPROLOL — chronic beta-blocker — at home
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — complicates management. Beta-blockers can blunt the response to epinephrine; refractory hypotension despite repeated IM epinephrine should trigger GLUCAGON 1–5 mg IV (bypasses the beta receptors by directly raising cAMP via glucagon receptors on cardiac and smooth muscle). Have glucagon drawn at the bedside.

opts.text: Anaphylaxis diagnostic criteria met (2+ organ systems after KNOWN allergen exposure: skin + respiratory + CV + GI; AND hypotension after known allergen)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the diagnosis is confirmed (any ONE of the three NIAID/FAAN criteria suffices; this patient meets TWO). Anaphylaxis is a CLINICAL diagnosis; no test is required to start treatment. Tryptase supports the diagnosis later but treatment does NOT wait for tryptase.

opts.text: Response to first IM epinephrine: BP 78 → 102, HR 132 → 116, stridor markedly diminished, SpO2 94% on NRB
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms epinephrine response. The improvement is expected within 5–10 min of correctly placed IM epi (lateral thigh / vastus lateralis — better absorption than deltoid). Be prepared to redose at 5–15 min intervals if symptoms recur or persist.

opts.text: Patient is alert and can communicate concerns clearly
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — preserved mentation now is critical for the next phase. Get a clear allergy history from the patient WHILE she can speak; the angioedema may worsen before it improves. Document the precise specifics of the original penicillin reaction for future safe medication planning.

opts.text: BMP and CBC pre-op baseline all WNL
opts.rat: Reassuring baseline labs do not directly affect anaphylaxis management. The diagnosis and treatment are clinical, not laboratory-based. Document and move on.

opts.text: Temperature 98.6°F
opts.rat: Normal. Not part of the anaphylaxis picture and not actionable. Anaphylaxis typically does NOT cause fever (helps distinguish from septic shock, which is also distributive but with fever, source of infection, and slower onset).

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: IgE-mediated mast cell and basophil degranulation in response to the cefazolin (or its metabolites) releases histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, prostaglandins, and platelet-activating factor systemically — producing the urticaria/angioedema (vasodilation + capillary leak), bronchospasm (lower airway smooth muscle), distributive shock (systemic vasodilation), and GI symptoms (smooth muscle activation)
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Note that some 'anaphylactoid' reactions (vancomycin red-man syndrome, radiocontrast media historically) are direct mast cell degranulation without IgE — but the bedside presentation and treatment are the same. Modern terminology uses 'anaphylaxis' for both.

opts.text: EPINEPHRINE is FIRST-LINE because it addresses ALL three life-threatening processes simultaneously: (1) alpha-1 → vasoconstriction restores BP and reverses mucosal angioedema; (2) beta-1 → inotropy/chronotropy supports cardiac output; (3) beta-2 → bronchodilation reverses bronchospasm AND stabilizes mast cells. Antihistamines and steroids do NOT replace this and should never delay it.
opts.rat: CORRECT and high-yield. The most common error in anaphylaxis management is delaying epinephrine in favor of diphenhydramine or steroids. Antihistamines treat ONLY the cutaneous symptoms; steroids have a 4–6 hr onset. Epinephrine is the only drug that can prevent death — it is THE intervention.

opts.text: Epinephrine for ANAPHYLAXIS is given IM 0.3 mg (0.3 mL of 1:1000 = 1 mg/mL) into the LATERAL THIGH (vastus lateralis — superior absorption to deltoid). IV bolus dosing (1 mg/10 mL = 1:10,000) is reserved for ACLS CARDIAC ARREST. Confusing the two routes/concentrations can cause fatal hypertensive crisis, intracerebral hemorrhage, or VF.
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield NCLEX trap. The error of pulling out a code-cart epi 1 mg/10 mL and pushing IV in anaphylaxis (rather than IM 1 mg/mL into the thigh) is well-documented as a sentinel event. IV BOLUS epi during anaphylaxis is reserved for refractory cardiovascular collapse and only at MUCH smaller doses (~10–50 mcg/min INFUSION, not 1 mg bolus).

opts.text: BIPHASIC REACTIONS occur in up to 23% of anaphylaxis cases — a recurrence of symptoms hours after the initial reaction has resolved, even without re-exposure. Median onset is 6–8 hours, occasionally up to 72 hours later. Predictors of higher biphasic risk: severe initial presentation, >1 epinephrine dose needed, delayed initial epi, hypotension at presentation, unknown trigger.
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield discharge consideration. Drives the minimum 4–6 hour observation period (longer for severe presentations); the rationale for TWO EpiPens at discharge (in case symptoms return); and steroid administration (may reduce biphasic risk, but evidence is mixed). Patient education on recognizing biphasic recurrence is essential.

opts.text: Beta-blockers (metoprolol, propranolol, etc.) blunt the response to epinephrine; in refractory anaphylaxis, GLUCAGON 1–5 mg IV bypasses the beta-receptors via direct activation of cardiac and smooth muscle glucagon receptors (raising cAMP)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the beta-blocker rescue. The mechanism: glucagon raises intracellular cAMP independent of beta receptors. Onset is rapid. Side effects: nausea/vomiting (give an antiemetic, position lateral to prevent aspiration). High-yield because beta-blockers are common chronic medications.

opts.text: TRUE penicillin allergy (IgE-mediated, hives/anaphylaxis as the documented reaction) carries a ~2% risk of cefazolin cross-reactivity (lower than historic estimates). Many 'penicillin allergy' chart labels are NOT IgE-mediated and are based on childhood rashes, GI upset, or fatigue — these are not allergic reactions. Formal de-labeling via allergy testing or graded oral challenge is increasingly recommended to expand antibiotic options.
opts.rat: CORRECT — the broader systems-level lesson. The Penicillin Allergy De-labeling movement aims to reduce inappropriate avoidance of beta-lactams. About 90% of childhood 'penicillin allergy' labels are NOT verified on testing. THIS patient's reaction confirms her cefazolin allergy and SHOULD be formally tested for related agents. The chart label update is part of the response.

opts.text: Antihistamines (diphenhydramine, famotidine) alone can manage moderate anaphylaxis without epinephrine if the patient has urticaria and mild angioedema
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and DANGEROUS. Antihistamines treat ONLY the cutaneous symptoms; they do NOT prevent airway or cardiovascular progression. Many anaphylaxis deaths are associated with reliance on antihistamines and delayed epinephrine. Once anaphylaxis criteria are met (multi-system + likely allergen, OR hypotension + allergen), give EPI.

opts.text: Corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) are first-line and should be given before epinephrine for severe reactions
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT. Steroids have a 4–6 hour onset and do nothing for the acute crisis. They MAY attenuate biphasic reaction risk (mixed evidence). NEVER substitute for epinephrine and NEVER given before epinephrine. Steroid-first protocols are an old practice that has been associated with deaths from delayed epi.

opts.text: The patient should be sat upright to help her breathe better
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT for HYPOTENSIVE anaphylaxis. Sudden sitting/standing in volume-depleted distributive shock can cause empty-vena-cava syndrome and sudden cardiac arrest. Position SUPINE with legs elevated if hypotensive; UPRIGHT only if vomiting or dyspnea is dominant and BP allows. The right answer is symptom-driven, not 'always upright.'

opts.text: Observation periods after successful epinephrine treatment should be MINIMUM 4–6 hours; longer (up to 24 hr) for severe presentations with airway compromise, hypotension, multiple epi doses, or known biphasic history
opts.rat: CORRECT — the disposition rule. The biphasic risk is the rationale. Discharge before adequate observation puts the patient at risk for unmonitored recurrence. The 4–6 hour minimum is from CDC/AAAAI/WAO guidelines. This patient with airway compromise + hypotension warrants 24 hours.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Airway Obstruction r/t laryngeal angioedema with stridor (airway can occlude within minutes)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Airway. Stridor and tongue/lip angioedema are the immediate killers. Have anesthesia at bedside for difficult airway intubation; awake fiberoptic intubation may be needed; surgical airway (cricothyrotomy) backup plan. Epinephrine reverses the angioedema if given promptly.

opts.text: Risk for Cardiovascular Collapse r/t distributive shock from massive mast cell degranulation (BP 78/44, MAP 55)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Circulation. The same epinephrine that addresses the airway also restores BP (alpha-1 vasoconstriction). NS 1–2 L IV bolus for distributive shock; supine with legs elevated; second/third IM epi doses if BP doesn't improve; glucagon for beta-blocker-blunted response; IV epi INFUSION (NOT bolus) for refractory cases.

opts.text: Risk for Refractory Anaphylaxis r/t chronic metoprolol blunting the response to epinephrine — glucagon at bedside as rescue
opts.rat: #3 — Significant. The beta-blocker complication is a foreseeable cause of treatment failure. Glucagon 1–5 mg IV is the bedside rescue and should be drawn BEFORE the second IM epi dose is needed. If a third epi dose is required, glucagon should be given concurrently.

opts.text: Risk for Biphasic Reaction r/t severe initial presentation with airway involvement — minimum 4–6 hr observation (longer for severe)
opts.rat: #4 — Significant for disposition. Biphasic reactions occur in up to 23% with median onset 6–8 hr. MICU observation x 24 hr is reasonable for this severe presentation. The TWO-EpiPen prescription at discharge addresses the home risk; a written action plan and family training reduce delays.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit r/t allergen identification, EpiPen technique, and recognition of biphasic recurrence — discharge teaching imperative
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in acute ranking but THE preventable factor for the next event. Comprehensive teaching: TWO EpiPens (always carry both; the second is for biphasic), MedicAlert bracelet, written action plan, family training, allergist referral for formal beta-lactam testing, avoidance list of all beta-lactams pending testing. This is leadership-level nursing.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: STOP the cefazolin infusion immediately and save the bag for incident review (already done)
opts.rat: Source control. Stop the trigger before continued absorption worsens the reaction. Saving the bag preserves the medication for incident review, root-cause analysis, and any subsequent allergy testing reference.

opts.text: EPINEPHRINE 0.3 mg IM (0.3 mL of 1:1000 / 1 mg/mL) lateral thigh; may repeat q5–15 min for ongoing symptoms (up to 3 doses before escalating to IV infusion)
opts.rat: FIRST-LINE, life-saving intervention. The right drug, the right route (IM > IV bolus), the right concentration (1:1000, not the 1:10,000 ACLS preparation), the right site (lateral thigh > deltoid for absorption). All other interventions are adjunctive.

opts.text: Position SUPINE with legs elevated (Trendelenburg if available); upright/lateral only if vomiting or severe dyspnea dominates
opts.rat: Hemodynamic-driven positioning. Sudden upright posture in distributive shock with empty IVC can cause cardiovascular collapse and sudden death. Document the rationale; advise the team to keep the client supine; tilt left lateral if vomiting; only allow upright if dyspnea is the dominant problem and BP is stable.

opts.text: 100% O2 via non-rebreather mask 10–15 L/min; prepare for difficult-airway intubation (anesthesia at bedside)
opts.rat: Hypoxia mitigation while waiting for epi to reverse the angioedema and bronchospasm. Anesthesia at bedside for difficult intubation; awake fiberoptic intubation if angioedema progressing; surgical airway team aware as backup.

opts.text: 0.9% NS 1–2 L IV bolus rapidly for distributive shock; reassess after each liter; total fluid often 2–4 L in severe cases
opts.rat: Aggressive fluid resuscitation for the distributive component of anaphylactic shock (capillary leak + vasodilation). Bolus, reassess, repeat. Avoid persistent fluid overload — once perfusion restored, transition to maintenance.

opts.text: Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV (H1) + famotidine 20 mg IV (H2) — second-line adjuncts for cutaneous symptoms
opts.rat: Adjunctive only. The combined H1+H2 blockade is more effective than either alone for itching, hives, and angioedema. They do NOT address the airway or cardiovascular components and SHOULD NEVER delay or replace epinephrine.

opts.text: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV — third-line; may attenuate biphasic reaction risk
opts.rat: Reasonable adjunct. Onset 4–6 hr; not acute. The evidence for biphasic reduction is mixed but most centers continue to use it. NEVER substitute for epinephrine and NEVER given before epinephrine.
