opts.rat: Nursing leadership essential. The family's experience of past undertreatment is real and shapes their trust in the team. Documenting the current adequate plan, communicating it across shifts, and addressing any deviations is part of the standard of care. This is a leadership-level intervention that improves outcomes.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Apply O2 to SpO2 ≥95% (start 4 L NC); blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics; administer empiric ceftriaxone + azithromycin within 1 hr; obtain stat CXR
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Oxygenation + early diagnostics + time-critical antibiotics. The 1-hr antibiotic rule applies to ANY sickle cell patient with fever (functional asplenia vulnerability). CXR confirms ACS criteria. None of these require waiting — bedside-nursing actions plus standing orders.

opts.text: Start around-the-clock multimodal analgesia: morphine PCA basal 1 mg/hr + demand 0.5 mg q10min; ketorolac 30 mg IV; acetaminophen 1 g IV; pain reassessment q1h
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Adequate pain control isn't optional — it's part of the ACS treatment (enables deep breathing, IS, mobilization). Scheduled and PCA, not PRN. Multimodal reduces total opioid exposure while increasing analgesia.

opts.text: Maintenance IVF D5 ½NS at 1.25x (~125 mL/hr) — NO boluses; bedside warming; incentive spirometry q1h while awake
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Conservative fluid strategy + warming + IS are the bedside nursing interventions that interrupt the vaso-occlusive cycle. No fluid boluses even with tachycardia. Cold avoidance. IS reduces atelectasis.

opts.text: Hematology consultation; order simple transfusion 1 unit PRBC (antigen-matched, leukoreduced, sickle-negative) targeting Hgb 9–10; have exchange transfusion ready on standby
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive intervention. Transfusion decision is hematology-driven; the goal Hgb ceiling 9–10 avoids hyperviscosity. Exchange transfusion (via apheresis) on standby if ACS progresses. Coordinate with blood bank for antigen-matched units.

opts.text: Patient/family teaching on triggers (cold + dehydration + infection clustering) and hydroxyurea adherence; case management for HU support; school nurse coordination; hematology follow-up in 1 week; document and address past undertreatment concerns
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management and prevention. Once acute pathway is in motion, the long-term levers (HU adherence, trigger avoidance, communication, advocacy against bias) determine the trajectory of the disease. Nursing leadership essential.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 12: SpO2 96% on 2 L NC (weaning), temp 100.0°F, pain 4/10 with PCA, post-transfusion Hgb 9.6 g/dL, repeat CXR shows resolving RLL infiltrate
opts.rat: Excellent trajectory. The bundle is working — oxygenation improving, pain controlled, infection resolving, transfusion successful. Continue current plan; wean O2 to RA; transition PCA to PO opioids; complete antibiotic course; plan discharge in 3–5 days.

opts.text: Day 5: discharged home on oral opioids (tapering plan), hydroxyurea resumed at home dose, school nurse coordination meeting scheduled, hematology follow-up in 1 week, case management has set up pill organizer + smartphone reminders + structured medication refill
opts.rat: Successful disposition with strong prevention bundle. The school nurse coordination addresses the trigger that produced this admission (cold weather sports without hydration). HU adherence support reduces future crisis frequency. Family report having felt heard about past undertreatment concerns.

opts.text: 3-month outpatient follow-up: no crises since discharge, HU adherence >95% by pill count, HbF level 18% (up from 8%), reports feeling 'best in years,' returning to sports with hydration plan
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. Sustained HU adherence produces measurable disease modification (rising HbF, fewer crises). The bedside admission has translated into long-term gains because the system (school, family, hematology, case management) is supporting the patient. Continue current plan; routine clinic visits.

opts.text: Hour 4: SpO2 dropping to 82% on 4 L NC, new LEFT lung infiltrate on CXR, RR 38, lethargic, lactate climbing to 4.8 — ACS progressing despite simple transfusion
opts.rat: ACS progression — escalation indication. EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION via apheresis NOW (goal HbS <30%); transfer to PICU for HFNC or BiPAP; consider intubation if respiratory failure continues; reassess infection coverage (broaden antibiotics if MRSA/Pseudomonas concern); call hematology and apheresis team. Mortality of severe ACS is significant — aggressive escalation.

opts.text: Hour 8: new focal weakness right side, expressive aphasia, GCS drops from 14 to 12 — concern for sickle cell stroke
opts.rat: STROKE in sickle cell — neurologic emergency. Stat MRI/MRA; emergency EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION (goal HbS <30%); avoid tPA (acute sickling lesions are not tPA targets); neurology consultation; ICU admission. Stroke is a known complication of sickle cell disease; ~10% of HbSS children have stroke. Chronic transfusion or HU intensification for secondary prevention.

opts.text: Hour 12: BP drops to 78/40 with mottled extremities, lactate 5.8, despite maintenance IVF; ICU intensivist considers vasopressor over additional fluid boluses
opts.rat: Septic-shock physiology developing — escalation. Norepinephrine drip 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP ≥65; CAUTIOUS additional fluid (small 250 mL challenges rather than 30 mL/kg bolus given ACS risk); broaden antibiotics; central line; ICU intensivist at bedside. The conservative fluid strategy is harder when shock develops but remains important — supplement with vasopressor.

opts.text: Day 7: develops large gallstone-related cholecystitis from pigment stones (chronic hemolysis); needs cholecystectomy
opts.rat: Pigment gallstones are a known complication of chronic hemolysis (~50% of HbSS children by adolescence). Cholecystectomy when symptomatic. Perioperative management: pre-op simple transfusion to goal Hgb 9–10, careful temperature/hydration/oxygenation management, ACS prophylaxis with IS, post-op pain control without inducing splinting.

opts.text: Day 2: bedside nurse on the night shift writes order for 'morphine 2 mg IV q4h PRN' without scheduled dosing; patient pain rises to 8/10 by morning
opts.rat: Bias-driven undertreatment. The night shift order is the documented pattern. ADVOCATE for the scheduled around-the-clock plan; communicate the patient's history of past undertreatment and the current adequate plan; document the pain response and the lapse in care; if appropriate, raise to charge nurse / clinical educator / sickle cell program leadership. Systems-level improvement is part of the standard of care.

opts.text: Mildly tender lower extremities for 1 week post-discharge despite resolved primary pain crisis
opts.rat: Mild residual post-VOC pain is expected for days to weeks after a major crisis. Acetaminophen and NSAID for breakthrough; gradual return to activity; avoid the triggers that precipitated the original event. Most patients have intermittent baseline pain between crises — chronic pain syndromes are common in sickle cell.

opts.text: 5-year follow-up: enrolled in clinical trial for gene therapy (lovotibeglogene autotemcel); successful transplant; HbS now <30% with HbF significantly elevated; pain crisis frequency reduced by >90%
opts.rat: Realistic long-term outcome with cutting-edge therapy. FDA-approved gene therapies (lovotibeglogene autotemcel — exa-cel; exagamglogene autotemcel — exa-cel; etc.) and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation are increasingly available for severe sickle cell disease. They are not curative for all but produce substantial disease modification. The patient's strong adherence and family support make him an excellent candidate.

title: Severe Alcohol Withdrawal with Delirium Tremens
subtitle: CIWA-Ar Protocol, Thiamine-Before-Glucose, and the Benzo Front-Load

patient.name: T. Jameson
patient.admitDate: Hospital Day 2 (admitted from PACU after elective inguinal hernia repair)
patient.room: Med-Surg 4B → step-down for safety / sitter pending

vitals.time: Hospital Day 1, 1800 (24 hr post-op — early withdrawal noted)
Hospital Day 2, 0600 (CURRENT — 48 hr since last drink — florid DTs)

labs.n: CIWA-Ar score (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol, revised)
labs.v: 32 — SEVERE withdrawal (nausea 5, tremor 6, sweats 7, anxiety 7, agitation 7, tactile 4, auditory 4, visual 5, headache 4, orientation/sensorium 3)
labs.r: 0–67 scale; <10 mild, 10–18 moderate, ≥19 severe; >15 needs benzodiazepine; reassess q1–4h

labs.v: 1.4 mg/dL
labs.r: 1.8–2.4 mg/dL; chronic alcohol use depletes Mg; low Mg WORSENS withdrawal and prevents K+ replacement

labs.v: 3.2 mEq/L
labs.r: 3.5–5.0 mEq/L; will NOT replete effectively until Mg corrected

labs.v: 2.1 mg/dL
labs.r: 2.5–4.5 mg/dL; chronic alcohol use depletes; refeeding-syndrome risk during nutritional repletion

labs.v: 134 mEq/L
labs.r: 136–145; mild hyponatremia from chronic alcohol effects on ADH and volume status

labs.v: 78 mg/dL
labs.r: 70–140; trending toward hypoglycemia — do NOT give dextrose until thiamine is on board (Wernicke prevention)

labs.v: AST 84 / ALT 56 / ratio 1.5 (AST > ALT)
labs.r: AST 10–35, ALT 7–35; AST > ALT with ratio >1.5 is the classic alcoholic-liver pattern

labs.v: Albumin 2.8 / INR 1.4 / Total bili 1.6
labs.r: Albumin 3.5–5.0; INR <1.1; total bili <1.2 — synthetic and excretory liver dysfunction
<200 U/L; mild rhabdomyolysis from prolonged agitation, restraints, or seizure-like activity

labs.v: 84 µg/dL
labs.r: <60 µg/dL; mildly elevated — concurrent hepatic encephalopathy could contribute to confusion in the differential

labs.v: 3.2 mmol/L
labs.r: 0.5–2.2 mmol/L; mildly elevated from agitation and increased muscle work

labs.v: 32 U/L (NORMAL)
labs.r: <60 U/L; rules out concurrent acute pancreatitis (alcoholic patients often have it)

labs.v: Ethanol level 28 mg/dL (residual, declining); cannabinoids positive; otherwise negative for opiates, cocaine, amphetamines, benzodiazepines
labs.r: Important to RULE OUT concurrent intoxication or polysubstance withdrawal

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 142; QTc 480 ms (mildly prolonged — caution with QT-prolonging drugs); no ischemic ST changes; nl axis
labs.r: QTc <450 male / <470 female

labs.n: Alcohol use history (from family on Hospital Day 2)
labs.v: Family reports patient drinks ~1 liter vodka daily x 20+ years; patient previously reported only 'a few beers on weekends' to the surgeon at pre-op (typical minimization)
labs.r: True alcohol use history is essential for predicting withdrawal severity and pre-treatment planning

labs.v: Last drink ~36 hr before admission + 48 hr in hospital = ~84 hr (3.5 days) since last drink at this moment of presentation — squarely in the DT window (48–96 hr after last drink)
labs.r: Withdrawal timeline: 6–12 hr early autonomic + tremor; 12–24 hr hallucinations (mostly visual); 12–48 hr seizures (usually generalized tonic-clonic); 48–96 hr DELIRIUM TREMENS

labs.v: Disoriented to PLACE and TIME (knows name only); CAM-positive (acute fluctuating attention deficit + disorganized thinking); visual hallucinations (sees 'snakes on the bed' and 'bugs on the walls'); profound psychomotor agitation with attempts to climb out of bed; coarse tremor of both upper extremities; gait not tested (unsafe); no focal neurologic deficits; pupils 4 mm, reactive
labs.r: Oriented x3; calm and cooperative; no hallucinations; no tremor

nursesNote: 0600 — 54 y/o male admitted to Med-Surg 2 days ago after uneventful elective laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair. Pre-op assessment documented 'mild social alcohol use, a few beers on weekends' — patient minimized his use to the surgeon (a common and clinically important pattern). On Day 1 at 1800 the night-shift nurse noted mild tremor, anxiety, mild diaphoresis, BP up to 158/96, HR 108; CIWA-Ar score was 12 (mild–moderate withdrawal) — diazepam 10 mg PO given per the symptom-triggered protocol. The team called the patient's wife at home to get a fuller history; she disclosed that her husband has been drinking 'a liter of vodka, sometimes more' daily for the last 20+ years, has had two prior episodes of 'the shakes' in past hospitalizations, and one prior seizure he attributed to 'low blood sugar.' Last drink confirmed ~36 hr before admission. The team upgraded the level of withdrawal-risk monitoring but the case progressed overnight: at 0200 the patient began experiencing visual hallucinations (snakes on the bed, bugs on the walls), HR climbed to 132, BP to 178/104, temperature rose to 101.4°F, tremor became coarse and continuous. At 0500 he attempted to climb out of bed and pulled out his IV; rapid response was called; new peripheral IV established; lorazepam 4 mg IV given but agitation continued. At the 0600 reassessment NOW: CIWA-Ar is 32 (severe), patient is markedly agitated, attempting to disrobe and 'fight the bugs,' disoriented to place and time, HR 142, BP 184/108, T 102.3°F, RR 28, profusely diaphoretic, coarse tremor of all extremities, multiple superficial scratches from picking at imagined skin pests. NPO confirmed. SAFETY FIRST: 1:1 sitter at bedside; bed alarm activated; quiet room with dim lighting (overstimulation worsens DTs); restraints AVOIDED if at all possible (worsens agitation; only as last resort with strict orders and reassessment). THIAMINE 500 mg IV (high-dose for active Wernicke risk, NOT the standard 100 mg) just given BEFORE any glucose-containing IVF — Wernicke prevention is non-negotiable in any alcohol-use disorder patient. FOLATE 1 mg IV, multivitamin IV. MAGNESIUM sulfate 2 g IV running (low Mg 1.4 — must be corrected before potassium replacement). LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV q15 min — front-loading per the severe-withdrawal protocol (rather than waiting for CIWA-driven dosing); first 4 doses (16 mg total over 60 min) given so far with only modest improvement in CIWA score (28 → 25). The hospitalist and ICU consultant are at bedside. Plan being made now: (1) escalate to PHENOBARBITAL load 5–10 mg/kg IV for benzodiazepine-resistant DTs and/or (2) transfer to ICU for DEXMEDETOMIDINE drip for autonomic and agitation control (alpha-2 agonist; does NOT suppress respiratory drive; does NOT prevent seizures so benzo MUST continue). Cardiology aware (QTc 480, baseline tachycardia, AST/ALT elevation, electrolyte derangements). Wife at bedside, exhausted and worried; she had not appreciated the severity of his drinking. Social work consulted for inpatient addiction medicine evaluation and discharge planning with naltrexone/acamprosate options + behavioral health + community AA/AA-equivalent + outpatient counseling. The bedside RN advocating against haloperidol (lowers seizure threshold, prolongs QTc — already 480) and against ETOH-titration protocols (not used in modern practice). This patient needs more benzodiazepine, ICU-level monitoring, and a comprehensive long-term plan — NOT physical restraints, NOT haloperidol, and NOT a 'wait and see' approach.
providerOrders: 1:1 SITTER continuous at bedside; bed alarm activated; bed in low position; quiet room with dim lighting (overstimulation worsens DTs); minimize traffic; soft voice tone
Physical restraints ONLY as last resort with explicit orders (q1h reassessment, q2h ROM, attempts at less restrictive measures documented); chemical sedation preferred when needed

THIAMINE 500 mg IV TID x 2–3 days (high-dose protocol for active Wernicke risk), then 100 mg IV/PO daily — give BEFORE ANY GLUCOSE-CONTAINING IVF; never give dextrose to a chronic alcohol-use patient without thiamine on board first
Folate 1 mg IV daily; multivitamin IV daily; pyridoxine (B6) 50 mg IV daily as part of 'banana bag' replacement

MAGNESIUM sulfate 2 g IV over 1 hr, then 1 g IV q4h until Mg ≥1.8; potassium replacement only AFTER Mg corrected (1 mEq/L K rise per 10 mEq KCl IV with cardiac monitoring; max 10 mEq/hr peripheral, 20 mEq/hr central); phosphorus replacement once Mg/K corrected

BENZODIAZEPINE FRONT-LOADING per severe-withdrawal protocol: LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV q15min UNTIL calm (target CIWA <15, RASS −1 to 0); document total dose given; reassess CIWA q1h once stable; switch to symptom-triggered (CIWA-Ar driven) dosing when stable

ALTERNATIVE / for hepatic dysfunction: LORAZEPAM preferred (no active metabolites; renally cleared); DIAZEPAM 10–20 mg IV q5–10 min has self-tapering effect via long half-life of active metabolites but accumulates in liver disease

PHENOBARBITAL load 5–10 mg/kg IV (slow over 30 min) for BENZODIAZEPINE-RESISTANT cases (>30 mg lorazepam or >100 mg diazepam in first hour without adequate effect); long half-life provides smooth taper; respiratory monitoring essential

DEXMEDETOMIDINE infusion 0.2–1.4 mcg/kg/hr IV titrated for refractory agitation (no respiratory depression; primary effect autonomic + sedation; DOES NOT PREVENT SEIZURES — continue benzo concurrently)
Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, EtCO2 if heavily sedated; have airway cart, naloxone, flumazenil (rarely used; can precipitate seizure) at bedside

AVOID haloperidol (lowers seizure threshold, prolongs QTc — already 480 here, risk torsades); AVOID first-generation antihistamines (anticholinergic worsens delirium); AVOID restraints unless absolutely necessary
AVOID ethanol-titration protocols (not used in modern practice — does not address withdrawal mechanism, complicates dosing, no clear benefit over benzodiazepines)
Reassess CIWA-Ar q1h until stable, then q2-4h; goal CIWA <8; recheck Mg, K, phos q4-6h until stable
Strict I&O; daily weights; fluid status reassessment (autonomic storm can produce significant insensible losses)
Seizure precautions: padded side rails up, suction at bedside, oxygen and bag-mask ventilation at bedside; reactive lorazepam 4 mg IV for breakthrough seizure
Acetaminophen 1 g IV/PO q6h scheduled for fever and pain (NOT NSAID — bleeding risk + liver/renal concerns in alcohol use disorder)

Pain management for surgical site: avoid opioid where possible (sedation + delirium worsening + respiratory depression); use scheduled acetaminophen + lidocaine patch + ketorolac (single dose) sparingly

Nutrition: NPO during acute DTs (aspiration risk); once oriented and tolerating sips, advance to soft diet; consider refeeding-syndrome risk (low phos baseline) — start at half-maintenance calories and titrate up over 5–7 days
Inpatient ADDICTION MEDICINE consult; social work for discharge planning; behavioral health; consider community AA/SMART Recovery referral; outpatient counseling

Discharge MAT (Medication-Assisted Treatment) options to discuss when oriented: NALTREXONE 50 mg PO daily (or 380 mg IM monthly Vivitrol; reduces cravings; cannot use with active opioid use); ACAMPROSATE 666 mg PO TID (reduces protracted withdrawal symptoms; useful in liver disease); DISULFIRAM 250 mg PO daily (aversion therapy; requires patient commitment — drinking causes severe acetaldehyde reaction)
Family/wife support; education on AUD as a chronic relapsing disease; honest discussion about recurrence risk; resources for spouse self-care (Al-Anon, etc.)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: CIWA-Ar score 32 — SEVERE withdrawal with high scores across all domains (tremor 6, sweats 7, anxiety 7, agitation 7, visual hallucinations 5, orientation/sensorium 3)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — quantifies severity. CIWA-Ar ≥19 is severe; ≥15 needs benzodiazepine; >25 is at high risk for progression to DTs and life-threatening complications. Drives benzodiazepine dose, monitoring frequency, and disposition (med-surg → step-down → ICU).

opts.text: Florid DTs picture: disoriented to place/time, visual hallucinations (snakes/bugs), profound psychomotor agitation, tachycardia 142, hypertension 184/108, hyperthermia 102.3°F, profuse diaphoresis, coarse tremor
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — DELIRIUM TREMENS, the most severe and life-threatening alcohol withdrawal manifestation. Mortality 5–15% UNTREATED, <5% WITH treatment. The autonomic storm alone can cause cardiovascular collapse, arrhythmia, or aspiration. Aggressive benzodiazepine + electrolytes + supportive care; consider ICU-level monitoring.

opts.text: Timeline: last drink ~84 hr ago (36 hr pre-admission + 48 hr in hospital) — squarely in the DT window (48–96 hr after last drink)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms the diagnosis. The withdrawal timeline is highly stereotyped: 6–12 hr early autonomic + tremor; 12–24 hr hallucinations; 12–48 hr seizures; 48–96 hr DTs. Knowing the timeline rules in withdrawal and helps anticipate next-step risks (seizure peak already passed; DT peak now).

opts.text: Hidden alcohol use disclosed by family on Day 2: 1 L vodka daily x 20+ years, patient minimized to 'a few beers on weekends' at pre-op
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — explains the severity AND the failure of pre-op screening. Routine pre-op screening (AUDIT-C, CAGE, single-question screen) misses many heavy drinkers due to minimization. Active family interview is often more accurate than patient self-report in major surgery planning. Future implication: routine pre-emptive thiamine + benzodiazepine prophylaxis in chronic drinkers undergoing surgery.

opts.text: Magnesium 1.4 mg/dL, potassium 3.2, phosphorus 2.1, sodium 134 — the classic chronic-alcohol-use electrolyte deficit profile
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — must be corrected. Chronic alcohol use depletes Mg, K, phos, and produces dilutional hyponatremia. Mg deficiency PROLONGS withdrawal and PREVENTS K+ replacement. Correct Mg first; K and phos second. Watch for refeeding syndrome when nutrition is reintroduced.

opts.text: QTc 480 ms (mildly prolonged) — caution with QT-prolonging agents
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — guides drug selection. Common DT meds that prolong QT: haloperidol (NOT recommended in DTs anyway), ondansetron, methadone, dexmedetomidine (modest effect). Lorazepam is QT-neutral. Monitor for torsades de pointes; check Mg level (low Mg prolongs QT).

opts.text: AST 84 / ALT 56 (AST > ALT ratio 1.5) with albumin 2.8 and INR 1.4 — alcoholic liver disease pattern with synthetic dysfunction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — affects drug metabolism choices. Diazepam (long half-life via hepatic metabolism + active metabolites) accumulates in liver disease. LORAZEPAM is preferred — no active metabolites, predictable kinetics, predominantly conjugated/renally cleared. Same logic for hepatic encephalopathy management.

opts.text: Glucose 78 mg/dL (drifting toward hypoglycemia)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — and a TRAP. The instinct is to give D50 IV push for low glucose. In a chronic alcohol-use patient with depleted thiamine stores, giving glucose BEFORE thiamine consumes the last thiamine and PRECIPITATES Wernicke encephalopathy (the triad: ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, confusion). ALWAYS give thiamine first (or concurrently). This is one of the highest-yield NCLEX pharmacology trips.

opts.text: Patient attempting to climb out of bed, picking at imaginary insects, self-injurious behavior (scratches)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — patient safety. 1:1 sitter, bed alarm, quiet room, minimal stimulation, low bed position. Restraints AVOIDED if at all possible (they worsen agitation and produce rhabdomyolysis); chemical sedation with benzodiazepines is the preferred control. Aspiration risk from disorganized swallowing — strict NPO during active DTs.

opts.text: Toxicology screen: ethanol 28 mg/dL (declining residual), cannabinoids positive, no benzodiazepines, no opiates, no cocaine/amphetamines
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rules out polysubstance withdrawal/intoxication that would complicate management. No prior benzo use (so no benzo tolerance complicating dosing). No active opioid concern (so naltrexone is a viable option for discharge MAT). The cannabis is unlikely to be acutely contributing to the picture but should be addressed long-term.

opts.text: Post-op surgical site (right inguinal) intact, no signs of bleeding or infection
opts.rat: Reassuring — no surgical complication contributing. Document and monitor; the surgical site is not the source of the autonomic storm here.

opts.text: Pupils 4 mm and briskly reactive
opts.rat: Reassuring (no opioid intoxication or anticholinergic effect, no herniation). Document. Note: alcohol withdrawal typically does NOT cause pupillary changes — pupillary dilation suggests sympathomimetic intoxication or anticholinergic toxicity (different differential).

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Chronic alcohol exposure enhances inhibitory GABA-A receptor function AND down-regulates excitatory NMDA glutamate receptors; abrupt cessation removes the GABA enhancement and unmasks NMDA hyperactivity, producing the autonomic storm, tremor, hallucinations, seizures, and DTs of withdrawal
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. The receptor adaptation explains WHY benzodiazepines (GABA agonists) are first-line — they restore the GABAergic tone temporarily while the receptors readapt. The same mechanism explains why barbiturates (phenobarbital — also GABA agonist) work in refractory cases.

opts.text: Withdrawal TIMELINE is highly stereotyped: 6–12 hr early autonomic + tremor; 12–24 hr hallucinations (mostly visual); 12–48 hr generalized tonic-clonic seizures (peak ~24 hr); 48–96 hr DELIRIUM TREMENS — the time since last drink predicts where the patient is in the trajectory
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield framework. The DT window (48–96 hr) is when this patient is presenting. Knowing the timeline lets you anticipate what's coming and what's already passed. Seizure peak already behind us (24 hr mark); DT peak now. Treatment is mostly the same across the stages but the intensity ramps up.

opts.text: THIAMINE must be given BEFORE any glucose in a chronic-alcohol-use patient — glucose without thiamine consumes the last thiamine stores and PRECIPITATES Wernicke encephalopathy (acute, sometimes reversible: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + confusion); if missed, can progress to KORSAKOFF SYNDROME (chronic, often permanent: anterograde amnesia + confabulation)
opts.rat: CORRECT and HIGH-YIELD. Thiamine 100 mg IV is the standard 'banana bag' dose; high-risk patients (active alcohol use disorder, malnutrition, suspected Wernicke) get 500 mg IV TID x 2–3 days. NEVER push D50 IV without thiamine in this population. Wernicke is potentially reversible with thiamine; Korsakoff usually is not.

opts.text: CIWA-Ar protocols use a 10-item scale (max 67) scored q1–4h to drive benzodiazepine dosing; for SEVERE withdrawal (CIWA ≥25 or with DT features), 'symptom-triggered' dosing is replaced by FRONT-LOADING (aggressive scheduled doses early until calm) to break the autonomic cycle
opts.rat: CORRECT. The shift from CIWA-driven PRN to front-loading for severe disease is high-yield. Front-loading: lorazepam 4 mg IV q15min until calm (RASS −1 to 0); document cumulative dose; refractory >30 mg lorazepam (or >100 mg diazepam) in first hour → escalate to phenobarbital or dexmedetomidine. Once stable, transition back to CIWA-driven dosing.

opts.text: Benzodiazepine choice in liver disease: LORAZEPAM is preferred (no active metabolites; predictable kinetics; predominantly hepatic conjugation/renal clearance) over diazepam (long half-life via active metabolites that accumulate in cirrhosis) and chlordiazepoxide (similar issue)
opts.rat: CORRECT. This patient's AST 84 / ALT 56 / albumin 2.8 / INR 1.4 picture is alcoholic liver dysfunction. Lorazepam's pharmacokinetics make it the safer choice. Diazepam's long half-life is sometimes leveraged as a 'self-taper' in patients with preserved liver function — not appropriate here.

opts.text: Magnesium must be replaced BEFORE potassium because hypomagnesemia prevents adequate K+ retention (Mg is required for the Na+/K+ ATPase) — giving KCl without correcting Mg results in continued urinary K+ loss
opts.rat: CORRECT — recurring theme in the curriculum (case-037 HF, case-045 TLS). The Mg-first principle holds whenever both are low. In alcohol withdrawal, Mg correction may also reduce seizure threshold and modestly attenuate autonomic symptoms.

opts.text: HALOPERIDOL is first-line antipsychotic for the agitation and hallucinations in DTs
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and AVOIDED. Haloperidol LOWERS the seizure threshold (problematic in a patient at risk for withdrawal seizures), prolongs QTc (already 480 here — torsades risk), worsens delirium in some patients, and does NOT address the underlying GABA-NMDA dysregulation. BENZODIAZEPINES are the answer. If antipsychotic is absolutely required, low-dose quetiapine has been used as adjunct (off-label, limited evidence).

opts.text: Restraints should be applied early to prevent the patient from harming himself or staff
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — restraints are a LAST resort. They worsen agitation, produce rhabdomyolysis, cause skin breakdown, are associated with cardiovascular events, and are psychologically traumatic. Chemical sedation with benzodiazepines, 1:1 sitter, bed alarm, quiet environment, and patient redirection are the standard. Restraints only with explicit orders, frequent reassessment, and after less-restrictive measures have failed.

opts.text: Ethanol titration protocols (giving the patient measured ETOH IV or PO) are an acceptable modern approach to manage withdrawal
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT. ETOH titration is NOT modern practice — does not address the receptor dysregulation, complicates dosing, no clear benefit over benzodiazepines, complications include hepatic worsening, hyperosmolar states, and reinforcement of the addictive pattern. Benzodiazepine front-loading is the standard.

opts.text: PHENOBARBITAL and DEXMEDETOMIDINE are alternative agents for benzodiazepine-resistant or refractory severe withdrawal — phenobarbital is a GABA agonist with long half-life (smooth taper); dexmedetomidine is an alpha-2 agonist (autonomic + sedation, NO respiratory depression, DOES NOT PREVENT seizures so benzo MUST continue)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the escalation pathway. Phenobarbital load 5–10 mg/kg IV for benzo-refractory cases. Dexmedetomidine for autonomic and agitation control in ICU; widely used as adjunct, NOT monotherapy (does not prevent seizures). Both require closer monitoring (respiratory for phenobarbital, hemodynamics for dexmedetomidine).

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Death / Cardiovascular Collapse r/t severe autonomic storm of DTs (HR 142, BP 184/108, T 102.3, profuse diaphoresis with electrolyte derangements)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. DTs mortality is 5–15% UNTREATED; deaths come from arrhythmia, MI, aspiration, hyperthermia, dehydration, and concurrent medical conditions. Aggressive benzodiazepine front-loading + electrolyte correction + supportive care + ICU monitoring. Stop the storm before complications develop.

opts.text: Risk for Wernicke Encephalopathy r/t chronic alcohol-use thiamine depletion + glucose drift toward hypoglycemia
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Glucose 78 will trigger D50 by reflex on a code call — and giving glucose without thiamine ACCELERATES Wernicke onset. Pre-emptive thiamine 500 mg IV is THE prevention. Wernicke is potentially reversible; Korsakoff is usually permanent. The thiamine-before-glucose rule is non-negotiable.

opts.text: Risk for Withdrawal Seizure r/t ongoing GABA/NMDA dysregulation (DTs phase but seizures can still occur)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Seizure peak is usually 24 hr after last drink (already passed), but seizures can still occur. Adequate benzodiazepine has dual benefit (controls DT and prevents seizure). Have lorazepam 4 mg IV at bedside for breakthrough. Magnesium correction also reduces seizure risk.

opts.text: Risk for Injury / Self-Harm r/t hallucinations, disorientation, and psychomotor agitation
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. 1:1 sitter, bed alarm, low bed position, removal of dangerous items, quiet environment, minimal stimulation. Chemical sedation (benzodiazepines) preferred over physical restraints. The picking at imaginary insects has already produced superficial skin injury; further escalation could be worse.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit / Risk for Recurrence r/t alcohol use disorder being a chronic relapsing disease — discharge planning with MAT, behavioral support, and family education is the prevention bundle
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in acute ranking but THE preventable factor for the next admission. Without a comprehensive long-term plan (naltrexone or acamprosate, behavioral support, AA/SMART Recovery, outpatient counseling, family education, primary care for ongoing addiction medicine), this patient will be back. AUD is a chronic relapsing disease — managed, not 'cured' by a single inpatient stay.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: THIAMINE 500 mg IV (high-dose protocol) TID x 2–3 days, then 100 mg IV/PO daily — given BEFORE any glucose-containing IVF
opts.rat: Non-negotiable. Wernicke prevention is the highest-leverage intervention. High-dose protocol (500 mg TID) for actively at-risk or symptomatic patients; routine 100 mg for prophylaxis. Folate 1 mg, multivitamin, B-vitamin replacement complete the 'banana bag.' NEVER push D50 without thiamine on board.

opts.text: LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV q15 min FRONT-LOADING until CIWA <15 and patient calm (RASS −1 to 0); document total dose
opts.rat: Front-loading protocol for severe withdrawal. The aggressive early dosing breaks the autonomic cycle and prevents progression. Document cumulative dose; reassess CIWA q1h until stable, then q2-4h. Switch to CIWA-driven dosing once stabilized. Patient may need 30+ mg in the first hours.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM sulfate 2 g IV over 1 hr, then 1 g IV q4h until Mg ≥1.8; potassium replacement AFTER Mg corrected; phosphorus replacement once Mg/K corrected
opts.rat: Standard electrolyte sequence. Mg-first principle. The autonomic storm of DTs is associated with significant electrolyte derangement; correcting them reduces arrhythmia and seizure risk. Refeeding-syndrome risk monitoring when nutrition is reintroduced.

opts.text: 1:1 sitter continuous; bed alarm; quiet room with dim lighting; minimize stimulation; reassess for restraint need with each new agitation episode
opts.rat: Standard safety bundle. The 1:1 sitter is the most expensive but most effective single intervention for safety in active DTs. Quiet environment reduces sensory overload that worsens hallucinations and agitation. Chemical sedation preferred over physical restraints.

opts.text: PHENOBARBITAL load 5–10 mg/kg IV (slow over 30 min) for benzodiazepine-resistant DTs (>30 mg lorazepam in first hour without adequate effect)
opts.rat: Escalation for benzo-refractory cases. Phenobarbital is a GABA agonist with long half-life (smooth taper). Watch respiratory depression — usually requires monitored bed (step-down or ICU). Have airway support ready.

opts.text: DEXMEDETOMIDINE infusion 0.2–1.4 mcg/kg/hr for refractory autonomic and agitation control in ICU
opts.rat: ICU-level adjunct. Dexmedetomidine targets the autonomic storm and provides sedation without respiratory depression. CRITICAL CAVEAT: does NOT prevent seizures — benzo must continue concurrently. Alpha-2 effect can cause bradycardia and hypotension; monitor.

opts.text: Acetaminophen 1 g IV/PO q6h scheduled for fever and pain (NOT NSAIDs given liver/renal dysfunction)
opts.rat: Reasonable choice. Acetaminophen at standard dose is acceptable in alcoholic liver dysfunction (max 2 g/day if active alcohol use or significant cirrhosis to reduce hepatotoxicity risk). NSAIDs are problematic (GI bleeding, renal). Avoid opioids where possible (worsen delirium).

opts.text: HALOPERIDOL 5 mg IV q4h for agitation and hallucinations
opts.rat: ⚠️ AVOID in DTs. Lowers seizure threshold (already at risk for withdrawal seizure); prolongs QTc (already 480 — torsades risk); worsens delirium in some patients; does NOT address the GABA-NMDA mechanism. Benzodiazepines are the answer. If antipsychotic absolutely required, low-dose quetiapine has been used as adjunct (limited evidence).

opts.text: Apply 4-point physical restraints immediately for patient and staff safety
opts.rat: ⚠️ Last resort only. Physical restraints worsen agitation, cause rhabdomyolysis, skin injuries, and are associated with cardiovascular events. Chemical sedation with benzodiazepines + 1:1 sitter + quiet environment are the standard. If restraints absolutely necessary, explicit orders + q1h reassessment + frequent attempts at less-restrictive measures + ROM/skin checks q2h are required.

opts.text: Push D50 IV bolus for the glucose of 78 to prevent hypoglycemia
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS in a chronic alcohol-use patient without thiamine on board. Glucose 78 is NOT hypoglycemia (low threshold is <70 mg/dL, or <55 mg/dL with symptoms in most protocols). If it drops further, thiamine 500 mg IV FIRST, then D50. The thiamine-before-glucose rule is the highest-yield NCLEX pharmacology point in addiction medicine.

opts.text: Start ethanol-titration drip (5% ethanol in D5W) to manage withdrawal
opts.rat: Not modern practice. Does not address the GABA-NMDA receptor adaptation; complicates dosing; no clear benefit over benzodiazepines; reinforces the addictive pattern; complications include hepatic worsening and hyperosmolar states. Benzodiazepine front-loading is the standard.

opts.text: Inpatient ADDICTION MEDICINE consult; social work; behavioral health; discuss MAT options (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram); community AA/SMART Recovery; family education
opts.rat: Essential — the prevention bundle. Without comprehensive long-term planning the patient will recur. AUD is a chronic relapsing disease requiring chronic management. Family education on AUD as disease (not moral failing) reduces stigma and supports adherence. Outpatient follow-up arranged before discharge.

opts.text: Maintain wife/family at bedside for orientation, comfort, and witness — even when the patient is hallucinating, familiar faces can ground him
opts.rat: High-leverage nursing intervention. Familiar voices and faces reduce agitation and improve orientation in delirium. Educate the family on what to expect (the hallucinations are not the 'real' patient), how to interact (calm reassurance, no arguing with hallucinations), and self-care during the long stay.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Establish 1:1 sitter at bedside; bed alarm; quiet room with dim lighting; bed in low position; remove all dangerous items from room; continuous cardiac monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety infrastructure. The patient is actively attempting to leave bed and climbing/picking at his skin. None of these interventions require new orders — they are bedside nursing decisions. Safety FIRST before any pharmacology has time to work.

opts.text: Administer THIAMINE 500 mg IV BEFORE any glucose-containing IVF; folate 1 mg IV; multivitamin IV; then magnesium 2 g IV over 1 hr; potassium replacement only after Mg corrected
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Wernicke prevention is non-negotiable. Banana-bag replacement addresses the chronic deficiencies. Mg-first principle for the electrolyte cascade. These can run concurrently with the benzodiazepine.

opts.text: Administer LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV; repeat q15 min FRONT-LOADING until CIWA <15 and RASS −1 to 0; document cumulative dose
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. The benzodiazepine is the disease-modifying therapy for the withdrawal mechanism. Front-loading breaks the autonomic cycle and prevents progression to seizure or cardiovascular collapse. Reassess CIWA q1h.

opts.text: If benzodiazepine-resistant after >30 mg lorazepam in first hour: escalate to PHENOBARBITAL load 5–10 mg/kg IV slow OR transfer to ICU for DEXMEDETOMIDINE drip; continue benzodiazepine concurrently (dexmedetomidine does NOT prevent seizures)
opts.rat: #4 — Escalation. The pathway when benzodiazepines alone are inadequate. Either phenobarbital adjunct or ICU-level dexmedetomidine. CRITICAL caveat about dexmedetomidine and seizures — keep the benzo running.

opts.text: Inpatient addiction medicine consultation, social work, behavioral health; family meeting to explain AUD as a chronic relapsing disease; discharge MAT planning (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram), outpatient counseling, community AA/SMART Recovery; primary care follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Once acute stabilization is in motion, the long-term planning starts. Without it, the patient returns with the next withdrawal episode (or worse). The prevention bundle reduces re-admission and improves long-term outcomes. Nursing leadership: advocate for this consultation BEFORE discharge.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 6 after front-loading (28 mg lorazepam total + dexmedetomidine drip in ICU): CIWA 14, HR 96, BP 142/82, T 100.4°F, no further hallucinations, oriented x2 (knows place again), pulled out his picking, sleeping intermittently
opts.rat: Excellent response to the bundle. Continue lorazepam scheduled or CIWA-driven; wean dexmedetomidine over 24 hr as autonomic storm resolves; continue electrolyte and vitamin replacement; reassess CIWA q2-4h. Plan transition out of ICU when CIWA stable <10 for 24 hr.

opts.text: Day 5: CIWA 4–6 on lorazepam taper, fully oriented, tolerating soft diet, mobilizing with PT, family at bedside, addiction medicine consultation completed, accepting naltrexone 50 mg PO daily plus outpatient counseling referral
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. The shift from acute DT survival to long-term AUD management is the highest-leverage phase. Naltrexone is well-tolerated, reduces cravings, can start before discharge. Behavioral support + community resources + family education complete the bundle.

opts.text: 3-month outpatient follow-up: 95 days sober, attending AA twice weekly, naltrexone adherent, returned to work, wife reports significantly improved relationship; PCP managing transitioned care
opts.rat: Realistic outcome with adequate support. Long-term recovery is possible but not guaranteed; relapse rates remain significant. The combination of MAT + behavioral support + community + family produces the best outcomes. Continued primary care addiction-medicine management.

opts.text: Hour 2: generalized tonic-clonic seizure for 90 sec; lorazepam 4 mg IV given; postictal but stable; immediately transferred to ICU; phenobarbital load 8 mg/kg IV initiated
opts.rat: Withdrawal seizure during active management. The benzo dose was below threshold for adequate prevention. Escalate per protocol; phenobarbital adds GABA effect on top of benzo; continuous EEG to detect subclinical seizures; consider higher target benzo dose. Mortality risk significantly elevated.

opts.text: Hour 4: HR climbs to 168, T 104.5°F, MAP 60 despite fluids; rhabdomyolysis worsening (CK 24,000); ICU intensivist initiates norepinephrine + cooling + continues benzo + dexmedetomidine
opts.rat: DTs progressing to multi-organ stress despite treatment. Hyperthermia + tachycardia + rhabdo + shock physiology requires intensive care. Aggressive cooling, hemodynamic support with vasopressor (cautious IVF given the autonomic storm and risk of pulmonary edema). Continue all withdrawal-management interventions.

opts.text: Day 1: bedside RN observes D50 25 g IV pushed for fingerstick glucose of 58, then notices Wernicke triad develop (horizontal nystagmus + ataxic gait when tried briefly + worsening confusion) — high-dose thiamine 500 mg IV given urgently and continued TID x 5 days
opts.rat: Iatrogenic Wernicke from glucose-before-thiamine — the classic preventable error. Acknowledge, treat aggressively (thiamine high-dose, prolonged duration), recognize that some symptoms may NOT fully reverse if treatment was delayed. Incident report; team education; system change to make thiamine-first the default order set.

opts.text: Hour 3: bedside nurse pushed haloperidol 5 mg IV for agitation (off the standing PRN order from earlier admission); patient develops torsades de pointes 30 min later; ACLS initiated with magnesium 2 g IV, defibrillation, and return of spontaneous circulation
opts.rat: Iatrogenic complication — haloperidol in DT with QTc 480 produces torsades. Acknowledge the contributing factors (already prolonged QT, low Mg, autonomic instability, haloperidol contraindicated in DT). Incident report; team education; remove haloperidol from active orders for this patient; review unit-level standing orders to flag the AUD context.

opts.text: Day 8 ready for discharge: patient declines outpatient addiction medicine + AA + naltrexone, 'doesn't believe' he has an alcohol problem despite the DT episode and family witness
opts.rat: Knowledge deficit / motivational stage. Motivational interviewing approach: meet the patient where he is, acknowledge his perspective, share information without judgment, plant seeds for future change. Refer to outpatient PCP for continued conversation. Provide naltrexone prescription and educational materials even if patient declines AA — the option remains available. Long-term, many patients move through stages of change over months to years.

opts.text: Day 4: minor residual tremor in hands during fine motor tasks but otherwise oriented, calm, eating regular diet, weaning off PO benzodiazepine taper
opts.rat: Expected residual finding during recovery. Mild post-DT tremor can persist for days to weeks; usually resolves fully. Continue taper; physical therapy for fine motor coordination if persistent; document and reassure.

opts.text: Mild post-withdrawal anxiety, low mood, sleep disturbance, and intermittent cravings persisting for 2–6 weeks ('protracted withdrawal' or 'PAWS' — post-acute withdrawal syndrome)
opts.rat: Expected protracted withdrawal pattern affecting many patients in early recovery. Acamprosate is particularly useful for PAWS (reduces protracted symptoms). Behavioral support, sleep hygiene, exercise, and continued MAT manage this phase. Pattern resolves over weeks to months.

title: Acute Variceal Hemorrhage in Decompensated Cirrhosis with Hepatic Encephalopathy
subtitle: Restrictive Transfusion, Octreotide Drip, and Why More Blood Can Make the Bleeding Worse

patient.name: R. Ashworth
patient.code: Full Code (advance directive: full code, transplant candidate evaluation in progress)
patient.admitDate: Today, 2230 (ED arrival via EMS — wife called 911 after he vomited 'a coffee mug full of blood')
patient.room: ED Resus Bay 2 → MICU pending bed

vitals.time: 0030 (CURRENT — post 2 units PRBC + 1 unit FFP + octreotide bolus + IV PPI, on 4 L NC; EGD bands placed)

labs.v: Admission Hgb 6.8 / Hct 21% → post 2 PRBC Hgb 7.2 (slow rise reflects ongoing bleed); baseline 3 months ago 11.8 / 35%
labs.r: Pregnancy/adult Hgb 12–16 men; the post-transfusion trajectory is what matters more than the absolute number

labs.v: 68 K/µL (chronic thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism + cirrhosis)
labs.r: 150–400 K/µL; transfuse if <50 K with active bleeding or planned procedure

labs.v: INR 2.4 / PT 28 / aPTT 38 / Fibrinogen 90 (low) / D-dimer 4.2 (high)
labs.r: INR <1.1; PT 11–13; fibrinogen 200–400; D-dimer <0.5 — the synthetic dysfunction of cirrhosis plus active hemorrhage

labs.n: AST / ALT / Total bilirubin / Albumin / GGT
labs.v: AST 132 / ALT 84 / Total bili 5.2 (direct 3.2) / Albumin 2.4 / GGT 442
labs.r: AST > ALT (alcoholic pattern); elevated bili; low albumin; high GGT — combined hepatocellular and cholestatic with marked synthetic dysfunction

labs.v: 132 µg/dL
labs.r: <60 µg/dL; elevated reflects portal-systemic shunting and reduced hepatic clearance — contributes to but doesn't perfectly correlate with hepatic encephalopathy severity

labs.v: Cr 1.8 (baseline 1.0) / BUN 48 / Na 128 (chronic; was 130 three months ago)
labs.r: Cr 0.7–1.3; BUN 7–20; Na 136–145 — concerning for hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) with GI bleed as the precipitant; chronic hyponatremia is a poor-prognosis marker in cirrhosis

labs.v: Lactate 4.6 mmol/L; ABG pH 7.30 / HCO3 18 / BE −9 — metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion
Glucose 78 / K 3.6 / Mg 1.5 / iCa 0.96 (low — citrate from transfused blood chelates calcium)
labs.r: Mg 1.8–2.4; iCa 1.12–1.30 — transfusion-related hypocalcemia is high-yield and treatable

labs.v: Massive bleeding from 3 columns of Grade 3 esophageal varices with red wale signs; 4 endoscopic bands placed; partial control with ongoing slow ooze noted on withdrawal of scope
labs.r: No varices; intact mucosa

labs.v: Small-to-moderate ascites; recanalized umbilical vein (caput medusae); shrunken nodular liver; splenomegaly 16 cm; no acute cholecystitis or biliary dilation; no obvious portal vein thrombus on this view
labs.r: Normal liver and spleen contour; no ascites

labs.v: Grade 2 (drowsy, oriented x1, slurred speech, ASTERIXIS positive, mild disorientation to place/time)
labs.r: Grade 0–4; Grade 2 = lethargy, asterixis, disoriented to time; Grade 3 = somnolent but rousable; Grade 4 = comatose

labs.v: Class C (10–11 points): bilirubin 5.2 (3 pts), albumin 2.4 (3 pts), INR 2.4 (3 pts before bleed; now elevated further), ascites moderate (2 pts), HE Grade 2 (2 pts)
labs.r: Class A 5–6 best; Class B 7–9; Class C 10–15 worst
<10 mild; 10–19 moderate; 20–29 severe; ≥30 critical — drives transplant priority

labs.v: NADOLOL 40 mg PO daily (primary variceal prophylaxis) NOT TAKEN x 4 weeks (after job loss + spouse separation + relapse to drinking); spironolactone + furosemide for ascites also missed; lactulose and rifaximin previously prescribed but 'didn't like the diarrhea' so stopped 6 months ago
labs.r: Adherence to GDMT in cirrhosis is the major modifiable factor

labs.v: Returned to drinking 4 weeks ago after 6 months sobriety; consuming ~750 mL whiskey/day per wife; last drink 8 hr ago (before vomiting blood)
labs.r: Alcohol relapse is a major precipitant of decompensation in cirrhosis

labs.v: No prior history of GI bleed (this is first); no portal vein thrombosis on prior imaging; no SBP on prior paracentesis 3 months ago (PMN 80, neg cx); no current focal neuro deficit (no stroke); no signs of MI on ECG
labs.r: Excludes alternatives in the differential

nursesNote: 0030 — 58 y/o male with known decompensated alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C, MELD-Na ~28) + chronic hepatitis C (untreated, awaiting transplant evaluation) brought to ED by EMS at 2230 after 'vomiting a coffee mug full of blood' at home. Last well 2 hr ago. PMH significant for 30+ years of heavy alcohol use, hep C diagnosed 5 yr ago, Grade 3 esophageal varices found on screening EGD 18 months ago (treated with band ligation at that time), ascites (3 prior paracenteses), prior hepatic encephalopathy episodes. WAS on optimal medical therapy 6 months ago after his last hospitalization: nadolol 40 mg PO daily for variceal prophylaxis, spironolactone + furosemide for ascites, lactulose + rifaximin for HE prevention, and outpatient hepatology + transplant evaluation. The wife disclosed on directed questioning that he stopped all his medications about 6 months ago because of 'the diarrhea from lactulose,' and then 4 weeks ago after job loss and a separation stressor he returned to heavy daily drinking (~750 mL whiskey/day). Last alcohol intake was 8 hr ago at home. The bleeding tonight started suddenly while he was sitting on the couch — sudden 'rush' of blood from his mouth, then ~4 episodes of large-volume hematemesis (estimated 1–1.5 L total) with melena passed in the ED. On EMS arrival: BP 78/44, HR 132, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6 — combative and disoriented), profoundly pale, cool clammy. Two large-bore peripheral IVs placed by EMS through unburned skin; 1 L NS bolus en route. On our ED arrival, ABCs prioritized: airway threatened by ongoing hematemesis with low GCS — INTUBATED at 2245 by anesthesia (rapid sequence with rocuronium reduced dose given chronic liver; awake fiberoptic considered but standard RSI used given low GCS and emergent airway protection). Two more large-bore IVs placed; A-line. Type and crossmatch (universal donor O-neg given immediately while crossmatch processing). MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL ACTIVATED at 2250: 1:1:1 ratio PRBC:FFP:platelets. So far received 2 units PRBC + 1 unit FFP + 1 unit platelets + cryoprecipitate 10 units (for fibrinogen 90). Resuscitation goal: SBP 90–100 / Hgb 7–8 (NOT >9 — overshoot raises portal pressure and worsens variceal bleeding — a counterintuitive 'less is more' point). OCTREOTIDE 50 mcg IV bolus given at 2255 followed by 50 mcg/hr continuous infusion x 3–5 days (splanchnic vasoconstriction reduces portal pressure and bleeding). PANTOPRAZOLE 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/hr infusion (was empirically started for upper GI bleed pre-EGD; continues for now). CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV given at 2300 (prophylactic antibiotic for SBP prevention — variceal bleed in cirrhosis is a strong indication; reduces mortality and bacterial infection in the next week). Emergent EGD at 0030 by GI: massive bleeding from 3 columns of Grade 3 varices with red wale signs (high-risk for rebleed); 4 endoscopic bands placed; partial hemostasis with ongoing slow ooze. Hepatic encephalopathy Grade 2 (asterixis positive, disoriented to time, slurred speech) — LACTULOSE 30 mL via OG q1h until first BM, then titrate to 2–3 soft BMs/day; RIFAXIMIN 550 mg PO/NG BID when oral access possible. Cr 1.8 from baseline 1.0 — HRS workup initiated (albumin 1 g/kg IV x 2 days, hold diuretics, terlipressin if available or midodrine + octreotide combo if not). MICU bed being prepared. Wife arrived, devastated; daughter en route from out of state. Hepatology and transplant team aware — the relapse to drinking complicates ongoing transplant candidacy (most centers require 6 months of documented sobriety pre-transplant). Goals-of-care discussion at appropriate moment in next 24–48 hr.
providerOrders: ICU admission; INTUBATED status maintained until bleeding controlled and HE clears
Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, end-tidal CO2; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP and ABG draws
Two LARGE-BORE peripheral IVs (≥18 g; preferably 16 g); central line if peripheral access inadequate; consider rapid-infuser/Belmont if MTP continues
Type and crossmatch 4 additional units PRBC + 4 FFP; maintain 2 units PRBC at bedside; activate MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL with 1:1:1 ratio PRBC:FFP:platelets
RESTRICTIVE transfusion target Hgb 7–8 (NOT 10) — over-transfusion in cirrhosis raises portal pressure and WORSENS variceal bleeding (counterintuitive but evidence-based, Villanueva 2013 NEJM)

Replace clotting factors per MTP: FFP for INR >1.5–2.0 with bleeding; CRYOPRECIPITATE 10 units IF fibrinogen <100 mg/dL; PLATELETS to keep >50 K with active bleeding (>100 K if intracranial bleed); CALCIUM gluconate 1–2 g IV per every 4 units PRBC (citrate chelates Ca → hypocalcemia → coagulopathy)

OCTREOTIDE 50 mcg IV bolus, then 50 mcg/hr continuous IV infusion x 3–5 days (splanchnic vasoconstriction; reduces portal pressure and variceal bleeding); equivalent: terlipressin (not US-available historically; now approved as Terlivaz for HRS) or vasopressin + nitroglycerin (older)
PANTOPRAZOLE 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/hr continuous infusion (empiric upper GI bleed coverage; useful if ulcer is the cause; continues until source confirmed and adequately controlled)
CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV daily x 5–7 days for SBP prophylaxis — variceal bleed in cirrhosis is a strong indication; reduces bacterial infection and mortality in the next week
EMERGENT EGD by GI (already performed): endoscopic band ligation (EBL) preferred over sclerotherapy; bands placed on actively bleeding columns + high-risk columns (red wale signs)

If bleeding refractory to EBL + medical therapy: BALLOON TAMPONADE with Sengstaken-Blakemore or Minnesota tube as BRIDGE to definitive therapy (max 24 hr; complications: aspiration, esophageal pressure necrosis, esophageal rupture); endotracheal intubation REQUIRED before placement

TIPS (Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt) if persistent rebleeding after EBL — definitive portal decompression; precipitates worsening HE in 30–50%; contraindicated in severe HE, advanced HF, severe pulm HTN

HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY treatment: LACTULOSE 30 mL via OG q1h until first BM, then titrate to 2–3 soft BMs/day (mechanism: traps NH3 as NH4+ in gut + cathartic effect); RIFAXIMIN 550 mg PO/NG BID (non-absorbable antibiotic, reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria); identify precipitant (this bleed!) and treat
AVOID sedatives, benzodiazepines, opioids during HE (worsen mental status); if needed for procedure, lowest dose with close monitoring

HRS (hepatorenal syndrome) workup: hold all diuretics; ALBUMIN 25% 1 g/kg IV x 2 days then 25 g/day; TERLIPRESSIN 1–2 mg IV q4–6h (or MIDODRINE 7.5 mg PO TID + OCTREOTIDE 100–200 mcg SubQ TID combo if terlipressin unavailable); fluid status reassessment q6h; avoid nephrotoxins
Strict I&O hourly; NG/OG decompression with low intermittent suction (also helps clear blood for HE management); daily weights once stable; UOP target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr
REPEAT CBC, CMP, coags, fibrinogen, ionized Ca q4h initially during active MTP; trend Hgb after each PRBC unit; trend INR/fibrinogen after each FFP/cryo
Resume nadolol once bleeding controlled and stable >24 hr (typically Day 3–5); secondary prevention of variceal rebleeding with combination beta-blocker + serial EBL until variceal eradication
Vitamin K 10 mg IV x 1 (may help INR slightly but most coagulopathy in cirrhosis is synthetic — won't normalize fully); thiamine 100 mg IV daily (alcohol-use precaution); folate 1 mg IV daily
GLUCOSE q4h fingerstick (cirrhotic patients have reduced gluconeogenesis); D5 maintenance once volume status stabilized

Hepatology consultation; TRANSPLANT TEAM consultation — the alcohol relapse complicates candidacy but does not preclude evaluation; most centers require 6 months sobriety + active engagement in treatment; explain to family

Addiction medicine consultation; social work for psychosocial assessment; behavioral health for depression after job/relationship loss; goals-of-care conversation when stable; consider IM Vivitrol if patient can engage; outpatient AA/SMART Recovery; pharmacotherapy (acamprosate well-tolerated in liver disease; naltrexone hepatotoxic — avoid; disulfiram contraindicated)
Family meeting in 24–48 hr to discuss prognosis (Child-Pugh C with active variceal bleeding has significant 1-year mortality ~50%); transplant candidacy timeline; advance directives

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Active large-volume hematemesis (1–1.5 L estimated) with melena and hemorrhagic shock (BP 78/44, HR 132, MAP 55)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — life-threatening hemorrhage. Variceal bleeding has 15–20% mortality per episode. Activate MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL; secure airway (already intubated here for active hematemesis with low GCS); two large-bore IVs; emergent EGD; octreotide drip; SBP-prophylactic antibiotics; resuscitate to MAP ≥65 / SBP 90–100 but DO NOT overshoot.

opts.text: Known Child-Pugh C cirrhosis with Grade 3 varices and NOT taken nadolol x 4 weeks; resumed drinking 4 weeks ago
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — identifies the high-risk physiology AND the precipitants. Primary variceal prophylaxis with nadolol (non-selective beta-blocker) reduces bleeding risk; missing it x 4 weeks loses that protection. Alcohol relapse raises portal pressure and inflammation. Both modifiable — but for now, treat the bleed and address adherence later.

opts.text: Hepatic encephalopathy Grade 2 with asterixis, slurred speech, disorientation, and ammonia 132
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — HE precipitated by the GI bleed. Blood in the gut is a massive nitrogen load; the cirrhotic liver cannot clear it. Treatment: LACTULOSE via OG (since intubated) titrated to 2–3 soft BMs/day; RIFAXIMIN; identify and treat precipitant (the bleed itself). Watch mental status; avoid sedatives/opioids that worsen HE.

opts.text: Coagulopathy: INR 2.4, platelets 68, fibrinogen 90 — all contributing to ongoing bleeding
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the cirrhotic patient has multiple synthetic defects (clotting factor deficiency, thrombocytopenia, decreased fibrinogen). Even without active bleeding, these are below normal. With active hemorrhage, replace via MTP 1:1:1 + CRYOPRECIPITATE for fibrinogen <100; recheck after replacement.

opts.text: Creatinine 1.8 (from baseline 1.0) with hyponatremia 128 and ascites — concerning for hepatorenal syndrome (HRS)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Type 1 HRS in the setting of GI bleed (the precipitant). HRS Type 1: rapidly progressive AKI in cirrhosis with no other identifiable cause, no improvement after 2 days of albumin + diuretic withdrawal. Treatment: albumin 1 g/kg IV x 2 days + terlipressin (or midodrine + octreotide combo); avoid nephrotoxins; the bleed must be controlled.

opts.text: EGD: Grade 3 varices with red wale signs and active bleeding from 3 columns; 4 bands placed with partial control
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms the source and the procedural status. Red wale signs are high-risk markers for rebleeding. 'Partial control' means the next 24–48 hr are critical — rebleed rates are high. Have balloon tamponade and TIPS available as backup; serial monitoring.

opts.text: Ionized calcium 0.96 (low) — citrate from transfused blood chelates calcium
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — transfusion-related hypocalcemia. Citrate in stored blood binds calcium → low ionized Ca → impaired clotting (calcium is factor IV) AND cardiac depression (negative inotropy). Replace with calcium gluconate 1–2 g IV per every 4 units PRBC; check ionized Ca after MTP volumes.

opts.text: Lactate 4.6, pH 7.30, HCO3 18 — shock physiology with metabolic acidosis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — tissue hypoperfusion. Resuscitation goal is to clear the lactate; trend it q2–4h. Persistent or rising lactate after resuscitation suggests ongoing bleeding or other end-organ failure. Bicarbonate not routinely given for lactic acidosis from hypoperfusion (correct the cause).

opts.text: MELD-Na ~28 with Child-Pugh C cirrhosis — high mortality (~50% 1-year for active variceal bleed in Child C)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — prognostic context. The MELD score drives transplant prioritization; ~28 is high priority. Family conversation about prognosis (carefully, when stable); transplant team consultation; goals-of-care discussion. AUD relapse complicates but doesn't preclude transplant evaluation — most centers require 6 months sobriety + engagement in treatment.

opts.text: Wife reports prior nonadherence to multiple medications (nadolol, spironolactone, furosemide, lactulose, rifaximin) — disengagement after job loss and separation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sets up secondary prevention planning. Nonadherence in cirrhosis is the single largest modifiable factor in recurrent decompensation. Address with: simpler regimen, social worker for adherence support, depression treatment, addiction medicine, family support. Disengagement is often a marker of comorbid depression — screen and treat.

opts.text: Temperature 98.4°F
opts.rat: Reassuring — no fever. SBP and bacterial infection remain risks, hence the empiric ceftriaxone. Monitor q4h; new fever would prompt fuller infection workup including diagnostic paracentesis.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no medication restrictions. Document and proceed.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Portal hypertension from cirrhotic obstruction of hepatic sinusoids drives blood through portosystemic collaterals (esophageal, gastric, anorectal, umbilical) — esophageal varices form, become tortuous and thin-walled, and rupture when portal pressure rises (alcohol intake, stress, valsalva, eating large meals); the bleeding worsens portal pressure (volume resuscitation paradox)
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Understanding this leads to the 'restrictive transfusion' principle: more blood → more portal pressure → MORE BLEEDING. Adequate but not over-resuscitation (Hgb 7–8, NOT 10) is the evidence-based approach (Villanueva NEJM 2013).

opts.text: OCTREOTIDE causes SPLANCHNIC VASOCONSTRICTION that reduces portal blood inflow → reduces portal pressure → reduces variceal bleeding; given as 50 mcg IV bolus then 50 mcg/hr infusion x 3–5 days
opts.rat: CORRECT — first-line pharmacology. Octreotide is the workhorse in the US (where terlipressin is now FDA-approved but historically unavailable). Vasopressin + nitroglycerin was the older alternative. Octreotide should be started BEFORE EGD if variceal bleed is suspected; continued x 3–5 days after band ligation.

opts.text: PROPHYLACTIC ANTIBIOTICS (ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily x 5–7 days) for ALL cirrhotic patients with GI bleeding reduce both bacterial infections AND mortality (the bleed temporarily disrupts gut barrier → bacterial translocation → SBP, pneumonia, UTI risk)
opts.rat: CORRECT — strong evidence (meta-analyses show ~25% mortality reduction). Antibiotic choice traditionally was ceftriaxone or quinolones; with rising resistance and concerns, ceftriaxone is widely preferred. Continue for 5–7 days regardless of culture results.

opts.text: RESTRICTIVE TRANSFUSION (Hgb target 7–8) is preferred over liberal (Hgb 10) in cirrhotic GI bleeding because over-transfusion raises portal pressure and WORSENS variceal bleeding — Villanueva NEJM 2013 showed lower mortality with restrictive strategy
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield NCLEX trap. The instinct is 'more blood for the bleeding patient' — and that's wrong in cirrhosis with portal hypertension. Restrict to Hgb 7–8 unless: active hemodynamic instability, severe anemia symptoms, ACS, planned surgery. The same principle applies in other restrictive-transfusion settings (e.g., upper GI bleed without cirrhosis).

opts.text: LACTULOSE works by: (1) acidifying the gut lumen, trapping NH3 as NH4+ which cannot cross into the bloodstream; (2) cathartic effect that removes ammonia-laden contents; goal is 2–3 soft BMs/day, NOT 'as many as possible' (over-titration → diarrhea + hypovolemia)
opts.rat: CORRECT. Many students dose lactulose 'high' to maximize effect, but the right titration is 2–3 soft BMs/day — too many causes dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, and rebound HE. Rifaximin adds non-absorbable antibiotic effect on gut bacteria. Together they are first-line for HE. Identify and treat precipitant (the GI bleed itself is a strong precipitant).

opts.text: HEPATORENAL SYNDROME (HRS) develops in cirrhosis from extreme renal vasoconstriction in response to splanchnic vasodilation; treatment: ALBUMIN 1 g/kg IV x 2 days then 25 g/day + VASOCONSTRICTOR (terlipressin OR midodrine + octreotide combo) + treat the precipitant; AVOID nephrotoxins
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield pharmacology. The 'paradox': giving vasoconstrictor IMPROVES renal flow by raising MAP and reducing renal vasoconstriction (which is the actual pathology). Albumin maintains intravascular volume. Hold diuretics. Type 1 HRS has poor prognosis without liver transplant; Type 2 is more chronic.

opts.text: BALLOON TAMPONADE with Sengstaken-Blakemore or Minnesota tube is a BRIDGE THERAPY for refractory bleeding before TIPS or definitive control; max duration 24 hr; complications include aspiration, esophageal necrosis, and esophageal rupture; ENDOTRACHEAL INTUBATION REQUIRED before placement
opts.rat: CORRECT — last-resort temporizing measure when EBL + octreotide fails to control bleeding. Requires intubated patient (aspiration prevention). The balloons compress varices mechanically. Time-limited use because tissue ischemia and necrosis develop after 12–24 hours. Bridges to definitive therapy (TIPS, second-look EGD, transplant).

opts.text: LIBERAL transfusion to Hgb 10 is the standard goal for any actively bleeding patient regardless of underlying liver disease
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT in cirrhosis (and increasingly in non-cirrhotic upper GI bleed). The Villanueva trial showed restrictive (Hgb 7) reduced mortality and rebleeding compared to liberal (Hgb 9). The over-transfusion raises portal pressure. This is one of the highest-yield modern updates over older textbook teaching.

opts.text: BENZODIAZEPINES are appropriate for the agitation and disorientation of hepatic encephalopathy
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — and harmful. Benzodiazepines WORSEN HE by enhancing GABAergic neurotransmission that contributes to HE pathology. AVOID benzodiazepines and opioids in HE. If sedation absolutely required (procedure, intubation), use lowest dose with close monitoring. Flumazenil (benzo reversal) has been used for benzo-precipitated HE — limited evidence.

opts.text: Resume nadolol immediately on admission for secondary prophylaxis of variceal rebleeding
opts.rat: INCORRECT during acute hemorrhage. Non-selective beta-blockers reduce variceal rebleed risk for LONG-TERM secondary prevention, but should be HELD during active bleeding and shock (drops BP and limits the compensatory tachycardia response). Resume once bleeding controlled and stable >24 hr (typically Day 3–5). Combination with serial EBL until variceal eradication is the gold standard.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Exsanguination / Hemorrhagic Shock r/t active variceal bleeding with coagulopathy (Hgb 6.8, INR 2.4, platelets 68, fibrinogen 90)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The bleed kills the patient if not controlled. MTP 1:1:1; octreotide drip; EGD with EBL; balloon tamponade or TIPS if refractory; restrictive Hgb 7–8 target. Each component of the bundle reduces mortality.

opts.text: Risk for Aspiration / Airway Compromise r/t active hematemesis with hepatic encephalopathy and depressed mental status
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Airway. Aspiration of blood worsens outcomes in any GI bleed, especially in a patient with depressed mental status. ALREADY INTUBATED for protection — keep intubated until bleeding controlled AND mental status improved. Aspiration precautions; NG decompression.

opts.text: Risk for Worsening Hepatic Encephalopathy r/t GI bleed providing massive nitrogen load + cirrhotic liver inability to clear ammonia
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The bleed drives the HE; treating the bleed is the primary HE intervention. Lactulose 30 mL OG q1h until BM then titrate; rifaximin once stable; avoid sedatives/opioids. Monitor mental status; serial ammonia not necessary for treatment (clinical exam more useful). Identify and treat precipitants.

opts.text: Risk for Hepatorenal Syndrome r/t volume depletion + splanchnic vasodilation + alcohol relapse + concurrent insults (Cr 1.8 from 1.0, Na 128)
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Type 1 HRS in this setting has high mortality without liver transplant. Treat: albumin + vasoconstrictor (terlipressin or midodrine+octreotide combo) + hold diuretics + avoid nephrotoxins + treat the precipitating bleed. Reassess Cr q12h; CRRT if refractory.

opts.text: Risk for Bacterial Infection (SBP, pneumonia, UTI, line infection) r/t cirrhotic immunocompromise + GI bleed disrupting gut barrier + intubation + central lines
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in acute ranking but actively addressed by ceftriaxone prophylaxis. SBP prophylaxis with ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily x 5–7 days reduces both infection and mortality in cirrhotic GI bleeding. Surveillance for new fever, source-specific findings (chest, abdomen, urine, lines). VAP precautions per protocol.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: OCTREOTIDE 50 mcg IV bolus, then 50 mcg/hr continuous infusion x 3–5 days
opts.rat: First-line variceal bleeding pharmacology. Splanchnic vasoconstriction reduces portal pressure. Start as soon as variceal bleeding is suspected — do not wait for EGD confirmation. Continue x 3–5 days after EBL. Side effects: nausea, hyperglycemia, mild abdominal discomfort.

opts.text: MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL 1:1:1 ratio PRBC:FFP:platelets; restrictive Hgb target 7–8 (NOT 10); cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100; calcium gluconate per every 4 units PRBC
opts.rat: Standard MTP. The restrictive Hgb target IS the evidence-based call in cirrhotic upper GI bleed — over-transfusion raises portal pressure. Calcium replacement counters citrate-induced hypocalcemia. Trend coagulation markers and adjust component ratios.

opts.text: EMERGENT EGD with endoscopic band ligation (EBL); have balloon tamponade and TIPS available as backup
opts.rat: Definitive procedural therapy. EBL is preferred over sclerotherapy (lower complications, equivalent efficacy). Within 12 hours of presentation is the standard timing. Balloon tamponade as bridge to TIPS if refractory; TIPS for true salvage.

opts.text: CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV daily x 5–7 days for SBP prophylaxis (variceal bleed + cirrhosis indication)
opts.rat: Evidence-based mortality reducer. The bleed disrupts gut barrier → bacterial translocation → SBP risk. Empiric ceftriaxone for all cirrhotic GI bleed. Continue for 5–7 days regardless of culture results.

opts.text: LACTULOSE 30 mL OG q1h until first BM, then titrate to 2–3 soft BMs/day; RIFAXIMIN 550 mg PO/NG BID once OG access established
opts.rat: Hepatic encephalopathy treatment bundle. Lactulose acidifies gut and traps NH3; rifaximin adds non-absorbable antibiotic effect. Target 2–3 soft BMs/day — over-titration → diarrhea → volume depletion → rebound HE. Identify and treat precipitant (the bleed itself).

opts.text: ALBUMIN 25% 1 g/kg IV x 2 days, then 25 g/day; TERLIPRESSIN 1–2 mg IV q4–6h (or midodrine 7.5 mg PO TID + octreotide 100–200 mcg SubQ TID combo if terlipressin unavailable); HOLD diuretics; avoid nephrotoxins
opts.rat: Hepatorenal syndrome treatment. Albumin maintains intravascular volume; vasoconstrictor restores renal perfusion by raising MAP and reducing the splanchnic 'steal' of renal flow. The combination has improved survival in HRS. Liver transplant is definitive for Type 1 HRS.

opts.text: PANTOPRAZOLE 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/hr continuous infusion (empiric upper GI bleed)
opts.rat: Standard empiric coverage for upper GI bleed pre-EGD. Useful if peptic ulcer is the cause; less direct benefit for variceal bleed but continued during active treatment. Transition to oral PPI once bleeding controlled and oral intake possible.

opts.text: Liberal transfusion to Hgb 10 with aggressive crystalloid bolus to restore BP rapidly
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT in cirrhotic variceal bleeding. Over-transfusion raises portal pressure and WORSENS variceal bleeding. Aggressive crystalloid bolus dilutes clotting factors and adds to the same problem. Restrictive Hgb 7–8 target with appropriate component replacement is evidence-based.

opts.text: Lorazepam 2 mg IV PRN agitation for the hepatic encephalopathy confusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ Benzodiazepines WORSEN hepatic encephalopathy and are AVOIDED. The agitation reflects the HE; treating HE (lactulose, rifaximin, identify precipitant) is the answer. If sedation absolutely required for safety or procedure, use lowest possible dose with close monitoring — but not as first-line for HE agitation.

opts.text: Resume nadolol immediately for variceal rebleed prophylaxis
opts.rat: Hold during acute bleeding and shock — beta-blocker drops BP and blunts compensatory tachycardia. Resume once bleeding controlled and patient stable for >24 hr (typically Day 3–5). Long-term secondary prevention is the combination of beta-blocker + serial EBL until variceal eradication.

opts.text: Naltrexone 50 mg PO daily started in hospital for the alcohol use disorder
opts.rat: ⚠️ Naltrexone is HEPATOTOXIC and CONTRAINDICATED in active liver disease (especially decompensated cirrhosis with elevated AST/ALT/bilirubin). For AUD in cirrhosis: ACAMPROSATE 666 mg PO TID is well-tolerated. Disulfiram contraindicated (hepatotoxic). Behavioral support + AA/SMART Recovery + addiction medicine consultation.

opts.text: Hepatology and transplant team consultation; addiction medicine consultation; family meeting in 24–48 hr to discuss prognosis and transplant candidacy; goals-of-care discussion
opts.rat: Multidisciplinary coordination. Child-Pugh C with active variceal bleed has ~50% 1-yr mortality without transplant. AUD relapse complicates but does not preclude evaluation — most centers require 6 months sobriety + active engagement. Honest, compassionate family communication; advance directive discussion.

opts.text: Strict NPO with NG/OG decompression with low intermittent suction (also helps clear blood for HE management); daily weights once stable; aspiration precautions
opts.rat: Bedside nursing essentials. NG/OG decompression reduces aspiration risk and clears the nitrogen-laden blood from the gut (helps HE). Daily weights track fluid balance in a patient with cirrhotic third-spacing. Aspiration precautions throughout.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: ACTIVATE MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL (1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets, with cryo for fibrinogen <100 and calcium gluconate per 4 units PRBC); two large-bore IVs; type-and-crossmatch + O-neg until matched; arterial line
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The bleed is the imminent killer. MTP gives the team the blood products and the structure to deliver them. Type-and-crossmatch takes time; O-neg covers the gap. Arterial line for beat-to-beat hemodynamic monitoring.

opts.text: OCTREOTIDE 50 mcg IV bolus then 50 mcg/hr infusion x 3–5 days; CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV; PANTOPRAZOLE 80 mg IV then 8 mg/hr infusion (start BEFORE EGD if variceal suspected)
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Octreotide reduces portal pressure pharmacologically while waiting for definitive EGD. Ceftriaxone is the mortality-reducing prophylaxis. Pantoprazole covers the empiric upper GI bleed pre-EGD. All can run concurrently with the MTP.

opts.text: Emergent EGD by GI within 12 hr (ideally within 6 hr) for endoscopic band ligation; have balloon tamponade tray ready as backup
opts.rat: #3 — Definitive intervention. EBL is preferred procedural therapy. Timing matters — earlier intervention reduces mortality but the patient must first be adequately resuscitated and the airway secured. Balloon tamponade ready as rescue bridge.

opts.text: Hepatic encephalopathy bundle: LACTULOSE 30 mL OG q1h until first BM, then titrate; RIFAXIMIN 550 mg PO/NG BID; AVOID benzodiazepines and opioids; serial mental status assessments
opts.rat: #4 — Concurrent with hemorrhage management. The HE is driven by the bleed (massive nitrogen load); treating both simultaneously is essential. Lactulose via OG since intubated. Identify and treat any additional precipitants (infection, electrolyte imbalance).
