opts.text: Hepatology + transplant team + addiction medicine + social work consultations; family meeting in 24–48 hr to discuss prognosis, transplant candidacy, alcohol use disorder management, and goals of care
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Multidisciplinary care plan. The transplant candidacy is the long-term hope; the AUD treatment is the prerequisite. Family meeting once stabilized — honest, compassionate, with all relevant specialists. Advance directives reviewed.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1 morning: BP 118/72, HR 92, lactate 2.0, Hgb stable at 7.6 over last 8 hr without additional transfusion; mental status improving (oriented x2, asterixis minimal, follows commands)
opts.rat: Excellent response to the bundle. Hemodynamic and lab stability + improving mental status indicates bleeding controlled and HE responding. Continue octreotide x 5 days; complete ceftriaxone course; titrate lactulose to 2–3 soft BMs/day; plan extubation when fully oriented and protecting airway.

opts.text: Day 5: extubated, transitioned to oral lactulose + rifaximin, eating soft diet, ambulating with PT, nadolol resumed at 20 mg PO daily (will titrate up), repeat EGD scheduled for surveillance band ligation in 2 weeks; hepatology + addiction medicine + social work coordinating discharge plan
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. The long-term management is now the focus: serial EBL until variceal eradication, beta-blocker secondary prevention, AUD treatment, transplant evaluation. Daily monitoring of mental status, BMs, ascites, weights. Discharge planning includes follow-up in 1 week.

opts.text: 3-month outpatient follow-up: 90 days sober (with acamprosate + AA + counseling), 3 of 4 planned EBL sessions completed with variceal grade reduced to 1, nadolol at goal dose 40 mg PO daily, no rebleed episodes, MELD-Na improved to 22, formal transplant evaluation in progress
opts.rat: Realistic outcome with full engagement. Sobriety + medical optimization + transplant evaluation = best-case trajectory. Many patients achieve transplant; some achieve compensated cirrhosis without transplant. The multidisciplinary coordination is what produces this outcome.

opts.text: Hour 6: brisk rebleed with new hematemesis 500 mL; BP drops to 76/40; Hgb 5.8; ongoing transfusion; balloon tamponade tube placed as bridge to repeat EGD
opts.rat: Variceal rebleed — high mortality. Continue MTP, place Sengstaken-Blakemore (intubated already), repeat EGD within hours, transition to TIPS if EBL fails again. Have surgical or IR teams aware. Each rebleed compounds mortality.

opts.text: Day 2: Cr climbs from 1.8 to 3.4 despite albumin + terlipressin + hold diuretics; UOP 12 mL/hr; metabolic acidosis worsening — Type 1 HRS progressing
opts.rat: HRS-AKI progressing despite medical therapy. Continue albumin + terlipressin combo; reassess nephrotoxin exposure; consider CRRT (renal replacement therapy as bridge); expedite transplant evaluation. Type 1 HRS without transplant has very high mortality.

opts.text: Day 3: temperature 102.4°F, new abdominal pain, diagnostic paracentesis shows ascitic PMN 380 cells/µL — SBP develops
opts.rat: SBP despite prophylactic ceftriaxone. Switch to broader empiric coverage (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem if hospital-acquired) until culture and sensitivity guide narrowing. ALBUMIN 1.5 g/kg IV on Day 1 + 1 g/kg on Day 3 of SBP reduces mortality and HRS. Continue full SBP treatment course.

opts.text: Hour 18: bedside RN attempts to give lorazepam 2 mg IV for 'agitation' despite the HE-AVOID note in chart; patient becomes more obtunded; team intervenes, flumazenil 0.2 mg IV given empirically, education delivered
opts.rat: Iatrogenic worsening of HE from benzodiazepine — preventable error. The 'AVOID benzodiazepines' rule in HE must be hard-coded into orders and bedside teaching. Flumazenil has been used for benzo-precipitated HE (limited evidence). Incident report; system change to flag the rule; team education.

opts.text: Day 4: new pleural effusion right side; diagnostic thoracentesis confirms hepatic hydrothorax (transudate, no infection); thoracic catheter drains 1.4 L; consider TIPS to address the underlying portal HT
opts.rat: Hepatic hydrothorax is a known complication of cirrhosis. Repeated thoracenteses provide symptomatic relief but are not sustainable; TIPS or transplant is the definitive intervention. Watch for spontaneous bacterial empyema (SBE) — similar to SBP but in pleural fluid; prophylactic and treatment antibiotics.

opts.text: Day 8: patient declines hospice discussion when 6-month sobriety contract for transplant is reviewed; says he's 'not sure if he can do this'
opts.rat: Motivational stage challenge. Meeting the patient where he is — depression after job/relationship loss is contributing. Behavioral health for treatment of comorbid depression; addiction medicine for transparent discussion of options; motivational interviewing approach. The transplant decision is staged — engagement now, formal decision later. Some patients are not transplant candidates due to inability to engage in pre-transplant requirements; honest framing of options including comfort-focused care.

opts.text: Persistent mild ascites and 1+ lower extremity edema on discharge despite optimization
opts.rat: Expected residual finding in cirrhosis. Sodium restriction <2 g/day + spironolactone + furosemide combination is the chronic management. Outpatient hepatology for ongoing adjustment; serial paracenteses if symptomatic; TIPS or transplant as definitive interventions.

title: Postpartum Hemorrhage from Uterine Atony After Prolonged Induction
subtitle: Fundal Massage First, Pitocin-Methergine-Hemabate-Cytotec Ladder, and Why You Don't Give Methergine to a Hypertensive Mom

patient.name: A. Delacruz
patient.admitDate: Yesterday, 0530 (admitted for induction at 41+2 weeks for postdates; SVD at 0412 today after 22 hr labor)
patient.room: Mother-Baby 6, Bed A (just transferred from L&D Room 3 at 0530)

vitals.time: 0545 (CURRENT — RN called to bedside for 'big gush' of blood)

labs.v: Delivery EBL 350 mL → 30-min postpartum check 150 mL → current gush 600 mL on pad/chux/floor → cumulative ~1100 mL in first 90 min postpartum
labs.r: Postpartum hemorrhage = >1000 mL EBL OR any blood loss with signs of hypovolemia after birth (ACOG reVITALize 2014); old SVD cutoff was 500 mL

labs.n: Fundal assessment (most recent — at the gush)
labs.v: Fundus BOGGY, deviated to the right, 2 finger-breadths ABOVE umbilicus; uterus not firm with light palpation; large clots expressed with massage
labs.r: Firm, midline, at or below umbilicus immediately postpartum, descending ~1 finger-breadth per day; boggy = atonic and the #1 cause of PPH (~80% of early PPH)

labs.v: Lochia rubra — heavy with clots (saturating peripad in <15 min); chux soaked
labs.r: Lochia rubra normal first 3 days but should be moderate, not heavy; saturating a peripad in <1 hr is a red flag, in <15 min is hemorrhage

labs.v: 2nd-degree midline laceration repaired at delivery; perineum intact, no expanding hematoma, no bright red trickle from the repair site
labs.r: Rules out laceration as primary source — the bleeding is from above (uterine), not below (perineal)

labs.v: Bladder distended on palpation (suprapubic dullness, displacing fundus to right); patient has not voided since delivery 90 min ago; no Foley in place
labs.r: Empty bladder allows uterus to contract effectively; a full bladder is a treatable contributor to atony and uterine displacement

labs.v: Admission Hgb 11.8 / Hct 35% → current (drawn at 0545) Hgb 8.4 / Hct 25%
labs.r: Adult female 12–16 g/dL Hgb / 36–48% Hct; postpartum Hgb typically drops 1–1.5 g/dL from delivery itself — a drop of 3.4 g/dL is consistent with significant blood loss

labs.v: Plt 188 K/µL / INR 1.1 / Fibrinogen 280 mg/dL
labs.r: Plt 150–400; INR <1.1; pregnancy fibrinogen normally elevated (300–600); fibrinogen <200 in PPH is associated with severe hemorrhage and DIC risk

labs.v: Blood type O+, antibody screen negative (on admission); 2 units PRBC crossmatched and held in blood bank per L&D protocol
labs.r: Standard for any laboring patient; allows rapid release of crossmatched product

labs.v: G3P3003 (this delivery); 22-hr induction with oxytocin (received 18 hr of pitocin augmentation), prolonged 2nd stage 3.5 hr, macrosomic infant 4280 g (9 lb 7 oz), 2nd-degree laceration. Previous deliveries: 2 prior SVDs, one with PPH requiring 1 unit PRBC in 2021.
labs.r: Major PPH risk factors present: prolonged labor + oxytocin exposure (receptor downregulation), macrosomia (overdistended uterus), grand multiparity (this is 3rd), prior PPH (recurrence risk ~3x)

labs.v: Gestational hypertension diagnosed at 36 weeks (BPs 140s–150s/90s); on labetalol 200 mg PO BID during pregnancy; NO preeclampsia (no proteinuria, normal labs antepartum); asthma (well-controlled, albuterol PRN)
labs.r: Hypertension history is CRITICAL for uterotonic selection — methylergonovine (Methergine) is CONTRAINDICATED in hypertension; carboprost (Hemabate) is CONTRAINDICATED in asthma

labs.v: Pale, mildly diaphoretic, anxious but alert and oriented x3; cap refill 3 sec; cool fingertips; reports feeling 'shaky and like I'm going to pass out'
labs.r: Class 2 hemorrhagic shock (15–30% blood volume loss) — tachycardia, narrowed pulse pressure, anxiety; the patient's compensatory mechanisms are working but failing — early signs require immediate action

labs.v: Term male, 4280 g, Apgar 8/9, breastfeeding within first hour, currently skin-to-skin with father who is bedside
labs.r: Newborn stable — separate the maternal emergency from infant care; father can continue skin-to-skin while team manages mom

nursesNote: 0545 — 34 y/o G3P3003 90 min postpartum from SVD of macrosomic male infant (4280 g) at 0412 after 22-hr induction (postdates 41+2 wk). Labor course: cervidil x 1, oxytocin augmentation x 18 hr, AROM at 6 cm, prolonged 2nd stage 3.5 hr, 2nd-degree midline laceration repaired at delivery, placenta delivered intact at 0418 with cord traction (3 vessel, examined complete by MD). Risk factors stacking for PPH: prolonged oxytocin exposure → uterine receptor downregulation, macrosomic infant → overdistended uterus, grand multiparity (G3P3), prior PPH with 2nd delivery (1 unit PRBC in 2021). On admission to Mother-Baby at 0500 — fundus firm midline at umbilicus, lochia moderate rubra, VS HR 104/BP 112/68 (mild tachycardia attributed to labor exhaustion). I went into the room at 0545 in response to call light from the partner — found patient with 'sudden gush' of blood, chux soaked through to bed, peripad saturated, large clots on the floor (~600 mL visible). Fundus on palpation BOGGY, deviated to the right, 2 finger-breadths above umbilicus; uterus not firm with light palpation; large clots expressed during fundal massage. Bladder palpably distended (suprapubic dullness, displacing the uterus rightward). VS HR 128/BP 92/54/RR 26/SpO2 96% — narrowed pulse pressure, mild tachypnea, patient pale and diaphoretic reporting 'shaky.' Initiated bimanual fundal massage with one hand on fundus and one hand suprapubically, expressing additional clots — fundus partially firms but rapidly relaxes again. Called for help (called rapid response/OB team), second RN entered. Asked partner to continue skin-to-skin with infant in the corner of the room so team has bedside access. OB resident en route. Existing PIV (18g R forearm) flushed and patent; second 16g PIV being placed in L AC. Labs sent: stat CBC, type-and-screen confirmation, coags, fibrinogen, comprehensive metabolic panel. Oxytocin 10 units IM given to thigh while second IV being placed; oxytocin 30 units in 500 mL LR running at high rate via the existing PIV. Foley catheter placement initiated by second RN to empty the distended bladder (treatable contributor). Awaiting OB at bedside for medication ladder decision — METHERGINE IS CONTRAINDICATED because of her gestational hypertension history (still on labetalol); HEMABATE IS CONTRAINDICATED because of asthma; MISOPROSTOL (Cytotec) 800–1000 mcg PR is the safe second-line uterotonic given her contraindications. Crossmatched 2 units PRBC in blood bank — request release. Have planned: if uterotonic ladder fails → intrauterine balloon tamponade (Bakri) → if still bleeding → uterine artery embolization (IR available) → laparotomy with B-Lynch suture or hysterectomy as last resort. Family aware and supported; chaplain available if needed. Will reassess fundus, VS, and EBL q5 min until stabilized.
providerOrders: ACTIVATE PPH PROTOCOL Stage 2 (cumulative EBL >1000 mL with abnormal VS); notify OB attending and anesthesia; have 2 RNs at bedside
Two large-bore IVs (≥18 g; one already in, second 16g being placed); LR wide-open 1–2 L bolus then reassess; do NOT use only D5W or hypotonic fluids for resuscitation

Release 2 units crossmatched PRBC from blood bank; type-specific can be substituted O-negative if delay; trend Hgb after each unit; activate MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL (1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets) if continued bleeding >1500 mL or hemodynamic instability persists

BIMANUAL FUNDAL MASSAGE — first-line, ALWAYS — one hand intravaginally compressing anterior uterine wall, the other hand suprapubically compressing posterior wall (or vigorous external fundal massage with bladder emptied); continue while pharmacologic interventions are being prepared
EMPTY THE BLADDER — straight-cath or insert Foley; distended bladder displaces uterus and prevents effective contraction (treatable contributor often overlooked)
UTEROTONIC LADDER — adapt to contraindications:
Step 1: OXYTOCIN (Pitocin) 10 units IM (or 10 units IV bolus diluted) PLUS 20–40 units in 500–1000 mL LR/NS infused at high rate; first-line for ALL patients with PPH
Step 2 (if oxytocin inadequate): METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM q2–4h — ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED in HYPERTENSION (including gestational HTN/preeclampsia) — DO NOT GIVE TO THIS PATIENT
Step 3 (if Methergine contraindicated or inadequate): CARBOPROST (Hemabate) 250 mcg IM q15–90 min, max 8 doses — ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED in ASTHMA — DO NOT GIVE TO THIS PATIENT

Step 4 (preferred for this patient given HTN + asthma contraindications): MISOPROSTOL (Cytotec) 800–1000 mcg per RECTUM single dose (off-label but widely used and recommended by ACOG/WHO for PPH); side effects: fever, shivering, transient
Adjunct: TRANEXAMIC ACID (TXA) 1 g IV over 10 min within 3 hr of PPH onset; repeat dose at 30 min if bleeding continues (WOMAN trial — mortality benefit when given early)
If uterotonic ladder fails to control bleeding within 15–30 min: INTRAUTERINE BALLOON TAMPONADE with Bakri balloon (300–500 mL saline fill); transfer to L&D for OR availability

If balloon tamponade fails: UTERINE ARTERY EMBOLIZATION by IR if available and patient stable, OR SURGICAL MANAGEMENT — exploratory laparotomy with B-Lynch compression suture, uterine artery ligation (O'Leary stitch), or hysterectomy as last resort to save life
Continuous cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry, BP q5 min initially during active hemorrhage; arterial line if persistent instability
Strict I&O with Foley to gravity; UOP target ≥30 mL/hr (≥0.5 mL/kg/hr); falling UOP is early sign of hypoperfusion
Repeat CBC, coags, fibrinogen after every 2 units PRBC during MTP; fibrinogen <200 mg/dL → CRYOPRECIPITATE 10 units; platelets <50 K with bleeding → platelet transfusion
Keep patient WARM — hypothermia worsens coagulopathy (lethal triad: hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy); use warmed fluids and forced-air warmer

Newborn care continues uninterrupted — partner can do skin-to-skin in corner of room or step out to nursery; breastfeeding postponed until mom stable; pumping can be initiated later to maintain supply if separated
Anesthesia bedside for airway/IV access support if escalation; OR availability confirmed; second OB attending notified
Family communication: keep partner informed in calm specific language; offer chaplain/social work; document next of kin for consent if surgical intervention needed

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Cumulative EBL ~1100 mL in first 90 minutes postpartum with active ongoing bleeding (saturating peripad in <15 min, clots on floor and chux)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — meets ACOG reVITALize definition of postpartum hemorrhage (>1000 mL OR any loss with signs of hypovolemia). Quantitative blood loss measurement is now standard — weigh pads and chux (1 g = 1 mL) for accuracy; visual estimation underestimates by 30–50%.

opts.text: Fundus BOGGY, deviated to the right, 2 finger-breadths above umbilicus, large clots expressed with massage
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — identifies UTERINE ATONY as the cause (the #1 cause of early PPH, ~80% of cases). Boggy = not contracted = not occluding the spiral arteries at the placental site → continued hemorrhage. The deviation to the right + above umbilicus suggests a contributing distended bladder.

opts.text: Bladder distended, suprapubic dullness on palpation, no void since delivery 90 min ago, no Foley in place
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — treatable contributor to atony. A full bladder mechanically displaces and prevents uterine contraction. Empty bladder via straight cath or Foley as part of the very first interventions (alongside fundal massage and oxytocin).

opts.text: Vital signs: HR 128, BP 92/54 (narrowed pulse pressure), RR 26, mild diaphoresis, pale, anxious — Class 2 hemorrhagic shock
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Class 2 hemorrhagic shock (15–30% blood volume loss). Narrowed pulse pressure is an early shock sign — diastolic rising while systolic falling. Tachycardia and tachypnea are compensatory. The patient is in early but reversible shock — act NOW before progression to Class 3 (hypotension, oliguria, confusion).

opts.text: Hgb dropped from 11.8 to 8.4 g/dL (3.4 g/dL drop) — concerning for significant blood loss exceeding visible EBL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — labs confirming clinical picture. Expected postpartum drop is 1–1.5 g/dL from delivery itself; the additional 2 g/dL suggests ~600–1000 mL additional loss beyond visible. The lab confirms but does not replace clinical judgment — treat the patient, not the number.

opts.text: Risk factor stack: prolonged 22-hr induction with 18 hr of oxytocin, macrosomic infant 4280 g, grand multiparity G3P3, prior PPH
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — risk-stratification context. Prolonged oxytocin exposure causes receptor downregulation, making the post-delivery uterus less responsive to its own oxytocin. Macrosomia and grand multiparity overdistend the uterus. Prior PPH carries ~3x recurrence risk. Multiple risk factors should have prompted active management of the 3rd stage and bedside oxytocin already running.

opts.text: Gestational hypertension on labetalol — methylergonovine (Methergine) is CONTRAINDICATED
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — drug selection safety. Methergine causes intense vasoconstriction → severe hypertension and risk of stroke, MI, seizure in patients with HTN, preeclampsia, eclampsia. Recognize this BEFORE the resident gives an order — RN responsibility to flag and refuse if needed.

opts.text: Asthma — carboprost (Hemabate) is CONTRAINDICATED
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — drug selection safety. Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) is a potent bronchoconstrictor → severe bronchospasm in asthmatics. Like Methergine, this is an absolute contraindication that the RN must catch in real time. With both Methergine and Hemabate off the table, MISOPROSTOL (Cytotec) 800–1000 mcg PR is the next uterotonic.

opts.text: Perineum intact with 2nd-degree laceration repaired, no expanding hematoma, no bleeding from repair site
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rules out the second of the '4 Ts' (Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin). Trauma (lacerations/hematoma) is the 2nd most common cause of PPH. Confirming the perineum is dry localizes the bleed to the uterus, supporting the atony diagnosis and the planned interventions.

opts.text: Newborn stable, skin-to-skin with father in the corner
opts.rat: Not a priority cue — reassuring. Continue skin-to-skin with the partner so the team has bedside access for mom. The infant care does not need RN attention right now; another RN or pediatric nurse can take over if escalation requires it.

opts.text: Temperature 99.0°F
opts.rat: Within expected range immediately postpartum (mild temperature elevation from labor work and fluid shifts). Endometritis would be a later concern (>24 hr postpartum, with foul lochia). Not contributing to the current emergency.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no drug allergies. The contraindications here are NOT allergies but pharmacologic safety issues (HTN, asthma). Document and proceed with the safer uterotonic alternatives.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: UTERINE ATONY is the failure of the postpartum myometrium to contract effectively after placental separation, leaving the open spiral arteries at the placental site bleeding freely — uterine contraction is the primary mechanism of postpartum hemostasis (not platelets/clotting)
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Pregnant uterus receives ~600–700 mL blood/min near term; without rapid contraction after placental separation, exsanguination is possible within minutes. Fundal massage + uterotonics restore tone → mechanical hemostasis. This is why TONE is the #1 of the '4 Ts' (Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin).

opts.text: Prolonged OXYTOCIN exposure during 18-hr induction causes downregulation of myometrial oxytocin receptors → the postpartum uterus is less responsive to its own endogenous oxytocin AND less responsive to exogenous pitocin → atony risk increases with duration of induction
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield pharmacology. This explains why prolonged inductions/augmentations carry PPH risk. Doesn't mean pitocin is wrong as first-line treatment, but it may be insufficient alone — escalate to other uterotonics quickly. Also explains why active management of the 3rd stage (10 units IM/IV at delivery of anterior shoulder) is standard in induced labors.

opts.text: Overdistension of the uterus (macrosomia, multiple gestation, polyhydramnios, grand multiparity) stretches the myometrium and impairs its ability to contract after delivery → atony risk
opts.rat: CORRECT — this patient has both macrosomia (4280 g) AND grand multiparity. Together, they make atony likely. Active management of the 3rd stage, bedside additional uterotonics, vigilant fundal checks q15 min for first 2 hr postpartum, and quantitative blood loss are the standard responses to known risk factors.

opts.text: METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) is a potent vasoconstrictor that causes hypertension; CONTRAINDICATED in HTN, preeclampsia, eclampsia, Raynaud's; risk of stroke, MI, seizure if given to a hypertensive patient
opts.rat: CORRECT — critical safety knowledge. This patient has gestational HTN on labetalol; Methergine is absolutely contraindicated. RN must recognize this and refuse to administer even if ordered; clarify with provider and offer safe alternative (Misoprostol).

opts.text: CARBOPROST (Hemabate, 15-methyl PGF2α) is a potent bronchoconstrictor; CONTRAINDICATED in ASTHMA; also relative contraindications in active hepatic/renal/cardiac disease
opts.rat: CORRECT — critical safety knowledge. The asthmatic with PPH gets Misoprostol, not Hemabate. The bronchospasm from Hemabate in an asthmatic can be severe. RN must catch this in real time; do not assume the ordering provider has cross-referenced the PMH.

opts.text: MISOPROSTOL (Cytotec) is a synthetic PGE1 analog; given 800–1000 mcg PR for PPH; side effects are fever, shivering, GI upset; SAFE in HTN and asthma — the right choice when Methergine and Hemabate are contraindicated
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield. Misoprostol is the safe uterotonic option when both Methergine and Hemabate are off the table. Rectal route bypasses the often hyperemetic postpartum patient. ACOG endorses; WHO uses globally as both prevention and treatment of PPH. The fever side effect is benign and self-limited.

opts.text: TRANEXAMIC ACID (TXA) is an antifibrinolytic that prevents clot breakdown; given 1 g IV over 10 min within 3 hr of PPH onset reduces death from bleeding (WOMAN trial 2017, ~31% reduction); not a uterotonic — works on clot stabilization
opts.rat: CORRECT — relatively newer addition to the PPH bundle. Time-sensitive (within 3 hr); a second 1 g dose can be given at 30 min if bleeding continues. Should be given concurrently with uterotonics and resuscitation, not in lieu of them.

opts.text: Bimanual fundal massage compresses the bleeding placental site directly through the uterine walls — mechanical hemostasis when pharmacology is not yet effective; ALWAYS the first action, continued until uterotonics take effect
opts.rat: CORRECT — the foundational intervention. Bimanual: one hand intravaginally as a fist in the anterior fornix compressing the anterior wall, the other hand suprapubically compressing the posterior wall — squeezes the uterus between the hands. External vigorous fundal massage with empty bladder is the simpler version. Continue while drugs are being drawn up and given.

opts.text: Trendelenburg position (legs above heart) is the preferred position for hemorrhagic shock to improve cerebral perfusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ Outdated teaching. Trendelenburg has been shown NOT to improve hemodynamics meaningfully and may impair respiratory mechanics + raise ICP. Modern recommendation is supine or modified Trendelenburg (head flat) with passive leg raise as a transient bridge. Focus on volume resuscitation and source control, not position.

opts.text: Withholding oxytocin until obstetrician arrives at bedside to make the clinical decision
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong — delays care. Oxytocin is the first-line uterotonic and is standard for any postpartum bleed beyond physiologic — it is on standing PPH protocols and within RN scope to initiate per protocol. Notify the provider while starting, not before.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Ineffective Tissue Perfusion / Risk for Hypovolemic Shock r/t ongoing uterine hemorrhage with EBL >1000 mL and Class 2 shock signs (HR 128, narrowed pulse pressure, pallor, diaphoresis)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The hemorrhage is the imminent killer. Source control (fundal massage + uterotonic ladder) AND volume resuscitation (LR bolus, PRBC release) must happen simultaneously. Every minute of ongoing bleeding compounds risk of Class 3/4 shock and DIC.

opts.text: Risk for Continued Hemorrhage r/t Uterine Atony from oxytocin receptor downregulation + uterine overdistension + bladder distention preventing contraction
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Treating the cause is the only way to stop the bleed. Bimanual fundal massage + empty the bladder + uterotonic ladder (oxytocin → misoprostol given her HTN/asthma contraindications) → Bakri balloon → surgical management if refractory. Each step nested in time.

opts.text: Risk for Medication Adverse Event r/t Methergine contraindication (HTN) and Hemabate contraindication (asthma) requiring careful uterotonic selection
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The wrong drug will harm this patient. RN responsibility to recognize and refuse the contraindicated agents in real time. Methergine in HTN → stroke/MI risk; Hemabate in asthma → severe bronchospasm. Communicate clearly with provider and propose misoprostol.

opts.text: Risk for Coagulopathy / DIC r/t large-volume blood loss with hemodilution and consumption of clotting factors; fibrinogen 280 currently adequate but trending matters
opts.rat: #4 — Significant if bleeding continues. PPH-associated DIC is a late finding; fibrinogen <200 is a marker of severe hemorrhage. MTP 1:1:1 ratio + cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <200 + platelets for <50 K. Send labs after every 2 units PRBC.

opts.text: Anxiety / Compromised Maternal-Newborn Bonding r/t emergency separating mother from newborn during first hour
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in acute ranking but actively addressed. Partner doing skin-to-skin maintains infant thermoregulation, bonding, and breastfeeding readiness. Once mom is stable, restore mother-infant contact; pumping can be initiated later to maintain supply if breastfeeding is delayed. Family communication and reassurance throughout.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Bimanual fundal massage; empty the bladder via straight-cath or Foley insertion
opts.rat: First-line, always — mechanical hemostasis and removal of a treatable contributor. Continue massage while drugs are being drawn up; the Foley both empties the bladder and allows ongoing UOP monitoring as a marker of perfusion.

opts.text: Oxytocin (Pitocin) 10 units IM and 20–40 units in 500–1000 mL LR/NS at high rate via large-bore PIV
opts.rat: First-line uterotonic for ALL PPH regardless of cause. The IM dose gives rapid onset while the IV infusion provides sustained effect. Avoid undiluted rapid IV push (can cause hypotension).

opts.text: Methylergonovine (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM as second-line uterotonic
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED — patient has gestational HTN on labetalol. Methergine is a potent vasoconstrictor; gives risk of severe HTN, stroke, MI, seizure in HTN patients. RN must recognize and refuse even if ordered; clarify with provider.

opts.text: Carboprost (Hemabate) 250 mcg IM as second-line uterotonic
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED — patient has asthma. Hemabate is a potent bronchoconstrictor; risk of severe bronchospasm. Like Methergine, this is an absolute contraindication that the RN must catch in real time.

opts.text: Misoprostol (Cytotec) 800–1000 mcg per rectum as second-line uterotonic
opts.rat: The safe second-line uterotonic for this patient given Methergine (HTN) and Hemabate (asthma) contraindications. PR route bypasses postpartum nausea. Self-limited fever and shivering are common; benign.

opts.text: Tranexamic acid (TXA) 1 g IV over 10 min, repeat dose at 30 min if bleeding continues
opts.rat: WOMAN trial (2017) showed mortality benefit if given within 3 hr of PPH onset. Adjunct to uterotonics, not a replacement. Antifibrinolytic — stabilizes clot. Continue resuscitation and source control concurrently.

opts.text: Lactated Ringer's 1–2 L bolus via large-bore IV; release 2 units crossmatched PRBC from blood bank; activate MTP 1:1:1 if bleeding continues beyond initial response
opts.rat: Volume resuscitation matches blood loss. LR is balanced crystalloid — preferred over NS for large-volume resuscitation (NS causes hyperchloremic acidosis). PRBC for ongoing loss + Hgb 8.4; FFP and platelets per MTP for continued bleeding.

opts.text: Trendelenburg position (legs elevated above heart) to improve venous return
opts.rat: Outdated practice. Trendelenburg does not improve hemodynamics meaningfully and may impair respiratory mechanics. Supine flat or modified Trendelenburg with passive leg raise (transient bridge only) — focus on volume and source control.

opts.text: Place 2nd large-bore IV (16g if possible); ensure existing IV is patent; send stat CBC, coags, fibrinogen, type-and-screen confirmation; send q30 min CBC during active hemorrhage
opts.rat: IV access redundancy is essential — if one fails during MTP, the patient cannot afford the delay. Serial labs guide blood product replacement (fibrinogen <200 → cryo; plt <50 → platelets; trending Hgb).

opts.text: If uterotonic ladder fails within 15–30 min, escalate to INTRAUTERINE BALLOON TAMPONADE (Bakri 300–500 mL saline fill); if balloon fails, uterine artery embolization (IR) or surgical management (B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy as last resort)
opts.rat: Defined escalation pathway. Bakri provides mechanical tamponade and bridges to definitive intervention. IR embolization preserves fertility; surgical options are last-resort. Have OR and IR teams aware EARLY, not when crashing.

opts.text: Withhold all uterotonics until OB attending is physically present at bedside to evaluate
opts.rat: Wrong — delays save no one. PPH protocols allow RN-initiated oxytocin and bimanual massage immediately. Notify the provider while acting. Time is uterine.; every minute of delay is more blood lost.

opts.text: Encourage breastfeeding immediately to stimulate endogenous oxytocin release
opts.rat: Wrong timing — mom is in active hemorrhage with Class 2 shock signs. Bring her to stability first; bonding and breastfeeding can resume after. Father continuing skin-to-skin with the infant maintains the infant's needs. Lactation support and pumping can be initiated later to maintain supply if separation continues.

opts.text: Keep patient WARM with warmed IV fluids and forced-air warmer; monitor temperature q15 min during MTP
opts.rat: Hypothermia worsens coagulopathy (lethal triad with acidosis and coagulopathy). Warmed fluids and active warming preserve clotting function. Critical in any patient receiving large-volume resuscitation.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: CALL FOR HELP (activate PPH protocol / rapid response / charge RN / OB and anesthesia notification); begin BIMANUAL FUNDAL MASSAGE; empty the bladder (straight-cath or Foley)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The fastest interventions with no preparation required. Calling for help mobilizes the team while you act. Bimanual massage is mechanical hemostasis. Emptying the bladder removes a treatable contributor. All three start within seconds and continue while everything else is being arranged.

opts.text: OXYTOCIN 10 units IM to thigh PLUS oxytocin 20–40 units in 500–1000 mL LR/NS infusing at high rate via existing large-bore PIV; confirm 2nd large-bore IV access
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical pharmacology + access. Oxytocin is the first-line uterotonic and is RN-initiated per PPH protocol. IM gives rapid onset; IV infusion gives sustained effect. Second IV is non-negotiable — MTP requires multiple access points.

opts.text: MISOPROSTOL (Cytotec) 800–1000 mcg PR (since Methergine contraindicated by HTN and Hemabate contraindicated by asthma); TRANEXAMIC ACID 1 g IV over 10 min
opts.rat: #3 — Concurrent escalation. Recognizing the contraindications and skipping to the safe second-line is the high-yield clinical decision in this case. TXA in parallel with uterotonics provides additional benefit. Do not give Methergine or Hemabate even if ordered — flag and substitute.

opts.text: Lactated Ringer's 1–2 L bolus via large-bore IV; release 2 units crossmatched PRBC from blood bank; activate MTP 1:1:1 if hemodynamic instability persists or bleeding >1500 mL
opts.rat: #4 — Resuscitation in parallel with source control. Volume + blood products as fast as the patient is losing them. Type-specific can be substituted with O-negative if delay; PRBC takes precedence over crystalloid for blood loss replacement once available.

opts.text: If uterotonic ladder fails within 15–30 min → INTRAUTERINE BALLOON TAMPONADE (Bakri); have IR/OR ready for embolization or surgical management (B-Lynch, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy as last resort)
opts.rat: #5 — Escalation pathway. Bakri bridges to definitive intervention. IR and OR teams should be alerted EARLY (not when crashing). Surgical management is last-resort, life-saving. The plan is staged so the team is ready before refractory bleeding.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 20 min after misoprostol + oxytocin drip + bladder emptied: fundus FIRM, midline, at umbilicus; lochia moderate; no new clots in last 10 min; HR 96, BP 108/68; cumulative EBL 1300 mL
opts.rat: Excellent response to the uterotonic ladder. Continued q15 min fundal checks for next 2 hr, then q30 min for 2 hr, then q1h. Continue oxytocin infusion x 8–24 hr. Trend Hgb in 6 hr; transfuse if symptomatic anemia or Hgb <7 in stable patient. Restore mother-infant contact; lactation support.

opts.text: 6 hr post-stabilization: Hgb 7.8 after 2 units PRBC, fundus firm midline 1 below umbilicus, lochia rubra moderate, breastfeeding initiated, VS WNL, ambulating with assistance to bathroom
opts.rat: Successful recovery trajectory. Continue close monitoring for 24 hr; iron supplementation; consider IV iron if Hgb <8; PPH education with patient on signs of delayed PPH (up to 12 wk postpartum); follow-up labs in 1–2 weeks.

opts.text: Discharge day 3: Hgb 8.6, ambulating independently, breastfeeding well, fundus 3 finger-breadths below umbilicus and firm, lochia rubra scant; on iron supplementation; postpartum education completed; follow-up in 1 week
opts.rat: Routine recovery. Discharge teaching: signs of delayed PPH (sudden heavy bleeding, large clots, foul lochia, fever, severe pain), endometritis, DVT risk, contraception, lactation, mood (PPD screening at 2 and 6 week visits). 1-week follow-up for repeat CBC and incision check.

opts.text: 10 min after initial interventions: fundus REMAINS BOGGY, bleeding continues, BP drops to 84/48, HR climbs to 138, EBL now 1600 mL
opts.rat: Refractory atony progressing to Class 3 shock. Escalate immediately: Bakri balloon insertion; activate MTP 1:1:1; alert IR for embolization; alert OR for possible laparotomy. Time is uterine — each minute of delay risks DIC, multi-organ failure, hysterectomy, death.

opts.text: Hour 2: oozing from IV sites and gums, bloody NG output, fibrinogen 130 mg/dL, INR 1.8, platelets 78 K — disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) developing
opts.rat: DIC from massive hemorrhage with consumption of clotting factors. Aggressive replacement: cryoprecipitate (10 units) for fibrinogen <200; FFP for INR >1.5; platelets for <50 K with active bleeding; continue PRBC. Treat the underlying cause (control the bleed) — products alone won't fix DIC.

opts.text: Despite RN voiced concern about her HTN history, the resident ordered Methergine 0.2 mg IM; the RN administered it; BP rapidly climbs to 198/118, patient develops severe headache and visual changes
opts.rat: ⚠️ Iatrogenic injury — preventable. The RN's role is to refuse the medication when contraindicated, clarify with provider, and document. This is a 'just culture' incident report scenario. Stop further Methergine; treat HTN urgently (IV labetalol or hydralazine); monitor for stroke/seizure/MI; neuro checks q15 min. System change: PPH protocols should flag drug contraindications in EHR.

opts.text: After 1 dose of misoprostol (PR), patient develops fever 100.8°F and mild shivering for 30 minutes, then resolves spontaneously
opts.rat: Expected misoprostol side effect — transient fever and shivering from prostaglandin effect. Self-limited within ~1 hr. Acetaminophen for comfort. Do not confuse with chorioamnionitis or endometritis (clinical context, no other infection signs). Reassure the patient and continue monitoring.

opts.text: Hour 1: fundus remains boggy despite oxytocin drip and misoprostol; Bakri balloon placed and inflated to 400 mL; bleeding visibly slowed; HR 102, BP 110/70 stable; cumulative EBL 1800 mL with 4 units PRBC transfused
opts.rat: Bakri achieving tamponade. Maintain balloon for 12–24 hr; continue oxytocin infusion; broad-spectrum antibiotics while balloon in place; do not exceed 24 hr (risk of pressure necrosis). Plan slow deflation with continued monitoring. If rebleed on deflation, escalate to embolization or surgical.

opts.text: Day 2: persistent oliguria (UOP 15 mL/hr), Cr rising 1.2 → 2.4, BUN 38, FENa <1% — acute kidney injury (prerenal/ATN from hypoperfusion during the hemorrhage)
opts.rat: AKI from prolonged hypoperfusion. Hold nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast, aminoglycosides); maintain euvolemia; consider nephrology consultation if Cr continues to rise or oliguria persists. Reversible if perfusion restored and additional insults avoided. Trend UOP and Cr q12h.

opts.text: Following stabilization, fundus firm, lochia moderate, but patient tearful saying she 'missed the first hour' with her baby and 'feels disconnected'
opts.rat: Emotional response — expected after a traumatic delivery experience. Acknowledge the feelings; reassure that bonding is not contingent on the first hour and there is plenty of opportunity ahead. Skin-to-skin now; rooming-in; lactation support; postpartum depression screening at 2 and 6 weeks; mental health follow-up if symptoms persist or escalate. Trauma-informed postpartum care.

title: Acute Manic Episode in Bipolar I with Lithium Toxicity
subtitle: Why a Level of 1.8 with Tremor and Confusion Is Not 'Mild' — Holding the Drug, Hydrating Carefully, and Recognizing the Sodium Trap

patient.name: M. Castellanos
patient.admitDate: Today, 0210 (involuntary 5150 hold — danger to self due to grandiose plans and not sleeping x 5 days; brought in by spouse and police)
patient.room: Inpatient Psych Unit, Bed 14B (door-side observation room)

vitals.time: 0700 (CURRENT — RN notes new fine tremor, slurred speech, mild gait unsteadiness)

labs.n: Lithium level (stat, drawn at 0530 — ~10 hr post last dose)
labs.v: 1.8 mEq/L
labs.r: Therapeutic 0.6–1.2 (acute) or 0.8–1.2 (maintenance); 1.5–2.0 mild toxicity; 2.0–2.5 moderate; >2.5 severe; >3.5 life-threatening

labs.v: 131 mEq/L (down from her baseline 138 three weeks ago)
labs.r: 136–145; LOW sodium causes the kidney to reabsorb more lithium (lithium is handled like sodium in the proximal tubule) → toxicity even on a stable dose

labs.v: Cr 1.4 (baseline 0.9) / BUN 32 / eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m² (baseline 78)
labs.r: Cr 0.6–1.1 female; reduced renal function = reduced lithium clearance = toxicity; lithium is renally excreted and is itself nephrotoxic with long-term use

labs.v: TSH 8.2 / Free T4 0.8 (low end of normal)
labs.r: TSH 0.4–4.0; lithium causes hypothyroidism in ~10–30% of long-term users; routine monitoring q6–12 mo; can present as worsening mood symptoms or fatigue

labs.v: Glucose 102 / K 3.9 / Mg 1.8 / Ca 9.6
labs.r: Within normal — rules out other causes of tremor/confusion

labs.v: UOP 4.2 L over last 24 hr (per spouse's report at home); urine SG 1.004 (dilute); patient drinking 'gallons of water' per spouse
labs.r: UOP >3 L/24 hr = polyuria; lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (NDI) blunts ADH response → polyuria → polydipsia → if not enough free water → hypernatremia OR if compensated → euvolemic; complicates dosing

labs.v: Sinus rhythm 104, QTc 460 ms (mildly prolonged), nonspecific T-wave changes
labs.r: Lithium can cause sinus node dysfunction, T-wave inversion/flattening, QT prolongation; usually benign and reversible; concerning if combined with other QT-prolonging meds

labs.v: Pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions ('I'm going to buy three businesses today, I just need a pen'); not slept x 5 nights; irritable when redirected; oriented x3 but distractible; YMRS estimated 32 (severe)
labs.r: YMRS 0–12 normal; 13–19 mild; 20–25 moderate; >25 severe; criteria for hospitalization satisfied (impairment + safety risk)

labs.v: Denies suicidal ideation; denies homicidal ideation; high-risk behaviors: spent $48,000 on online purchases in last 4 days; left infant son alone for 3 hr to 'check the new property'; spouse reports she has been driving 95 mph; no recent weapons access; no prior suicide attempts; one prior psych hospitalization 3 years ago for mania
labs.r: Risk of harm to self (impulsivity, sleep deprivation, judgment impairment) and others (infant left alone) — meets 5150 criteria (gravely disabled / danger to self)

labs.v: Lithium carbonate 900 mg PO BID x 8 years (last dose 2030 last night) — stable level 0.9 historically; lisinopril 20 mg daily started 3 weeks ago (new!) for newly diagnosed HTN by PCP; ibuprofen 400 mg TID x 4 days for back pain (self-medicating); levothyroxine NOT prescribed despite TSH 8.2
labs.r: ACEi (lisinopril) AND NSAIDs (ibuprofen) BOTH reduce lithium clearance — the perfect storm for elevated level on a stable dose. Diuretics (especially thiazides) do the same. Tetracyclines and metronidazole also raise lithium level.

labs.v: Stopped sleeping 5 days ago after stressful work meeting; husband reports she has been agitated for 'about 2 weeks' (prodrome); has been increasingly noncompliant with appointments; mother died last year (anniversary in 2 weeks); infant son age 8 months at home — spouse has taken FMLA
labs.r: Sleep deprivation is both consequence and precipitant of mania; anniversary reactions are recognized triggers; postpartum/perinatal period (within 1 year of birth) is a high-risk window for bipolar relapse

labs.v: Fine tremor of outstretched hands (NEW from baseline — historically had mild action tremor), slight slurring of speech, mild gait unsteadiness on tandem walk, hyperreflexia 3+ in lower extremities, no clonus, no nystagmus, no muscle rigidity
labs.r: Early-to-moderate lithium toxicity neuro signs; if it progresses: coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, seizures, encephalopathy, coma; absence of rigidity/hyperthermia distinguishes from NMS/serotonin syndrome

labs.v: No fever >100°F (rules out infection precipitating delirium); no muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, or clonus (rules out NMS, serotonin syndrome); no focal neuro deficit; no recent head trauma; UDS positive for benzodiazepines (ED gave lorazepam 2 mg) only; no alcohol, no other substances
labs.r: Differential cleared: rules out infection, NMS, serotonin syndrome, stroke

nursesNote: 0700 — 41 y/o female G2P2 (infant son age 8 months at home) with bipolar I disorder (dx age 25, 2 prior hospitalizations) on chronic lithium 900 mg PO BID x 8 years admitted to inpatient psych unit at 0430 on 5150 hold (involuntary psychiatric hold for danger to self / grave disability) brought in by police and spouse for acute manic episode. Mania features per spouse and patient interview: not slept x 5 nights, pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions ('I'm going to buy three businesses today'), online spending $48,000 in last 4 days, left infant alone for 3 hr to 'check the new property,' driving 95 mph, mood elevated but irritable when redirected. YMRS estimated 32 (severe). Denies SI/HI, no recent weapons access, no prior suicide attempts. Precipitants likely multifactorial: anniversary of mother's death in 2 weeks, perinatal period (postpartum window <1 year remains high-risk for relapse), and — importantly — lithium toxicity from drug interactions: PCP started lisinopril 20 mg daily 3 weeks ago for newly diagnosed HTN and she has been self-medicating with ibuprofen 400 mg TID x 4 days for back pain. BOTH ACE inhibitors AND NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance and raise serum levels even on a stable dose. Last lithium dose was last night at 2030; stat level drawn at 0530 (~9 hr post-dose, near trough) is 1.8 mEq/L — well above her historical baseline of 0.9 — confirming mild-to-moderate toxicity. Sodium is 131 (down from baseline 138) which further drives lithium reabsorption (kidney handles lithium like sodium). Cr 1.4 from baseline 0.9 with eGFR 48 — reduced clearance compounding the level. TSH 8.2 (lithium-induced hypothyroidism — never started on levothyroxine; another contributor to mood instability). UOP 4.2 L in last 24 hr at home with dilute urine and polydipsia (lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus). On exam at 0700, NEW fine tremor of outstretched hands beyond her usual baseline, mild slurring of speech, gait slightly unsteady on tandem walk, hyperreflexia 3+ in LE, no clonus, no rigidity, no hyperthermia, no muscle stiffness (rules out NMS and serotonin syndrome). ECG sinus 104, QTc 460 mildly prolonged, nonspecific T-changes (lithium ECG effects). Mental status: oriented x3, distractible, pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions persisting; received lorazepam 2 mg IV in ED with mild reduction in agitation but mania features persist. Plan in coordination with psychiatry: HOLD lithium x 24–48 hr and recheck level q6h; gentle IV hydration with isotonic saline at 100 mL/hr (avoid dextrose which can worsen hyponatremia; isotonic saline corrects sodium and increases lithium clearance — but do NOT 'force diuresis' or use loop diuretics, which can paradoxically worsen toxicity); DISCONTINUE lisinopril and ibuprofen; offer acetaminophen for back pain; alternative antihypertensive (CCB like amlodipine is safe with lithium); add olanzapine 10 mg PO daily for adjunctive mania control (also helps with sleep); milieu management with low-stimulation environment, frequent reorientation, limit-setting that is firm but non-confrontational, redirection of grandiose plans to here-and-now tasks; close 1:1 observation initially given impulsivity and dehydration risk; reassess for seclusion or restraint ONLY if behaviors threaten self or others and less restrictive options have failed (last resort, time-limited orders with 1:1 monitoring per CMS); levothyroxine to be started for the TSH 8.2 hypothyroidism after psychiatry/endocrine coordination. Spouse at bedside intermittently, also caring for infant; social work to coordinate child welfare check and family support; chaplain offered. Hemodialysis is NOT indicated at this level (1.8) — reserved for >4.0 with severe symptoms, >2.5 in setting of renal failure or chronic toxicity with neuro signs, or refractory toxicity. Will continue to monitor level, mental status, neuro exam, I&O, and electrolytes q4–6h.
providerOrders: ADMIT to inpatient psych unit on 5150 (72-hr involuntary hold); psychiatry as primary; internal medicine consult for the toxicity and metabolic management

HOLD LITHIUM x 24–48 hr (do NOT continue at any dose while toxic); recheck lithium level q6h until trending down; resume only after level <1.2 and symptoms resolved, at a lower dose with re-evaluation of interacting medications

DISCONTINUE LISINOPRIL (ACEi reduces lithium clearance) and IBUPROFEN (NSAID same effect); substitute AMLODIPINE 5 mg PO daily for HTN (CCBs are safe with lithium); ACETAMINOPHEN 650 mg PO q6h PRN for back pain (safe with lithium)

IV access: PIV 20g; ISOTONIC SALINE (0.9% NS) at 100 mL/hr to gently rehydrate, correct hyponatremia, and increase lithium clearance — DO NOT 'force diuresis' or use loop diuretics; AVOID dextrose-containing fluids as monotherapy (worsen hyponatremia and free water excess)
Strict I&O hourly; daily weights; serum electrolytes (Na, K, Cl, HCO3, BUN, Cr, glucose) q6h; lithium level q6h until <1.2 mEq/L; reassess TSH/Free T4 once stable
Continuous cardiac monitoring x 24 hr (lithium can cause sinus dysfunction and QT prolongation, especially in toxicity)
Neuro checks q2h x 12 hr (tremor severity, gait, mental status, reflexes, seizure precautions); HOLD other CNS depressants and serotonergic agents
ADJUNCTIVE MANIA CONTROL: olanzapine 10 mg PO daily (helps mania and sleep); lorazepam 1–2 mg IV/PO q4–6h PRN agitation (use sparingly given toxicity is the partial cause)
Hold any other QT-prolonging medications (haloperidol high-dose, IV ondansetron > 4 mg, citalopram, etc.); review home med list

Mental health team: q-shift safety assessment; SAD PERSONS or Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale documented; 1:1 observation initially given impulsivity, falls risk from gait unsteadiness, and dehydration; downgrade to q15-min checks once neurologically stable

Milieu management: low-stimulation environment (single room initially, reduce visitors, dim lights, calm staff voice), limit access to phones/devices for impulse-buying, redirect grandiose plans to here-and-now activities, firm but non-confrontational limit-setting
Therapeutic communication: short clear sentences, avoid debate of delusional content (don't argue, don't reinforce), acknowledge feelings, redirect to safety and basic needs

Seclusion / restraint LAST RESORT ONLY after less-restrictive options failed; time-limited orders per CMS (≤4 hr adult); 1:1 monitoring with q15-min checks during restraint; document attempted alternatives; consider chemical restraint (haloperidol IM or olanzapine IM) before physical

Nutrition: regular diet, encourage oral intake, consistent salt intake (avoid sudden low-salt diet — drops lithium clearance — counterintuitive); finger food options if patient too distractible to sit and eat

Sleep hygiene: dim lights at 2100, no caffeine after 1400, low-stimulation evening routine, melatonin 5 mg PO at bedtime as needed (sleep is itself therapeutic for mania; restoration of sleep can break the cycle)

Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily TO BE STARTED for lithium-induced hypothyroidism (TSH 8.2 with low-normal Free T4) — coordinate with psychiatry given potential to slightly increase mania risk; start low, recheck TSH in 6 weeks

Social work: coordinate infant care welfare check with spouse; mandated reporting to child protective services may be required given infant was left alone for 3 hr (jurisdiction-dependent — coordinate with hospital legal/ethics); spousal support; financial counseling for the spending impact ($48,000 in 4 days)

Family meeting once mental status improves: education on bipolar I, medication adherence, lithium drug interactions (especially NSAIDs, ACEi, thiazides, dehydration, low-salt diets), warning signs of relapse, the 'three R's' (rest, routine, recognize early)

Hemodialysis NOT indicated at this level/severity (reserve for level >4.0, level >2.5 with severe symptoms or renal failure, refractory chronic toxicity, or rapidly rising level despite supportive care); consult nephrology to be aware in case escalation needed

Outpatient follow-up plan: psychiatry within 1 week of discharge, PCP within 2 weeks for HTN management, lithium level + TSH + BMP at 1 week post-discharge, mood-disorder support group referral, perinatal mood/anxiety screening given <1 year postpartum window

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Lithium level 1.8 mEq/L (well above her baseline of 0.9) with new fine tremor, slurred speech, and mild gait unsteadiness
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms mild-to-moderate lithium toxicity. Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index (0.6–1.2). Neuro signs at 1.5–2.0 are the warning that the level is high enough to cause harm. Hold the drug, recheck q6h, support clearance with isotonic fluids.

opts.text: Drug interactions: new lisinopril 3 weeks ago + ibuprofen 4 days ago — BOTH reduce lithium clearance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the cause of the toxicity. ACE inhibitors and NSAIDs both reduce renal lithium clearance and raise levels even on a stable dose. Thiazide diuretics, tetracyclines, metronidazole have the same effect. Discontinue both; substitute amlodipine and acetaminophen.

opts.text: Sodium 131 (down from baseline 138) with reduced eGFR 48 (baseline 78)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — compounds the toxicity. Lithium is handled like sodium in the proximal tubule; LOW sodium = MORE lithium reabsorption = higher level. Reduced eGFR = less clearance. Both are independent and additive contributors here.

opts.text: Severe acute mania: YMRS 32, pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions, not slept x 5 nights, impulsive high-risk behaviors ($48K spending, left infant alone, driving 95 mph)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the reason for hospitalization. Severe mania is itself dangerous: exhaustion, dehydration, accidents, financial ruin, harm to dependents, suicide risk in mixed states. Adjunctive mania control + safety + milieu + sleep restoration.

opts.text: Lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus: UOP 4.2 L/24 hr, dilute urine (SG 1.004), polydipsia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — chronic complication of lithium. NDI blunts ADH response → polyuria → polydipsia → if free water intake is inadequate during the toxicity, hypernatremia can develop. Replace water carefully; monitor I&O and sodium closely.

opts.text: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism: TSH 8.2, Free T4 low-normal, never started on levothyroxine
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — contributing to mood instability. Lithium causes hypothyroidism in 10–30% of long-term users; routine monitoring every 6–12 months. Untreated hypothyroidism worsens depression and can destabilize bipolar mood; start levothyroxine 50 mcg daily; recheck in 6 weeks.

opts.text: Safety: high-risk impulsive behaviors with infant son left alone 3 hr; spouse caring for infant; possible mandated CPS reporting
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — duty to report. Many jurisdictions require nurses and mental health professionals to report when a child has been left unsupervised. Coordinate with social work and ethics/legal; do not delay reporting if statute requires; this is also a clinical-care issue because the spouse needs support.

opts.text: QTc 460 ms with nonspecific T-changes on ECG; sinus tachycardia 104
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — lithium ECG effects. Lithium can cause sinus node dysfunction and mild QT prolongation; usually benign and reversible. Concerning if combined with other QT-prolonging medications (haloperidol IV, IV ondansetron > 4 mg, citalopram, methadone). Avoid QT-prolonging adjuncts; continuous cardiac monitoring x 24 hr.

opts.text: Absence of muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, clonus, or autonomic instability — rules out NMS and serotonin syndrome
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — differential narrows. The neuro signs (tremor, hyperreflexia, gait unsteadiness, confusion) overlap with NMS and serotonin syndrome. Absence of rigidity + hyperthermia + clonus + autonomic instability supports lithium toxicity over those alternatives. Important — treatment diverges (NMS: stop dopamine blockers; SS: stop serotonergics).

opts.text: Precipitants stacking: anniversary of mother's death in 2 weeks, <1 year postpartum window, recent psychosocial stressor at work, missed appointments
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — context for relapse and discharge planning. Anniversary reactions are recognized triggers; perinatal period within 1 year of birth is a high-risk window for bipolar relapse; nonadherence and stress are the most modifiable. Address with relapse-prevention planning, family meeting, peer support.

opts.text: Patient denies SI/HI; no recent weapons access; no prior suicide attempts
opts.rat: Reassuring — but does not preclude suicide risk in bipolar (especially in mixed/depressive switches). Continue routine suicide screening per shift; safety planning at discharge; mixed-state monitoring as mania resolves (the depressive switch carries highest suicide risk). Document but do not over-weight as 'safe.'

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no drug allergies. Document and proceed. The medication issues here are interactions, not allergies.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / pharmacology.

opts.text: LITHIUM has a NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX (0.6–1.2 mEq/L); toxicity at 1.5–2.0 is mild (tremor, GI upset, lethargy), 2.0–2.5 moderate (confusion, ataxia, slurred speech), >2.5 severe (seizures, coma, renal failure, death); chronic toxicity (gradual rise on stable dose) can cause neurologic damage at lower levels than acute toxicity
opts.rat: CORRECT — foundational pharmacology. Levels alone don't tell the whole story; chronic toxicity from interactions can cause permanent neurologic damage at 'lower' levels than an acute overdose. Always trend the level AND the clinical signs together. Stop the drug at toxic level + symptoms regardless of severity grade.

opts.text: Lithium is handled by the kidney like SODIUM — reabsorbed in the proximal tubule; LOW sodium (or volume depletion) → MORE lithium reabsorption → HIGHER level; AVOID low-salt diets and dehydration in patients on lithium
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield teaching. Tell patients to maintain consistent salt and fluid intake; avoid crash diets, excessive sweating without fluid replacement, fluid losses (D/V, diuretics). The patient's hyponatremia 131 is contributing to her elevated level — restoring sodium with isotonic saline helps clearance.

opts.text: ACE INHIBITORS (lisinopril, enalapril, etc.), NSAIDS (ibuprofen, naproxen, celecoxib, etc.), and THIAZIDE DIURETICS all REDUCE lithium clearance and raise serum levels even on a stable dose — perfect storm here is ACEi + NSAID
opts.rat: CORRECT — most common cause of chronic lithium toxicity. Patient was started on lisinopril and self-medicated with ibuprofen — both raise levels independently. Other interactions to know: tetracyclines, metronidazole, ARBs, calcium channel blockers (mild). Acetaminophen is safe.

opts.text: ISOTONIC SALINE (0.9% NS) at 100 mL/hr is the supportive treatment of choice — corrects hyponatremia, restores volume, and INCREASES lithium clearance; DO NOT use loop diuretics or 'force diuresis' (paradoxically reduces clearance and worsens toxicity)
opts.rat: CORRECT — common pitfall. The instinct 'flush it out with lasix' is wrong — loop diuretics cause volume depletion and reabsorption of lithium. Isotonic saline maintains volume AND provides sodium that competes with lithium for reabsorption. Hypotonic fluids alone worsen hyponatremia.

opts.text: HEMODIALYSIS is indicated for: level >4.0 mEq/L (acute) OR >2.5 with severe symptoms OR >2.5 in setting of renal failure OR chronic toxicity with severe neuro signs OR refractory levels; lithium is small enough to be dialyzed effectively
opts.rat: CORRECT — escalation criteria. At 1.8 with mild neuro signs and adequate kidney function for clearance, supportive care suffices. Levels rebound after HD (lithium redistributes from cells), so prolonged or extended HD may be needed in severe cases.

opts.text: Lithium-induced NEPHROGENIC DIABETES INSIPIDUS (NDI): chronic lithium use blunts kidney response to ADH → polyuria + polydipsia + dilute urine; affects 20–40% of long-term users; usually reversible but can become permanent; AMILORIDE (K-sparing diuretic) is the specific treatment if patient remains on lithium
opts.rat: CORRECT — common chronic complication. Counseling: maintain adequate fluid intake to keep up with losses; if NDI severe, switch off lithium OR add amiloride (paradoxically a diuretic that helps); monitor renal function long-term.

opts.text: Lithium-induced HYPOTHYROIDISM: 10–30% of long-term users develop subclinical or overt hypothyroidism; lithium inhibits thyroid hormone release and synthesis; ROUTINE MONITORING every 6–12 months; can present as worsening mood symptoms, fatigue, cold intolerance; treatment is levothyroxine, not stopping lithium
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield monitoring teaching. Lithium-induced hypothyroidism is treatable and lithium can usually be continued. The hypothyroidism here may be contributing to her relapse risk. Start levothyroxine; recheck in 6 weeks.

opts.text: MANIA NURSING: low-stimulation environment, short clear directions, firm non-confrontational limit-setting, redirect to here-and-now tasks, do NOT argue with delusions or reinforce them, restore sleep (sleep is therapeutic for mania), finger foods if too distractible to sit, frequent reorientation
opts.rat: CORRECT — milieu and therapeutic communication. Mania burns calories — patient may not eat enough sitting at a table; finger foods solve this. Sleep restoration breaks the mania cycle. Limit-setting without arguing — 'I hear you want to leave; the doctor will be in at 9 — let's wait together.'

opts.text: DURING active mania, ANTIDEPRESSANTS are CONTRAINDICATED — they can precipitate mania, mixed states, or rapid cycling; if patient was on an antidepressant for bipolar depression, hold during mania
opts.rat: CORRECT. The treatment of bipolar depression is nuanced — mood stabilizers (lithium, lamotrigine, quetiapine, lurasidone) preferred over antidepressants. SSRIs/SNRIs can flip mood. In acute mania, all antidepressants are held.

opts.text: Continue lithium at full dose during this episode to maintain therapeutic level — never stop suddenly because of risk of mania rebound
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong in TOXICITY. The general principle of avoiding sudden lithium discontinuation applies in stable patients (gradual taper preferred to avoid rebound mania). IN TOXICITY, the drug is causing harm and must be held immediately. Resume at a lower dose once level <1.2 and symptoms resolved, after addressing the precipitant (interacting drugs).

opts.text: Furosemide 40 mg IV will flush the lithium out faster — give it to speed recovery
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS misconception. Loop diuretics cause volume depletion → kidney compensates by reabsorbing more lithium → WORSENS toxicity. Same applies to thiazides for long-term use (they raise level on a stable dose). Use ISOTONIC SALINE for hydration; if dialysis needed, that's the escalation, not diuretics.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Lithium Toxicity Progression r/t continued absorption + reduced clearance + drug interactions (level 1.8 with neuro signs)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST physiologic priority. If lithium continues and the level climbs to 2.5+, neuro injury becomes possible. HOLD lithium NOW; D/C lisinopril and ibuprofen; isotonic saline; recheck q6h; have nephrology aware for possible HD if level rises despite supportive care.

opts.text: Risk for Harm to Self or Others r/t severe mania with impulsivity, sleep deprivation, and judgment impairment (left infant alone, $48K spending, driving 95 mph)
opts.rat: #2 — Safety. This is why she is hospitalized. Maintain 1:1 observation initially; remove access to phones/finances during inpatient stay; limit setting; safety planning; mandated CPS reporting if statute requires for infant being left alone.

opts.text: Risk for Dehydration / Electrolyte Imbalance r/t lithium-induced NDI + polyuria + reduced PO intake during mania
opts.rat: #3 — Compounds toxicity. Volume depletion raises lithium level; hyponatremia also raises it. Isotonic saline (not free water alone); strict I&O; daily weights; electrolytes q6h. Encourage oral intake when patient cooperative.

opts.text: Risk for Falls / Injury r/t mild gait unsteadiness from neurotoxicity + distractibility + impulsivity
opts.rat: #4 — Fall prevention. Mania + ataxia + impulsivity = high fall risk. Bed in low position, bed alarm, call light in reach, frequent rounding, non-slip footwear, assist with ambulation. Resolve as neurotoxicity clears.

opts.text: Risk for Disturbed Sleep Pattern r/t mania (not slept x 5 days) and high-stimulation environment
opts.rat: #5 — Sleep IS therapeutic for mania, but ranked lowest in the acute priority because the safety and toxicity issues come first. Once stable, sleep restoration accelerates mania resolution: low-stim evening, melatonin, olanzapine for sleep effect, consistent routine. Dim lights at 2100.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: HOLD lithium x 24–48 hr; recheck level q6h until <1.2 mEq/L; resume at lower dose once symptoms resolve and precipitants addressed
opts.rat: Core management. Drug is causing harm; stop the drug. Recheck q6h to confirm trend. Resume after addressing precipitating drug interactions; lower the dose; reassess goal level.

opts.text: Discontinue lisinopril and ibuprofen; substitute amlodipine 5 mg PO daily for HTN; acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN for back pain
opts.rat: Removes the cause of the toxicity. CCBs are safe with lithium. Acetaminophen is safe (NSAIDs are not). Educate patient to ALWAYS check with pharmacist before adding OTC pain meds while on lithium.

opts.text: Isotonic saline (0.9% NS) at 100 mL/hr; strict I&O; daily weights; electrolytes q6h
opts.rat: Supportive care of choice for mild-moderate lithium toxicity. Corrects sodium, maintains volume, supports clearance. Avoid dextrose-only fluids (worsen hyponatremia) and loop diuretics (worsen toxicity).

opts.text: Furosemide 40 mg IV q12h to 'flush out' the lithium
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong — loop diuretics cause volume depletion → kidney reabsorbs more lithium → WORSENS toxicity. This is a classic exam trap and a real-world clinical error to avoid. Use isotonic saline; escalate to HD if criteria met.

opts.text: Adjunctive mania control: OLANZAPINE 10 mg PO daily (also helps sleep); LORAZEPAM 1–2 mg IV/PO q4–6h PRN agitation, used sparingly given toxicity contribution
opts.rat: Antipsychotic adjuncts work faster than waiting for lithium to take effect alone. Olanzapine has sedating effect that helps sleep restoration. Benzos for agitation as needed. AVOID adding lithium back during toxicity; AVOID antidepressants in mania.

opts.text: Add sertraline 50 mg PO daily for underlying depression risk
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED during active mania. Antidepressants can precipitate or worsen mania, induce mixed states, and cause rapid cycling. If she has bipolar depression after the mania resolves, treat with a mood stabilizer (lamotrigine, quetiapine, lurasidone) — not an SSRI as monotherapy.

opts.text: Hemodialysis stat for the lithium level 1.8
opts.rat: Not at this level/severity. HD criteria: level >4.0 (acute), >2.5 with severe symptoms or renal failure, refractory chronic toxicity. At 1.8 with mild neuro signs and adequate kidney function, supportive care is appropriate. Nephrology aware in case escalation needed.

opts.text: 1:1 observation initially; bed alarm; non-slip footwear; remove access to phones/finances; suicide precautions per facility protocol
opts.rat: Safety bundle. Mania impulsivity + ataxia + sleep deprivation = high risk for harm. Restrict access to means of impulsive harm (financial, vehicular, weapons-equivalent). Suicide precautions because mixed-state depressive switches carry the highest risk.

opts.text: Low-stimulation environment; firm non-confrontational limit-setting; redirect grandiose plans to here-and-now; do not argue with delusions; consistent staff if possible; finger foods if too distractible to sit
opts.rat: Milieu management bundle. Reduces stimulation that fuels mania; structures the environment; avoids power struggles. Therapeutic communication: 'I can see you have a lot of ideas right now. Let's focus on resting first; we can talk about your plans tomorrow.'

opts.text: Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily for the TSH 8.2 lithium-induced hypothyroidism
opts.rat: Treats a treatable contributor to mood instability. Lithium-induced hypothyroidism does not require stopping lithium — just supplement. Coordinate with psychiatry given slight theoretical risk of worsening mania (clinically negligible at replacement doses); recheck TSH in 6 weeks.

opts.text: Apply 4-point physical restraints prophylactically given the agitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ Restraints are LAST RESORT after less-restrictive options (verbal de-escalation, redirection, environmental change, voluntary medication) have failed AND behavior poses imminent harm. Time-limited orders per CMS; 1:1 monitoring during; document attempted alternatives; chemical restraint preferred over physical when escalation needed.

opts.text: Sleep hygiene bundle: dim lights at 2100, no caffeine, low-stimulation evening routine, melatonin 5 mg PO at bedtime, environmental quiet
opts.rat: Sleep is therapeutic for mania. Restoring sleep can break the cycle. Olanzapine's sedating effect supports this. Once she sleeps a full night, mania often substantially improves the next day.

opts.text: Social work consult: coordinate infant care welfare check; mandated reporting to CPS per state statute given infant left alone for 3 hr; spousal support; financial counseling for spending impact
opts.rat: Multidisciplinary safety net. The infant's welfare and the family's psychosocial recovery from this episode are part of the discharge plan. Mandated reporting is jurisdiction-dependent — work with hospital legal/ethics; do not delay if required.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: HOLD lithium dose; DISCONTINUE lisinopril and ibuprofen; verify with provider and document on MAR; flag drug interaction list to pharmacy
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stop the harm. Drug-related toxicity resolves only when the offending agents are stopped. This is a 5-second nursing decision once recognized; do not wait for level to climb. The RN can hold a medication pending provider clarification when there is a clinical safety concern.

opts.text: Start IV access (PIV 20g); isotonic saline (0.9% NS) at 100 mL/hr; draw stat repeat lithium level, BMP, magnesium, calcium
opts.rat: #2 — Supportive treatment and monitoring. Isotonic saline addresses sodium, volume, and clearance simultaneously. Repeat labs guide trajectory. Trend lithium q6h; trend electrolytes q6h.

opts.text: Safety bundle: 1:1 observation; remove access to phones/finances; bed alarm; non-slip footwear; fall precautions; document SAD PERSONS or C-SSRS
opts.rat: #3 — Safety. Concurrent with treatment. Impulsivity + ataxia + judgment impairment require active prevention. Suicide screening per facility protocol because mixed-state depressive switches carry high risk.

opts.text: Milieu and adjunctive mania control: low-stimulation environment; OLANZAPINE 10 mg PO; LORAZEPAM 1–2 mg PRN agitation (used sparingly); firm non-confrontational limit-setting; redirect to here-and-now
opts.rat: #4 — Mania control while lithium clears. Olanzapine works in hours; helps sleep restoration. Lorazepam PRN for agitation. Milieu reduces fuel for the mania. Avoid antidepressants and high-dose haloperidol IV (QT).

opts.text: Multidisciplinary coordination: social work for infant welfare check + CPS reporting if required + spousal support; psychiatry team note; family meeting once mental status improves; outpatient follow-up plan
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained recovery and discharge planning. Social work and family meeting once she is stabilized. Discharge plan: psychiatry within 1 week, PCP within 2 weeks, lithium + TSH + BMP at 1 week post-discharge, peer support, relapse-prevention education, perinatal mood screening (still within 1-year postpartum window).

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 12 hr after holding lithium + isotonic saline + D/C of lisinopril/ibuprofen: lithium level 1.4, sodium 134, Cr 1.2, tremor reduced, gait steady, speech clearer
opts.rat: Excellent response. Continue supportive care, recheck level q6h until <1.2 mEq/L. Plan to resume lithium at a lower dose once <1.2 and symptoms resolved; amlodipine for HTN; acetaminophen for pain; education on interactions.

opts.text: Day 3: lithium 0.8, mania features substantially reduced (YMRS 14), sleeping 6 hr/night with olanzapine, eating regular meals, participating in milieu groups, oriented x3, no tremor, gait normal, levothyroxine started
opts.rat: Successful recovery trajectory. Continue olanzapine adjunct; resume lithium at lower dose with consistent salt/fluid intake; address discharge plan including outpatient follow-up, family meeting, relapse-prevention education.

opts.text: Discharge day 6: stable mood (YMRS 8), insight improving, agreed to outpatient psychiatry follow-up within 1 week, family meeting completed with spouse, mood-disorder peer support group referral made, financial counseling scheduled, infant welfare check completed by CPS with safety plan in place
opts.rat: Comprehensive recovery. Discharge teaching: lithium drug interactions (NSAIDs, ACEi, thiazides, dehydration, low-salt), warning signs of relapse (decreased sleep is the earliest), three R's (rest, routine, recognize early), perinatal mood watchfulness.

opts.text: Hour 12: despite supportive care, lithium climbs to 2.6, new confusion, coarse tremor, vomiting, one brief generalized seizure
opts.rat: Refractory toxicity progressing. Consult nephrology STAT for HEMODIALYSIS (criteria met: level >2.5 with severe symptoms / seizure). Continue isotonic saline; airway/breathing support; benzo for seizures; check for additional precipitants (volume status, hidden meds).

opts.text: Day 2: patient takes ibuprofen 800 mg given by a visiting friend who 'wanted to help with her back'; level climbs from 1.4 to 2.1
opts.rat: Iatrogenic/visitor-introduced precipitant. Re-education on the absolute avoidance of NSAIDs while on lithium; visitor policy clarification; document; reinforce family meeting teaching. This is a real-world failure mode that the discharge teaching MUST cover.

opts.text: Day 4: mania features improving but patient becomes tearful, says she 'wants to disappear,' admits passing suicidal ideation without plan — mixed-state features emerging as mania resolves
opts.rat: Mixed-state depressive switch — this is the highest-suicide-risk period in bipolar I. Reassess Columbia Suicide Severity Rating; increase observation; safety plan; consider quetiapine or lurasidone for bipolar depression (mood-stabilizer-based, NOT SSRI monotherapy); behavioral health intensification.

opts.text: Day 5: TSH rechecked at 7.4 (down from 8.2 after 4 days of levothyroxine 50 mcg); patient reports slightly more energy and clearer thinking
opts.rat: Hypothyroidism responding to replacement. Continue levothyroxine; recheck TSH in 6 weeks for full equilibration; adjust dose if needed. Educate on the lifetime need for thyroid monitoring on lithium.

opts.text: Patient gets up at night, refuses staff redirection, attempts to leave the unit ('I have to check on my businesses') — verbal de-escalation succeeds after 15 min and PRN olanzapine 5 mg PO accepted; no restraints needed
opts.rat: Expected acute-mania behavior; least-restrictive intervention succeeded — exactly the goal. Document de-escalation steps and patient response; reinforce milieu plan; review trigger (likely environmental stimulation level); continue 1:1 observation.

opts.text: Day 7 discharge: patient reports she 'feels back to herself,' goals discussed for spousal communication, return to work plan, agreement to weekly therapy and monthly psychiatry, lithium level 0.9 on resumed 600 mg PO BID (lower dose), TSH 5.2
opts.rat: Stable discharge. Reinforce the modifiable factors: drug interactions, consistent salt/fluid intake, sleep routine, stress management, perinatal vigilance (still within 1-year window), early-warning signs (decreased sleep first), and outpatient follow-up structure.

opts.text: Persistent mild fine tremor of outstretched hands at discharge despite normal lithium level — patient's chronic baseline
opts.rat: Expected — many long-term lithium users have a baseline benign action tremor distinct from toxicity. Educate patient to recognize the difference: baseline tremor stable over time; toxicity tremor is NEW or WORSENING + other symptoms (slurred speech, ataxia, confusion, GI). Beta-blocker (propranolol 10–20 mg PO PRN) can suppress the baseline tremor if bothersome.

title: Severe Hyperkalemia with EKG Changes in ESRD Patient Who Missed Dialysis
subtitle: Calcium Stabilizes the Membrane (Buys Minutes), Insulin/D50 + Albuterol Shift It (Buys Hours), but Only Dialysis Removes It

patient.name: B. Okafor
patient.admitDate: Today, 0820 (ED arrival via wife's car — weakness and palpitations after missing his Saturday HD)
patient.room: ED Resus Bay 4 → Tele/Step-down pending HD initiation
