vitals.time: Last HD session Friday (3 days ago, baseline)
0905 (CURRENT — 45 min into resuscitation; calcium gluconate given, insulin/D50 running)

labs.v: POC 7.4 mEq/L (drawn at 0825) → confirmatory venous 7.2 mEq/L (drawn 0840, run on lab analyzer)
labs.r: 3.5–5.0 mEq/L; 5.1–6.0 mild; 6.1–6.9 moderate; ≥7.0 severe / life-threatening; any K with EKG changes = severe regardless of value

labs.v: Sinus bradycardia rate 52 with PEAKED, NARROW-BASED T waves V2–V5; widened QRS 132 ms (baseline 96 ms on prior EKG); flattened P waves in lead II; PR interval 220 ms; NO sine wave pattern yet
labs.r: Hyperkalemia EKG progression: peaked T waves (≥5.5) → flattened P / prolonged PR (≥6.5) → widened QRS (≥7.0) → sine wave / VF / asystole (≥8.0+); rate and order are not strict but the trajectory is

labs.v: BUN 88 / Cr 9.4 (baseline post-HD Cr ~6.0) / eGFR <10 mL/min (ESRD)
labs.r: ESRD on HD — patient has no native renal clearance; missed HD = no K removal = inevitable rise

labs.v: Na 134 / Cl 100 / HCO3 16 (baseline ~22 post-HD)
labs.r: Metabolic acidosis from accumulation of uremic acids; acidosis drives K+ OUT of cells into blood (H+/K+ exchange) → worsens hyperkalemia

labs.v: 108 mg/dL (pre-treatment)
labs.r: 70–100 fasting; mildly elevated; important baseline before insulin/D50 administration to prevent iatrogenic hypoglycemia

labs.v: iCa 1.06 (low-normal) / Mg 2.4 / PO4 7.2 (markedly high)
labs.r: iCa 1.12–1.30 — borderline low; Mg WNL; PO4 typically high in ESRD (kidney can't excrete) — phosphate binders given with meals; the hyperphosphatemia + low Ca itself can contribute to arrhythmia risk

labs.v: ABG pH 7.28 / pCO2 30 / HCO3 14 / BE −10; Lactate 1.6
labs.r: Compensated/partially compensated metabolic acidosis; lactate normal — rules out shock as primary acidosis driver; uremic acidosis is the cause

labs.v: Hgb 9.6 (chronic anemia of CKD; baseline 10.2) / Hct 28% / Plt 188
labs.r: Hgb 13.5–17.5 men; chronic anemia of CKD from reduced erythropoietin; usually treated with darbepoetin or epoetin alfa; not the emergency

labs.v: Troponin 0.04 (baseline 0.03; ESRD patients have chronically mildly elevated trop) / CK 142 / BNP 1840
labs.r: Troponin 0–0.04; ESRD patients have stable baseline elevation — interpret as trend, not single value; BNP chronically elevated in HD-dependent patients with volume issues; not an acute MI here

labs.v: Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily, spironolactone 25 mg PO daily (started 3 weeks ago for HFrEF EF 35%), metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, sevelamer 1600 mg with meals (phosphate binder), darbepoetin q2 wk; trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS BID started 5 days ago by urgent care for UTI
labs.r: FOUR independent K-raising medications stacking: ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), aldosterone antagonist (spironolactone, especially with ACEi), Bactrim (TMP blocks ENaC → K-sparing effect), and missed HD removing none of it; metoprolol can also blunt the cellular K-shift

labs.v: Missed Saturday HD because his ride fell through; planned to 'just wait until Monday'; ate banana with breakfast, orange juice Sat/Sun, used a low-sodium salt substitute (potassium chloride based) thinking it was healthier; took an extra dose of spironolactone Sunday 'to keep up'
labs.r: Stacked dietary insults during the no-clearance window: salt substitutes are concentrated KCl (~10 mEq/g); high-K foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, tomatoes, leafy greens, dried fruit, beans, chocolate) accumulate without HD; medication adherence in the wrong direction

labs.v: Left upper-arm AV fistula (mature, in use x 4 years; bruit and thrill +); HD 3x/week MWF schedule at outpatient center; ESRD 8 years (HTN nephropathy + type 2 DM); last 'gain' between sessions usually 2–3 kg
labs.r: Working access available — direct cannulation possible for emergent HD; today's 'gain' likely 4+ kg; assess for volume overload signs

labs.v: Crackles at bilateral bases up to mid-lung; JVP elevated to angle of mandible at 45°; 2+ pitting edema to mid-shin; weight today 88 kg (post-HD dry weight 84 kg = ~4 kg fluid gain in 3 days)
labs.r: Volume overload from missed HD; complicates the picture but the K is the immediate killer; ultrafiltration during emergent HD will address both

labs.v: Generalized weakness (cannot get out of bed unassisted); palpitations; mild nausea; muscle 'twitchiness'; no chest pain, no syncope, no LOC; alert and oriented x3
labs.r: Hyperkalemia symptoms: weakness (proximal > distal), paresthesias, palpitations, GI upset; cardiac arrhythmias the most dangerous and may have NO warning — EKG monitoring is essential

nursesNote: 0905 — 62 y/o male with 8-year history of ESRD on HD 3x/week MWF (HTN nephropathy + type 2 DM), HFrEF EF 35%, mature L upper-arm AV fistula, presented to ED at 0820 with wife after MISSING his Saturday HD session ('ride fell through, planned to wait until Monday') and developing progressive weakness, palpitations, and 'twitchy' muscles since Sunday evening. Dietary indiscretion during the no-clearance window: banana with breakfast, orange juice Sat/Sun, low-sodium SALT SUBSTITUTE (concentrated KCl ~10 mEq/g) used 'because it was healthier,' and self-administered an EXTRA dose of his spironolactone Sunday 'to keep up.' Medication stack contributing to K elevation: lisinopril 20 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily (added 3 weeks ago for HFrEF), and TMP-SMX DS BID started 5 days ago by urgent care for UTI (TMP blocks ENaC → K-sparing). Metoprolol succinate can also blunt cellular K-shift. POC K at triage 7.4 mEq/L; venous confirmatory K 7.2; Cr 9.4, BUN 88, HCO3 14, pH 7.28 (uremic metabolic acidosis driving more K extracellular). 12-lead EKG at 0830: sinus brady 52, peaked narrow-based T waves V2–V5, widened QRS 132 ms (baseline 96), flattened P waves, PR 220 ms — severe hyperkalemia with conduction effects, no sine wave yet. Volume status: 4 kg gain since last HD, JVP elevated, bibasilar crackles, 2+ pitting edema — volume overload concurrent. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV slow push given at 0835 (membrane stabilization — buys 30–60 min, does NOT lower K); REGULAR INSULIN 10 units IV bolus PLUS 25 g D50 IV simultaneously given at 0845 (insulin drives K intracellularly via Na/K-ATPase; D50 prevents iatrogenic hypoglycemia in this patient with glucose 108); albuterol 20 mg nebulized started at 0850 (β2-agonist shifts K intracellularly via different pathway, additive to insulin); SODIUM BICARBONATE 50 mEq IV is being held — limited utility unless severe acidosis WITH normal anion gap; here uremic acidosis + risk of fluid overload makes it lower-priority while emergent HD is being arranged. PATIROMER 25.2 g PO ordered for the binder step (LOKELMA / sodium zirconium cyclosilicate is the newer alternative; KAYEXALATE / sodium polystyrene sulfonate avoided due to risk of intestinal necrosis especially in volume-overloaded patients; this is the high-yield update from older teaching). LISINOPRIL, SPIRONOLACTONE, and TMP-SMX HELD — coordinated with provider; UTI antibiotic switched to nitrofurantoin (after confirming sufficient eGFR for it — borderline here; cephalexin alternative). Renal team paged; the outpatient HD unit notified; the nephrology fellow is enroute; preparing for EMERGENT HD via the AV fistula in the dialysis suite within the hour (ultrafiltration will also address the volume overload). Continuous cardiac monitoring throughout; one PIV existing, working access is the AV fistula (DO NOT cannulate the fistula arm for IV/BP — protect access); STAT repeat K at 30 min post-insulin/D50 to verify response; blood glucose checks q15–30 min for 4 hr (insulin's K-shifting effect lasts 4–6 hr → hypoglycemia delayed risk). Wife at bedside, coordinating with daughter who is en route; chaplain offered if needed. Cardiologist not currently consulted (no acute MI); the bradycardia and EKG changes are expected to resolve as K corrects.
providerOrders: Continuous cardiac monitoring; 12-lead EKG NOW, repeat after each intervention and at 30 min post-correction, then per protocol
STAT K+ q30 min until <5.5 mEq/L, then q2h until stable; recheck glucose, magnesium, ionized calcium with each K draw; repeat ABG/lactate as needed
AVOID drawing K from the fistula arm (or hemolysis-prone source); use peripheral venous from contralateral arm; protect fistula — no BP cuff, no IV, no blood draws on that arm
STEP 1 — MEMBRANE STABILIZATION (immediate, 1–3 min onset, 30–60 min duration):

CALCIUM GLUCONATE 10% 1 g (10 mL) IV slow push over 2–5 min via peripheral IV; may repeat in 5 min if EKG changes persist; if central access available, calcium chloride 10% 1 g IV can be substituted (provides 3x more elemental Ca per dose); DO NOT use peripheral CaCl (vesicant)
⚠️ If patient is on DIGOXIN: give calcium MORE SLOWLY (over 30 min in 100 mL D5W) — rapid Ca can precipitate dig toxicity arrhythmias (this patient is NOT on dig — proceed normally)
STEP 2 — SHIFT K+ INTRACELLULARLY (10–30 min onset, 4–6 hr duration):
REGULAR INSULIN 10 units IV bolus PLUS DEXTROSE 50% 25 g (50 mL = 1 amp D50) IV simultaneously (give insulin only if glucose >250 mg/dL to avoid hypoglycemia); recheck BG q15–30 min x 4 hr
ALBUTEROL 10–20 mg via nebulizer over 15 min (10x normal asthma dose); ADDITIVE to insulin (different mechanism); avoid in tachyarrhythmias

SODIUM BICARBONATE 50 mEq (1 amp) IV — limited utility EXCEPT in severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2) WITH normal anion gap; routine use in chronic acidosis (this patient) is controversial and not standard; risk of volume overload in this patient with HF + missed HD
STEP 3 — REMOVE K+ FROM BODY (slower; minutes-to-hours; only this actually lowers total body K):

EMERGENT HEMODIALYSIS via AV fistula (definitive treatment; arrange within hour; coordinate with nephrology, dialysis nurse, vascular access); ultrafiltration will simultaneously address volume overload

PATIROMER 25.2 g PO single dose (or LOKELMA / sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO) — gut binder; reduces K over hours-to-days; safer than Kayexalate (which has risk of intestinal necrosis especially in post-op, ileus, or critically ill patients)

⚠️ KAYEXALATE (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) AVOIDED — FDA boxed warning for intestinal necrosis; if used, never co-administer with sorbitol (worsens necrosis); modern practice favors Patiromer or Lokelma
Loop diuretics (furosemide) are useful in patients with intact urine output; THIS PATIENT IS ANURIC ON HD — diuretics WILL NOT WORK; do not give
STEP 4 — STOP THE SOURCE:
HOLD lisinopril, spironolactone, TMP-SMX; switch UTI antibiotic to cephalexin or nitrofurantoin (verify renal dosing for nitro borderline in ESRD)

Strict K-restricted diet (<2 g/day K) during hospitalization; remove salt substitutes; review high-K foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, tomatoes, leafy greens, dried fruit, beans, chocolate, dairy in large amounts); RD consult
Two PIVs (peripheral, NOT on fistula arm); central line if hemodynamic instability or rapid escalation needed; do not delay HD for line placement
Continuous pulse oximetry; q15-min vital signs during active treatment; defibrillator at bedside; ACLS team aware
Strict I&O; daily weights; UOP — note patient is anuric on HD (no UOP expected); volume status reassessment q4h until stable
Cardiology consultation NOT routinely indicated (bradycardia and EKG changes expected to resolve with K correction); reassess if arrhythmia persists post-correction

Renal team primary; coordinate with outpatient HD center for ongoing care; patient education before discharge on never missing HD, dietary K, medication review, salt substitute avoidance, signs of hyperkalemia, transportation backup plan
Social work: explore transportation barriers to HD (this is the actionable root cause); paratransit services, ride-share programs for dialysis patients, patient navigator, charity transport options

Discharge planning: outpatient HD scheduled for tomorrow per usual MWF schedule (or earlier if K remains high); medication reconciliation — likely permanent D/C of spironolactone, reduced ACEi or substitute; nephrology follow-up within 1 week; primary care coordination

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Potassium 7.4 (POC) / 7.2 (confirmatory) — severe hyperkalemia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — life-threatening. Any K ≥7.0 OR any K with EKG changes is a medical emergency regardless of absolute number. POC vs lab discrepancy can occur (hemolysis, lab delay) — treat the higher value with EKG correlation. Begin the 3-step ladder immediately: stabilize → shift → remove.

opts.text: EKG: peaked T waves V2–V5, widened QRS 132 ms (baseline 96), flattened P, PR 220 ms — full conduction abnormality progression
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms severity and indicates IMMEDIATE need for calcium. EKG progression: peaked T (≥5.5) → flattened P / prolonged PR (≥6.5) → widened QRS (≥7.0) → sine wave / VF / asystole (≥8.0+). This patient is at the widened-QRS stage — minutes from sine wave / arrest without intervention.

opts.text: Missed Saturday HD; ESRD with no native renal clearance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — explains the no-clearance window. Patient has NO way to excrete K without HD; every meal and every dose of K-raising medication accumulated since Friday's session. Definitive treatment is HD via the existing AV fistula — coordinate emergently.

opts.text: Four K-raising medications: lisinopril (ACEi) + spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist) + TMP-SMX (TMP blocks ENaC) + extra spironolactone dose taken yesterday
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the polypharmacy storm. ACEi + aldosterone antagonist together is well-known to raise K (the RALES trial limit); adding TMP-SMX compounds it (TMP acts like a K-sparing diuretic on ENaC); the extra spironolactone dose was an overdose. ALL must be HELD; UTI antibiotic switched to a non-K-raising option.

opts.text: Dietary indiscretion: salt substitute (concentrated KCl), banana, OJ, daily during the no-HD window
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — modifiable behavioral contributor. Salt substitutes are ~10 mEq K per gram (one teaspoon ≈ 60 mEq K) — a major hidden K source. Patient education on label reading; identify high-K foods; consultation with renal dietitian; remove from the home.

opts.text: Metabolic acidosis pH 7.28 / HCO3 14 — uremic acidosis driving more K extracellular
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — compounds the hyperkalemia. Acidosis shifts K out of cells via H+/K+ exchange. Correcting acidosis pulls some K back into cells (one rationale for bicarb in select cases). Definitive correction is HD, which also corrects the acidosis.

opts.text: Volume overload: 4 kg gain in 3 days, JVP elevated, bibasilar crackles, 2+ pitting edema
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — concurrent emergency. Pulmonary edema is a parallel complication of missed HD. Limits the use of IV fluid resuscitation and bicarbonate (high-Na, volume-adding). Ultrafiltration during HD addresses both K and volume simultaneously.

opts.text: Bradycardia 52 bpm (down from baseline 78), generalized weakness, palpitations, muscle twitchiness
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — hyperkalemia clinical signs. Bradycardia and conduction blocks are the late sign before arrest. Weakness is from membrane depolarization disrupting neuromuscular function. Symptomatic hyperkalemia is always an indication for treatment.

opts.text: AV fistula in L upper arm — patient's dialysis access
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — both opportunity and protection issue. Mature fistula = immediate HD possible. PROTECTION: NO BP cuff, NO IV, NO blood draws, NO restraints on the fistula arm. Verify bruit and thrill q shift. Loss of access in ESRD is devastating.

opts.text: Transportation barrier — missed HD because 'ride fell through'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the actionable root cause. Without solving the transportation issue, this will happen again. Social work consult for paratransit, dialysis-center transport partnerships, patient navigator, charity transport; back-up plan documented before discharge.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no drug allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Glucose 108 mg/dL
opts.rat: Within normal — but important BASELINE before giving IV insulin. If glucose were >250, insulin alone would suffice; with glucose 108, D50 must be co-administered to prevent iatrogenic hypoglycemia. Monitor BG q15–30 min x 4 hr.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Severe hyperkalemia depolarizes the resting membrane potential of cardiomyocytes, decreasing the gradient required for Na+ channel activation; result: slowed conduction (peaked T → prolonged PR → widened QRS) → eventual sine wave → ventricular fibrillation or asystole
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. The EKG changes are direct evidence of the depolarized state. Untreated, the progression is to arrest. Calcium gluconate restores the gradient by antagonizing the depolarizing effect — does NOT lower K, but stabilizes the membrane until shift/removal interventions work.

opts.text: CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV antagonizes the cardiac membrane effects of hyperkalemia within 1–3 minutes; effect lasts 30–60 minutes; DOES NOT lower potassium — only buys time while other interventions take effect; may repeat in 5 min if EKG changes persist
opts.rat: CORRECT — most-missed point. Calcium is membrane stabilization, NOT K removal. Many students think they have 'treated' hyperkalemia by giving calcium — they have not. They have a 30-minute window. The K MUST be shifted and removed during that window.

opts.text: REGULAR INSULIN drives K+ INTO cells by activating Na+/K+-ATPase (the same mechanism that drives glucose uptake); onset 10–30 min; duration 4–6 hr; co-administer DEXTROSE 50% 25 g IV to prevent iatrogenic hypoglycemia (unless glucose >250 mg/dL); monitor BG q15–30 min x 4 hr
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield pharmacology. Insulin lowers serum K by ~0.5–1.0 mEq/L. The hypoglycemia risk is the most common adverse event; even with D50, delayed hypoglycemia at 2–4 hours is well-documented. Patients with ESRD have impaired insulin clearance — extended monitoring is essential.

opts.text: ALBUTEROL via nebulizer (10–20 mg, 10x asthma dose) shifts K+ intracellularly via β2-agonist activation of Na+/K+-ATPase; ADDITIVE to insulin via a different pathway; lowers K by ~0.5–1.0 mEq/L; avoid in tachyarrhythmias and ischemic heart disease
opts.rat: CORRECT — adjunct shift therapy. Mechanism is parallel to insulin but works on a different receptor system. Useful as add-on when insulin/D50 alone is insufficient; not enough on its own for severe hyperkalemia. Note the dose is 10x the asthma dose — common point of confusion.

opts.text: EMERGENT HEMODIALYSIS is the only intervention that REMOVES potassium from the body; everything else (calcium, insulin/D50, albuterol, bicarbonate) is buying time; in ESRD with missed HD, HD is the definitive treatment and must be initiated within hours
opts.rat: CORRECT — the strategic framing. Calcium/shift therapies are bridges to dialysis. Patiromer/Lokelma binders also remove K but more slowly (hours to days). In severe hyperkalemia with EKG changes in an ESRD patient, EMERGENT HD is the destination intervention.

opts.text: Modern PATIROMER (Veltassa) or SODIUM ZIRCONIUM CYCLOSILICATE (Lokelma) are the preferred gut potassium binders; they have replaced KAYEXALATE (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) in most settings due to the FDA boxed warning for intestinal necrosis associated with Kayexalate — especially in critically ill, post-op, or volume-overloaded patients
opts.rat: CORRECT — modern update over older teaching. Kayexalate-sorbitol combinations have well-documented colonic necrosis cases; FDA boxed warning. Patiromer onset 4–7 hr, sustained effect; Lokelma onset 1 hr, faster. Both safer alternatives. Older nursing textbooks still teach Kayexalate first-line — this is outdated.

opts.text: ACE INHIBITORS, ALDOSTERONE ANTAGONISTS (spironolactone, eplerenone), and TMP-SMX (the TMP component blocks ENaC like amiloride) all RAISE potassium; the combination is well-documented to cause severe hyperkalemia — this patient had ALL THREE plus missed HD
opts.rat: CORRECT — polypharmacy pattern. The RALES trial showed the spironolactone + ACEi combination is potent. TMP-SMX added is a known cause of hyperkalemia in renal patients. Other K-raisers to know: ARBs, K-sparing diuretics (amiloride, triamterene), heparin (suppresses aldosterone), NSAIDs, beta-blockers (blunt shift), digoxin in toxicity, succinylcholine (in burns/crush/neuro injury).

opts.text: SALT SUBSTITUTES are typically potassium chloride based (~10 mEq K per gram, ~50–60 mEq per teaspoon); a hidden but major K source in dialysis patients told to 'cut back on sodium'; must be specifically warned against
opts.rat: CORRECT — patient education point. ESRD patients receive sodium-restriction teaching but may then turn to salt substitutes thinking they're safer. They contain a critical dose of K. Educate on label reading (look for 'potassium chloride' as the first or only ingredient).

opts.text: LOOP DIURETICS (furosemide) effectively lower K through urinary excretion and should be used in any patient with hyperkalemia
opts.rat: ⚠️ Partially wrong. Loop diuretics lower K in patients WITH PRESERVED URINE OUTPUT — they don't work in anuric ESRD patients on HD. This patient is on HD and produces no urine — diuretics will have no effect. The 'use diuretics' answer is a trap when the patient is ESRD/anuric.

opts.text: Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV is the most effective shift therapy and should be given immediately in any patient with severe hyperkalemia
opts.rat: ⚠️ Overstated. Bicarbonate has LIMITED utility — modest effect, only useful in severe metabolic acidosis with NORMAL anion gap. In this patient with volume overload and uremic acidosis, the risk of volume gain outweighs benefit. NOT first-line; many guidelines have moved away from routine use. Insulin/D50 and albuterol are the workhorse shift therapies.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Lethal Arrhythmia (VF / asystole / sine wave progression) r/t severe hyperkalemia K 7.2 with widened QRS, peaked T, flattened P, and bradycardia
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. This is what kills the patient in the next 30 minutes. CALCIUM GLUCONATE IMMEDIATELY for membrane stabilization. Continuous cardiac monitoring, defibrillator at bedside, ACLS team aware. The widened QRS is the warning that arrest is imminent.

opts.text: Risk for Failure of K Removal r/t ESRD with anuria + missed HD; only HD removes K from the body in this patient
opts.rat: #2 — Source control. The shift therapies (insulin, albuterol) buy hours; the K returns to serum as their effect wears off. EMERGENT HD is the destination intervention. Coordinate immediately — fistula, nephrology, dialysis nurse.

opts.text: Risk for Iatrogenic Hypoglycemia r/t IV insulin given for K shift in an ESRD patient with impaired insulin clearance
opts.rat: #3 — Treatment-related risk. Hypoglycemia from insulin/D50 occurs in up to 20% of patients, often delayed at 2–4 hours after the dose. ESRD impairs insulin clearance — extended monitoring (BG q15–30 min x 4 hr) is required. D50 co-administration mitigates but does not eliminate.

opts.text: Risk for Volume Overload Decompensation (flash pulmonary edema) r/t 4 kg fluid gain in 3 days, HFrEF EF 35%, bibasilar crackles, JVP elevation
opts.rat: #4 — Parallel complication. Volume overload is dangerous but is addressed concurrently by ultrafiltration during HD. Avoid IV fluid boluses; sit patient upright; oxygen as needed; do not delay HD coordination. Bicarbonate (high Na) should be avoided here.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit r/t missed HD precipitant + dietary K (salt substitute) + medication management; need for relapse prevention planning
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained recovery. Once stabilized, education + social work for transportation + medication reconciliation + renal dietitian consult prevent the next admission. Single most important discharge piece is the transportation backup plan.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: CALCIUM GLUCONATE 10% 1 g (10 mL) IV slow push over 2–5 min via peripheral IV; may repeat in 5 min if EKG changes persist
opts.rat: FIRST — membrane stabilization. Onset 1–3 min, duration 30–60 min. Does NOT lower K but buys the window for shift/removal interventions to work. Use CaGluc peripherally; CaCl2 only via central line (vesicant). Repeat if EKG changes persist.

opts.text: REGULAR INSULIN 10 units IV bolus PLUS DEXTROSE 50% 25 g IV simultaneously; recheck BG q15–30 min x 4 hr
opts.rat: Core shift therapy. Insulin drives K into cells via Na/K-ATPase; D50 prevents hypoglycemia (glucose 108 here — clearly needs D50). Lowers K ~0.5–1.0 mEq/L. ESRD patients require EXTENDED BG monitoring due to impaired insulin clearance.

opts.text: ALBUTEROL 10–20 mg via nebulizer over 15 min
opts.rat: Adjunctive shift therapy — additive to insulin via different receptor pathway (β2-agonist on Na/K-ATPase). 10x normal asthma dose. Lowers K ~0.5–1.0 mEq/L. Avoid in active tachyarrhythmias or significant ischemic heart disease.

opts.text: PATIROMER 25.2 g PO or LOKELMA 10 g PO single dose
opts.rat: Modern gut K binders. Preferred over Kayexalate due to FDA boxed warning for colonic necrosis. Onset 1 hr (Lokelma) or 4–7 hr (Patiromer); sustained effect. Useful adjunct while awaiting HD.

opts.text: KAYEXALATE (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) 30 g PO with sorbitol
opts.rat: ⚠️ Outdated and unsafe. FDA boxed warning for intestinal necrosis, especially in volume-overloaded, post-op, or critically ill patients. Sorbitol co-administration worsens the necrosis risk. Use Patiromer or Lokelma instead. Many older textbooks still teach Kayexalate first-line — this is outdated practice.

opts.text: EMERGENT HEMODIALYSIS via the L upper-arm AV fistula; coordinate with nephrology, dialysis nurse, and vascular access; arrange within the hour
opts.rat: DEFINITIVE therapy. Only intervention that removes K from the body in an ESRD patient. Concurrently addresses volume overload through ultrafiltration. Do not delay other interventions while arranging HD — but HD is the destination.

opts.text: FUROSEMIDE 80 mg IV to lower K through urinary excretion
opts.rat: ⚠️ Will not work in an anuric ESRD patient on HD. Loop diuretics require kidney function and urine output to act. This patient produces no urine. Common trap — useful in NON-ESRD hyperkalemia with intact urine output.

opts.text: HOLD lisinopril, spironolactone, and TMP-SMX; switch UTI antibiotic to cephalexin or nitrofurantoin (verify renal dosing)
opts.rat: Stop the source. All four medications raise K; without removal they will continue to do so. Replace TMP-SMX with non-K-raising option. Coordinate with provider; document on MAR. Consider permanent D/C of spironolactone given recurrence risk.

opts.text: Strict K-restricted diet <2 g/day; remove salt substitutes; renal dietitian consult
opts.rat: Dietary correction. ESRD patients require lifelong K restriction (and PO4 and Na). Salt substitutes are a high-yield hidden K source. RD consult for individualized teaching and food list.

opts.text: 1 L LR bolus to support BP
opts.rat: ⚠️ LR contains 4 mEq K per liter — raises K. Patient is already volume-overloaded (4 kg gain, JVP up, crackles). Patient's BP is adequate (108/64); fluid bolus is not needed and would worsen the volume overload. If volume support were needed, NS preferred over LR — but here, NEITHER is needed.

opts.text: Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV push for the metabolic acidosis pH 7.28
opts.rat: Limited utility here. Bicarbonate has modest K-lowering effect, only useful in severe acidosis with NORMAL anion gap. Patient has uremic acidosis (high anion gap from accumulated organic acids); volume overload makes the high-Na bicarb load undesirable. Definitive correction comes from HD.

opts.text: Protect the AV fistula: no BP cuff, no IV, no blood draws, no restraints on the L arm; verify bruit and thrill q shift
opts.rat: Access protection. Fistula loss is a major morbidity in ESRD (months of recovery to mature a new one). Mark the arm; communicate at hand-off; educate the patient and family; document protective measures.

opts.text: Social work consult for transportation barriers; explore paratransit, dialysis-center transport, charity rides; document a backup plan before discharge
opts.rat: Root-cause prevention. The missed HD happened because of a transportation failure — without solving it, this will recur. Document the plan in discharge instructions. Patient navigator can help with ongoing logistics.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: CALCIUM GLUCONATE 10% 1 g IV slow push (2–5 min); continuous cardiac monitoring; defibrillator at bedside; verify peripheral IV access (NOT fistula arm)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Membrane stabilization buys you the window for everything else. 1–3 min onset. Repeat if EKG doesn't improve in 5 min. Cardiac monitoring is non-negotiable throughout treatment. Protect the fistula arm.

opts.text: REGULAR INSULIN 10 units IV + DEXTROSE 50% 25 g IV simultaneously; ALBUTEROL 10–20 mg neb over 15 min; baseline BG before insulin
opts.rat: #2 — Shift therapy. Concurrent with #1 setup, immediately after calcium. Both interventions can run together. Onset 10–30 min for insulin; albuterol additive via different pathway. BG q15–30 min x 4 hr — delayed hypoglycemia is the main risk.

opts.text: HOLD lisinopril, spironolactone, TMP-SMX; switch UTI antibiotic; PATIROMER or LOKELMA PO single dose; strict K-restricted diet
opts.rat: #3 — Stop the source + start removal. Each missed dose of K-raisers is a future K-spike prevented. Gut binder begins K removal over hours. Diet correction immediately. Coordinate with provider for med list.

opts.text: Arrange EMERGENT HEMODIALYSIS via AV fistula; page nephrology; notify dialysis nurse and vascular access; transport to HD suite once stable
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive treatment. The destination intervention. Coordinate while shift therapies are working. Concurrent ultrafiltration also addresses volume overload. Stable for transport once EKG improves with calcium + shift therapies.

opts.text: Social work consult for transportation; renal dietitian consult; medication reconciliation with permanent regimen changes; patient/family education before discharge
opts.rat: #5 — Discharge planning and relapse prevention. The transportation issue is the actionable root cause. Education on dietary K, salt substitutes, medication interactions, signs of hyperkalemia, and the absolute importance of not missing HD. Document backup plan.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 30 min after calcium + insulin/D50 + albuterol: K 6.2 (down from 7.2), EKG with narrower QRS 108 ms, less peaked T waves, HR 72, BP 122/74; en route to HD suite
opts.rat: Response to shift therapy. K dropping; EKG narrowing toward baseline; rate improving. Continue to HD for definitive removal. Maintain cardiac monitoring; BG q15–30 min x 4 hr; expect transient K-rebound after shift effect wears off if HD delayed.

opts.text: Post-HD (3-hr session): K 4.1, EKG returned to baseline (sinus rhythm 78, narrow QRS, normal T waves, normal P-R), 3 kg fluid removed via ultrafiltration, crackles cleared, JVP normal, patient feeling 'much better'
opts.rat: Successful definitive treatment. K normalized; volume status corrected; EKG normal. Plan for return to usual MWF outpatient HD schedule (or modified schedule short-term). Discharge planning and education are now the focus.

opts.text: Discharge day 2: K 4.4 after second HD; medication list updated (spironolactone permanently D/C, lisinopril reduced, TMP-SMX replaced with cephalexin for UTI); transportation plan in place with paratransit confirmed; renal dietitian session completed; follow-up with nephrology in 1 week
opts.rat: Comprehensive discharge. Education includes: signs of hyperkalemia (weakness, palpitations, twitchiness), the absolute requirement of never missing HD, salt substitute avoidance, high-K food list, medication review with every provider, calling renal team before starting any new medication.

opts.text: 2 hours after calcium + insulin/D50: blood glucose 42 mg/dL; patient diaphoretic and confused — severe iatrogenic hypoglycemia
opts.rat: Iatrogenic complication of K shift therapy. D50 25 g IV push immediately; recheck BG in 15 min; identify the missed q15-min check; reinforce ESRD-specific extended monitoring (q15–30 min x 4 hr). Reportable event; education for staff.

opts.text: Hour 4 (before HD arranged): K rises from 6.2 back to 6.9; EKG widening again; patient reports renewed palpitations and weakness — shift therapy wearing off without removal
opts.rat: Predictable rebound — shift therapy wears off in 4–6 hours; K returns to serum from cells. The lesson: shift therapy is a bridge, NOT a destination. EMERGENT HD must follow within hours, not be delayed. Repeat calcium and shift therapy if needed; expedite HD.

opts.text: During treatment: patient develops VF; defibrillation x 2 with ROSC; ACLS run; emergent HD initiated immediately afterward
opts.rat: The feared outcome. The widened QRS on initial EKG signaled this risk. After ROSC: continue calcium, shift therapy, immediate HD, full ACLS workup, ICU admission, transthoracic echo, cardiology consult. Post-arrest care including targeted temperature management per protocol.

opts.text: Patient continues to refuse to discontinue spironolactone, saying 'my cardiologist gave it to me for my heart'; nurse-led education session with patient and wife, contact cardiologist to coordinate alternative HF regimen
opts.rat: Common conflict — multiple specialists, conflicting priorities. Coordinate: cardiology can manage HF with non-K-raising regimen (sacubitril/valsartan is also K-raising; SGLT2-inhibitors are safer K-wise; metoprolol stays). The patient's understanding of WHY each medication is prescribed (and why some now can't be) is the key teaching moment. Document the discussion; multi-disciplinary team meeting if needed.

opts.text: Week 2 follow-up: K 4.6 on MWF HD schedule with new transportation plan in place; no missed sessions; medication regimen reconciled; patient verbalizes understanding of dietary K and salt substitute avoidance
opts.rat: Sustained recovery. The transportation fix is the highest-yield change. Continue ongoing monitoring, dietitian follow-up, nephrology q1–3 mo as usual ESRD care. Reinforce education at every encounter; patient handout for emergency contacts and hyperkalemia warning signs.

opts.text: Persistent chronic anemia Hgb 9.6 at discharge despite stable HD; on darbepoetin q2 wk
opts.rat: Expected — anemia of CKD does not resolve quickly even with ESA therapy. Target Hgb 10–11 g/dL (NOT 'normal') per KDIGO guidelines — over-correction worsens cardiovascular outcomes and stroke risk in ESRD. Continue darbepoetin, iron studies, monitor.

opts.text: Bilateral 2+ pitting edema persists at discharge despite ultrafiltration during HD
opts.rat: Expected in HD patients between sessions if dry weight is set higher than ideal, or if persistent volume overload. Reassess dry weight at outpatient unit; verify adherence to fluid restriction (typically 1–1.5 L/day); educate on weighing daily and reporting >2 kg gain between sessions.

title: Severe ARDS from Bacterial Pneumonia Requiring Prone Positioning
subtitle: P/F < 100, Lung-Protective TV 4–6 mL/kg IBW, and the 16-Hour Proning Clock — Plus Why You Don't Disconnect the Vent to Reposition

patient.name: L. Mancini
patient.admitDate: Day 4 of MICU stay; intubated Day 2; transferred from medicine floor with worsening hypoxemia
patient.room: MICU Bed 7 (negative-pressure capable, currently on droplet precautions for pneumonia)

vitals.time: Day 1 admission (medicine floor, on 6 L NC)
Day 2 (intubation in MICU after failed HFNC + NIV trial)
Day 4, 0600 (CURRENT — pre-proning ABG and assessment; P/F 78 on FiO2 0.8 PEEP 14)
vitals.rr: Vent set rate 26; total rate 26 (paralyzed, no overbreathing)

labs.n: ABG (0600, just prior to proning decision)
labs.v: pH 7.28 / pCO2 56 / pO2 62 / HCO3 22 / SaO2 86% on FiO2 0.80 PEEP 14
labs.r: pH 7.35–7.45; pCO2 35–45; pO2 80–100. PERMISSIVE HYPERCAPNIA acceptable to pH 7.20 during lung-protective ventilation; the goal is lung protection not normal numbers

labs.v: 78 (PaO2 62 / FiO2 0.80) — moderate–severe ARDS by Berlin criteria
labs.r: Berlin definition ARDS: mild 200–300; moderate 100–200; severe ≤100. P/F <150 on FiO2 ≥0.60 PEEP ≥5 with sedation/paralysis = formal indication for prone positioning (PROSEVA trial)

labs.v: VC-AC, TV 380 mL (= 6 mL/kg IBW of 63 kg for height 174 cm), set rate 26, FiO2 0.80, PEEP 14, FLOW 60 L/min; plateau pressure 28 (target <30); driving pressure (Pplat − PEEP) 14 (target <15)
labs.r: ARDSnet protocol: TV 4–6 mL/kg IDEAL body weight (NOT actual), plateau <30, driving pressure <15, FiO2/PEEP ladder titrated to SpO2 88–95%. Permissive hypercapnia to pH 7.20 acceptable

labs.v: Portable CXR (this AM): bilateral diffuse alveolar infiltrates worse than yesterday (R > L); no pneumothorax; ET tube tip 4 cm above carina; right IJ TLC tip in upper SVC; OG tube in stomach
labs.r: Bilateral infiltrates not fully explained by cardiac failure or volume overload — meets Berlin ARDS criteria (acute onset, bilateral opacities, PaO2/FiO2 ≤300, not CHF)

labs.v: Sputum cx + blood cx GROW STREPTOCOCCUS PNEUMONIAE (pansusceptible); SARS-CoV-2 PCR negative; influenza/RSV neg; Legionella urinary antigen neg; pneumococcal urinary antigen positive
labs.r: Pneumococcal pneumonia — community-acquired severe pneumonia. Treat: CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV daily x 7–10 days (PCN allergy with rash only, cephalosporin generally safe with low cross-reactivity for non-anaphylactic PCN reactions); avoid IV PCN itself

labs.v: Propofol 35 mcg/kg/min (RASS −5 deep sedation needed for ventilator synchrony); fentanyl 75 mcg/hr (pain control); CISATRACURIUM (Nimbex) 3 mcg/kg/min started this morning to facilitate proning and abolish dyssynchrony
labs.r: Lung-protective ventilation requires deep sedation; paralysis indicated for refractory dyssynchrony, severe ARDS, or proning facilitation. ALWAYS sedate BEFORE paralyzing — paralyzed-but-awake is torture; monitor train-of-four target 1–2/4 twitches

labs.v: On NOREPINEPHRINE 4 mcg/min (started yesterday for septic hypotension); CVP 12; lactate 2.4 (down from 4.8 on admission); UOP 0.6 mL/kg/hr
labs.r: Septic shock from pneumonia driving need for vasopressor support; lactate improving with source control; UOP at target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr

labs.v: WBC 22.4 (up from 18 admission), Hgb 9.8, Plt 178; INR 1.3; Cr 1.6 (baseline 0.9); Na 138, K 4.4, HCO3 22, glucose 168 (on insulin gtt)
labs.r: Leukocytosis from sepsis; AKI from septic kidney injury; insulin gtt for stress hyperglycemia (target 140–180 in ICU per SCC guidelines)

labs.v: Type 2 DM, BMI 36 (obesity), former smoker 30 pack-year (quit 5 yr ago), no prior pneumococcal vaccination (refused at PCP visit 2 years ago), HTN, hyperlipidemia
labs.r: Multiple risk factors for severe pneumonia: obesity, diabetes, smoking history, no vaccination. PCV13 + PPSV23 indicated for all adults ≥65 and high-risk adults — would have prevented or attenuated this

labs.v: Stage 1 pressure injury sacrum (developed Day 3 supine), no other wounds, ET tube secured at 22 cm, OG tube at 60 cm, right IJ TLC, L radial arterial line, R foot SpO2 probe; foley to gravity
labs.r: Pressure injury risk markedly increased during proning — face, ears, chest, knees, shins, dorsum of feet, genitals are pressure points. Pre-proning skin assessment + protective padding required

labs.v: 1) ET tube; 2) OG tube; 3) R IJ triple-lumen central line with norepi + propofol + maintenance fluids; 4) L radial arterial line; 5) Foley; 6) R foot SpO2 probe; 7) telemetry leads
labs.r: Every line is a dislodgement risk during proning — pre-prone inventory + securement check + designated team member to manage each line during the turn is the standard PROSEVA-trial protocol

labs.v: Wife is healthcare proxy; daughter (RN) flew in from out of state; family meeting scheduled today to discuss prognosis, escalation (ECMO consultation if proning fails), and goals of care; he is currently 'Full Code' but family open to revisiting if trajectory poor
labs.r: Severe ARDS mortality remains ~40% even with optimal care; ECMO referral criteria: P/F <80 after optimization including proning, plateau >30, refractory hypercapnic acidosis pH <7.20

nursesNote: 0600 — 58 y/o male MICU Day 4 / intubated Day 2 with severe pneumococcal pneumonia and progressively worsening ARDS now meeting BERLIN SEVERE criteria with P/F 78 on FiO2 0.80 PEEP 14. Pre-proning ABG: pH 7.28 / pCO2 56 / pO2 62 (permissive hypercapnia accepted to pH 7.20 per lung-protective strategy). On VC-AC TV 380 mL (= 6 mL/kg IBW for height 174 cm, ideal body weight 63 kg) set rate 26, FiO2 0.80, PEEP 14, FLOW 60 L/min; plateau pressure 28 (target <30); driving pressure 14 (target <15). On norepi 4 mcg/min for sepsis-related hypotension (CVP 12, MAP 71, lactate 2.4 trending down from 4.8, UOP 0.6 mL/kg/hr — sufficient). Sedation: propofol 35 mcg/kg/min and fentanyl 75 mcg/hr to RASS −5 deep sedation; CISATRACURIUM 3 mcg/kg/min started at 0530 to abolish ventilator dyssynchrony and facilitate proning (always sedate first; paralyzed-but-awake is unacceptable). Train-of-four 2/4 — at target. Intensivist and respiratory therapy decision made at 0530 round: PRONE POSITIONING indicated by PROSEVA criteria (P/F <150 on FiO2 ≥0.60 PEEP ≥5 with sedation/paralysis). Target proning duration ≥16 hours / 24-hour cycle, repeat for up to 7–10 days if benefit. Pre-proning bundle in progress: pre-prone ABG (above, baseline for comparison), eye care with lubricating ointment + tape closed, sacrum pressure injury (Stage 1) noted and prophylactic foam dressings applied to face/chest/iliac crests/knees/shins/dorsum of feet/genitals; ET tube secured at 22 cm reverified; oral care; OG tube position confirmed and clamped; tube feeds held x 1 hr pre-prone; central line dressing intact; arterial line dressing intact; foley to gravity and emptied; all lines re-secured and slack adjusted; SpO2 probe moved from finger to ear (more reliable in prone). PROSEVA-style turn team assembled: 5 staff total — 1 RN at head/airway (the team leader), 2 RNs/staff on each side (chest and legs), 1 RT at the vent. Sequence: pre-oxygenate FiO2 1.0 x 5 min, brief disconnect AVOIDED (use closed-suction circuit, do NOT routinely disconnect vent to reposition — desaturation cascade risk), log-roll patient onto side then onto prone position with arms in 'swimmer's position' (one up, one down) — alternate q2h. Anticipate transient hemodynamic perturbation in first 5–10 min after turning (often improves at 30 min as recruitment occurs). Post-prone monitoring: HR/BP/SpO2 q5min x 30 min then q15 min x 2 hr; ABG at 1 hr post-prone to assess response; if SpO2 fails to improve by 4 hr, prone-non-responder — discuss further escalation including ECMO referral. ABCDEF bundle ongoing: Assess pain (CPOT score q4h), Both spontaneous awakening + spontaneous breathing trials are HELD during paralysis but will resume when paralytic off, Choice of sedation reviewed daily, Delirium (CAM-ICU not assessable during paralysis), Early mobility (held during paralysis but PT consult for ROM), Family engagement (meeting today). Antibiotic regimen: ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily Day 3 of 7–10 day course (pcn allergy with rash — cephalosporin generally safe for non-anaphylactic PCN reactions; consult pharmacy if any anaphylactic history). Insulin gtt for stress hyperglycemia targeting 140–180 mg/dL. Family meeting scheduled 1400 with intensivist, palliative care, wife and RN daughter; will review trajectory, prognosis (severe ARDS mortality ~40%), and decision points for escalation vs. comfort-focused care if not improving.
providerOrders: MICU admission; intensivist primary; pulmonology and infectious disease consultations; palliative care consultation for family support and goals-of-care alignment

Lung-protective mechanical ventilation per ARDSnet: VC-AC mode, TV 4–6 mL/kg IDEAL body weight (calculated from height, NOT actual weight), set rate to maintain pH ≥7.20 with permissive hypercapnia accepted, FiO2/PEEP ladder titrated to SpO2 88–95% / PaO2 55–80 mmHg, plateau pressure <30 cm H2O, driving pressure (Pplat − PEEP) <15 cm H2O

PRONE POSITIONING indicated (PROSEVA criteria: P/F <150 on FiO2 ≥0.60 PEEP ≥5 with sedation/paralysis); target duration ≥16 hours per 24-hour cycle; repeat cycles for up to 7–10 days if responder; arms in alternating swimmer's position q2h to prevent pressure injury and brachial plexus stretch

PROSEVA proning team: minimum 5 staff (1 RN team leader at head/airway, 2 on each side, 1 RT at vent); pre-prone bundle: pre-oxygenate FiO2 1.0 x 5 min, eye care with lubricant + tape closed, foam dressings on pressure points, line/tube inventory and securement, OG tube clamp and feeds held x 1 hr, SpO2 probe to ear
DO NOT routinely disconnect vent circuit during proning maneuver (desaturation cascade); use closed-suction circuit; brief inline disconnect only if absolutely necessary

Sedation: PROPOFOL 5–80 mcg/kg/min titrated to RASS −4 to −5 during proning/paralysis (then lighten to RASS −2 to 0 once paralytic off); FENTANYL 25–150 mcg/hr for analgesia; CISATRACURIUM 1–5 mcg/kg/min for paralysis only with deep sedation confirmed FIRST
Train-of-four q4h, target 1–2/4 twitches (avoid prolonged 0/4 — risk of post-ICU weakness); document; eye care q2h to prevent corneal abrasion during paralysis
AVOID benzodiazepines as primary sedation (associated with delirium and longer mech vent days); reserve for refractory cases or alcohol/benzo withdrawal

Sepsis bundle ongoing: CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV daily x 7–10 days (PCN allergy rash only — cephalosporin appropriate); blood and sputum cx already growing pansusceptible S. pneumoniae; narrow as appropriate; pharmacy consult if anaphylactic PCN history surfaces

Vasopressors: NOREPINEPHRINE titrated to MAP ≥65 mmHg (current 4 mcg/min); add vasopressin 0.03 units/min if escalating beyond moderate-dose norepi; second pressor before escalating norepi beyond 20 mcg/min

Conservative fluid strategy (post-resuscitation): once shock resolved and lactate improving, target negative balance with diuresis as tolerated (FACTT trial — conservative beat liberal in ARDS for ventilator-free days)
Closed-suction (in-line) ETT suctioning only as clinically indicated (not on schedule); NO routine instillation of saline (worsens hypoxemia and infection risk)
Continuous capnography on ventilator (EtCO2); trend with ABG to ensure correlation; ABG at 1 hr post-prone, 4 hr post-prone, q6h thereafter while prone

Pressure injury prevention bundle for prone position: foam dressings on FACE (forehead, cheeks, chin), EARS (rotate head q2h), CHEST, ILIAC CRESTS, KNEES, SHINS, DORSUM OF FEET, GENITALS; skin assessment q2h with each repositioning of arms
Eye care q2h with lubricant ointment + closure tape; reassess q-shift for chemosis and conjunctival exposure (face-down increases periorbital edema)

OG tube clamp and tube feeds held x 1 hr pre-prone and x 1 hr post-prone (aspiration risk during turn); restart tube feeds 1 hr after prone position confirmed stable; reverse Trendelenburg 15–20° to reduce facial edema and aspiration risk

ABCDEF bundle: Assess pain (CPOT q4h), Both SAT/SBT daily (held during paralysis, resume when off), Choice of analgesia/sedation reviewed daily, Delirium screening (CAM-ICU when assessable), Early mobility (ROM during paralysis; sit-to-edge once stable), Family engagement (open visitation, family meeting today)
DVT prophylaxis: SCDs bilateral lower extremities (functioning continuously during proning); pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin or heparin) once not actively bleeding
GI prophylaxis: pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily (mechanically ventilated patient = stress ulcer risk indication)
Glycemic control: insulin gtt titrated to 140–180 mg/dL; hourly BG checks initially; transition to subcutaneous when off pressors and oral/tube feeds tolerated
Daily CXR; daily labs (CBC, CMP, lactate); cultures repeated if temp >38.5°C or rising leukocytosis; procalcitonin trend for antibiotic de-escalation

Family meeting today: intensivist + palliative + wife (proxy) + daughter (RN); discuss prognosis, prone responder vs non-responder pathway, ECMO referral criteria (P/F <80 after optimization, plateau >30, refractory hypercapnia pH <7.20), goals of care, advance directive review
ECMO consultation criteria if not responding to prone + lung-protective ventilation + paralysis after 24–48 hours; transfer to ECMO-capable center may be required

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: P/F ratio 78 (PaO2 62 / FiO2 0.80) — meets Berlin severe ARDS criteria and PROSEVA proning criteria (P/F <150 on FiO2 ≥0.60 PEEP ≥5)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the need for prone positioning. P/F <100 = severe ARDS; proning has Class I evidence (PROSEVA 2013) for mortality reduction in this category. The number drives the intervention; track it q-shift and after each major change.

opts.text: ABG pH 7.28 / pCO2 56 — partially compensated respiratory acidosis (permissive hypercapnia within accepted limits down to pH 7.20)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — within lung-protective acceptable range. Permissive hypercapnia is the trade-off for low tidal volume strategy; do NOT raise the TV to 'correct' the pCO2 (would cause volutrauma). Watch the pH boundary 7.20; if breached, consider rate increase or sodium bicarbonate or escalation.

opts.text: Vent settings: TV 380 mL = 6 mL/kg IBW; plateau 28 (target <30); driving pressure 14 (target <15)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these are the lung-protective benchmarks. TV is calculated from IDEAL body weight (NOT actual; actual body weight in obesity would harm). Plateau and driving pressure are the lung-stress surrogates that correlate with mortality (driving pressure >15 = worse outcomes per Amato 2015 NEJM).

opts.text: Bilateral diffuse alveolar infiltrates on CXR worse than yesterday; ET tube tip 4 cm above carina; lines/tubes inventoried
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms ARDS imaging criteria and verifies airway position. ET tube 3–5 cm above carina is correct position; verify with each chest film. Bilateral infiltrates + acute onset + not explained by CHF + P/F <300 = Berlin ARDS definition met.

opts.text: Pneumococcal pneumonia confirmed; on ceftriaxone Day 3 of 7–10 day course; PCN allergy is rash (not anaphylactic)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — source identified, narrow antibiotic confirmed appropriate. PCN allergy 'rash only' has low cross-reactivity (~1–2%) with cephalosporins; ceftriaxone is appropriate. If patient had anaphylactic PCN history, levofloxacin or vancomycin + aztreonam would be alternatives.

opts.text: On norepi 4 mcg/min for septic shock; lactate 2.4 trending down from 4.8; UOP 0.6 mL/kg/hr; MAP 71
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — septic shock improving on appropriate therapy. MAP at target (≥65), lactate clearing, urine output adequate. Continue current support; conservative fluid strategy now that shock is resolved (FACTT trial).

opts.text: On propofol + fentanyl + cisatracurium paralysis; TOF 2/4 (target 1–2/4); paralyzed-but-sedated confirmed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms safe paralysis. Always SEDATE BEFORE PARALYZING — paralyzed-but-awake is torture. TOF 1–2/4 is the target (deeper than 0/4 risks prolonged post-ICU weakness). Eye care q2h essential during paralysis (cannot blink). Document depth of sedation by physiologic surrogates.

opts.text: Lines inventory: ET tube, OG tube, R IJ TLC, L radial A-line, foley, R foot SpO2, telemetry leads — all dislodgement risks during proning
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — proning safety checklist. EVERY line and tube must be inventoried and assigned a team member during the turn. Pre-prone re-securement; SpO2 probe moved from finger to ear (more reliable in prone); arterial line waveform verified pre/post.

opts.text: Stage 1 pressure injury sacrum (developed Day 3 supine); proning will load face/chest/iliac/knees/feet/genitals — entirely different pressure map
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pressure injury bundle for prone. Foam dressings on FACE (forehead, cheeks, chin), EARS (alternate head turn q2h), CHEST, ILIAC CRESTS, KNEES, SHINS, DORSUM OF FEET, GENITALS. Q2h arm-position alternation (swimmer's position alternating sides) also prevents brachial plexus injury.

opts.text: Family meeting scheduled today; daughter is RN; goals of care to be discussed including ECMO referral pathway vs. comfort-focused care if non-responder
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — concurrent psychosocial/ethical priority. Severe ARDS mortality ~40% even optimized. ECMO criteria: P/F <80 after optimization, refractory hypercapnia. Family understanding of the trajectory and decision points BEFORE crises is the goal of proactive palliative integration.

opts.text: Glucose 168 on insulin gtt; CBC WBC 22.4; Cr 1.6 (baseline 0.9)
opts.rat: Important monitoring data — but ranks below the immediate proning bundle. Stress hyperglycemia at target (140–180 per SCC ICU guidelines); leukocytosis explained by source already known; AKI from septic insult, currently being managed conservatively. Continue trending; not driving immediate action.

opts.text: Allergies: PCN rash only
opts.rat: Documented and addressed — cephalosporin is being used appropriately. Document and proceed.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / management principles.

opts.text: ARDS pathophysiology: diffuse alveolar damage from inflammation → loss of surfactant → atelectasis → V/Q mismatch and shunt → refractory hypoxemia; the lung is heterogeneously affected (dependent zones consolidate; non-dependent zones overinflate)
opts.rat: CORRECT — mechanistic basis. The heterogeneity is why both lung-protective TV (avoid over-distending the healthy zones) AND prone positioning (recruits dependent zones, reduces shunt) work. Same lung disease but different lung regions need different things — manage the whole lung.

opts.text: PRONE POSITIONING improves oxygenation by: (1) recruiting dorsal lung units that were dependent and atelectatic when supine; (2) more homogeneous distribution of ventilation; (3) improved drainage of secretions; (4) reduced ventilator-induced lung injury via uniform stress distribution
opts.rat: CORRECT — multiple mechanisms. The dorsal lung is the larger and was more atelectatic in the supine ARDS patient; flipping makes those units now ventral and recruitable. The PROSEVA trial showed 16-hour daily proning reduced 28-day and 90-day mortality in severe ARDS.

opts.text: LUNG-PROTECTIVE VENTILATION (ARDSnet): TV 4–6 mL/kg IDEAL body weight, plateau <30, driving pressure <15; PERMISSIVE HYPERCAPNIA to pH 7.20; FiO2/PEEP ladder titrated to SpO2 88–95%; reduces ventilator-induced lung injury (volutrauma + barotrauma + atelectrauma + biotrauma)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the foundational ARDS protocol (ARMA / ARDSnet 2000 NEJM). The 4 ventilator injuries: VOLUTRAUMA (high TV stretches alveoli), BAROTRAUMA (high plateau pressure rupture), ATELECTRAUMA (cyclic opening/closing — addressed by adequate PEEP), BIOTRAUMA (cytokine release from injured lung systemically).

opts.text: TV is calculated from IDEAL body weight (height-based formula: 50 + 2.3 × [height in inches − 60] for men, 45.5 + 2.3 × [height − 60] for women), NOT actual body weight — this is critical in obesity where actual weight would massively overshoot
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield trap. An obese patient with actual weight 100 kg and IBW 65 kg would receive 600 mL TV instead of 390 mL if you used actual weight — a major harm. Always calculate IBW from HEIGHT, not weight, when programming lung-protective ventilation.

opts.text: PERMISSIVE HYPERCAPNIA: accept higher pCO2 (and lower pH) as the trade-off for lung-protective low TV; lower limit pH ~7.20; do NOT raise TV to 'correct' the pCO2; can increase rate if pCO2 problematic, but only if it doesn't cause auto-PEEP
opts.rat: CORRECT — the philosophy shift in ARDS management since the 1990s. The lung is the patient, not the ABG. Increase rate cautiously (watch for auto-PEEP / breath stacking); sodium bicarb is a temporizing option for severe acidosis but does not address cause. ECMO is the salvage option for refractory respiratory acidosis.

opts.text: Paralysis with cisatracurium (or rocuronium) in severe ARDS is indicated to (1) abolish ventilator dyssynchrony, (2) facilitate prone positioning, (3) reduce O2 consumption; must ALWAYS be combined with deep sedation (propofol/fentanyl/midazolam) — paralyzed-but-awake is unacceptable; monitor train-of-four 1–2/4 twitches
opts.rat: CORRECT — paralysis safety. ACURASYS (2010) and ROSE (2019) trials clarified paralysis role; recent guidelines reserve for refractory dyssynchrony and proning facilitation rather than blanket use. Continuous infusions should be capped to 48 hours when possible; longer durations associated with post-ICU weakness.

opts.text: BENZODIAZEPINES (midazolam, lorazepam) are first-line for ICU sedation in ARDS because they reduce delirium and improve outcomes
opts.rat: ⚠️ Opposite of truth. Benzodiazepines INCREASE delirium risk, prolong mech ventilation days, and worsen outcomes (PAD/PADIS guidelines). PROPOFOL or DEXMEDETOMIDINE preferred. Reserve benzos for alcohol/benzo withdrawal or refractory cases. This is a high-yield outdated-teaching reversal.

opts.text: Tidal volume should be raised to 10 mL/kg actual body weight to 'recruit' the atelectatic lung in ARDS
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS. High TV ventilation kills ARDS patients via volutrauma (ARMA trial showed 22% absolute mortality reduction with 6 mL/kg vs 12 mL/kg IBW). Recruitment is done through PEEP and prone positioning, NOT through high TV. This is one of the most important lessons of modern critical care.

opts.text: PROSEVA criteria for prone positioning: P/F <150 on FiO2 ≥0.60 PEEP ≥5 with deep sedation/paralysis; target 16 hours per 24-hour cycle; repeat for up to 7–10 days if responder; arms in alternating swimmer's position q2h
opts.rat: CORRECT — the operational criteria. Track P/F, plateau, driving pressure, hemodynamics. Non-responder defined as no improvement in P/F at 4 hours. Proning is uncomfortable for nurses but lifesaving for the patient — protocol-driven approach with 5-person team is the standard.

opts.text: DO NOT routinely disconnect the ventilator circuit when repositioning a prone patient; use closed-suction (in-line) circuit; brief disconnect causes loss of PEEP and rapid derecruitment with desaturation cascade
opts.rat: CORRECT — fundamental ARDS nursing principle. Loss of PEEP for even seconds in severe ARDS causes alveolar collapse that takes minutes to recruit. Closed-suction circuit; brief in-line disconnect only if absolutely necessary (e.g., obvious circuit failure); pre-oxygenate to FiO2 1.0 x 5 min before any planned brief disconnect.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Impaired Gas Exchange / Refractory Hypoxemia r/t severe ARDS with bilateral infiltrates, P/F 78, and progressive hypoxemia despite lung-protective ventilation
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The hypoxemia is the proximate killer. Proning + lung-protective ventilation + paralysis + adequate PEEP + FiO2 titration are the bundled response. P/F is the score to track. If non-responder by 4 hours, escalate to ECMO consultation.

opts.text: Risk for Ventilator-Associated Lung Injury (VILI) r/t mechanical ventilation in ARDS (volutrauma, barotrauma, atelectrauma, biotrauma)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Iatrogenic risk that is preventable with lung-protective settings. Monitor plateau <30, driving pressure <15, TV 4–6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP adequate, permissive hypercapnia accepted. Every shift verify settings against patient's IBW (recalculate from height).

opts.text: Risk for Line/Tube Dislodgement and Pressure Injury r/t prone positioning of patient with multiple lines/tubes, sedation, and paralysis
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Proning carries real risks: ET tube dislodgement is potentially fatal (cannot reintubate prone — emergency supine). Pressure injuries on face/chest/iliac/knees/feet. Eye care critical (cannot blink). PROSEVA-style 5-person team protocol + foam dressings + q2h repositioning of head/arms.

opts.text: Risk for Septic Shock Progression / End-Organ Dysfunction r/t severe pneumococcal pneumonia with current vasopressor requirement (norepi 4 mcg/min), AKI (Cr 1.6 from 0.9)
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Sepsis is improving (lactate trending down, MAP at target, UOP adequate) but the patient is not yet 'safe.' Continue source control (antibiotics), MAP target ≥65, conservative fluids now that shock resolved. Reassess for cardiogenic component if worsening.

opts.text: Family Distress / Goals-of-Care Uncertainty r/t critical illness with poor prognosis and pending decisions about escalation (ECMO) vs. comfort-focused care
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in the immediate physiologic stack. Family meeting today integrates intensivist + palliative + family for prognostic communication and shared decision-making. Open visitation, family at bedside, daughter (RN) as informed family member — proactive integration prevents crisis decisions.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Prone positioning ≥16 hours per 24-hour cycle, with arms in alternating swimmer's position q2h, repeated for up to 7–10 days if responder
opts.rat: PROSEVA criteria met (P/F 78 on FiO2 0.80 PEEP 14 with sedation/paralysis). Class I evidence for mortality reduction in severe ARDS. Repeat daily cycles for up to 7–10 days; reassess responder vs non-responder at 4 hours each session.

opts.text: Maintain TV 4–6 mL/kg IDEAL body weight, plateau <30, driving pressure <15, PEEP per FiO2/PEEP ladder, permissive hypercapnia to pH 7.20
opts.rat: ARDSnet lung-protective protocol — the foundational intervention. Recalculate IBW from height (not actual weight) every shift to verify TV. The 'do less harm with the vent' principle reduces VILI.

opts.text: Increase tidal volume to 10 mL/kg actual body weight to recruit atelectatic lung
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — high TV causes volutrauma and worsens ARDS mortality. Recruitment is achieved via PEEP and proning, NOT high TV. This is a fundamental lung-protective principle that must not be violated.

opts.text: Deep sedation with propofol + fentanyl to RASS −4 to −5; cisatracurium paralysis 1–5 mcg/kg/min titrated to TOF 1–2/4; ALWAYS sedate before paralyzing
opts.rat: Required during proning and for refractory dyssynchrony in severe ARDS. Always sedate FIRST — paralyzed-but-awake is unacceptable. TOF target prevents prolonged 0/4 (post-ICU weakness risk). Cap continuous paralytic at 48 hours when possible.

opts.text: Continuous midazolam (Versed) gtt as primary sedation for the ARDS patient on mechanical ventilation
opts.rat: ⚠️ Benzodiazepine sedation increases delirium and ventilator days; PADIS guidelines recommend propofol or dexmedetomidine instead. Reserve benzos for alcohol/benzo withdrawal or refractory cases. Outdated practice.

opts.text: Pre-prone bundle: pre-oxygenate FiO2 1.0 x 5 min, eye care + tape closure, foam dressings to pressure points (face, ears, chest, iliac, knees, shins, feet, genitals), line/tube inventory and securement, OG clamp + tube feeds held x 1 hr, SpO2 probe to ear
opts.rat: Standardized PROSEVA-style bundle — reduces complications. Each element addresses a specific complication: eye care (corneal abrasion), pressure dressings (injury), line inventory (dislodgement), feed hold (aspiration), ear SpO2 (signal reliability). Document each.

opts.text: Routine disconnection of the ventilator circuit during the prone turn to facilitate repositioning
opts.rat: ⚠️ Causes loss of PEEP, alveolar derecruitment, desaturation cascade. Use closed-suction (in-line) circuit; brief disconnect only if absolutely necessary; if disconnection happens, pre-oxygenate FiO2 1.0 x 5 min and reconnect ASAP.

opts.text: PROSEVA proning team: minimum 5 staff (1 RN team leader at head/airway, 2 on each side, 1 RT at vent); team leader counts the turn; everyone has a designated line/tube to manage
opts.rat: Standardized team approach reduces dislodgement and physical injury (to staff and patient). 5-person minimum; rehearse the turn; clear communication; abort and supine if any critical complication during turn (e.g., ET tube dislodgement).

opts.text: Norepinephrine titrated to MAP ≥65 mmHg; conservative fluid strategy now that septic shock resolved (lactate down, MAP at target)
opts.rat: Post-resuscitation conservative fluid approach (FACTT trial — fewer ventilator days in ARDS). Continue norepi at lowest effective dose; reassess for weaning daily; add vasopressin if escalating beyond moderate norepi rather than continued upward titration of norepi alone.

opts.text: 1 L crystalloid bolus to improve oxygenation by augmenting cardiac output and oxygen delivery
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong in current state. Patient has resolved septic shock with MAP at target and adequate UOP; lactate clearing. ARDS lung is leaky — fluid will worsen pulmonary edema and oxygenation. Conservative fluid strategy is correct now. Reserve fluid for acute hypotension/shock.

opts.text: Continue ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily x 7–10 days (PCN rash only — cephalosporin appropriate); no broadening unless clinical deterioration or new culture data
opts.rat: Narrow, source-directed antibiotic. Pansusceptible S. pneumoniae well-covered. Avoid unnecessary broadening (resistance, C. diff, candida risk). De-escalate if procalcitonin trends down and clinical improvement.

opts.text: Family meeting with intensivist + palliative + wife (proxy) + RN daughter to discuss prognosis, ECMO referral criteria, and goals of care; proactive integration of palliative care
opts.rat: Proactive palliative consultation is associated with better family outcomes and avoids crisis decision-making. ARDS mortality ~40% even optimized. Honest framing of trajectory; review what the patient would want; document plan including triggers for revisiting code status.

opts.text: Q2h alternation of head turn direction and arm position (swimmer's: one up, one down); foam dressings adjusted with each repositioning; eye care q2h
opts.rat: Pressure injury prevention bundle for prone. Without alternation: ear necrosis, brachial plexus injury, corneal abrasion. Each q2h cycle: reverify line positions, ET tube depth, eye care, dressings, document skin assessment. Time-consuming but lifesaving.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm deep sedation (RASS −5) + paralysis (TOF 1–2/4); pre-oxygenate FiO2 1.0 x 5 min; document baseline ABG and vent settings
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety prerequisites for proning. NEVER paralyze without deep sedation. Pre-oxygenation provides reserve through the brief perturbation of the turn. Baseline values for comparison are essential to detect post-prone response.

opts.text: Pre-prone bundle: eye care + tape closure; foam dressings on face/ears/chest/iliac/knees/shins/feet/genitals; line/tube inventory and securement; ET tube depth re-verified; OG clamp + feeds held; SpO2 probe to ear; foley emptied
opts.rat: #2 — Complete the pre-prone checklist. Each element prevents a specific complication. Document. The bundle takes 15–20 min; do not shortcut. Communicate with the team that everyone has a designated line/tube role.

opts.text: Assemble 5-person proning team (1 RN team leader at head/airway, 2 on each side, 1 RT at vent); briefing on roles and abort criteria; perform the prone turn with log-roll technique; arms in initial swimmer's position
opts.rat: #3 — Execute the turn. Standardized team and process. Team leader calls the turn; abort and supine if ET tube dislodgement or critical decompensation during the maneuver. Settle in prone; reverify line positions; head turned to one side; one arm up, one down.

opts.text: Post-prone monitoring: HR/BP/SpO2 q5min x 30 min then q15min x 2 hr; ABG at 1 hr post-prone; document P/F ratio response (responder if P/F improves >20%); continue lung-protective vent settings
opts.rat: #4 — Verify response. Expect transient hemodynamic perturbation in first 5–10 min then improvement by 30 min. P/F improvement >20% at 1 hour defines responder; if no improvement by 4 hours, non-responder — escalate to ECMO consultation.

opts.text: Pressure injury prevention bundle ongoing during prone time: q2h head turn alternation, q2h swimmer's-arm switch, foam dressing reassessment, eye care q2h, ET tube depth, full skin assessment q2h with each repositioning; family meeting at 1400
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care during prone duration (16 hours). Time-consuming nursing care; the family meeting integrates prognosis and goals of care. Daily reassessment of need for continued proning, sedation depth, paralysis necessity, and antibiotic course.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1 hr post-prone: ABG pH 7.32 / pCO2 48 / pO2 88 / SaO2 94% on FiO2 0.60 PEEP 12; P/F now 147 (up from 78); plateau 26
opts.rat: Prone responder — major P/F improvement. Continue prone for full 16-hour cycle. Wean FiO2 toward 0.5; consider PEEP wean as oxygenation tolerates. Plan for supine cycle, then reassess for repeat prone cycles for up to 7–10 days if benefit sustained.

opts.text: Day 6 of proning cycles: P/F 220 supine on FiO2 0.4 PEEP 8; paralytic D/C'd Day 5; sedation lightened to RASS −2; CXR clearing; off norepi for 24 hr; passing SAT trial
opts.rat: ARDS resolution trajectory. Discontinue proning when P/F ≥150 reliably supine; wean PEEP and FiO2; lighten sedation; daily SAT/SBT trials toward extubation. Watch for post-paralytic weakness (ICU-acquired weakness) — early PT mobilization.

opts.text: Day 10: extubated to HFNC; ambulating with PT 50 ft; eating soft diet; transferring to step-down; antibiotics completed; family verbalizes understanding of post-ICU syndrome and rehabilitation plan
opts.rat: Successful recovery. Post-ICU syndrome (cognitive, physical, psychological dimensions) is common after severe ARDS — anticipate weakness, delirium-related memory issues, possible PTSD-spectrum symptoms; ICU diary, rehabilitation, post-ICU clinic referral as appropriate.

opts.text: 30 min into prone position: ET tube dislodged during a coughing maneuver; SpO2 dropped to 62% before team supined and reintubated
opts.rat: Severe iatrogenic complication. Emergency supine and reintubation; root-cause analysis (was depth re-verified pre-prone? was securement adequate? was team aware of ET tube role?); document; this is the most-feared proning complication. Reinforce team-leader role and ET-tube securement bundle.

opts.text: 4 hr post-prone: P/F unchanged at 80; plateau 32 (now exceeding target); pCO2 climbing to 68 / pH 7.18; norepi escalating to 12 mcg/min
opts.rat: Prone non-responder + decompensation. ECMO consultation criteria potentially met (P/F <80 after optimization, plateau >30, refractory hypercapnia). Coordinate with ECMO-capable referral center; family meeting; reassess goals of care. Decision pathway: ECMO vs. continued conventional vent vs. comfort-focused care.

opts.text: Hour 8 of prone: new pressure injury (Stage 2) on forehead despite foam dressings; ear erythema; corneal exposure noted on R eye
opts.rat: Skin and eye complications of prolonged prone. Reposition head turn direction immediately; reapply foam to forehead with different padding; reassess eye care (lubricant + closure tape adequacy); skin team consult. Document; system-level review of pressure injury bundle adherence.

opts.text: Hour 12 prone: ABG pH 7.34 / pCO2 44 / pO2 90 / SaO2 95% on FiO2 0.50 PEEP 10; P/F 180; norepi weaning to 2 mcg/min; UOP 1.2 mL/kg/hr
opts.rat: Sustained prone response with continued improvement. Continue cycle to full 16 hours; plan supine reassessment; trend toward weaning PEEP and FiO2. Consider easing sedation/paralysis as oxygenation allows; reassess need for continued cycles daily.

opts.text: After family meeting: family elects to continue full critical care including ECMO referral if non-responder; documented discussion in chart; advance directive review with daughter (RN) as healthcare advocate
opts.rat: Goals of care reaffirmed. Documented family understanding of prognosis and escalation pathway. Continue current care plan; revisit decision points at clear triggers (ECMO criteria met, non-responder at 4 hours, major complication). Proactive integration of palliative continues.

opts.text: Day 7 post-extubation: profound proximal weakness (cannot lift arms or roll independently); ICU-acquired weakness from prolonged sedation, paralysis, and immobility
opts.rat: Expected post-ARDS sequela. Aggressive PT/OT mobilization; nutrition optimization; expect recovery over weeks to months; reassure patient and family that proximal weakness is the typical pattern and is recoverable with rehab. Post-ICU clinic referral.

opts.text: Persistent Stage 1 sacral pressure injury at extubation despite all the proning
opts.rat: Existed pre-proning (developed Day 3 supine). Continue pressure injury bundle: turning q2h once stable supine, off-loading, nutrition (protein and zinc), barrier creams, wound consult if Stage progresses. Likely to heal as mobility returns.

title: Acute UTI Presenting as Delirium Superimposed on Dementia
subtitle: No Fever, No Dysuria — Just 'She's Not Herself': Geriatric Atypical Presentation, the Beers List, and Why You Don't Treat Asymptomatic Bacteriuria

patient.name: E. O'Sullivan
patient.code: DNR / DNI (advance directive 2024; daughter POA; comfort-focused goals with treatment of reversible conditions)
patient.allergies: Sulfa (rash); listed PCN (clarified — was a 'rash' as a child, no documented anaphylaxis)
patient.admitDate: Today, 1420 (ED arrival from Memory Care assisted living; daughter brought her for 'she's not herself' for 3 days)
patient.room: Geri Med-Surg 5, Bed 12 (door-side, near nursing station for closer observation)

vitals.time: Memory Care log (3 days ago, baseline)
1800 (CURRENT — admitted to geri-med floor; after 1 L NS, ceftriaxone given in ED)

labs.v: Cloudy, foul-smelling; leukocyte esterase LARGE, nitrites POSITIVE, WBC >50/hpf, RBC 0–5/hpf, bacteria many, no squamous epithelial cells (good specimen)
labs.r: Normal: neg LE, neg nitrites, <5 WBC/hpf. SYMPTOMATIC UTI + positive UA = treat. ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA in elderly should NOT be treated (Choosing Wisely) — but acute delirium IS a symptom in geriatrics

labs.v: E. coli growing >100,000 CFU/mL; sensitivities pending tomorrow
labs.r: >100K = significant growth; E. coli is the most common pathogen (~80% community-acquired UTI). Sensitivities will guide narrowing. Empirically chose ceftriaxone given mild systemic signs

labs.v: WBC 13.4 (with bandemia 8% — mild left shift), Hgb 11.2, Plt 248
labs.r: WBC 4.5–11; mild leukocytosis with bandemia consistent with bacterial infection. In elderly, WBC may be LOW or NORMAL despite serious infection — clinical correlation essential. Hgb at her baseline of chronic anemia

labs.v: Na 134, K 4.0, Cl 100, HCO3 22, BUN 32, Cr 1.2 (baseline 0.9), Glucose 142
labs.r: Cr 1.2 mildly elevated from baseline 0.9 = acute prerenal AKI from dehydration (poor PO intake x 3 days); Na borderline low (84 y/o on no fluids vulnerable); BUN/Cr ratio elevated supports prerenal pattern. eGFR ~48 mL/min/1.73m² — important for drug dosing

labs.v: Lactate 1.8 (normal); VBG pH 7.36 HCO3 22
labs.r: Lactate <2.2 normal — rules out septic shock or major hypoperfusion; supports localized UTI/pyelonephritis-spectrum infection rather than urosepsis at this moment

labs.n: Cognitive baseline (per daughter and Memory Care record)
labs.v: Baseline MMSE 16/30 (moderate Alzheimer's dementia); oriented to self only; recognizes daughter; needs help with most ADLs but ambulates with walker; sleeps 8 hr/night with usual sundowning. ALZHEIMER'S diagnosed 5 years ago
labs.r: Establishes baseline for comparison; delirium is acute and FLUCTUATING change from this baseline — not the baseline itself. CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) requires baseline knowledge

labs.v: Disoriented to person, place, time; talking to people who are not there ('my mother is here'); inattentive (cannot follow 'count backward from 10'); fluctuating between drowsy and agitated; refusing meals x 2 days; new urinary incontinence x 3 days (Memory Care had attributed to dementia progression); fell once last night (caught by staff, no injury)
labs.r: CAM positive: (1) ACUTE onset + FLUCTUATING course ✓; (2) INATTENTION ✓; PLUS (3) disorganized thinking ✓ OR (4) altered LOC ✓ — DELIRIUM diagnosed. Hypoactive periods alternating with hyperactive = mixed delirium (often missed)

labs.v: T 99.6°F (NOT febrile per geriatric standards), HR 102 (mild tachycardia), BP 108/68 (low-normal for her baseline 132/74), RR 22 (mildly tachypneic), SpO2 94% RA
labs.r: GERIATRIC ATYPICAL PRESENTATION: many elders mount NO fever (or low-grade only) despite serious infection due to blunted immune response; baseline temperature in elderly is ~36.7°C (98°F) — so 99.6°F is actually a relative elevation. Trust the DELIRIUM as the symptom, not the absent fever

labs.v: Donepezil 10 mg PO daily, memantine 10 mg PO BID, amlodipine 5 mg PO daily, atorvastatin 20 mg PO daily, vitamin D 1000 IU daily, calcium carbonate 500 mg TID, MULTIVITAMIN, omeprazole 20 mg daily, OXYBUTYNIN 5 mg PO BID (started 2 weeks ago for the new incontinence — BEERS!), LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg PO PRN agitation (BEERS!), DIPHENHYDRAMINE 25 mg PO PRN sleep (BEERS!), trazodone 50 mg PO at bedtime
labs.r: GERIATRIC POLYPHARMACY with multiple BEERS LIST inappropriate meds: oxybutynin (anticholinergic — worsens cognition, contributes to delirium), lorazepam (benzo — Beers; falls + delirium risk), diphenhydramine (anticholinergic + sedating). Each contributes to delirium susceptibility AND the oxybutynin was given to treat what was actually a UTI clue (incontinence)

labs.v: 1 fall last night at Memory Care (caught by staff, no injury); 1 fall 6 weeks ago at Memory Care (no injury); using walker for ambulation; has had bilateral hearing aids x 5 years (left aid is at home — not in ear today); glasses but daughter forgot to bring them
labs.r: High fall risk: dementia + new delirium + age + polypharmacy + sensory impairment (missing hearing aid and glasses). Sensory deprivation worsens delirium and falls — call daughter for the aid and glasses
