labs.v: New urinary incontinence x 3 days (Memory Care attributed to dementia progression); foul-smelling urine; suprapubic tenderness on exam; no flank pain; foley catheter is NOT in place and SHOULD NOT BE PLACED (CAUTI risk)
labs.r: AVOID indwelling foley unless strict indication (urinary retention, accurate UOP for shock, comfort in end-of-life). CAUTI risk + dementia + delirium = patient will pull on it = trauma. Use scheduled toileting q2h, condom catheter in men, external female catheter (PureWick) if needed

labs.v: Mucous membranes dry, skin tenting +, refusing PO meals x 2 days, sips of water only; daughter reports she normally drinks 'maybe 4 glasses a day' which is already low; weight 52 kg (down from 55 kg 1 month ago at Memory Care weigh-in)
labs.r: Volume depletion contributing to AKI, dizziness, falls, and delirium. Geriatric thirst response is blunted. Gentle rehydration with isotonic fluids; encourage PO when able; nutrition consult; aspiration precautions given fluctuating LOC

labs.v: Widowed; daughter (sole child, RN background) is POA and healthcare proxy; advance directive 2024 specifies DNR/DNI, no artificial nutrition long-term, treat reversible conditions, hospitalize for comfort/quality not aggressive ICU-level care; lives at memory care for 18 mo; daughter visits 2–3x/week
labs.r: Goals of care: COMFORT WITH TREATMENT OF REVERSIBLE CAUSES. UTI is reversible — treat. Delirium is reversible — manage. Avoid aggressive ICU escalation. Discuss with daughter at each decision point

labs.v: Sulfa rash (confirmed by daughter — moderate widespread rash 2018, not anaphylactic); PCN listed but daughter clarifies it was a rash 'sometime in childhood — never tested'; tolerated amoxicillin without issue 4 years ago for tooth infection
labs.r: Daughter's history clarifies the PCN listing is likely not a true allergy. Ceftriaxone (cephalosporin) was appropriate empirically; if culture returns showing sensitive to nitrofurantoin, switch (but verify eGFR — nitrofurantoin avoided if eGFR <30, borderline here at 48)

nursesNote: 1800 — 84 y/o female with moderate Alzheimer's dementia (baseline MMSE 16/30) admitted to geriatric med-surg from ED for ACUTE DELIRIUM x 3 days superimposed on dementia, brought by daughter (POA, RN) from Memory Care assisted living. The presentation is a classic GERIATRIC ATYPICAL INFECTION: no fever (T 99.6 — relative elevation from her usual 97.8 baseline), no dysuria reported (she can't articulate it), no frequency complaints (she's already incontinent), just an acute change from baseline — 'talking to her dead mother,' refusing meals, NEW urinary incontinence x 3 days (Memory Care mistakenly attributed to dementia progression and the PCP added OXYBUTYNIN 2 weeks ago — an anticholinergic on the BEERS list that worsens cognition AND was prescribed for what was actually a UTI clue, completing the diagnostic miss), one near-fall last night caught by staff. CAM positive: acute onset + fluctuating course + inattention + altered LOC = DELIRIUM diagnosed. ED workup confirmed UTI: cloudy foul urine, LE large, nitrites +, WBC >50/hpf; urine culture growing E. coli >100K (sensitivities pending). WBC 13.4 with 8% bands (mild left shift). Cr 1.2 from baseline 0.9 (mild prerenal AKI from poor PO x 2 days — dehydrated on exam with skin tenting, dry mucosa, weight 52 kg down from 55 kg). Lactate 1.8 (normal — not in shock). Received 1 L NS bolus and CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV in ED (renally dosed appropriately for eGFR ~48); started on cefdinir-equivalent PO would be considered once she can swallow safely. Polypharmacy review with daughter at bedside: ELEVEN home medications, of which THREE are inappropriate Beers-list items contributing to delirium susceptibility: OXYBUTYNIN (anticholinergic — held), LORAZEPAM PRN (benzo — D/C as PRN; do not give for any 'agitation' here), DIPHENHYDRAMINE PRN sleep (anticholinergic — D/C); also avoid future haloperidol or quetiapine for delirium agitation given black-box mortality warning in dementia (only as absolute last resort, time-limited, at lowest dose, after non-pharm measures exhausted). Non-pharmacologic delirium bundle initiated: door-side room near nursing station, well-lit room during day with curtains open, dim lights and quiet at night, large-print clock and calendar in view, frequent reorientation by staff and daughter, glasses and hearing aid (daughter going home to retrieve), early mobilization with walker assist as tolerated, scheduled q2h toileting (NO indwelling foley — CAUTI risk + she'll pull it), encourage PO intake with small sips and her preferred foods, sleep hygiene with no awakening for routine vitals between 2200–0600 unless clinically necessary, family at bedside as comforting presence, music she enjoys (daughter brought iPad with her favorite Irish folk songs). Sitter ordered for safety given fall risk + new delirium. DNR/DNI status confirmed; goals of care per daughter: 'treat the reversible UTI, manage the delirium without sedating her, get her back to baseline at Memory Care.' Will monitor for signs of progression to urosepsis (rising lactate, worsening hypotension, new organ dysfunction) and escalate per goals (no ICU per directive — emphasis on comfort + reversible treatment). Reassess CAM q-shift; track delirium trajectory. Family meeting tomorrow morning to review trajectory and discharge planning back to Memory Care once delirium resolves (typically 3–7 days after infection cleared, sometimes longer in dementia).
providerOrders: ADMIT to geriatric medicine; DNR/DNI status per advance directive 2024; daughter as POA/healthcare proxy; goals of care: treat reversible conditions (UTI) with comfort-focused approach; no ICU escalation per directive

CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV daily x 7–14 days (renally dosed for eGFR ~48); transition to oral antibiotic when she can swallow safely and sensitivities back — preferred PO options NITROFURANTOIN 100 mg PO BID (avoid if eGFR <30 — borderline here, recheck), CEFPODOXIME or CEFDINIR PO, or AMOXICILLIN-CLAVULANATE per sensitivities; AVOID fluoroquinolones in elderly when alternative available (tendon rupture, delirium, QTc, C. diff risk)

HOLD or DISCONTINUE on admission: OXYBUTYNIN (anticholinergic — Beers; worsens cognition; was prescribed for what was actually UTI clue); LORAZEPAM PRN (Beers; worsens delirium and falls); DIPHENHYDRAMINE PRN (anticholinergic + sedating — Beers); DO NOT GIVE for sleep or agitation while inpatient

AVOID for delirium agitation: ANTIPSYCHOTICS (haloperidol, quetiapine, olanzapine) have FDA BLACK-BOX WARNING for increased mortality in dementia patients; reserve as absolute last resort after non-pharm exhausted, lowest dose, time-limited, after informed family discussion; document attempted alternatives
Avoid future BENZODIAZEPINES (alcohol/benzo withdrawal aside); avoid future opioids except for documented pain at lowest effective dose; minimize sedating agents

Hydration: ISOTONIC SALINE 0.9% NS at 75–100 mL/hr until adequately rehydrated (UOP ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, Cr trending toward baseline); then transition to PO with frequent encouragement; small sips of preferred beverages; nutrition consult; AVOID FORCING fluids (aspiration risk in delirium)
Continuous pulse oximetry; vitals q4h while stable (NOT q2h disrupting sleep cycle — sleep is therapeutic); cluster nursing care
STRICT I&O; monitor UOP target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; daily weights; reassess hydration q-shift

NO INDWELLING FOLEY unless strict indication (urinary retention >300 mL on bladder scan, end-of-life comfort, accurate UOP for unstable hemodynamics) — CAUTI risk + dementia patient pulling on it; use scheduled toileting q2h with assistance; external female catheter (PureWick) only if accurate output critical and skin can tolerate

FALL PRECAUTIONS: bed in lowest position with bed alarm, fall risk armband, non-slip footwear, walker at bedside, sitter at bedside (or 1:1 if available), room near nursing station, frequent rounding q1h, call light within reach (though she may not use it), call daughter to bring home walker if used

SENSORY: daughter to bring glasses and hearing aid from home (left hearing aid at home today — KEY priority); apply hearing aid at start of day and remove at night; clean as needed; ensure glasses on when awake (sensory deprivation worsens delirium)

NON-PHARMACOLOGIC DELIRIUM BUNDLE: room with large-print clock and calendar visible; reorient at every interaction ('Mrs. O'Sullivan, you're in the hospital, today is...'); well-lit during day with curtains open; dim and quiet at night with no awakening 2200–0600 unless clinically necessary; music she enjoys (daughter has iPad with Irish folk); family at bedside as comforting presence; objects from home (favorite blanket, framed photos)
MOBILITY: out of bed to chair for meals; assisted ambulation with walker as tolerated 3x/day; PT consult; immobility worsens delirium and accelerates functional decline

NUTRITION: regular diet with her preferred foods (daughter knows preferences); soft texture if any swallow concern; aspiration precautions (sit upright >30°, supervised feeding); SLP consult if any signs of swallow dysfunction with the fluctuating LOC; encourage small frequent intake; NO NPO for routine — feed her when she's lucid

CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) screening q-shift; document delirium trajectory; goal is RESOLUTION over 3–7 days as the underlying UTI clears and supportive bundle works (may take longer in dementia — sometimes 2–4 weeks)

Pain assessment with PAINAD (Pain Assessment in Advanced Dementia) scale q4h — verbal report unreliable; agitation may be unrecognized pain; TYLENOL 650 mg PO/PR q6h scheduled (not PRN) if any signs of discomfort
Skin: daily skin assessment; pressure injury prevention (turn q2h if bed-bound, padded boots, padded bony prominences, moisturize); already at risk given thin elderly skin and low weight
Bowel regimen: scheduled docusate + senna; monitor BMs (constipation is delirium-precipitating in elderly); rectal exam if no BM x 3 days to rule out impaction

Sleep hygiene: dim lights 2100, no caffeine after noon, melatonin 1–3 mg PO at bedtime (preferred over diphenhydramine or benzos); minimize awakening overnight; trazodone 25 mg PO at bedtime (lower than her home 50 mg given delirium) PRN only if non-pharm fails — geriatric dosing always lower

Family-centered care: open visitation, daughter at bedside as comforting and reorienting; family meeting tomorrow morning to review care plan, expected trajectory (delirium may take days-weeks to clear in dementia), discharge planning back to Memory Care, advance care planning for any future episodes (avoid sending to ED if comfort-focused)

Discharge planning early: Memory Care unit notified; transition plan with completed antibiotic course, medication reconciliation REMOVING the Beers-list meds permanently, alternative for incontinence (scheduled toileting, NOT oxybutynin), follow-up with PCP within 1 week, geriatric clinic consult if not already established

If clinical deterioration (worsening lactate, hypotension not responsive to fluids, new organ dysfunction): RECONFIRM goals of care with daughter before any escalation; ICU is OUTSIDE her advance directive — palliative consultation if trajectory worsening despite treatment

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Acute onset (3 days) of FLUCTUATING confusion superimposed on baseline dementia; inattention; visual hallucinations; altered LOC — CAM positive for delirium
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the delirium IS the symptom. CAM requires: (1) acute onset + fluctuating course, (2) inattention, AND either (3) disorganized thinking OR (4) altered LOC. In elderly, delirium is often the ONLY sign of serious illness — trust it. The goal is to find and treat the precipitant.

opts.text: UTI confirmed: cloudy foul urine, LE large, nitrites +, WBC >50/hpf, E. coli >100K growing; mild bandemia 8%
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the precipitant identified. Treat the UTI. In an elderly patient with SYMPTOMS (delirium counts as a symptom), positive UA + culture = treat. ASYMPTOMATIC bacteriuria (positive UA, no symptoms) in elderly = DO NOT TREAT (Choosing Wisely). The distinction is the symptoms — and delirium is one.

opts.text: Atypical presentation: NO fever (T 99.6 — relative elevation from her 97.8 baseline), no dysuria reported (she can't articulate), no frequency (she's incontinent)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — geriatric atypical pattern. Many elders mount no fever despite serious infection due to blunted immune response; their baseline temp is ~36.7°C (98°F), so 99.6°F is a relative elevation. NEVER use 'no fever' to rule out infection in elderly — trust delirium, anorexia, falls, new incontinence as symptoms.

opts.text: BEERS LIST medications she's on: oxybutynin (anticholinergic), lorazepam PRN (benzo), diphenhydramine PRN (anticholinergic + sedating) — all contributing to delirium susceptibility
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — polypharmacy contributing. The Beers list (American Geriatrics Society 2023) identifies meds with disproportionate harm in elderly. Hold all three; do not give for any agitation while inpatient. The OXYBUTYNIN was prescribed for new incontinence — which was actually the UTI presenting; the medication then worsened cognition. Two errors compounded.

opts.text: AKI with Cr 1.2 from baseline 0.9; volume depletion (skin tenting, dry mucosa, weight loss 3 kg in 1 month, poor PO x 2 days)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — prerenal AKI from dehydration; reversible with gentle isotonic rehydration. Geriatric thirst response is blunted; rely on offering fluids regularly. eGFR ~48 is important for antibiotic and other med dosing.

opts.text: High fall risk: dementia + new delirium + recent fall + walker user + missing hearing aid + missing glasses
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — falls in delirium are a major morbidity (hip fracture). Sensory deprivation (missing hearing aid and glasses) WORSENS delirium and falls — call daughter to bring them. Sitter, bed alarm, low bed position, walker at bedside, frequent rounding, room near nursing station.

opts.text: Refusing PO x 2 days; weight down 3 kg in 1 month; aspiration risk with fluctuating LOC
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — malnutrition and aspiration risk. Address by offering preferred foods, frequent small meals when lucid, daughter's involvement in feeding (familiar face = better intake), aspiration precautions (>30° upright, supervised), SLP if any swallow concern. Do not NPO routinely — feed when she's lucid.

opts.text: DNR/DNI advance directive with daughter as POA; goals of care: treat reversible UTI + manage delirium with comfort focus; no ICU per directive
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — goals of care must drive every decision. UTI is reversible — treat. Delirium is reversible — manage non-pharmacologically. Aggressive ICU escalation is OUT of scope. Reconfirm with daughter at each decision point; involve palliative care early.

opts.text: No indwelling foley currently; she should NOT be catheterized unless strict indication; CAUTI risk + dementia patient pulling on it
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — avoid iatrogenic harm. Foley indications are narrow (retention >300 mL, end-of-life comfort, accurate UOP for unstable shock). 'For convenience' or 'because she's incontinent' = NOT indications. Use scheduled toileting q2h + external female catheter (PureWick) if accurate output critical.

opts.text: Allergies — sulfa rash, PCN listed but unconfirmed; daughter clarifies tolerated amoxicillin 4 yr ago
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — allergy de-labeling opportunity. PCN listing that's actually 'rash as a child, never tested' is a common over-listing that limits effective antibiotic choices; tolerated amoxicillin = not truly allergic. Cephalosporins are appropriate empirically; document clarification.

opts.text: Daughter at bedside, RN background, well-informed and engaged
opts.rat: Reassuring and helpful — but not in itself a priority cue requiring intervention. Engaged family is a resource: she can help reorient, feed, identify deterioration, and clarify goals of care. Partner with her; do not view her as a 'difficult family member' just because she has medical knowledge.

opts.text: Lactate 1.8 (normal)
opts.rat: Reassuring — rules out septic shock at this moment; supports treating localized UTI rather than urosepsis. Continue to trend; rising lactate would indicate progression and need to reconfirm goals of care with daughter.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What is happening physiologically and clinically?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / geriatric principles.

opts.text: DELIRIUM is acute and FLUCTUATING; DEMENTIA is chronic and progressive; DELIRIUM SUPERIMPOSED on DEMENTIA is common, often missed (the family says 'she always has dementia, this is just worse') — anchor on the ACUTE CHANGE from baseline, not the current state
opts.rat: CORRECT — foundational geriatric concept. Delirium is reversible (often) — dementia is not. Mistaking delirium for 'just more dementia' = missed reversible cause = preventable bad outcome. Always ask family/caregiver: 'How is this different from her usual?' Use CAM systematically.

opts.text: Atypical infection presentation in elderly: NO fever (or low-grade), NO classic localizing symptoms; delirium, falls, new incontinence, anorexia, decreased function are the geriatric 'infection symptoms'; baseline body temperature in elderly is ~36.7°C, so 'no fever' (37°C) may still be a relative elevation
opts.rat: CORRECT — never dismiss infection workup just because afebrile in elderly. UTI, pneumonia, cellulitis, intra-abdominal infection can all present without fever. Look for the FUNCTIONAL change (delirium, falls, decreased PO) as the symptom. Test for what fits the clinical picture.

opts.text: BEERS LIST (American Geriatrics Society 2023) identifies medications with disproportionate harm in adults ≥65; key categories: ANTICHOLINERGICS (oxybutynin, diphenhydramine, TCAs), BENZODIAZEPINES (any), Z-DRUGS (zolpidem), ANTIPSYCHOTICS in dementia (black-box mortality), PPIs long-term, NSAIDs in CKD, sliding-scale insulin alone
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield. Anticholinergic burden is cumulative — patients on 3+ anticholinergics have markedly higher delirium and fall risk. The 'AC Burden Score' is a tool. Patient on TWO anticholinergics (oxybutynin, diphenhydramine) plus a benzo (lorazepam) is set up for delirium.

opts.text: ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA in elderly should NOT be treated — common (20–50% in nursing home residents) and treatment does not reduce mortality but DOES increase resistance, C. diff, and ADEs (Choosing Wisely IDSA 2019); SYMPTOMATIC UTI IS treated — and in elderly DELIRIUM counts as a symptom
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield modern practice. The hard part is distinguishing the two in someone who can't articulate. Use clinical judgment: new acute change from baseline + positive UA + ruling out other causes of delirium = symptomatic, treat. Asymptomatic, stable elderly with just incidental pyuria = do not treat.

opts.text: Non-pharmacologic delirium management is the FIRST-LINE bundle: reorientation, glasses/hearing aids, sleep-wake cycle, mobility, hydration, familiar objects, family presence, music; pharmacology is LAST RESORT due to harm
opts.rat: CORRECT — HELP (Hospital Elder Life Program) and ABCDEF bundle elements. Studies show 30–40% reduction in delirium incidence with non-pharm interventions. Antipsychotics do NOT shorten or improve outcomes in delirium and carry black-box mortality risk in dementia.

opts.text: ANTIPSYCHOTICS (haloperidol, quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone) have an FDA BLACK-BOX WARNING for increased mortality in dementia patients — increased stroke, cardiac death, infection death; reserve as absolute last resort for severe agitation threatening safety after non-pharm exhausted, lowest dose, time-limited, with informed family discussion
opts.rat: CORRECT — frequently missed. Non-pharm first; if absolute last resort, low-dose short-course only, document attempted alternatives, document informed family consent. Haloperidol 0.25–0.5 mg PO/IM is the lowest reasonable dose in this scenario. Avoid IV haloperidol (QT).

opts.text: Indwelling foley catheter is NOT indicated for incontinence alone — CAUTI risk + restraint-like effect; alternatives: scheduled toileting q2h, external female catheter (PureWick), brief check + assist, condom catheter for men; only use foley for retention >300 mL, hemodynamic instability needing accurate UOP, end-of-life comfort, or stage III/IV pressure injury requiring closed wound
opts.rat: CORRECT — CAUTI prevention. Reframing: incontinence in dementia is a NURSING CARE issue, not an indication for tubing. The patient with delirium will pull at any line/tube — urethral trauma is real. Standard toileting protocol + skin care + prompted voiding.

opts.text: Sensory deprivation (missing hearing aid, missing glasses, dim room) WORSENS delirium; addressing sensory access (glasses on, hearing aid in, lights on during day) is a free, high-impact intervention
opts.rat: CORRECT — often missed. The first thing a busy nurse may overlook is whether the patient has her glasses and hearing aid. These are the simplest delirium interventions and have measurable impact. Daughter going home to get them is the immediate priority.

opts.text: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 25 mg PO at bedtime is the appropriate sleep aid choice for an 84-year-old with delirium
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — diphenhydramine is on the Beers list. Strong anticholinergic; worsens cognition, causes urinary retention, falls. NEVER use for sleep in elderly. Better options: non-pharm sleep hygiene first; melatonin 1–3 mg; low-dose trazodone 12.5–25 mg if needed.

opts.text: Lorazepam 0.5 mg IV PRN agitation is the appropriate first-line for delirium agitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — benzodiazepines WORSEN delirium and increase falls. Beers list. Reserve benzos for alcohol/benzo withdrawal only. Non-pharm first; if absolutely necessary for severe agitation threatening safety, low-dose antipsychotic (haloperidol 0.25–0.5 mg) is preferred over a benzo (except in withdrawal).

opts.text: She has a documented PCN allergy on the chart; therefore ceftriaxone must be avoided due to cross-reactivity
opts.rat: Outdated and overly cautious. PCN-listed patients have ~1–2% cross-reactivity with cephalosporins (lower than once thought); for a non-anaphylactic 'rash' history, cephalosporins are safe. Allergy de-labeling is a quality initiative. Document the clarification and proceed.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Acute Delirium Superimposed on Dementia r/t UTI + dehydration + Beers-list medications + sensory deprivation; reversible if precipitants addressed
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Delirium is reversible if precipitants are treated. Antibiotics for the UTI, hydration, hold the Beers-list meds, sensory restoration, non-pharm bundle. The goal is return to baseline at Memory Care; success metric is resolved delirium.

opts.text: Risk for Falls / Injury r/t delirium + dementia + new mobility decline + missing hearing aid and glasses + recent fall
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Hip fracture in this population has 25% 1-year mortality. Active prevention: sitter, bed alarm, low bed, walker bedside, frequent rounding, room near nursing station, sensory restoration. Daughter to bring hearing aid and glasses.

opts.text: Risk for Aspiration / Malnutrition r/t fluctuating LOC + refusing PO + 3 kg weight loss + delirium reducing PO intake
opts.rat: #3 — Significant. Aspiration in delirium can rapidly precipitate aspiration pneumonia, worsening the spiral. Aspiration precautions, supervised feeding when lucid, preferred foods, daughter at meals, SLP if any swallow concern. Hold NPO orders — feed when she can.

opts.text: Risk for CAUTI / Iatrogenic Catheter Injury r/t inappropriate indwelling foley placement
opts.rat: #4 — Preventable iatrogenic risk. Hold the line on no foley; scheduled toileting q2h; external female catheter only if accurate output critical. Educate any staff member who proposes 'putting in a foley because she's incontinent' — that is NOT an indication.

opts.text: Family Distress / Goals-of-Care Confirmation r/t daughter as POA, complex care decisions, anticipated discharge planning back to Memory Care
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Daughter is engaged and informed (RN background) — partner with her. Family meeting tomorrow morning to align expectations: delirium may take days-weeks to clear in dementia; discharge to Memory Care with med list reconciled.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV daily x 7–14 days; transition to oral antibiotic when sensitivities return and patient can swallow safely
opts.rat: Symptomatic UTI (delirium IS the symptom) + positive UA + culture = treat. PCN allergy clarified as non-anaphylactic; cephalosporin safe. Renally dosed for eGFR ~48. Plan to narrow per culture sensitivities (likely nitrofurantoin if eGFR remains ≥30, or oral cefpodoxime/cefdinir).

opts.text: HOLD oxybutynin, lorazepam PRN, diphenhydramine PRN; D/C from inpatient orders permanently; reconcile at discharge
opts.rat: Beers-list polypharmacy removal. Each contributes to delirium and fall risk. Replace incontinence management with scheduled toileting, not anticholinergics. Replace sleep aid with non-pharm + melatonin or low-dose trazodone. Replace agitation PRN with non-pharm bundle (no benzo).

opts.text: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 25 mg PO at bedtime for sleep
opts.rat: ⚠️ Beers list anticholinergic — worsens delirium, urinary retention, falls. Use non-pharm sleep hygiene; if needed, melatonin 1–3 mg or low-dose trazodone 12.5–25 mg. Never use diphenhydramine for sleep in elderly.

opts.text: Lorazepam 0.5 mg IV PRN agitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ Worsens delirium and falls; Beers list. Reserve benzos for alcohol/benzo withdrawal only. Non-pharm bundle first; if absolutely necessary low-dose antipsychotic (haloperidol 0.25–0.5 mg) preferred over benzo (with informed family discussion given black-box dementia mortality).

opts.text: Haloperidol 5 mg IM stat for the visual hallucinations
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong on TWO counts: (1) dose far too high for an 84 y/o with dementia (start 0.25–0.5 mg), (2) the hallucinations are part of the delirium, NOT distressing to her, NOT threatening anyone — they don't require pharmacologic suppression. Non-pharm reorientation, family presence. Reserve antipsychotic for severe agitation threatening safety after non-pharm fails.

opts.text: Isotonic saline 0.9% NS at 75–100 mL/hr until adequately rehydrated (UOP ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, Cr toward baseline); transition to PO with frequent encouragement
opts.rat: Gentle rehydration for prerenal AKI + delirium contribution. Avoid aggressive boluses in elderly (risk of pulmonary edema in occult HF). Encourage PO with preferred beverages; daughter knows her preferences.

opts.text: Insert indwelling foley catheter to manage urinary incontinence and accurately monitor UOP
opts.rat: ⚠️ Incontinence alone is NOT an indication. CAUTI risk + restraint-like effect + delirium patient pulling on tubing. Use scheduled toileting q2h + external female catheter (PureWick) if accurate output critical. Bladder scan if retention suspected.

opts.text: Non-pharmacologic delirium bundle: reorientation at every interaction, glasses + hearing aid (daughter retrieving), sleep-wake cycle (lights on day / dim and quiet night, no awakenings 2200–0600 unless clinical), familiar objects, family at bedside, music she enjoys, scheduled q2h toileting
opts.rat: First-line and most effective for delirium. HELP and ABCDEF bundle elements. 30–40% delirium reduction in studies. Document each element. Train every staff member to reorient at start of each interaction.

opts.text: Mobility: out of bed to chair for meals; assisted ambulation with walker 3x/day; PT consult
opts.rat: Immobility worsens delirium and accelerates functional decline ("dwindles"). Get her out of bed; she has a walker. PT for safe gait reassessment. Family at bedside encourages participation.

opts.text: Bed restraints (wrist) for safety given fall risk and pulling at IV line
opts.rat: ⚠️ Restraints WORSEN delirium and increase falls/injury. Use less restrictive alternatives first: sitter, bed alarm, low bed, mittens (less restrictive than wrist restraints), enclosed bed (Posey bed), padded boots. Restraints last resort with time-limited orders, q15-min monitoring, attempted alternatives documented.

opts.text: Sitter at bedside (1:1) for close observation and reorientation given fall risk + delirium + pulling at lines; daughter can substitute when present
opts.rat: Least-restrictive safety intervention. Sitter provides constant supervision, reorientation, immediate response to fall-risk movement. Less restrictive than restraints. Daughter at bedside is even better — familiar face reduces agitation. Document sitter rationale.

opts.text: Family meeting with daughter (POA) tomorrow morning: review trajectory, expected delirium course (days to weeks in dementia), discharge planning back to Memory Care with reconciled med list (Beers-list meds permanently removed), advance care planning for future episodes
opts.rat: Goal-aligned care planning. Daughter is engaged and informed; partner with her. Anticipate discharge planning early; expected delirium course; medication reconciliation; future-episode planning (avoid sending to ED for comfort-focused goals; treat at Memory Care if possible).

opts.text: Scheduled acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR q6h with PAINAD assessment q4h (verbal pain report unreliable in delirium + dementia; agitation may be unrecognized pain)
opts.rat: Geriatric pain assessment. The PAINAD scale evaluates breathing, vocalization, facial expression, body language, consolability — not self-report. Untreated pain is a delirium precipitant. Scheduled (not PRN) tylenol covers baseline analgesia; reassess; opioids only if needed at lowest dose.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: HOLD all Beers-list home medications (oxybutynin, lorazepam PRN, diphenhydramine PRN); confirm with provider; reconcile MAR; flag to pharmacy; document
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stop the iatrogenic delirium contributors. Each missed dose of an anticholinergic or benzo is one less obstacle to delirium clearing. Provider clarification within minutes; document MAR change.

opts.text: Initiate non-pharm delirium bundle: room near nursing station, sitter, reorientation at every interaction, large-print clock, daughter at bedside, daughter to bring hearing aid + glasses from home; safety bundle (bed alarm, low bed, walker)
opts.rat: #2 — Bundle that is free and high-impact. The sensory restoration (hearing aid + glasses) is the single most overlooked intervention. The sitter prevents falls. Reorientation reduces agitation. Daughter is a major asset; partner with her.

opts.text: Confirm ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily x 7–14 days; gentle isotonic rehydration 75–100 mL/hr; q2h scheduled toileting (NO foley); trend UOP and Cr
opts.rat: #3 — Treat the precipitant. Antibiotics for the UTI. Rehydration for AKI and delirium. NO foley. Scheduled toileting maintains continence rhythm and avoids catheter. UOP and Cr trended q-shift.

opts.text: Falls + aspiration precautions: bed in low position, sitter, walker at bedside, non-slip footwear, sit upright >30° for meals, supervised feeding when lucid, daughter assists with meals; preferred foods; nutrition consult
opts.rat: #4 — Concurrent safety bundles. Falls are the #2 priority; aspiration is the #3. Both addressed with positioning, supervision, and family involvement. Nutrition consult for preferred foods + small frequent meals.

opts.text: Schedule family meeting tomorrow morning with daughter, intensivist/geriatrician, palliative; review goals of care, expected delirium trajectory, discharge planning back to Memory Care, advance care planning for future episodes; document
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care alignment. Family meeting integrates the medical plan with the daughter's understanding and the advance directive. Discharge planning starts on day of admission — Memory Care notified, transition plan, reconciled meds, follow-up scheduled.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 2: CAM negative for first time in 4 days; oriented to person and place (not date — but baseline); recognizes daughter; eating ~50% of meals; voiding in toilet with assistance; off IV fluids; on PO cefdinir per sensitivities; afebrile
opts.rat: Delirium clearing. UTI responding. PO transition successful. Continue non-pharm bundle, complete antibiotic course, mobilize, prepare for discharge back to Memory Care. May still have residual fluctuations — delirium in dementia takes longer to fully clear.

opts.text: Day 5 discharge to Memory Care: at baseline cognitively (oriented to self, recognizes daughter, sleeping 8 hr, ambulating with walker, PO meds tolerated, eating regular diet); completed 7 days of antibiotics; med list reconciled (Beers meds permanently removed); follow-up with PCP in 1 week
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. Return to baseline. Discharge with reconciled med list — oxybutynin permanently off, no benzos or diphenhydramine PRN, sleep hygiene + low-dose melatonin or trazodone if needed. PCP follow-up within 1 week. Memory Care updated on plan.

opts.text: 2-week phone follow-up: at baseline, no further UTI symptoms, family verbalizes understanding of avoiding the Beers-list meds at any future encounter, Memory Care implementing scheduled toileting protocol
opts.rat: Sustained recovery. Education has translated to behavior change at Memory Care. Family is empowered to advocate at future provider visits ('please do not restart oxybutynin or diphenhydramine'). The next UTI presentation will be caught earlier with the lessons of this episode.

opts.text: Hour 6: cross-cover provider orders diphenhydramine 25 mg PO at bedtime for sleep; nurse questions order; physician reinforces; nurse administers; patient becomes more confused overnight and falls trying to climb out of bed (no injury)
opts.rat: Iatrogenic worsening — Beers-list med given despite chart documentation. The RN should have HELD the medication, escalated to charge RN or attending. 'Stop the line' authority is the standard for known patient-harm orders. Incident report; system flag; education for cross-cover; family informed.

opts.text: Day 3: new fever 101.4, increased respiratory rate, new productive cough, CXR shows RLL infiltrate — aspiration pneumonia from her fluctuating LOC
opts.rat: Complication. Aspiration precautions failed (or were not maintained). SLP consult for swallow evaluation; head of bed >30°; supervised feeding; modified diet if recommended; broaden antibiotic coverage to cover aspiration organisms; verify advance directive for any care escalation.

opts.text: Day 4: lactate rising to 3.8, BP 88/52 not improving with fluid, new oliguria — progressing to urosepsis
opts.rat: Sepsis progression. Per advance directive (DNR/DNI, no ICU, comfort with treatment of reversible conditions), URGENT family meeting with daughter: continue antibiotics, escalate antibiotic spectrum if needed, gentle fluid resuscitation on the floor BUT NOT ICU transfer. Palliative consultation; reframe care goals; pain and dyspnea management.

opts.text: Hour 12: patient calmer with daughter at bedside, hearing aid and glasses now in place; no agitation despite withholding of all Beers meds and no antipsychotic; sleeping at night; less hallucinatory content
opts.rat: Non-pharm bundle effective. Validates the approach: sensory restoration + family + reorientation + holding the contributing meds is sufficient most of the time. Documents the success — useful for educating staff who default to 'give haldol' for any delirium agitation.

opts.text: Day 2 PT consult: still safe with walker, can ambulate 25 ft with one-person assist, working on transfers and balance
opts.rat: Maintained baseline mobility despite acute illness — a win. Continued PT through hospitalization and after discharge protects against deconditioning. The major risk in elders is hospital-acquired functional decline that doesn't recover.

opts.text: Persistent mild proximal weakness at discharge (hospital deconditioning); requires more assist than baseline for transfers
opts.rat: Expected post-hospitalization in elderly. PT/OT at Memory Care; nutrition with protein; early mobilization at facility; risk of further decline if not addressed. Memory Care informed of new baseline.

opts.text: Hour 24: still requires q2h reorientation; visual hallucinations less but present; PAINAD score 0 with scheduled acetaminophen; eating only 25% but accepts sips of preferred beverages; daughter at bedside most of day
opts.rat: Expected slow trajectory in delirium-on-dementia. Improvement takes days-weeks, not hours. Continue bundle; do not panic and add antipsychotic. Track CAM q-shift. Document non-pharm interventions effectiveness.

title: Severe Digoxin Toxicity from Amiodarone Drug Interaction with Hyperkalemia
subtitle: DigiFab Is the Antidote, the Hyperkalemia Is a Sign Not a Target — and Why You DO NOT Give Calcium for the High K in This Specific Hyperkalemia

patient.name: C. Beauchamp
patient.admitDate: Today, 1045 (ED via EMS — found by daughter confused, vomiting, with HR 38)
patient.room: Cardiac Monitored Bed 9 — Telemetry pending CCU bed

vitals.time: 1230 (CURRENT — post 1 amp atropine, 1 L NS, DigiFab being mixed in pharmacy)
vitals.hr: 46 bpm (still junctional, slightly faster after atropine)

labs.v: 4.2 ng/mL (drawn at 1100, last dose was last night at 2100 — ~14 hr post-dose, near trough)
labs.r: Therapeutic for AFib rate control: 0.5–2.0 ng/mL; narrower for HFrEF: 0.5–0.9 ng/mL (DIG trial — higher levels associated with worse outcomes); toxic >2.0 with symptoms or >3.0 absolute; severely toxic >4.0

labs.v: 6.4 mEq/L
labs.r: 3.5–5.0; hyperkalemia in ACUTE dig toxicity reflects severity — dig inhibits Na/K-ATPase, K leaves cells, serum K rises; K >5.5 in acute dig toxicity = PROGNOSTIC marker of severity; K >5.0 historically defined indication for DigiFab in acute overdose

labs.v: Mg 1.4 (LOW), iCa 1.10, PO4 4.4
labs.r: Mg 1.7–2.4; HYPOMAGNESEMIA PREDISPOSES to digoxin toxicity at any level and worsens arrhythmias; replace ASAP. Ca borderline — but DO NOT supplement aggressively in dig toxicity (relative contraindication for IV calcium — 'stone heart' theoretical risk in animal models; modern human data softer but still avoid bolus IV calcium when DigiFab available)

labs.v: Cr 1.8 (baseline 1.3 — chronic Stage 3b CKD); BUN 38; eGFR 28 mL/min/1.73m² (down from baseline ~38; AKI on CKD)
labs.r: Cr 0.6–1.1 female; her CKD baseline + acute AKI from prerenal (vomiting + diuretic + low intake) — reduced renal clearance compounds digoxin accumulation; dig is ~70% renally excreted; CKD requires reduced dose (0.0625–0.125 mg daily, not 0.25 mg)

labs.v: Na 136 / Cl 100 / HCO3 22 / Glucose 124
labs.r: Within normal — rules out other metabolic causes of altered mental status

labs.v: Sinus arrest with junctional escape rate 38; PR cannot be measured (no P waves consistently); QRS 92 ms; downsloping/scooped 'Salvador Dali mustache' ST depression in V4–V6 (digoxin effect); QTc 380 (shortened); no VF/VT currently; baseline pacer not capturing (pacemaker output threshold issue per cardiology bedside check)
labs.r: Hallmark dig toxicity rhythm patterns: junctional rhythm (any rate), AV blocks, ventricular ectopy, atrial tachycardia WITH block (highly specific), bidirectional VT (rare, specific). 'Scooped/sagging' ST depression is the 'dig effect' (present at therapeutic levels too — not by itself diagnostic of toxicity)

labs.v: DIGOXIN 0.25 mg PO daily x 8 yr for AFib + HFrEF (EF 35%) — dose unchanged despite CKD progression; AMIODARONE 200 mg PO daily STARTED 3 WEEKS AGO by cardiologist (no dig dose reduction at that time — KEY MISS); furosemide 40 mg PO daily; lisinopril 10 mg PO daily; carvedilol 12.5 mg PO BID; warfarin 4 mg PO daily (INR target 2–3 for AFib); KCl 20 mEq PO daily (often forgets); atorvastatin 20 mg daily; senna PRN
labs.r: AMIODARONE is a classic dig-level raiser — should trigger dig dose REDUCTION by 30–50%; the cardiologist did not adjust. FUROSEMIDE causes hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia (both predispose to dig toxicity); she 'often forgets' the KCl. PVCs from electrolyte imbalance + dig toxicity. Multiple contributing factors stacked

labs.n: Medications that raise digoxin level (drug interactions to know)
labs.v: Patient on AMIODARONE; other classic interactions include verapamil, diltiazem, quinidine, propafenone, spironolactone, clarithromycin/erythromycin, itraconazole, cyclosporine; the cardiologist did not reduce dig when amiodarone was added 3 weeks ago
labs.r: Mechanism: most reduce renal/P-glycoprotein clearance; amiodarone displaces from tissue binding AND inhibits Pgp/CYP. When any of these added, dig dose typically halved with level check in 7 days

labs.v: Yellow-green visual halos around lights (xanthopsia — classic dig toxicity), nausea, 3 episodes of vomiting overnight, fatigue and weakness x 3 days, confusion since this morning, no chest pain, no syncope but feeling 'about to pass out' when standing
labs.r: Dig toxicity triad: GI (nausea/vomiting/anorexia) + neuro (confusion, weakness, fatigue) + visual (xanthopsia — yellow-green halos around lights, 'color vision changes'); cardiac symptoms (palpitations, near-syncope) are the most dangerous

labs.v: AFib paroxysmal x 12 yr; HFrEF EF 35% x 8 yr (ischemic cardiomyopathy); 3-vessel CABG 2018; permanent dual-chamber pacemaker 2019 (sick sinus syndrome); HTN; CKD Stage 3b baseline Cr 1.3; chronic anemia of CKD Hgb 10.4 baseline
labs.r: Multiple cardiovascular comorbidities — both supports the indication for dig + amiodarone AND amplifies the consequences of toxicity. Pacemaker may not pace through severe dig toxicity (threshold issues)

labs.n: Reason for amiodarone addition 3 weeks ago
labs.v: Recurrent breakthrough AFib with RVR despite digoxin + carvedilol; cardiologist added amiodarone as rate/rhythm-control adjunct. Amiodarone has known interaction with both digoxin (raises level 50–100%) and warfarin (raises INR by 25–50%). Dig dose not reduced and INR not specifically monitored at that visit
labs.r: Highly common scenario — adding amiodarone without proportional reduction of digoxin and without close INR/dig follow-up. This is a tested 'medication safety' point and a sentinel-event pattern

labs.v: INR 4.8 (target 2–3; baseline at last check 4 weeks ago was 2.4)
labs.r: Supratherapeutic INR from AMIODARONE-WARFARIN INTERACTION (same root cause as dig toxicity — amiodarone added without proportional reductions). Holds warfarin; if no active bleeding and INR <10, vitamin K NOT urgently needed; recheck in 24 hr

labs.v: Confused, oriented to self only (baseline oriented x3 per daughter); pale, mildly diaphoretic; cap refill 3 sec; cool extremities; mild dehydration (skin tenting, dry mucosa); no peripheral edema worse than baseline; lungs clear; abdomen soft non-tender; no focal neuro deficit; no signs of bleeding (no melena, no bruising)
labs.r: Confusion + hypoperfusion signs from bradycardia. No evidence of warfarin-related bleeding despite supratherapeutic INR. Hypovolemia from vomiting + missed PO intake.

labs.v: No melena/hematemesis/gross bleeding despite INR 4.8; no chest pain; no recent infection signs; no medication overdose intent (chronic toxicity, not acute ingestion); no other recent med changes
labs.r: Rules out alternative diagnoses; clarifies this is CHRONIC dig toxicity (accumulation over weeks since amiodarone added) rather than ACUTE intentional overdose — DigiFab dosing differs (lower for chronic)

nursesNote: 1230 — 78 y/o female with paroxysmal AFib + HFrEF EF 35% (ischemic) + permanent pacemaker + CKD Stage 3b on chronic digoxin 0.25 mg PO daily x 8 yr presented to ED at 1045 via EMS after daughter found her confused, vomiting, and bradycardic (EMS HR 38). The unifying diagnosis is CHRONIC DIGOXIN TOXICITY precipitated by AMIODARONE 200 mg PO daily STARTED 3 WEEKS AGO by her cardiologist for breakthrough AFib — without proportional reduction of the digoxin dose (a classic medication-safety miss; amiodarone raises dig levels 50–100% by inhibiting Pgp clearance). Compounding factors stacked over the 3-week window: furosemide 40 mg daily causing hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia (both predispose to dig toxicity even at therapeutic levels), KCl 20 mEq daily 'often forgotten' per daughter (worsening the K depletion until acute toxicity), and progression of her CKD (Cr 1.8 from baseline 1.3, eGFR 28 — reduced renal clearance compounds dig accumulation). Symptoms over the past 3 days: fatigue, weakness, nausea, anorexia (the classic GI prodrome); then yellow-green visual halos around lights (XANTHOPSIA — highly specific for dig toxicity); then this morning vomiting x 3 episodes and confusion; brought to ED by daughter when found unable to stand. Initial vitals: HR 38 junctional escape, BP 84/52, GCS E4V4M6. EKG: sinus arrest with junctional escape rate 38, scooped/Salvador-Dali ST depression V4–V6 (the 'dig effect'), QTc 380 (dig shortens QT). Stat labs: digoxin level 4.2 ng/mL (severely toxic; therapeutic 0.5–2.0 for AFib, 0.5–0.9 for HF), K 6.4 (hyperkalemia from Na/K-ATPase inhibition — a PROGNOSTIC marker of severity NOT a treatment target per se), Mg 1.4 (low — replace), Cr 1.8 (AKI on CKD), INR 4.8 incidentally (supratherapeutic from concurrent amiodarone–warfarin interaction). At 1100 atropine 1 mg IV given for symptomatic bradycardia with partial response (HR 38 → 46); 1 L NS bolus for volume; pacemaker interrogated bedside — capture issues at threshold (dig affects myocardial excitability). DIGOXIN IMMUNE FAB (DigiFab) is being mixed by pharmacy — dose calculation based on serum level + body weight: 2 vials initial for chronic toxicity (chronic dosing is lower than acute overdose dosing; acute overdose with known mg ingested or level uses different formula). Empiric 2 vials = 80 mg DigiFab IV over 30 min; reassess in 1 hr; can re-dose up to 6–10 vials for refractory. THE HYPERKALEMIA IS NOT TREATED WITH CALCIUM in dig toxicity — historical animal-model concern of 'stone heart' (severe sustained contracture from intracellular calcium overload combined with dig's already-inhibited Na/K-ATPase); modern human data are softer but the consensus is still to AVOID bolus IV calcium when DigiFab is available — the DigiFab will resolve the hyperkalemia by reactivating the Na/K-ATPase as it binds dig out of tissues. Insulin 10 units + D50 25 g IV can be given if K rising critically or EKG progressing; albuterol 10 mg neb adjunct; HD does NOT remove digoxin effectively (large Vd, tissue-bound, lipophilic). Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV given for the low Mg (also has antiarrhythmic effect). HOLD digoxin, amiodarone, furosemide, warfarin pending. Vitamin K NOT given for the INR 4.8 since no bleeding and INR <10 — recheck in 24 hr; hold warfarin. Continuous cardiac monitoring; defibrillator at bedside; ACLS team aware. If progression to VT/VF: lidocaine preferred over amiodarone (already on amiodarone — this is what got us here); transcutaneous pacing pads on chest as backup if pacemaker fails to capture (severe dig toxicity can cause pacemaker capture failure). Atropine and isoproterenol have limited efficacy in severe dig toxicity — DigiFab is THE intervention. Daughter at bedside, very informed; cardiology fellow notified — will likely admit to CCU. Family meeting later today to review medication safety, the amiodarone–dig interaction lesson, and discharge plan with cardiology follow-up + medication reconciliation including reduced dig dose (or dig discontinuation given the narrow therapeutic window in this patient).
providerOrders: ADMIT to CCU (Cardiac Care Unit); continuous cardiac monitoring with arrhythmia alarms ON; transcutaneous pacing pads on chest; defibrillator at bedside; ACLS team aware

DIGOXIN IMMUNE FAB (DigiFab) IV — empiric initial dose 2 vials (80 mg) for chronic toxicity with level 4.2 ng/mL; mix per pharmacy in NS; infuse over 30 min unless cardiac arrest then push; reassess clinically and EKG in 1 hr; can re-dose up to 6–10 vials for refractory toxicity

DigiFab indications: hemodynamically significant arrhythmia, K >5.0 in acute dig toxicity (less clear cutoff in chronic), end-organ dysfunction (renal failure, altered mental status), acute ingestion ≥10 mg adult / ≥4 mg pediatric, level >10 ng/mL post-acute or >6 in chronic with symptoms

DigiFab dosing formulas: Acute known ingestion = (mg ingested × 0.8) / 0.5; Acute or chronic with level = (level × weight in kg) / 100, rounded up to whole vials; empiric for unknown = 10–20 vials acute, 2–6 vials chronic

HOLD digoxin, amiodarone, furosemide, warfarin pending toxicology resolution; coordinate with cardiology; PERMANENT D/C or dose-reduce digoxin at discharge (narrow therapeutic index + amiodarone interaction make this unsafe at 0.25 mg daily with CKD — consider 0.0625 mg daily or discontinue entirely)

Hyperkalemia management in DIG TOXICITY: STANDARD K-LOWERING THERAPIES OK (insulin 10 units IV + D50 25 g IV; albuterol 10 mg neb; sodium bicarbonate not routine), but ⚠️ DO NOT GIVE BOLUS IV CALCIUM — historical 'stone heart' concern; if Ca needed for documented hypocalcemia, give over hours, not as push; DigiFab will resolve the hyperK by reactivating Na/K-ATPase
MAGNESIUM SULFATE 2 g IV over 15 min for hypomagnesemia (Mg 1.4); recheck after; Mg has additional antiarrhythmic effect; repeat to target Mg 2.0+; replace orally once tolerated

Atropine 0.5–1 mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia (partial response only — limited efficacy in dig toxicity); avoid isoproterenol (can precipitate arrhythmia); transcutaneous pacing as bridge if needed; do NOT rely on pacemaker capture in severe toxicity (myocardial excitability altered)

Lidocaine 1 mg/kg IV bolus then 1–4 mg/min infusion for ventricular arrhythmias (PREFERRED in dig toxicity); AVOID class IA antiarrhythmics (quinidine, procainamide — raise dig level further); AVOID amiodarone (already a contributor and raises levels)
Hemodialysis is NOT effective for digoxin removal (large Vd ~5–7 L/kg, lipophilic, tissue-bound); do not consult nephrology for this purpose

Hold warfarin; INR 4.8 with NO bleeding and INR <10 = HOLD only; do NOT give vitamin K routinely (overcorrects, makes warfarin less effective later); recheck INR in 24 hr; if any bleeding develops, low-dose oral vitamin K 1–2.5 mg; PCC only for major bleeding
IV access: 2 PIVs (≥18 g); central access if hemodynamic instability persists; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP and ABG access if needed
Strict I&O hourly; daily weights; UOP target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; trend Cr q12h (CKD on AKI baseline)

Recheck digoxin level AFTER DigiFab is INVALID (assay measures both free AND Fab-bound dig — appears falsely high); clinical response and K trend are the better markers post-DigiFab; if dig level needed, use free-digoxin assay if available; serum dig level typically returns to safe range in 48–72 hr
Repeat CBC, CMP, magnesium, ionized calcium q4h; troponin baseline and 6 hr (rule out concurrent ischemia); BNP
Continuous pulse oximetry; vitals q15 min until stable then q1h x 12 hr; cardiac monitor strip q4h and with any rhythm change

Nutrition: NPO initially given vomiting and altered LOC; reassess for clear liquids when alert and not vomiting; nutrition consult for advanced regimen; antiemetic ondansetron 4 mg IV q6h PRN (watch QTc — dig shortens QT but ondansetron prolongs — balance)

Fall precautions; geriatric care bundle: ✓ AVOID Beers-list meds for any agitation (no benzos, no diphenhydramine); ✓ Sensory restoration (her glasses are at home — call daughter); reorientation; family presence; sleep hygiene

Cardiology consultation primary; pharmacy consult for medication reconciliation focused on the amiodarone–dig and amiodarone–warfarin interactions; consider electrophysiology consultation for long-term rate-control strategy that doesn't require digoxin

Family meeting after stabilization: review the amiodarone–digoxin interaction lesson with daughter; medication management plan; cardiology follow-up; INR monitoring; future-prevention plan including specific questions to ask any provider before accepting any new prescription with her existing regimen

If clinical deterioration despite 2 vials DigiFab: re-dose 2 more vials, consult toxicology / poison control; if cardiac arrest, full ACLS PLUS push DigiFab full empiric dose (10 vials); CALL POISON CONTROL 1-800-222-1222 (US) for any complex toxicology consultation

Discharge planning: medication reconciliation (likely PERMANENT D/C of digoxin OR drastically reduced dose 0.0625 mg daily with monthly level monitoring; alternative AFib rate control with carvedilol up-titration or rate-control with diltiazem); strict communication with cardiologist regarding any future medication addition; pharmacist medication review before any new prescription; cardiology + PCP follow-up within 1 week

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Digoxin level 4.2 ng/mL (toxic; therapeutic 0.5–2.0 for AFib, 0.5–0.9 for HF) with classic toxicity symptoms (GI, neuro, visual, cardiac)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms severe dig toxicity. Level alone is not the whole story but combined with symptoms = treat with DigiFab. The triad of GI (nausea/vomit) + neuro (confusion/fatigue) + visual (yellow-green halos / xanthopsia) is highly specific. Cardiac signs are the most dangerous.

opts.text: EKG: sinus arrest with junctional escape rate 38, scooped/Salvador-Dali ST depression, QTc 380 — dig effect with bradyarrhythmia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the arrhythmic burden. Classic dig toxicity rhythms: junctional rhythm (any rate), AV blocks, atrial tachycardia WITH BLOCK (highly specific), bidirectional VT (specific but rare), ventricular ectopy. Continuous monitoring; defibrillator at bedside; ACLS aware.

opts.text: Hyperkalemia K 6.4 in the setting of acute dig toxicity — reflects severity and is a treatment indicator for DigiFab (NOT a target for calcium therapy)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high K in acute dig toxicity reflects severity (dig inhibits Na/K-ATPase → K leaves cells). It's a prognostic marker AND historically indicated DigiFab. Treat with DigiFab; standard shift therapies OK (insulin/D50, albuterol); AVOID bolus IV calcium ('stone heart' concern).

opts.text: Hypomagnesemia Mg 1.4 — predisposes to digoxin toxicity at any level and worsens arrhythmias
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — modifiable factor. Hypomagnesemia (often from chronic furosemide without replacement) is a major predisposing factor to dig toxicity even at therapeutic levels. Replace IV (Mg sulfate 2 g IV); additional antiarrhythmic benefit. Hypokalemia also predisposes (her KCl was 'often forgotten').

opts.text: AMIODARONE started 3 weeks ago without proportional digoxin dose reduction — classic drug interaction missed by prescribing cardiologist
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the proximate cause. Amiodarone raises dig levels 50–100% by inhibiting P-glycoprotein and tissue binding. When added, dig dose is typically halved with level check in 7 days. This is a tested medication-safety scenario. Concurrent amiodarone-warfarin interaction also raised her INR.

opts.text: CKD Stage 3b with AKI on chronic baseline (Cr 1.8 from 1.3, eGFR 28) — dig is ~70% renally excreted and accumulates in renal dysfunction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — third contributing factor. Renal clearance is the primary route for dig elimination; with CKD progression and acute AKI from vomiting, accumulation accelerates. CKD requires reduced dig dose (0.0625–0.125 daily, not 0.25); her dose was not adjusted as her CKD progressed over years.

opts.text: Hypotension BP 84/52 with hypoperfusion signs (cap refill 3 sec, cool extremities, confusion) — cardiogenic from bradyarrhythmia + relative volume depletion from vomiting
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — hemodynamic compromise. Indicates urgency of restoring rate (DigiFab + cautious atropine + transcutaneous pacing pads ready). Gentle volume resuscitation 1 L NS bolus given. Do not over-resuscitate (HFrEF EF 35% — pulmonary edema risk).

opts.text: Pacemaker capture issue identified at bedside interrogation — severe dig toxicity affects myocardial excitability and pacing thresholds
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — cannot rely on the pacemaker as safety net during severe toxicity. Transcutaneous pacing pads as backup; document; pacemaker will likely capture again once toxicity resolves. Avoid raising voltage output excessively (battery drain, painful stimulation).

opts.text: INR 4.8 (target 2–3) from concurrent amiodarone–warfarin interaction; no active bleeding
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sister problem from same root cause. Amiodarone raises INR 25–50%. No bleeding + INR <10 = HOLD warfarin only; vitamin K NOT urgently needed (overcorrects); recheck in 24 hr. This case is a teaching moment: one prescribing decision (adding amiodarone without dose adjustments of both digoxin and warfarin) created two parallel toxicities.

opts.text: Xanthopsia (yellow-green visual halos around lights) — highly specific for dig toxicity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pathognomonic-ish visual symptom. Color vision changes including yellow-green halos around lights ('xanthopsia') are highly specific for dig toxicity. Famous association with Van Gogh's late paintings (theorized he was on digitalis for epilepsy). Reversible with treatment.

opts.text: Allergies: sulfa rash
opts.rat: Reassuring — documented and not relevant to current treatment. No sulfa antibiotics planned. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 97.6°F (normal)
opts.rat: Reassuring — rules out fever / infection as primary driver. Note that elderly baseline is ~36.7°C (98°F) so 97.6 is mildly hypothermic for normal range but consistent with hypoperfusion. Not the primary issue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pharmacology / pathophysiology.

opts.text: DIGOXIN has a NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX (therapeutic 0.5–2.0 ng/mL for AFib, 0.5–0.9 for HF; toxic >2.0 with symptoms or >3.0 absolute); ~70% renally excreted; half-life 36–48 hr (longer in CKD); large Vd ~5–7 L/kg (lipophilic, tissue-bound) — explains why HD does NOT remove it
opts.rat: CORRECT — foundational pharmacology. The narrow window + renal clearance + long half-life + tissue binding all combine to make dig toxicity slow to develop AND slow to resolve. DigiFab is the antidote; standard supportive care fills the gap until DigiFab takes effect.

opts.text: Drug interactions that raise digoxin level: AMIODARONE (50–100%), VERAPAMIL, DILTIAZEM, QUINIDINE, PROPAFENONE, SPIRONOLACTONE, CLARITHROMYCIN/ERYTHROMYCIN, ITRACONAZOLE, CYCLOSPORINE — when any added, dig dose typically halved with level check in 7 days
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield NCLEX/pharm content. Mechanism varies (Pgp inhibition, displaced tissue binding, reduced renal clearance) but result is the same. The pattern: NEW medication added + no proportional dig adjustment + symptoms 1–3 weeks later. Cardiology and pharmacy must coordinate.

opts.text: HYPOKALEMIA and HYPOMAGNESEMIA PREDISPOSE to digoxin toxicity at any level — digoxin and K compete for the Na/K-ATPase binding site; low K (e.g., from chronic loop diuretic without K replacement) increases dig binding to the enzyme = more inhibition = more toxicity
opts.rat: CORRECT — explains why diuretic users on dig are high-risk. Always replete K (target 4–5) and Mg (target 2+) in dig-treated patients. Furosemide + dig without K supplement is a 'set up' for toxicity. The KCl she 'often forgot' is what unmasked the dig toxicity.

opts.text: Acute digoxin toxicity causes HYPERKALEMIA (dig blocks Na/K-ATPase → K leaks out of cells); K >5.0 in acute overdose is a PROGNOSTIC marker of severity and historically defined DigiFab indication; modern guidelines use K >5.0 with EKG changes or hemodynamic compromise
opts.rat: CORRECT — and high-yield trap. Chronic dig toxicity has less consistent hyperkalemia than acute overdose (renal handling adapts). Bower's classic paper: K >5.0 in acute dig overdose without treatment had 100% mortality. DigiFab dramatically improved outcomes.

opts.text: ⚠️ AVOID bolus IV CALCIUM for hyperkalemia in DIGOXIN TOXICITY — historical animal-model concern for 'stone heart' (severe sustained myocardial contracture from intracellular calcium overload + dig-inhibited Na/K-ATPase); modern human data are less stark but consensus is still to avoid bolus calcium when DigiFab is available
opts.rat: CORRECT — important safety point. The DigiFab will resolve the hyperkalemia by reactivating Na/K-ATPase as it binds dig out of tissues. If Ca is needed for documented hypocalcemia (rare in dig toxicity), give over hours, not as push. Standard hyperK shift therapies (insulin/D50, albuterol) are fine.

opts.text: DIGOXIN IMMUNE FAB (DigiFab) is the antidote — Fab antibody fragments bind digoxin in plasma → equilibrium pulls dig out of tissues → reactivates Na/K-ATPase → resolves hyperK and arrhythmias; renal excretion of the Fab-dig complex; onset 20–30 min, complete in 1–2 hr
opts.rat: CORRECT — the antidote pharmacology. After DigiFab, the serum dig level assay is FALSELY HIGH (measures both free and bound dig); use clinical response and K trend instead. Free-dig assay if available. Toxic dig level returns to safe range in 48–72 hr.

opts.text: HEMODIALYSIS effectively removes digoxin and should be used for severe toxicity
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Digoxin has a LARGE volume of distribution (~5–7 L/kg, tissue-bound, lipophilic) — HD does NOT meaningfully remove it. This is a common trap. DigiFab is the answer for severe toxicity; HD is for the hyperkalemia ONLY if K not responding to other measures (rare with DigiFab) AND for the AKI if it progresses.

opts.text: Treat dig-toxicity ventricular arrhythmias with LIDOCAINE preferred over amiodarone or class IA agents (procainamide, quinidine) — class IA agents raise dig level and can worsen toxicity
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield distinction. Amiodarone is already a contributor in this case. Class IA agents (procainamide, quinidine) raise dig levels and exacerbate. Lidocaine is the preferred ventricular antiarrhythmic in dig toxicity. Beta-blockers can be considered but risky given bradycardia.

opts.text: After DigiFab administration, the SERUM DIGOXIN LEVEL ASSAY measures both FREE and Fab-BOUND digoxin → appears FALSELY HIGH; do not use this number to titrate care; use clinical response and K trend; free-digoxin assay is available at some centers
opts.rat: CORRECT — important laboratory medicine point. Many nurses and trainees see the post-DigiFab level and panic. Communicate this to the team: the assay no longer reflects active drug. Clinical improvement is the indicator. Re-dose DigiFab if no improvement; not because the level 'didn't come down.'

opts.text: Give calcium gluconate 1 g IV push to stabilize the cardiac membrane for the hyperkalemia of K 6.4
opts.rat: ⚠️ The standard hyperK answer is wrong here. In dig toxicity, avoid bolus IV calcium — historical 'stone heart' concern. Standard hyperK protocols include calcium; the dig toxicity exception is what makes this case distinctive. DigiFab + shift therapies (insulin/D50, albuterol) without bolus calcium.

opts.text: Atropine, isoproterenol, and transcutaneous pacing are highly effective for the bradyarrhythmias of severe digoxin toxicity
opts.rat: ⚠️ Overstated. Atropine has PARTIAL effect (mild improvement, often inadequate). Isoproterenol should be AVOIDED (can precipitate ventricular arrhythmias). Transcutaneous pacing is a bridge but capture may fail in severe toxicity. DigiFab is the actual treatment; the others are temporizing.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Lethal Arrhythmia (VT/VF/asystole) r/t severe digoxin toxicity with junctional escape, hyperkalemia K 6.4, hypomagnesemia, and BP 84/52 hemodynamic compromise
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The arrhythmic risk is the imminent killer. DigiFab is the antidote and should be running. Continuous cardiac monitoring; defibrillator at bedside; transcutaneous pacing pads on chest; ACLS team aware. K and Mg replaced; volume resuscitation cautiously.

opts.text: Decreased Cardiac Output r/t severe bradycardia (junctional rate 38) with hypotension and hypoperfusion signs
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Restoring rate is essential. Atropine had partial effect; DigiFab is the definitive answer. Cautious IV fluids (HFrEF EF 35% — pulmonary edema risk); transcutaneous pacing if rate doesn't improve with DigiFab. Reassess after 1 hr of DigiFab.

opts.text: Risk for Adverse Medication Event r/t Beers-list–style polypharmacy stacking (digoxin + amiodarone + furosemide without K replacement + warfarin) and missed dose adjustments
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The root cause. Until the medication regimen is corrected, this will recur. Pharmacy consult for full reconciliation; discontinue or reduce digoxin permanently; cardiology re-strategize rate control; INR monitoring frequency increased.

opts.text: Acute Kidney Injury on Chronic CKD r/t volume depletion from vomiting and reduced renal clearance of digoxin accumulating further
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Gentle isotonic rehydration; hold nephrotoxins; trend Cr; the AKI will resolve as volume restored and once vomiting stops. The AKI is BOTH a consequence of the toxicity AND a contributor to its persistence (less dig clearance) — both directions matter.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit r/t medication safety, drug interactions, signs of digoxin toxicity, and the need for proactive communication with prescribers before any new medication is added
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Discharge teaching: signs of dig toxicity (nausea, anorexia, visual halos, weakness, palpitations), the amiodarone–dig and amiodarone–warfarin interaction lesson, always ask before any new prescription, K-supplement adherence.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: DIGOXIN IMMUNE FAB (DigiFab) 2 vials IV over 30 min initial; reassess in 1 hr; re-dose to 6+ vials for refractory toxicity
opts.rat: ANTIDOTE — the core intervention. Empiric chronic-toxicity dose 2–6 vials (acute overdose uses higher empiric 10–20). Resolves arrhythmias and hyperK by binding dig out of tissues and reactivating Na/K-ATPase. Onset 20–30 min.

opts.text: HOLD digoxin, amiodarone, furosemide, warfarin; cardiology and pharmacy consultation for medication reconciliation
opts.rat: Stop the contributors. Each missed dose of dig is one less to accumulate. Amiodarone and furosemide are co-contributors. Warfarin held pending INR recheck. Pharmacy reviews the full med list for safer alternatives going forward.

opts.text: Calcium gluconate 1 g IV bolus for hyperkalemia membrane stabilization
opts.rat: ⚠️ DIG TOXICITY EXCEPTION. The standard hyperK answer is wrong here — historical 'stone heart' concern (animal data) + DigiFab will resolve the hyperK by reactivating Na/K-ATPase. If absolutely needed for documented hypocalcemia, give over hours. Avoid bolus push.

opts.text: Insulin 10 units IV + D50 25 g IV; ALBUTEROL 10 mg nebulized; standard K-shift therapies (without bolus calcium)
opts.rat: Standard shift therapies safe in dig toxicity. Useful while DigiFab takes effect. Recheck BG q15–30 min x 4 hr (hypoglycemia risk). Albuterol additive via β2-agonist Na/K-ATPase activation.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE 2 g IV over 15 min for hypomagnesemia (Mg 1.4); recheck after
opts.rat: Replacement + antiarrhythmic effect. Hypomagnesemia predisposes to dig toxicity and arrhythmias. Target Mg 2.0+; oral repletion when tolerated; address the chronic cause (furosemide without supplementation).

opts.text: Hemodialysis stat to remove the digoxin
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong. Digoxin has large Vd, lipophilic, tissue-bound — HD does NOT meaningfully remove it. DigiFab is the answer. HD only if K not responding (rare with DigiFab) or for AKI progression. Common trap.

opts.text: Lidocaine 1 mg/kg IV for ventricular arrhythmias if they develop; AVOID class IA antiarrhythmics (quinidine, procainamide) and continued amiodarone for arrhythmia management in dig toxicity
opts.rat: Antiarrhythmic of choice in dig toxicity. Class IA drugs raise dig levels. Amiodarone is part of the cause. Lidocaine 1 mg/kg bolus then 1–4 mg/min infusion is the preferred option for VT/PVCs.

opts.text: Atropine 0.5–1 mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia; transcutaneous pacing pads on chest as backup if rate fails to respond
opts.rat: Bridge therapy. Atropine has partial effect in dig toxicity. Pacing pads on as backup if hemodynamic compromise persists. Internal pacemaker has capture issues — don't rely on it. DigiFab is the actual answer.

opts.text: Isoproterenol IV infusion for the bradycardia
opts.rat: ⚠️ AVOID — can precipitate ventricular arrhythmias in dig-toxic myocardium. Use atropine (partial effect) + transcutaneous pacing + DigiFab as the actual answer.

opts.text: Vitamin K 10 mg IV push for the INR 4.8
opts.rat: ⚠️ Overcorrects + makes warfarin less effective for weeks afterward. No bleeding + INR <10 = HOLD warfarin only and recheck in 24 hr. If any bleeding develops, low-dose oral vitamin K 1–2.5 mg PO. PCC only for major bleeding.

opts.text: Cardiology consultation; pharmacy consultation for medication reconciliation focused on the amiodarone–digoxin and amiodarone–warfarin interactions; electrophysiology consultation for long-term rate-control strategy not requiring digoxin
opts.rat: Multidisciplinary correction. Pharmacy review of the full med list. Cardiology re-strategizes rate-control (carvedilol up-titration, diltiazem, or rhythm control if appropriate). Permanent D/C of dig or drastically reduced dose with monthly levels.

opts.text: Gentle IV fluids 1 L NS over 1 hr for volume depletion (vomiting + poor PO); reassess for additional fluids carefully (HFrEF EF 35% — risk of pulmonary edema)
opts.rat: Cautious volume restoration. Helps the prerenal AKI and supports BP. Reassess after; do not over-resuscitate (HFrEF). Trend lungs, JVP, weight, BNP.

opts.text: Discharge teaching with daughter: signs of dig toxicity (nausea, anorexia, visual halos, weakness, palpitations); medication interaction awareness (always ask before any new prescription); K-supplement adherence; cardiology and PCP follow-up within 1 week
opts.rat: Prevent recurrence. Daughter as informed family member helps with future episode prevention. Pharmacist-led medication review before any new prescription. Cardiology to address long-term rate-control strategy without dig.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm DIGOXIN IMMUNE FAB (DigiFab) 2 vials IV being mixed by pharmacy and prepare to administer over 30 min once delivered; meanwhile continuous cardiac monitoring, defibrillator at bedside, transcutaneous pacing pads on chest
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. DigiFab is THE antidote and will resolve the toxicity. Setup for arrhythmia management runs in parallel. Pacing pads on chest pre-emptively (don't wait for asystole to apply). ACLS team aware.

opts.text: HOLD digoxin, amiodarone, furosemide, warfarin; magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 15 min; recheck Mg and K after
opts.rat: #2 — Stop the contributors + replace Mg. Each held dose of dig is one less to accumulate. Mg replacement addresses hypomagnesemia AND has antiarrhythmic benefit. Recheck after.

opts.text: Atropine 1 mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia (partial effect expected); insulin 10 units IV + D50 25 g IV + albuterol 10 mg neb for hyperK (no bolus calcium); 1 L NS gentle bolus for volume
opts.rat: #3 — Temporizing interventions while DigiFab takes effect. Atropine for the brady (limited effect). Standard hyperK shift therapies MINUS calcium. Gentle volume for hypotension (HFrEF caution).

opts.text: After 1 hr of DigiFab: reassess clinically (HR, BP, mental status, arrhythmia trend); recheck K and Mg (the dig assay will now be unreliable — falsely high from Fab-bound dig); plan re-dose of DigiFab if no clinical improvement
opts.rat: #4 — Reassessment and re-dose decision. Clinical response is the key indicator post-DigiFab — the level assay is no longer reliable. Re-dose 2 more vials if no improvement; consult toxicology / poison control if complex.

opts.text: Cardiology + pharmacy + EP consultations; family meeting with daughter to review the medication safety lesson; discharge plan with reduced or D/C dig, alternative rate-control strategy, INR monitoring, and follow-up appointments scheduled
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained recovery. Multidisciplinary medication reconciliation. Discharge teaching emphasizing the amiodarone–dig interaction lesson. Cardiology + PCP follow-up within 1 week.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1 hr after DigiFab: HR 68 (back to sinus, paced), BP 116/72, K 5.4 (down from 6.4), Mg 2.0 after replacement, mental status improving (oriented x2, recognizes daughter), no further vomiting
opts.rat: Excellent response to DigiFab + supportive care. Continue monitoring; K and Mg trending normal; the dig assay is unreliable post-DigiFab. Plan medication reconciliation; discharge planning; cardiology + EP for alternative rate-control strategy.

opts.text: Day 2: at baseline cognitively per daughter; tolerating PO; eating regular diet; ambulating with walker; off all temporizing therapies; cardiology has decided to permanently D/C digoxin and up-titrate carvedilol for rate control; alternative AFib plan being developed
opts.rat: Successful recovery. Permanent D/C of dig is the safest call given the narrow window + CKD + recent toxicity. Carvedilol up-titration or diltiazem alternative for rate control. AFib rhythm-control strategy (cardioversion + ablation) may be considered by EP if appropriate.

opts.text: Day 4 discharge: medication list reconciled with PERMANENT D/C of digoxin, amiodarone continued for AFib rhythm control with monitoring, warfarin restarted at reduced dose with INR check in 1 week, KCl supplementation adherence reinforced, signs of dig toxicity and medication interaction teaching completed with daughter; cardiology + PCP follow-up scheduled within 1 week
opts.rat: Comprehensive discharge. Daughter is empowered to advocate ('please review every new medication with the cardiologist FIRST'). Family medication review process established. PCP and cardiology aware of the event and the simplified regimen.

opts.text: 30 min into DigiFab: develops sustained VT degenerating to VF; full ACLS; second amp DigiFab pushed empirically (10 vials); defibrillation x 2 with ROSC; transferred urgent to CCU
opts.rat: Critical event. Severe dig toxicity causes refractory arrhythmias; push full empiric DigiFab dose (10 vials) for cardiac arrest scenarios. Continue lidocaine for ventricular arrhythmia (NOT amiodarone — contributor). Post-arrest CCU care; reassess goals of care with family.

opts.text: Hour 4: another nurse, unaware of dig toxicity, gave calcium gluconate 1 g IV push for the K 6.0; patient develops sustained junctional tachycardia and brief run of VT
opts.rat: ⚠️ Iatrogenic worsening. The dig-toxicity exception to standard hyperK protocols was not communicated. Flag the chart prominently; team education; reinforce the principle. Treat the arrhythmia with lidocaine; additional DigiFab; magnesium. System change: EHR alert for dig-toxicity hyperK orders.

opts.text: Day 3: persistent AKI with Cr climbing to 2.4 despite stopping diuretics and gentle hydration; nephrology consult for possible CKD progression vs intrinsic AKI
opts.rat: Renal complication. AKI from the volume depletion + cardiogenic compromise + possible dig effects + her chronic CKD. Nephrology evaluation; renal-dose adjust all meds; monitor for indications for HD (electrolytes, volume, uremia).

opts.text: Hour 12: dig level reported as 5.8 ng/mL (HIGHER than admission) by lab; family panics thinking treatment failed; nurse explains the post-DigiFab assay is unreliable (Fab-bound dig appears in the assay) and clinical improvement is what matters
opts.rat: Predictable laboratory artifact, NOT a clinical worsening. Educate family + reinforce with team. Use clinical response (HR, BP, K, mental status, EKG) — not the dig level — to titrate DigiFab. Free-digoxin assay if center has it.

opts.text: Day 1 evening: mild residual fatigue and 'still a little confused' per patient; otherwise stable; HR sinus 78 (paced), BP 122/70, K 4.4, off temporizing therapies
opts.rat: Expected slow resolution. Dig is tissue-bound and takes 48–72 hr to fully wash out even with DigiFab. Continue monitoring; gradual cognitive recovery; family at bedside for reassurance and reorientation. Plan ongoing physical therapy mobilization.

opts.text: Persistent xanthopsia (yellow-green visual halos) for 24 hr post-treatment then resolves
opts.rat: Expected. Visual symptoms of dig toxicity (xanthopsia) take longer to resolve as dig redistributes out of retinal tissue. Reassure patient; reassess. Permanent visual changes would prompt ophthalmology evaluation but typical course is full reversal.

opts.text: Persistent CKD Stage 3b at discharge with Cr 1.5 (slightly higher than baseline 1.3 but improved from peak 1.8) — expected residual
opts.rat: Reasonable residual. The AKI on CKD improved; the chronic baseline is what it is. Reinforce hydration, avoid nephrotoxins, careful dosing of any new medication. Nephrology follow-up for ongoing CKD management.

title: Wrong-Patient Insulin Glargine Administration Sentinel Event with Severe Prolonged Hypoglycemia
subtitle: Two Identifiers + BCMA + Independent Double-Check Are the Three Failures — Plus Why D50 Alone Won't Save This Patient (Lantus Lasts 24 Hours)

patient.name: M. Ramirez (Maria, 68 F — room 412)
patient.admitDate: Yesterday, 1330 (admitted for COPD exacerbation; bronchoscopy scheduled tomorrow 0800; she has been NPO since 2400)
patient.room: Med-Surg 412, Bed A — admitted to medicine service; the INTENDED recipient of the insulin was a DIFFERENT patient (Mario Ramirez, 71 M, room 414) on the same unit

vitals.time: 1700 (time of WRONG insulin administration — patient stable on admission COPD plan)
1830 (CURRENT — 90 min after wrong dose; rapid response activated)

labs.v: Baseline this AM 142 mg/dL (on metformin only); routine 1830 finger-stick (per unit Q-shift protocol for diabetics) = 38 mg/dL — CRITICAL value reported by lab
