labs.r: Adult fasting 70–100; <70 hypoglycemia; <55 severe; <40 critical / brain injury risk. Drop from 142 to 38 in 90 min in a non-insulin-user signals exogenous insulin

labs.v: Diaphoretic, tremulous, anxious, mild confusion (oriented x2 — knew name, knew she was in hospital, didn't know date); pallor; tachycardia; reports 'I feel terrible — sweaty and shaking'; able to swallow but per NPO order has not eaten since 2400
labs.r: Classic adrenergic symptoms (sweating, tremor, tachycardia, anxiety) + neuroglycopenic symptoms (confusion). Adrenergic typically first, then neuroglycopenic at lower glucose; severe hypoglycemia can cause seizure, coma, permanent neuro deficit, death

labs.n: Reconciliation with home medications and inpatient MAR
labs.v: HOME MEDS (verified by daughter and pharmacy review): METFORMIN 1000 mg PO BID (currently held inpatient due to recent IV contrast scheduled for bronchoscopy — appropriate hold), albuterol/ipratropium nebs, fluticasone/salmeterol, prednisone taper (Day 3 of 5 for COPD exacerbation), atorvastatin. INPATIENT MAR: NO INSULIN ORDERED. The Lantus 30 units administered at 1700 was NEVER ordered for this patient
labs.r: WRONG PATIENT EVENT confirmed — no insulin order exists for Maria Ramirez (room 412). The intended recipient was Mario Ramirez (room 414, type 1 DM, on Lantus 30 units PM as home med continued inpatient)

labs.v: INSULIN GLARGINE (LANTUS) 30 UNITS SubQ administered to Maria Ramirez (room 412) by the evening shift RN at 1700 — intended for Mario Ramirez (room 414); injected to the abdomen; no immediate effect noted; nurse moved on to other patients
labs.r: Lantus is a LONG-ACTING (basal) insulin with ~24-hour duration; peakless flat profile. 30 units in a non-diabetic-on-insulin person who is NPO is a major exogenous load that will continue to lower BG for 18–24 hr — single D50 bolus is INSUFFICIENT; requires continuous dextrose infusion

labs.n: The 'Three Failures' that allowed the event
labs.v: FAILURE 1 — TWO PATIENT IDENTIFIERS: Nurse used room number only ('the Ramirez in 412 — that's the one'); did NOT verify name + DOB (or name + MRN) per The Joint Commission NPSG 01.01.01. FAILURE 2 — BCMA BYPASS: Bar-code scanner was 'acting up' on this medication; nurse manually charted administration instead of scanning. FAILURE 3 — INDEPENDENT DOUBLE-CHECK: insulin is a HIGH-ALERT MEDICATION per ISMP; institutional policy requires two-RN independent verification of patient + drug + dose + route + time before administration; this was not performed
labs.r: Sentinel event analysis frame — multiple barriers failed in sequence (Swiss cheese model). Each barrier is designed to catch errors that get past the others; when all align, the error reaches the patient. No single individual is fully at fault — the SYSTEM failed

labs.v: TWO patients with the same last name on the same unit: MARIA Ramirez (68 F, room 412) and MARIO Ramirez (71 M, room 414); both Hispanic; both diabetic (Maria type 2 on metformin only, Mario type 1 on Lantus + aspart sliding scale); the EMR has a 'Patient Alert: Similar Name on Unit' banner — was the nurse aware?
labs.r: Name confusion is a documented contributor to wrong-patient events. ISMP recommendation: separate similar-name patients to different units when possible; if not, EMR alerts + bedside name alerts + heightened verification protocol

labs.v: Day shift RN said in handoff: 'Ramirez in 414 gets his Lantus 30 units at 1700 — he's the type 1 diabetic.' Evening RN (10 yr experience, picked up the unit at shift change with 6 patients) understood 'Ramirez' but did not clarify which Ramirez at the bedside; did not check the MAR carefully (assumed she remembered correctly); went into 412 directly thinking 'that's where Ramirez is'
labs.r: Cognitive shortcut error: 'Ramirez' as a single-identifier shortcut, failure to use both identifiers at the bedside. SBAR handoff structure with explicit room AND patient details prevents some of this; recipient verification ('you said 414 — is that Mario or someone else?') would have caught

labs.n: The nurse who made the error
labs.v: 10-year experienced RN, no prior medication errors documented, working a regular 12-hour shift (not extended), patient load of 6 (within facility ratio for med-surg), no overt fatigue or distraction reported; recognized her own error at 1900 when reviewing the chart and self-reported to charge RN — immediate self-disclosure
labs.r: JUST CULTURE FRAMEWORK: distinguishes (1) human error (slip/lapse in attention — coachable, system fix), (2) at-risk behavior (taking a shortcut, like BCMA bypass — coachable + system improvement), (3) reckless behavior (conscious disregard of safety, e.g., never checks IDs intentionally — disciplinary). Most errors fall in categories 1 and 2; punishment for these damages future reporting and worsens safety culture

labs.v: WBC 11.2, Hgb 11.4, Plt 244; Na 138, K 4.2, Cl 100, HCO3 26, BUN 22, Cr 0.9; ABG (drawn for COPD baseline AM) pH 7.36, pCO2 48 (her chronic baseline), HCO3 28
labs.r: Otherwise stable — confirms hypoglycemia is the acute issue; not other electrolyte/acid-base or organ dysfunction. K trend will be important — insulin shifts K intracellularly; recheck after dextrose infusion

labs.v: Husband at bedside (notified of admission, present for last 4 hr); daughter en route (40 min away). Family unaware of the medication error at the moment the rapid response activated; transparent disclosure required per institutional policy and patient-safety best practice (CANDOR model — Communication AND Optimal Resolution)
labs.r: Honest, prompt disclosure improves outcomes for patient/family trust, reduces litigation risk, supports staff well-being. The hospital risk management and patient safety officer should be paged; disclosure conversation typically led by attending physician with charge RN present; document carefully

labs.v: Type 1 DM, on Lantus 30 units PM nightly + aspart sliding scale per CHO; baseline BG well-controlled; he was due his Lantus at 1700; received it 90 min late after the error was identified and a fresh dose drawn; he is fine; his concern is for the other Ramirez
labs.r: Important to also document the originally-intended dose status — the right patient eventually received the right dose. The error here is the wrong recipient

nursesNote: 1830 — Rapid response activated for hypoglycemia BG 38 in Maria Ramirez, 68 y/o female, room 412, admitted yesterday for COPD exacerbation, NPO since 2400 for bronchoscopy tomorrow AM. Patient on metformin 1000 mg PO BID at home (HELD inpatient pending bronchoscopy — appropriate); inpatient MAR has NO INSULIN ORDERED for her. Routine q-shift finger-stick at 1830 returned 38 mg/dL — patient symptomatic with diaphoresis, tremor, mild confusion (oriented x2), tachycardia 118, BP 104/64. On rapid response evaluation and chart review, identified that at 1700 the evening shift RN administered INSULIN GLARGINE (LANTUS) 30 UNITS SubQ to this patient — a dose NEVER ordered for her. The intended recipient was the other Ramirez on the unit — MARIO Ramirez, 71 M, type 1 DM, room 414, who has Lantus 30 units PM as a chronic order. WRONG-PATIENT MEDICATION ADMINISTRATION CONFIRMED. The 'three failures' that allowed the event: (1) TWO PATIENT IDENTIFIERS not verified (nurse used room number alone — 'the Ramirez in 412'); per The Joint Commission NPSG 01.01.01 must use TWO identifiers (name + DOB or name + MRN) before any medication; (2) BCMA (bar-code med admin) BYPASS — scanner was 'acting up,' nurse manually charted; BCMA is the system check designed to catch wrong-patient errors; (3) INDEPENDENT DOUBLE-CHECK not performed — insulin is on the ISMP HIGH-ALERT MEDICATION list and institutional policy requires two-RN independent verification of patient + drug + dose + route + time before administration. The administering RN self-recognized the error at 1900 reviewing the chart and self-disclosed to charge RN — immediate self-reporting (a positive culture indicator). At 1900 initial response: D50 25 g IV bolus given via newly-placed PIV; BG repeat at 1910 was 84; D10W continuous infusion at 100 mL/hr STARTED at 1915 because LANTUS HAS ~24-HOUR DURATION and a single D50 bolus will be transient — without ongoing dextrose, BG will fall again within minutes-to-hours; checking BG q15 min initially then q30 min as stable; continue dextrose infusion for AT LEAST 18–24 hours and likely longer until full Lantus duration past; will admit to step-down or ICU for closer monitoring overnight. K rechecked given insulin causes intracellular shift — K 3.6 (mild drop from 4.2 baseline); replace with KCl 20 mEq PO when alert + tolerating PO; if oral not possible, IV KCl 10 mEq IV over 1 hr. Husband at bedside; daughter en route; ATTENDING NOTIFIED, hospital safety officer paged, charge RN at bedside, error disclosure conversation planned with attending and patient/family when patient mentally clear and family present. Incident report (occurrence report) being completed; Root Cause Analysis (RCA) will be scheduled within 48–72 hr per Joint Commission sentinel-event protocol (wrong-patient medication = automatic RCA trigger); JUST-CULTURE framework applied — the administering RN reports human error (slip in attention) + at-risk behavior (BCMA bypass); coachable + system improvement, NOT disciplinary by default. The administering RN is being supported emotionally (second victim concept — clinicians involved in errors suffer significant moral distress; peer support and EAP referral offered; brief debriefing). Bronchoscopy CANCELED for tomorrow — patient cannot be NPO + on dextrose; reschedule once safe. Patient education will be done with both patient and family later about what happened, what was done, and the system changes being made. Will track Lantus duration (it was given at 1700 today — expected effective duration through ~1700 tomorrow at minimum, may extend); plan extended monitoring 30+ hr from administration time to ensure no rebound hypoglycemia after dextrose infusion is stopped.
providerOrders: STAT D50 (50%) 25 g IV via newly placed PIV; recheck BG at 15 min after bolus

D10W (10% Dextrose in Water) continuous IV infusion at 75–100 mL/hr started AFTER initial D50 — DO NOT stop after D50 alone (Lantus duration ~24 hr; single bolus is transient); continue minimum 18–24 hr, monitor BG q15–30 min initially, q1h once stable target >100 mg/dL; titrate up to D20W if needed for refractory hypoglycemia (requires central line — peripheral max D12.5W due to vein sclerosis risk)

If IV access lost or unavailable: GLUCAGON 1 mg IM/SubQ (intranasal Baqsimi alternative); will raise BG transiently 60–90 min; less effective in malnourished/alcoholic patients (depleted glycogen); not a long-term answer for Lantus toxicity — re-establish IV access ASAP

Capillary BG (finger-stick) q15 min until BG >100 stable; then q30 min x 2 hr; then q1h x 4 hr; then q2h while dextrose infusion ongoing; q4h for 6 hr after dextrose stopped to verify no rebound hypoglycemia

AVOID 'just give her some orange juice' — this is SEVERE hypoglycemia from 30 units of LONG-ACTING insulin; oral simple sugars are appropriate for mild hypoglycemia in an alert patient who can swallow, but not adequate for this magnitude/duration
Reassess K+ q4h — insulin causes intracellular K shift; replace with KCl 20 mEq PO or 10 mEq IV over 1 hr if K <3.5
Continuous cardiac monitoring; pulse oximetry; vital signs q15 min until stable then q1h x 12 hr
RAPID RESPONSE TEAM at bedside; ICU consultation for possible transfer if BG refractory, mental status worsening, or seizure activity develops

AVOID giving any additional insulin under any circumstances during this hospitalization unless specifically ordered by endocrinology after the Lantus duration passes — flag chart prominently with 'INSULIN HOLD — RECENT EVENT' alert

Strict NPO ORDER CANCELED — patient may eat complex carbohydrate + protein meal once alert, oriented, and able to swallow safely (continued dextrose covers the insulin until the eating starts; oral intake then supplements)
BRONCHOSCOPY CANCELED for tomorrow (cannot be NPO + dextrose infusion); reschedule with proceduralist once Lantus duration past and patient stable
Continue COPD regimen as ordered (albuterol/ipratropium nebs, fluticasone/salmeterol, prednisone taper); do not hold COPD meds for this event
NOTIFY: Attending physician (admitting hospitalist); patient safety officer; risk management; nursing supervisor; pharmacy supervisor; institutional sentinel-event protocol activated within 24 hr

ERROR DISCLOSURE conversation (CANDOR model) with patient + husband + daughter once patient mentally clear and family present: attending leads, charge RN attends, document carefully; honest, prompt, compassionate; explain what happened, what was done, what is being monitored, what is being done to prevent recurrence; offer answers to questions; do NOT speculate on cause beyond known facts; do NOT minimize

INCIDENT REPORT (occurrence report) filed by the administering RN within shift; ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS (RCA) scheduled within 48–72 hours per Joint Commission sentinel-event protocol; multi-disciplinary RCA team (charge RN, ICU/floor RN, pharmacy, IT for BCMA, risk management, patient safety officer); SECOND-VICTIM peer support and EAP referral for the administering RN — emotional support is part of culture of safety

JUST-CULTURE assessment of the administering RN's behavior: (1) Human error (slip in attention) — coachable + system fix; (2) At-risk behavior (BCMA bypass — coachable + system improvement); (3) NOT reckless (no conscious disregard of safety); response is coaching and system improvement, NOT punitive (unless RCA reveals reckless behavior — does not appear to in this case)

SYSTEM CHANGES to be considered by patient safety committee and RCA team: (1) EMR ALERT for similar-name patients on same unit (already exists — verify it's enabled), (2) ROOM ASSIGNMENT POLICY to separate similar-name patients when feasible, (3) BCMA DOWNTIME PROCEDURE clear — if scanner not working, requires charge RN involvement + two-RN verification + chart double-check, (4) INSULIN HIGH-ALERT POLICY enforcement (two-RN independent double-check), (5) Standardized handoff template (SBAR or I-PASS) with explicit room + patient + drug + dose details, (6) Recurring staff education + medication safety drills

After patient stabilizes and Lantus duration past (typically 30+ hr from administration), arrange follow-up: outpatient endocrinology if any concern about insulin sensitivity; patient education on safety advocacy ('ask every staff member what medication they are giving you and what it's for'); continue home metformin per outpatient regimen once COPD resolves

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Blood glucose 38 mg/dL with symptoms (diaphoresis, tremor, confusion, tachycardia) — severe hypoglycemia in a patient on METFORMIN ONLY who has NEVER been ordered insulin
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — immediate physiologic emergency. Severe hypoglycemia from exogenous insulin in a non-insulin-user signals medication error. Treat first (D50 IV bolus + D10 infusion), investigate cause concurrently. BG <40 carries seizure, coma, brain injury, death risk if prolonged.

opts.text: 30 units of LANTUS (insulin glargine) was administered at 1700 — a LONG-ACTING basal insulin with ~24-hour duration; single D50 bolus is INSUFFICIENT — needs continuous dextrose infusion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — drives the treatment plan. Lantus is peakless, lasts 24 hr; treating only with a D50 push will produce transient BG rise then drop again. CONTINUOUS DEXTROSE (D10W at 75–100 mL/hr) for at least 18–24 hr is essential. Monitor q15 min initially, extend over 30+ hr from time of administration.

opts.text: Three system safety failures identified: (1) Two patient identifiers not verified, (2) BCMA scanner bypassed, (3) High-alert med two-RN independent double-check not performed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — root cause framework. The Joint Commission NPSG, ISMP high-alert med policy, and BCMA system are designed as overlapping barriers; when all three fail, errors reach patients (Swiss cheese model). RCA will examine each barrier and what allowed the bypass.

opts.text: Two patients with same last name on unit: Maria Ramirez (room 412 — WRONG recipient) and Mario Ramirez (room 414 — INTENDED recipient); EMR has similar-name alert
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sound-alike/look-alike contributor. Similar-name pairings are a documented contributor to wrong-patient events. ISMP recommends separating to different units when feasible; if not, EMR alerts + heightened verification protocol + handoff communication clarity.

opts.text: Patient was NPO since midnight for bronchoscopy tomorrow — making the exogenous insulin even more dangerous (no carbohydrate substrate to balance)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — NPO + 30 units Lantus + no insulin order = profound prolonged hypoglycemia. Bronchoscopy should be CANCELED for tomorrow — patient cannot remain NPO + on dextrose infusion. Reschedule after Lantus duration past and patient stable.

opts.text: The administering RN self-recognized the error at 1900 and self-disclosed to charge RN — immediate self-reporting (a positive culture-of-safety indicator)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — informs Just-Culture response. Self-reporting is a hallmark of healthy safety culture; punitive response would damage future reporting and worsen safety overall. The error pattern (slip + at-risk behavior) is coachable + system fix, not disciplinary.

opts.text: Family at bedside (husband present; daughter en route); error disclosure conversation needed once patient mentally clear
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — error disclosure obligation. CANDOR model (Communication AND Optimal Resolution): honest, prompt, compassionate disclosure improves outcomes for patient/family AND reduces litigation risk. Attending leads with charge RN; risk management involved; document carefully.

opts.text: Sentinel event criteria triggered (wrong-patient medication is automatic) — RCA within 48–72 hr; institutional safety committee involvement
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — regulatory and quality framework. Joint Commission defines sentinel events (unexpected occurrences involving death or serious physical/psychological injury OR the risk thereof); wrong-patient medication is among the auto-trigger categories. RCA is mandatory and time-bound.

opts.text: Administering RN is at risk of 'second victim' moral distress — needs peer support, brief debriefing, EAP referral; emotional support is part of culture of safety
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — staff well-being. Clinicians involved in errors suffer significant moral distress (the 'second victim'); without support, risk for depression, burnout, leaving the profession. Charge RN/nurse manager should provide immediate emotional support; institutional EAP referral; peer support program if available.

opts.text: K+ 4.2 baseline — recheck because insulin causes intracellular K shift; mild hypokalemia is expected
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — predictable secondary effect. Insulin drives K into cells; expect K to drop by 0.5–1.0 mEq/L. Recheck q4h; replace KCl PO 20 mEq when alert and tolerating PO; IV KCl 10 mEq over 1 hr if PO not possible. Avoid critical hypokalemia.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no drug allergies. Not relevant to this event. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 98.0°F
opts.rat: Reassuring — rules out infection / fever as confounding factor. Not relevant to the medication safety event.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What systems failed, and what is the physiology?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying safety principles / pharmacology.

opts.text: TWO PATIENT IDENTIFIERS rule (The Joint Commission NPSG 01.01.01): before any medication, procedure, transfusion, or blood draw, verify TWO identifiers (e.g., NAME + DATE OF BIRTH, or NAME + MEDICAL RECORD NUMBER); ROOM NUMBER IS NOT AN IDENTIFIER (patients move; rooms swap)
opts.rat: CORRECT — foundational safety rule. The most common violation is 'I know my patient' shortcut. Bedside verification with the patient (when able) or with the wristband band when unable. Patient name + DOB is the standard pairing. Room number, bed number, diagnosis are NOT acceptable identifiers.

opts.text: BCMA (BAR-CODE MEDICATION ADMINISTRATION) is the system check designed to catch wrong-patient, wrong-drug, wrong-dose, wrong-time, wrong-route errors at the bedside; bypassing BCMA (manual charting when scanner 'acts up') removes the safety net and requires escalation to charge RN + alternative verification
opts.rat: CORRECT — BCMA reduces medication errors by 40–60% when used. Workarounds (taping scan codes to nurse uniforms, manual override) defeat the purpose. When BCMA fails (scanner broken, code unreadable), institutional policy should require: charge RN involvement, two-RN verification, IT consult to fix the underlying issue.

opts.text: INSULIN is on the ISMP HIGH-ALERT MEDICATION list — drugs that bear heightened risk of significant patient harm when used in error; institutional policy typically requires TWO-RN INDEPENDENT DOUBLE-CHECK of patient + drug + dose + route + time for high-alert meds
opts.rat: CORRECT — ISMP high-alert categories include: anticoagulants, opioids, insulin, neuromuscular blockers, chemotherapy, IV concentrated electrolytes (KCl), thrombolytics, IV sedatives. Two-RN independent check means each RN independently verifies — not 'I'll just take a look at what you have' (collaborative check less effective).

opts.text: Insulin GLARGINE (Lantus) is a LONG-ACTING basal insulin with ~24-hour PEAKLESS duration; SINGLE D50 BOLUS will produce transient BG rise then drop again in minutes-to-hours; CONTINUOUS DEXTROSE (D10W) at 75–100 mL/hr for at least 18–24 hr is required
opts.rat: CORRECT — pharmacokinetic-driven treatment. Treating Lantus toxicity with a single D50 bolus is undertreatment. Monitor q15 min, extend over 30+ hr from time of administration, watch for rebound hypoglycemia AFTER dextrose stopped. ICU monitoring may be required for refractory cases.

opts.text: INSULIN ASPART (NovoLog), LISPRO (Humalog), and GLULISINE (Apidra) are RAPID-acting (onset 15 min, peak 1–2 hr, duration 3–5 hr); REGULAR INSULIN is SHORT-acting (onset 30 min, peak 2–4 hr, duration 6–8 hr); NPH is intermediate (peak 4–10 hr, duration 12–18 hr); GLARGINE / DETEMIR / DEGLUDEC are LONG-acting (24+ hr, peakless)
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield insulin pharmacology. Duration drives the hypoglycemia recovery plan: rapid/short-acting may need only brief support; long-acting needs prolonged monitoring + continuous dextrose. Names matter — 'Lantus' the long-acting is different from 'Lispro' the rapid-acting; sound-alike LISA pairing is itself an error contributor.

opts.text: JUST CULTURE framework distinguishes: (1) HUMAN ERROR (slip or lapse — unintended action; coachable + system fix); (2) AT-RISK BEHAVIOR (taking a shortcut without recognizing the risk — coachable + system improvement); (3) RECKLESS BEHAVIOR (conscious disregard of substantial risk — disciplinary). The administering RN's error is human error + at-risk behavior, NOT reckless
opts.rat: CORRECT — patient safety quality concept. Punishing the first two types damages future reporting and erodes safety culture. Reckless behavior (e.g., consistently never checking IDs as a habit, ignoring known warnings) deserves accountability. The third type is rare. Most events fall in the first two.

opts.text: ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS (RCA) is a multi-disciplinary structured review of a sentinel event to identify system contributors (not individuals to blame); within 48–72 hr typically; the goal is system improvements that prevent recurrence
opts.rat: CORRECT — quality improvement methodology. Five-Whys, fishbone diagrams, and process flow mapping are common RCA tools. Outputs: action items with assigned owners and deadlines (e.g., 'IT to verify EMR similar-name alert is enabled on all units by [date]'). RCA findings are confidential under peer-review protection in most jurisdictions.

opts.text: ERROR DISCLOSURE to patient/family (CANDOR model — Communication AND Optimal Resolution): be HONEST, PROMPT, COMPASSIONATE; explain what happened, what was done, what is being monitored, what is being done to prevent recurrence; do NOT speculate on cause; do NOT minimize; document carefully
opts.rat: CORRECT — ethical obligation + risk management evidence-based practice. Honest disclosure improves patient/family trust AND reduces litigation (counterintuitively to old 'deny and defend' approach). Attending physician typically leads; risk management involved; chaplain or social work for family support.

opts.text: The administering RN should be fired immediately for this serious error
opts.rat: ⚠️ Anti-just-culture. Knee-jerk discipline for human error + at-risk behavior is destructive to safety culture. Self-reporting is a positive sign; punishment for human errors damages future reporting; system fixes prevent recurrence better than firing individuals. Discipline reserved for reckless behavior — does not appear here.

opts.text: A single glass of orange juice (15 g carb) is the appropriate treatment for this hypoglycemia in an alert patient who can swallow
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong for the magnitude AND duration. Oral 15g rule is for MILD hypoglycemia (e.g., outpatient diabetic feeling shaky). This is SEVERE hypoglycemia from 30 units of LONG-ACTING insulin lasting 24 hr. Requires IV D50 + continuous D10W. Oral carbs supplement once safe but cannot manage alone.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Severe Hypoglycemia / Risk for Neurologic Injury r/t exogenous 30 units of Lantus in a non-insulin-user who is NPO — BG 38 with symptoms, ongoing 24-hour insulin effect
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The hypoglycemia is the imminent harm. D50 bolus + continuous D10W; monitor q15 min; continue 30+ hr. Brain injury risk increases with duration of severe hypoglycemia. ICU consultation if BG refractory or mental status worsens.

opts.text: Risk for Prolonged / Rebound Hypoglycemia r/t Lantus 24-hour duration extending beyond initial D50 bolus effect
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Pharmacokinetic predictability. CONTINUOUS dextrose infusion for at least 18–24 hr; do not stop after BG normalizes briefly; recheck q15 min initially, extend monitoring 6 hr beyond dextrose-stop. Plan for 30+ hr of total monitoring from time of administration.

opts.text: Risk for Continued or Repeat Medication Error r/t identified system failures (BCMA bypass culture, similar-name patients on unit, high-alert med policy non-adherence)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The system failures must be addressed or the next error is coming. RCA + system improvements + staff education + EMR similar-name alerts + BCMA downtime policy clarity + insulin two-RN check enforcement. Charge RN and patient safety officer in real-time involvement.

opts.text: Family Distress / Need for Error Disclosure r/t the medication error and its consequences
opts.rat: #4 — Important. CANDOR-model disclosure once patient mentally clear and family available. Attending leads; charge RN attends; document. Honest disclosure improves outcomes; do not delay or minimize. Provide emotional support; chaplain or social work.

opts.text: Staff Moral Distress / Second-Victim Syndrome r/t the administering RN's involvement in the error
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Peer support, brief debriefing, EAP referral. The administering RN needs support to remain functional in patient care; punitive response without support increases risk of permanent disengagement. Just-culture framework applied.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: D50 (50%) 25 g IV via newly placed PIV STAT; recheck BG at 15 min
opts.rat: Immediate reversal of severe hypoglycemia. Will produce BG rise within 5–10 min. Insufficient ALONE for Lantus toxicity — continuous dextrose follows.

opts.text: D10W continuous IV infusion at 75–100 mL/hr started AFTER initial D50; continue minimum 18–24 hr; monitor BG q15–30 min initially, q1h once stable
opts.rat: Core treatment for long-acting insulin overdose. Lantus duration drives the requirement. Titrate up to D20W (central line needed for >D12.5W) if refractory. Continue until Lantus duration past and BG stable >100 for 4+ hr post-dextrose-stop.

opts.text: Single glass of orange juice (15 g carb) — patient is alert and can swallow
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong magnitude. 15g rule applies to MILD hypoglycemia from physiologic causes. This is SEVERE hypoglycemia from 30 units of LONG-ACTING insulin. IV D50 + continuous D10W is required. Oral carb is a supplement at most, not the answer.

opts.text: Glucagon 1 mg IM if IV access cannot be obtained immediately
opts.rat: Backup if IV access fails. Transient effect 60–90 min (mobilizes hepatic glycogen). Re-establish IV access ASAP. Less effective in malnourished or alcoholic patients (depleted glycogen). Intranasal Baqsimi alternative.

opts.text: Capillary BG (finger-stick) q15 min until BG >100 stable, then q30 min x 2 hr, then q1h x 4 hr, then q2h while dextrose ongoing, then q4h for 6 hr after dextrose stopped
opts.rat: Vigilant monitoring for the duration of insulin effect plus post-dextrose-stop window. Catches rebound hypoglycemia before symptoms. Document trend in chart.

opts.text: Give another dose of insulin to 'correct any hyperglycemia from the D50 bolus'
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. The patient has 30 units of Lantus on board. Any additional insulin = exponentially worse hypoglycemia. Chart flag: 'INSULIN HOLD — RECENT EVENT.' Mild transient hyperglycemia from D50 is expected and acceptable.

opts.text: Recheck K+ q4h; replace KCl 20 mEq PO when alert and tolerating PO, or IV KCl 10 mEq over 1 hr if PO not possible
opts.rat: Insulin shifts K intracellularly; predictable hypokalemia. Replace to maintain K 3.5–5.0. Important for cardiac function and arrhythmia prevention.

opts.text: Cancel tomorrow's bronchoscopy and reschedule once Lantus duration past and patient stable
opts.rat: Cannot be NPO + on dextrose infusion. Reschedule with proceduralist once Lantus duration past (typically 30+ hr from administration). Notify procedural team and the patient/family.

opts.text: Activate rapid response team; notify attending physician, patient safety officer, risk management, nursing supervisor, pharmacy supervisor; initiate institutional sentinel-event protocol
opts.rat: Wrong-patient medication is an auto-trigger sentinel event. Multiple disciplines need to be in the loop simultaneously. Incident report filed by administering RN; RCA scheduled within 48–72 hr.

opts.text: Discipline the administering RN with immediate termination given the serious nature of the error
opts.rat: ⚠️ Anti-just-culture. Self-reported human error + at-risk behavior (BCMA bypass) is coachable + system fix, not reckless. Punishing self-disclosure destroys safety culture. RCA + system improvements + coaching are the correct response. Reckless behavior would warrant discipline — does not appear here.

opts.text: Error disclosure (CANDOR model) to patient + husband + daughter once patient mentally clear and family present; attending physician leads, charge RN attends, document carefully
opts.rat: Ethical and best-practice obligation. Honest, prompt, compassionate. Explain what happened, what was done, what is being monitored, what is being done to prevent recurrence. Do not minimize; do not speculate on cause beyond facts.

opts.text: Peer support, brief debriefing, and EAP referral for the administering RN (second-victim concept)
opts.rat: Staff well-being is part of culture of safety. Without support, second-victim distress can lead to depression, burnout, leaving the profession. Many institutions have formal peer-support programs. Charge RN/manager provides immediate support; EAP for longer-term.

opts.text: Flag the chart with 'INSULIN HOLD — RECENT EVENT' alert; ensure all subsequent shifts and providers see the alert prominently
opts.rat: Prevents 'second hit' error. EMR banner alert; verbal communication at shift change; physical sign at bedside if institutionally appropriate. Endocrinology consult before any future insulin in this hospitalization.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Activate RAPID RESPONSE TEAM; D50 25 g IV bolus STAT; place 2nd PIV if needed; recheck BG at 15 min; ensure airway/breathing/circulation continuously monitored
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The hypoglycemia is the imminent harm. D50 bolus produces fastest BG rise. Rapid response team brings additional clinical support and decision-making. ABCs continuously.

opts.text: Start D10W continuous IV infusion at 75–100 mL/hr; flag chart with 'INSULIN HOLD'; CANCEL bronchoscopy for tomorrow; reassess K and replace as needed
opts.rat: #2 — Critical pharmacology-driven plan. Continuous dextrose for Lantus duration. Chart alert prevents second insulin error. Bronchoscopy cancel allows dextrose infusion continuation. K monitoring for insulin-shift effect.

opts.text: Notify attending physician, charge RN, patient safety officer, risk management, nursing supervisor, pharmacy supervisor; file incident report; activate institutional sentinel-event protocol
opts.rat: #3 — System escalation in parallel. Multiple disciplines need to be in the loop simultaneously. The administering RN files the incident report within shift. Patient safety officer and risk management coordinate the institutional response.

opts.text: ERROR DISCLOSURE conversation (CANDOR) with patient + family once patient mentally clear and family present; attending leads, charge RN attends; document carefully; honest, prompt, compassionate; offer answers to questions
opts.rat: #4 — Ethical obligation + best-practice. Once patient is mentally clear (post-hypoglycemia recovery), and family is present (daughter en route — wait if appropriate). Do not delay beyond the period needed for medical stabilization. Document carefully.

opts.text: Support the administering RN with peer support and brief debriefing; offer EAP referral; apply just-culture framework; schedule RCA within 48–72 hr; coordinate system improvements (EMR alerts, BCMA downtime policy, similar-name patient policy, high-alert med two-RN check enforcement)
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained safety culture. Staff support prevents second-victim attrition. RCA outputs concrete system improvements that prevent recurrence. The system is what fails (Swiss cheese model) and the system is what gets fixed.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1 hr post-D50 + D10W running: BG 124 mg/dL, symptoms resolved (no diaphoresis, no tremor, oriented x3); K 3.8 after KCl replacement; vital signs stabilized
opts.rat: Successful initial response. Continue D10W; monitor BG q30 min next 2 hr, then q1h. Do NOT discontinue dextrose — Lantus still active. Plan extended monitoring 30+ hr from time of administration; rebound hypoglycemia possible after dextrose stops.

opts.text: 24 hr later: BG stable 110–140 on D10W; reducing to D5W at 75 mL/hr; mental status fully recovered; tolerating clear liquids and gradually advancing to soft diet; bronchoscopy rescheduled in 4 days
opts.rat: Lantus duration mostly past. Step-down on dextrose support. Maintain BG monitoring q1–2h, then q4h, with extended observation 6 hr after dextrose stopped to verify no rebound. Resume normal diet as tolerated.

opts.text: Discharge day 3: at baseline; no residual neurologic deficit; CANDOR conversation completed with patient + family; institutional letter of acknowledgment + apology delivered; RCA completed with 6 system improvement action items assigned (including EMR similar-name alert enabled hospital-wide, BCMA downtime policy revised, high-alert med two-RN check audited monthly)
opts.rat: Comprehensive recovery + institutional learning. The most important measure: ZERO neurologic deficit at discharge. Patient and family supported; institutional response transparent. System improvements documented and tracked. Just-culture maintained for involved RN.

opts.text: 2 hr post-D50 only (D10W not started): BG dropped back to 52 with returning symptoms; brief seizure activity; full resuscitation including 2nd D50 bolus and STAT D10W infusion + ICU transfer
opts.rat: Predictable and preventable. Single D50 bolus without continuous dextrose for Lantus toxicity = transient effect = rebound hypoglycemia. The error: stopping at the D50 bolus alone. ICU transfer with continuous dextrose; ABCDE assessment; seizure precautions; neurology consult for the brief seizure.

opts.text: Hour 6: another nurse (not aware of the chart alert) administered insulin lispro 10 units SubQ on a sliding scale that was 'mistakenly entered for room 412 by the night provider'; BG dropped to 28; full code initiated
opts.rat: ⚠️ Catastrophic second-hit error. Chart alert insufficient; system failure compounded. The 'insulin hold' must be enforced through multiple channels (EMR alert, pharmacy verification, two-RN check), not just a chart banner. Full code; full ACLS; ICU; extended monitoring; RCA expanded.

opts.text: After CANDOR conversation, family responds with appreciation for the transparency and asks specific questions about prevention; risk management documents conversation; patient and family satisfied with response and disclosure
opts.rat: Successful disclosure outcome. Transparency builds trust and is associated with reduced litigation. Document the conversation carefully; offer ongoing communication; provide written summary of what happened and what is being done. Patient/family advocate continued accessibility.

opts.text: RCA reveals: EMR similar-name alert was DISABLED on this unit due to 'alarm fatigue' complaint 6 months ago; BCMA scanner issues had been reported 3 times in the past month without resolution; high-alert med two-RN check is audited only quarterly and last audit showed 64% compliance
opts.rat: Multiple latent system failures identified. Action items: (1) Re-enable EMR alerts hospital-wide with alarm-fatigue redesign instead of disabling, (2) IT to fix BCMA scanners and track repair time, (3) High-alert med two-RN check audited monthly with feedback. The administering RN's error happened in a system that allowed it; system fix is the answer.

opts.text: Administering RN attends peer support, debriefing, and EAP; returns to work after 2 days with full unit support; engaged in unit safety committee; presents her experience at the institutional safety conference 3 months later as a 'lessons learned' case
opts.rat: Successful second-victim recovery. Just-culture preserved. The RN converts a difficult experience into institutional learning. Other staff see that self-reporting + system response + support is the model — encourages future reporting. Culture of safety strengthens.

opts.text: Persistent mild anxiety in patient about being in hospital ('what if it happens again?'); patient remains alert and engaged but verbalizes the worry; nursing addresses with reassurance, education on the system improvements, and offering chaplaincy/social work support
opts.rat: Expected psychological impact. The medical recovery is complete but trust takes time. Trauma-informed care: validate feelings, do not dismiss, explain what's being done. Ongoing communication; patient navigator or social work; PTSD screening if symptoms persist or escalate.

opts.text: K+ remains mildly low at 3.4 despite 20 mEq KCl PO; requires additional 10 mEq IV; resolves
opts.rat: Expected — insulin's K-shifting effect can persist throughout the dextrose support window. Replace; recheck. Not unexpected; not dangerous if monitored and replaced; trend back to normal as insulin effect wanes.

title: Kawasaki Disease with Coronary Artery Dilation in a 4-Year-Old
subtitle: Fever ≥5 Days + 4 of 5 Classic Criteria — IVIG within 10 Days, High-Dose Aspirin (the Pediatric Exception), and Why You Echo Now and at 6–8 Weeks

patient.name: K. Mendoza (Kai, 4 y/o)
patient.admitDate: Today, 1000 (admitted from PCP office for persistent fever x 7 days unresponsive to antipyretics + new rash + red eyes; pediatric hospitalist accepting)
patient.room: Pediatric Unit 8, Bed B (parent rooming-in)

vitals.time: Day 1 of illness (per parents, fever began at home)
Day 7 of illness — PCP office this morning
1200 (CURRENT — on admit unit; after acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO at 1030)

labs.n: FEVER duration and pattern (the CARDINAL criterion)
labs.v: Day 7 of high fever (38.5–40.0°C), unresponsive to alternating acetaminophen and ibuprofen, no clear localizing source despite full workup; no rigors, no diaphoretic crises
labs.r: FEVER ≥5 days is REQUIRED for classic Kawasaki Disease (KD) diagnosis; one of the most prolonged unexplained fevers in pediatric practice. By Day 5+ with other criteria, treatment is initiated to prevent coronary complications. AAP criteria

labs.n: CLASSIC criteria met (≥4 of 5 plus the fever)
labs.v: 1) Bilateral NON-purulent CONJUNCTIVITIS (bulbar; perilimbal sparing); 2) ORAL changes — strawberry tongue + cracked red lips + diffuse oral/pharyngeal injection; 3) Polymorphous RASH — truncal erythematous maculopapular spreading to extremities (not vesicular); 4) Extremity changes — REDDENED + EDEMATOUS hands and feet earlier in week; now PERIUNGUAL DESQUAMATION of fingertips beginning today (late finding, Day 7+); 5) Cervical LYMPHADENOPATHY — unilateral L cervical 2 cm tender node (often the most variable criterion)
labs.r: CLASSIC KD diagnosis: fever ≥5 days + 4 of 5 criteria (or fever + 3 criteria + coronary findings on echo). 'CRASH and BURN' mnemonic: Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue, Hands/feet — BURN = fever

labs.v: Mildly DILATED LEFT MAIN CORONARY ARTERY z-score +2.4 (normal <2.0); right coronary z-score +1.8 (borderline); no aneurysm yet; normal LV systolic function EF 62%; no pericardial effusion; no valvar regurgitation
labs.r: Coronary z-score: <2.0 normal; 2.0–2.5 dilated (borderline); 2.5–5.0 small aneurysm; 5.0–10.0 medium aneurysm; >10.0 giant (highest thrombosis risk). Treatment lowers progression risk. Echo repeated at 2 weeks and 6–8 weeks; longer surveillance if abnormalities persist

labs.v: CRP 18.4 mg/dL (highly elevated); ESR 88 mm/hr (highly elevated); WBC 18.6 with neutrophilic predominance (78% segs), platelets 614 K/µL (elevated — characteristic of Week 2 of KD); ferritin 480; albumin 2.8 (low, also characteristic)
labs.r: KD lab pattern: WBC elevated with left shift; PLATELETS often LOW or normal early, then RISE in Week 2 (often to 600K–1M+); CRP and ESR markedly elevated; albumin low; ALT mildly elevated common; sterile pyuria common (rules in inflammation, rules out UTI as source)

labs.v: AST 64 (mildly elevated), ALT 78 (mildly elevated), total bilirubin 1.2, GGT 88, sodium 134 (mild hyponatremia — common in KD), glucose 92, BUN 12, Cr 0.3 (age-appropriate)
labs.r: Mild hepatitis pattern with hypoalbuminemia and hyponatremia — common in KD inflammation. Rules out other causes when severity does not fit the limited derangement

labs.v: Sterile PYURIA — WBC 12/hpf, LE positive, nitrites negative, urine culture growing nothing; SG 1.020
labs.r: Sterile pyuria (WBC in urine without bacterial growth) is characteristic of KD — reflects urethritis-like inflammation; rules out UTI as the alternative fever source

labs.v: Throat culture — group A strep NEGATIVE; rapid strep negative; respiratory viral panel (RSV/influenza/adenovirus/parainfluenza) NEGATIVE; blood culture x 2 negative at 24 hr; SARS-CoV-2 PCR negative; ASO titer pending; EBV/CMV serology negative
labs.r: Excludes alternatives in the differential: bacterial cervical adenitis, scarlet fever, viral exanthems (measles, adenovirus, EBV), toxic shock syndrome, staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, drug reaction (DRESS, SJS), juvenile idiopathic arthritis, MIS-C (multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children — post-COVID, distinguish via SARS-CoV-2 testing)

labs.v: Weight 17.8 kg (50th percentile for age); height 102 cm (50th percentile); BMI WNL
labs.r: Weight is critical for ALL pediatric medication dosing — every dose is mg/kg. Use admission weight as the dosing weight throughout the stay

labs.v: Day 1–3: high fever, malaise, decreased appetite, irritable. Day 4: rash appeared on trunk; eyes 'really red but no goop'; cracked red lips. Day 5: hands and feet swelling and red. Day 6: very irritable, refusing food and most fluids. Day 7 (today): skin starting to peel off fingertips; lymph node 'lump' in neck noted
labs.r: Classic KD timeline: fever first → mucocutaneous findings days 2–5 → desquamation week 2 (late finding, often DIAGNOSTIC in retrospect). Extreme irritability is a hallmark of KD (more than typical viral illness)

labs.v: PMH: unremarkable; full-term healthy birth; no chronic illness; on track developmentally (preschool, age-appropriate language and social skills); IMMUNIZATIONS up to date INCLUDING MMR and varicella (timing matters — IVIG will defer next live vaccines by 11 months); no sick contacts identified; no recent travel; no recent medications other than antipyretics
labs.r: IVIG passive antibody administration interferes with attenuated live virus vaccines (MMR, varicella) for ~11 months — defer those vaccines if due. Inactivated vaccines (flu, DTaP, IPV, Hib, pneumococcal, hepatitis) can continue on schedule

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 126; PR 116 ms; QRS 76 ms; QTc 410; no ST changes; no T-wave abnormalities
labs.r: Baseline ECG to monitor for arrhythmias, ischemia, or coronary involvement progression; KD can cause myocarditis with PR prolongation, T-wave changes, low voltage; serial ECGs during admission

labs.v: Mild dehydration (refusing fluids x 1 day; dry mucous membranes; cap refill 2 sec; tears decreased; UOP per parents 'less than usual'); weight stable at 17.8 kg (no specific recent weights for comparison)
labs.r: Dehydration common from poor PO intake in highly inflammatory state with cracked lips. Gentle IV maintenance fluids; encourage cold preferred PO (popsicles, smoothies) due to oral pain

labs.v: NOT: bacterial sepsis (blood cx neg, no obvious source), measles (immunized, no Koplik spots), scarlet fever (strep neg, rash not sandpaper), TSS (no severe hypotension/end-organ), SJS/DRESS (no medication trigger, no severe mucosal sloughing), JIA (no joint findings — yet), MIS-C (SARS-CoV-2 PCR neg; no prior COVID exposure history; would have more cardiac dysfunction)
labs.r: Classic KD diagnosis is CLINICAL — no specific lab test; ruling out mimics is part of the workup. MIS-C (post-COVID) is the most important modern mimic — overlaps clinical features but has SARS-CoV-2 antibody/PCR evidence + more severe cardiac dysfunction

nursesNote: 1200 — 4 y/o previously healthy male admitted to Pediatric Unit 8 from PCP office for evaluation and treatment of suspected KAWASAKI DISEASE (now confirmed clinically). Day 7 of high fever (38.5–40.0°C) unresponsive to alternating acetaminophen and ibuprofen, with progression of mucocutaneous features over the past 4 days: bilateral non-purulent bulbar conjunctivitis (perilimbal sparing), strawberry tongue + cracked red lips + diffuse oral injection, polymorphous truncal erythematous rash spreading to extremities, hand and foot erythema/edema mid-week now evolving to PERIUNGUAL DESQUAMATION of fingertips today (late finding, often the 'aha' diagnosis-clarifying feature), unilateral left cervical lymphadenopathy 2 cm. Meets CLASSIC KD diagnostic criteria: fever ≥5 days + 4 of 5 of the principal features (CRASH and BURN — Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue, Hands/feet + BURN/fever). Urgent ECHOCARDIOGRAM at noon: mildly DILATED LEFT MAIN CORONARY ARTERY z-score +2.4 (normal <2.0), right coronary z-score +1.8 (borderline), no aneurysm yet, normal LV systolic function EF 62%, no pericardial effusion. The coronary involvement increases the urgency of treatment — IVIG initiated within Day 10 of illness has the highest efficacy in preventing coronary artery aneurysms (~25% incidence without treatment, ~5% with treatment). Labs: CRP 18.4, ESR 88, WBC 18.6 with 78% segs, platelets 614 (characteristic Week 2 rise), albumin 2.8 (low), Na 134 (mild hyponatremia), AST/ALT mildly elevated, sterile pyuria — all consistent with KD inflammation pattern. Infection workup negative: strep neg, viral panel neg, blood cx neg at 24 hr, SARS-CoV-2 PCR neg (rules out MIS-C, the most important modern mimic). TREATMENT initiated: IV IMMUNOGLOBULIN (IVIG) 2 g/kg = 35.6 g over 10–12 hours (slow infusion, premedicate with acetaminophen 15 mg/kg + diphenhydramine 1 mg/kg PO 30 min before start to reduce reaction); IVIG started at 1230 via PIV; monitoring for infusion reactions (rate-related — chest tightness, dyspnea, flushing, fever, headache, back pain; slow rate or pause if symptoms; can usually restart at lower rate); first set of vitals checked at 15 min into infusion and q15 min x 1 hr, then q30 min x 2 hr, then q1h. HIGH-DOSE ASPIRIN 80 mg/kg/day divided q6h = 360 mg PO q6h started (anti-inflammatory dose, NOT antiplatelet; this is one of the few PEDIATRIC indications for aspirin — typically AVOIDED in children due to Reye syndrome risk with viral illness; in KD the benefit outweighs theoretical risk and Reye syndrome has not been demonstrated to be increased with KD-dose ASA). Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO q6h scheduled for fever and discomfort; IBUPROFEN HELD during IVIG and ASA therapy (interferes with antiplatelet effect of ASA when transitioned to low-dose). Cool compresses, lubricant ointment to cracked lips, oral care with bicarbonate rinse, encouraging cold soft foods (popsicles, smoothies, applesauce) due to oral pain. Maintenance IVF D5 1/2 NS at 56 mL/hr (calculated by Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 rule: 4 mL/kg/hr first 10 kg + 2 mL/kg/hr next 10 kg + 1 mL/kg/hr beyond — for 17.8 kg = 40 + 15.6 = 55.6 ≈ 56 mL/hr); reassess when PO intake improving. Continuous cardiac monitoring during IVIG infusion and 24 hr after; serial ECG q-shift to monitor for myocarditis/ischemia evolution. Family education being provided (parent rooming-in): what KD is, why fever is the cardinal symptom, what to watch for in the coming weeks (desquamation extending, possible irritability, mild arthritis can occur), the IMPORTANCE of follow-up echo at 2 weeks and 6–8 weeks (longer if coronary abnormalities persist), the ASPIRIN regimen (high-dose for 48 hr after defervescence, then transition to LOW-DOSE 3–5 mg/kg daily for at least 6–8 weeks until labs normal and echo clear; longer if persistent coronary involvement), the AVOIDANCE of live vaccines (MMR, varicella) for 11 MONTHS after IVIG (defer scheduled vaccines, document for school), and the WARNING SIGNS of cardiac complications post-discharge (chest pain, severe abdominal pain, syncope, unusual fatigue — return immediately). Pediatric cardiology consultation completed; will follow longitudinally. Considering high-yield 'second-dose IVIG' scenarios — if persistent fever >36 hr after IVIG completion = consider IVIG refractory; treatment options include 2nd dose IVIG, infliximab, or IV methylprednisolone. Parents anxious but appropriately engaged; child sleeping on and off, irritable when awake, mom at bedside continuously, dad bringing older sibling to grandparents and returning this evening; Child Life consultation for age-appropriate explanation and coping support.
providerOrders: ADMIT to Pediatric Unit; weight 17.8 kg confirmed and documented as DOSING WEIGHT for all medications; allergy NKDA; isolation Standard precautions (KD not infectious — fever workup negative; no isolation needed but acknowledge family fear that it might be 'catching' — reassure)

IV IMMUNOGLOBULIN (IVIG) 2 g/kg = 35.6 g IV ONE DOSE infused over 10–12 hours via PIV; premedicate with ACETAMINOPHEN 15 mg/kg PO + DIPHENHYDRAMINE 1 mg/kg PO 30 min before start (reduces infusion reactions); start rate 0.5 mL/kg/hr first 30 min, advance gradually if tolerated to goal rate to complete in 10–12 hr

Monitor for IVIG infusion reactions: vitals at 15 min into infusion, then q15 min x 1 hr, then q30 min x 2 hr, then q1h until complete; watch for rate-related reactions (chest tightness, dyspnea, flushing, headache, back pain, fever, rash) — SLOW or PAUSE infusion if symptoms; can usually restart at lower rate after symptoms resolve
Severe reactions (anaphylaxis-like, severe hypotension) — STOP infusion, manage as anaphylaxis (epinephrine, diphenhydramine, steroids, fluids); rare but reportable

HIGH-DOSE ASPIRIN 80 mg/kg/day divided q6h = 360 mg PO q6h x 48 hr after FEVER RESOLUTION, then TRANSITION to LOW-DOSE 3–5 mg/kg PO once daily (~60 mg/day) for at least 6–8 WEEKS until labs normalized and echo without coronary abnormality; longer if persistent coronary involvement (lifelong in some)

Aspirin in PEDIATRICS is normally AVOIDED due to Reye syndrome risk with viral illness; KD is one of the few PEDIATRIC INDICATIONS for ASA — anti-inflammatory at high dose + antiplatelet at low dose for coronary thrombosis prevention
HOLD ibuprofen and other NSAIDs while on ASA (would interfere with antiplatelet effect); ACETAMINOPHEN 15 mg/kg PO q4–6h PRN for fever and discomfort is appropriate

URGENT and SERIAL ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY: baseline (done at admission), repeat at 2 WEEKS, repeat at 6–8 WEEKS; longer follow-up (q6–12 mo) if coronary abnormalities persist; pediatric cardiology following longitudinally
ECG q-shift during admission to monitor for arrhythmia, myocarditis-related PR prolongation, T-wave changes, low voltage; pediatric cardiology consultation

Maintenance IV fluids D5 1/2 NS at 56 mL/hr (Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 calculation for 17.8 kg = 56 mL/hr); reassess when PO intake improving; encourage cool soft preferred foods due to oral pain (popsicles, smoothies, applesauce, soft pasta)
Comfort measures for cracked lips (lubricant ointment); oral care with bicarbonate rinse (avoid acidic mouthwashes); cool compresses for fever; loose clothing; quiet environment for irritable child
Continuous pulse oximetry; cardiac monitor during IVIG infusion and 24 hr post; q4h vitals when not on continuous; STRICT I&O; daily weights
REPEAT CBC, CMP, CRP, ESR daily during admission and at the 2-week visit to track resolution

DEFER LIVE VACCINES (MMR, varicella) for 11 MONTHS AFTER IVIG (passive antibodies interfere with live virus immunization); INACTIVATED vaccines (flu shot — NOT FluMist live nasal, DTaP, IPV, pneumococcal, Hib, hepatitis) continue on schedule; document and notify PCP and school
Avoid live vaccines in close contacts? — generally not required (KD is not infectious) but live vaccines for close contacts can continue normally; ROTAVIRUS in younger siblings continues per schedule

If PERSISTENT FEVER >36 HR AFTER IVIG completion = IVIG REFRACTORY: consider SECOND DOSE IVIG (2 g/kg), or IV METHYLPREDNISOLONE pulse (30 mg/kg x 3 days), or INFLIXIMAB (anti-TNFα) — pediatric rheumatology / infectious disease / cardiology consultation

FAMILY EDUCATION during admission and pre-discharge: (1) what KD is — vasculitis of medium-sized arteries especially coronary; (2) recovery timeline — desquamation continues in 2nd–3rd week, mild arthralgia can occur, Beau's lines on nails 1–2 months later (normal); (3) ASPIRIN regimen — when high-dose, when transitioned to low-dose, never combine with NSAIDs, watch for bleeding signs; (4) WARNING SIGNS post-discharge to return for: new fever >38.5, chest pain, severe abdominal pain, syncope, unusual fatigue, severe irritability; (5) FOLLOW-UP — pediatric cardiology echo at 2 weeks and 6–8 weeks; PCP within 1 week; (6) ACTIVITY — normal activity as energy allows; avoid contact sports until cleared by cardiology if coronary abnormalities; (7) AVOIDANCE OF LIVE VACCINES x 11 months
Child Life consultation for age-appropriate explanation, distraction, coping support during IVIG infusion and any procedures

Discharge criteria: afebrile x 48 hr post-IVIG, tolerating PO, ASA dosing established, family verbalizes understanding of follow-up plan, pediatric cardiology follow-up scheduled, PCP follow-up scheduled, return-precautions reviewed

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Day 7 of high fever unresponsive to antipyretics in a previously healthy 4-year-old with no clear localizing source
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fever ≥5 days is the cardinal criterion for Kawasaki Disease diagnosis. Prolonged unexplained fever in pediatric practice is one of the longest considered DDx — KD always on the list. Once 5 days reached without source, KD workup activated.

opts.text: Bilateral non-purulent bulbar conjunctivitis with perilimbal sparing — classic KD feature
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — 1 of 5 classic criteria. 'Non-purulent' is key (purulent suggests infection); 'perilimbal sparing' (clear ring around iris) is a classic KD finding. Other criteria need to be checked systematically.

opts.text: Strawberry tongue + cracked red lips + diffuse oral/pharyngeal injection (no exudate, no Koplik spots)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — oral changes — 1 of 5 classic criteria. The combination of strawberry tongue + cracked lips is characteristic. Distinguish from scarlet fever (sandpaper rash, strep+) and measles (Koplik spots, conjunctivitis with light sensitivity, no live vaccines).

opts.text: Polymorphous truncal rash spreading to extremities (maculopapular, not vesicular)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rash — 1 of 5 classic criteria. 'Polymorphous' means it can take many forms — morbilliform, scarlatiniform, urticarial — but NOT vesicular (which would suggest viral exanthem like varicella). No bullae, no target lesions (rules out SJS/erythema multiforme).

opts.text: Extremity changes: reddened, edematous hands and feet earlier in week; NOW periungual desquamation of fingertips beginning today (Day 7+) — late finding
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — extremity changes — 1 of 5 classic criteria. Desquamation is a LATE finding (Week 2+) — when it appears, often clinches the diagnosis retrospectively. Patient is presenting with the constellation appearing in this order over the week.

opts.text: Unilateral L cervical lymphadenopathy 2 cm, tender
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — adenopathy — 1 of 5 classic criteria. Often the MOST VARIABLE criterion (can be absent in classic KD). Unilateral, >1.5 cm, often firm, sometimes tender. Distinguish from infectious lymphadenitis (suppurative, with overlying redness — would have positive culture or response to antibiotics).

opts.text: Echocardiogram: mildly DILATED left main coronary artery z-score +2.4 (normal <2.0)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — coronary involvement raises urgency. Z-score 2.0–2.5 is dilated (borderline); 2.5–5.0 small aneurysm; 5.0–10.0 medium; >10.0 giant. Treatment with IVIG within Day 10 of illness substantially reduces progression risk (~25% to ~5% aneurysm rate).

opts.text: Inflammatory markers markedly elevated: CRP 18.4, ESR 88, WBC 18.6, platelets 614, albumin 2.8 — KD lab pattern
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — supports KD diagnosis. Characteristic KD pattern: WBC ↑, platelets ↑ in Week 2 (often 600K–1M+), CRP ↑↑, ESR ↑↑, albumin ↓, mild hepatitis pattern. No single lab confirms KD (clinical diagnosis), but lab profile supports.

opts.text: Sterile pyuria (WBC 12/hpf, LE positive, nitrites negative, urine culture growing nothing) — characteristic of KD
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — supportive finding. Sterile pyuria reflects urethritis-like inflammation common in KD; rules out UTI as alternative fever source. Useful for ruling in/out.

opts.text: Infection workup NEGATIVE (strep neg, viral panel neg, blood cx neg at 24 hr, SARS-CoV-2 PCR neg)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rules out the most important alternative diagnoses. KD is a CLINICAL diagnosis (no single confirming test); ruling out mimics is part of the workup. SARS-CoV-2 PCR negative + no prior COVID history is important to rule out MIS-C (the most relevant modern mimic).

opts.text: Weight 17.8 kg — needed for ALL pediatric medication dosing (every dose is mg/kg)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pediatric dosing principle. Weight is the dosing weight for IVIG (2 g/kg = 35.6 g), ASA (80 mg/kg/day = 360 mg q6h initially), and maintenance fluids (Holliday-Segar). Confirm and document admission weight; recheck if rapid weight change concern.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no drug allergies. Document and proceed. Helpful given the IVIG and ASA being initiated.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / management.

opts.text: KAWASAKI DISEASE is an ACUTE MEDIUM-VESSEL VASCULITIS of childhood; preferentially affects coronary arteries; cause unknown (likely infectious trigger in genetically predisposed children); peak age 6 months to 5 years; classic diagnosis requires FEVER ≥5 days + 4 of 5 principal criteria (or fever + 3 criteria + coronary findings on echo)
opts.rat: CORRECT — disease definition. The peak age and clinical-diagnosis nature are high-yield. Older or younger atypical-age cases exist (INCOMPLETE KD) with similar treatment based on fewer criteria + supportive labs/echo.

opts.text: Untreated KD has ~25% risk of CORONARY ARTERY ANEURYSM, with subsequent thrombosis, infarction, sudden death; treatment with IVIG WITHIN DAY 10 of illness reduces aneurysm rate to ~5%; treatment after Day 10 still beneficial but lower efficacy
opts.rat: CORRECT — drives the urgency of diagnosis and treatment. Day 7 (today) is within the optimal window. IVIG mechanism is unclear but thought to modulate immune response. Repeat echocardiogram at 2 weeks and 6–8 weeks to detect aneurysms.

opts.text: IVIG dose for KD is 2 g/kg IV over 10–12 hours as a SINGLE infusion; premedicate with acetaminophen + diphenhydramine; monitor for rate-related infusion reactions (chest tightness, dyspnea, flushing, headache, back pain, fever, rash); slow or pause if symptoms
opts.rat: CORRECT — IVIG administration high-yield. Slow rate initially, advance gradually. Most reactions are rate-related and resolve with slowing. Severe reactions are rare. Monitor vitals frequently during infusion.

opts.text: ASPIRIN in KD: HIGH-DOSE 80 mg/kg/day divided q6h x 48 hr after fever resolution (anti-inflammatory dose), then TRANSITION to LOW-DOSE 3–5 mg/kg PO once daily (antiplatelet dose) for at least 6–8 weeks until labs normal and echo clear; longer if persistent coronary involvement
opts.rat: CORRECT — KD is one of the few PEDIATRIC indications for aspirin. High dose for anti-inflammatory effect during acute phase; transition to low antiplatelet dose for coronary protection during convalescent phase. Reye syndrome theoretical concern has not been substantiated in KD.

opts.text: PEDIATRIC ASPIRIN GENERAL RULE: AVOID in children with viral illness due to RISK OF REYE SYNDROME (encephalopathy + fatty liver after viral illness — influenza, varicella); KD is one of the very few pediatric ASA INDICATIONS; other rare ones include rheumatic fever, certain cardiac conditions, post-Fontan procedure
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield nursing teaching. Parents should NEVER give ASA to a child with viral illness. KD-treatment ASA is an exception under medical guidance; verify with family the rationale and the timing of low-dose transition. Pneumococcal and influenza vaccines important for ASA-treated children (prevent triggers).

opts.text: IVIG passive antibodies interfere with attenuated LIVE VIRUS VACCINES (MMR, varicella) for ~11 MONTHS; defer those vaccines for 11 months after IVIG; INACTIVATED vaccines (flu shot — NOT FluMist nasal which is live, DTaP, IPV, pneumococcal, Hib, hepatitis) continue on schedule
opts.rat: CORRECT — important family education and EMR documentation. Document the IVIG date and the 'no live vaccines' end date in EMR; notify PCP and school. The inactivated flu shot is recommended in ASA-treated children to reduce flu risk and theoretical Reye risk.

opts.text: Maintenance IV fluid calculation for pediatrics — HOLLIDAY-SEGAR 4-2-1 rule: 4 mL/kg/hr for first 10 kg + 2 mL/kg/hr for next 10 kg + 1 mL/kg/hr beyond; for this 17.8 kg child = 40 + 15.6 = 55.6 ≈ 56 mL/hr
opts.rat: CORRECT — pediatric fluid foundation. D5 1/2 NS is standard maintenance; D5 NS is acceptable per recent AAP guidance (isotonic preferred to prevent hyponatremia); avoid hypotonic in acutely ill children. Reassess as PO intake improves; do not over-resuscitate.

opts.text: PERSISTENT FEVER >36 hr after IVIG completion = IVIG REFRACTORY (10–20% of cases); options for treatment: SECOND DOSE IVIG (2 g/kg), or IV METHYLPREDNISOLONE pulse (30 mg/kg x 3 days), or INFLIXIMAB (anti-TNFα); pediatric rheumatology / cardiology consultation
opts.rat: CORRECT — escalation pathway. Refractory KD has higher coronary aneurysm risk; aggressive escalation justified. Track fever curve during and after IVIG; rapid involvement of subspecialty teams.

opts.text: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6h continues alongside high-dose aspirin for combined anti-inflammatory and fever control
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — NSAIDs (ibuprofen) AVOIDED in KD treatment because they INTERFERE with the antiplatelet effect of aspirin (especially when transitioning to low-dose ASA). Acetaminophen is the appropriate antipyretic; NSAIDs HELD during ASA therapy.

opts.text: MMR booster vaccine should be given before discharge to maximize protection before the IVIG-induced 11-month deferral period passes
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — exactly backward. IVIG INTERFERES with live virus vaccines for ~11 months; giving MMR right after IVIG would render the vaccine INEFFECTIVE (no immune response generated). Defer live vaccines 11 months; document deferral; PCP plans re-vaccination.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Coronary Artery Aneurysm / Cardiac Complications r/t Kawasaki vasculitis with current LMCA z-score +2.4; need urgent IVIG treatment within Day 10 of illness
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The whole point of treatment is preventing this. IVIG within Day 10 reduces aneurysm rate from ~25% to ~5%. Serial echocardiography at 2 weeks and 6–8 weeks tracks evolution. ASA antiplatelet effect protects coronary thrombosis.

opts.text: Risk for IVIG Infusion Reaction r/t the prolonged infusion of a blood product (immunoglobulin) over 10–12 hours
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Most reactions are rate-related and manageable (slow or pause infusion). Severe anaphylaxis-like reactions are rare. Premedicate; monitor vitals frequently (q15 min early, q30 min, then q1h); be alert for chest tightness, dyspnea, flushing, headache, back pain, fever, rash.

opts.text: Pain / Comfort / Hydration r/t cracked lips, oral pain, sore mouth → refusing PO → dehydration
opts.rat: #3 — Significant. Mild dehydration on admission; oral pain from KD inflammation. Lubricant ointment for lips; cool soft preferred foods (popsicles, smoothies); IV fluids during the acute phase; acetaminophen for pain. Resolves as inflammation responds to treatment.

opts.text: Family Distress / Caregiver Knowledge Deficit r/t new diagnosis, complex treatment regimen, prolonged follow-up requirements, and fear
opts.rat: #4 — Important. Parents need clear education on: what KD is, why ASA in this case, watching for warning signs (chest pain, severe abdominal pain, syncope, unusual fatigue), follow-up echo schedule, vaccine deferral, normal activity guidance. Child Life for age-appropriate explanation to the child.

opts.text: Risk for Vaccine Mismanagement r/t IVIG interference with live virus vaccines for 11 months
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate stack. Document IVIG date and live-vaccine-defer end date in EMR; notify PCP and school; inactivated vaccines (flu — NOT live nasal FluMist) continue on schedule. Plan re-vaccination timing with PCP.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: IVIG 2 g/kg IV (35.6 g for 17.8 kg) over 10–12 hours via PIV; premedicate with acetaminophen + diphenhydramine 30 min before; monitor vitals q15 min x 1 hr, then q30 min x 2 hr, then q1h
opts.rat: Core treatment of KD; reduces aneurysm rate from ~25% to ~5% when given within Day 10. Slow rate initially; advance gradually; monitor for infusion reactions (slow/pause if symptoms; can usually restart at lower rate).

opts.text: HIGH-DOSE ASPIRIN 80 mg/kg/day divided q6h (= 360 mg PO q6h for 17.8 kg) x 48 hr after fever resolution, then transition to LOW-DOSE 3–5 mg/kg once daily for at least 6–8 weeks until labs normal and echo clear
opts.rat: KD is one of the few PEDIATRIC ASA indications. Anti-inflammatory dose during acute phase; antiplatelet dose for coronary protection in convalescent phase. Hold NSAIDs (interfere with antiplatelet effect).

opts.text: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6h scheduled for additional anti-inflammatory effect alongside the aspirin
opts.rat: ⚠️ HOLD NSAIDs while on ASA in KD — they interfere with the antiplatelet effect of aspirin (especially during low-dose transition). Use ACETAMINOPHEN for fever and pain. NSAIDs may resume after ASA discontinuation.

opts.text: Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO q4–6h PRN fever and discomfort; cool compresses; lubricant ointment to cracked lips; bicarbonate mouth rinse
opts.rat: Comfort and supportive care during the acute phase. Acetaminophen is safe with ASA (different mechanism, no antiplatelet interference). Oral care eases the discomfort that's limiting PO intake.

opts.text: Maintenance D5 1/2 NS at 56 mL/hr (Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 calculation for 17.8 kg = 56 mL/hr); reassess as PO improves; encourage cool soft preferred foods (popsicles, smoothies, applesauce)
opts.rat: Pediatric maintenance fluids during the inflammatory phase + mild dehydration. Cool soft foods bypass oral pain. Reassess fluid needs as PO improves; do not over-resuscitate.

opts.text: Echocardiogram NOW (baseline), repeat at 2 WEEKS, repeat at 6–8 WEEKS; pediatric cardiology consultation; longer surveillance if coronary abnormalities persist
opts.rat: Standard KD surveillance. Baseline at diagnosis; 2-week and 6-8 week follow-up are routine. Coronary abnormalities at any visit prompt longer surveillance and possibly continued antiplatelet therapy.

opts.text: Give MMR booster vaccine BEFORE discharge to maximize protection before the IVIG-induced deferral period
opts.rat: ⚠️ Exactly backward. IVIG INTERFERES with live virus vaccines for ~11 months; MMR given right after IVIG would be ineffective. DEFER live vaccines (MMR, varicella, FluMist nasal) for 11 months; inactivated vaccines (flu shot, DTaP, IPV, pneumococcal, Hib) continue on schedule.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring during IVIG infusion and 24 hr after; ECG q-shift; pediatric cardiology consultation
opts.rat: Surveillance for cardiac complications (arrhythmia, myocarditis, ischemia). KD can cause myocarditis with PR prolongation, T-wave changes, low voltage. Continuous monitoring during and after IVIG.

opts.text: If persistent fever >36 hr after IVIG completion: second-dose IVIG 2 g/kg OR IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg pulse x 3 days OR infliximab (anti-TNFα); pediatric rheumatology/cardiology consultation
opts.rat: Escalation for IVIG-refractory KD (10–20% of cases). Higher aneurysm risk in refractory cases. Document fever curve; escalate within 36 hr if no defervescence after IVIG.

opts.text: Child Life consultation for age-appropriate explanation, distraction during IVIG infusion, and coping support
opts.rat: Pediatric standard of care. Age-appropriate communication ('your body is fighting something'); distraction (toys, games, parents reading); coping support for fear/pain. Parents rooming-in are central to comfort.

opts.text: Family education on warning signs to return for: new fever >38.5, chest pain, severe abdominal pain, syncope, unusual fatigue, severe irritability — ANY of these = return immediately
opts.rat: Discharge teaching. Coronary complications can occur in weeks-to-months after acute KD; parents must know what to watch for. Written instructions; verbal teach-back; PCP and cardiology follow-up confirmed.

opts.text: Strict isolation (contact + droplet + airborne) precautions to prevent transmission
opts.rat: KD is NOT infectious. Standard precautions only. Family fear of transmission is understandable — reassure clearly. The negative infection workup (strep, viral panel, blood cx, COVID PCR) supports this.

opts.text: Document IVIG date in EMR with 'NO LIVE VACCINES x 11 months' alert; notify PCP and school; plan re-vaccination after deferral period
opts.rat: Critical post-treatment documentation. Without the alert, MMR/varicella might be given inappropriately and rendered ineffective. PCP coordinates re-vaccination at the 11-month mark; school awareness for any vaccine-related school policy.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm weight 17.8 kg; calculate IVIG dose 35.6 g; pharmacy verifies; premedicate with acetaminophen 15 mg/kg + diphenhydramine 1 mg/kg PO 30 min before infusion; ensure PIV patency
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Weight-based dosing verification is the foundational pediatric safety step. Premedication reduces infusion reactions. PIV must be patent and well-secured for the 10–12 hr infusion.

opts.text: START IVIG 2 g/kg over 10–12 hr at low initial rate (0.5 mL/kg/hr first 30 min); first vitals at 15 min into infusion, then q15 min x 1 hr, then q30 min x 2 hr, then q1h; continuous pulse oximetry; cardiac monitor
opts.rat: #2 — Begin the core treatment with vigilant monitoring. Most infusion reactions occur in the first hour. Rate advances gradually if tolerated. Slow or pause if symptoms; can usually restart at lower rate.

opts.text: Begin HIGH-DOSE ASPIRIN 360 mg PO q6h; HOLD ibuprofen and other NSAIDs; acetaminophen scheduled for fever and discomfort; lubricant to lips; cool soft preferred foods; maintenance D5 1/2 NS at 56 mL/hr
opts.rat: #3 — Adjunctive treatment + comfort. ASA is the second component of the KD treatment bundle. Comfort measures reduce distress and support oral intake. Maintenance fluids during the acute phase.

opts.text: Family education during admission: what KD is, why ASA is being given to a child, IVIG monitoring, follow-up echo schedule at 2 weeks and 6–8 weeks, vaccine deferral 11 months, warning signs to return for, Child Life consultation for the patient
opts.rat: #4 — Education while there is time and continuity with the unit team. Build understanding over the admission days; teach-back; written materials. Child Life for the child's coping.

opts.text: Track fever curve during and after IVIG; if persistent fever >36 hr after IVIG completion = IVIG REFRACTORY → consult rheumatology / cardiology / ID for second dose IVIG, IV methylprednisolone, or infliximab; document in EMR; coordinate discharge with cardiology follow-up scheduled, ASA transition plan, vaccine deferral documented
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care planning. Escalation pathway if refractory; discharge planning for sustained outpatient management. Cardiology owns the longitudinal coronary surveillance; PCP coordinates general care + vaccine timing.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 24 hr after IVIG completion: afebrile, alert, eating soft foods, less irritable, conjunctivitis resolving, lips healing; CRP 8.2 (down from 18.4), ESR 60 (down from 88); transition to low-dose ASA 60 mg PO daily planned
opts.rat: Excellent response to IVIG. Continue low-dose ASA for 6–8 weeks until labs normal and echo clear; longer if coronary abnormalities persist. Plan discharge with cardiology follow-up at 2 weeks and 6–8 weeks. Family education on home regimen.

opts.text: Discharge day 3 (Day 9 of illness): afebrile x 48 hr, tolerating regular diet, ambulating normally, low-dose ASA daily, vaccine deferral documented in EMR, pediatric cardiology echo follow-up scheduled at 2 weeks; parents verbalize understanding of warning signs and return precautions
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. Discharge criteria met. Continued outpatient monitoring with serial echocardiograms. Parents armed with knowledge and follow-up plan. Schools and PCP notified.

opts.text: 2-week post-discharge follow-up: afebrile, eating normally, energy returning, fingertip desquamation spreading (expected), Beau's lines on nails developing (expected late finding), repeat echo shows LMCA z-score +1.6 (down from +2.4, now normal); CRP 1.2, ESR 22 (normal)
opts.rat: Excellent outcome. Coronary dilation has resolved. Continue low-dose ASA until 6–8 week echo confirms continued normalization; transition off ASA only after cardiology clearance. Normal activity. Continue regular pediatric care.

opts.text: 36 hr after IVIG completion: persistent fever 38.8°C, ongoing irritability, no improvement in oral or extremity findings — IVIG-REFRACTORY KD
opts.rat: Refractory KD — 10–20% of cases. Higher coronary aneurysm risk. Escalate to second-dose IVIG (2 g/kg), or IV methylprednisolone pulse (30 mg/kg x 3 days), or infliximab (anti-TNFα). Pediatric rheumatology/cardiology/ID consultation.

opts.text: 1 hr into IVIG infusion: develops chest tightness, dyspnea, flushing, BP 92/52 (mild drop), HR 138 — infusion reaction
opts.rat: Rate-related infusion reaction. SLOW or PAUSE the infusion; additional diphenhydramine PRN; reassess; can usually restart at lower rate after symptoms resolve. Severe reaction with hypotension/airway compromise = STOP infusion and manage as anaphylaxis (epinephrine, steroids, fluids).

opts.text: Week 4 post-discharge: 6-week echo shows new SMALL coronary aneurysm z-score +3.8; cardiology continues low-dose ASA; adds clopidogrel for dual antiplatelet; plan exercise stress testing later
opts.rat: Progression despite treatment. Dual antiplatelet for moderate-larger aneurysms; consider warfarin for giant aneurysms (z >10). Activity restrictions per cardiology (no contact sports). Long-term cardiology surveillance every 6–12 months; lifelong monitoring for some patients.

opts.text: Family asks the discharge nurse if they should give MMR booster (which is due next month) — nurse re-educates that live vaccines deferred for 11 months post-IVIG; documents teach-back; PCP plans rescheduled MMR
opts.rat: Expected teaching reinforcement. Documentation of vaccine deferral is critical. Without the alert, PCP might administer MMR and it would be ineffective. School should be informed of the temporary delay. Inactivated flu vaccine in season is recommended.

opts.text: Periungual desquamation extends from fingertips to toes over the next week (Day 14 of illness); skin coming off in sheets
opts.rat: Expected — desquamation is a LATE feature of KD (Week 2–3), benign, sometimes dramatic in appearance but not concerning. Reassure family; gentle skin care; no treatment needed. Often the most-asked-about finding by parents post-discharge.
