opts.rat: Iatrogenic miss — concurrent hypoglycemia worsening mental status. Check BG on any altered mental status patient REGARDLESS of obvious primary cause. D50 25 g IV bolus + D5W maintenance; identify cause (insulin? poor oral intake? hypermetabolic state depletion?); recheck q15 min.

opts.text: Day 3: ED resident orders 4 mg ondansetron IV for nausea — pharmacist intercepts the order and flags the serotonin syndrome history and the 5-week fluoxetine washout requirement; alternative metoclopramide 10 mg IV substituted
opts.rat: System safety win. Pharmacy verification + EHR alert + the patient's documented event in the chart caught the would-be repeat exposure. Reinforce the importance of pharmacy review for ALL prescriptions during the washout. The hard-stop on ondansetron-high-dose is well-established.

opts.text: Day 4: patient asks the nurse 'when can I restart my fluoxetine, I really need it for my depression' — nurse uses teach-back to explain the 5-week washout, the long fluoxetine half-life, and the alternative non-serotonergic options bridge with psychiatry
opts.rat: Expected teaching opportunity. The patient's depression is a legitimate concern. Psychiatry has already started bupropion as bridge. Reassure that depression treatment continues; psychiatry will guide reintroduction of SSRI after washout if appropriate. Provide written timeline.

opts.text: Persistent mild fine tremor of hands at discharge; reflexes 2+; otherwise resolved — residual
opts.rat: Expected residual finding as serotonergic effect washes out gradually (fluoxetine + norfluoxetine). Will resolve over weeks. Reassure; document at discharge baseline; psychiatry to reassess at 1-week visit.

opts.text: Family meeting at Day 2: roommate verbalizes understanding of warning signs, agreed to help patient track medications, has the agent-avoidance written list on the refrigerator; PCP notified of event with summary letter; pharmacy interaction profile updated
opts.rat: Comprehensive discharge structure. Multiple checks reduce recurrence risk. Roommate as engaged informal caregiver; PCP aware; pharmacy alerted. Patient/roommate education was effective.

opts.text: Week 2 follow-up: depression score (PHQ-9) 14 (moderate) on bupropion — fluoxetine washout incomplete; psychiatry continues bupropion + supportive psychotherapy and adjusts plan; safety planning reviewed
opts.rat: Expected during washout period. MDD symptoms may temporarily worsen during med transition. Continued psychiatric care; safety planning reinforced; reassess at 4 weeks; coordinate with patient on next medication step (often a different SSRI with shorter half-life so easier to manage interactions in future).

title: Newly Diagnosed Type 1 DKA in Young Adult with Severe Anion-Gap Acidosis
subtitle: Fix the K BEFORE the Insulin, the Two-Bag Method for the Dextrose Tipping Point, Overlap IV Drip with SubQ Long-Acting Insulin x 2–4 Hours — and Why Bicarbonate Is NOT the Answer

patient.name: B. Okonkwo
patient.admitDate: Today, 0610 (ED via ambulance; roommate called 911 for confusion and vomiting; patient had been ill x ~3 weeks with polyuria/polydipsia/weight loss)
patient.room: ED Resus Bay 2 → MICU pending bed (severe DKA)

vitals.time: Baseline (per PCP visit 6 months ago — pre-illness)
0830 (CURRENT — after 2 L NS bolus, K 40 mEq replaced, insulin gtt running 1 hr)

labs.n: Initial venous blood glucose (POC and confirmatory)
labs.v: POC at triage glucometer 'HI' (>500 — out of meter range); confirmatory venous lab glucose 642 mg/dL
labs.r: Adult fasting 70–100; DKA diagnosis BG >250; HHS BG often >600 with minimal ketones. POC meters max out at 500–600 — always send lab glucose when meter reads 'HI'

labs.v: pH 7.18 / pCO2 24 (respiratory compensation via Kussmaul) / HCO3 8 / BE −20; lactate 1.4 (normal — rules out lactic acidosis as primary)
labs.r: DKA criteria: pH <7.30, HCO3 <18, anion gap >12, ketones positive. Severity: MILD (pH 7.25–7.30), MODERATE (7.0–7.24), SEVERE (<7.0) — this patient is moderate-severe. Kussmaul respirations = deep sighing pattern to blow off CO2 for compensation

labs.v: Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 132 − (96 + 8) = 28 (markedly elevated); β-hydroxybutyrate 7.4 mmol/L (severe ketonemia); urine ketones LARGE
labs.r: Anion gap >12 = anion gap metabolic acidosis. In DKA, beta-hydroxybutyrate is the dominant ketone (not measured by urine dipstick which detects acetoacetate); BHB level >3 mmol/L = DKA. Anion gap CLOSURE is the primary marker of DKA resolution (more than BG normalization)

labs.v: Measured Na 132 (low); CORRECTED Na for glucose = measured Na + 1.6 × ((glucose − 100)/100) = 132 + 1.6 × ((642 − 100)/100) = 132 + 8.7 = 141 (NORMAL)
labs.r: Hyperglycemia causes pseudohyponatremia by drawing water from cells into vasculature, diluting Na. CORRECT the Na for the glucose to assess true Na status. If corrected Na is normal/high, the patient is at risk for HYPERNATREMIA as glucose drops with treatment

labs.n: Potassium — THE MOST CRITICAL initial number for treatment sequencing
labs.v: K 5.4 mEq/L (mildly elevated despite TOTAL BODY K DEPLETION from osmotic diuresis + insulin deficiency)
labs.r: K 3.5–5.0; in DKA, serum K is often NORMAL or HIGH despite massive total-body K DEFICIT (3–5 mEq/kg deficit) because acidosis + insulin deficiency push K extracellular; INSULIN WILL SHIFT K BACK INTRACELLULAR → serum K WILL DROP DRAMATICALLY during treatment. RULE: if K <3.3, HOLD INSULIN and replace K first (insulin into a hypokalemic patient = cardiac arrest)

labs.v: Cl 96, HCO3 8, BUN 38 (elevated — prerenal), Cr 1.4 (mildly elevated — prerenal AKI from dehydration); Mg 1.8 (low-normal); Phos 4.4 (will DROP with insulin treatment — monitor)
labs.r: Mild prerenal AKI from osmotic diuresis-induced dehydration; will resolve with fluid resuscitation. Phosphate WILL DROP with insulin (intracellular shift) — replace if Phos <1 OR if symptomatic (muscle weakness, respiratory weakness, hemolysis). Mg often deficit; replace if low

labs.v: WBC 14.8 (stress leukocytosis common in DKA even without infection; bands 6%); Hgb 14.8, Plt 312; UA glucose 4+, ketones LARGE, no LE, no nitrites, sterile pyuria; blood cx pending; CXR clear; throat clear; no focal infection signs
labs.r: Stress leukocytosis is COMMON in DKA from cortisol/catecholamine surge — does NOT prove infection. Look for actual infection (UTI, pneumonia, cellulitis, oral) as a DKA precipitant. Sterile pyuria common from glycosuria

Normal 275–295; in DKA mildly elevated; in HHS markedly elevated (often >320). DKA vs HHS: DKA is acidosis-predominant (ketones), HHS is osmolality-predominant (BG often >600, minimal ketones). Some patients have mixed picture

labs.v: 12.4% (markedly elevated; corresponds to ~3-month average BG ~310 mg/dL)
labs.r: Normal <5.7%; DM ≥6.5%. A1c 12.4% with new-presentation DKA indicates chronic hyperglycemia over months — this is NEW-ONSET DIABETES, not a missed insulin dose in a known diabetic. ADA: 'new diabetes presents as DKA in 25–35% of type 1 patients'

labs.v: Pending: C-peptide (low in T1DM, normal/high in T2DM), GAD-65 antibody, IA-2 antibody, ICA (islet cell antibody), insulin autoantibody — all sent; results in 24–48 hr
labs.r: Clinical picture (young adult, lean, severe DKA, no family DM history, 3-week prodrome of polyuria/polydipsia/weight loss) = TYPE 1 DM. C-peptide and antibodies will confirm; treatment is INSULIN regardless of subtype during DKA

labs.v: 3 weeks: increasing thirst ('drinking water and Gatorade constantly'), urinating every 1–2 hours, getting up at night to urinate, blurry vision, weight loss ~10 kg ('clothes falling off') unintentional, fatigue, decreased appetite. 24 hr: nausea, vomiting (5–6 episodes), severe abdominal pain (diffuse), confusion since 0400 (roommate called 911 when patient couldn't answer simple questions)
labs.r: Classic new-onset T1DM prodrome: polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia (here decreased), weight loss, fatigue. DKA precipitants: insulin omission, infection, new-onset DM (this case), MI/CVA, pregnancy, alcohol, drugs. Abdominal pain is COMMON in DKA (~50%) and resolves with treatment — do NOT misdiagnose surgical abdomen prematurely

labs.v: PMH: unremarkable; no chronic illness; lean (BMI 22, weight pre-illness 76 kg → now 66 kg); occasional EtOH on weekends; no smoking; no illicit drug use. FAMILY HX: maternal aunt with type 1 DM (relevant — T1DM has familial component, lower than T2DM); no other endocrine disease
labs.r: Demographics fit T1DM (young, lean, family hx of T1DM, acute presentation with severe DKA, weight loss). T2DM more typically gradual onset, obese, family hx of T2DM, can present with HHS

labs.v: Drowsy, oriented to self only, slow response, follows simple commands, mumbling words; GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6); no focal neuro deficit
labs.r: Altered mental status in DKA from acidosis + dehydration + electrolyte disturbance. Monitor for CEREBRAL EDEMA (especially in younger patients and pediatric DKA) — manifests as headache, vomiting, BP rise, HR fall, change in mental status DURING TREATMENT (paradoxical worsening) — emergent mannitol or hypertonic saline if suspected

labs.v: No fever (rules out infection as DKA precipitant, though afebrile DKA infection is possible); no focal neuro (rules out stroke); no chest pain or ST changes on ECG (rules out MI precipitant — important in older DKA patients); no pregnancy (male); no abdominal surgical findings (peritonitis, rebound — abd pain likely DKA-related, will reassess as treatment progresses)
labs.r: Rules out concurrent precipitants/complications. Stress ECG important especially in elderly DKA patients (MI can precipitate)

nursesNote: 0830 — 24 y/o previously-healthy male admitted to ED at 0610 via ambulance after roommate called 911 for confusion and vomiting in setting of 3-week prodrome of polyuria, polydipsia, blurry vision, fatigue, 10-kg weight loss. NEW-ONSET TYPE 1 DM presenting as SEVERE DKA. Initial labs: BG 642 (POC 'HI'; confirmatory lab); pH 7.18, HCO3 8, BE −20; ANION GAP 28; β-hydroxybutyrate 7.4; corrected Na 141 (measured 132 — pseudohyponatremia from hyperglycemia); K 5.4 (mildly elevated, but TOTAL BODY K DEPLETED — will drop with insulin); BUN 38, Cr 1.4 (prerenal AKI from dehydration); effective osmolality 300; HbA1c 12.4% confirming chronic hyperglycemia over months. Kussmaul respirations (RR 32, deep) for compensation. Mental status drowsy, oriented to self only, GCS 13. Treatment initiated per institutional DKA protocol (mirroring ADA/AACE guidelines): (1) FLUIDS: NS 0.9% 1 L bolus over 1 hr × 1, then 1 L over second hr (total 2 L given); transitioning to 0.45% NS (1/2 NS) at 250 mL/hr per protocol once corrected Na adequate and patient hemodynamically stable; total estimated fluid deficit 5–10 L for severe DKA; replace gradually over 24–48 hr (rapid correction → cerebral edema risk); (2) K REPLACEMENT — KCl 40 mEq added to second liter of NS (because K 5.4 is OK to start insulin without delay, but will need ongoing replacement as insulin shifts K intracellularly); will check K q2h initially and add KCl per protocol (target K 4–5; if K drops <3.3, HOLD INSULIN and replace aggressively); (3) INSULIN: REGULAR INSULIN IV GTT 0.1 unit/kg/hr = 7.6 units/hr started at 0730 after the second K-containing liter began (do NOT start insulin if K <3.3); current ADA approach DOES NOT routinely use a loading bolus (per recent guidelines); titrate to BG drop of 50–75 mg/dL/hr (faster = cerebral edema risk); (4) WHEN BG REACHES ~200–250: switch to D5 1/2 NS (or use 'TWO-BAG METHOD' with NS and D10 1/2 NS run simultaneously to titrate dextrose without changing total rate) — insulin must CONTINUE until anion gap closes; the dextrose maintains BG ~200 while insulin reverses ketosis; (5) BICARBONATE NOT GIVEN — pH 7.18 above the 6.9 threshold below which bicarb is considered (and even then controversial); routine bicarb in DKA has NO benefit and may worsen cerebral edema, hypokalemia, paradoxical CNS acidosis; (6) PHOSPHATE 4.4 — will monitor; replace if drops <1 OR symptomatic (muscle weakness, respiratory weakness, hemolysis) with K-phos in IV fluid; (7) ABG/BMP/Mg/Phos q2h initially; trend anion gap; monitoring for CEREBRAL EDEMA (especially in younger patients — headache, vomiting, BP rise/HR fall, mental status worsening during treatment — emergent mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg or hypertonic saline if suspected). Continuous cardiac monitoring during K shifts; pulse oximetry; q15-min vitals during active resuscitation then q1h. The DKA RESOLUTION criteria: BG <200 + 2 of: (a) HCO3 ≥15, (b) venous pH >7.30, (c) anion gap ≤12 (the most important) — note ANION GAP CLOSURE is the goal, NOT just BG <200. TRANSITION to SUBCUTANEOUS INSULIN when resolution criteria met AND patient eating: give SubQ LONG-ACTING (glargine 0.2–0.3 units/kg/day) and OVERLAP with IV insulin gtt for 2–4 HOURS (long-acting needs time to take effect; turning off IV gtt without overlap causes rebound DKA); plan SubQ basal-bolus regimen for outpatient with endocrinology consultation and diabetes educator (new-onset T1DM requires extensive education on insulin injection, BG monitoring, carb counting, hypoglycemia management, sick-day rules, glucagon kit). Looking for DKA precipitant: stress leukocytosis 14.8 + bands 6 — not necessarily infection (DKA itself causes), but blood cx and UA pending; CXR clear; no clear infection source; the precipitant here appears to be UNRECOGNIZED NEW-ONSET T1DM presenting as DKA (25–35% of T1DM cases present this way). Endocrinology consult requested; diabetes educator consulted; type 1 DM diagnostic workup (C-peptide, GAD-65, IA-2, ICA, insulin autoantibodies) sent for confirmation. Roommate and family arriving (parents 4 hr away); social work for psychosocial support and outpatient resource coordination; mental health screening at psych follow-up (new chronic disease can cause depression/anxiety, especially in young adult population). MICU bed coordinated for first 12–24 hr; downgrade once stable on transitioned regimen.
providerOrders: ADMIT to MICU for severe DKA management; continuous cardiac monitoring; pulse oximetry; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP and ABG access (option, depending on stability)

FLUID RESUSCITATION: NS 0.9% 1 L IV over 1 hr × 2 doses (total 2 L first 2 hr); then assess hemodynamic status, corrected Na, and UOP; transition to 0.45% NS (1/2 NS) at 250–500 mL/hr if corrected Na ≥135 and patient hemodynamically stable; transition to D5 1/2 NS when BG reaches 200–250 mg/dL (see two-bag method below); total deficit 5–10 L over 24–48 hr (avoid rapid correction — cerebral edema risk)
POTASSIUM management — CRITICAL SEQUENCING:
K >5.3 (current): start insulin without delay; defer KCl until K <5.3; recheck q2h
K 3.3–5.3: add KCl 20–30 mEq per liter of IV fluid to maintain K 4–5 (this patient — currently K 5.4 but expected to drop)
K <3.3: HOLD INSULIN and replace K (20–30 mEq/hr IV) until K >3.3 then start/resume insulin (insulin in a hypokalemic patient → cardiac arrest)
Check K q2h initially, then q4h once stable; ECG monitoring for K shifts

INSULIN: REGULAR INSULIN IV INFUSION 0.1 unit/kg/hr (= 7.6 units/hr for 76 kg) — current ADA does NOT routinely use loading bolus; titrate to BG drop 50–75 mg/dL/hr (faster = cerebral edema risk); if drop <50/hr or BG plateau, double infusion rate

DEXTROSE titration — TWO-BAG METHOD (preferred for fine control): once BG reaches 200–250, run NS and D10 1/2 NS via two pumps simultaneously, adjusting the proportion of each to maintain BG ~150–200 while keeping total rate constant; alternative is to switch IV fluid to D5 1/2 NS at the BG transition point; goal is to MAINTAIN insulin infusion to close anion gap WITHOUT causing hypoglycemia
Insulin infusion continues until DKA RESOLUTION criteria met: BG <200 AND two of: (a) HCO3 ≥15, (b) venous pH >7.30, (c) anion gap ≤12 — ANION GAP CLOSURE is the MOST important marker

TRANSITION to SUBCUTANEOUS INSULIN when DKA resolved AND patient eating: give SubQ long-acting (glargine 0.2–0.3 units/kg/day, typically 0.3 in T1DM, divided basal + bolus once stable) AND OVERLAP with IV insulin gtt for 2–4 HOURS (long-acting glargine needs ~2 hr onset; turning off IV gtt without overlap → rebound DKA)

BICARBONATE NOT INDICATED routine — only consider if pH <6.9 AND severe acidosis with hemodynamic instability AND clinician judgment; even then, controversial — recent guidelines de-emphasize; risks: cerebral edema (especially in peds/young), hypokalemia, paradoxical CNS acidosis, sodium load. PATIENT pH 7.18 — DO NOT GIVE

PHOSPHATE — monitor q4h; replace ONLY if Phos <1 mg/dL OR symptomatic (muscle weakness, respiratory weakness, hemolysis); typical replacement K-phos 30 mmol IV over 3–4 hr; do not over-replace (hypocalcemia risk)
MAGNESIUM — replace to ≥1.8 mg/dL; often deficit; use IV MgSO4 1–2 g over 1 hr
Foley catheter for accurate UOP; goal UOP 0.5–1 mL/kg/hr (= 38–76 mL/hr for 76 kg); strict I&O hourly
BMP, magnesium, phosphate, ABG q2h initially during active treatment; ANION GAP calculation and TREND with each draw; once stable transition to q4–6h then q6–8h
Glucose finger-stick q1h during insulin gtt; lab glucose q2h initially for accuracy (POC meters lose accuracy at high BG)

MONITOR FOR CEREBRAL EDEMA (highest risk in young patients and pediatric DKA, especially first 4–12 hr of treatment): NEW headache, vomiting, BP rise + HR fall (Cushing reflex), lethargy/coma, decreased reactivity to pain, pupil changes; EMERGENT MANNITOL 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 20 min OR hypertonic saline 3% 5–10 mL/kg; head CT after stabilization

Identify DKA PRECIPITANT: infection workup (blood cx, UA cx, CXR), MI workup (ECG, troponin — especially in older patients), missed insulin doses, pregnancy test (if female), pancreatitis (lipase), illicit drug use (UDS), new-onset DM (HbA1c, C-peptide, antibodies)
Endocrinology consult for inpatient management coordination and transition planning

Diabetes educator consult for new-onset T1DM teaching: insulin injection technique, BG monitoring, carb counting, hypoglycemia recognition + treatment, sick-day rules, glucagon kit prescription, continuous glucose monitor (CGM) discussion, MDI vs pump for outpatient management
Diet: NPO until DKA resolved and patient alert with good gag reflex; advance to clear liquids → regular diet with carb counting as DKA resolves; nutrition consult for diabetes meal planning
DVT prophylaxis with SCDs initially; LMWH once not actively bleeding and stable; insulin gtt is not contraindication

Mental health screening: new chronic disease diagnosis in young adult carries depression/anxiety risk; offer social work, peer support, T1DM patient resources (JDRF, ADA patient communities); family support during diagnostic adjustment

Discharge teaching: comprehensive new T1DM education completed with diabetes educator; outpatient endocrinology follow-up within 1 week; PCP follow-up within 2 weeks; diabetes educator outpatient follow-up; emergency contact info; insulin supplies + glucagon kit at discharge; CGM consideration; medical alert ID encouraged

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Severe anion-gap metabolic acidosis: BG 642, pH 7.18, HCO3 8, anion gap 28, β-hydroxybutyrate 7.4, urine ketones large — classic DKA pattern
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the emergency. DKA criteria: BG >250, pH <7.30, HCO3 <18, anion gap >12, ketones positive. Severity classification: mild 7.25–7.30, moderate 7.0–7.24, severe <7.0; this patient is moderate-severe. Anion gap closure is the resolution marker (not BG alone).

opts.text: Potassium 5.4 (mildly elevated) — but TOTAL BODY K DEPLETED; insulin WILL SHIFT K intracellularly, dropping serum K dramatically
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the most critical sequencing decision. Despite elevated serum K, total body K is depleted (3–5 mEq/kg). Insulin shifts K intracellularly → serum K crashes. Rule: K <3.3 = HOLD INSULIN and replace K first; K 3.3–5.3 = add KCl to fluids alongside insulin; K >5.3 = start insulin and defer KCl. Recheck q2h.

opts.text: Kussmaul respirations (RR 32, deep sighing) — respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — physiological compensation. The deep rapid breathing is compensatory (blowing off CO2 to raise pH). DO NOT 'fix' the tachypnea — it's helping. As acidosis corrects with treatment, RR will normalize. Watch for fatigue/respiratory failure in severe acidosis.

opts.text: Tachycardia HR 128, narrowed pulse pressure BP 98/58 (MAP 71), prerenal AKI Cr 1.4 (baseline ~0.9) — significant volume depletion from osmotic diuresis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fluid deficit. DKA volume deficit is typically 5–10 L. Initial NS 1 L over 1 hr × 1–2 then transition based on hemodynamics and corrected Na. Replace gradually over 24–48 hr to avoid cerebral edema.

opts.text: Pseudohyponatremia: measured Na 132 but CORRECTED Na 141 (normal) — important for fluid choice
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — drives the transition from NS to 1/2 NS. Hyperglycemia draws water into vasculature, diluting Na. Corrected Na ≥135 → switch to 1/2 NS; corrected Na <135 → continue NS. As glucose drops with treatment, Na will rise — watch for hypernatremia.

opts.text: Altered mental status (GCS 13, oriented to self only) — risk for CEREBRAL EDEMA especially in younger patients
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — emergent complication risk. Cerebral edema is the feared complication of DKA treatment (especially in pediatric and young adult patients), highest risk in first 4–12 hr. Signs: NEW headache, vomiting, BP rise + HR fall, mental status deterioration DURING treatment. Treat: emergent mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV or hypertonic saline.

opts.text: HbA1c 12.4% — chronic hyperglycemia x months; this is NEW-ONSET DM (25–35% of T1DM cases present as DKA)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines this as new-onset T1DM, not a known DM with missed insulin. Endocrinology consult; diabetes educator; comprehensive teaching needed; family/social support for new chronic disease diagnosis.

opts.text: 3-week prodrome of polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss (10 kg), blurry vision, fatigue — classic new-onset DM presentation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — historical confirmation. The classic 'Ps' of new diabetes: polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia, weight loss, fatigue, blurry vision. 3-week duration is typical of new T1DM (more acute than T2DM). Family aunt with T1DM increases concordance.

opts.text: Effective osmolality 300, lactate 1.4 normal — rules out HHS-predominant picture and lactic acidosis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — refines the diagnosis. DKA vs HHS distinction: DKA has ketosis + acidosis as predominant features (this patient); HHS has osmolality >320 with minimal ketones/acidosis. Some patients have mixed; treatment overlaps significantly.

opts.text: Abdominal pain (diffuse) and vomiting — DKA-related (~50%) and will resolve with treatment
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — distinguishes DKA-related from surgical abdomen. DKA causes abdominal pain in ~50% of cases (mechanism unclear, possibly ileus, electrolyte, or ketosis-related). Reassess after several hours of treatment; if pain worsens or new peritoneal signs, surgical workup (rare but important not to miss true pathology like pancreatitis or appendicitis).

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented drug allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 98.0°F
opts.rat: Reassuring — rules out obvious infection (though afebrile infection is possible). Stress leukocytosis 14.8 + bands 6 in DKA is from cortisol/catecholamine surge, not necessarily infection. Continue to monitor for fever; complete infection workup (blood cx, UA cx, CXR).

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What is happening physiologically and pharmacologically?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / management principles.

opts.text: DKA pathophysiology: ABSOLUTE insulin deficiency (T1DM) or relative deficiency + counter-regulatory hormones (cortisol, glucagon, catecholamines, GH) → hyperglycemia + LIPOLYSIS → free fatty acids to liver → KETOGENESIS (β-hydroxybutyrate + acetoacetate) → anion-gap metabolic acidosis + osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia → dehydration + electrolyte deficit
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Insulin deficiency drives both the hyperglycemia AND the lipolysis (insulin normally inhibits both). Treatment targets all components: insulin (stops ketogenesis and lowers BG), fluids (corrects dehydration), electrolytes (corrects deficit, especially K).

opts.text: DKA vs HHS: DKA = TYPE 1 DM predominantly, BG 250–600, anion gap acidosis with KETONES, occurs faster (hours-days); HHS = TYPE 2 DM, BG often >600, OSMOLALITY >320, MINIMAL ketones, MINIMAL acidosis (some insulin available prevents ketogenesis), occurs more gradually (days-weeks); treatment overlaps but HHS needs more careful Na/osmolality management
opts.rat: CORRECT — clinical distinction. DKA is the body without enough insulin to suppress ketogenesis; HHS is the body with enough insulin to suppress ketogenesis but not enough to control BG. Some patients have features of both.

opts.text: POTASSIUM rule in DKA: serum K is often NORMAL or HIGH despite TOTAL BODY DEPLETION (3–5 mEq/kg deficit) — insulin and acidosis correction WILL SHIFT K intracellularly causing serum K to drop dramatically; RULE: K <3.3 = HOLD INSULIN and replace K first; K 3.3–5.3 = add KCl to fluids alongside insulin; K >5.3 = start insulin and defer KCl; recheck q2h
opts.rat: CORRECT — single most important sequencing decision. Insulin into a hypokalemic patient causes cardiac arrest. The K rule must be drilled. Continuous cardiac monitoring through K shifts.

opts.text: INSULIN gtt 0.1 unit/kg/hr regular insulin IV — current ADA approach does NOT routinely use a loading bolus (older protocols did); titrate to BG drop 50–75 mg/dL/hr (faster drop = cerebral edema risk); if drop <50/hr, double infusion rate; continue until DKA RESOLUTION (not just BG <200)
opts.rat: CORRECT — modern protocol. Loading bolus dropped due to lack of benefit and cerebral edema concern. Slow, controlled BG drop reduces cerebral edema risk especially in young/pediatric patients. Continue insulin to close anion gap (the actual goal).

opts.text: DKA RESOLUTION criteria (when to stop the insulin gtt): BG <200 + TWO of: (a) HCO3 ≥15, (b) venous pH >7.30, (c) anion gap ≤12 — the ANION GAP closure is the MOST important; BG normalization alone is INSUFFICIENT (rebound DKA if insulin stopped too early)
opts.rat: CORRECT — key teaching. Many new clinicians stop the insulin gtt when BG <200, only to have the patient slip back into DKA hours later. The insulin must continue (with dextrose added to the fluid) until the anion gap closes. Then transition to SubQ with 2-4 hr overlap.

opts.text: TWO-BAG METHOD for dextrose titration: when BG reaches 200–250, run NS and D10 1/2 NS via two pumps simultaneously, adjusting the proportion of each to maintain BG ~150–200 while keeping total rate constant; alternative is single-bag switch to D5 1/2 NS at the transition point
opts.rat: CORRECT — practical nursing technique. The two-bag method allows fine-tuning of dextrose without changing total rate (avoids volume overload). Single-bag switch is acceptable; two-bag gives more flexibility. Either way, insulin continues.

opts.text: TRANSITION to SUBCUTANEOUS INSULIN: when DKA resolved AND patient eating, give SubQ LONG-ACTING (glargine) and OVERLAP with IV insulin gtt for 2–4 HOURS (long-acting takes time to onset; stopping IV gtt before SubQ active = rebound DKA)
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield transition rule. Glargine has 2-hr onset and 24-hr duration; turning off IV gtt without 2-4 hr overlap = period of no insulin = rebound DKA. The transition is a frequent miss when patient seems 'fine' clinically.

opts.text: BICARBONATE for DKA — NOT routinely indicated; consider only if pH <6.9 with severe acidosis and hemodynamic instability; risks: CEREBRAL EDEMA (especially peds/young), hypokalemia, paradoxical CNS acidosis, sodium load; modern guidelines de-emphasize
opts.rat: CORRECT — important departure from older practice. Routine bicarb in DKA has NO benefit (trial data); the acidosis corrects as ketogenesis stops (insulin treats this). Bicarb reserved for the rare extreme case (pH <6.9 with shock) and even then debated. NCLEX trap if you assume 'low pH = give bicarb.'

opts.text: CEREBRAL EDEMA is the feared complication of DKA treatment — highest in young/pediatric patients; first 4–12 hr; signs = new headache, vomiting, BP rise + HR fall (Cushing reflex), mental status deterioration during treatment; emergent mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg or hypertonic saline 3%
opts.rat: CORRECT — most critical complication to recognize. Paradoxical deterioration during treatment is the clue. Modify fluid plan (slower replacement) and BG drop (slower correction) in young patients. Mortality up to 25% if not promptly treated.

opts.text: Give regular insulin SubQ 10 units q6h as first-line treatment for DKA — easier than continuous IV gtt
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. DKA requires CONTINUOUS IV INSULIN gtt — SubQ insulin has unpredictable absorption in dehydrated DKA patients (poor perfusion at the SubQ injection site), and timing/dose adjustment is much harder. SubQ insulin only after DKA resolved and patient stable; even then, transition with 2-4 hr overlap with IV gtt.

opts.text: Stop the insulin gtt as soon as BG reaches 200 — then resume the patient's home insulin regimen
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — REBOUND DKA. Insulin must continue UNTIL ANION GAP CLOSES (BG alone is insufficient marker). Add dextrose to fluids when BG drops to 200; keep insulin running. Transition to SubQ with 2-4 hr overlap. Stopping too early = patient comes back to ED in DKA hours later.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Lethal Hypokalemia r/t insulin-driven intracellular K shift from baseline K 5.4 with total-body K depletion; cardiac arrest risk if K drops critically
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The K rule governs treatment sequencing. K 3.3 is the safety floor below which insulin is held. Continuous cardiac monitoring; recheck K q2h; add KCl per protocol; respond promptly to drops. This is the single most preventable DKA mortality cause.

opts.text: Risk for Cerebral Edema r/t aggressive fluid/glucose correction in a young adult patient with severe DKA (highest risk in first 4–12 hr of treatment)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Modify treatment to reduce risk: gradual fluid replacement (5–10 L over 24–48 hr, not in hours), controlled BG drop (50–75 mg/dL/hr, not faster), watch for warning signs (new headache, vomiting, Cushing reflex, mental status worsening). Emergent mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV if suspected.

opts.text: Severe Metabolic Acidosis / Ongoing Ketogenesis r/t insulin deficiency; pH 7.18, anion gap 28; needs continuous insulin until anion gap closes
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The insulin gtt addresses the ketogenesis directly (turns off lipolysis). Continue until anion gap ≤12 (the most important resolution marker). Add dextrose to fluids when BG drops to maintain insulin without hypoglycemia. Bicarbonate NOT indicated at this pH.

opts.text: Volume Depletion / Prerenal AKI r/t osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia; 5–10 L total fluid deficit
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Initial NS replacement; transition to 1/2 NS once corrected Na adequate; add dextrose when BG <200–250. Restoring volume improves UOP, perfusion, and AKI. Avoid over-aggressive fluid (cerebral edema). Foley for accurate UOP.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit / Psychosocial Adjustment r/t new-onset T1DM diagnosis in a young adult requiring lifelong insulin therapy
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Endocrinology + diabetes educator + mental health screening + family support + outpatient resources. Lifelong impact requires comprehensive education and ongoing support; addressed after acute stabilization.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Fluid resuscitation NS 0.9% 1 L over 1 hr × 1, then 1 L over 2nd hr; transition to 0.45% NS (1/2 NS) at 250–500 mL/hr once corrected Na ≥135 and hemodynamically stable; total deficit 5–10 L over 24–48 hr (avoid rapid correction)
opts.rat: Standard DKA fluid management. NS first for volume; 1/2 NS once Na adequately corrected. Gradual replacement over 24–48 hr to avoid cerebral edema. Adjust based on hemodynamics, UOP, and corrected Na trend.

opts.text: Regular INSULIN IV gtt 0.1 unit/kg/hr (= 7.6 units/hr for 76 kg); titrate to BG drop 50–75 mg/dL/hr; NO loading bolus per current ADA
opts.rat: Standard DKA insulin management. K 5.4 OK to start without delay. Slow controlled BG drop reduces cerebral edema risk. Continue until anion gap closes.

opts.text: Regular insulin SubQ 10 units q6h as first-line treatment for DKA
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong route for DKA. SubQ absorption unpredictable in dehydrated patient. Continuous IV insulin gtt is standard. SubQ only after DKA resolved with 2–4 hr overlap with IV gtt.

opts.text: K management: K 5.4 (current) → start insulin without delay, defer KCl until K <5.3, recheck q2h; if K drops to 3.3–5.3 add KCl 20–30 mEq per liter of IV fluid; if K <3.3 HOLD INSULIN and replace K first
opts.rat: The K rule for DKA. Continuous cardiac monitoring throughout K shifts. Recheck q2h initially. Insulin into hypokalemic patient = cardiac arrest. The single most important sequencing decision.

opts.text: Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV push for the metabolic acidosis pH 7.18
opts.rat: ⚠️ NOT indicated at pH >6.9. Routine bicarb in DKA has no benefit and risks cerebral edema, hypokalemia, paradoxical CNS acidosis, sodium load. Acidosis corrects as ketogenesis stops with insulin. Reserve bicarb for the rare extreme case pH <6.9 with shock.

opts.text: Two-bag method: when BG reaches 200–250, run NS and D10 1/2 NS simultaneously, adjusting proportion to maintain BG ~150–200 while keeping total rate constant; insulin continues
opts.rat: Allows fine titration of dextrose without changing rate. Continue insulin gtt to close anion gap. Single-bag switch to D5 1/2 NS at BG transition is an acceptable alternative.

opts.text: Stop the insulin gtt when BG reaches 200 mg/dL — then resume home insulin regimen
opts.rat: ⚠️ REBOUND DKA. Insulin must continue until ANION GAP CLOSES (not just BG <200). Add dextrose to fluids when BG drops. Transition to SubQ with 2–4 hr overlap. Stopping at BG 200 alone = patient slips back into DKA.

opts.text: Transition to SubQ insulin: glargine 0.2–0.3 units/kg/day SubQ AND OVERLAP IV gtt for 2–4 HOURS before stopping IV; long-acting needs onset time
opts.rat: Critical transition rule. Long-acting glargine has 2-hr onset; turning off IV insulin without overlap = period of no insulin = rebound DKA. Time the SubQ dose, wait 2–4 hr, then stop IV.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring throughout K shifts; BMP/Mg/Phos/ABG q2h initially with anion gap calculation each time; finger-stick BG q1h on insulin gtt
opts.rat: Vigilant monitoring is the foundation of safe DKA management. K shifts, anion gap closure, BG trend all guide hour-by-hour treatment decisions. Document trends; communicate changes to provider promptly.

opts.text: Monitor for CEREBRAL EDEMA: NEW headache, vomiting, BP rise + HR fall (Cushing reflex), mental status deterioration, papilledema — EMERGENT mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV or hypertonic saline 3% 5–10 mL/kg if suspected
opts.rat: Critical complication monitoring especially in young/pediatric DKA. Paradoxical worsening during treatment is the clue. Slow fluid replacement and BG drop reduce risk. Mannitol or hypertonic saline emergent if suspected.

opts.text: Phosphate replacement IV potassium phosphate routinely added to fluids for all DKA patients
opts.rat: ⚠️ NOT routine. Replace ONLY if Phos <1 mg/dL OR symptomatic (muscle weakness, respiratory weakness, hemolysis). Over-replacement risks hypocalcemia. Monitor q4h; replace per criteria; current Phos 4.4 — observe.

opts.text: Endocrinology consult + diabetes educator consult for inpatient management and new-onset T1DM teaching; outpatient endocrine follow-up within 1 week; diabetes educator outpatient follow-up; mental health screening
opts.rat: New-onset T1DM requires comprehensive education and ongoing care. Diabetes educator covers insulin injection, BG monitoring, carb counting, sick-day rules, glucagon kit, CGM. Mental health screening for adjustment to new chronic disease.

opts.text: Foley catheter for accurate UOP measurement; goal UOP 0.5–1 mL/kg/hr
opts.rat: Hourly UOP is a key measure of resuscitation adequacy. Foley provides accurate measurement (DKA patients have polyuria which mimics adequate UOP from osmotic diuresis vs true volume response). Discontinue once stable and able to void.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm K 5.4 (>3.3 so OK to start insulin); 2 PIVs (≥18 g); start NS 0.9% 1 L IV over 1 hr; place foley for UOP; obtain baseline ECG and continuous cardiac monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Establish access, verify K (safety prerequisite for insulin), begin volume resuscitation. Cardiac monitoring throughout. Foley for UOP measurement. Document baseline vitals + ECG.

opts.text: Start REGULAR INSULIN IV gtt 0.1 unit/kg/hr (= 7.6 units/hr); ensure pump primed; finger-stick BG q1h; lab glucose q2h; titrate per BG drop 50–75 mg/dL/hr
opts.rat: #2 — Begin insulin therapy. K is safe (5.4). Insulin addresses ketogenesis at the source. Slow controlled BG drop reduces cerebral edema risk. Document infusion start time and rate.

opts.text: Send BMP, Mg, Phos, ABG q2h initially; calculate ANION GAP each time and TREND; recheck K q2h; add KCl 20–30 mEq per liter of fluid when K <5.3
opts.rat: #3 — Vigilant monitoring of the trend. Anion gap closure is the goal; K shifts must be caught early; phosphate and magnesium monitored. Communicate trends to provider; adjust fluids/insulin/K per protocol.

opts.text: When BG reaches 200–250: TWO-BAG METHOD with NS and D10 1/2 NS simultaneously to maintain BG ~150–200; INSULIN CONTINUES until anion gap closes; monitor for cerebral edema signs throughout (new headache, vomiting, BP rise + HR fall, mental status deterioration)
opts.rat: #4 — Critical transition point. Adding dextrose allows insulin to continue closing the anion gap without hypoglycemia. Continue cerebral edema vigilance — especially in this young patient. Slow correction wins.

opts.text: When DKA resolved (BG <200 + 2 of: HCO3 ≥15, pH >7.30, anion gap ≤12) AND patient eating: TRANSITION to SubQ INSULIN with 2–4 hr OVERLAP of IV gtt; endocrinology consult; diabetes educator for new-onset T1DM teaching; outpatient follow-up scheduled
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained recovery and discharge planning. Overlap prevents rebound DKA. New T1DM education is extensive — diabetes educator, endocrinology, mental health screening, family support, outpatient resources. Discharge with insulin supplies, glucagon kit, CGM consideration, medical alert ID.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 4 hr into treatment: BG 320 (down from 642), pH 7.26, HCO3 12, anion gap 22 (down from 28); K 4.2 (with KCl replacement); mental status improving (oriented x2, follows commands); UOP 80 mL/hr
opts.rat: On the expected trajectory. BG drop ~80 mg/dL/hr (in the acceptable range). Anion gap closing gradually. K replacement keeping pace with insulin shift. Continue current regimen; recheck labs in 2 hr; will add dextrose to fluids when BG drops to 200.

opts.text: Hour 10: BG 180 on D10 1/2 NS + insulin 0.1 unit/kg/hr; pH 7.34, HCO3 18, anion gap 11 — DKA RESOLVED; transitioning to SubQ glargine + lispro sliding scale with 3-hr IV overlap; patient eating clear liquids
opts.rat: Resolution criteria met. SubQ transition initiated correctly with overlap. Continue diabetes education; advance diet as tolerated; psychiatry/mental health screening for new chronic disease. Plan downgrade from MICU once stable on SubQ for 12 hr.

opts.text: Discharge day 4: comprehensive new-onset T1DM education completed; patient verbalizes understanding of insulin technique, carb counting, BG monitoring, hypoglycemia management, sick-day rules; has glucagon kit; endocrinology + diabetes educator outpatient follow-up scheduled; family at bedside supportive
opts.rat: Comprehensive recovery. New T1DM education is a major undertaking; teach-back demonstrated mastery of essential skills. Outpatient structure (endocrine, educator, PCP, mental health) supports adjustment. Medical alert ID; CGM discussion; family understanding.

opts.text: Hour 6: K crashes to 2.8 (from 4.2) — insulin was continued at the same rate despite the protocol-mandated K recheck not being done; ECG shows new flattened T waves and U waves; on cardiac monitor
opts.rat: Preventable hypokalemia. HOLD INSULIN immediately; aggressive K replacement IV 20–30 mEq/hr until K >3.3 then resume insulin. ECG monitoring throughout; risk of cardiac arrhythmia. Q2h K recheck protocol violated — system root cause review.

opts.text: Hour 8: nurse stops the insulin gtt because BG is now 175 and 'looking great'; 4 hr later patient develops nausea, headache, recurrent ketosis, BG climbing back to 240 with worsening acidosis — REBOUND DKA
opts.rat: Insulin stopped too early — REBOUND DKA. BG normalization alone is INSUFFICIENT — anion gap must close. Restart insulin gtt; add dextrose to fluids; transition only after anion gap closes AND with 2-4 hr SubQ overlap. Re-educate team on DKA resolution criteria.

opts.text: Hour 5: NEW severe headache, vomiting, BP rises from 112/70 to 162/92, HR drops from 108 to 64 — CEREBRAL EDEMA developing
opts.rat: Cerebral edema — classic Cushing reflex (BP rise + HR fall) signals raised ICP. EMERGENT mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 20 min OR hypertonic saline 3% 5–10 mL/kg; head CT after stabilization; slow fluid/glucose correction; ICU level of care; neurosurgery consult; mortality up to 25% if not promptly recognized.

opts.text: Day 2: patient verbalizes 'I just want my old life back, I don't want to do this forever' — emotional adjustment to new T1DM diagnosis
opts.rat: Expected psychological response. Validate the feelings; reassure that adjustment takes time; mental health referral; T1DM peer support; family support; explore his fears and unanswered questions. Adjustment to chronic disease is a process — not a checkbox.

opts.text: Patient with Cr 1.4 at admission down to 0.9 by Hour 12 with adequate UOP — prerenal AKI resolved with rehydration
opts.rat: Expected response to volume resuscitation. UOP confirms perfusion restored; Cr trending to baseline. Continue monitoring; should stay normal once euvolemic.

opts.text: Stress leukocytosis WBC 14.8 with bands 6 at admission; blood cx and UA cx returned negative at 48 hr — no infection found
opts.rat: Expected DKA finding — stress leukocytosis from cortisol/catecholamine surge, NOT necessarily infection. Negative cultures confirm no occult infection as DKA precipitant. The precipitant here was unrecognized new-onset T1DM (25-35% of T1DM cases present this way).

opts.text: 1-week post-discharge endocrinology visit: BG 110–180 on basal-bolus insulin (glargine 24 units HS + lispro 1:10 carb ratio); HbA1c plan to recheck in 3 months; using CGM; no recurrent DKA; PCP follow-up next week; mental health appointment scheduled
opts.rat: Successful outpatient transition. CGM provides real-time BG feedback. Endocrine + PCP + mental health coordinated care. Continue education refinement; sick-day plan reinforced; insurance coverage verified; ongoing support structure in place.

title: Acute Liver Failure from Acetaminophen Overdose with Cerebral Edema and Coagulopathy
subtitle: NAC Even Days Late, Don't Give FFP for the INR Alone (Lose Prognostic Value), Hyperammonemic Cerebral Edema Is the Killer — Transplant Referral by King's College Criteria

patient.name: J. Vasquez
patient.admitDate: Today, 1320 (ED via family who found her vomiting and confused; she had ingested ~30 g extra-strength acetaminophen 3 days ago after a breakup; did not tell anyone until interview today)
patient.room: MICU Bed 3 (transplant ICU pending bed availability)

vitals.time: 1320 (ED triage today — Stage III acetaminophen toxicity, ALF)
1520 (CURRENT — NAC running, intubated 30 min ago for HE Grade 3)
vitals.rr: Vent set rate 18 (controlled hyperventilation to pCO2 ~30 for ICP)

labs.v: Stat APAP level at 1325 = 18 mcg/mL; ingestion was ~72 hours ago per patient interview (took ~60 extra-strength 500 mg tablets = 30 g)
labs.r: Normal therapeutic 10–20 mcg/mL; ANY DETECTABLE level at 72 hr post single ingestion = SIGNIFICANT exposure; Rumack-Matthew nomogram is only valid for SINGLE acute ingestion with KNOWN time within 24 hr — NOT useful here at 72 hr; rely on clinical picture + INR + ALT to guide management

labs.v: AST 8200 (markedly elevated; baseline ~25), ALT 9400 (markedly elevated; baseline ~22) — 'transaminitis in the thousands' = hepatic necrosis pattern; total bilirubin 4.8 (rising); alk phos 168; GGT 188; albumin 2.2 (low — but takes weeks to drop, so this is partly chronic-on-acute)
labs.r: AST/ALT >1000 = severe hepatocellular injury (toxin, ischemia, viral); AST/ALT >3000 = massive necrosis (typical acetaminophen pattern). The transaminases will PEAK around Day 3–4 then DECLINE — declining transaminases with WORSENING SYNTHETIC FUNCTION (rising INR, falling glucose) = BAD prognostic sign (no functional liver left to release enzymes)

labs.v: INR 4.2 (severely elevated); PT 38; aPTT 56; fibrinogen 142 (low); platelets 84
labs.r: INR >1.5 with hepatic encephalopathy in patient WITHOUT preexisting cirrhosis = ACUTE LIVER FAILURE (ALF) definition. INR is the BEST prognostic marker — DO NOT correct routinely with FFP (loses prognostic value; only correct for active bleeding or planned procedure). Trend INR q6–12h

labs.v: Grade 3 (somnolent, arousable to vigorous stimulation, marked confusion, asterixis disappearing as drowsiness deepens, dysarthria, hyperreflexia); progressing from Grade 2 on arrival (lethargic, asterixis +, mild disorientation) over 2 hours — RAPID PROGRESSION
labs.r: West Haven HE grades: 0 normal, 1 trivial, 2 lethargy + asterixis + disorientation to time, 3 somnolent but rousable + disoriented to time/place, 4 coma. Grade 3–4 in ALF = INTUBATE for airway protection; cerebral edema risk rises with HE grade

labs.n: Ammonia (correlate with HE and cerebral edema risk)
labs.v: Ammonia 168 µmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <50)
labs.r: Ammonia >150 µmol/L in ALF = HIGH RISK for cerebral edema and intracranial hypertension; correlates with HE but not perfectly. Aggressive ammonia reduction strategies in ALF less well-established than in cirrhosis (lactulose less helpful, rifaximin less helpful, CRRT can lower ammonia). Hyperventilation to pCO2 ~30 reduces cerebral edema short-term

labs.v: Glucose 56 mg/dL (LOW); 1 amp D50 given at 1340; D10 maintenance running; recheck q1h
labs.r: ALF liver cannot do GLUCONEOGENESIS or GLYCOGENOLYSIS → HYPOGLYCEMIA common and dangerous; check BG q1h, maintain BG ≥100 with D10 or D20 infusion (central line needed for D20). Hypoglycemia worsens cerebral edema

labs.v: Na 134, K 4.0, Cl 100, HCO3 16 (mild metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion), BUN 38, Cr 2.4 (baseline ~0.7; acute kidney injury — likely hepatorenal-like syndrome accompanying ALF); lactate 5.2 (elevated — tissue hypoperfusion + impaired hepatic clearance of lactate)
labs.r: AKI common in ALF (~70%); from hepatorenal-like physiology + direct acetaminophen renal tubular toxicity. Lactate elevation in ALF reflects impaired hepatic clearance (liver normally clears 50% of lactate); lactate >3.5 after fluid resuscitation = poor prognosis (King's College Criteria component)

labs.v: pH 7.30 / pCO2 30 (intubated, mild hyperventilation for ICP) / pO2 142 / HCO3 14 / lactate 5.2; pH WAS 7.22 on admission before intubation
labs.r: Mixed picture: metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion + respiratory alkalosis from intubator hyperventilation; pH <7.30 + lactate >3.5 after fluid resuscitation = King's College Criteria component for transplant referral

labs.n: King's College Criteria for emergent liver transplant referral (ACETAMINOPHEN-INDUCED ALF)
labs.v: Met if: pH <7.30 (or arterial lactate >3.0 after fluid resuscitation) OR ALL THREE of: (1) INR >6.5, (2) Cr >3.4 mg/dL, (3) Grade 3–4 hepatic encephalopathy. Patient currently: pH 7.30 (borderline), lactate 5.2 (>3.5), INR 4.2 (<6.5), Cr 2.4 (<3.4), HE Grade 3 — MEETS lactate-based criterion → emergent transplant referral
labs.r: King's College Criteria are decision rules for emergent liver transplant referral; sensitivity ~70%, specificity ~90% for non-survival without transplant; alternatives include MELD-Na and Clichy criteria. EMERGENT referral to transplant center if criteria met

labs.v: Patient took ~60 extra-strength acetaminophen tablets (500 mg each = ~30 g; toxic dose >150 mg/kg = >9 g for 60 kg patient — this is 3x lethal dose) 72 hr ago after breakup with girlfriend; intentional self-harm; did not tell anyone; over next 2 days had nausea, vomiting, vague malaise (Stage II); today became confused (Stage III) when family found her and brought her in; admits to suicidal ideation at time of ingestion; current suicidality unclear given encephalopathy
labs.r: Acetaminophen toxicity stages: I (0–24 hr) asymptomatic or mild GI; II (24–72 hr) GI symptoms resolve, LFTs start rising, RUQ pain develops; III (72–96 hr) fulminant hepatic failure with peak transaminases, jaundice, coagulopathy, HE; IV (4 days–2 weeks) recovery OR death. Patient is Stage III. Late presentation increases risk but NAC is still indicated

labs.v: IV NAC PROTOCOL started at 1400: 150 mg/kg = 9000 mg IV over 1 hr (loading), then 50 mg/kg = 3000 mg IV over 4 hr, then 100 mg/kg = 6000 mg IV over 16 hr (total 21 hr / 300 mg/kg standard regimen); EXTEND beyond 21 hr if ongoing hepatic injury (most ALF cases continue NAC until INR <2 and clinical improvement); some institutions use prolonged NAC at 6 mg/kg/hr for extended therapy
labs.r: NAC replenishes glutathione (depleted by NAPQI, the toxic acetaminophen metabolite); MOST EFFECTIVE within 8–10 hr of ingestion BUT STILL BENEFICIAL even days late in ALF (improves survival in established hepatic failure independent of acetaminophen mechanism — antioxidant + microcirculation effects); side effects: anaphylactoid reactions (slow rate or pause), nausea/vomiting

labs.v: Major depressive disorder dx 2 yr ago, on FLUOXETINE 20 mg PO daily x 6 months; 1 prior ED visit for suicidal ideation 8 months ago (discharged with outpatient follow-up); college student, 3rd year, breakup 4 days ago; lives with roommate; family arrived from out of state
labs.r: Pre-existing depression + breakup + access to OTC acetaminophen + impulsivity = the most common acetaminophen overdose pattern in young women. Post-recovery psychiatric admission and intensive outpatient program likely. Fluoxetine + future medications during recovery — pharmacy review essential

labs.v: No other medications/substances on UDS (no ETOH, no benzo, no opiates, no cocaine/amphetamines); no other co-ingestion (negative salicylate, negative TCA, negative iron); no chronic liver disease (no risk factors, baseline LFTs ~normal at recent clinic visit); not pregnant; HBV/HCV/HIV serologies pending; no recent travel; not on any other hepatotoxic medication
labs.r: Rules out co-ingestions that would complicate the picture (salicylate, ETOH, TCA all common in intentional overdose). Negative pregnancy test = no obstetric considerations. No chronic liver disease = the acetaminophen toxicity caused the failure (vs decompensation of chronic disease)

nursesNote: 1520 — 22 y/o female college student presented to ED at 1320 with family who found her vomiting and confused, after she disclosed during interview that she ingested ~60 extra-strength acetaminophen tablets (~30 g total = 3× the lethal dose threshold for her weight) 72 hours ago after a breakup with intent of self-harm. Did not tell anyone until today. Stage II symptoms over Day 2 (GI). Now Stage III: hepatic encephalopathy Grade 2 on admission progressing to Grade 3 in 2 hr (somnolent, arousable only to vigorous stim, marked confusion, asterixis disappearing as drowsiness deepened); intubated for airway protection at 1450 (RSI with ketamine + rocuronium — avoided succinylcholine because of K considerations and potential AKI hyperkalemia; avoided propofol bolus for hemodynamic stability though propofol gtt now running for sedation). Stat APAP level 18 mcg/mL at 72 hr post ingestion = significant ongoing exposure. LFTs: AST 8200, ALT 9400 — massive hepatocellular necrosis pattern. INR 4.2 (severe synthetic dysfunction), platelets 84, fibrinogen 142 — significant coagulopathy. Ammonia 168 µmol/L — high cerebral edema risk. Glucose 56 (hypoglycemia from impaired hepatic gluconeogenesis) — 1 amp D50 given, D10 maintenance running, recheck q1h. AKI: Cr 2.4 (baseline ~0.7) — hepatorenal-like syndrome. Lactate 5.2 — impaired hepatic clearance + hypoperfusion. ABG pH 7.30 / pCO2 30 (intubated mild hyperventilation for cerebral edema management) / HCO3 14. Now meets KING'S COLLEGE CRITERIA for acetaminophen-induced ALF: lactate >3.5 after fluid resuscitation (also Grade 3 HE) → EMERGENT liver transplant referral to regional transplant center initiated; transplant team contacted; hepatology consult at bedside. N-ACETYLCYSTEINE (NAC) IV PROTOCOL started at 1400 — 150 mg/kg over 1 hr loading dose completed at 1500, now infusing 50 mg/kg over 4 hr (second bag), will follow with 100 mg/kg over 16 hr (standard 21 hr / 300 mg/kg regimen); HOWEVER given established ALF, NAC will likely EXTEND beyond 21 hr until INR <2 + clinical improvement (or transplant); monitored for anaphylactoid reaction during loading (flushing/dyspnea — none so far). HE/cerebral edema management: head of bed 30° (HOB elevation reduces ICP); intubated with mild controlled hyperventilation to pCO2 30 (reduces cerebral vasodilation transiently — bridging strategy not for chronic ICP control); avoiding sedation that obscures neuro exam (propofol gtt at low dose 20 mcg/kg/min, minimal fentanyl for ET tube tolerance); hypertonic saline 3% available bedside for ICP spikes (250 mL bolus or 30 mL boluses for hyperosmolar therapy; target Na 145–155); mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV as alternative; CRRT (continuous renal replacement therapy) being considered for AKI + ammonia removal + osmotic management; ICP MONITOR being discussed by neurosurgery (controversial in ALF — coagulopathy increases bleeding risk; if placed, must correct coagulopathy with FFP/cryo/platelets specifically for the procedure). COAGULOPATHY MANAGEMENT — IMPORTANT EXCEPTION: do NOT routinely correct INR with FFP in ALF — INR is the BEST PROGNOSTIC MARKER for transplant decision; correction LOSES prognostic value; only correct for ACTIVE bleeding or PLANNED procedure (e.g., central line placement, ICP monitor); vitamin K 10 mg IV given empirically (may help if any vit-K-deficient component but will not normalize ALF coagulopathy). HYPOGLYCEMIA: D10 maintenance at 50 mL/hr; D50 PRN if BG <70; goal BG ≥100; central line being placed for D20 infusion if needed (peripheral max D12.5). AKI / lactate / fluid: gentle isotonic resuscitation (NS bolus 500 mL given × 1, reassess; do not over-resuscitate — cerebral edema worsens with volume overload); CRRT consultation; pressors (norepi 4 mcg/min) for MAP ≥75 (higher MAP target in ALF for cerebral perfusion). Empiric antibiotics being considered (CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV daily) given infection risk in ALF (immunocompromised, gut translocation, invasive lines); blood/urine cultures sent. Mother and brother at bedside, devastated; sister flying in; chaplain at bedside; palliative care consultation also requested (parallel pathway — transplant vs comfort if transplant not feasible; the patient is critically ill with ~50% mortality even with optimal care; conversation framing). Psychiatry consultation pending mental status improvement — currently cannot assess capacity; if she survives, intensive outpatient mental health treatment is mandatory before psych clearance for transplant (transplant evaluation requires sustained psychiatric stability; the suicide attempt is a relative contraindication that many transplant centers require ~6 months of psychiatric stability + treatment engagement to overcome). Pharmacy review: all hepatotoxic medications discontinued (acetaminophen of course, also temporarily holding fluoxetine — metabolized by liver, no urgent need during this acute illness); minimize all medications metabolized by liver.
providerOrders: ADMIT to MICU (or transplant ICU if available); transplant center notified — EMERGENT liver transplant referral per King's College Criteria; transplant team in transit; hepatology + critical care + neurology + transplant surgery + transplant hepatology coordinating care; palliative care also consulted (parallel pathway)

N-ACETYLCYSTEINE (NAC) IV PROTOCOL — standard 21 hr / 300 mg/kg regimen: (1) LOADING 150 mg/kg IV over 1 hr, (2) 50 mg/kg IV over 4 hr, (3) 100 mg/kg IV over 16 hr — EXTEND beyond 21 hr in established ALF until INR <2 AND clinical improvement (or transplant); some institutions use prolonged 6 mg/kg/hr infusion for ALF

NAC side effects: anaphylactoid reactions during loading (flushing, urticaria, dyspnea, bronchospasm) — SLOW or PAUSE infusion, can usually restart at slower rate; severe = STOP and treat as anaphylaxis (epinephrine, diphenhydramine, steroids); nausea/vomiting common; rotten egg smell of IV NAC is normal

ORAL NAC alternative if IV unavailable: 140 mg/kg loading PO/NG then 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 doses (72 hr) — diluted in juice/soda due to bad smell; vomiting common; reserved for situations where IV unavailable

HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY / cerebral edema management: HOB ELEVATION to 30°; INTUBATE for HE Grade 3–4 (airway protection); MILD CONTROLLED HYPERVENTILATION to pCO2 ~30 (transient bridging — reduces cerebral vasodilation; not for chronic ICP control); minimize stimulation; cluster nursing care; AVOID sedation that obscures neuro exam (propofol short-acting acceptable; AVOID benzodiazepines which worsen HE)

ICP control: HYPERTONIC SALINE 3% 250 mL IV bolus (or 30 mL repeated) for ICP spikes; target serum Na 145–155 (osmotic gradient); alternative MANNITOL 0.5–1 g/kg IV bolus over 20 min; AVOID over-correction of Na (controlled with q4h Na monitoring)

ICP MONITOR consideration — controversial in ALF due to bleeding risk from coagulopathy; if placed, must correct coags specifically for procedure (FFP to INR <1.5, platelets >50 K, fibrinogen >100 mg/dL) and neurosurgery places; monitor for catheter-related hemorrhage
COAGULOPATHY MANAGEMENT — KEY EXCEPTION to standard practice:
DO NOT routinely correct INR with FFP — INR is the BEST PROGNOSTIC MARKER for transplant decisions; correction LOSES prognostic value
ONLY correct for ACTIVE bleeding or PLANNED procedure (specify the indication; document)
VITAMIN K 10 mg IV daily × 3 days — may help if any vit-K-deficient component; will not normalize ALF coagulopathy alone
Platelet transfusion for active bleeding or procedure with platelet count <50 K (<100 K for intracranial)
Cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100 mg/dL with active bleeding
HYPOGLYCEMIA management: BG check q1h; D10W maintenance at 50–100 mL/hr; D50 amp PRN BG <70; D20 infusion via central line if D10 inadequate (peripheral max D12.5); target BG ≥100

FLUID resuscitation: GENTLE isotonic 0.9% NS — initial 500 mL bolus, reassess hemodynamics, lactate, UOP, neuro status; do NOT over-resuscitate (cerebral edema worsens with volume); norepinephrine for MAP ≥75 mmHg (higher MAP target in ALF for cerebral perfusion)

AKI / renal management: hepatorenal-like physiology common; CRRT (continuous renal replacement therapy) for AKI WITH ammonia removal (preferred CVVHDF mode for ammonia removal); hold ALL nephrotoxins; renally dose all medications; avoid IV contrast unless absolutely necessary

EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTICS in ALF: CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV daily (infection risk in ALF from immunocompromise, gut translocation, invasive lines); FLUCONAZOLE 400 mg IV daily for antifungal coverage; reassess daily based on cultures and clinical course

Strict NPO; nasogastric/orogastric tube for stomach decompression and lactulose administration (NOTE: lactulose less established in NON-CIRRHOTIC ALF compared to cirrhotic HE; some centers still use; some prefer CRRT-based ammonia removal); rifaximin similarly less established
STRICT I&O hourly; daily weights; UOP target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; foley to gravity
BMP, magnesium, phosphate, ABG, ammonia, INR, fibrinogen, lactate q6h initially; CBC q12h; trend the synthetic function (INR, glucose, lactate) — these define prognosis
Continuous EEG monitoring (rule out non-convulsive status epilepticus; common in ALF with HE)
Empiric H2 blocker or PPI for stress ulcer prophylaxis (famotidine 20 mg IV BID); mechanical SCDs for VTE prophylaxis (LMWH/heparin contraindicated due to coagulopathy)

Psychiatric assessment when mental status allows — capacity, suicidality, future psychiatric admission post-medical clearance, intensive outpatient program; suicide attempt is a relative contraindication for transplant at many centers (typically 6 months psychiatric stability + active treatment engagement required); psychiatry must clear before transplant

Pharmacy review: D/C all hepatotoxic medications (acetaminophen — never again for this patient; HOLD fluoxetine — metabolized by liver, can resume when LFTs improve under psychiatry guidance); minimize all medications metabolized by liver; pharmacy verifies any new prescription

Family meeting with hepatology + transplant + palliative + family: review trajectory, transplant evaluation pathway, parallel comfort-focused pathway if transplant not feasible (psychiatric criteria, organ availability, candidacy assessment); honest framing of ~50% mortality even with optimal care

Suicide precautions during recovery: 1:1 observation, remove access to means, psychiatry consultation, safety planning before discharge from hospital; mandatory hold (5150 in California, similar mental health holds in other states) until psychiatric clearance; long-term outpatient mental health program

If patient survives WITHOUT transplant: follow-up hepatology at 1 week, 1 month, 3 months; LFTs/INR trended; psychiatric care intensified; family counseling; substance use evaluation; future risk factor education (NEVER acetaminophen, alcohol moderation, hepatotoxic medication avoidance)

If patient receives transplant: post-transplant immunosuppression protocol (typically tacrolimus + mycophenolate + steroids), psychiatry follow-up for adherence to immunosuppression + ongoing mental health; transplant clinic q1 week initially

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Acute liver failure: AST 8200, ALT 9400, INR 4.2, hepatic encephalopathy Grade 3 in young patient without preexisting cirrhosis — meets ALF definition
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the emergency. ALF = INR ≥1.5 + HE in patient without preexisting cirrhosis within 26 weeks of first symptoms. Massive transaminitis (>3000) consistent with toxin-induced massive hepatic necrosis. ~50% mortality without transplant; emergent referral mandatory.

opts.text: Acetaminophen overdose ~30 g (3× lethal dose) ingested 72 hr ago — established Stage III toxicity with established ALF
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the cause + treatment pathway. Massive single ingestion; Rumack-Matthew nomogram NOT VALID at 72 hr post ingestion (only for <24 hr single acute ingestion). N-acetylcysteine (NAC) IV protocol started — STILL beneficial even days late in established ALF (independent mechanism of antioxidant + microcirculation in addition to glutathione replenishment).

opts.text: HE Grade 3 with rapid progression (Grade 2 → 3 in 2 hr) — intubated for airway protection
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neurologic deterioration. West Haven HE: Grade 3–4 in ALF = INTUBATE. Cerebral edema risk rises with HE grade; signs progression include disappearance of asterixis as drowsiness deepens, hyperreflexia, eventual decerebrate posturing. Hyperventilate transiently to pCO2 ~30 for ICP bridging.

opts.text: Ammonia 168 µmol/L (markedly elevated; >150 = high cerebral edema risk)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — drives cerebral edema management. Ammonia correlates with HE and cerebral edema risk. CRRT (CVVHDF mode) is preferred for ammonia removal; lactulose and rifaximin less established in non-cirrhotic ALF compared to cirrhotic HE. Hyperammonemic cerebral edema is a leading cause of death in ALF.

opts.text: Hypoglycemia BG 56 — liver cannot do gluconeogenesis or glycogenolysis in ALF; this is the SILENT killer
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — preventable mortality cause. ALF patients lose hepatic glucose production. Check BG q1h, maintain ≥100 with D10 or D20 (central for D20). Hypoglycemia worsens cerebral edema and obscures neuro exam. Easy to miss if not specifically checked.

opts.text: Coagulopathy INR 4.2 — DO NOT routinely correct with FFP (loses prognostic value); only for active bleeding or procedure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — important exception to standard practice. INR is the BEST prognostic marker for transplant decisions in ALF; correction with FFP obscures the marker and does not improve outcomes in the absence of bleeding. Only correct for active bleeding or planned procedure (e.g., central line, ICP monitor). Vitamin K 10 mg IV (may help vit-K-deficient component but will not normalize ALF coagulopathy).

opts.text: King's College Criteria MET (lactate >3.5 after fluid resuscitation + Grade 3 HE) — EMERGENT liver transplant referral required
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — triggers transplant referral. King's College acetaminophen criteria: pH <7.30 (or lactate >3.5 after fluid resus) OR all three of (INR >6.5 + Cr >3.4 + Grade 3–4 HE). Without transplant, mortality is high; with transplant, 1-year survival ~70%. Time-sensitive.

opts.text: AKI Cr 2.4 (baseline ~0.7) — hepatorenal-like physiology common in ALF; consider CRRT for renal support + ammonia removal
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multi-organ failure picture. AKI occurs in ~70% of ALF. Avoid nephrotoxins; renally dose meds; CRRT for renal support has dual benefit of ammonia removal. Avoid contrast if possible.

opts.text: Intentional overdose for self-harm — suicidality assessment + safety + psychiatric considerations + transplant candidacy implications
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial dimensions. Suicidality is a relative contraindication for transplant at many centers (typically requires 6 months psychiatric stability + active treatment engagement). When mental status allows, psychiatric evaluation; suicide precautions; family/friend involvement; long-term mental health care.

opts.text: Lactate 5.2 — impaired hepatic clearance + tissue hypoperfusion; component of King's College Criteria
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multi-purpose marker. Trends inform fluid/pressor decisions and contribute to prognostic scoring. Liver normally clears 50% of lactate; ALF + lactic acidosis from hypoperfusion = double hit. Persistent lactate >3.5 after fluid resuscitation = poor prognosis.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed; relevant for NAC (no historical reaction); empiric antibiotics safer.

opts.text: Temperature 99.4°F
opts.rat: Mildly elevated — common in ALF from inflammatory response; not necessarily infection. Cultures sent; empiric antibiotics started given ALF infection risk; continue to monitor for true fever.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / management principles.

opts.text: ACETAMINOPHEN TOXICITY mechanism: at therapeutic doses, acetaminophen is metabolized by glucuronidation (50%) + sulfation (30%) + CYP2E1 to NAPQI (~10%, the toxic metabolite); NAPQI is neutralized by GLUTATHIONE. In overdose, glucuronidation/sulfation pathways are saturated, MORE acetaminophen → NAPQI, glutathione is depleted → NAPQI accumulates and binds hepatocyte proteins → centrilobular HEPATIC NECROSIS
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Understanding NAPQI explains why NAC works (replenishes glutathione). Risk factors that enhance toxicity: chronic alcohol use (induces CYP2E1), fasting/malnutrition (depletes glutathione baseline), isoniazid co-use (CYP induction), chronic acetaminophen use (chronic toxicity at lower doses).

opts.text: ACETAMINOPHEN TOXICITY STAGES: I (0–24 hr) asymptomatic or mild GI; II (24–72 hr) GI symptoms resolve, LFTs start rising, RUQ pain develops, often patient feels 'better' (deceptive); III (72–96 hr) fulminant hepatic failure with peak transaminases, jaundice, coagulopathy, HE; IV (4 days–2 weeks) recovery OR death
opts.rat: CORRECT — clinical course. The 'better' phase in Stage II is the deceptive window when patients may not seek care; presentation in Stage III with established ALF is more dangerous than Stage I (NAC most effective <8–10 hr). Patient here presented Stage III — late but NAC still beneficial.

opts.text: N-ACETYLCYSTEINE (NAC) mechanism: REPLENISHES GLUTATHIONE which then neutralizes NAPQI; also has independent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and microcirculatory effects in established hepatic failure (benefit in ALF independent of acetaminophen mechanism); MOST EFFECTIVE within 8–10 hr of ingestion BUT STILL BENEFICIAL even days late in ALF
opts.rat: CORRECT — antidote pharmacology. The independent ALF benefit explains why NAC is given even days post-ingestion. Standard 21 hr / 300 mg/kg IV protocol; extend in established ALF until INR <2 + clinical improvement (or transplant). Some institutions use prolonged 6 mg/kg/hr.

opts.text: RUMACK-MATTHEW NOMOGRAM is valid ONLY for SINGLE ACUTE ingestion with KNOWN time, with serum level measured BETWEEN 4 AND 24 HOURS post-ingestion; NOT valid for: multiple ingestions, staggered ingestions, unknown time, presentation >24 hr, or chronic toxicity
opts.rat: CORRECT — important limitations. The 'treatment line' on the nomogram (formerly known as the 150 line, sometimes Rumack-Matthew or '200 line' in UK) guides NAC decision for valid presentations. Outside the valid window, treat based on clinical picture + LFTs + INR. This patient at 72 hr is outside the nomogram — treat based on clinical ALF.

opts.text: ACUTE LIVER FAILURE (ALF) definition: INR ≥1.5 + ANY DEGREE OF HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY in a patient WITHOUT preexisting cirrhosis, within 26 weeks of first symptoms (US definition); HYPERACUTE if <7 days, ACUTE if 8–28 days, SUBACUTE if 28 days–26 weeks; acetaminophen ALF is typically hyperacute
opts.rat: CORRECT — defining criteria. Distinct from acute-on-chronic liver failure (decompensation of cirrhosis). Causes: acetaminophen (~40% of US ALF), idiosyncratic drug reactions, viral hepatitis, autoimmune, Wilson disease, Budd-Chiari, ischemic, mushroom poisoning, pregnancy-related.

opts.text: KING'S COLLEGE CRITERIA for emergent liver transplant referral in ACETAMINOPHEN-INDUCED ALF: pH <7.30 (or lactate >3.0 after fluid resuscitation) OR ALL THREE of: (1) INR >6.5, (2) Cr >3.4, (3) Grade 3–4 HE
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield decision rule. Identifies patients who will likely die without transplant. Sensitivity ~70%, specificity ~90%. Alternative: Clichy criteria (used in France), MELD-Na score. Emergent referral to regional transplant center triggered by criteria.

opts.text: ALF COAGULOPATHY — DO NOT routinely correct INR with FFP — INR is the BEST PROGNOSTIC MARKER for transplant decisions and correction LOSES prognostic value; reserve FFP for ACTIVE BLEEDING or PLANNED PROCEDURE only; vitamin K 10 mg IV may help vit-K-deficient component
opts.rat: CORRECT — important exception to standard practice. Many nurses and trainees see INR 4.2 and think 'give FFP' — wrong in ALF unless bleeding/procedure. The transplant team needs the INR trend to assess severity. Patients are paradoxically NOT massively bleeding despite elevated INR — synthesis defects offset by parallel anticoagulant pathway defects.

opts.text: Cerebral edema in ALF: HYPERAMMONEMIA + cytotoxic edema in astrocytes; ammonia >150 µmol/L = high risk; management = HOB elevation 30°, mild hyperventilation pCO2 ~30 (transient), hypertonic saline 3% (target Na 145–155), mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg, CRRT for ammonia removal; AVOID over-correction of Na; AVOID benzodiazepines (worsen HE)
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield management. Cerebral edema is a leading cause of death in ALF. Slow controlled measures; avoid abrupt swings. Lactulose and rifaximin less established in non-cirrhotic ALF compared to cirrhotic HE; CRRT effective for both renal support and ammonia removal.

opts.text: Give FFP 2 units IV stat to correct the INR 4.2 — coagulopathy must be normalized before any further treatment
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. INR is the BEST prognostic marker in ALF; routine correction with FFP obscures the marker for transplant decisions and does NOT improve outcomes. Only correct for ACTIVE BLEEDING or PLANNED PROCEDURE. This is one of the most-tested ALF exceptions to standard hematology rules.

opts.text: Hemodialysis effectively removes acetaminophen and is the primary treatment for severe toxicity
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. HD does not meaningfully remove acetaminophen (large Vd, well-distributed). HD/CRRT useful in ALF for renal support and ammonia removal (CVVHDF mode), NOT for acetaminophen removal. NAC is the actual treatment for acetaminophen toxicity.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Cerebral Edema / Intracranial Hypertension / Brain Death r/t ALF with hyperammonemia (ammonia 168), HE Grade 3, AKI
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Cerebral edema is the leading cause of death in ALF. HOB 30°, mild hyperventilation pCO2 30, hypertonic saline ready, CRRT for ammonia removal, avoid sedation that obscures exam, hourly neuro checks. Time-critical.

opts.text: Risk for Death from Liver Failure r/t established ALF meeting King's College Criteria; mortality ~50% without transplant; emergent referral required
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Transplant referral is the destination intervention; NAC + supportive care bridge to transplant. Coordinate transplant team; family meeting; parallel palliative pathway. Multi-organ support (renal, hemodynamic, neuro) buys time.

opts.text: Hypoglycemia / Multi-organ Dysfunction r/t loss of hepatic gluconeogenesis, AKI, hemodynamic compromise
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Hypoglycemia is preventable mortality cause — q1h BG checks; D10 maintenance; D50 PRN. AKI managed with CRRT; hemodynamic support with norepi to MAP ≥75. Multi-system bundle care.

opts.text: Risk for Bleeding r/t severe coagulopathy (INR 4.2, fibrinogen 142, platelets 84) and invasive lines/procedures
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Note: paradoxically patients with ALF coagulopathy rarely bleed massively without trigger (synthesis defects offset by parallel anticoagulant defects). Reserve FFP/cryo/platelets for active bleeding or procedure. Mechanical VTE prophylaxis (SCDs); avoid heparin.

opts.text: Suicidality / Mental Health Care during recovery (if survives) r/t intentional overdose for self-harm + depression + breakup; future safety planning
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Acute psychiatric evaluation when mental status allows; suicide precautions during hospitalization; intensive outpatient mental health treatment post-discharge; transplant team requires sustained psychiatric stability + treatment engagement (typically 6 months) for candidacy.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) IV protocol — 150 mg/kg over 1 hr loading, 50 mg/kg over 4 hr, 100 mg/kg over 16 hr (21 hr / 300 mg/kg); extend beyond 21 hr in established ALF until INR <2 + clinical improvement
opts.rat: Standard treatment for acetaminophen toxicity AND beneficial in established ALF regardless of cause (independent antioxidant + microcirculation mechanism). Monitor for anaphylactoid reaction during loading. Extend in ALF until improvement or transplant.

opts.text: Emergent liver transplant referral to regional transplant center; transplant team coordination
opts.rat: King's College Criteria met (lactate >3.5 + HE Grade 3). Without transplant, ~50% mortality. Time-sensitive; expedite evaluation and transfer to transplant-capable center if not at one.

opts.text: FFP 4 units + cryoprecipitate to correct the INR 4.2 and fibrinogen 142 prophylactically
opts.rat: ⚠️ KEY ALF EXCEPTION. INR is the BEST prognostic marker for transplant decisions in ALF; correction LOSES prognostic value. Only correct for ACTIVE bleeding or PLANNED procedure. Vitamin K 10 mg IV (may help vit-K-deficient component) acceptable.

opts.text: Intubation for HE Grade 3 airway protection; mild controlled hyperventilation to pCO2 ~30 for cerebral edema bridging; minimize sedation that obscures neuro exam (propofol short-acting OK; AVOID benzos)
opts.rat: Standard ALF neurocritical care. HE Grade 3–4 requires intubation for airway. Mild hyperventilation is transient ICP-reducing measure. Propofol vs avoiding sedation balance. Benzodiazepines worsen HE.

opts.text: D10W maintenance at 50 mL/hr; D50 IV PRN BG <70; check BG q1h; central line for D20 if needed (peripheral max D12.5)
opts.rat: Hypoglycemia management — liver cannot do gluconeogenesis in ALF. Easy-to-miss preventable mortality cause. Goal BG ≥100. Central line for higher dextrose concentrations.

opts.text: Hypertonic saline 3% 250 mL bolus or mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV for ICP spikes; target Na 145–155 (osmotic gradient); AVOID over-correction of Na
opts.rat: Hyperosmolar therapy for cerebral edema. Both effective; hypertonic saline preferred in many centers (no diuretic effect, hypotension risk lower). Q4h Na monitoring; controlled correction.

opts.text: Hemodialysis for acetaminophen removal
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong indication. HD does not effectively remove acetaminophen (large Vd, well-distributed). CRRT useful in ALF for renal support and ammonia removal (CVVHDF mode), NOT for acetaminophen removal. NAC is the actual treatment for acetaminophen toxicity.

opts.text: CRRT (continuous renal replacement therapy) in CVVHDF mode for AKI + ammonia removal in ALF
opts.rat: Dual-purpose intervention. AKI common in ALF (~70%). CVVHDF mode removes ammonia more effectively than intermittent HD. Slower correction reduces hemodynamic and cerebral perturbation. Nephrology + critical care coordination.

opts.text: Empiric antibiotics CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV daily + FLUCONAZOLE 400 mg IV daily; blood and urine cultures sent
opts.rat: Infection risk in ALF from immunocompromise, gut translocation, invasive lines. Empiric broad-spectrum coverage common. Reassess daily based on cultures and clinical course. SBP-like prophylaxis adapted from cirrhotic care.

opts.text: Acetaminophen 1 g IV q6h scheduled for fever and discomfort
