opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — non-heparin anticoagulant selection. Argatroban hepatically cleared → preferred for renal impairment. Bivalirudin for renal + hepatic concerns or cardiac cath. Fondaparinux off-label option. DOACs after platelet recovery. Pharmacy + hematology coordination.

opts.text: HIT antibody testing sent: PF4 ELISA (4–6 hr turnaround, sensitive but not specific), SRA confirmatory (2–5 days, sensitive + specific) — but ACT on 4Ts ≥4 clinical probability, don't wait
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — laboratory confirmation pathway. ELISA optical density value matters (>2.0 strongly suggests true HIT; lower OD weaker). SRA gold standard. Act clinically based on 4Ts; labs confirm or revise diagnosis over days.

opts.text: Document HIT permanently in EHR allergy list as 'HEPARIN — HIT — NO HEPARIN OR LMWH EVER'; medical alert ID encouraged; HIT wallet card; family/patient education for lifelong avoidance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sentinel safety documentation. Future re-exposure within 100 days = RAPID HIT; after 100 days antibodies may decline but recurrence possible. Default = lifelong heparin avoidance. Daughter as advocate; PCP/cardiology aware; medical alert ID.

opts.text: Allergies: sulfa rash + adding HIT today
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — allergy list updated for the new HIT diagnosis. Documentation in EHR + bracelet + verbal communication at handoff. The pre-existing sulfa allergy is documented; HIT becomes the second.

opts.text: Temperature 99.0°F
opts.rat: Mildly elevated but not dramatic — does not change management. Continue to monitor; HIT itself does not typically cause fever (sepsis would, but workup negative). DVT can have low-grade fever.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / pharmacology.

opts.text: HIT TYPE II is an IMMUNE-MEDIATED reaction: heparin binds PLATELET FACTOR 4 (PF4); the heparin-PF4 complex is a neoantigen; IgG antibodies form against the complex; antibodies bind to PF4-heparin on platelets and activate them via FcγRIIa receptor; activated platelets release procoagulant microparticles and aggregate → BOTH thrombocytopenia (consumption) AND PARADOXICAL THROMBOSIS
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Distinguishes HIT II (immune, 5–10 day onset, thrombocytopenia + thrombosis, severe) from HIT I (non-immune, transient, mild plt drop early, no thrombosis, no treatment needed). HIT II is THE diagnosis of concern.

opts.text: 4Ts SCORE: Thrombocytopenia (magnitude of fall + nadir), Timing (5–10 days typical; <24 hr if prior exposure within 100 days), Thrombosis (new venous or arterial), oTher causes (sepsis, DIC, drugs, marrow); score 0–8; ≥4 = act on it, don't wait for labs
opts.rat: CORRECT — standardized clinical probability score. Acting on 4Ts ≥4 without waiting for antibody labs is standard. Many patients with low 4Ts (<4) won't need treatment change; intermediate (4–5) may benefit; high (6–8) almost always real.

opts.text: STOP ALL HEPARIN EXPOSURES including LINE FLUSHES (replace with NS flushes); ALL heparin-coated catheters must be removed; LMWH cross-reacts in 90%+ of HIT cases and is contraindicated; FONDAPARINUX has minimal cross-reactivity (off-label use in HIT)
opts.rat: CORRECT — sole most important step. The most-missed exposure is line flushes; the most-missed cross-reactivity is LMWH. Communicate across all departments. Pharmacy + nursing + IT (EMR flag) coordination.

opts.text: DO NOT give PLATELET TRANSFUSION even with low platelets + active thrombosis — platelets provide more substrate for HIT-mediated activation, worsening thrombosis; EXCEPTION: active life-threatening bleeding only
opts.rat: CORRECT — counterintuitive but evidence-based. The standard reflex (low platelets → transfuse) is wrong in HIT. The platelets are being CONSUMED by antibody-mediated activation; adding more = more clotting. Reserve for life-threatening bleed.

opts.text: DO NOT START WARFARIN until platelets recover ≥150 — warfarin depletes proteins C/S faster than other clotting factors causing transient hypercoagulability → MICROTHROMBOSIS → SKIN NECROSIS, VENOUS GANGRENE, LIMB LOSS (warfarin-induced skin necrosis)
opts.rat: CORRECT — counterintuitive but devastating if violated. Once platelets recover and patient on adequate non-heparin anticoagulation, can bridge to warfarin with 5-day overlap. DOACs are increasingly used after platelet recovery (no protein C/S issue, no INR overlap complexity).

opts.text: ARGATROBAN — DIRECT THROMBIN INHIBITOR; HEPATICALLY metabolized → preferred when renal function impaired (no renal dose adjustment); dose 2 mcg/kg/min IV; reduce starting dose to 0.5 mcg/kg/min in HEPATIC impairment, HF, anasarca; titrate aPTT q6h to 1.5–3x baseline; falsely PROLONGS INR during warfarin transition (interpret with caution)
opts.rat: CORRECT — argatroban pharmacology. ATT to baseline normal aPTT for target. INR effect during transition is a common point of confusion (the prolonged INR doesn't necessarily mean warfarin is therapeutic — some protocols hold argatroban for ~4 hr before INR check).

opts.text: BIVALIRUDIN — alternative direct thrombin inhibitor; preferred when both renal AND hepatic impairment, OR when patient needs cardiac catheterization (used widely in PCI); shorter half-life than argatroban (25 min vs 45 min)
opts.rat: CORRECT — alternative non-heparin anticoagulant for specific contexts. FONDAPARINUX (synthetic factor Xa inhibitor) also option in stable HIT patients (off-label). DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) after platelet recovery.

opts.text: HIT ANTIBODY testing: PF4 ELISA (sensitive but not specific — many false positives especially after cardiac surgery; OD value matters); SRA serotonin release assay (sensitive AND specific — gold standard, 2–5 day turnaround at reference lab); HIPA alternative confirmatory assay
opts.rat: CORRECT — laboratory workup. ELISA is the screen, SRA is the confirm. Don't wait for either to act on 4Ts ≥4 clinical probability — if HIT real, every hour of continued heparin = continued thrombosis risk.

opts.text: Document HIT in EHR allergy/interaction list permanently; future re-exposure within 100 days = RAPID HIT within 24 hr (antibodies persist); after 100 days antibodies often decline but recurrence with re-exposure possible; default = LIFELONG HEPARIN AVOIDANCE
opts.rat: CORRECT — patient safety documentation. Medical alert ID + wallet card + family education. Future cardiac surgery, dialysis, prophylaxis, hospital line flushes — all must avoid heparin. Special protocols (bivalirudin for cardiopulmonary bypass) exist if heparin re-exposure unavoidable.

opts.text: Continue heparin gtt at reduced rate and add platelet transfusion to bring count above 100 K/µL
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG on both counts. STOP heparin entirely (don't continue at lower dose) AND DO NOT TRANSFUSE platelets (worsens thrombosis). Classic NCLEX trap — these are the most-tested HIT exception rules.

opts.text: Start warfarin 5 mg PO daily immediately to bridge to oral anticoagulation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Warfarin in active HIT causes skin necrosis, venous gangrene, limb loss. Wait for platelet recovery ≥150 + adequate non-heparin anticoagulation. The classic warfarin-induced skin necrosis case is the HIT patient started on warfarin without platelet recovery — DEVASTATING.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Continued / Extending Thrombosis (DVT extension, new PE, arterial events including limb ischemia, stroke, MI) r/t active HIT with ongoing heparin exposure
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The HIT thrombosis is the imminent killer (PE, stroke, limb loss). STOP all heparin including flushes IMMEDIATELY; START non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban); bilateral US for additional clots; assess for arterial events. Every hour of continued heparin = continued thrombosis.

opts.text: Risk for Inadvertent Heparin Re-exposure r/t multiple care areas and easily-missed exposures (line flushes, heparin-coated catheters, LMWH alternative)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Easy to miss the flushes and the cross-reactivity. EHR alert; chart flag; verbal handoff; communication with all units (ICU, ED, OR, radiology, lab, transport, dialysis if applicable). Pharmacy verification of all medications.

opts.text: Risk for Warfarin-Induced Skin Necrosis r/t starting warfarin in active HIT before platelet recovery
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Counterintuitive but devastating if violated. Wait for plt ≥150 + adequate argatroban or bivalirudin coverage before any warfarin. DOACs after platelet recovery is increasingly preferred to avoid this risk entirely.

opts.text: Risk for Bleeding r/t therapeutic-dose non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban) with active DVT requiring full anticoagulation
opts.rat: #4 — Significant but manageable. Argatroban titrated to aPTT 1.5–3x baseline (not higher). Monitor for bleeding signs (gums, urine, stool, neuro, hematoma). Have antidote (no specific reversal for argatroban — discontinuation + supportive care; recombinant factor VIIa for life-threatening bleed). Document any bleeding.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit r/t HIT lifelong heparin avoidance, future medical encounter advocacy, signs of recurrence, anticoagulation continuation 6 months
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Discharge teaching: HIT permanently documented, medical alert ID, wallet card, no heparin OR LMWH ever, future surgery requires hematology consultation, anticoagulation continuation 6 months for HIT + DVT. Daughter as advocate.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: STOP ALL HEPARIN EXPOSURES IMMEDIATELY: D/C UFH gtt, D/C UFH SubQ, REPLACE line flushes with NS, REMOVE heparin-coated catheters; AVOID LMWH (cross-reacts)
opts.rat: Sole most important intervention. Each missed exposure perpetuates HIT. EHR alert + chart flag + communication across all care areas. LMWH cross-reacts 90%+ — also contraindicated.

opts.text: START ARGATROBAN 2 mcg/kg/min IV gtt; titrate aPTT q6h to 1.5–3x baseline (48–96 for this patient with baseline 32)
opts.rat: First-line non-heparin anticoagulant for HIT with normal hepatic function. Hepatic clearance — no renal adjustment. Reduce starting dose if hepatic impairment, HF, anasarca, post-cardiac-surgery.

opts.text: PLATELET transfusion 1 unit IV to raise count from 68 toward 100 K/µL
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Platelets provide more substrate for HIT-mediated activation and worsen thrombosis. EXCEPTION: active life-threatening bleeding only. This patient has thrombosis (DVT), not bleeding — do NOT transfuse.

opts.text: Start WARFARIN 5 mg PO daily today for transition to oral anticoagulation
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG in active HIT. Warfarin depletes proteins C/S faster than other clotting factors → transient hypercoagulability → skin necrosis, venous gangrene, limb loss. Wait for plt ≥150 + adequate non-heparin anticoagulation; then bridge with 5-day overlap; OR use DOAC after platelet recovery.

opts.text: Send PF4 ELISA + SRA HIT antibody testing; while waiting for labs, ACT on 4Ts ≥4 clinical probability
opts.rat: Standard workup pathway. Don't delay treatment for lab confirmation. ELISA sensitive but not specific (false positives common after cardiac surgery); SRA confirmatory but slow.

opts.text: Continue heparin gtt at reduced rate of 5 units/kg/hr to maintain some anticoagulation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. STOP all heparin entirely — any heparin perpetuates HIT. Replace with non-heparin anticoagulant. Reduced-dose heparin is not safer; it's just less anticoagulation while continuing the HIT trigger.

opts.text: Replace ALL heparin line flushes with NORMAL SALINE flushes; verify across all units (ICU, ED, OR, radiology, lab, transport); flag patient chart prominently
opts.rat: The most-missed exposure. Heparin in line flushes (typically 10–100 units/mL) is enough to perpetuate HIT. ALL units must know; pharmacy verifies; EHR alert.

opts.text: Bilateral lower extremity duplex ultrasound to assess for additional asymptomatic DVT; CT chest with contrast if respiratory symptoms develop; head CT if neuro symptoms; assess for arterial events (limb, mesenteric)
opts.rat: HIT can cause multiple thromboses (often clinically silent). Bilateral US standard. Arterial events less common but more morbid (limb ischemia, stroke, MI, mesenteric ischemia). Workup based on symptoms.

opts.text: Daily platelet count; expect recovery within 7–14 days after stopping heparin; continue argatroban until platelets recover ≥150 then transition
opts.rat: Standard monitoring. If plt continues to drop or doesn't recover, reassess for ongoing heparin exposure (most common reason) or alternative diagnosis. Recovery is the criterion for bridging to oral anticoagulant.

opts.text: DOCUMENT HIT in EHR allergy list permanently as 'HEPARIN — HIT — NO HEPARIN OR LMWH EVER'; medical alert ID encouraged; HIT wallet card; PCP + cardiology notified; daughter as advocate
opts.rat: Sentinel safety documentation. Future re-exposure within 100 days = RAPID HIT; after 100 days HIT can still recur with re-exposure. Default = lifelong heparin avoidance. Special protocols (bivalirudin for cardiopulmonary bypass) if heparin re-exposure unavoidable.

opts.text: Total anticoagulation duration for HIT WITH thrombosis (this patient with DVT) = 6 months minimum; HIT alone without thrombosis = 3 months minimum
opts.rat: Standard duration. After bridge to oral anticoagulant (warfarin or DOAC after platelet recovery), continue 6 months minimum. Reassess for ongoing risk factors; some patients may need longer.

opts.text: Family education with husband + daughter: HIT permanently documented, lifelong heparin/LMWH avoidance, signs of recurrent thrombosis to watch for, anticoagulation duration, future medical encounter advocacy
opts.rat: Discharge teaching essential. Daughter as informed advocate prevents future iatrogenic re-exposure. Written documentation; wallet card; medical alert ID. PCP coordination.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: STOP all heparin exposures immediately: D/C UFH gtt, D/C UFH SubQ, REPLACE line flushes with NS, REMOVE heparin-coated catheters; communicate to all staff and units; FLAG patient chart 'NO HEPARIN'
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stop the cause. Each minute of continued heparin = continued thrombosis. Communicate broadly; EHR alert; chart flag; pharmacy verification. The flushes are the most-missed exposure.

opts.text: Start ARGATROBAN 2 mcg/kg/min IV gtt; aPTT baseline + q6h titration to target 1.5–3x baseline (48–96 for this patient)
opts.rat: #2 — Replace anticoagulation. Patient has active DVT requiring full anticoagulation; non-heparin anticoagulant must start as heparin is stopped. Pharmacy + hematology coordination. Reduce starting dose if hepatic impairment.

opts.text: Send PF4 ELISA + SRA HIT antibody testing; bilateral lower extremity duplex US for additional clots; consider CT chest if any respiratory symptoms; head CT if neuro symptoms
opts.rat: #3 — Confirm and assess scope. Labs confirm or revise diagnosis (act on 4Ts in meantime). Ultrasound for additional thromboses (often clinically silent). CT for organ-specific events based on symptoms.

opts.text: Document HIT in EHR allergy list permanently; hematology consult; pharmacy consult; daily plt count monitoring; await platelet recovery to ≥150 before considering oral anticoagulant transition
opts.rat: #4 — Sustained safety and care planning. EHR alert prevents future re-exposure. Hematology guides ongoing management. Daily plt count tracks recovery. Oral anticoagulant transition after platelet recovery.

opts.text: Family education with husband + daughter: HIT permanently, lifelong heparin/LMWH avoidance, signs of recurrent thrombosis, anticoagulation 6 months for HIT + DVT, medical alert ID, wallet card, advocacy at every future medical encounter
opts.rat: #5 — Discharge planning + recurrence prevention. Daughter empowered. Written documentation; medical alert ID. PCP + cardiology + future surgical/medical providers notified. Hematology follow-up coordinated.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: POD 9 (2 days after stopping heparin + starting argatroban): plt 124 (up from 68); aPTT 72 on argatroban (in target range); DVT stable, no extension on US; no new thrombosis; PF4 ELISA returned positive OD 2.4 (strongly suggests HIT); SRA pending
opts.rat: On the expected trajectory. Platelets recovering as heparin is removed. Anticoagulation in target range. No new thrombosis. ELISA confirms clinical suspicion. Continue argatroban; plan transition to oral anticoagulant when plt ≥150.

opts.text: POD 12: plt 168; transitioning from argatroban to apixaban 10 mg PO BID x 7 days then 5 mg BID; cardiology continuing amiodarone for AFib; CABG recovery on track; DVT resolving; hematology following; SRA returned positive confirming HIT
opts.rat: Successful transition. Platelet recovery ≥150 allows DOAC initiation (no warfarin overlap complexity, no skin necrosis risk). Apixaban for HIT + DVT = increasingly common modern choice. Hematology following for 6-month course management.

opts.text: Discharge day 14: home with apixaban 5 mg PO BID for 6 months; HIT permanently documented in EHR + medical alert ID worn; wallet card issued; daughter as advocate verbalizes lifelong heparin avoidance; PCP + cardiology + hematology follow-up scheduled within 1 week; family understands warning signs of recurrent thrombosis and bleeding
opts.rat: Comprehensive successful discharge. Patient + family educated. Multidisciplinary outpatient follow-up. Documentation in place for future medical encounter safety. Apixaban for total 6 months from DVT diagnosis.

opts.text: POD 8: cross-cover nurse continues UFH flushes despite chart alert ('I thought we were only stopping the gtt'); plt drops further to 52 (from 68); new L upper extremity swelling — extension to axillary vein clot
opts.rat: ⚠️ Iatrogenic continuation of HIT — system safety failure. The 'flushes don't count' misconception. Reinforce education + EHR alert + verbal communication. RCA appropriate. Switch all line flushes to NS immediately + continue argatroban + reassess for additional thromboses.

opts.text: POD 8: cross-cover physician orders warfarin 5 mg PO daily for 'transition to oral' despite plt 68; nurse questions and refuses to administer; charge RN + pharmacy + hematology intervene; order is canceled; education provided
opts.rat: System safety win. The nurse held the medication appropriately given the contraindication. Warfarin in active HIT = warfarin-induced skin necrosis risk. Educate cross-cover team; reinforce protocol; document escalation.

opts.text: POD 9: develops severe right calf pain with dusky discoloration and weak pulses — venous gangrene from extending DVT despite argatroban; emergent vascular consultation; therapeutic adjustment
opts.rat: HIT complication progression. Reassess argatroban level (aPTT in target?); reassess for ongoing heparin exposure; vascular surgery for limb assessment; consider IVC filter (controversial in HIT — increases thrombosis risk); thrombolytic therapy considered for limb-threatening clots; possible amputation if irreversible.

opts.text: POD 8 evening: new dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, mild hypoxia SpO2 91% on 2 L NC; STAT CT-PA confirms acute segmental PE — HIT-associated pulmonary embolism
opts.rat: Additional thrombotic event from HIT. Continue argatroban (already on anticoagulation); reassess argatroban dosing/aPTT level; supplemental O2; monitor for hemodynamic compromise (right heart strain on echo). IVC filter controversial in HIT — increases additional thrombosis risk.

opts.text: After thorough patient education, family meeting completed; husband + daughter both verbalize the 'no heparin ever' rule; daughter has written a list for the patient's wallet of all heparin-containing products to avoid (including over-the-counter, dental procedures, etc.); medical alert ID ordered
opts.rat: Successful education outcome. Daughter as informed advocate is the long-term protection against iatrogenic re-exposure. Written documentation; medical alert ID; PCP awareness. Reduces lifetime risk of rapid-onset HIT recurrence.

opts.text: Persistent mild bruising at injection sites and mild gum bleeding on argatroban; no major bleeding; aPTT in target range
opts.rat: Expected mild bleeding risk on therapeutic anticoagulation. Monitor for major bleeding (significant blood loss, hemodynamic instability, intracranial hemorrhage, GI bleed, hematuria). Continue current management; document bleeding signs; reassess aPTT if concerning.

opts.text: 1-month outpatient follow-up: at home on apixaban; no new thrombosis; bruising minimal; DVT symptoms resolved; CABG recovery good; AFib controlled; PCP coordinated; hematology continues for the 6-month course; medical alert ID worn daily
opts.rat: Sustained recovery. Apixaban for HIT + DVT for 6 months total. Lifelong heparin avoidance. Future medical encounter education ongoing. Daughter and patient empowered. Document continued education at each visit.

title: Central Line–Associated Bloodstream Infection (CLABSI) from Bundle Breach in MICU Patient
subtitle: Remove the Line Is Definitive Source Control, Scrub-the-Hub 15 Seconds Every Access, Daily Necessity Review — and the Differentiating Time-to-Positivity Rule Tells Us It's the Line

patient.name: T. Hartmann
patient.admitDate: MICU Day 8 (admitted 8 days ago for septic shock from CAP pneumonia; recovered initial sepsis; on Day 8 with persistent right IJ triple-lumen central line)
patient.room: MICU Bed 5

vitals.time: Yesterday (Day 7 — recovering from initial septic shock)
Today 0630 (Day 8 — NEW FEVER + rigors during nursing assessment)
0900 (CURRENT — 2.5 hr into evaluation; line removed; antibiotics broadened; norepi initiated)

labs.n: BLOOD CULTURES (the diagnostic anchor — DIFFERENTIAL TIME-TO-POSITIVITY)
labs.v: Two sets drawn AT 0700 — ONE from the central line (right IJ port), ONE from peripheral venipuncture; LINE blood culture positive at 6 hr (Gram-positive cocci in clusters); PERIPHERAL blood culture positive at 9 hr (same Gram-positive cocci in clusters); Gram stain consistent with Staphylococcus species; final speciation/sensitivity 24–72 hr
labs.r: DIFFERENTIAL TIME-TO-POSITIVITY (DTP): if line culture positive ≥2 HOURS BEFORE peripheral culture (with same organism), suggests LINE is the SOURCE (more organisms in line lumen = faster growth). DTP 6 hr vs 9 hr = 3 hr earlier line = CLABSI very likely. CONFIRMS need to REMOVE LINE as source control

labs.v: (1) Central line in place >2 calendar days at time of infection (LINE PRESENT x 8 days ✓); (2) Bloodstream infection with recognized pathogen from one or more blood cultures AND organism not related to another infection site (S. aureus growing in blood, no other apparent source ✓); (3) Patient has fever, chills, or hypotension (fever 39.4°C, hypotension 98/58, tachycardia ✓) — clinical criteria MET
labs.r: CDC NHSN definition for surveillance; primary BSI not secondary to another infection site; line in place >2 days. Distinguish from PRIMARY BSI from other source (UTI, pneumonia, intra-abdominal) — culture each potential source. CLABSI is a HOSPITAL-ACQUIRED INFECTION (HAI) tracked for quality metrics and reimbursement

labs.n: BUNDLE COMPLIANCE BREACHES identified on review
labs.v: (1) LINE DRESSING wet/soiled and not changed within 24 hr (last documented dressing change was 3 days ago — should be q7 days OR sooner if wet/soiled/loose; CHG-impregnated dressing fell off but was not replaced); (2) HUB DISINFECTION not consistently performed before each line access (multiple shifts no documentation of 'scrub the hub 15 seconds with alcohol or CHG before each access'); (3) DAILY NECESSITY REVIEW not consistently documented (line was used for vasopressors Days 1–3, but Days 4–8 only used for routine maintenance fluids that could go peripherally — should have been removed); (4) tubing changed q72h appropriately ✓; (5) hand hygiene compliance documented appropriately ✓; (6) chlorhexidine bathing daily NOT consistently performed (only 3 of last 6 days)
labs.r: CDC/IHI/SHEA Central Line Bundle: hand hygiene, MAXIMAL STERILE BARRIER precautions during insertion, CHLORHEXIDINE skin antisepsis for insertion + maintenance, OPTIMAL SITE SELECTION (subclavian > IJ > femoral when feasible), DAILY REVIEW of necessity, hub disinfection before each access ('scrub the hub' 15 sec), DRESSING change q7 days or sooner if wet/soiled, dressing-impregnated CHG for adults, tubing change q72–96h (q24h for blood/lipids/propofol), daily CHG bathing in adults

labs.v: Right INTERNAL JUGULAR (IJ) triple-lumen central venous catheter placed Day 1 of admission for septic shock; in place x 8 days; was used for vasopressors Days 1–3 (now off pressors yesterday until new fever today); last 4 days only used for routine maintenance IV fluids, intermittent IV antibiotics, daily K replacement
labs.r: Site preference (CDC, lowest CLABSI risk): SUBCLAVIAN > internal jugular > FEMORAL (highest CLABSI risk). Duration of line is single biggest modifiable risk factor — DAILY REVIEW: 'does this patient still need a central line?' Once peripheral access feasible + no need for central infusions (vasopressors, TPN, irritants), REMOVE

labs.v: 0800: line REMOVED, tip sent for semi-quantitative culture (>15 colony-forming units of same organism = CLABSI confirmation); new peripheral IV (20g R AC) placed for ongoing access; broad-spectrum antibiotics broadened from ceftriaxone (PNA coverage) to add VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading dose then 15 mg/kg q12h titrated to trough 15–20 mcg/mL (MRSA coverage pending sensitivities); norepinephrine started at 4 mcg/min for septic hypotension; lactate, CMP, CBC, ABG, repeat blood cultures sent; volume resuscitation 1 L NS bolus given
labs.r: LINE REMOVAL is the DEFINITIVE source control for CLABSI in most cases (especially S. aureus, Candida, gram-negatives). Some salvageable infections (CoNS, certain organisms) may allow antibiotic lock therapy with line retention — RARE in MICU. Sepsis bundle: cultures BEFORE antibiotics, antibiotics within 1 hr of recognition, fluid resuscitation 30 mL/kg, lactate trend, vasopressor for MAP ≥65, source control

labs.v: qSOFA 2 (HR >100 + altered mental status); SIRS criteria met (T >38.3, HR >90, RR >20, WBC TBD); LACTATE 3.8 (up from 1.6 yesterday) — confirms SEPSIS; meeting criteria for SEPTIC SHOCK if requires vasopressor + lactate >2 after fluid resuscitation (which she does)
labs.r: Sepsis-3 definition: life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection; SOFA score increase ≥2. Septic shock: persistent hypotension requiring vasopressor for MAP ≥65 + lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Hour-1 bundle: cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressor, lactate, source control

labs.v: CXR shows resolving R lower lobe infiltrate from original pneumonia (no new infiltrate); UA: no LE, no nitrites, clear (no UTI); foley removed POD 4; no abdominal tenderness or diarrhea (no C diff or intra-abdominal source); no decubitus injury; no skin/soft tissue source; no IV site cellulitis (the OLD peripheral IV is patent and clean); no nuchal rigidity (no meningitis); no joint findings (no septic arthritis)
labs.r: Ruling out alternative sources confirms CLABSI as primary source. Important step in CLABSI surveillance — if another source explains the bacteremia, it's not classified as CLABSI (secondary BSI from primary source like UTI or pneumonia)

labs.v: Admitted 8 days ago for community-acquired pneumonia with septic shock; intubated POD 1, extubated POD 4; vasopressors POD 1–3; on ceftriaxone + azithromycin (CAP regimen Day 8); blood cultures POD 1–2 grew Strep pneumoniae (de-escalated to ceftriaxone monotherapy); was recovering well until today's fever
labs.r: Recovery trajectory interrupted by new HAI. Critical to distinguish from the original sepsis (which had been resolving). New fever + new positive blood cultures + line in place = think CLABSI

labs.v: Type 2 DM (well-controlled, HbA1c 7.2%); HTN on lisinopril; HFpEF EF 55%; mild CKD baseline Cr 1.2 (admission Cr 1.0 after volume); BMI 28; former smoker (quit 10 yr ago); no immunosuppression; up to date on PCV13 + PPSV23 + flu shot
labs.r: Standard PMH; no overwhelming immunocompromise; standard CAP risk factors. DM is a known CLABSI risk factor (hyperglycemia impairs immune function). HbA1c reasonable; tight glucose control inpatient (140–180 per SCC)

labs.v: WBC 16.8 with bands 14% (left shift) — up from 11.4 yesterday; Hgb 10.4 (chronic mild anemia, stable); Plt 248; CRP 18.2 (elevated; was 4.2 yesterday); procalcitonin 4.8 (elevated, supports bacterial infection); lactate 3.8 (was 1.6 yesterday)
labs.r: Sepsis lab pattern: leukocytosis with left shift, elevated CRP/procalcitonin, elevated lactate. Trending values informative — yesterday was recovering, today is acute decompensation. Procalcitonin >2 suggests bacterial infection; trends help with antibiotic stewardship

labs.v: Original ceftriaxone + azithromycin for CAP being continued; ADDED VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading + 15 mg/kg q12h for MRSA coverage pending sensitivities (vanco trough 15–20 mcg/mL target); pharmacy + ID coordination; will narrow once organism speciated and sensitivities back
labs.r: Empiric coverage for staph (vancomycin = MRSA + MSSA coverage). If MSSA back, switch to NAFCILLIN or CEFAZOLIN (cidal, better for MSSA than vanco). If MRSA back, continue vanco. If CoNS (coagulase-negative staph) back and contaminant-like (only 1 set positive), may be skin contamination — repeat cultures. Treatment duration: 14 days minimum from first NEGATIVE blood culture for S. aureus bacteremia (longer for complicated cases — endocarditis, abscess, prosthetic device, persistent bacteremia)

labs.n: Echocardiogram (assessment for endocarditis given S. aureus bacteremia)
labs.v: TTE planned for tomorrow once stable (S. aureus bacteremia ALWAYS prompts echo for endocarditis evaluation — vegetations, valve dysfunction); TEE more sensitive if TTE inconclusive or high suspicion
labs.r: S. aureus bacteremia is high-risk for endocarditis (5–25% incidence); always evaluate. TTE first; TEE if inconclusive, prosthetic valve, persistent bacteremia, or new murmur. Endocarditis changes treatment duration (4–6 weeks vs 2 weeks) and requires longer hospitalization

labs.v: Repeat blood cultures sent at 0800 (post line removal); plan q24–48h until 2 sets NEGATIVE; persistent bacteremia (positive >72 hr after appropriate antibiotic + source control) raises concern for endocarditis, abscess, retained source, or antibiotic failure
labs.r: Persistent bacteremia management: extend antibiotic course, evaluate for endocarditis (TEE), evaluate for metastatic abscesses (MRI/CT), reassess organism susceptibilities, consider ID consult for non-standard regimens

nursesNote: 0900 — 72 y/o male MICU Day 8 admitted 8 days ago for community-acquired pneumococcal pneumonia with septic shock; had recovered well from initial sepsis (extubated POD 4, off pressors POD 5, transferring to step-down today as planned). NEW FEVER 39.4°C with rigors at 0630 during routine nursing assessment, accompanied by tachycardia 118, hypotension 98/58 (down from 128/72 yesterday), tachypnea 26, mild altered mental status (drowsy, slow responses). qSOFA 2 + SIRS criteria met + lactate 3.8 (up from 1.6 yesterday) = SEPSIS (likely SEPTIC SHOCK as norepinephrine started for MAP ≥65 with lactate >2). RAPID SEPSIS WORKUP: Two sets of blood cultures sent at 0700 — ONE from RIGHT IJ central line port, ONE from peripheral venipuncture; CXR showed resolving R lower lobe infiltrate (no new pneumonia); UA neg (no UTI; foley removed POD 4); no abdominal or other infection signs. LINE blood culture turned positive at 6 hr (Gram-positive cocci in clusters); PERIPHERAL blood culture positive at 9 hr (same organism); DIFFERENTIAL TIME-TO-POSITIVITY of 3 hr (line ≥2 hr before peripheral) STRONGLY SUGGESTS the line is the SOURCE — CLABSI very likely. Gram stain consistent with Staphylococcus; speciation pending (likely Staph aureus given Gram-pos cocci in clusters in clinical context). LINE REMOVED at 0800; tip sent for semi-quantitative culture (>15 CFU same organism confirms CLABSI). New peripheral IV (20g R AC) placed. Antibiotics broadened: original ceftriaxone for CAP continued; ADDED VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading dose at 0815 (then 15 mg/kg q12h with trough target 15–20 mcg/mL) for empiric MRSA coverage pending sensitivities. Volume resuscitation 1 L NS bolus given; norepinephrine 4 mcg/min for MAP ≥65; lactate to be rechecked in 2 hr to assess clearance. Repeat blood cultures at 0800 (post-line-removal) and will continue q24–48h until 2 sets negative. TTE planned tomorrow once stable (S. aureus bacteremia ALWAYS prompts echo evaluation for endocarditis — 5–25% incidence). REVIEW OF CARE revealed CLABSI BUNDLE COMPLIANCE BREACHES: (1) the CHG-impregnated line dressing fell off Day 5 and was not replaced (just covered with regular dressing); not changed in past 3 days despite being soiled; (2) hub disinfection ('scrub the hub' 15 seconds with alcohol or CHG before each line access) inconsistently documented across multiple shifts; (3) daily necessity review for the line not documented Days 4–8 — the line was being used only for routine maintenance fluids and intermittent antibiotics for the past 4 days, which could have gone peripherally; should have been removed by Day 4–5; (4) daily CHG bathing only documented 3 of last 6 days. Bundle elements that were maintained: hand hygiene compliance, tubing change q72h, sterile technique for med admin (mostly). RCA (root cause analysis) to be scheduled within 48–72 hr (CLABSI is a hospital-acquired infection tracked as a quality metric and triggers institutional review); RCA team will include critical care, infection prevention, nurse manager, pharmacy, central line team, quality department; goal = system improvements, not individual punishment (Just Culture framework). The nursing team has been educated on the bundle breach findings: education + workflow redesign + ongoing audit. Patient and family informed of new infection development (compassionate, honest disclosure — CANDOR model); medical alert about S. aureus bacteremia + CLABSI documented in chart; ID consultation requested. Anticipated course: vancomycin x at least 14 days from first NEGATIVE blood culture for S. aureus bacteremia (longer if endocarditis or complicated); if MSSA back on speciation, switch to nafcillin or cefazolin (cidal, better for MSSA than vanco). If a new central line is needed for ongoing care (e.g., if pressors persist or TPN needed), place at NEW SITE (subclavian preferred > new IJ > femoral last choice) with maximum sterile barrier precautions + CHG prep + CHG-impregnated dressing + bundle compliance. Family at bedside (wife and son), informed and supportive; ID + critical care coordinating; case management for discharge planning once bacteremia cleared and antibiotic course determined.
providerOrders: STAT REMOVE the central line (Day 8 right IJ triple-lumen); send tip for SEMI-QUANTITATIVE CULTURE (>15 CFU same organism = CLABSI confirmation); document removal time + site assessment (no redness, exudate, induration)

Establish PERIPHERAL IV access (20g R AC placed) for ongoing fluids and medications; reassess need for central access — if ongoing pressors, TPN, irritants, multiple incompatible drips, place NEW central line at DIFFERENT site (SUBCLAVIAN preferred > IJ > femoral; lowest to highest CLABSI risk) with MAXIMUM STERILE BARRIER precautions + CHG prep + CHG-impregnated dressing + full bundle compliance

Empiric antibiotic broadening: continue CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV daily (CAP coverage), ADD VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading dose then 15 mg/kg q12h with trough target 15–20 mcg/mL (MRSA + MSSA empiric coverage pending sensitivities); pharmacy + ID consultation; renally dose if Cr changes
Once organism speciated and sensitivities back (24–72 hr): de-escalate or adjust
MSSA: switch to NAFCILLIN 2 g IV q4h OR CEFAZOLIN 2 g IV q8h (cidal beta-lactams; better outcomes than vancomycin for MSSA bacteremia)
MRSA: continue VANCOMYCIN, or alternatives DAPTOMYCIN or LINEZOLID per ID
CoNS (coagulase-negative staph, especially Staph epidermidis): often skin contaminant; if true infection, vancomycin
Gram-negative: broaden if appropriate (cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or carbapenem); de-escalate per sensitivities

TREATMENT DURATION: Staph aureus bacteremia = MINIMUM 14 days from first NEGATIVE blood culture (longer 4–6 weeks if endocarditis, deep tissue abscess, prosthetic device, persistent bacteremia); CoNS often 7–14 days; gram-negatives 7–14 days uncomplicated

REPEAT BLOOD CULTURES q24–48h until 2 sets NEGATIVE; PERSISTENT BACTEREMIA (>72 hr after appropriate antibiotic + source control) raises concern for endocarditis, abscess, retained source, antibiotic failure — expand workup (TTE then TEE, MRI/CT for abscess, repeat ID consult)

TTE (transthoracic echocardiogram) — order today for tomorrow once stable; S. aureus bacteremia ALWAYS prompts echo (5–25% endocarditis incidence); TEE if TTE inconclusive, prosthetic valve, persistent bacteremia, or new murmur

SEPSIS BUNDLE (Hour-1): cultures already sent (DONE), antibiotics broadened within 1 hr (DONE), FLUID RESUSCITATION 30 mL/kg crystalloid (1 L bolus given, continue per response; cautious in HFpEF — reassess between boluses with US/PE); LACTATE recheck q2h until clearing; VASOPRESSOR (norepinephrine 4 mcg/min IV; titrate for MAP ≥65); SOURCE CONTROL (line removed)
Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP and ABG access; SpO2; q15-min vitals during active resuscitation then q1h
Strict I&O hourly; UOP target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; daily weights; trend Cr (sepsis-related AKI risk)
Lactate q2h until <2 mmol/L sustained; ABG; BMP, CBC, procalcitonin daily; ID input on duration
Glycemic control: insulin gtt for stress hyperglycemia (target BG 140–180 per SCC); home metformin held during acute illness
DVT prophylaxis: continue SCDs; LMWH or heparin SubQ if no bleeding contraindication; resume once stable
GI prophylaxis: pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily (mechanically ventilated previously; now extubated and stable, can reassess); reduce risk of stress ulcer
Family notification (CANDOR model — compassionate, honest disclosure): wife + son informed of new infection development, line removal, antibiotic changes; questions answered; chaplain available

INFECTION PREVENTION + RCA (Root Cause Analysis): CLABSI is a hospital-acquired infection tracked as quality metric; RCA scheduled within 48–72 hr; multidisciplinary team (critical care, infection prevention, nurse manager, pharmacy, central line team, quality department); identify system contributors to bundle breaches; develop action items; JUST CULTURE framework (system fix, not individual punishment)

BUNDLE EDUCATION + AUDIT: re-emphasize CLABSI prevention bundle to nursing team: (1) HAND HYGIENE before any line contact, (2) MAXIMUM STERILE BARRIER precautions during insertion (mask, cap, sterile gown, sterile gloves, large drape), (3) CHLORHEXIDINE skin antisepsis for insertion + maintenance, (4) OPTIMAL SITE SELECTION (subclavian > IJ > femoral; avoid femoral when possible), (5) DAILY REVIEW of necessity ('does this patient still need a central line today?'), (6) 'SCRUB THE HUB' 15 seconds with alcohol or CHG before EVERY line access — including for med admin, blood draws, IV fluid changes, (7) DRESSING change q7 days OR sooner if wet/soiled/loose; use CHG-impregnated dressing for adults, (8) TUBING change q72–96h (q24h for blood/lipid/propofol/parenteral nutrition), (9) DAILY CHG BATHING in adults; education + workflow redesign + ongoing audit + feedback to staff

Patient and family education: explain what happened, what is being done, the new antibiotic course, expected recovery trajectory, signs of complications, when to return for outpatient follow-up; PCP and outpatient ID coordination

Discharge planning early: ID follow-up for completion of antibiotic course (often PICC line at home for IV vanco, OR transition to oral if appropriate organism + sensitivity); home health for IV antibiotic administration if PICC; outpatient TTE follow-up if endocarditis cannot be ruled out; PCP within 1 week of discharge

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: NEW fever 39.4°C with rigors + hypotension 98/58 + tachycardia 118 + altered mental status on a patient who was recovering — septic decompensation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — interrupted recovery trajectory. Sudden onset of fever + sepsis signs in a previously-recovering patient triggers immediate workup for new infection source. qSOFA + SIRS + lactate trend defines sepsis severity. Hour-1 bundle: cultures, antibiotics, fluids, lactate, vasopressor.

opts.text: Right IJ triple-lumen central line in place x 8 days — primary suspicion for source as CLABSI
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the likely source. Central line duration is the single biggest modifiable CLABSI risk factor. Daily necessity review SHOULD have removed this line earlier (Days 4–8 it was only used for routine maintenance fluids — peripheral-deliverable).

opts.text: DIFFERENTIAL TIME-TO-POSITIVITY: line blood culture positive at 6 hr, peripheral at 9 hr (line ≥2 hr earlier) — strongly suggests LINE is the source
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms CLABSI diagnosis. DTP >2 hr (line earlier than peripheral) = more organisms in line lumen than peripheral blood = line is the source. Confirms need to REMOVE LINE as source control + speciate organism.

opts.text: BUNDLE COMPLIANCE BREACHES: dressing wet/soiled past 24 hr, hub disinfection inconsistent, daily necessity review not done, CHG bathing only 3 of last 6 days
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the root cause. Each breach individually raises CLABSI risk; together they cascade. RCA + bundle re-education + ongoing audit are the system response. Just Culture framework (system fix, not individual punishment) for the involved staff.

opts.text: Lactate 3.8 (up from 1.6 yesterday) + qSOFA 2 + SIRS criteria met — confirms sepsis; likely septic shock with norepinephrine started
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines severity. Sepsis-3 definition met; septic shock criteria (persistent hypotension requiring vasopressor + lactate >2 after fluid resuscitation). Hour-1 sepsis bundle ongoing. Trend lactate q2h.

opts.text: Remove the line as definitive source control; send tip for semi-quantitative culture (>15 CFU same organism = CLABSI confirmation)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — definitive intervention. Antibiotics + source control = sepsis treatment foundations. Line removal for CLABSI in most cases (especially S. aureus, Candida, gram-negatives). Line salvage with antibiotic lock only for specific organisms in specific clinical contexts.

opts.text: Empiric VANCOMYCIN added for MRSA coverage (Gram-positive cocci in clusters consistent with Staph); continue CEFTRIAXONE for original CAP; broaden if Gram stain or culture changes
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — empiric coverage pending sensitivities. Hour-1 antibiotic administration. Will narrow once organism speciated and sensitivities back. MSSA: switch to nafcillin/cefazolin (better than vanco). MRSA: continue vanco.

opts.text: S. aureus bacteremia (suspected) — ALWAYS evaluate for endocarditis with TTE (then TEE if needed); 5–25% incidence
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — important complication assessment. S. aureus bacteremia is high-risk for endocarditis; always evaluate. Endocarditis changes treatment duration (4–6 weeks vs 2 weeks). TEE more sensitive if TTE inconclusive or high suspicion.

opts.text: Need for new central line vs peripheral only — assess based on ongoing clinical needs (pressors, TPN, irritants, multiple drips); if needed, place at DIFFERENT SITE with full bundle compliance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — re-access decision. If new line needed: SUBCLAVIAN > IJ > femoral (avoid femoral when possible); maximum sterile barrier; CHG prep; CHG-impregnated dressing; bundle from start. Reassess daily for removal.

opts.text: Repeat blood cultures sent at 0800 (post-line removal); plan q24–48h until 2 sets negative; persistent bacteremia >72 hr after antibiotics + source control = worry for endocarditis, abscess, retained source
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — clearance monitoring. Negative cultures define when to count from for treatment duration. Persistent bacteremia triggers expanded workup (TEE, imaging for abscess, ID reconsultation, alternative regimens).

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed. Helpful for empiric vancomycin (no PCN allergy concern).

opts.text: Recovering R lower lobe infiltrate on CXR (original CAP)
opts.rat: Reassuring — resolving prior infection, not new source. Rules out new pneumonia as alternative source. Continue current CAP coverage; assess for resolution.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What is happening epidemiologically and clinically?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles of CLABSI prevention and management.

opts.text: CLABSI (central line-associated bloodstream infection) CDC/NHSN surveillance definition: (1) central line in place >2 calendar days at time of infection, (2) bloodstream infection with recognized pathogen, (3) organism NOT related to another infection site, (4) patient has fever, chills, OR hypotension
opts.rat: CORRECT — surveillance definition. Primary BSI not secondary to another source. Tracked as HAI for quality and reimbursement. Cost ~$45,000 per CLABSI; mortality 12–25%. Prevention bundles reduce CLABSI rates by 50–90%.

opts.text: CDC/IHI/SHEA CENTRAL LINE BUNDLE (insertion + maintenance): (1) HAND HYGIENE, (2) MAXIMUM STERILE BARRIER precautions during insertion (mask, cap, sterile gown, sterile gloves, large drape), (3) CHLORHEXIDINE skin antisepsis, (4) OPTIMAL SITE SELECTION (subclavian > IJ > femoral), (5) DAILY REVIEW of necessity, (6) HUB DISINFECTION ('scrub the hub' 15 sec before each access), (7) DRESSING change q7 days or sooner if wet/soiled, (8) TUBING change q72–96h (q24h for blood/lipids/propofol/PN), (9) DAILY CHG BATHING in adults
opts.rat: CORRECT — bundle elements. The Pronovost ICU CLABSI study (Michigan Keystone Project) showed bundle adherence reduced CLABSI rates by ~66%. Each element matters; together they prevent most CLABSIs.

opts.text: SITE SELECTION risk (lowest to highest CLABSI): SUBCLAVIAN < INTERNAL JUGULAR < FEMORAL; SUBCLAVIAN PREFERRED when feasible (lowest CLABSI + lowest DVT risk); femoral AVOIDED when possible (highest CLABSI + highest DVT risk); ultrasound guidance for IJ standard of care
opts.rat: CORRECT — site selection drives risk. Subclavian carries higher pneumothorax risk on insertion but lowest infection risk. Femoral easier to place urgently but highest infection. IJ middle ground, ultrasound-guided. Each site has trade-offs.

opts.text: DAILY REVIEW of LINE NECESSITY — 'Does this patient still need a central line today?' — single biggest modifiable risk factor; remove ASAP once peripheral access feasible AND no need for central infusions (vasopressors, TPN, irritants like vancomycin, multiple incompatible drips)
opts.rat: CORRECT — most-missed element. Duration of line is the single biggest risk factor. Daily multidisciplinary rounding to ask 'why is this line still in?' is the standard. Documentation of necessity required. Lines often stay in longer than needed out of habit.

opts.text: 'SCRUB THE HUB' protocol: disinfect each line access port with alcohol or CHG for 15 SECONDS (60 seconds for needleless connectors per some manufacturers) before EVERY line access — including for med admin, blood draws, fluid changes; allow to dry before access
opts.rat: CORRECT — frequently-missed step. Skin/hub organisms can be introduced into the line with each access. Scrubbing 15 sec mechanically removes biofilm. Each unscrubbed access is a potential introduction event. Documentation + audit.

opts.text: DIFFERENTIAL TIME-TO-POSITIVITY (DTP) for CLABSI diagnosis: blood cultures drawn SIMULTANEOUSLY from line AND peripheral; if LINE culture turns positive ≥2 HOURS BEFORE peripheral (with same organism), the LINE is likely the source; if simultaneous or peripheral earlier, source is elsewhere
opts.rat: CORRECT — diagnostic strategy. The DTP is based on bacterial growth kinetics: more organisms in line lumen → faster growth → positive earlier. Useful for distinguishing CLABSI from secondary BSI. Semi-quantitative tip culture (>15 CFU same organism) also helps.

opts.text: LINE REMOVAL is DEFINITIVE SOURCE CONTROL for most CLABSI cases (especially S. aureus, Candida, P. aeruginosa, multidrug-resistant gram-negatives); LINE SALVAGE with antibiotic lock therapy reserved for certain organisms (CoNS, some gram-negatives) in specific clinical contexts (limited vascular access, dialysis catheters)
opts.rat: CORRECT — management principle. Antibiotics alone without source control = treatment failure for most CLABSIs. New line at DIFFERENT SITE if continued central access needed. Salvage only with ID input + specific organisms.

opts.text: S. AUREUS BACTEREMIA: ALWAYS evaluate for endocarditis with TTE (then TEE if inconclusive or high suspicion); 5–25% endocarditis incidence; treatment duration MINIMUM 14 DAYS for uncomplicated bacteremia (from first NEGATIVE blood culture); 4–6 WEEKS for endocarditis, deep abscess, prosthetic device, persistent bacteremia
opts.rat: CORRECT — workup and duration. S. aureus bacteremia is high-morbidity; ID consultation reduces mortality. Repeat blood cultures q48h until clear; persistent bacteremia triggers expanded workup. Right-sided endocarditis (line-associated) often tricuspid; left-sided more morbid.

opts.text: Just leave the line in and treat with antibiotics — removing the line is too invasive and risks losing access
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Antibiotics without source control = treatment failure for most CLABSI (especially S. aureus). Line removal is definitive. Loss of access is a relative concern; new line at different site with full bundle is the answer if ongoing need.

opts.text: Discipline the responsible nurses for the bundle breaches with formal disciplinary action and write-ups
opts.rat: ⚠️ Anti-Just Culture. Bundle breaches typically reflect SYSTEM failures (workflow, staffing, training, supply availability), not individual failures. Just Culture framework: human error + at-risk behavior = coachable + system fix; reckless behavior = disciplinary (rare). Punishing breaches damages safety culture and reduces future reporting. RCA + system improvement is the right response.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Septic Shock / Hemodynamic Compromise r/t CLABSI with active bacteremia and inadequate source control until line is removed
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Sepsis is the imminent killer. Hour-1 bundle: cultures, antibiotics, fluids, lactate, vasopressor, SOURCE CONTROL (line removal). MAP ≥65 target. Trend lactate. Continuous monitoring; ICU level of care.

opts.text: Risk for Continued / Worsening Bacteremia r/t central line as ongoing source; need for line removal as source control
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Antibiotics alone without source control = treatment failure for most CLABSI (especially S. aureus). LINE REMOVAL is the definitive intervention. Tip culture confirms diagnosis. New line at different site if continued central access needed.

opts.text: Risk for Endocarditis / Metastatic Infection r/t Staph aureus bacteremia (5–25% endocarditis incidence)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. S. aureus bacteremia ALWAYS prompts TTE for endocarditis evaluation. Persistent bacteremia + endocarditis = 4–6 week course vs 2-week minimum. ID consultation reduces mortality. TEE if TTE inconclusive.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit / System Failure r/t CLABSI bundle compliance breaches identified during care review
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. RCA scheduled within 48–72 hr; multidisciplinary team; Just Culture framework. Bundle re-education + workflow redesign + ongoing audit + feedback to staff. This prevents the next CLABSI.

opts.text: Family Distress / Need for Honest Disclosure r/t complication during admission, change in trajectory
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. CANDOR model — honest, prompt, compassionate disclosure. Family understanding of trajectory and treatment course. Chaplain and social work support.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: REMOVE the central line; send tip for semi-quantitative culture (>15 CFU same organism = CLABSI confirmation); establish peripheral IV access; place new central line at DIFFERENT SITE with full bundle compliance only if ongoing central access need
opts.rat: DEFINITIVE SOURCE CONTROL for CLABSI. Antibiotics + line removal = standard management. Tip culture confirms diagnosis. New line decisions based on ongoing clinical need; if placed, full bundle from start.

opts.text: Leave the line in place and treat with antibiotics alone — line removal is too invasive
opts.rat: ⚠️ Antibiotics alone without source control = treatment failure for most CLABSI (especially S. aureus, Candida, gram-negatives). Line removal is the standard. Salvage with antibiotic lock only for specific organisms in specific contexts (with ID guidance).

opts.text: Empiric VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading then 15 mg/kg q12h (trough 15–20 mcg/mL) for MRSA coverage pending sensitivities; CONTINUE ceftriaxone for original CAP
opts.rat: Hour-1 antibiotic with broad empiric coverage. Vanco for MRSA + MSSA. De-escalate once sensitivities back (MSSA: switch to nafcillin/cefazolin; MRSA: continue vanco).

opts.text: Send blood cultures from BOTH the line and a peripheral site simultaneously to assess for differential time-to-positivity
opts.rat: Standard CLABSI workup. DTP >2 hr (line earlier) suggests line is source. Standardized workup. Two sets minimum; can repeat from peripheral after line removal for clearance assessment.

opts.text: TTE (transthoracic echocardiogram) to evaluate for endocarditis given S. aureus bacteremia
opts.rat: S. aureus bacteremia ALWAYS prompts echo (5–25% endocarditis incidence). TTE first; TEE if inconclusive, prosthetic valve, persistent bacteremia, or new murmur. Endocarditis changes treatment duration.

opts.text: Sepsis Hour-1 bundle: cultures (DONE), antibiotics broadened (DONE), fluid resuscitation 30 mL/kg, lactate q2h, vasopressor for MAP ≥65, source control (line removal)
opts.rat: Standard sepsis management. Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 bundle. Cautious fluid resuscitation in HFpEF (reassess between boluses; PE/lung U/S helpful). Source control essential.

opts.text: Repeat blood cultures q24–48h until 2 sets NEGATIVE; persistent bacteremia >72 hr triggers expanded workup (TEE, imaging for abscess, ID reconsultation)
opts.rat: Clearance monitoring. Defines when to count treatment duration from. Persistent bacteremia changes management substantially. ID involvement throughout.

opts.text: RCA (Root Cause Analysis) scheduled within 48–72 hr; multidisciplinary team (critical care, infection prevention, nurse manager, pharmacy, central line team, quality); Just Culture framework (system fix not individual punishment)
opts.rat: Standard institutional response to HAI. Identify system contributors. Action items with owners and deadlines. Track outcomes (CLABSI rate per 1000 line-days). Prevent the next event.

opts.text: Disciplinary action against the responsible nurses for the bundle breaches
opts.rat: ⚠️ Anti-Just Culture. Bundle breaches typically reflect SYSTEM failures (workflow, staffing, training, supply). Just Culture: human error + at-risk behavior = coachable + system fix; reckless behavior = disciplinary (rare). Discipline damages safety culture, reduces future reporting.

opts.text: CLABSI bundle re-education for the unit: scrub the hub 15 sec, dressing change q7 days or sooner if wet/soiled, daily necessity review, CHG bathing daily; workflow redesign; ongoing audit + feedback to staff
opts.rat: System improvement response. Bundle adherence reduces CLABSI rates 50–90%. Education + workflow + audit + feedback creates sustainable change. Quarterly audits; positive reinforcement; barrier identification.

opts.text: Family notification (CANDOR — Communication AND Optimal Resolution): wife + son informed of new infection development, line removal, antibiotic changes; questions answered; chaplain available
opts.rat: Honest, prompt, compassionate disclosure. Improves family understanding and trust. Document conversation. Chaplain and social work support.

opts.text: Treatment duration MINIMUM 14 days from first NEGATIVE blood culture for S. aureus bacteremia uncomplicated; 4–6 weeks if endocarditis, deep abscess, prosthetic device, persistent bacteremia; ID consultation guides individualized duration
opts.rat: Standard duration. ID consultation reduces mortality in S. aureus bacteremia. Outpatient continuation often requires PICC line or home IV antibiotic via OPAT program. PCP coordination.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Send TWO blood cultures simultaneously (one from line port, one peripheral); STAT lactate; basic sepsis workup (CBC, BMP, ABG, CRP, procalcitonin, UA, CXR); document sepsis criteria
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Cultures BEFORE antibiotics is the standard sepsis bundle. Line + peripheral pair for DTP assessment. Stat lactate confirms sepsis severity. Identify sepsis criteria for documentation and trajectory.

opts.text: Broaden antibiotics within 1 hr: add VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading dose; continue CEFTRIAXONE; begin fluid resuscitation 30 mL/kg crystalloid (cautious in HFpEF, reassess between boluses); start norepinephrine for MAP ≥65 if hypotension persists after fluids
opts.rat: #2 — Hour-1 sepsis bundle. Antibiotic within 1 hr of recognition reduces mortality. Empiric vanco for Gram-positive cocci in clusters. Fluid + pressor for hemodynamic support.

opts.text: REMOVE the central line as source control; send tip for semi-quantitative culture; establish peripheral IV access; determine need for new central line at DIFFERENT SITE
opts.rat: #3 — Source control. Definitive intervention for CLABSI. Tip culture confirms diagnosis. New line at different site if ongoing central access need (subclavian > IJ > femoral); full bundle compliance from start.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP and ABG; q15-min vitals during resuscitation; foley if not in place; strict I&O; lactate q2h until clearing; daily blood cultures until 2 sets negative; TTE tomorrow once stable
opts.rat: #4 — Vigilant monitoring and complication assessment. Lactate trend = response to treatment. Repeat cultures define duration. TTE for endocarditis (S. aureus bacteremia).

opts.text: RCA scheduled within 48–72 hr; bundle re-education + workflow redesign + ongoing audit; family notification (CANDOR); patient education; discharge planning with ID follow-up + PICC for IV antibiotic completion if needed
opts.rat: #5 — System improvement + sustained care. RCA prevents the next CLABSI. Bundle education is institutional. Family disclosure. Discharge structure with ID + PCP coordination.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 9 (24 hr after line removal + vancomycin): T 37.8°C; HR 92; BP 124/74 off pressors; lactate 1.8 (down from 3.8); repeat blood cultures negative at 24 hr; organism speciated as MSSA → switched from vancomycin to NAFCILLIN 2 g IV q4h (better for MSSA)
opts.rat: Excellent response to source control + antibiotics. MSSA switch to cidal beta-lactam improves outcomes vs vancomycin. Repeat cultures clearing. Continue antibiotic course 14 days from first negative culture; TTE for endocarditis.

opts.text: Day 10: TTE negative for vegetations; transferred to step-down; tolerating PO; discharge planning to complete IV nafcillin course at home via PICC (OPAT program); home health for daily infusion; ID + PCP follow-up scheduled within 1 week
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. TTE rules out endocarditis (TEE not needed). Transition to outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT). Coordinate PICC, home health, ID and PCP follow-up. Continue 14 days total from first negative culture.

opts.text: 1-month follow-up: completed 14-day IV nafcillin course; recovered well; CXR clear; PICC removed; PCP coordinated outpatient care; family understanding of what happened; institutional RCA completed with 6 action items (CHG-impregnated dressings stocked, daily necessity review automated in EHR, hub disinfection audit weekly, simulation training for new RNs)
opts.rat: Sustained recovery and institutional learning. RCA action items prevent future CLABSI. Outpatient follow-up structure. Family informed and supportive. Continue monitoring CLABSI rates.

opts.text: Day 11: persistent bacteremia despite line removal + 4 days of vancomycin; blood cultures still positive; ID consultation expanded: TEE shows mitral valve VEGETATION 8 mm — endocarditis confirmed; antibiotic extended to 6 weeks; consider cardiac surgery consultation
opts.rat: Endocarditis complication. Persistent bacteremia >72 hr after appropriate antibiotic + source control triggers expanded workup. TEE more sensitive than TTE for endocarditis. Treatment 4–6 weeks; cardiac surgery consultation for valve assessment, possible repair/replacement; cardiology + ID + CT surgery coordination.

opts.text: Day 10: develops new mid-thoracic back pain and fever; MRI spine reveals VERTEBRAL OSTEOMYELITIS at T7–T8 (metastatic infection from bacteremia); treatment extended to 6–8 weeks IV antibiotic; neurosurgery consultation for evaluation
opts.rat: Metastatic infection from S. aureus bacteremia. Bone, joint, deep abscess, endocarditis are all possible. Reassess thoroughly for all metastatic sites if persistent symptoms. Extended treatment course; specialty consultation. ID + ortho/neurosurgery.

opts.text: RCA reveals system contributors: CHG-impregnated dressings frequently out of stock on unit; EHR daily necessity review checkbox often skipped; nurse-to-patient ratios 1:3 in MICU on nights frequently 1:4 due to staffing shortages; new RN orientation does not include CLABSI bundle simulation
opts.rat: Multiple latent system failures identified. Action items: (1) CHG dressings stocked + 2x weekly check; (2) EHR daily necessity review hard-stop, can't close shift without completing; (3) nurse staffing review with management; (4) new RN simulation training for CLABSI bundle. System improvements should reduce future CLABSI rates.

opts.text: Family meeting at Day 9: wife + son verbalize understanding of CLABSI, what was done, expected antibiotic duration, signs of complications to watch for at home; PCP coordination confirmed; wife will be informed advocate at all future medical encounters
opts.rat: Successful communication and discharge preparation. Family understanding + advocacy reduces future risk. Document conversation. Open visitation; chaplain available; social work coordination for OPAT and follow-up.

opts.text: Day 9: another patient on the unit develops CLABSI with same MSSA strain on subtyping — possible unit-level contamination or breakdown in practice; infection prevention investigates
opts.rat: Cluster/outbreak signal. Infection prevention urgent investigation: same strain on subtyping suggests common source (could be contaminated equipment, water source, staff colonization, or shared procedure room). Reinforce standard precautions; enhanced surveillance; potential unit-level intervention.

opts.text: 1-month outpatient ID follow-up: completed antibiotic course; cleared from infectious standpoint; no recurrence; PICC removed without complications
opts.rat: Outpatient completion. Document end of treatment, response. Patient/family verbalize understanding. PCP for ongoing primary care. Reinforces success of OPAT approach for IV antibiotic completion at home.

opts.text: Continued sleep disturbance and ICU-acquired weakness at 1-month visit despite physical recovery — post-ICU syndrome
opts.rat: Expected post-ICU syndrome after prolonged MICU stay. Includes cognitive, physical, psychological dimensions. Post-ICU clinic referral if available; PT/OT for weakness; mental health screening; reassure patient that recovery takes weeks-to-months.

title: Pediatric Septic Shock from Meningococcemia with Fluid-Refractory Cold Shock
subtitle: PALS Push-Pull 20 mL/kg Boluses to 60 mL/kg in First Hour, EPINEPHRINE First-Line for Pediatric Cold Shock (Not Norepi), Antibiotic Within 1 Hour, and Droplet Precautions Plus Chemoprophylaxis for Close Contacts

patient.name: M. Adebayo (Malik, 5 y/o)
patient.admitDate: Today, 0420 (ED via EMS — parents called 911 for high fever, lethargy, and spreading rash that started overnight)
patient.room: Peds ED Resus Bay 1 → PICU pending bed (severe septic shock)

vitals.time: 0600 (CURRENT — after 60 mL/kg NS bolus + ceftriaxone + epi gtt started; intubated 30 min ago)

labs.v: 20 kg (50th percentile for age 5); dosing weight for all medications and fluids; height 110 cm
labs.r: PEDIATRIC PRINCIPLE: ALL doses calculated per kg. Confirm weight at admission; recheck if rapid weight change concern. Use ideal body weight in obesity for ventilator TV; actual weight for most other dosing

labs.v: Age 5 normal: HR 80–120, RR 20–28, BP systolic 90–110, MAP ≥65; SHOCK criteria for age 5: SBP <80 (hypotension), HR >140 (tachycardia), poor cap refill, cool extremities, altered mental status
labs.r: Pediatric vital signs vary by age — hypotension is LATE sign in peds shock (compensate longer than adults via tachycardia/vasoconstriction). 'Normal' BP in shocked child = compensated (preceded decompensation by minutes-to-hours)

labs.v: SEPSIS = SIRS + suspected/proven infection; SEVERE SEPSIS = sepsis + cardiovascular dysfunction OR ARDS OR ≥2 other organ dysfunctions; SEPTIC SHOCK = sepsis + CARDIOVASCULAR DYSFUNCTION (hypotension despite ≥40 mL/kg fluid resuscitation in 1 hr, OR requirement for vasopressor, OR 2 of: metabolic acidosis with BD >5 / lactate >2x ULN / oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/hr / prolonged cap refill / core-peripheral temp gap >3°C); PATIENT MEETS SEPTIC SHOCK (hypotension after 60 mL/kg fluids + on epinephrine)
labs.r: Pediatric sepsis definitions differ from adult Sepsis-3. PALS focuses on clinical recognition (poor perfusion, tachycardia, mental status). Goal-directed therapy: fluid resuscitation, early antibiotics, vasopressor for fluid-refractory shock, mechanical ventilation if needed, monitor end-organ perfusion

labs.n: COLD vs WARM SHOCK distinction (drives vasopressor choice in peds)
labs.v: PATIENT'S SHOCK TYPE = COLD SHOCK: cool extremities, capillary refill 4–5 sec (prolonged), narrow pulse pressure (78/42 = pulse pressure 36 — narrow), weak peripheral pulses, mottled skin; central temp 40.1°C but peripheral temp 32°C (>3°C gap) — vasoconstricted, high SVR
labs.r: COLD SHOCK (vasoconstricted, cold extremities, narrow pulse pressure, prolonged cap refill, high SVR) → EPINEPHRINE first-line in peds (improves contractility + cardiac output). WARM SHOCK (vasodilated, warm flushed extremities, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure, low SVR) → NOREPINEPHRINE. This is DIFFERENT from adult sepsis where norepi is first-line regardless. Peds: cold shock is most common in young children with intact compensation; warm shock more in adolescents

labs.n: Spreading PETECHIAL / PURPURIC RASH (the diagnostic clue)
labs.v: Onset overnight: started as small petechiae on trunk + extremities (parents took photos at 2200 — was sparse), now extensive PURPURIC LESIONS (1–2 cm dark non-blanching spots) on extremities, trunk, face; spreading visibly during exam; new lesions appearing every 15–30 min — PURPURA FULMINANS pattern; NO buccal involvement; no visible nuchal rigidity yet (will assess once intubated and Kernig/Brudzinski possible)
labs.r: MENINGOCOCCEMIA (Neisseria meningitidis) classically presents with spreading petechial/purpuric rash + fever + sepsis in previously-healthy child. PURPURA FULMINANS = rapidly progressive purpura from DIC; high mortality; can progress to skin necrosis and limb loss. Differential: meningococcal, pneumococcal, H. influenzae, group A strep, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, Ehrlichia, viral exanthems (less concerning)

labs.n: Blood culture and additional pathogen workup
labs.v: STAT blood culture x 2 sets drawn at 0430 (Gram stain at 0530 showed GRAM-NEGATIVE DIPLOCOCCI — consistent with Neisseria meningitidis); LP DEFERRED due to hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy + concern for cerebral edema (would do if stable + no contraindications); urine cx; nasopharyngeal swab; meningococcal PCR sent; respiratory virus panel pending
labs.r: Blood cultures positive in ~50–60% of meningococcal disease. Gram-negative diplococci on Gram stain = strong evidence. LP traditionally done to confirm meningitis but DEFERRED in unstable patient (would cause harm) — treat empirically. Meningococcal PCR more sensitive than culture

labs.v: CBC: WBC 22.4 with 18% bands (left shift), Hgb 11.2, PLT 68 (LOW — DIC); INR 1.8 (elevated — DIC); aPTT 56 (elevated); FIBRINOGEN 92 (LOW — DIC); D-DIMER markedly elevated; BMP: Na 132, K 4.4, glucose 68 (hypoglycemia — common in peds sepsis), HCO3 14 (acidosis), BUN 22, Cr 0.6 (age-appropriate; mild prerenal AKI from baseline 0.4); LACTATE 6.2 (severely elevated)
labs.r: DIC criteria: thrombocytopenia + elevated INR/aPTT + low fibrinogen + elevated D-dimer + clinical context (sepsis with purpura fulminans). Hypoglycemia common in peds sepsis (reduced glycogen stores + hypermetabolic demand). Treat: D25W 2–4 mL/kg IV bolus for BG <60

labs.v: pH 7.20 / pCO2 22 (compensatory hyperventilation pre-intubation) / pO2 84 / HCO3 9 / lactate 6.2; post-intubation pH 7.28 / pCO2 32 / HCO3 14
labs.r: Severe metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion + lactate production. Compensatory hyperventilation pre-intubation (Kussmaul). Lactate clearance trend with resuscitation is a key outcome marker. pH improving post-intubation + early resuscitation

labs.v: CEFTRIAXONE 100 mg/kg/day = 2 g IV given at 0440 (within 20 min of arrival — meets Surviving Sepsis pediatric Hour-1 goal of antibiotic within 1 hr); empiric coverage for meningococcal + pneumococcal + H. flu + GBS (less likely at age 5); will narrow once organism confirmed; vancomycin added 25 mg/kg/dose IV due to pneumococcal resistance concern in some regions (per local antibiogram); DEXAMETHASONE 0.15 mg/kg IV NOT routinely given for meningococcal sepsis (data for H. flu meningitis better than meningococcal); HYDROCORTISONE 2 mg/kg IV q6h CONSIDERED for catecholamine-resistant shock or known adrenal insufficiency (Waterhouse-Friderichsen risk in meningococcemia)
labs.r: ANTIBIOTIC WITHIN 1 HOUR for septic shock is standard. Ceftriaxone covers meningococcal, pneumococcal, H. flu — first-line empiric. Vancomycin for pneumococcal resistance concern. Dexamethasone for known/suspected H. flu or pneumococcal meningitis (reduces hearing loss). Hydrocortisone for shock not responding to fluid + vasopressor (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome: adrenal hemorrhage from meningococcal sepsis)

labs.v: Started 0425: 20 mL/kg NS = 400 mL bolus over 5 min via large-bore PIV (push-pull with 60 mL syringe; quick-pressure infuser as alternative); REASSESSED — perfusion poor, BP still low; second 20 mL/kg bolus at 0435; third 20 mL/kg bolus at 0445; TOTAL 60 mL/kg = 1.2 L given in first 30 min. After 60 mL/kg: BP improved transiently but dropped again at 0500 → FLUID-REFRACTORY SHOCK → start vasopressor
labs.r: PALS RECOMMENDATION: 20 mL/kg ISOTONIC CRYSTALLOID (NS or LR) bolus over 5–10 min via push-pull or pressure bag; reassess perfusion after EACH bolus; repeat up to 60 mL/kg in first hour; if hypotensive/poor perfusion persist after 60 mL/kg = FLUID-REFRACTORY → start VASOPRESSOR. Watch for fluid overload signs (hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs) — limit further fluid if seen. Recent FEAST trial in Africa showed concerns about aggressive bolus in resource-limited settings without ventilator support — but in high-resource ICU setting, standard PALS still applies

labs.n: VASOPRESSOR selection in pediatric COLD shock
labs.v: EPINEPHRINE gtt started at 0510 — initial 0.05 mcg/kg/min IV, titrated up to 0.1 mcg/kg/min for MAP age-appropriate target ≥65; via central access (femoral CVC placed at 0500 by intensivist using ultrasound guidance — femoral preferred in peds shock for speed and access; risks accepted given urgency); will discontinue when peripheral perfusion adequate + lactate clearing + BP sustained
labs.r: EPINEPHRINE is FIRST-LINE vasopressor for PEDIATRIC COLD SHOCK (different from adult sepsis where norepi is first-line). Mechanism: beta-1 increases contractility/HR + alpha increases SVR — addresses both poor perfusion and hypotension in cold shock. NOREPINEPHRINE first-line for WARM SHOCK (vasodilated, low SVR, wide pulse pressure). Catecholamine-resistant shock (despite epi or norepi) → add hydrocortisone for possible adrenal insufficiency

labs.v: If hemodynamics not improving on epinephrine + adequate volume: add VASOPRESSIN 0.0002–0.002 units/kg/min in pediatric shock; add HYDROCORTISONE 2 mg/kg IV q6h for catecholamine-resistant shock (concern for adrenal insufficiency / WATERHOUSE-FRIDERICHSEN syndrome in meningococcemia — bilateral adrenal hemorrhage); cortisol level can be sent but treat empirically
labs.r: WATERHOUSE-FRIDERICHSEN SYNDROME = bilateral adrenal hemorrhage in fulminant meningococcemia → acute adrenal insufficiency contributing to refractory shock. Empiric hydrocortisone in catecholamine-resistant shock; cortisol level can confirm

labs.v: INTUBATED at 0530 (after second fluid bolus completed) for impending respiratory failure + need for procedural sedation for line placement + airway protection given altered mental status (GCS 11 — E3 V3 M5); ETT size 5.0 cuffed (formula for age >2: age/4 + 4 = 5.25 ≈ 5.0); RSI used KETAMINE 2 mg/kg + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg (avoided etomidate due to adrenal suppression concern in sepsis; AVOIDED succinylcholine due to hyperkalemia risk); confirmed by capnography + CXR (tip 1 cm above carina)
labs.r: ETT SIZE formula (uncuffed): age/4 + 4 for >2 years; CUFFED tubes: age/4 + 3.5 (or downsize 0.5 from uncuffed). Ketamine preferred over etomidate in peds septic shock (hemodynamic stability + no adrenal suppression). Rocuronium for paralysis (succinylcholine avoided in hyperkalemia, prolonged immobility, burns, crush injury, neuro injury). Confirm with capnography + CXR

labs.v: Lives with parents, 2 siblings (age 3 and 7); attends kindergarten; outbreak history per parents: classmate was 'home sick with fever' last week (parents called school for any health alerts); no recent travel; immunizations UP TO DATE for age including MenACWY would NOT be due until adolescence per US schedule (some children at high risk get earlier dose); not vaccinated against MenB
labs.r: MENINGOCOCCAL DISEASE close contact prophylaxis required for: household contacts (parents, siblings), daycare/school close contacts (depends on age + classroom configuration), HCWs with mouth-to-mouth or unprotected airway exposure; chemoprophylaxis: CIPROFLOXACIN 500 mg PO x 1 adult / 20 mg/kg PO single dose in <18 y/o; OR RIFAMPIN 10 mg/kg PO BID x 2 days; OR CEFTRIAXONE 250 mg IM single dose (preferred in pregnancy or young children where cipro avoided); notify public health department
labs.r2: MenACWY routine at age 11–12 and 16 in US; MenB optional adolescent vaccine 16–23. Younger children at high risk (asplenia, complement deficiency, HIV) get earlier doses

labs.v: BG 68 on admission → improved to 92 after D25W 2 mL/kg = 40 mL IV bolus at 0440; D5 1/2 NS maintenance at age-appropriate rate (per Holliday-Segar 4-2-1: 4 × 20 = 80 mL/hr for 20 kg first 10 + 2 mL/kg/hr next 10 = 60 mL/hr; calculated 60 mL/hr); BG q1h
labs.r: PEDS SEPSIS HYPOGLYCEMIA is common (reduced glycogen stores + hypermetabolic demand). Treat with D25W 2–4 mL/kg IV bolus (NOT D50 — too concentrated for peds peripheral veins). Recheck q1h; maintain BG 80–150

labs.v: On arrival: lethargic, responding only to painful stimuli (GCS 11 — E3 V3 M5); pupils equal reactive 3 mm; no focal motor deficit; meningeal signs not assessable due to lethargy; post-intubation sedated on midazolam + fentanyl gtt
labs.r: Neuro exam often impaired in septic shock. LP deferred due to hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy + concern for cerebral edema. If meningitis present, supportive care + antibiotics treat both meningitis and sepsis. Watch for cerebral edema signs (Cushing reflex: HTN + bradycardia + irregular respirations — late sign)

labs.v: No focal neuro deficit; no abdominal source on exam; no other obvious focal infection; no recent immunization causing reaction; UDS negative; no toxic ingestion suspected; immunizations up to date per CDC schedule for age; no chronic illness; pregnancy not applicable (5 y/o male)
labs.r: Rules out alternative sources/diagnoses. Routine immunizations would not include MenACWY until adolescence per US schedule; some high-risk younger children get earlier dose. MenB optional adolescent vaccine

nursesNote: 0600 — 5 y/o male brought to peds ED at 0420 via EMS for high fever (40.1°C), lethargy, and rapidly spreading PETECHIAL → PURPURIC RASH that started overnight. Previously healthy; immunizations up to date for age per US schedule (MenACWY not due until age 11–12). MENINGOCOCCEMIA recognized clinically based on classic presentation; Gram stain at 0530 confirmed GRAM-NEGATIVE DIPLOCOCCI in blood consistent with Neisseria meningitidis; full speciation/sensitivity 24–48 hr. WEIGHT 20 kg (50th percentile age 5) — dosing weight for ALL medications and fluids. Initial vitals: HR 186 (severe tachycardia for age), BP 78/42 (HYPOTENSION for age — below 5th percentile), RR 44 (tachypnea), SpO2 94% on 4 L NC, T 40.1°C. COLD SHOCK identified: cool extremities, cap refill 4–5 sec, narrow pulse pressure 36, mottled skin, central-peripheral temp gap >3°C. PALS algorithm initiated: SEPSIS RECOGNITION at 0425 → fluid resuscitation 20 mL/kg NS (= 400 mL) bolus over 5 min via push-pull large-bore PIV; reassessed perfusion poor → second 20 mL/kg at 0435; third 20 mL/kg at 0445; TOTAL 60 mL/kg = 1.2 L in first 30 min. ANTIBIOTIC within Hour-1: CEFTRIAXONE 100 mg/kg/day = 2 g IV given at 0440 (within 20 min of arrival) + VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV added for pneumococcal resistance concern per local antibiogram.
