After 60 mL/kg = FLUID-REFRACTORY SHOCK (BP brief improvement then dropped) → started EPINEPHRINE gtt 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to 0.1 mcg/kg/min via newly-placed femoral CVC (epinephrine is FIRST-LINE vasopressor for PEDIATRIC COLD SHOCK; differs from adult sepsis where norepi is first-line). HYPOGLYCEMIA at admission BG 68 (common in peds sepsis from reduced glycogen + hypermetabolic demand) → D25W 2 mL/kg = 40 mL IV bolus given at 0440 (D25 NOT D50 in peds peripheral access); BG 92 after; D5 1/2 NS maintenance at 60 mL/hr per Holliday-Segar 4-2-1; BG q1h. INTUBATED at 0530 for impending respiratory failure + need for procedural sedation + airway protection (GCS 11) — KETAMINE 2 mg/kg + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg RSI (avoided etomidate due to adrenal suppression in sepsis; avoided succinylcholine due to hyperK risk); ETT 5.0 cuffed (age/4 + 3.5 formula); confirmed by capnography + CXR (tip 1 cm above carina); sedation with midazolam + fentanyl gtt. DIC labs critical: PLT 68, INR 1.8, fibrinogen 92, D-dimer markedly elevated = ACTIVE DIC with PURPURA FULMINANS pattern; will support with PRBC if Hgb <7, FFP for INR >1.5 with bleeding/procedure, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100, platelets >50 K with bleeding (purpura fulminans IS bleeding). Hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg IV q6h INITIATED given catecholamine-resistant shock criteria approaching (concern for WATERHOUSE-FRIDERICHSEN SYNDROME = bilateral adrenal hemorrhage in fulminant meningococcemia → adrenal insufficiency contributing to refractory shock); cortisol level sent. ISOLATION: DROPLET PRECAUTIONS initiated (mask within 6 feet, private room) until 24 HR of effective antibiotic therapy (meningococcal disease is droplet-transmitted; respiratory secretions are infectious). CLOSE CONTACT NOTIFICATION + CHEMOPROPHYLAXIS coordinated: parents notified, siblings (age 3, 7) need prophylaxis, kindergarten class needs evaluation; CIPROFLOXACIN 20 mg/kg PO single dose (for <18 y/o) OR CEFTRIAXONE 250 mg IM (for very young or pregnant contacts) OR RIFAMPIN 10 mg/kg PO BID x 2 days; PUBLIC HEALTH DEPARTMENT notified (mandatory reportable disease in all states); the school's other classmates may need prophylaxis depending on classroom configuration and exposure assessment. Family at bedside in PICU room (parents distraught but coherent; chaplain at bedside; child life specialist coordinating support); social work for psychosocial support; siblings staying with grandparents. ANTICIPATED COURSE: PICU x 5–7 days minimum; mortality for invasive meningococcal disease 5–10% (higher with fulminant purpura fulminans + DIC + Waterhouse-Friderichsen); long-term sequelae include hearing loss, neurodevelopmental issues, skin necrosis/limb loss from purpura fulminans (5–15%), psychological trauma for child and family. Plan: continue PALS bundle, monitor for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs — limit further fluids if seen), trend lactate q2h (clearance is outcome marker), continuous monitoring for purpura fulminans progression (limb perfusion checks q1h, mark borders of skin lesions for tracking, vascular surgery consultation if limb ischemia), repeat blood cultures at 24 hr, plan for prolonged antibiotic course (typically 7 days for meningococcemia, longer if endovascular complications), audiology testing post-recovery for hearing loss (common sequela of meningococcal meningitis even without classic meningitis presentation), neurology follow-up for developmental assessment.
providerOrders: ADMIT to PICU; pediatric intensivist primary; pediatric infectious disease consultation; pediatric neurology consultation if meningitis suspected; pediatric hematology if DIC severe; vascular surgery consultation if purpura fulminans threatens limb
WEIGHT 20 kg confirmed and documented as DOSING WEIGHT for all medications and fluids; recheck if rapid change

ISOLATION: DROPLET PRECAUTIONS (surgical mask within 6 feet, private room) until 24 HR of effective antibiotic therapy (meningococcal disease is droplet-transmitted via respiratory secretions); use mask + standard precautions for staff and visitors

ANTIBIOTIC within Hour-1: CEFTRIAXONE 100 mg/kg IV once daily (= 2 g IV daily for 20 kg, max 4 g/day); ADD VANCOMYCIN 15–20 mg/kg IV q6h (= 300–400 mg q6h) for pneumococcal resistance coverage pending culture; narrow to meningococcal-specific therapy (PENICILLIN G OR CEFTRIAXONE) once organism confirmed and sensitivities back; treatment duration 7 days for uncomplicated meningococcemia; longer if endovascular complications

FLUID RESUSCITATION per PALS: 20 mL/kg NS bolus over 5–10 min via push-pull or pressure bag; REASSESS perfusion (cap refill, mental status, BP, HR, UOP) after EACH bolus; repeat up to 60 mL/kg in first hour; if fluid-refractory (poor perfusion or hypotension after 60 mL/kg) → start VASOPRESSOR; WATCH FOR FLUID OVERLOAD (hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs, weight gain) — limit further fluids if seen

VASOPRESSOR for FLUID-REFRACTORY shock: EPINEPHRINE 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min IV gtt for COLD SHOCK (this patient — first-line); NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min IV gtt for WARM SHOCK; VASOPRESSIN 0.0002–0.002 units/kg/min for additional support if catecholamine-resistant; via central venous access (femoral CVC placed); reassess shock type q15 min and titrate accordingly

STRESS-DOSE STEROIDS for catecholamine-resistant shock or concern for adrenal insufficiency / WATERHOUSE-FRIDERICHSEN SYNDROME (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage in fulminant meningococcemia): HYDROCORTISONE 2 mg/kg IV q6h (= 40 mg q6h); cortisol level can be sent but treat empirically when criteria met

INTUBATION criteria: impending respiratory failure, altered mental status with airway compromise, need for procedural sedation; RSI: KETAMINE 1–2 mg/kg IV (preferred in peds septic shock — hemodynamic stability + no adrenal suppression; AVOID etomidate which suppresses adrenals in sepsis) + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg IV (AVOID succinylcholine due to hyperK risk especially in DIC); ETT size 5.0 CUFFED (age/4 + 3.5 formula for cuffed; age/4 + 4 for uncuffed); confirm with capnography + CXR

MECHANICAL VENTILATION per pediatric lung-protective: TV 6–8 mL/kg IDEAL body weight (= 120–160 mL for 20 kg); set rate age-appropriate; PEEP 5–8 initially; FiO2 titrated to SpO2 ≥92%; permissive hypercapnia accepted if needed

HYPOGLYCEMIA management (common in peds sepsis): BG q1h initially; D25W 2–4 mL/kg IV bolus for BG <60 (NOT D50 in peds peripheral access — too concentrated; D25W via peripheral OK); D5 1/2 NS or D5 NS maintenance at age-appropriate rate per Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 (= 60 mL/hr for 20 kg)

DIC management: PLT transfusion if <50 K with active bleeding (purpura fulminans IS bleeding); FFP if INR >1.5 with bleeding or procedure; CRYOPRECIPITATE if fibrinogen <100; PRBC if Hgb <7 OR symptomatic; trend coags q4h; vitamin K 1 mg IV daily
Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP and ABG access; SpO2; q15-min vitals during active resuscitation then q1h
STRICT I&O hourly; UOP target 1–2 mL/kg/hr (HIGHER target in peds than adults); foley to gravity; daily weights
LACTATE q2h until clearing — primary outcome marker for resuscitation; BMP, CBC, coags, ABG q4h
Continuous neuro checks q1h for cerebral edema signs (Cushing reflex: HTN + bradycardia + irregular respirations — LATE sign of herniation); head CT if mental status worsens after stabilization

PURPURA FULMINANS / limb perfusion monitoring: hourly assessment of perfusion to all extremities (cap refill, pulses, skin color, temperature); MARK BORDERS of skin lesions with marker and track progression; vascular surgery consultation if limb ischemia develops (some children require fasciotomy or amputation for limb-threatening purpura fulminans); skin care + protective barrier

CHEMOPROPHYLAXIS for close contacts: COORDINATE with public health department (mandatory reportable disease in all states); HOUSEHOLD contacts (parents, siblings) + DAYCARE/KINDERGARTEN close contacts + HCWs with mouth-to-mouth or unprotected airway exposure require prophylaxis; OPTIONS: CIPROFLOXACIN 500 mg PO x 1 (adult; 20 mg/kg single dose for children if needed); RIFAMPIN 10 mg/kg PO BID x 2 days (4 doses); CEFTRIAXONE 250 mg IM single dose (preferred for pregnant contacts or very young children); ideally within 24 hr of exposure

Public health department notification (MANDATORY REPORTABLE DISEASE in all 50 states); school notification for class contact assessment; parents notified about chemoprophylaxis for themselves + siblings

Family-centered care: parents at bedside (open visitation); child life specialist; chaplain; social work; sibling support (staying with grandparents currently); psychological support for the parents who witnessed the rapid progression

Audiology testing post-recovery: hearing loss is common sequela of meningococcal disease (5–10% sensorineural hearing loss) even without classic meningitis; brainstem auditory evoked response (BAER) test before discharge and at 1-month follow-up; outpatient audiology
Neurology follow-up: developmental assessment, monitoring for neurodevelopmental sequelae (especially if meningitis or significant CNS involvement); outpatient pediatric neurology

Discharge planning: parental education on completion of antibiotic course (likely IV ceftriaxone via PICC if continued OR transition to oral if appropriate organism); follow-up with pediatrician within 1 week, ID within 2 weeks, audiology pre-discharge + 1 month, neurology if indicated; psychological support for child and family (PICU stay + critical illness can cause PTSD); vaccine update if MenACWY indicated

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Pediatric septic shock with hypotension (78/42 for age 5 < 5th percentile), severe tachycardia (HR 186), poor perfusion (cap refill 4–5 sec, mottled skin), altered mental status (GCS 11), severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.20, lactate 6.2)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the emergency. Hypotension is a LATE sign in peds shock (kids compensate longer than adults via tachycardia/vasoconstriction). When hypotension present, decompensation imminent. PALS bundle: fluid + antibiotic + vasopressor + airway.

opts.text: COLD SHOCK pattern (cool extremities, cap refill 4–5 sec, narrow pulse pressure 36, weak peripheral pulses, mottled skin, central-peripheral temp gap >3°C) — vasoconstricted, high SVR
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — drives vasopressor choice. COLD SHOCK → EPINEPHRINE first-line in pediatrics (different from adult sepsis where norepi is first-line). WARM SHOCK (vasodilated, warm flushed, wide pulse pressure) → NOREPINEPHRINE. Cold shock most common in young children; warm in adolescents.

opts.text: Spreading petechial → purpuric rash with PURPURA FULMINANS pattern (1–2 cm dark non-blanching lesions appearing every 15–30 min); classic for MENINGOCOCCEMIA
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic clincher. Spreading petechiae/purpura + fever + sepsis in previously-healthy child = meningococcemia until proven otherwise. Purpura fulminans = DIC + skin necrosis; high mortality; can progress to limb loss. Gram stain at 0530 confirmed Gram-negative diplococci.

opts.text: DIC labs: PLT 68, INR 1.8, fibrinogen 92 (low), D-dimer markedly elevated — active DIC with PURPURA FULMINANS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — coagulopathy needs management. Transfuse: PRBC if Hgb <7, FFP for INR >1.5 with bleeding/procedure, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100, platelets >50 K with bleeding. Purpura fulminans IS bleeding so threshold for transfusion lowered. Trend coags q4h.

opts.text: Antibiotic administered within Hour-1: CEFTRIAXONE 100 mg/kg IV (= 2 g) at 0440 (within 20 min of arrival) + VANCOMYCIN for pneumococcal resistance coverage
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Hour-1 antibiotic is standard. Ceftriaxone covers meningococcal + pneumococcal + H. flu first-line empiric. Vancomycin added per local antibiogram. Will narrow once organism confirmed and sensitivities back (typically penicillin G or ceftriaxone for meningococcal).

opts.text: Fluid resuscitation 60 mL/kg NS in first 30 min (3 × 20 mL/kg push-pull boluses); FLUID-REFRACTORY shock → vasopressor needed; monitor for fluid overload signs
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — PALS algorithm. 20 mL/kg boluses up to 60 mL/kg in first hour. Reassess after each bolus. Fluid-refractory = epinephrine for cold shock or norepi for warm shock. Watch for hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs (fluid overload).

opts.text: EPINEPHRINE gtt 0.05–0.1 mcg/kg/min started for fluid-refractory cold shock via newly-placed femoral CVC
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — first-line vasopressor for peds cold shock. Beta-1 increases contractility/HR + alpha increases SVR. Femoral access acceptable in pediatric shock for speed; protect site; remove ASAP once not needed. Catecholamine-resistant = add hydrocortisone.

opts.text: Hypoglycemia BG 68 — common in peds sepsis from reduced glycogen + hypermetabolic demand; treated with D25W 2 mL/kg IV bolus
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — easy-to-miss problem. Worsens shock and brain function. Treat with D25W (NOT D50 in peds peripheral access — too concentrated); recheck BG q1h; maintenance D5 1/2 NS or D5 NS at age-appropriate rate.

opts.text: DROPLET ISOLATION + chemoprophylaxis for close contacts (household, daycare/school) + public health department notification (mandatory reportable disease)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — public health emergency. Meningococcal disease is droplet-transmitted; respiratory secretions infectious. Close contacts need ciprofloxacin (single dose) or rifampin x 2 days or ceftriaxone IM x 1. Mandatory reportable in all 50 states.

opts.text: Weight 20 kg confirmed as dosing weight for ALL pediatric medications and fluids
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pediatric dosing principle. EVERY drug and fluid is mg/kg or mL/kg. Confirm at admission; document; use for ALL calculations. Use ideal body weight in obesity for ventilator TV; actual weight for most other dosing.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed. Helpful for empiric antibiotics.

opts.text: Routine immunizations up to date for age
opts.rat: Reassuring but note: MenACWY not due until age 11–12 per US schedule (some high-risk younger children get earlier dose); MenB optional adolescent vaccine. So 'up to date for age' doesn't mean protected against meningococcus at age 5 — this is an unfortunate gap in routine vaccination coverage for this age group.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / pediatric principles.

opts.text: PEDIATRIC SHOCK physiology: children compensate longer than adults via TACHYCARDIA and VASOCONSTRICTION; HYPOTENSION is a LATE sign in peds and indicates IMMINENT decompensation; recognize early on perfusion signs (cap refill, mental status, UOP, peripheral pulses) BEFORE BP drops
opts.rat: CORRECT — central pediatric principle. The pediatric vital sign 'sweet spot' is narrower than adults; once BP drops, the child is decompensating. Early recognition on perfusion + mental status + UOP saves lives. PALS focuses on clinical recognition over numbers.

opts.text: PALS SEPTIC SHOCK ALGORITHM: 20 mL/kg ISOTONIC bolus (NS or LR) over 5–10 min via push-pull or pressure bag; REASSESS perfusion after each; repeat up to 60 mL/kg in first hour; if hypotensive/poor perfusion after 60 mL/kg = FLUID-REFRACTORY → start VASOPRESSOR; antibiotic within 1 hour; monitor for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs)
opts.rat: CORRECT — standard pediatric resuscitation. Surviving Sepsis Campaign Pediatric Guidelines (2020). Reassessment after each bolus prevents over-resuscitation. FEAST trial in low-resource settings raised concerns about aggressive bolus without ventilator support — in high-resource ICU, PALS standard applies.

opts.text: COLD SHOCK vs WARM SHOCK distinguishes vasopressor choice in pediatrics: COLD (cool extremities, narrow pulse pressure, prolonged cap refill, high SVR) → EPINEPHRINE first-line (beta-1 contractility + alpha SVR); WARM (warm flushed, wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses, low SVR) → NOREPINEPHRINE first-line (alpha SVR) — DIFFERENT from adult sepsis where norepi is first-line regardless
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield pediatric pharm distinction. Most young children with septic shock have cold shock pattern (intact compensatory vasoconstriction); adolescents more likely warm shock (vasodilated). Reassess shock type q15 min and titrate accordingly. Switch agents if pattern changes.

opts.text: MENINGOCOCCEMIA (Neisseria meningitidis) classic presentation: previously healthy child with HIGH FEVER + LETHARGY + spreading PETECHIAL → PURPURIC RASH; rapid progression to PURPURA FULMINANS (DIC + skin necrosis); high mortality 5–10% (higher with fulminant disease); transmission via respiratory secretions (droplet)
opts.rat: CORRECT — disease recognition. Spreading rash in a febrile child = meningococcemia until proven otherwise. Other causes of similar presentation: pneumococcal sepsis, H. flu, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, viral exanthems (lower acuity). Empiric ceftriaxone covers most.

opts.text: WATERHOUSE-FRIDERICHSEN SYNDROME = bilateral adrenal hemorrhage in fulminant meningococcemia → acute adrenal insufficiency contributing to refractory shock; treat empirically with HYDROCORTISONE 2 mg/kg IV q6h in catecholamine-resistant shock; can confirm with cortisol level but don't wait
opts.rat: CORRECT — important complication. Adrenal hemorrhage from sepsis. Empiric hydrocortisone in shock not responding to fluid + vasopressor. Random cortisol <18 mcg/dL in critically ill = relative adrenal insufficiency. Continue stress-dose steroids while in shock.

opts.text: DROPLET PRECAUTIONS for meningococcal disease until 24 HR of effective antibiotic therapy; CLOSE CONTACTS (household, daycare/school close contacts, HCWs with mouth-to-mouth or unprotected airway exposure) need CHEMOPROPHYLAXIS: CIPROFLOXACIN single dose, RIFAMPIN x 2 days, or CEFTRIAXONE IM single dose; MANDATORY REPORTABLE disease in all 50 states; public health notification
opts.rat: CORRECT — public health response. Droplet precautions for 24 hr post effective antibiotic. Close contact prophylaxis ideally within 24 hr of exposure. Public health coordinates broader contact tracing and outbreak prevention. School/daycare contacts based on classroom configuration and exposure assessment.

opts.text: PEDIATRIC ETT SIZE formulas (uncuffed: age/4 + 4 for >2 years; cuffed: age/4 + 3.5 OR downsize 0.5 from uncuffed); RSI drugs in peds septic shock: KETAMINE 1–2 mg/kg preferred (hemodynamic stability, no adrenal suppression — vs etomidate which suppresses adrenals); ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg for paralysis (succinylcholine AVOIDED in hyperK, DIC, prolonged immobility, burns, crush injury, neuro injury)
opts.rat: CORRECT — pediatric airway management. Cuffed tubes now preferred over uncuffed in pediatrics (better seal, similar safety with low-pressure cuffs). Ketamine over etomidate in sepsis. Rocuronium over succinylcholine in many peds emergencies.

opts.text: HYPOGLYCEMIA management in peds (common in sepsis from reduced glycogen stores + hypermetabolic demand): D25W 2–4 mL/kg IV bolus for BG <60 (NOT D50 in peds peripheral access — too concentrated, vein damage); D5 1/2 NS or D5 NS maintenance at HOLLIDAY-SEGAR rate (4 mL/kg/hr first 10 kg + 2 mL/kg/hr next 10 kg + 1 mL/kg/hr beyond = 60 mL/hr for 20 kg patient)
opts.rat: CORRECT — pediatric dosing. D25 in peripheral, D10 in neonates, D50 only via central access. Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 calculation for maintenance. Some recent guidance favors isotonic maintenance (D5 NS) in acutely ill children to prevent hyponatremia.

opts.text: Start NOREPINEPHRINE first-line for this child's fluid-refractory septic shock (because norepi is first-line in adult sepsis)
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG for pediatrics. Pediatric COLD SHOCK (this patient) → EPINEPHRINE first-line. Pediatric WARM SHOCK → norepi first-line. Adult sepsis defaults to norepi regardless. This is one of the most-tested pediatric–adult differences.

opts.text: Use D50W 2 mL/kg via peripheral IV for the hypoglycemia in this 5-year-old
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong concentration in peds peripheral access. D50W is TOO CONCENTRATED for peripheral veins in children (causes vein damage and tissue injury if extravasated). D25W 2–4 mL/kg via peripheral OK. D50W only via central access in pediatrics. D10W for neonates.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Septic Shock with Fluid-Refractory Hemodynamic Compromise r/t meningococcemia; lactate 6.2, MAP requiring vasopressor support
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The shock is the imminent killer. PALS bundle: fluid resuscitation 60 mL/kg + antibiotic within 1 hr + epinephrine for cold shock + airway management + hydrocortisone for catecholamine-resistant + monitor end-organ perfusion.

opts.text: Risk for DIC / Purpura Fulminans Progression r/t meningococcal sepsis; PLT 68, INR 1.8, fibrinogen 92, D-dimer markedly elevated; risk of skin necrosis, limb loss, internal hemorrhage
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Coagulopathy management + monitoring for limb-threatening lesions. Mark borders of purpura q1h. Vascular surgery consultation if limb ischemia develops. Blood product support per criteria. Some children require fasciotomy or amputation.

opts.text: Risk for Continued Bacteremia / Inadequate Source Control r/t meningococcemia; need for continued antibiotic + supportive care + monitoring for endovascular complications
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Antibiotic within 1 hr (DONE), continued; will narrow once organism speciated and sensitivities back. Repeat blood cultures at 24 hr to confirm clearance. Treatment 7 days for uncomplicated meningococcemia; longer for endovascular complications.

opts.text: Risk for Disease Transmission to Close Contacts r/t meningococcal disease (droplet-transmitted) — household, daycare/school close contacts, HCWs
opts.rat: #4 — Important public health. DROPLET PRECAUTIONS until 24 hr of effective antibiotic therapy. Close contact chemoprophylaxis ideally within 24 hr of exposure. Public health notification (mandatory reportable). Parent + sibling + classmate evaluation.

opts.text: Parental Distress / Family Trauma r/t rapid progression of life-threatening illness in young child
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Family-centered care: parents at bedside, child life specialist, chaplain, social work, sibling support, anticipated PTSD risk for the parents who witnessed the rapid progression. Long-term psychological support.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: PALS fluid resuscitation: 20 mL/kg NS bolus over 5–10 min via push-pull; reassess perfusion after each bolus; repeat up to 60 mL/kg in first hour; watch for fluid overload signs
opts.rat: Standard PALS algorithm. Aggressive volume resuscitation for septic shock with reassessment between boluses. Fluid overload signs (hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs) limit further fluids. FEAST trial concerns apply to low-resource settings without ventilator support.

opts.text: CEFTRIAXONE 100 mg/kg IV (= 2 g for 20 kg) within Hour-1; ADD VANCOMYCIN 15–20 mg/kg IV q6h for pneumococcal resistance coverage; narrow to penicillin G or ceftriaxone once meningococcal confirmed
opts.rat: Standard empiric broad-spectrum coverage for peds severe sepsis. Hour-1 antibiotic standard. De-escalate once organism speciated and sensitivities back.

opts.text: NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05 mcg/kg/min IV gtt as first-line vasopressor for fluid-refractory shock in this 5-year-old
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong for peds COLD shock. EPINEPHRINE is first-line for pediatric cold shock (this patient). Norepinephrine first-line for pediatric warm shock (vasodilated, wide pulse pressure). Adult sepsis defaults to norepi regardless — peds is shock-type dependent.

opts.text: EPINEPHRINE 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min IV gtt via central access for fluid-refractory cold shock; titrate to age-appropriate MAP target
opts.rat: First-line vasopressor for pediatric cold shock. Beta-1 contractility + alpha SVR addresses both poor perfusion and hypotension. Central access (femoral acceptable in peds shock for speed).

opts.text: HYDROCORTISONE 2 mg/kg IV q6h for catecholamine-resistant shock concern (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome)
opts.rat: Empiric stress-dose steroids for catecholamine-resistant shock. Bilateral adrenal hemorrhage from meningococcal sepsis = Waterhouse-Friderichsen. Cortisol level can confirm but don't wait.

opts.text: INTUBATION with KETAMINE 2 mg/kg + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg RSI; cuffed ETT size 5.0 (age/4 + 3.5); confirm with capnography + CXR
opts.rat: Standard peds septic shock airway management. Ketamine preferred over etomidate (no adrenal suppression). Rocuronium preferred over succinylcholine (hyperK risk in DIC). Cuffed tubes now preferred in peds.

opts.text: ETOMIDATE 0.3 mg/kg IV for induction; SUCCINYLCHOLINE 1 mg/kg IV for paralysis
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong RSI choices in this case. Etomidate suppresses adrenals — bad in sepsis (especially Waterhouse-Friderichsen concern). Succinylcholine causes hyperK — bad in DIC + critically ill. Use ketamine + rocuronium.

opts.text: D25W 2 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (BG 68); maintenance D5 1/2 NS at 60 mL/hr (Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 for 20 kg); BG q1h
opts.rat: Standard peds hypoglycemia management. D25 via peripheral OK; D50 only via central access in peds; D10 for neonates. Maintenance per Holliday-Segar. Q1h BG checks.

opts.text: Sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV bolus for the severe metabolic acidosis pH 7.20
opts.rat: Not routinely indicated. Bicarbonate in sepsis-related metabolic acidosis has not been shown to improve outcomes; risks include cerebral edema, hyperK, paradoxical CSF acidosis, sodium overload. Treat the cause (resuscitation + antibiotics + source control); acidosis resolves with restored perfusion.

opts.text: DIC support: PLT transfusion if <50 K with bleeding; FFP for INR >1.5 with bleeding/procedure; cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100 with bleeding; PRBC if Hgb <7 OR symptomatic; vitamin K 1 mg IV daily; trend coags q4h
opts.rat: Standard DIC support in active bleeding (purpura fulminans IS bleeding). Lower transfusion threshold than non-bleeding DIC. Concurrent treat the cause (sepsis, antibiotics).

opts.text: DROPLET ISOLATION until 24 hr of effective antibiotic therapy; PRIVATE ROOM; surgical mask within 6 feet for staff and visitors; standard precautions
opts.rat: Meningococcal disease is droplet-transmitted. Standard infection control. Education for staff and visitors. Lift droplet precautions after 24 hr of effective antibiotic.

opts.text: Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts: parents, siblings, daycare/kindergarten contacts (per public health department); CIPROFLOXACIN 20 mg/kg PO x 1 (children); RIFAMPIN 10 mg/kg PO BID x 2 days; CEFTRIAXONE 250 mg IM x 1 (for pregnant contacts or very young children); ideally within 24 hr; PUBLIC HEALTH NOTIFICATION (mandatory reportable disease)
opts.rat: Standard public health response. Close contact prophylaxis prevents secondary cases. Public health coordinates broader investigation. School/daycare contact assessment based on configuration and exposure.

opts.text: Family-centered care: parents at bedside, child life specialist, chaplain, social work, sibling support, anticipated psychological support post-discharge for the family
opts.rat: Family integration is part of pediatric critical care. Parents witnessing rapid progression of life-threatening illness face PTSD risk. Long-term psychological support; sibling support; financial counseling if needed.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm weight 20 kg + establish large-bore PIV (or IO if PIV cannot be obtained in 90 sec); STAT blood cultures x 2; 20 mL/kg NS bolus via push-pull over 5–10 min; reassess perfusion after bolus; CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV within 1 hr
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. PALS bundle Hour-1: access, cultures, antibiotics, fluids. IO access acceptable if PIV unavailable (PALS limit 90 sec to attempt PIV before IO). Reassess after each bolus. Antibiotic at the SAME time as cultures, not after.

opts.text: Continue PALS fluid resuscitation up to 60 mL/kg in first hour; reassess perfusion (cap refill, mental status, BP, HR, UOP) after each 20 mL/kg bolus; monitor for fluid overload signs (hepatomegaly, crackles); start EPINEPHRINE 0.05 mcg/kg/min IV gtt via central access for fluid-refractory cold shock
opts.rat: #2 — Vasopressor for fluid-refractory shock. PALS algorithm. Epi first-line for peds cold shock. Femoral CVC acceptable in peds shock for speed. Reassess shock type q15 min.

opts.text: INTUBATION for impending respiratory failure / altered mental status — KETAMINE 2 mg/kg + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg RSI; ETT 5.0 cuffed; confirm with capnography + CXR; sedation with midazolam + fentanyl gtt; hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg IV q6h for catecholamine-resistant shock concern; D25W bolus for hypoglycemia
opts.rat: #3 — Airway + adjuncts. Intubation as condition deteriorates or anticipated need. Avoid etomidate (adrenal suppression in sepsis) and succinylcholine (hyperK in DIC). Hydrocortisone for catecholamine-resistant shock. D25W for hypoglycemia.

opts.text: DROPLET ISOLATION; public health notification; coordinate chemoprophylaxis for close contacts (parents, siblings, classroom); DIC support per criteria; trend lactate q2h; mark purpura fulminans borders for tracking; vascular surgery if limb ischemia
opts.rat: #4 — Concurrent public health + DIC management + purpura fulminans monitoring. Droplet precautions until 24 hr of effective antibiotic. Public health coordinates contact tracing. DIC support per active bleeding criteria.

opts.text: Family-centered care: parents at bedside (open visitation), child life specialist, chaplain, social work, sibling support; anticipated 5–7 day PICU stay; audiology pre-discharge for hearing loss screening; neurology follow-up for developmental assessment; psychological support post-discharge
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care and family integration. PICU stay 5–7 days. Audiology for hearing loss (5–10% incidence in meningococcal disease). Neurology for developmental assessment. Psychological support for parental PTSD.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 4: lactate 2.8 (down from 6.2); HR 124 (down from 186); MAP 70 on epinephrine 0.05 mcg/kg/min (weaning); cap refill 2 sec; UOP 1.2 mL/kg/hr; purpura fulminans NOT extending (borders stable); BG 110 on D5 maintenance
opts.rat: Excellent response to PALS bundle. Hemodynamics stabilizing, lactate clearing, perfusion improving, UOP adequate, purpura stable. Continue epinephrine wean as tolerated; trend lactate q2h; continue antibiotics; monitor for fluid overload, DIC progression, cerebral edema.

opts.text: Day 3: off vasopressors, extubated, eating soft diet, ambulating with PT, purpura fulminans not progressing (some lesions starting to demarcate), antibiotic transitioning to penicillin G (organism confirmed as Neisseria meningitidis, pen-sensitive), repeat blood cultures negative
opts.rat: Successful recovery trajectory. Narrowed antibiotic per sensitivities. Repeat cultures negative confirms clearance. Continued vigilance for sequelae (hearing loss, neurodevelopmental, skin necrosis). Plan transfer to peds floor; audiology pre-discharge.

opts.text: Discharge day 7: completed 7-day antibiotic course; audiology shows mild bilateral high-frequency hearing loss (sensorineural — common meningococcal sequela); neurology follow-up scheduled; skin lesions healing without surgical intervention; psychological support for family in place; chemoprophylaxis confirmed completed by all close contacts; PCP + ID follow-up scheduled
opts.rat: Successful discharge with sequelae management. Hearing loss is common sequela (5–10%); audiology + ENT follow-up. Neurology for developmental tracking. Skin healing without amputation = best-case for purpura fulminans. Family support continues.

opts.text: Hour 2: 4th 20 mL/kg fluid bolus given (total 80 mL/kg); new hepatomegaly (liver edge 4 cm below costal margin, was 1 cm); new pulmonary crackles bilateral; SpO2 dropping to 91% on 4 L NC — fluid overload
opts.rat: Fluid overload from over-resuscitation. STOP further boluses; consider diuretic (furosemide 1 mg/kg IV) if hemodynamics stable; increase respiratory support (intubation if needed); monitor for ARDS development. Fluid overload signs in peds = hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs, weight gain.

opts.text: Hour 6: limb perfusion deteriorating in R lower extremity — toes dusky, no palpable pulses, cap refill >6 sec; purpura fulminans extending; vascular surgery consulted; possible amputation if irreversible
opts.rat: Limb-threatening purpura fulminans. Vascular surgery consult; consider fasciotomy if compartment syndrome; some children require amputation. Continue DIC support; aggressive perfusion management; family discussion of possible limb loss. Common sequela of fulminant meningococcal disease (5–15%).

opts.text: Hour 8: developing increased intracranial pressure signs — bradycardia HR 78 (down from 124), HTN BP 140/88 (up from 70 MAP), irregular respirations, pupils slightly sluggish — CUSHING REFLEX from cerebral edema or hemorrhage
opts.rat: Cerebral edema with impending herniation (Cushing reflex = late sign). EMERGENT mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV OR hypertonic saline 3% 3–5 mL/kg; HOB 30°; mild hyperventilation to pCO2 30; neurosurgery consult; head CT once stable; continue resuscitation; consider EVD placement.

opts.text: Parents and siblings (age 3, 7) received ciprofloxacin chemoprophylaxis as recommended; the kindergarten classmates received public health-coordinated prophylaxis based on classroom configuration; no secondary cases reported
opts.rat: Successful public health response. Chemoprophylaxis prevented secondary cases. Public health continues surveillance for outbreak signals. Parental + sibling protection. School and broader community informed appropriately.

opts.text: Family verbalizing distress about the rapid progression: 'one day she was at school, the next she was in PICU' — emotional response to traumatic event
opts.rat: Expected emotional response to acute critical illness. Validate feelings; chaplain support; child life specialist; sibling support (staying with grandparents); psychological support both inpatient and outpatient. Parents at risk of PTSD; provide referral for ongoing support.

opts.text: Persistent mild thrombocytopenia 110 K at discharge (chronic from DIC recovery)
opts.rat: Expected residual finding as DIC resolves. Will normalize over days-to-weeks. Monitor; no transfusion needed; no bleeding signs.

opts.text: 1-month outpatient follow-up: developmental assessment normal for age; audiology stable mild high-frequency hearing loss; PCP coordinating ongoing care; family reports child back at kindergarten with accommodations; parents engaged with family therapy for PTSD symptoms
opts.rat: Best-case long-term recovery. Developmental assessment reassuring. Hearing loss being managed with audiology. Family addressing psychological sequelae. Continue serial assessments; school re-entry support; vaccination update (MenACWY when due at age 11–12); meningococcal disease education for life.

title: Placental Abruption with DIC at 34 Weeks Requiring Emergent Cesarean Delivery
subtitle: Concealed Bleeding Can Be Massive Without Visible Loss, Board-Like Uterus + Non-Reassuring FHR = Delivery Now, Massive Transfusion Protocol Activates Before the Coagulopathy Wins

patient.name: D. Okafor-Reyes
patient.admitDate: Today, 1810 (L&D triage via ambulance — sudden severe constant abdominal pain x 90 min with dark vaginal bleeding)
patient.room: L&D Bed 2 → OR pending emergent cesarean

vitals.time: 1900 (CURRENT — after 2 L LR bolus + 2 U PRBC + transferring to OR for emergent cesarean)

labs.v: G3P2 at 34+2 weeks; presented with SUDDEN SEVERE CONSTANT abdominal pain x 90 min + dark vaginal bleeding (~200 mL on pad, but may be massively under-estimating concealed bleeding); admitted to using COCAINE today (snorted ~3 hours before pain onset — vasoconstriction precipitant); chronic HTN on labetalol (poor adherence past 2 weeks); prior placental abruption with last pregnancy (2 yr ago, delivered preterm at 35 wk); current smoker (1 pack/day x 12 years); BMI 28
labs.r: PLACENTAL ABRUPTION risk factors: chronic HTN/preeclampsia, prior abruption (recurrence ~10x baseline), cocaine/methamphetamine (vasoconstriction → placental hemorrhage), abdominal trauma, smoking, advanced maternal age, multiparity, polyhydramnios, premature rupture of membranes. Recurrence risk after 1 prior abruption ~10–15%; after 2 prior ~25%

labs.v: REVEALED ~200 mL dark blood on pad (NOT bright red — suggests longer-standing accumulation); patient appears MORE shocky than 200 mL would explain → CONCEALED hemorrhage suspected (blood trapped between placenta and uterine wall, not exiting vagina); abdominal girth increasing by 2 cm in 30 min observation = expanding hemoperitoneum or retroplacental hematoma
labs.r: CONCEALED ABRUPTION = bleeding contained behind placenta, no visible vaginal bleeding OR visible bleeding under-represents total loss; can lose 1–2 L without visible bleeding. CLUES: shock out of proportion to visible loss, board-like uterus, expanding abdominal girth, fetal distress. REVEALED = blood exits vagina (dark vs bright in placenta previa). Many abruptions are MIXED

labs.v: Uterus FIRM, TENSE, BOARD-LIKE ("wooden" — pathognomonic for abruption); EXTREMELY TENDER to palpation; tetanic contractions on tocometer (no relaxation between contractions); uterine height appears larger than 34 weeks (?distended by hematoma); no palpable fetal parts (deep behind firm uterus)
labs.r: BOARD-LIKE TENSE UTERUS with TETANIC CONTRACTIONS = classic placental abruption (vs placenta previa which is PAINLESS BRIGHT RED bleeding with soft uterus). Couvelaire uterus = blood extravasates into myometrium, uterus appears purple/blue at C-section, often causes postpartum hemorrhage from uterine atony

labs.v: Continuous EFM: baseline 105 (bradycardic — was 145 1 hr ago per L&D triage initial strip), minimal variability, RECURRENT LATE DECELERATIONS to 70, then PROLONGED DECELERATION to 60 lasting 4+ minutes (current) = CATEGORY III (non-reassuring); CRITICAL fetal status
labs.r: Category III FHR = abnormal/ominous = EMERGENT DELIVERY indication. Prolonged decel + bradycardia + minimal variability in setting of placental abruption = uteroplacental insufficiency from disrupted maternal-fetal circulation. Decision-to-incision time goal <30 min for Cat III + time-sensitive emergency

labs.v: PT/INR 1.9 (elevated; was 1.0 baseline 1 week ago); aPTT 56 (elevated); FIBRINOGEN 152 (LOW — normal pregnancy fibrinogen is ELEVATED 300–600); D-DIMER markedly elevated; PLT 124 (low for pregnancy where normal is 150–400); CBC: WBC 16.2, Hgb 8.4 (down from 12.0 last week — confirms major blood loss including concealed component), Hct 25%
labs.r: DIC in placental abruption: massive placental thromboplastin release → consumptive coagulopathy. Pregnancy fibrinogen normally ELEVATED to 300–600 — a level <200 in pregnancy = significantly LOW + impending coagulopathy. Trend coags q1–2h initially; replace with FFP/cryo/platelets per criteria

labs.v: Blood type O-NEGATIVE (universal donor); MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL ACTIVATED; 4 U PRBC available now (2 given), 4 U FFP available now (2 thawing), 1 dose PLATELETS available, CRYOPRECIPITATE 10 units coming up; blood bank notified; aim for 1:1:1 ratio PRBC:FFP:platelets per MTP
labs.r: OB hemorrhage MTP standard: 1:1:1 ratio of PRBC:FFP:platelets; CRYOPRECIPITATE for fibrinogen <200 mg/dL (or <150 — institutional protocol); TXA 1 g IV within 3 hr of bleeding (WOMAN trial — mortality benefit); CALCIUM gluconate for citrate-related hypocalcemia after multiple transfusions

labs.v: 2 large-bore PIVs (16 g R AC, 18 g L forearm) placed at 1815; 2 L LR bolus given; BP 88/52 → 98/60 after fluids + 2 U PRBC; lactate 3.6 (elevated, mild shock); will avoid over-resuscitation with crystalloid (dilutes clotting factors)
labs.r: OB hemorrhage resuscitation: large-bore access (≥18 g, preferably 16 g); balanced approach — sufficient crystalloid for perfusion but transition to BLOOD PRODUCTS EARLY (crystalloid dilution of clotting factors worsens coagulopathy); MTP for ongoing hemorrhage; cautious target SBP 80–100 (avoid hypertension that may worsen abruption + maintain uteroplacental perfusion if fetus still in utero) until source controlled

labs.v: OR alerted at 1815, ready by 1900; anesthesia at bedside (general anesthesia planned given hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy — neuraxial relatively contraindicated with coagulopathy + maternal hypotension); pediatrics/neonatology notified for late preterm delivery 34+2 wk with non-reassuring FHR; antenatal betamethasone NOT FEASIBLE given emergent indication
labs.r: ANESTHESIA choice in OB hemorrhage: GENERAL preferred over NEURAXIAL when hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy + emergent (no time for spinal placement, risk of hypotension from spinal blockade in hypovolemic patient, epidural hematoma risk with coagulopathy). Pediatrics at delivery for late preterm + Cat III FHR

labs.v: 34+2 wk by certain dating (1st trimester ultrasound and LMP concordant); estimated fetal weight 2.1 kg by growth ultrasound 2 weeks ago (50th percentile); head-down per Leopold; NICU at delivery for late preterm care (potential respiratory issues, hypoglycemia, thermoregulation, NICU admission likely)
labs.r: LATE PRETERM 34–36+6 weeks: generally good outcomes but increased risk of respiratory issues (RDS, transient tachypnea), hypoglycemia, hypothermia, feeding difficulties, jaundice; neonatology presence at delivery

labs.v: O-negative; antibody screen negative; Rh-negative mother → RHIG (RhoGAM) postpartum if Rh-positive baby (verify; if abruption with significant fetomaternal hemorrhage, may need additional doses calculated by Kleihauer-Betke test); blood type and crossmatch confirmed for transfusion
labs.r: Rh-negative mothers receive RHIG within 72 hr of delivery if Rh-positive baby OR at any antenatal bleeding episode; Kleihauer-Betke test quantifies fetomaternal hemorrhage to determine dose (extra RHIG for significant fetomaternal bleed)

labs.n: Magnesium for fetal neuroprotection (controversial in this case)
labs.v: MgSO4 4 g IV bolus + 1 g/hr maintenance gtt is given for FETAL NEUROPROTECTION when delivery imminent at <32 weeks; THIS PATIENT IS 34+2 WEEKS — beyond the typical 32-week cutoff, so mag for neuroprotection NOT routinely indicated; would NOT delay emergent delivery for any reason
labs.r: Magnesium sulfate fetal neuroprotection for delivery <32 weeks (some centers extend to 33–34 weeks); reduces cerebral palsy risk. At 34+2 weeks, mag NOT routine for neuroprotection. Delivery cannot be delayed for the 4-hour ideal pre-delivery window when the patient is decompensating

labs.v: Cocaine use today (snorted ~3 hr before pain onset); admits to using cocaine 'occasionally' during pregnancy (last several months); also smokes cigarettes 1 ppd; lives with husband, two children at home (3 and 6); social work consult activated; mandatory reporting to CPS for cocaine use in pregnancy per state law; addiction medicine consultation; UDS sent (will confirm cocaine + screen other substances)
labs.r: Cocaine in pregnancy: vasoconstriction → placental abruption (this case), preterm labor, low birth weight, IUGR; neonatal abstinence less than opioid; mandatory reporting requirements vary by state but generally yes for cocaine; addiction medicine consultation for treatment + medication; SBIRT (Screening, Brief Intervention, Referral to Treatment) standard of care; family-centered nonjudgmental approach

labs.v: Husband at bedside (notified, on his way); patient is alert + oriented but anxious; verbal consent for emergent cesarean obtained; advance directive not in chart (patient is young, no recent serious illness); social work for family coordination + children's care; husband will need significant support
labs.r: Emergent OB situations require RAPID consent — verbal consent in true emergencies acceptable; husband as proxy if patient unable; document carefully; family-centered care; postpartum family support and bonding facilitation

labs.v: Cervix examined gently (no digital exam if placenta previa — but speculum exam if needed): no visible placental tissue at os, no os dilation evidence of imminent delivery (still 34 wk); ultrasound bedside at 1830 showed retroplacental hematoma + intact placental position (NOT placenta previa); no signs of uterine rupture (no fetal parts palpable extra-uterine, no sudden cessation of contractions); not in active labor with normal cervical change
labs.r: DIFFERENTIAL OF 3RD-TRIMESTER BLEEDING: (1) PLACENTAL ABRUPTION (painful, dark blood, board-like uterus); (2) PLACENTA PREVIA (painless, bright red blood, soft uterus, no digital exam); (3) UTERINE RUPTURE (sudden severe pain, loss of contractions, fetal parts palpable extra-uterine, prior C-section scar risk); (4) vasa previa (bright red bleeding at ROM); (5) bloody show of labor (less severe); (6) cervical/vaginal source (trauma, infection)

nursesNote: 1900 — 32 y/o G3P2 at 34+2 weeks presented to L&D triage at 1810 via ambulance for SUDDEN SEVERE CONSTANT ABDOMINAL PAIN x 90 min with dark vaginal bleeding ~200 mL on pad; admits to COCAINE use ~3 hours before pain onset (snorted; uses 'occasionally'); chronic HTN on labetalol (poor adherence past 2 weeks); 1 prior placental abruption with last pregnancy (delivered preterm 35 wk); 1 pack/day smoker. PLACENTAL ABRUPTION recognized clinically: sudden severe constant pain (not contraction-like), dark vaginal bleeding LESS than expected for degree of shock (suggesting concealed component), uterus FIRM-TENSE-BOARD-LIKE with extreme tenderness + tetanic contractions on tocometer (no relaxation), expanding abdominal girth (2 cm in 30 min), shock out of proportion to visible blood loss (HR 128, BP 88/52, lactate 3.6) — all classic for major abruption with CONCEALED hemorrhage. Bedside ultrasound at 1830 confirmed retroplacental hematoma + intact placental position (NOT placenta previa). Cervix examined per protocol (no digital exam until previa ruled out — done with US first). Continuous fetal monitoring shows CATEGORY III non-reassuring FHR (baseline drop from 145 to 105, minimal variability, recurrent late decels then prolonged deceleration to 60 lasting 4+ min currently) = EMERGENT DELIVERY indication. DIC LABS DEVELOPING: PT/INR 1.9 (baseline 1.0 1 week ago), aPTT 56, FIBRINOGEN 152 (low for pregnancy where normal 300–600), PLT 124, D-dimer markedly elevated, Hgb 8.4 (down from 12.0 last week confirming major loss including concealed component). MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL activated at 1820: 1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets ratio; cryoprecipitate 10 units for fibrinogen <200; TRANEXAMIC ACID (TXA) 1 g IV given at 1825 (WOMAN trial shows mortality benefit if within 3 hr of bleeding onset); 2 large-bore PIVs (16 g R AC, 18 g L forearm) placed at 1815; 2 L LR bolus given; 2 U PRBC + 2 U FFP completed; CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV given for citrate-related hypocalcemia after multiple transfusions; type-and-crossmatch confirmed O-negative blood (universal donor) used initially while crossmatched product being prepared. EMERGENT CESAREAN: OR alerted at 1815, ready by 1900 — anesthesia at bedside (GENERAL anesthesia planned given hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy + emergent — neuraxial relatively contraindicated with coagulopathy due to epidural hematoma risk + hypotension risk in hypovolemic patient); decision-to-incision time goal <30 min for Category III FHR — meeting goal at ~50 min from triage to incision (acceptable given simultaneous resuscitation needed); pediatrics/neonatology at delivery for late preterm 34+2 wk with non-reassuring FHR (antenatal betamethasone NOT feasible given emergent indication — would have benefit if 24+ hr available); foley placed; antibiotics CEFAZOLIN 2 g IV given for surgical prophylaxis (within 60 min before incision standard); H2 blocker famotidine 20 mg IV for aspiration prophylaxis; sodium citrate 30 mL PO non-particulate antacid immediately pre-op. ANTICIPATED: postpartum hemorrhage risk EXTREMELY HIGH due to (a) preexisting blood loss + DIC, (b) possible COUVELAIRE UTERUS (myometrial blood extravasation causing atony) discovered at C-section, (c) ongoing DIC consumption — postpartum continue MTP, oxytocin gtt + uterotonic ladder (METHERGINE contraindicated in HTN like this patient — use carboprost if no asthma OR MISOPROSTOL 800–1000 mcg PR), possible hysterectomy as last resort if uterine atony refractory + life-threatening hemorrhage. RHIG (RhoGAM) within 72 hr postpartum if Rh-positive baby (mother Rh-negative); Kleihauer-Betke test quantifies fetomaternal hemorrhage to determine if additional RHIG doses needed (significant fetomaternal bleed possible with abruption). SUBSTANCE USE: cocaine in pregnancy — UDS sent, mandatory reporting to CPS per state law (varies by state but generally required), addiction medicine consultation activated, family-centered nonjudgmental approach to support recovery + maintain family unit goals. Husband at bedside, distressed but supportive; chaplain at bedside; social work coordinating care for the two children at home (3 and 6) currently with grandmother. Pediatric team aware of expected NICU admission for late preterm baby with non-reassuring FHR delivery. Family meeting scheduled postoperatively to review course, prognosis (mother + baby), addiction medicine follow-up, postpartum mental health support (depression/PTSD risk after emergent OB hemorrhage and emergent cesarean), and discharge planning.
providerOrders: EMERGENT CESAREAN SECTION for Category III FHR + major placental abruption + maternal hemodynamic instability; decision-to-incision time goal <30 min; OR ready; anesthesia, pediatrics/neonatology at bedside; foley to gravity

ANESTHESIA: GENERAL ANESTHESIA preferred (hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy + emergent — neuraxial relatively contraindicated due to epidural hematoma risk with coagulopathy + hypotension risk in hypovolemic patient with spinal blockade); ASA aspiration prophylaxis (famotidine, sodium citrate)

MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL: 1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets ratio for ongoing OB hemorrhage; CRYOPRECIPITATE 10 units IF fibrinogen <200 mg/dL (or <150 per institutional protocol); TXA 1 g IV within 3 hr of bleeding onset (WOMAN trial mortality benefit); CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1–2 g IV per every 4 units PRBC (citrate-related hypocalcemia → impaired coagulation + cardiac function)

FLUID RESUSCITATION: large-bore PIVs (≥18 g, preferably 16 g) x 2; ISOTONIC CRYSTALLOID (LR or NS) 1–2 L initial then TRANSITION TO BLOOD PRODUCTS EARLY (crystalloid dilutes clotting factors and worsens coagulopathy); target SBP 80–100 antepartum (avoid hypertension that worsens abruption + maintain uteroplacental perfusion until delivery); cell saver NOT used in OB hemorrhage (amniotic fluid contamination concern)
TYPE & CROSSMATCH 4–6 units PRBC + 4 U FFP + 1 dose platelets + cryoprecipitate; have on standby; O-negative universal donor blood while crossmatch pending; blood bank engaged
TRANEXAMIC ACID (TXA) 1 g IV over 10 min within 3 hr of bleeding onset (WOMAN trial — mortality benefit when given early); repeat dose at 30 min if bleeding continues

ANTIBIOTICS: CEFAZOLIN 2 g IV within 60 min before incision (surgical prophylaxis); azithromycin 500 mg IV may be added per institutional protocol; metronidazole if BMI >30 or chorioamnionitis suspected; continue per protocol after delivery
Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring during transport to OR (intermittent if not feasible); monitor for further decel pattern; cesarean decision is final once Cat III FHR confirmed
Continuous maternal cardiac monitoring; pulse oximetry; q15-min vitals during active treatment then q1h

Magnesium sulfate for fetal NEUROPROTECTION NOT INDICATED at 34+2 weeks (cutoff typically <32 weeks; some extend to 33–34); would NOT delay emergent delivery for any reason; MgSO4 dosing different from eclampsia prophylaxis (lower for neuroprotection)
POSTPARTUM uterotonic ladder for atony (anticipated due to possible Couvelaire uterus + DIC + tissue distress):
STEP 1: OXYTOCIN 10 units IM at delivery of anterior shoulder + 20–40 units in 1 L LR infusion at high rate
STEP 2: METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM — ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED IN HYPERTENSION (this patient has chronic HTN — DO NOT GIVE)
STEP 3: CARBOPROST (Hemabate) 250 mcg IM q15–90 min, max 8 doses — ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED IN ASTHMA (verify; if no asthma, can use)
STEP 4: MISOPROSTOL (Cytotec) 800–1000 mcg PR — safe option for this patient given HTN contraindication to Methergine

Adjunct: continued TXA dosing, bimanual fundal massage, intrauterine balloon tamponade (Bakri) if refractory, uterine artery embolization or surgical management (B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy as life-saving last resort)

RHIG (RhoGAM) within 72 hr postpartum if Rh-positive baby (mother Rh-negative); KLEIHAUER-BETKE TEST to quantify fetomaternal hemorrhage and determine if additional RHIG doses needed (significant fetomaternal bleed possible with abruption — calculate vials based on volume of fetal blood)

DIC management: trend coags q1–2h initially; replace per criteria (PRBC for Hgb <7 OR symptomatic, FFP for INR >1.5 with bleeding/procedure, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100, platelets <50 K with bleeding); treat the underlying cause (delivery is the cure for OB DIC); vitamin K 10 mg IV (slight benefit)
Strict I&O; foley to gravity for accurate UOP; goal ≥30 mL/hr; daily weights once stable

Pediatrics/neonatology at delivery for late preterm 34+2 wk with non-reassuring FHR — expect possible respiratory issues (RDS, transient tachypnea), hypoglycemia, hypothermia, NICU admission; antenatal betamethasone NOT feasible given emergent indication
VTE prophylaxis postpartum: pneumatic compression devices intraop + continued postop; LMWH (enoxaparin) once not actively bleeding (preeclampsia or major OB hemorrhage + cesarean = elevated VTE risk)
Postpartum monitoring for Sheehan syndrome (pituitary necrosis from major hypotension) — watch for failure of milk production, fatigue, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency over weeks-months

SUBSTANCE USE management: addiction medicine consultation; SBIRT (Screening, Brief Intervention, Referral to Treatment); mandatory reporting to CPS per state law for cocaine in pregnancy (varies — coordinate with social work and ethics); family-centered nonjudgmental approach; postpartum outpatient addiction medicine + counseling
Mental health: postpartum depression + PTSD risk increased after emergent OB hemorrhage; screening at 2 and 6 week postpartum visits; perinatal mental health resources; family support

Family-centered care: husband at bedside, chaplain, social work for children's care coordination; postpartum bonding facilitation; lactation consult for breastfeeding (may be impaired by maternal illness + late preterm baby + medications)

Discharge planning: PCP within 1 week; OB postpartum visit 1–2 weeks AND 6 weeks; addiction medicine within 1 week; mental health follow-up; pediatrician for baby; close BP monitoring for postpartum hypertension; future pregnancy counseling (placental abruption recurrence ~25% after 2 prior abruptions — counsel; consider low-dose aspirin in future pregnancy 81 mg daily starting 1st trimester)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Sudden severe constant abdominal pain + dark vaginal bleeding + BOARD-LIKE TENSE UTERUS with tetanic contractions = classic PLACENTAL ABRUPTION
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic recognition. Placental abruption presents with PAINFUL DARK bleeding with TENSE BOARD-LIKE uterus (vs placenta previa which is PAINLESS BRIGHT RED bleeding with soft uterus). Constant pain (not contractions) is characteristic. Time-sensitive: emergent delivery for Cat III FHR + maternal hemodynamic compromise.

opts.text: SHOCK OUT OF PROPORTION to visible blood loss (HR 128, BP 88/52, lactate 3.6 with only 200 mL visible) — CONCEALED hemorrhage suspected
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — concealed bleeding is the trap. Concealed abruption (blood behind placenta) can be massive (1–2 L) without visible vaginal loss. Clinical signs: shock magnitude, expanding abdominal girth, board-like uterus, fetal distress. Visible loss UNDER-estimates total.

opts.text: Category III FHR (baseline 105, minimal variability, prolonged deceleration to 60 lasting 4+ min) — non-reassuring; EMERGENT delivery indication
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fetal status drives delivery urgency. Cat III = abnormal = emergent delivery. Decision-to-incision goal <30 min. Position left lateral tilt, O2 by NRB, IV fluids while transitioning to OR.

opts.text: DIC labs developing: PT/INR 1.9, aPTT 56, FIBRINOGEN 152 (low for pregnancy where normal is 300–600), D-dimer markedly elevated, PLT 124
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — coagulopathy from placental thromboplastin release. Pregnancy fibrinogen normally ELEVATED to 300–600 — a level 152 in pregnancy is LOW. MTP 1:1:1 + cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <200 + TXA. Replace per criteria; trend q1–2h.

opts.text: Risk factors stacked: prior placental abruption (~10-15% recurrence after 1), chronic HTN, cocaine use today (vasoconstriction), smoker, poor labetalol adherence
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — risk-stratification context. Cocaine use is the proximate trigger (vasoconstriction → placental hemorrhage). Multiple stacked risk factors. Discharge planning includes addiction medicine + future pregnancy counseling (recurrence ~25% after 2 prior abruptions).

opts.text: Bedside US confirmed retroplacental hematoma + intact placental position — rules out PLACENTA PREVIA; NO digital exam was performed before US (correct approach when undifferentiated 3rd-trimester bleeding)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — differential narrowing. Ultrasound before digital exam is the rule for unexplained 3rd-trimester bleeding (digital exam can precipitate massive hemorrhage in placenta previa). Confirmed abruption pathway.

opts.text: MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL activated; 2 large-bore PIVs; 2 U PRBC + 2 U FFP given; TXA 1 g IV within 3 hr of bleeding (WOMAN trial mortality benefit); calcium gluconate for citrate hypocalcemia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — resuscitation in progress. OB hemorrhage MTP standard: 1:1:1 ratio + cryo for fibrinogen <200 + TXA + Ca for citrate. Transition from crystalloid to blood products EARLY (crystalloid dilutes clotting factors and worsens coagulopathy).

opts.text: EMERGENT CESAREAN — OR ready by 1900; GENERAL ANESTHESIA preferred given hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy + emergent (neuraxial relatively contraindicated with coagulopathy due to epidural hematoma risk + hypotension risk)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — definitive treatment. Delivery is the cure for OB DIC. General anesthesia is preferred when neuraxial contraindications present (active major bleeding, coagulopathy, hemodynamic instability, time pressure). Pediatrics/neonatology at delivery for late preterm + Cat III FHR.

opts.text: Postpartum hemorrhage risk EXTREMELY HIGH due to preexisting blood loss + DIC + possible Couvelaire uterus + ongoing DIC consumption — METHERGINE CONTRAINDICATED (HTN) — use MISOPROSTOL or carboprost (if no asthma)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — anticipated complication. Couvelaire uterus = myometrial blood extravasation causing atony. Methergine contraindicated in HTN (this patient). Hemabate contraindicated in asthma (verify). Misoprostol PR is the safe option in HTN.

opts.text: Rh-negative mother → RHIG (RhoGAM) within 72 hr postpartum if Rh-positive baby; Kleihauer-Betke test to quantify fetomaternal hemorrhage and determine additional RHIG doses
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Rh sensitization prevention. Significant fetomaternal hemorrhage possible in abruption requiring extra RHIG doses calculated by Kleihauer-Betke (each vial of RHIG covers ~30 mL fetal whole blood).

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 98.6°F
opts.rat: Within normal — rules out chorioamnionitis as concurrent process (which would have fever, uterine tenderness, foul-smelling discharge). Continue to monitor.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: PLACENTAL ABRUPTION pathophysiology: PREMATURE separation of normally-implanted placenta from uterine wall → hemorrhage between placenta and uterine wall (retroplacental hematoma) → blood may exit vagina (REVEALED) OR stay trapped (CONCEALED) OR mixed; placental thromboplastin released into maternal circulation → DIC; fetal compromise from disrupted uteroplacental circulation
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. The concealed component is the most dangerous and most-missed. Maternal mortality from abruption ~1%; perinatal mortality much higher (15-20% overall, much higher in severe abruption with Cat III FHR). Time-sensitive.

opts.text: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF 3RD-TRIMESTER BLEEDING: (1) PLACENTAL ABRUPTION (painful, dark, board-like uterus, fetal distress); (2) PLACENTA PREVIA (PAINLESS, bright red, soft uterus, NO digital exam — ultrasound first); (3) UTERINE RUPTURE (sudden severe pain, loss of contractions, fetal parts palpable extra-uterine, prior C-section scar risk); (4) vasa previa (bright red at ROM); (5) bloody show; (6) cervical/vaginal source
opts.rat: CORRECT — clinical distinction matters. Each has different management. Placenta previa: NO digital exam (ultrasound first); abruption: emergent delivery for Cat III FHR; uterine rupture: emergent laparotomy. Differentiating PAINFUL vs PAINLESS bleeding is the first clinical step.

opts.text: COCAINE in pregnancy causes vasoconstriction → uterine and placental vessel vasoconstriction → placental hemorrhage and ABRUPTION; also preterm labor, low birth weight, IUGR; mandatory reporting to CPS for cocaine in pregnancy per most state laws
opts.rat: CORRECT — cocaine is a classic precipitant. Methamphetamine similar. Education + addiction medicine consultation + family-centered nonjudgmental approach + mandatory reporting per state. Counsel future pregnancy includes complete substance abstinence.

opts.text: OB DIC management: pregnancy fibrinogen normally ELEVATED to 300–600 mg/dL (NOT 200–400 like nonpregnant); a level <200 in pregnancy = LOW; CRYOPRECIPITATE for fibrinogen <100 (some institutional protocols use <150 or even <200 cutoff in OB hemorrhage); MASSIVE TRANSFUSION 1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets; TXA 1 g within 3 hr (WOMAN trial)
opts.rat: CORRECT — important OB-specific pearl. Pregnancy is a hypercoagulable state with elevated fibrinogen; baseline values differ from nonpregnant. Use OB-specific reference ranges. TXA is mortality-reducing in OB hemorrhage; underutilized in many settings.

opts.text: COUVELAIRE UTERUS (uteroplacental apoplexy) = blood from abruption extravasates into myometrium causing uterine purple/blue discoloration at C-section; the disrupted myometrium fails to contract effectively → POSTPARTUM ATONY → POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE; managed with uterotonic ladder + bimanual massage + Bakri balloon + surgical options including hysterectomy as last resort
opts.rat: CORRECT — important complication. Couvelaire uterus is a sign found at C-section; not seen on prenatal exam. Plan postpartum monitoring assuming high atony/hemorrhage risk. Uterotonic ladder modified per patient contraindications (Methergine in HTN, Hemabate in asthma — use Misoprostol in patients with both contraindications).

opts.text: POSTPARTUM UTEROTONIC LADDER in this HTN patient: OXYTOCIN first (standard); METHERGINE CONTRAINDICATED in HTN; HEMABATE contraindicated in asthma (verify); MISOPROSTOL 800–1000 mcg PR is the safe second-line in patients with HTN; bimanual fundal massage continuous throughout; TXA continued; balloon tamponade (Bakri) if refractory; uterine artery embolization or surgical management
opts.rat: CORRECT — patient-specific protocol. Always cross-reference uterotonic with contraindications (Methergine = HTN/preeclampsia; Hemabate = asthma). Misoprostol is the safe fallback in patients with both contraindications. RN responsibility to flag contraindicated medications before administration.

opts.text: GENERAL ANESTHESIA is preferred over NEURAXIAL when major hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy + emergent + active bleeding — neuraxial in coagulopathy risks epidural hematoma; spinal blockade in hypovolemia risks severe hypotension; emergency time pressure favors GA
opts.rat: CORRECT — anesthesia choice in OB emergency. Neuraxial often preferred for routine cesarean (less neonatal depression, better postop pain, mother awake for delivery), but contraindications listed favor GA in this case. ASA aspiration prophylaxis (famotidine + sodium citrate) is standard.

opts.text: TRANEXAMIC ACID (TXA) 1 g IV within 3 hr of bleeding ONSET in OB hemorrhage has MORTALITY BENEFIT (WOMAN trial, Lancet 2017); repeat dose at 30 min if bleeding continues; antifibrinolytic — stabilizes clot
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield OB pharm. WOMAN trial: 1 g TXA IV in first 3 hr of OB hemorrhage onset reduced death from bleeding (~30% reduction). Time-sensitive — earlier = better. Often UNDERUTILIZED; nursing advocacy to ensure given timely.

opts.text: Sheehan syndrome = pituitary necrosis from major maternal hypotension and shock; presents weeks-months postpartum with failure of milk production, fatigue, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency — patient with major OB hemorrhage is at risk; postpartum counseling to watch for symptoms; endocrinology referral if signs develop
opts.rat: CORRECT — important long-term complication of major OB hemorrhage. Pituitary is sensitive to hypoperfusion in pregnancy (enlarged + vulnerable). Symptoms develop weeks-to-months postpartum. Educate patient; reassess at postpartum visits.

opts.text: Perform DIGITAL CERVICAL EXAM immediately to determine cervical dilation in this 3rd-trimester bleeding patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. NO digital exam in undifferentiated 3rd-trimester bleeding until PLACENTA PREVIA is RULED OUT (typically by ultrasound). Digital exam can precipitate massive hemorrhage if placenta is over the os. Ultrasound first; if previa, plan delivery; if abruption ruled in, manage accordingly. (In this patient, US already done — confirmed retroplacental hematoma, NOT previa — so cervical exam could be done if needed for delivery planning.)

opts.text: Give METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM for postpartum uterotonic in this patient with HTN
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED — Methergine is a potent vasoconstrictor that causes severe HTN, MI, stroke, seizure in patients with HTN, preeclampsia, eclampsia. This patient has chronic HTN — DO NOT GIVE. Use MISOPROSTOL 800–1000 mcg PR or carboprost (if no asthma) instead. RN responsibility to refuse the contraindicated medication and offer the safer alternative.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Fetal Demise r/t Category III FHR with placental abruption and disrupted uteroplacental circulation
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. EMERGENT cesarean delivery is the only intervention that can save the fetus. Decision-to-incision time goal <30 min. Maternal resuscitation simultaneously. Pediatrics/neonatology at delivery.

opts.text: Risk for Maternal Exsanguination / Hemorrhagic Shock r/t placental abruption with concealed + revealed hemorrhage and developing DIC
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Maternal life-threatening hemorrhage. MTP 1:1:1, TXA, calcium replacement, large-bore access, source control (delivery). Concealed component means visible loss under-estimates total. Watch trends + hemodynamics.

opts.text: Risk for DIC / Coagulopathy r/t placental thromboplastin release in abruption + ongoing hemorrhage and consumption of clotting factors
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. DIC management: MTP 1:1:1 + cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <200 in pregnancy (elevated normal) + TXA; trend coags q1–2h; treat the cause (delivery is the cure for OB DIC); vitamin K may help marginally.

opts.text: Risk for Postpartum Hemorrhage r/t preexisting blood loss + DIC + possible Couvelaire uterus + atony; need for HTN-appropriate uterotonic ladder (NO Methergine)
opts.rat: #4 — Anticipated complication. Plan ahead: oxytocin first; MISOPROSTOL second-line in HTN (Methergine contraindicated); bimanual massage; balloon tamponade; surgical options including hysterectomy as life-saving last resort. Continued MTP + TXA postpartum.

opts.text: Family Distress / Substance Use / Mandatory Reporting r/t complex psychosocial situation with cocaine use, 2 children at home, husband, addiction medicine + CPS coordination
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Addiction medicine consultation; family-centered nonjudgmental approach; mandatory reporting per state law; social work for children's care; postpartum mental health support (depression/PTSD risk). Long-term recovery planning.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: EMERGENT CESAREAN section for Category III FHR + major placental abruption + maternal hemodynamic instability; decision-to-incision <30 min; GENERAL anesthesia preferred
opts.rat: Definitive intervention. Delivery is the cure for OB DIC and the only way to save the fetus in Cat III FHR + abruption. General anesthesia given coagulopathy + instability + time pressure.

opts.text: MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL 1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets; CRYOPRECIPITATE for fibrinogen <200 (OB threshold); TRANEXAMIC ACID (TXA) 1 g IV within 3 hr; CALCIUM GLUCONATE per every 4 units PRBC
opts.rat: Standard OB MTP for major hemorrhage with DIC. Pregnancy fibrinogen threshold higher (normal 300–600). TXA mortality benefit within 3 hr (WOMAN trial). Calcium for citrate-related hypocalcemia.

opts.text: Perform DIGITAL CERVICAL EXAM immediately to determine dilation in this undifferentiated 3rd-trimester bleeding patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG without ruling out previa first. Ultrasound was already done and confirmed retroplacental hematoma + NOT previa — so cervical exam could now be done if needed, but the general rule is NO digital exam in undifferentiated 3rd-trimester bleeding until placenta previa is ruled out by ultrasound.

opts.text: METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM as second-line uterotonic if oxytocin inadequate after delivery
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED — chronic HTN. Methergine causes severe HTN, MI, stroke, seizure. Use MISOPROSTOL 800–1000 mcg PR as second-line in HTN patients (or Hemabate if no asthma).

opts.text: OXYTOCIN 10 units IM + 20–40 units in 1 L LR infusion at high rate at delivery of anterior shoulder; MISOPROSTOL 800–1000 mcg PR for second-line uterotonic (Methergine contraindicated in HTN); BIMANUAL FUNDAL MASSAGE continuous; Bakri balloon if refractory
opts.rat: Modified uterotonic ladder for HTN patient. Misoprostol is the safe second-line in patients with Methergine contraindication. Bimanual massage continuous throughout. Bakri balloon for refractory bleeding bridges to surgical management.

opts.text: Two large-bore PIVs (16 g R AC, 18 g L forearm); 2 L LR initial bolus then transition to BLOOD PRODUCTS EARLY (crystalloid dilutes clotting factors); cautious target SBP 80–100 antepartum
opts.rat: Standard OB hemorrhage resuscitation. Large-bore access non-negotiable. Crystalloid dilution worsens coagulopathy — transition to blood products as soon as available. Cautious SBP target maintains uteroplacental perfusion antepartum + avoids hypertension that worsens abruption.

opts.text: GENERAL ANESTHESIA for emergent cesarean given hemodynamic instability + coagulopathy + emergent; ASA aspiration prophylaxis (famotidine + sodium citrate)
opts.rat: Anesthesia choice. Neuraxial contraindications present (coagulopathy → epidural hematoma; hypovolemia → spinal blockade hypotension; emergent → time pressure). General with aspiration prophylaxis. Pediatrics aware (neonatal depression risk).

opts.text: CEFAZOLIN 2 g IV within 60 min before incision for surgical prophylaxis; H2 blocker famotidine 20 mg IV for aspiration prophylaxis; sodium citrate 30 mL PO immediately pre-op
opts.rat: Standard cesarean prophylaxis. Cefazolin within 60 min before incision reduces post-cesarean infection. Aspiration prophylaxis for general anesthesia (full stomach, pregnancy).

opts.text: Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV bolus then 1 g/hr maintenance for fetal NEUROPROTECTION at 34+2 weeks
opts.rat: Not routinely indicated at 34+2 weeks (cutoff typically <32 weeks; some centers extend to 33–34). Would NOT delay emergent delivery for any reason. Mag for eclampsia prophylaxis is a different indication (not applicable here — no severe preeclampsia features).

opts.text: RHIG (RhoGAM) within 72 hr postpartum if Rh-positive baby (mother O-negative); KLEIHAUER-BETKE TEST to quantify fetomaternal hemorrhage and determine if extra RHIG needed
opts.rat: Rh sensitization prevention. Standard for Rh-negative mothers with potentially Rh-positive babies. Significant fetomaternal hemorrhage possible with abruption — extra RHIG doses calculated by Kleihauer-Betke.

opts.text: VTE prophylaxis postpartum: mechanical SCDs intraop + continued postop; LMWH (enoxaparin) once not actively bleeding and coagulation stable
opts.rat: OB hemorrhage + cesarean + multiple risk factors = elevated VTE risk postpartum. Mechanical first; pharm once bleeding controlled and coagulation stable. Continue both for at least 7 days postop.

opts.text: Addiction medicine consultation; SBIRT (Screening, Brief Intervention, Referral to Treatment); mandatory reporting to CPS per state law; family-centered nonjudgmental approach; postpartum outpatient addiction medicine + counseling
opts.rat: Comprehensive substance use response. Mandatory reporting per state law (varies). Family-centered approach maximizes recovery and maintains family unit goals. Addiction medicine for evidence-based treatment + medication. Social work for psychosocial coordination.

opts.text: Family meeting with husband + social work + addiction medicine + chaplain postoperatively to review course, prognosis, addiction medicine plan, future pregnancy counseling, mental health support; coordinate children's care during postpartum recovery
opts.rat: Family integration is part of recovery. Husband as engaged partner; children's care coordinated (currently with grandmother); postpartum mental health support; future pregnancy counseling (recurrence ~25% after 2 prior abruptions; low-dose aspirin in future pregnancy starting 1st trimester).

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Activate massive transfusion protocol; establish 2 large-bore PIVs (16 g and 18 g); send type-and-crossmatch + DIC labs + Hgb/Hct; begin LR bolus 1–2 L; bedside ultrasound to rule out previa; continuous FHR monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. MTP activation triggers blood bank readiness. Access for resuscitation. Diagnostic labs. US to rule out previa (NO digital exam first). FHR monitoring guides delivery urgency.

opts.text: TXA 1 g IV within 3 hr of bleeding onset; 2 U PRBC + 2 U FFP transfusion via large-bore IV; calcium gluconate per every 4 units PRBC; trend coags q1–2h; cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <200 (OB threshold)
opts.rat: #2 — Concurrent resuscitation. TXA mortality benefit time-sensitive. MTP 1:1:1 + cryo. Calcium for citrate. Trend labs to guide further replacement. Continue while transitioning to OR.

opts.text: Transition to OR for EMERGENT CESAREAN with GENERAL anesthesia; pediatrics/neonatology at delivery; foley placed; cefazolin within 60 min before incision; consent obtained; family informed
opts.rat: #3 — Definitive intervention. Decision-to-incision <30 min for Cat III FHR. General anesthesia given coagulopathy + instability + emergent. Surgical prophylaxis. Pediatrics for late preterm + Cat III delivery.

opts.text: Postpartum management: continue MTP if bleeding ongoing; uterotonic ladder STARTING with oxytocin (MISOPROSTOL 800–1000 mcg PR for second-line — Methergine CONTRAINDICATED in HTN); bimanual fundal massage; Bakri balloon if refractory; consider hysterectomy as last resort if life-threatening atony refractory
opts.rat: #4 — Postpartum hemorrhage management with patient-specific uterotonic ladder (NO Methergine in HTN). Bimanual massage + Bakri + surgical management as needed. Continue TXA + blood products as needed.

opts.text: RHIG (RhoGAM) within 72 hr if Rh-positive baby + Kleihauer-Betke for extra dosing; addiction medicine consultation; mandatory reporting to CPS per state law; social work for children's care + family support; family meeting; mental health screening; discharge planning with multidisciplinary follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care + family + substance use response. Long-term outpatient structure: PCP, OB postpartum 1–2 wk + 6 wk, addiction medicine within 1 wk, mental health, pediatrician for baby, future pregnancy counseling (recurrence + aspirin prophylaxis).

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Post-cesarean (90 min after admission): live infant girl 2.1 kg, Apgar 6/8, NICU on HFNC for transient tachypnea; mother stable BP 118/74, fundus firm with oxytocin gtt, lochia moderate, total EBL 1800 mL, coags improving (INR 1.4, fibrinogen 220); no further transfusion needed; transferring to PACU
opts.rat: Successful emergent delivery + resuscitation. Baby in NICU for late preterm care; mother hemodynamically stable; uterotonic ladder effective with oxytocin (no need for second-line); DIC labs improving with delivery (the cure). Continue close postpartum monitoring x 24 hr for delayed hemorrhage.

opts.text: Discharge day 4: mother stable on PO labetalol, lochia normal, baby still in NICU expected discharge in 2 wk, addiction medicine engagement initiated, mental health follow-up scheduled, social work coordinated children's care; family meeting completed
opts.rat: Successful recovery trajectory. Multidisciplinary coordination. Addiction medicine engagement is critical for sustained recovery. Mental health follow-up (postpartum depression + PTSD risk after emergent OB hemorrhage). Future pregnancy counseling on recurrence + aspirin prophylaxis.
