opts.text: 6-week postpartum visit: BP controlled on PO labetalol, lochia resolved, breastfeeding (with addiction medicine guidance on safe medications during lactation), baby home from NICU thriving, addiction medicine continued, mental health stable, family unit intact, future pregnancy plan discussed (low-dose aspirin from 1st trimester + cocaine cessation + close monitoring)
opts.rat: Sustained recovery. Addiction recovery is multi-month process. Lactation safe with most addiction treatment medications (consult pharmacy). Future pregnancy counseling on recurrence risk and prophylaxis. Continued mental health support.

opts.text: At cesarean: COUVELAIRE UTERUS noted (purple-blue myometrial discoloration); after delivery, uterus boggy and atonic despite oxytocin; misoprostol 1000 mcg PR given; Bakri balloon placed 500 mL; continued bleeding requires uterine artery embolization (IR) within hour
opts.rat: Couvelaire uterus complication. Atony refractory to first-line uterotonics. Misoprostol used (Methergine contraindicated in HTN). Bakri as bridge. IR embolization preserves fertility (better than hysterectomy when feasible). If still refractory, hysterectomy as life-saving measure.

opts.text: Hour 4 postoperative: BP drops to 88/52 with persistent vaginal bleeding 800 mL last hour despite oxytocin + misoprostol + Bakri; INR climbs back to 2.1, fibrinogen drops to 110, platelets 65 — RECURRENT DIC; mass transfusion continued; emergent reoperation for hysterectomy
opts.rat: Refractory hemorrhage + recurrent DIC. Hysterectomy as life-saving last resort when atony refractory and life-threatening hemorrhage continues. Continue MTP + TXA + correction of coags. Postoperative care intensified; ICU level.

opts.text: Hour 12: another L&D nurse, unaware of HTN history, prepares Methergine 0.2 mg IM for the new mother's heavy lochia; charge RN intercepts and educates; misoprostol given instead
opts.rat: System safety catch. Charge RN/pharmacy verification prevented contraindicated medication. Chart flag prominent; verbal communication at handoff. Reinforce education on Methergine HTN contraindication. Document near-miss.

opts.text: Day 3 postpartum: persistent fatigue + headache + difficulty initiating milk supply — concerning for early SHEEHAN SYNDROME (pituitary necrosis from major peripartum hypotension); endocrinology consulted for workup
opts.rat: Sheehan syndrome concern. Pituitary necrosis from major OB hemorrhage. Symptoms typically weeks-months postpartum but early signs possible. Endocrinology workup: TSH, free T4, AM cortisol, prolactin, FSH/LH; replacement as needed (thyroid, glucocorticoid, sex hormone). Lactation support adjusted (mother may not produce adequate milk).

opts.text: Husband expresses anger at the patient for the cocaine use that 'almost killed our baby'; social work and mental health support intervene to support family communication and constructive conflict resolution; couples counseling referred for after discharge
opts.rat: Expected family dynamics. Substance use disorders create significant relationship strain. Social work + mental health + couples counseling support the family unit. Recovery is more sustainable with family engagement (not blame); educate husband on addiction as a disease, recovery process, and his role in support.

opts.text: Persistent mild anemia Hgb 8.6 at discharge (down from 12.0 pre-event); iron supplementation prescribed; outpatient follow-up Hgb in 1 month; reassure may take months to fully recover
opts.rat: Expected residual from major hemorrhage. Iron supplementation; nutritional counseling; reassure recovery over months. Outpatient lab tracking. If profound anemia + symptoms, consider IV iron.

opts.text: Mother engaged in 6-month addiction medicine program; sustained sobriety verified by random UDS; family unit intact; baby thriving at home; future pregnancy planned with addiction medicine + OB high-risk consultation for low-dose aspirin prophylaxis
opts.rat: Best-case long-term outcome. Sustained recovery; family intact; baby thriving; future pregnancy plan in place. Recurrence risk for abruption ~25% after 2 prior — aspirin 81 mg from 1st trimester reduces preeclampsia + likely abruption risk. Continued multidisciplinary support throughout future pregnancy.

title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome from Haloperidol Decanoate Dose Increase
subtitle: Gradual Onset Over Days (vs Serotonin Syndrome Hours), LEAD-PIPE Rigidity + BRADYREFLEXIA (vs Hyperreflexia + Clonus), Stop the Antipsychotic, Bromocriptine + Dantrolene + Supportive Care — and Massive CK Says Rhabdomyolysis Is Already Underway

patient.name: R. Chen
patient.admitDate: Today, 1340 (ED via family — 2-day progression of fever, muscle stiffness, confusion; PRECEDED by haloperidol decanoate dose INCREASE 7 days ago)
patient.room: MICU Bed 8 (severe presentation; coordinated with psych consult)

vitals.time: Psych clinic 2 weeks ago (baseline, dose-increase visit)
1500 (CURRENT — after cooling + IV fluids + benzo + bromocriptine; intubated 30 min ago)

labs.v: HALOPERIDOL DECANOATE (long-acting depot antipsychotic) IM dose INCREASED from 100 mg q4wk to 150 mg q4wk by outpatient psychiatry 7 days ago (for breakthrough symptoms); also on chronic oral haloperidol 5 mg PO BID; no recent oral medication changes; no recent benzodiazepine cessation
labs.r: NMS RISK FACTORS: high-potency typical antipsychotics (HALOPERIDOL, FLUPHENAZINE) > atypicals; RAPID DOSE ESCALATION; PARENTERAL > oral routes; depot/long-acting formulations (cannot be 'stopped' immediately — drug effect persists weeks); concurrent lithium; dehydration; physical exhaustion; prior NMS episode (recurrence ~30%). Atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, ziprasidone, aripiprazole, clozapine) can also cause NMS but less commonly

labs.v: (1) Exposure to dopamine ANTAGONIST within 72 hr (HALOPERIDOL ✓ — chronic + recent dose increase + depot persists weeks); (2) SEVERE MUSCLE RIGIDITY — LEAD-PIPE rigidity throughout extremities (generalized, NOT lower-predominant); (3) FEVER (>38.5°C; >40°C in this patient — severe); (4) at least 2 of: diaphoresis, dysphagia, tremor, incontinence, altered mental status, mutism, tachycardia, elevated/labile BP, leukocytosis, elevated CK (PATIENT HAS: diaphoresis, altered mental status, tachycardia, labile BP, leukocytosis, markedly elevated CK — meets all)
labs.r: NMS DIAGNOSIS criteria. Key features that DISTINGUISH FROM SEROTONIN SYNDROME (case-060 reference): ONSET (DAYS vs hours for SS), RIGIDITY (LEAD-PIPE generalized vs LE-predominant for SS), REFLEXES (BRADYREFLEXIA or normo for NMS vs HYPERREFLEXIA + CLONUS for SS), TRIGGER (dopamine antagonist for NMS vs serotonergic for SS), TREATMENT (bromocriptine/dantrolene for NMS vs cyproheptadine for SS)

labs.v: Symptoms began ~5 days ago (initially family noticed 'slower than usual,' some muscle stiffness, sweating) — gradual progression over DAYS; today (Day 7 after dose increase) acute deterioration with high fever + severe rigidity + altered mental status prompting ED visit
labs.r: NMS ONSET TIME COURSE: gradual over 1–3 days typically (sometimes up to 2 weeks after antipsychotic initiation or dose change); CONTRAST with SEROTONIN SYNDROME = rapid onset within HOURS of triggering medication. The slow onset is a key distinguishing feature

labs.v: GENERALIZED LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY throughout all four extremities (constant resistance throughout range of motion; NOT cogwheel — no superimposed tremor; NOT spasticity — no velocity-dependence); NO CLONUS (key finding); reflexes 1+ (BRADYREFLEXIC); fine tremor present; cogwheel rigidity on wrists; bradykinesia (cannot follow simple motor commands quickly); NO ocular clonus, NO inducible clonus, NO LE-predominant rigidity
labs.r: NMS neuromuscular pattern: GENERALIZED LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY + BRADYREFLEXIA + bradykinesia + tremor. Compare SEROTONIN SYNDROME: hyperreflexia + clonus (spontaneous, inducible, ocular) + LE-predominant rigidity + tremor. Compare MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA: similar to NMS but perioperative anesthetic trigger. Compare ANTICHOLINERGIC TOXIDROME: NO rigidity, dry skin, NORMOREFLEXIA

labs.v: T 40.6°C (severe hyperthermia — >40°C is life-threatening); HR 132 (tachycardia, labile — has been swinging 90s–140s); BP 168/108 → 140/82 → 184/118 within 30 min (LABILE BP — autonomic instability hallmark); RR 26 (tachypnea); diaphoretic (drenching); flushed skin; mydriasis bilateral; UOP scant brown-tinged (myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis)
labs.r: AUTONOMIC INSTABILITY: hyperthermia + tachycardia + labile BP + diaphoresis + flushing + mydriasis. The lability of BP (swinging hypertension and normo/hypotension) is characteristic. Hyperthermia >40°C is life-threatening (multi-organ failure risk: rhabdomyolysis, AKI, DIC, hepatic injury, cardiac)

labs.v: Stuporous on arrival (responding only to vigorous stimulation, GCS 9 — E2 V2 M5); WAS more alert + mute on family report 6 hr ago (catatonic-like); now stuporous; intubated at 1430 for airway protection + procedural sedation for line placement + impending respiratory failure
labs.r: ALTERED MENTAL STATUS in NMS spectrum from catatonic-like (mute, stuporous, akinetic) to coma. The 'stuporous catatonia' pattern is characteristic. Compare serotonin syndrome which often has agitated delirium pattern. Both can progress to coma in severe cases

labs.v: CK 38,400 U/L (massively elevated; normal <200) — RHABDOMYOLYSIS from sustained muscle rigidity + hyperthermia; myoglobinuria evident in brown-tinged urine; Cr 2.4 (baseline 0.9) — AKI from myoglobinuric tubular injury
labs.r: MASSIVE CK ELEVATION (often >1000, sometimes >100,000) is characteristic of NMS — rhabdomyolysis from sustained intense muscle activity + hyperthermia. Myoglobin filters through glomerulus → tubular toxicity → AKI. Treatment: aggressive isotonic fluid 200–300 mL/hr to maintain UOP >1–2 mL/kg/hr; consider sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine pH >6.5 if severe (>10,000 CK)

labs.v: Na 138, K 5.4 (rhabdo-induced; risk of rising), Cl 102, HCO3 18 (metabolic acidosis from hypermetabolic state + lactate), BUN 38, Cr 2.4 (AKI from rhabdo), glucose 142, Ca 8.4 (mildly low — Ca binds in damaged muscle); LACTATE 4.8 (elevated)
labs.r: RHABDOMYOLYSIS LAB PATTERN: elevated K (released from damaged muscle — risk of cardiac arrhythmia), elevated phosphate (released from muscle), HYPOCALCEMIA (Ca binds in damaged muscle + chelated by released phosphate), AKI (myoglobinuric), metabolic acidosis. AGGRESSIVE HYDRATION + monitor K + ECG for hyperK signs + alkalinize urine for severe

labs.v: WBC 16.2 with bands 12% (leukocytosis from sepsis-like inflammatory response — NOT actual infection); Hgb 13.4 (stable); Plt 188; CRP elevated; procalcitonin mildly elevated (NMS itself causes inflammation; need to rule out concurrent infection)
labs.r: Stress leukocytosis common in NMS without infection; however, infection MUST be ruled out (NMS shares features with severe sepsis — both have fever, tachycardia, AMS, leukocytosis). Empiric blood cultures + UA + CXR; broad-spectrum antibiotics commonly started empirically until infection ruled out (after a few days of negative cultures and clinical context fitting NMS, discontinue)

labs.v: pH 7.28 / pCO2 32 / pO2 142 / HCO3 14 / lactate 4.8 — mixed metabolic acidosis (from hypermetabolic state + rhabdo + early AKI) + respiratory compensation; post-intubation pH 7.32 / pCO2 30 / HCO3 16
labs.r: Mixed picture: metabolic acidosis from hypermetabolic state + lactate + early AKI; mild respiratory alkalosis from compensation pre-intubation. pH improves with cooling + hydration + resolution of muscle activity

labs.n: Differential rule-outs (CRITICAL — NMS shares features with multiple conditions)
labs.v: Ruled OUT: (a) SEROTONIN SYNDROME — NO hyperreflexia, NO clonus, gradual onset over DAYS not hours, no serotonergic agent (no SSRI, SNRI, MAOI, tramadol, ondansetron, linezolid); (b) MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA — no perioperative anesthetic exposure (no succinylcholine, no halogenated inhaled anesthetic); (c) ANTICHOLINERGIC — patient is DIAPHORETIC (anticholinergic = dry skin), no recent anticholinergic medication; (d) HEAT STROKE — no environmental exposure; (e) MENINGITIS/ENCEPHALITIS — LP planned once stable + coags OK; (f) SEPSIS — empiric cultures + antibiotics, NMS shares features but specific medication trigger + classic exam pattern = NMS
labs.r: NMS is a CLINICAL diagnosis based on dopamine antagonist exposure + tetrad (hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, AMS) + lab support (CK ↑) + ruling out mimics. No single confirmatory lab. Differential diagnosis is the key cognitive task. Each differential has different treatment — misdiagnosis = wrong treatment

labs.v: Schizoaffective disorder dx age 24, on haloperidol decanoate IM for ~10 years (good response with 100 mg q4wk for past 5 years); breakthrough psychotic symptoms in last 3 months (increased family stress + work loss) → outpatient psychiatry increased depot dose to 150 mg q4wk 1 week ago; also takes oral haloperidol 5 mg PO BID, benztropine 1 mg PO BID (for EPS), sertraline 50 mg PO daily (for depression — NOT a serotonin syndrome culprit at this dose alone); no prior NMS episode; no recent illness; lives with brother (POA)
labs.r: PMH provides context. Long-term antipsychotic + recent dose increase = setup for NMS. Sertraline at standard dose alone unlikely to cause serotonin syndrome (and patient lacks hyperreflexia/clonus). Benztropine (anticholinergic) for chronic EPS — should be HELD during NMS (worsens hyperthermia by impairing sweating). No prior NMS = first episode (recurrence after future antipsychotic re-exposure is real risk ~30%)

labs.v: Foley catheter placed; UOP 25 mL/hr (low); urine dark brown-tinged (myoglobinuria); skin tenting + dry mucous membranes — dehydration from diaphoresis x days + reduced PO intake during prodrome; volume resuscitation initiated 200 mL/hr isotonic saline
labs.r: Volume depletion compounds NMS: contributes to AKI from myoglobinuria, reduces drug clearance, worsens electrolyte imbalances. AGGRESSIVE hydration with isotonic saline; goal UOP >1–2 mL/kg/hr; consider bicarbonate alkalinization for severe rhabdo. Foley for accurate UOP measurement

labs.v: No nuchal rigidity (rules out meningitis pending LP); no focal neuro deficit (no stroke); no recent surgery or anesthetic exposure (rules out MH); no environmental heat exposure (rules out heat stroke); no recent illicit drug use; no recent serotonergic medication addition; UDS negative
labs.r: Differential narrows. Rules out the alternative hyperthermic syndromes. UDS negative excludes stimulants/sympathomimetics. Antipsychotic exposure + tetrad + characteristic exam = NMS

nursesNote: 1500 — 56 y/o male with chronic schizoaffective disorder on chronic haloperidol decanoate IM (recently INCREASED from 100 mg to 150 mg q4wk 7 days ago for breakthrough symptoms) plus chronic oral haloperidol 5 mg PO BID, presented to ED at 1340 via family (brother is POA) with 2-day progression of high fever, severe muscle stiffness, drenching diaphoresis, and progressive altered mental status. Initial vitals: T 40.6°C (severe hyperthermia >40°C — life-threatening), HR 132, BP 168/108 (LABILE — autonomic instability hallmark), RR 26; physical exam SEVERE GENERALIZED LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY throughout all four extremities + BRADYREFLEXIA 1+ + drenching diaphoresis + flushing + mydriasis + stuporous mental status (GCS 9). Recognized clinically as NEUROLEPTIC MALIGNANT SYNDROME — meets diagnostic criteria: (1) exposure to dopamine antagonist within 72 hr (HALOPERIDOL chronic + recent dose increase + depot persists), (2) severe LEAD-PIPE muscle rigidity, (3) fever, (4) multiple supporting features (diaphoresis, AMS, tachycardia, labile BP, leukocytosis, markedly elevated CK 38,400). Gradual onset over DAYS (vs serotonin syndrome which would be HOURS). Key NEUROMUSCULAR DISTINCTIONS from serotonin syndrome: NMS has LEAD-PIPE GENERALIZED rigidity + BRADYREFLEXIA + NO CLONUS; SS has LE-predominant rigidity + HYPERREFLEXIA + CLONUS. Differential ruled out: no serotonergic agents (sertraline 50 mg standard dose alone unlikely + lacks hyperreflexia/clonus); no anesthetic exposure (rules out MH); diaphoretic (rules out anticholinergic); no environmental heat exposure; LP deferred until stable + coags acceptable. RHABDOMYOLYSIS confirmed (CK 38,400) with AKI (Cr 2.4, baseline 0.9) + myoglobinuria + hyperkalemia 5.4 (cardiac risk). Treatment initiated 1410: (1) STOPPED all dopamine antagonists — held next haloperidol PO dose, held oral haloperidol, depot cannot be reversed (will persist 4–6 weeks; consider hospitalization throughout effect); D/C benztropine (anticholinergic worsens hyperthermia); (2) AGGRESSIVE COOLING: ice packs axilla/groin/neck, cooling blanket, cool IV fluids, exposed skin, fan; target T ≤38.5°C; reassess q15 min; (3) BROMOCRIPTINE 2.5 mg PO/NG q8h started at 1420 (D2 dopamine agonist — replaces the blocked dopamine signaling that's central to NMS pathophysiology); will titrate up to 5–10 mg q8h based on response; (4) DANTROLENE 1–2 mg/kg IV considered for severe muscle rigidity or hyperthermia not responding to other measures (skeletal muscle relaxant — works directly on muscle; max 10 mg/kg/day); (5) LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV given for sedation + muscle relaxation (benzodiazepines effective adjunct for NMS catatonia; safe in NMS unlike serotonin syndrome); (6) AGGRESSIVE ISOTONIC FLUIDS 200–300 mL/hr NS for rhabdomyolysis prevention (target UOP >1–2 mL/kg/hr); foley placed; (7) MONITORED hyperkalemia + arrhythmia (K 5.4, ECG with mild peaked T's — gave insulin 10 units + D50 25 g IV, calcium gluconate 1 g IV for cardiac membrane stabilization since this hyperK is NOT dig-induced); (8) AVOID antipyretics (acetaminophen ineffective for muscle-activity hyperthermia — same principle as serotonin syndrome); (9) AVOID restraints (worsen muscle activity and heat); (10) Empiric BROAD-SPECTRUM ANTIBIOTICS (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) started for sepsis coverage pending workup (NMS clinically overlaps with severe sepsis; rule out both); blood cultures + UA + CXR sent. INTUBATED at 1430 for airway protection + procedural sedation + impending respiratory failure (RSI with KETAMINE 2 mg/kg + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg — avoided succinylcholine due to hyperK + rhabdo; avoided etomidate due to adrenal suppression). Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line being placed; femoral CVC for vasopressor support if needed; trending labs q4h initially. Brother at bedside, deeply distressed (helped care for patient for years, advocated for the dose increase); chaplain at bedside; social work for psychosocial support. ANTICIPATED COURSE: typically 5–14 days resolution of NMS once trigger removed + supportive care; the DEPOT formulation complicates this — drug effect persists 4–6 weeks so NMS may persist or recur during that window; PICU stay 5–10 days; mortality 10–20% if untreated, <5% with aggressive supportive care. Long-term: psychiatry will reassess medication regimen — recurrence risk with future antipsychotic re-exposure ~30%; if antipsychotic needed in future, use ATYPICAL (lower NMS risk) at LOW DOSE with slow titration; CLOZAPINE often used post-NMS (lowest NMS risk among antipsychotics, though has other risks); brother and patient education on NMS recognition for future medication changes.
providerOrders: ADMIT to MICU; psychiatric consultation; coordinate with outpatient psychiatry for ongoing management; family meeting with brother (POA)

STOP all dopamine antagonists: HOLD oral haloperidol; the haloperidol DECANOATE depot CANNOT be reversed and will persist 4–6 weeks (long-term hospitalization throughout this window may be needed); D/C BENZTROPINE (anticholinergic worsens hyperthermia by impairing sweating); D/C any other dopamine-blocking agents (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, promethazine — common antiemetics that can worsen NMS)

BROMOCRIPTINE 2.5 mg PO/NG q8h starting; titrate up to 5–10 mg q8h based on clinical response; D2 dopamine agonist — replaces the blocked dopamine signaling; continue for 10 days then taper slowly to avoid rebound NMS

DANTROLENE 1–2 mg/kg IV q6h for severe muscle rigidity or persistent hyperthermia; skeletal muscle relaxant — works directly on muscle by inhibiting calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum; max 10 mg/kg/day; monitor liver function (hepatotoxicity); typical course 7–10 days then taper
LORAZEPAM 1–2 mg IV q4–6h PRN agitation + muscle relaxation; benzodiazepines are SAFE in NMS (unlike serotonin syndrome where benzos also work); midazolam infusion if continuous needed

AGGRESSIVE COOLING for hyperthermia >38.5°C: ice packs axilla/groin/neck, cool IV fluids, cooling blanket, exposed skin, fan to facilitate evaporative cooling; TARGET ≤38.5°C; reassess q15 min; ANY temp >40°C is emergent (life-threatening at >41°C)

⚠️ AVOID ANTIPYRETICS (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) — the hyperthermia is from MUSCLE ACTIVITY not hypothalamic set-point reset; antipyretics do NOT WORK for NMS hyperthermia; same principle as serotonin syndrome; treat the muscle activity with bromocriptine + dantrolene + benzo + cooling
⚠️ AVOID physical restraints — worsen muscle activity and heat generation; chemical sedation with benzo; 1:1 observation; bed alarm; padded side rails
AGGRESSIVE IV HYDRATION with isotonic 0.9% NS 200–300 mL/hr; goal UOP >1–2 mL/kg/hr to prevent myoglobinuric AKI from rhabdomyolysis; foley to gravity for accurate UOP
Trend CK q4h; if CK >5000 or rising, consider URINE ALKALINIZATION with sodium bicarbonate (target urine pH >6.5) — controversial but used in severe rhabdomyolysis

Reassess K+ q2–4h — hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis; standard management (insulin/D50, albuterol, calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilization — note: calcium gluconate IS appropriate for non-dig hyperkalemia, unlike case-056 dig toxicity exception); hemodialysis for severe refractory hyperK or rhabdo-induced AKI
Continuous cardiac monitoring (hyperK + autonomic instability); arterial line for beat-to-beat BP; SpO2; q15-min vitals during active treatment then q1h

Empiric BROAD-SPECTRUM ANTIBIOTICS (CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV daily + VANCOMYCIN 15 mg/kg IV q12h titrated to trough 15–20) pending infection workup — NMS clinically overlaps with severe sepsis; rule out both; D/C antibiotics if cultures negative after 48–72 hr and clinical picture clearly NMS
Blood cultures x 2, UA + culture, CXR, procalcitonin; LP if meningitis/encephalitis suspected (defer until stable + coags acceptable)

INTUBATION criteria: airway protection (severe AMS), procedural sedation needed for line/foley/imaging, impending respiratory failure (severe muscle rigidity of chest wall); RSI drugs: KETAMINE 1–2 mg/kg IV (hemodynamic stability + no adrenal suppression); ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg IV for paralysis (AVOID SUCCINYLCHOLINE — hyperK risk in rhabdo, malignant hyperthermia concern in muscle-rigidity disorder; AVOID ETOMIDATE — adrenal suppression in stress state)
Mechanical ventilation per lung-protective: TV 6–8 mL/kg IDEAL body weight; PEEP 5–8 initially; FiO2 titrated to SpO2 ≥92%
Stress ulcer prophylaxis: pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily; mechanical ventilation = stress ulcer risk
VTE prophylaxis: SCDs initially; LMWH or heparin SubQ once not actively bleeding and stable (immobility + critical illness = high VTE risk)
Trend labs q4h: BMP, magnesium, phosphate (rhabdo releases phosphate), ionized calcium, ABG, lactate, CK, CBC, coags (DIC possible in severe NMS); LFTs (dantrolene hepatotoxicity)
Repeat ECG q4h for hyperK and rhythm changes

BROTHER (POA) at bedside; psychiatry consultation; long-term medication planning (recurrence risk with future antipsychotic re-exposure ~30%; if needed, use ATYPICAL antipsychotic at LOW DOSE with slow titration starting 2 weeks AFTER NMS resolution; CLOZAPINE often used post-NMS for lowest NMS risk among antipsychotics, but has other risks including agranulocytosis requiring monitoring)

Family education: what NMS is, how to recognize early signs in future (fever + muscle stiffness + sweating + altered mental status starting days after antipsychotic dose change), medical alert ID for NMS history (notify any future provider before any new antipsychotic OR dopamine-blocking antiemetic), psychiatry-pharmacy coordination at every medication change going forward
ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) is a treatment option for catatonic NMS not responding to medical management — discuss with psychiatry; can be lifesaving in refractory cases

Discharge planning: psychiatry close follow-up, outpatient medication management with shared decision-making about future antipsychotic choices, brother as informed family advocate, medical alert ID for NMS, written wallet card with medication-avoidance list including high-potency typical antipsychotics + dopamine-blocking antiemetics

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Triggering medication: HALOPERIDOL decanoate IM dose INCREASE 7 days ago + chronic oral haloperidol — high-potency typical antipsychotic; classic NMS trigger
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the cause. High-potency typical antipsychotics (haloperidol, fluphenazine) carry highest NMS risk; depot formulations complicate management (effect persists 4–6 weeks, cannot be reversed). Rapid dose escalation is a major risk factor.

opts.text: Severe HYPERTHERMIA T 40.6°C — life-threatening, multi-organ failure risk
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — emergency. Hyperthermia >40°C is life-threatening (rhabdomyolysis, DIC, AKI, hepatic injury, cardiac). Aggressive cooling + treat muscle activity (bromocriptine + dantrolene + benzo) + remove cause.

opts.text: GENERALIZED LEAD-PIPE rigidity + BRADYREFLEXIA — the NMS neuromuscular pattern; distinguishes from serotonin syndrome (hyperreflexia + clonus + LE-predominant)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic clincher. NMS = lead-pipe + bradyreflexia; SS = hyperreflexia + clonus. Treatment diverges (NMS: bromocriptine/dantrolene; SS: cyproheptadine). Misdiagnosis = wrong treatment.

opts.text: AUTONOMIC INSTABILITY: HR 132, labile BP swinging 140s–180s, diaphoresis, flushing, mydriasis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — tetrad component. Autonomic instability requires close hemodynamic monitoring; labile BP can swing to hypotension requiring pressors or extreme hypertension requiring nitroprusside or similar (avoid Methergine-class drugs).

opts.text: Altered mental status / catatonic-like presentation (stuporous, GCS 9, was mute earlier); 4th tetrad component
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — tetrad component. Mental status spectrum: catatonia → stupor → coma. Intubation for airway protection at this severity. Benzodiazepines safe + effective for catatonia in NMS (unlike many other delirium scenarios).

opts.text: CK 38,400 (massive rhabdomyolysis) + Cr 2.4 (AKI) + brown-tinged urine (myoglobinuria) + K 5.4 (rhabdo-induced hyperkalemia)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rhabdomyolysis complications. Aggressive isotonic fluids 200–300 mL/hr for UOP >1–2 mL/kg/hr to prevent myoglobinuric AKI; consider bicarbonate alkalinization if CK >5000 or rising; standard hyperK management (calcium IS appropriate here — not dig-induced).

opts.text: GRADUAL ONSET over DAYS (started 5 days ago, escalating; 7 days post dose increase) — distinguishes NMS from serotonin syndrome (rapid HOURS)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic time course. NMS develops over 1–3 days typically. SS develops within hours of triggering medication. Compound this with the medication history and exam pattern for diagnostic confidence.

opts.text: DEPOT formulation (haloperidol decanoate) — drug effect persists 4–6 WEEKS; NMS can persist or recur during this window — long-term hospitalization planning
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — chronic exposure cannot be quickly removed. Bromocriptine + dantrolene + supportive care must continue through drug effect window; ICU may be needed for extended period. Plan inpatient management duration accordingly.

opts.text: Differential ruled out (serotonin syndrome — no hyperreflexia/clonus + no serotonergic trigger; malignant hyperthermia — no anesthetic; anticholinergic — patient diaphoretic; heat stroke — no exposure; sepsis — empiric workup + antibiotics started)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic narrowing. NMS is a clinical diagnosis; rule out mimics. Each has different treatment. Sepsis particularly important to rule out because of overlap with NMS clinical picture; empiric antibiotics until cultures back.

opts.text: Brother (POA) at bedside; psychiatric history + medication adherence + family advocacy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial + medication continuity. Brother as informed POA participates in care decisions, future medication planning, NMS recognition for future medication changes. Family-centered care + chaplain + social work support.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Will be updated post-NMS: 'haloperidol — NMS history — AVOID high-potency typical antipsychotics; if antipsychotic needed, use atypical at low dose with slow titration.'

opts.text: UDS negative
opts.rat: Reassuring — rules out stimulant/sympathomimetic intoxication as alternative cause. Document and proceed with NMS workup.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / pharmacology.

opts.text: NMS pathophysiology: D2 DOPAMINE RECEPTOR BLOCKADE (by antipsychotic) → loss of dopaminergic stimulation in: HYPOTHALAMUS (thermoregulation), NIGROSTRIATAL pathway (muscle tone — extrapyramidal rigidity), MESOLIMBIC pathway (autonomic dysregulation, mental status) → TETRAD: HYPERTHERMIA + RIGIDITY + AUTONOMIC INSTABILITY + ALTERED MENTAL STATUS; massive CK from sustained rigidity
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Dopamine antagonist (antipsychotic) blocks dopamine signaling → loss of central dopaminergic regulation → tetrad. Treatment with bromocriptine restores dopamine signaling at receptor level (D2 agonist). Dantrolene works peripherally on muscle.

opts.text: NMS RISK FACTORS: HIGH-POTENCY TYPICAL antipsychotics (HALOPERIDOL, FLUPHENAZINE) > atypicals; RAPID DOSE ESCALATION; PARENTERAL > oral; depot/long-acting formulations; concurrent lithium; dehydration; physical exhaustion; warm environment; PRIOR NMS (recurrence ~30%); atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, ziprasidone, aripiprazole, clozapine) can also cause but less commonly; clozapine has lowest NMS risk among antipsychotics
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk stratification. High-potency typicals are highest risk; clozapine lowest. Other dopamine antagonists also cause NMS: METOCLOPRAMIDE (Reglan), PROCHLORPERAZINE (Compazine), PROMETHAZINE (Phenergan) — common antiemetics that need to be flagged for NMS patients.

opts.text: DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA for NMS (DSM-5 / Levenson): exposure to dopamine antagonist within 72 hr + severe muscle rigidity + fever >38°C + at least 2 of (diaphoresis, dysphagia, tremor, incontinence, AMS, mutism, tachycardia, labile BP, leukocytosis, elevated CK) — clinical diagnosis, no single confirmatory test
opts.rat: CORRECT — diagnostic framework. Multiple criteria sets exist (DSM-5, Levenson, Caroff-Mann); they overlap. Key is recognizing the constellation in the right clinical context (recent dopamine antagonist). No single confirmatory test.

opts.text: KEY DISTINCTIONS NMS vs SEROTONIN SYNDROME: ONSET (DAYS vs hours), RIGIDITY (LEAD-PIPE GENERALIZED vs LE-predominant), REFLEXES (BRADYREFLEXIA or normo vs HYPERREFLEXIA + CLONUS), TRIGGER (dopamine antagonist vs serotonergic), TREATMENT (bromocriptine/dantrolene vs cyproheptadine); both have hyperthermia + autonomic + AMS; both AVOID antipyretics (muscle activity, not set-point reset) and restraints
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield exam distinction. Both are hyperthermic syndromes with overlapping autonomic + AMS features. The neuromuscular pattern is the key clinical distinguisher. Treatment diverges; misdiagnosis = wrong treatment. Patient's medication history + reflex pattern + onset speed clinch.

opts.text: BROMOCRIPTINE 2.5–10 mg PO/NG q8h — D2 DOPAMINE AGONIST; replaces blocked dopamine signaling at receptor level; central mechanism for NMS; titrate up based on response; continue 10 days then taper slowly to avoid rebound NMS
opts.rat: CORRECT — first-line specific therapy for NMS. Cabergoline, amantadine, and apomorphine are other D2 agonists less commonly used. Bromocriptine is most accessible. Combine with dantrolene for severe cases.

opts.text: DANTROLENE 1–2 mg/kg IV q6h — SKELETAL MUSCLE RELAXANT; inhibits calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum directly in muscle; effective for severe rigidity + hyperthermia not responding to other measures; same drug used for MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA; max 10 mg/kg/day; monitor liver function (hepatotoxicity)
opts.rat: CORRECT — peripheral therapy adjunct. Dantrolene works directly on muscle. Combine with bromocriptine (central) for severe cases. Hepatotoxicity risk requires LFT monitoring. Available oral or IV.

opts.text: Benzodiazepines (LORAZEPAM) are SAFE and EFFECTIVE in NMS for sedation + muscle relaxation (unlike in delirium-on-dementia where benzos worsen the picture); first-line for NMS catatonia
opts.rat: CORRECT — NMS exception to 'benzos worsen delirium' rule. NMS catatonia responds well to benzos; safe to use. Lorazepam 1–2 mg IV q4–6h PRN; midazolam infusion if continuous needed. Document response.

opts.text: HYPERTHERMIA in NMS (and serotonin syndrome) is from MUSCLE ACTIVITY (sustained rigidity), NOT hypothalamic set-point reset — ANTIPYRETICS (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) DO NOT WORK; treat the muscle activity (bromocriptine + dantrolene + benzo + cooling)
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield exception to standard 'fever → tylenol' reflex. Sets NMS + SS apart from infection-driven fever. Active cooling + treating the muscle activity is the only way to reduce temp.

opts.text: ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) is contraindicated in NMS because of cardiac risks
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. ECT is actually a TREATMENT OPTION for catatonic NMS not responding to medical management. Can be lifesaving in refractory cases. Discuss with psychiatry. ECT acts to reset the dopaminergic dysregulation.

opts.text: Once NMS resolves, the patient can immediately restart the SAME haloperidol decanoate at the same dose with no special precautions
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Recurrence risk ~30% with re-exposure. If antipsychotic is needed, use ATYPICAL (lower NMS risk) at LOW DOSE with SLOW titration starting at least 2 WEEKS after NMS resolution; CLOZAPINE often used post-NMS for lowest NMS risk; shared decision-making with psychiatry; family education for early NMS recognition.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Severe Hyperthermia / Multi-Organ Failure r/t NMS with T 40.6°C, rhabdomyolysis, AKI, autonomic instability
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Hyperthermia >40°C is life-threatening. Aggressive cooling + treat muscle activity (bromocriptine + dantrolene + benzo) + remove cause (stop antipsychotic, but depot persists). Target ≤38.5°C.

opts.text: Rhabdomyolysis / AKI r/t sustained muscle rigidity + hyperthermia; CK 38,400, Cr 2.4, myoglobinuria, hyperK 5.4
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Aggressive isotonic fluids 200–300 mL/hr for UOP >1–2 mL/kg/hr; bicarbonate alkalinization for severe; standard hyperK management (calcium IS appropriate here unlike dig toxicity). Foley for UOP measurement; trend CK, K, Cr q4h.

opts.text: Risk for Persistent / Prolonged NMS r/t depot antipsychotic (haloperidol decanoate) effect persisting 4–6 weeks; ongoing dopamine blockade
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The depot cannot be reversed. Bromocriptine + dantrolene + supportive care must continue through the drug effect window. Plan extended ICU stay. Watch for recurrence as initial therapy is tapered.

opts.text: Risk for Inadvertent Re-exposure / Cascade r/t common antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, promethazine) are dopamine antagonists and can WORSEN NMS
opts.rat: #4 — Important system safety. Flag chart prominently: 'NMS history — AVOID all dopamine antagonists including high-potency antipsychotics + metoclopramide + prochlorperazine + promethazine.' EMR alert; allergy list update; pharmacy verification. Use ondansetron for antiemetic if needed.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit / Family Distress r/t NMS recognition for future medication changes; brother as long-term advocate; recurrence risk ~30% with future antipsychotic re-exposure
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Brother education on NMS early signs, medication safety advocacy, medical alert ID, wallet card with avoidance list, psychiatry coordination at every future medication change.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: STOP all dopamine antagonists: hold haloperidol PO; depot cannot be reversed (will persist 4–6 weeks); D/C BENZTROPINE (anticholinergic worsens hyperthermia); D/C any other dopamine-blocking agents
opts.rat: Removes the modifiable cause. Depot persistence requires ongoing supportive therapy. Anticholinergic worsens hyperthermia by impairing sweating — must D/C. Common antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, promethazine) are dopamine antagonists — flag chart.

opts.text: BROMOCRIPTINE 2.5 mg PO/NG q8h titrated up to 5–10 mg q8h; D2 dopamine agonist; continue 10 days then slow taper
opts.rat: First-line specific therapy. Restores dopamine signaling at receptor level. Slow taper to avoid rebound NMS. Combine with dantrolene for severe cases.

opts.text: DANTROLENE 1–2 mg/kg IV q6h for severe muscle rigidity + hyperthermia; max 10 mg/kg/day; monitor LFTs (hepatotoxicity)
opts.rat: Peripheral muscle relaxant; same drug used for malignant hyperthermia. Combine with bromocriptine (central) for severe cases. LFT monitoring for hepatotoxicity.

opts.text: Acetaminophen 1 g IV q6h scheduled for fever
opts.rat: ⚠️ DOES NOT WORK for NMS hyperthermia (muscle activity origin, not hypothalamic set-point reset). Same principle as serotonin syndrome. Treat the muscle activity with bromocriptine + dantrolene + benzo + active cooling.

opts.text: LORAZEPAM 1–2 mg IV q4–6h PRN agitation + muscle relaxation; midazolam infusion if continuous needed
opts.rat: Benzodiazepines are SAFE and EFFECTIVE in NMS (unlike delirium-in-dementia where benzos worsen). NMS catatonia responds to benzos. Safe adjunct.

opts.text: Aggressive COOLING: ice packs axilla/groin/neck, cool IV fluids, cooling blanket, exposed skin, fan; target T ≤38.5°C; reassess q15 min
opts.rat: Active external cooling combined with treating muscle activity. Continuous reassessment. Monitor for shivering — adds heat; deepen sedation if needed. Critical for >40°C.

opts.text: Physical 4-point restraints for agitation + safety
opts.rat: ⚠️ WORSEN hyperthermia by increasing muscle activity. Use chemical sedation with benzo; 1:1 observation; bed alarm; padded side rails. Severe cases needing pharmacologic paralysis use non-depolarizing agents (NOT succinylcholine due to hyperK + MH concern).

opts.text: Aggressive IV NS 200–300 mL/hr for rhabdomyolysis prevention; goal UOP >1–2 mL/kg/hr; foley to gravity for UOP measurement; consider bicarbonate alkalinization if CK >5000 or rising
opts.rat: Prevents myoglobinuric AKI. Adequate hydration is primary. Bicarbonate alkalinization for severe; mannitol historically used; current evidence weak.

opts.text: Hyperkalemia management: insulin 10 units IV + D50 25 g IV + albuterol 10 mg neb + CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV for cardiac membrane stabilization
opts.rat: Standard hyperK protocol — and calcium IS appropriate here because this is NOT dig-induced hyperkalemia (the dig toxicity exception from case-056 does not apply). Shift therapies safe; calcium for cardiac membrane stabilization standard.

opts.text: If intubation needed for severe progression: SUCCINYLCHOLINE 1 mg/kg IV for rapid sequence intubation
opts.rat: ⚠️ AVOID — hyperK risk in rhabdomyolysis + potential malignant hyperthermia concern in muscle-rigidity disorder. Use NON-DEPOLARIZING paralytic (ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg, or CISATRACURIUM). Ketamine for induction; AVOID etomidate (adrenal suppression in stress).

opts.text: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (CEFTRIAXONE 2 g IV daily + VANCOMYCIN 15 mg/kg IV q12h) pending infection workup — NMS clinically overlaps with severe sepsis
opts.rat: Common practice — NMS and sepsis share features (fever, tachycardia, AMS, leukocytosis); empiric coverage until cultures back. D/C if cultures negative after 48–72 hr and clinical picture clearly NMS.

opts.text: Document NMS in EHR allergy/interaction list permanently as 'HALOPERIDOL — NMS — AVOID high-potency typical antipsychotics; if antipsychotic needed, atypical at low dose with slow titration starting ≥2 weeks after NMS resolution; CLOZAPINE often chosen post-NMS (lowest NMS risk)'; flag common antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, promethazine) as dopamine antagonists to avoid
opts.rat: Permanent safety documentation. Medical alert ID + wallet card. Brother as informed advocate. Pharmacy verification of every new prescription. Future psychiatry coordination at every medication change. EMR alert prevents inadvertent re-exposure.

opts.text: ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) as a treatment option for catatonic NMS not responding to medical management
opts.rat: ECT IS a treatment option for refractory NMS — can be lifesaving. Discuss with psychiatry. Reserved for cases not responding to standard medical management.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: STOP all dopamine antagonists; AGGRESSIVE COOLING (ice packs, cool IV fluids, cooling blanket, exposed skin, fan; target ≤38.5°C); document
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stop the cause + treat the most life-threatening manifestation (severe hyperthermia). Cooling is bedside immediately. Hold next antipsychotic dose; D/C anticholinergics.

opts.text: LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV for sedation + muscle relaxation; BROMOCRIPTINE 2.5 mg PO/NG q8h to start (D2 agonist replaces blocked signaling); DANTROLENE 1–2 mg/kg IV q6h for severe rigidity
opts.rat: #2 — Specific NMS pharmacology. Benzos are safe in NMS. Bromocriptine restores dopamine. Dantrolene relaxes muscle directly. Combination therapy for severe cases.

opts.text: Aggressive IV NS 200–300 mL/hr; foley to gravity; goal UOP >1–2 mL/kg/hr; trend CK, BMP, lactate q4h; standard hyperK management (insulin/D50, albuterol, calcium gluconate)
opts.rat: #3 — Rhabdomyolysis + AKI prevention; hyperK management. Calcium IS appropriate here (not dig-induced). Trend labs to guide further care. UOP measurement essential.

opts.text: Coordinate MICU transfer; intubate if criteria met (RSI with KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM, AVOID succinylcholine + etomidate); arterial line; continuous cardiac monitoring; empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for sepsis coverage pending workup; flag chart with NMS alert
opts.rat: #4 — Higher-level monitoring + simultaneous sepsis workup. ICU level for severe NMS. Avoid succinylcholine (hyperK + MH) and etomidate (adrenal suppression). Empiric antibiotics until cultures back.

opts.text: Document NMS in EHR permanently; psychiatry consult; family meeting with brother (POA) on long-term medication planning (recurrence risk 30%, future use of atypical at low dose with slow titration, or clozapine; medical alert ID; wallet card with avoidance list including dopamine-blocking antiemetics); coordinate outpatient follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained recurrence prevention + family advocacy. Brother empowered as informed advocate. Psychiatry leads long-term medication planning. Discharge structure includes medication safety education, EMR alert, medical alert ID, written wallet card.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 3 (3 days of bromocriptine + dantrolene + benzo + cooling + IV fluids): T 38.4°C (down from 40.6); HR 92 (down from 132); BP 124/74 (less labile); reflexes 2+ (improved from 1+); rigidity less pronounced; CK 12,400 (down from 38,400); Cr 1.6 improving; mental status improving (responding to voice, following commands)
opts.rat: Excellent response to NMS treatment. Continue bromocriptine + dantrolene; taper as clinical improves; remember depot persists 4–6 weeks so vigilance required for recurrence. Continue rhabdomyolysis monitoring. Plan extubation when fully alert and protecting airway.

opts.text: Day 10: at baseline cognitively; extubated; eating regular diet; ambulating with PT; CK 480 (resolving); Cr 0.9 (baseline); medication regimen reviewed with psychiatry — DO NOT restart haloperidol; plan to introduce CLOZAPINE 2 weeks post-NMS at low dose with slow titration + close monitoring
opts.rat: Successful recovery. Long-term medication plan with safer alternative (clozapine lowest NMS risk; requires WBC monitoring for agranulocytosis). Family education completed. Outpatient psychiatry follow-up coordinated.

opts.text: Discharge day 14: stable on clozapine 25 mg daily (low starting dose with planned slow titration); medical alert ID worn; brother has written list of medications to avoid (high-potency typicals + dopamine-blocking antiemetics); pharmacy verification process in place; outpatient psychiatry weekly x 6 weeks then standard interval; PCP coordinated
opts.rat: Comprehensive discharge. Long-term safety plan. Brother as informed advocate. Pharmacy verification process. Outpatient structure with intensive early monitoring. NMS-history alerts in place at multiple levels.

opts.text: Hour 8: cross-cover nurse gives PROMETHAZINE 25 mg IV for nausea despite chart alert; patient's temperature climbs back to 39.8°C + rigidity worsens within 4 hr — IATROGENIC RE-EXPOSURE
opts.rat: Iatrogenic worsening — Beers/NMS-list dopamine-blocking antiemetic given despite chart alert. Promethazine is dopamine antagonist. Should have used ondansetron instead. RN responsibility to refuse contraindicated medication. Document near-miss; system flag enhancement; education for cross-cover.

opts.text: Day 5: develops pneumonia (CXR with new R lower lobe infiltrate; new fever; rising WBC); sputum grows Klebsiella; antibiotics broadened to piperacillin-tazobactam; continue NMS management; mechanical ventilation continues
opts.rat: Hospital-acquired infection complication during prolonged critical illness. Broaden antibiotic coverage per pathogen and sensitivities. Continue NMS-specific management. Risk factor for prolonged ICU stay + further deconditioning.

opts.text: Day 7: family decides to honor patient's previously-expressed wishes for no aggressive ICU escalation given prolonged trajectory and patient's pre-illness functional status; transitions to comfort-focused care with bromocriptine continued for NMS symptom relief; chaplain + palliative care + brother as POA leading
opts.rat: Goals-of-care decision by family per patient's wishes. Comfort-focused care. Continue NMS-symptom-directed therapy (bromocriptine for muscle relaxation + comfort). Chaplain + palliative care + family support. Document goals carefully; respect patient autonomy through POA.

opts.text: Day 4: develops mild liver enzyme elevation (AST 142, ALT 168) on dantrolene; dantrolene reduced; LFTs trend down over next few days; continues bromocriptine + supportive care; clinical NMS improving
opts.rat: Dantrolene hepatotoxicity — common side effect; usually reversible with dose reduction or discontinuation. Reduce dose or hold; reassess LFTs daily; bromocriptine + supportive care continue. If severe, D/C dantrolene; rely on bromocriptine + benzo + cooling.

opts.text: Day 14 (depot still active): NMS symptoms recur with mild fever + slight rigidity reemergence as bromocriptine + dantrolene tapered; restart full-dose bromocriptine + dantrolene; symptoms resolve again; plan to continue full-dose therapy through depot effect window
opts.rat: Recurrence as tapering attempted during depot effect window. Restart full-dose; continue through depot effect (~4–6 weeks). The taper itself is the cause; vigilance is required. Document recurrence; communicate to outpatient team.

opts.text: Persistent mild residual rigidity at 1-month outpatient visit; otherwise back at baseline; CK normal; renal function recovered; clozapine titrating up; brother as advocate continuing
opts.rat: Expected slow resolution. Some residual symptoms can persist for weeks even after depot effect fully gone. Continue clozapine titration. Outpatient psychiatry close monitoring. Brother as advocate at every medical encounter.

opts.text: 6-month follow-up: stable on clozapine at therapeutic dose; weekly WBC monitoring for agranulocytosis per clozapine protocol; psychiatric symptoms controlled; family unit intact; no NMS recurrence; outpatient psychiatry + PCP + pharmacy coordination ongoing
opts.rat: Best-case long-term outcome. Clozapine effectively manages schizoaffective disorder without NMS recurrence. Required weekly CBC monitoring (agranulocytosis risk ~1%); long-term cost is worthwhile vs NMS recurrence on typical antipsychotic. Ongoing multidisciplinary coordination.

title: Myxedema Coma in 72-Year-Old with Stopped Levothyroxine + UTI Precipitant
subtitle: Hypothermia + AMS + Precipitant = Myxedema Coma; HYDROCORTISONE BEFORE LEVOTHYROXINE (Adrenal Crisis Risk), PASSIVE Rewarming (Not Active — Shock Risk), Hypertonic Saline for Severe Hyponatremia, Avoid Sedatives

patient.name: G. Larsen
patient.code: Full Code (POA: son; advance directive 'full code, comfort if poor prognosis')
patient.admitDate: Today, 0610 (ED via EMS — daughter found mom obtunded, cold, in cold house overnight; thermostat broken; daughter does daily check-in)
patient.room: MICU Bed 4

vitals.time: Last endocrine clinic visit 9 months ago (baseline on levothyroxine)
0830 (CURRENT — passive rewarming, IV fluids, IV hydrocortisone + levothyroxine given; intubated 60 min ago)

labs.v: TSH 124 mU/L (markedly elevated; normal 0.4–4.0) — confirms PRIMARY hypothyroidism with severe undertreatment/non-adherence; FREE T4 0.2 ng/dL (very low; normal 0.8–1.8); FREE T3 0.8 pg/mL (low; normal 2.3–4.2); ANTI-TPO antibody positive (chronic Hashimoto's thyroiditis baseline diagnosis)
labs.r: Myxedema coma is the extreme end of hypothyroidism spectrum; TSH usually markedly elevated in PRIMARY hypothyroidism (this patient — pituitary intact, thyroid failure). SECONDARY (central/pituitary) hypothyroidism has LOW or inappropriately normal TSH despite low T4 — this would require different management because the pituitary-adrenal axis is also at risk. Both require hydrocortisone before T4

labs.v: Patient stopped LEVOTHYROXINE 75 mcg PO daily ~6 WEEKS ago because she 'felt fine and didn't think she needed it anymore'; was diagnosed with Hashimoto's hypothyroidism 8 years ago; baseline TSH on therapy was 2.4 (well-controlled); had been on levothyroxine continuously until 6 weeks ago
labs.r: MEDICATION NON-ADHERENCE in chronic conditions like hypothyroidism is a major preventable cause of decompensation. Levothyroxine has long half-life (~7 days); decompensation typically takes weeks to develop after stopping. Patient education + family advocacy + simplified dosing + linking to chronic disease consequences is key for prevention

labs.v: URINARY TRACT INFECTION confirmed: UA cloudy, LE large, nitrites POSITIVE, WBC >50/hpf; urine culture growing >100K E. coli (Gram stain Day 1, full sensitivities pending). Plus COLD EXPOSURE (broken thermostat, ambient ~50°F overnight per family) which compounded the already-decompensated state. UTI symptoms were missed by patient due to baseline confusion + hypothyroidism-related lethargy
labs.r: MYXEDEMA COMA PRECIPITANTS: infection (most common — UTI, pneumonia), cold exposure, surgery, trauma, MI/CVA, GI bleed, SEDATIVES (opioids, benzos), MISSED THYROID HORMONE doses, hypothyroidism that progressed undiagnosed. Often MULTIPLE precipitants stack (this patient: stopped meds + cold exposure + UTI = perfect storm)

labs.v: Obtunded on arrival (GCS 7 — E1 V2 M4; responding only to deep painful stimuli); pupils sluggish but equal 3 mm; no focal neuro deficit; INTUBATED at 0720 for airway protection + impending respiratory failure (hypoventilation RR 8 + CO2 retention on ABG)
labs.r: MYXEDEMA COMA AMS spectrum from confusion → lethargy → stupor → coma. CO2 NARCOSIS from hypoventilation contributes to AMS. The 'COMA' in myxedema coma is somewhat misleading — most patients are stuporous/lethargic rather than truly comatose. Intubation often needed for airway protection + hypoventilation

labs.v: Core T 32.4°C (90.3°F) on rectal thermometer — SEVERE hypothermia (32–34 mild, 28–32 moderate, <28 severe; this patient at threshold); PASSIVE REWARMING initiated with warmed blankets, room temperature increased, warmed IV fluids; goal slow rewarming 0.5–1°C/hr; avoid active external rewarming (vasodilation → shock); avoid active internal rewarming unless very severe (<28°C)
labs.r: PASSIVE REWARMING (warm blankets, warm environment, warmed IV fluids) is preferred for myxedema-coma hypothermia. ACTIVE EXTERNAL rewarming (warming pads, immersion) can cause vasodilation → shock + acidosis from cold blood return. ACTIVE INTERNAL rewarming (peritoneal/pleural lavage, ECMO) reserved for severe hypothermia <28°C with cardiac instability

labs.v: Na 116 mEq/L (severe — normal 135–145; symptomatic at <120); chloride 88; HYPONATREMIA from impaired free water excretion in hypothyroidism (reduced GFR + impaired ADH suppression) + UTI-related ADH effects
labs.r: HYPONATREMIA in myxedema coma is multifactorial: impaired free water excretion, reduced GFR, SIADH-like effect. Treatment: HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NS) 100 mL bolus over 10 min for severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizure, coma) — repeat as needed; goal rise 4–6 mEq/L in first 6 hr to relieve symptoms; AVOID OVERCORRECTION (>10–12 mEq/L in 24 hr) → OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (formerly central pontine myelinolysis — permanent neurologic injury)

labs.v: Glucose 56 mg/dL (low — fasted state + hypothyroidism reduces gluconeogenesis + adrenal axis concern) → D50W 1 amp IV bolus at 0620; D10W maintenance at 75 mL/hr; recheck BG q1h
labs.r: HYPOGLYCEMIA in myxedema coma from reduced hepatic gluconeogenesis + glycogen stores + concurrent adrenal insufficiency (cortisol normally raises glucose). Treat with D50W bolus + D10W maintenance; recheck q1h; monitors for adrenal contribution

labs.v: STAT cortisol drawn at 0625 — pending (4 hr turnaround); EMPIRIC HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV given at 0630 BEFORE LEVOTHYROXINE — because: (1) patient may have undiagnosed PRIMARY ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type II often pairs Hashimoto's + Addison's), (2) may have CENTRAL hypothyroidism with concurrent adrenal axis failure, (3) giving T4 first to a patient with adrenal insufficiency precipitates ADRENAL CRISIS by increasing metabolic demand on already-failing adrenals
labs.r: THE GOLDEN RULE OF MYXEDEMA COMA TREATMENT: HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV BEFORE LEVOTHYROXINE — covers possible undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency and prevents adrenal crisis precipitated by thyroid hormone replacement increasing metabolic demand. Continue hydrocortisone 50–100 mg IV q6–8h pending cortisol result; if cortisol high, taper; if low or borderline, continue replacement

labs.v: LEVOTHYROXINE (L-T4) 400 mcg IV LOADING DOSE given at 0700 (after hydrocortisone), then 100 mcg IV daily maintenance; some centers also give LIOTHYRONINE (T3) 5–20 mcg IV q8h x 24–48 hr for faster effect (T4 takes days to convert to active T3); cardiac monitoring during T4 administration (arrhythmia risk in elderly with cardiac disease)
labs.r: T4 LOADING for myxedema coma: 200–400 mcg IV initial then 50–100 mcg IV daily; lower starting dose (50–100 mcg) in patients with cardiac disease to avoid precipitating MI/arrhythmia. Some institutions add T3 for faster effect. Oral T4 not effective in coma (poor absorption from edematous gut); transition to oral once awake + bowel function returned

labs.v: Pre-intubation ABG: pH 7.18 / pCO2 78 (severe HYPERCAPNIA from hypoventilation — CO2 narcosis contributing to AMS) / pO2 64 / HCO3 28 / SpO2 88% RA — RESPIRATORY ACIDOSIS from hypoventilation; post-intubation pH 7.32 / pCO2 44 / pO2 142 (improved)
labs.r: MYXEDEMA COMA respiratory: hypoventilation from reduced central respiratory drive + muscle weakness + obesity contribution; CO2 retention → CO2 narcosis (worsens AMS); pleural effusions common (myxedema causes mucinous infiltration). Intubation for airway protection + hypercapnia + hypoxia. Mechanical ventilation with monitoring for trend

labs.v: Sinus bradycardia HR 44 on arrival; ECG: prolonged QT 480 ms, low voltage in all leads (pericardial effusion may contribute), nonspecific T-wave changes; bedside ECHO: small pericardial effusion (~150 mL — common in myxedema), reduced LV function EF 40% (chronic from hypothyroidism, will improve with treatment); no acute MI on troponin/ECG
labs.r: MYXEDEMA CARDIAC: BRADYCARDIA, low voltage on ECG (effusion), prolonged QT (hypokalemia + hypothyroidism), pericardial effusion (mucinous; not usually tamponade-causing), reduced LV function (improves with thyroid replacement). Monitor for arrhythmia during T4 replacement (sudden metabolic increase). Cardiology consultation if EF significantly reduced

labs.v: CBC: WBC 14.6 (UTI), Hgb 10.4 (chronic anemia of hypothyroidism), Plt 188; BMP: K 3.4 (mildly low — supports QT), Cl 88, HCO3 28, BUN 38, Cr 1.4 (baseline 1.0, AKI from dehydration); CK 480 (elevated — myxedema causes myopathy + hypothermia adds); albumin 2.4 (low — chronic illness + frailty); cholesterol 312 (markedly elevated, classic for hypothyroidism)
labs.r: MYXEDEMA LAB PATTERN: hyponatremia (from impaired free water excretion), hypoglycemia, anemia (chronic from reduced erythropoietin response), mild AKI, elevated CK (myopathy), elevated cholesterol (reduced LDL clearance), hypoalbuminemia (chronic), prolonged INR can occur (hepatic)

labs.v: Dry skin, doughy NON-PITTING EDEMA of face + hands + lower extremities (mucinous infiltration — true 'myxedema'); coarse hair (some loss); macroglossia (large tongue contributing to airway concerns); hoarse voice (recalled by daughter from before coma); hypoactive bowel sounds (ileus from reduced gut motility); cold, dry, scaly skin; delayed deep tendon reflexes (slow relaxation phase — 'hung-up' reflexes); hair loss in eyebrows (lateral 1/3)
labs.r: MYXEDEMA = mucinous edema from glycosaminoglycan deposition (non-pitting, doughy); distinguishes from cardiogenic/renal edema (pitting). Macroglossia + edema = airway concerns. 'Hung-up' reflexes (slow relaxation) classic for hypothyroidism. Ileus = impaired gut motility (NPO + IV fluids until resolved)

labs.v: CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV daily started at 0640 for UTI (E. coli); coverage adjusted once full sensitivities back; treat 7–14 days for complicated UTI in elderly; UA cleared by Day 3; cultures negative by Day 5
labs.r: TREAT THE PRECIPITANT alongside the myxedema coma. Empiric ceftriaxone for E. coli UTI in elderly female (good coverage; appropriate renal dose for eGFR ~40). Will narrow per sensitivities (ampicillin/sulbactam, nitrofurantoin avoided if eGFR <30, cephalexin etc.)

labs.v: DAUGHTER (POA) and SON at bedside; advance directive 'full code, comfort if poor prognosis'; mother is widowed, lives alone; daughter does daily check-in (which today identified the problem); medication non-adherence has been ongoing concern over past year; family open to discussing transition to assisted living after recovery
labs.r: Goals of care + family advocacy. Daughter as informed POA, engaged. Discharge planning should address medication adherence (pill box, family monitoring, medication reconciliation), living situation (independent vs assisted living), safety (cold exposure prevention, fall risk), close follow-up

nursesNote: 0830 — 72 y/o female with known Hashimoto's hypothyroidism brought to ED at 0610 via EMS after daughter (who does daily check-ins) found her obtunded, cold, in a cold house overnight (thermostat broken, ambient ~50°F). Initial vitals: T 32.4°C (severe hypothermia), HR 44 (severe bradycardia), BP 88/52, RR 8 (hypoventilation), SpO2 88% RA, GCS 7. Recognized as MYXEDEMA COMA — the extreme decompensation of severe hypothyroidism — with the classic TRIAD: hypothermia + altered mental status + identified precipitant (in this case THREE precipitants stacked: stopped levothyroxine 6 weeks ago because she 'felt fine,' cold exposure overnight from broken thermostat, and now-confirmed E. coli UTI). Confirmed labs: TSH 124 (markedly elevated, primary hypothyroidism), Free T4 0.2 (very low), Free T3 0.8 (low), Na 116 (severe hyponatremia), glucose 56 (hypoglycemia), pH 7.18 with pCO2 78 (severe CO2 retention/respiratory acidosis from hypoventilation — contributing to CO2 narcosis and AMS), Cr 1.4 (mild AKI), CK 480 (myopathy + hypothermia), albumin 2.4 (frailty), cholesterol 312 (classic hypothyroid lipid pattern). ECG: sinus bradycardia, prolonged QT 480, low voltage (small pericardial effusion ~150 mL on bedside echo, EF 40%). Physical exam shows classic myxedema features: dry skin, doughy NON-PITTING EDEMA of face/hands/extremities (mucinous infiltration), macroglossia, hoarse voice (per daughter), hypoactive bowel sounds (ileus), hung-up reflexes (slow relaxation phase), lateral eyebrow hair loss. Treatment initiated per myxedema coma protocol in correct sequence: (1) STAT cortisol drawn at 0625 (pending; covers possible undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency — autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type II often pairs Hashimoto's + Addison's); (2) EMPIRIC HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV given at 0630 — BEFORE LEVOTHYROXINE — to prevent adrenal crisis precipitated by thyroid hormone replacement increasing metabolic demand on already-failing adrenals (THE GOLDEN RULE OF MYXEDEMA COMA TREATMENT); continue hydrocortisone 50–100 mg IV q6–8h pending cortisol result + ongoing management; (3) LEVOTHYROXINE 400 mcg IV LOADING DOSE given at 0700 (after hydrocortisone), then 100 mcg IV daily maintenance; lower starting dose can be used in cardiac patients but loading dose appropriate here; cardiac monitoring during T4 administration; (4) PASSIVE REWARMING with warmed blankets, room temperature increased, warmed IV fluids; goal slow rewarming 0.5–1°C/hr — AVOIDED active external rewarming (vasodilation → shock + cold blood return → acidosis); active internal rewarming reserved for severe hypothermia <28°C with cardiac instability; (5) HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NS) 100 mL bolus over 10 min given at 0640 for severe symptomatic hyponatremia (Na 116 with coma/seizure risk), goal Na rise 4–6 mEq/L in first 6 hr to relieve symptoms — AVOID OVERCORRECTION (>10–12 mEq/L in 24 hr → osmotic demyelination syndrome, permanent neurologic injury); recheck Na q2h; (6) HYPOGLYCEMIA treated with D50W 1 amp IV at 0620 + D10W maintenance at 75 mL/hr; BG q1h; (7) CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV at 0640 for UTI (E. coli on Gram stain, full sensitivities pending); (8) AVOID SEDATIVES (opioids, benzodiazepines worsen hypoventilation + AMS); when intubated at 0720 used KETAMINE for induction + ROCURONIUM for paralysis (avoided succinylcholine due to hypothermia + AKI hyperK risk; avoided etomidate due to adrenal suppression in compromised adrenal axis). Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line placed for beat-to-beat BP + ABG; femoral CVC placed for vasopressors + reliable access; DOPAMINE 5 mcg/kg/min IV gtt for inotropic + chronotropic support (preferred over pure vasoconstrictor in bradycardia + hypotension; can switch to norepi if SVR-only need); foley placed for accurate UOP; warming bear hugger to room and bedding (passive); intubated patient kept on lung-protective ventilation. ANTICIPATED COURSE: myxedema coma mortality is HIGH 30–60% even with treatment; aggressive supportive care + correct sequence of hormones + treating the precipitant gives best chance. Slow correction over days-weeks; hypothermia resolves over hours-days; mental status improves over days-weeks; muscle/skin findings improve over weeks-months. Family meeting with daughter (POA) and son to discuss prognosis, expected trajectory, discharge planning. Long-term: medication adherence is the modifiable factor — pill box, family medication oversight, dose simplification if possible, education on chronic disease consequences ('feeling fine' on chronic disease meds doesn't mean cured); social work for living situation reassessment (independent vs assisted living); cold exposure prevention (working thermostat, neighbor check-ins, family weekly visits); psychiatric screening for cognitive impairment contributing to medication non-adherence.
providerOrders: ADMIT to MICU; geriatrics + endocrinology + infectious disease consultations; advance directive 'full code, comfort if poor prognosis' confirmed; daughter (POA) as healthcare proxy
MYXEDEMA COMA TREATMENT (correct sequence — HYDROCORTISONE BEFORE LEVOTHYROXINE):
STAT CORTISOL level drawn (pending; covers possible undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency)

EMPIRIC HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV (or hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h) given BEFORE levothyroxine; continue 50–100 mg IV q6–8h pending cortisol result; if cortisol high (>18 mcg/dL), taper hydrocortisone over 24–48 hr; if low or borderline, continue replacement and workup for primary vs secondary adrenal insufficiency

LEVOTHYROXINE (L-T4) LOADING DOSE 200–400 mcg IV (typically 400 mcg in younger patients; 200 mcg in elderly or cardiac patients to avoid precipitating MI/arrhythmia), then 50–100 mcg IV daily maintenance; cardiac monitoring during T4 administration
Some centers also give LIOTHYRONINE (T3) 5–20 mcg IV q8h x 24–48 hr for faster effect (T4 takes days to convert to T3) — controversial, some evidence supports in severe cases; cardiac risk higher
Transition to ORAL levothyroxine once awake + bowel function returned (oral not effective in coma due to poor absorption from edematous gut + ileus)
PASSIVE REWARMING for hypothermia: warm blankets, room temperature increased, warmed IV fluids (40–42°C); goal slow rewarming 0.5–1°C/hr
AVOID ACTIVE EXTERNAL REWARMING (warming pads, immersion) for moderate hypothermia 28–32°C — risks vasodilation → shock + cold acidotic blood return; can use for severe <28°C with cardiac instability
ACTIVE INTERNAL REWARMING (warm IV fluids, warm humidified O2, peritoneal/pleural lavage, ECMO) reserved for severe hypothermia <28°C with cardiac arrest/instability

HYPONATREMIA (Na 116, severe symptomatic): HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NS) 100 mL bolus over 10 min for symptomatic severe hyponatremia (seizure, coma); repeat 100 mL boluses up to 3 doses if symptoms persist; GOAL Na RISE 4–6 mEq/L in first 6 hr to relieve symptoms; AVOID OVERCORRECTION >10–12 mEq/L in 24 hr → OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (formerly central pontine myelinolysis, permanent neurologic injury); recheck Na q2h initially
Once symptoms relieved + Na ≥120, switch to fluid RESTRICTION + ISOTONIC saline (cautious — myxedema can worsen with too much fluid) to slowly correct remaining Na deficit; trend Na q4–6h
HYPOGLYCEMIA: D50W 1 amp IV bolus for BG <70; D10W maintenance at 75 mL/hr until stable; BG q1h initially then q4h; treat any concurrent adrenal insufficiency contributing
AVOID SEDATIVES — opioids, benzodiazepines worsen hypoventilation + AMS; if sedation needed (intubation), use short-acting (propofol low dose) with close monitoring; avoid long-acting agents

Intubation criteria: airway protection (severe AMS), impending respiratory failure (hypoventilation + CO2 retention + hypoxia), need for procedural sedation; RSI: KETAMINE 1–2 mg/kg IV (hemodynamic stability + no adrenal suppression — AVOID etomidate which suppresses adrenals in compromised axis); ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg IV for paralysis (AVOID succinylcholine due to hypothermia + AKI hyperK risk); cuffed ETT; confirm with capnography + CXR

Mechanical ventilation per lung-protective standards; FiO2 titrated to SpO2 ≥92%; avoid hyperoxia (free radical injury); permissive hypercapnia limits adjusted for the severe respiratory acidosis baseline

Hemodynamic support: dopamine 5–10 mcg/kg/min for inotropic + chronotropic in bradycardia + hypotension (preferred over pure vasoconstrictor for bradycardia); can switch to NOREPINEPHRINE if pure SVR need; goal MAP ≥65; avoid over-resuscitation with crystalloid (can worsen hyponatremia + pulmonary edema from cardiac compromise)

Empiric BROAD-SPECTRUM ANTIBIOTICS for UTI precipitant: CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV daily x 7–14 days for complicated UTI in elderly; narrow per sensitivities; nitrofurantoin avoided if eGFR <30; ID involvement
Strict NPO until myxedema improves + ileus resolves + extubated + alert + protecting airway; foley to gravity for accurate UOP
Trend labs q4h: TFTs (after 24 hr — too soon before then), BMP, magnesium, phosphate, glucose, cortisol (off hydrocortisone), CBC, ABG, lactate, CK
Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line; central venous access; SpO2; q15-min vitals during active treatment then q1h
Stress ulcer prophylaxis with pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily; mechanically ventilated patient
VTE prophylaxis: SCDs initially; LMWH or heparin SubQ once stable; high VTE risk in immobile + critically ill + hypothyroid (procoagulant state)

WORKUP for autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type II (Hashimoto's + Addison's pair commonly): cortisol response to ACTH stimulation test once stable (off hydrocortisone for several days); anti-21-hydroxylase antibodies; consider type 1 DM screening (HbA1c, fasting glucose); plan endocrinology long-term follow-up

FAMILY MEETING with daughter (POA) and son: review trajectory expectations (myxedema coma mortality 30–60% even treated), expected slow recovery (mental status days-weeks, muscle/skin weeks-months), discharge planning (medication adherence + family monitoring + living situation reassessment + cold exposure prevention + close follow-up)

DISCHARGE PLANNING (start day of admission): medication adherence intervention (weekly pill box, family medication oversight, possible simplified once-weekly dosing in extreme non-adherence cases though daily standard, education on chronic disease consequences); LIVING situation reassessment (independent vs assisted living with daughter input); HOME SAFETY (working thermostat, neighbor or daily family check-ins, fall prevention, medical alert device); ENDOCRINE follow-up within 1 week post-discharge then standard interval; PCP within 1 week; mental health/cognitive screening if non-adherence reflects underlying cognitive decline

Patient + family education: NEVER stop levothyroxine without medical guidance; recognize early signs of decompensation (cold intolerance, fatigue, weight gain, dry skin, constipation, depression); recognize myxedema coma warning signs (severe lethargy, confusion, hypothermia, edema, slowed movement) — return immediately; medical alert ID with 'Hashimoto's hypothyroidism — never stop levothyroxine'

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: MYXEDEMA COMA TRIAD met: HYPOTHERMIA 32.4°C + ALTERED MENTAL STATUS (GCS 7) + PRECIPITATING EVENT (stopped levothyroxine 6 wk ago + cold exposure overnight + active UTI)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the emergency. Myxedema coma is the extreme decompensation of severe hypothyroidism, mortality 30–60% even treated. Hypothermia is the cardinal feature; AMS spans confusion to coma; precipitant is almost always identifiable (often multiple stacked).

opts.text: TSH 124 (markedly elevated) + Free T4 0.2 (very low) + Free T3 0.8 (low) — confirms PRIMARY hypothyroidism with severe undertreatment from non-adherence
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic confirmation. Primary hypothyroidism (high TSH) is the most common pattern. Secondary (central/pituitary) hypothyroidism has low/inappropriately normal TSH despite low T4 — would require different workup including pituitary axis. Both treated with hydrocortisone-before-T4.

opts.text: Bradycardia HR 44 + hypotension BP 88/52 + low voltage ECG + pericardial effusion + reduced EF 40% — cardiac compromise from hypothyroidism
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — hemodynamic instability. Bradycardia + hypotension typical of myxedema. Pericardial effusion (mucinous) common; usually not tamponade-causing. Reduced EF chronic from hypothyroidism, improves with replacement. Dopamine for inotropic + chronotropic support.

opts.text: HYPOVENTILATION (RR 8) with severe respiratory acidosis pH 7.18 / pCO2 78 — CO2 narcosis contributing to AMS — intubated for airway protection + respiratory failure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — respiratory failure. Hypothyroidism reduces central respiratory drive + muscle weakness + pleural effusions; result is hypoventilation → CO2 retention → CO2 narcosis (worsens AMS in cyclic fashion). Intubation for airway + ventilation.

opts.text: Severe HYPONATREMIA Na 116 with symptomatic features (coma, hypotension) — risk of seizure, ICP
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neurologic risk. Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (Na <120 with coma/seizure/severe AMS) = HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NS) 100 mL bolus over 10 min, goal Na rise 4–6 mEq/L in first 6 hr; AVOID overcorrection >10–12 mEq/L in 24 hr (osmotic demyelination syndrome — permanent neuro injury).

opts.text: HYPOGLYCEMIA BG 56 (reduced hepatic gluconeogenesis + glycogen + concurrent adrenal axis concern); treated with D50 + D10 maintenance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — worsens AMS + brain function. D50W bolus + D10W maintenance + BG q1h. Concurrent adrenal insufficiency (Addison's pair with Hashimoto's) contributes; hydrocortisone replacement also helps glucose.

opts.text: GIVE HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV BEFORE LEVOTHYROXINE — covers possible undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency (Hashimoto's + Addison's autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome) and prevents adrenal crisis precipitated by T4 increasing metabolic demand
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — THE GOLDEN RULE of myxedema coma treatment. Cortisol level drawn first then hydrocortisone given empirically. Continue hydrocortisone 50–100 mg IV q6–8h. T4 only AFTER hydrocortisone. Most-tested principle in myxedema management.

opts.text: Levothyroxine (T4) LOADING DOSE 400 mcg IV given AFTER hydrocortisone, then 100 mcg IV daily maintenance; cardiac monitoring during administration (arrhythmia risk in elderly with cardiac disease)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — replaces the deficient hormone. Loading dose appropriate; lower (200 mcg) in cardiac patients. Cardiac monitoring during T4 administration; AVOID sudden full physiologic replacement which can precipitate MI/arrhythmia. T4 takes days to convert to T3 (some centers add T3 for faster effect).

opts.text: PASSIVE REWARMING for hypothermia (warm blankets, warm room, warmed IV fluids) — goal slow rewarming 0.5–1°C/hr; AVOID active external rewarming (vasodilation → shock + cold acidic blood return)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — proper rewarming strategy. Passive is safer for moderate hypothermia (28–32°C). Active external (warming pads, immersion) for severe (<28°C) only. Active internal (warm fluids, lavage, ECMO) for very severe with cardiac instability.

opts.text: UTI as precipitant (E. coli, leukocytosis); treat with CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV daily for complicated UTI in elderly; treat the precipitant alongside the myxedema
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — must treat trigger. Myxedema coma improves only when precipitant is also treated. UTI workup + appropriate antibiotic. Most common precipitants: infection (UTI, pneumonia), cold exposure, surgery, medication non-adherence, sedatives, trauma, MI/CVA.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document; helpful for empiric antibiotic + medications.

opts.text: Cholesterol 312 (markedly elevated — classic for hypothyroidism)
opts.rat: Expected finding in hypothyroidism (reduced LDL clearance); supports the diagnosis but does not require acute intervention. Will improve with thyroid replacement over weeks-months. Address chronically with statin if persists after thyroid corrected.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / pharmacology.

opts.text: MYXEDEMA COMA = extreme decompensation of severe hypothyroidism, characterized by HYPOTHERMIA + ALTERED MENTAL STATUS + PRECIPITATING EVENT (infection most common, cold exposure, surgery, sedatives, medication non-adherence, trauma, MI/CVA); mortality 30–60% even treated; ICU level of care; multi-organ effects
opts.rat: CORRECT — clinical syndrome definition. Multiple precipitants often stack. Despite name, most patients are stuporous rather than truly comatose. Time-sensitive — early recognition + treatment improves survival significantly.

opts.text: THE GOLDEN RULE: HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV BEFORE LEVOTHYROXINE — covers possible undiagnosed primary adrenal insufficiency (Hashimoto's + Addison's autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type II) AND prevents adrenal crisis precipitated by T4 increasing metabolic demand on already-failing adrenals; continue hydrocortisone 50–100 mg IV q6–8h pending cortisol
opts.rat: CORRECT — most-tested principle. Cortisol level drawn THEN hydrocortisone given empirically. T4 only after. If cortisol turns out high, taper hydrocortisone; if low, continue replacement. The cost of empiric hydrocortisone is low; the cost of unrecognized adrenal crisis is high.

opts.text: LEVOTHYROXINE (T4) LOADING DOSE 200–400 mcg IV (lower 200 in cardiac patients) then 50–100 mcg IV daily maintenance; cardiac monitoring during administration; AVOID sudden full physiologic replacement (precipitates MI/arrhythmia in elderly with cardiac disease); some centers add LIOTHYRONINE (T3) for faster effect (T4 takes days to convert)
opts.rat: CORRECT — replacement strategy. IV needed in coma (oral not effective due to edematous gut + ileus). Loading dose to replenish stores; maintenance daily. T3 controversial — faster effect but higher cardiac risk; some studies show survival benefit, others not.

opts.text: PASSIVE REWARMING preferred for moderate hypothermia (28–32°C): warm blankets, warm environment, warmed IV fluids, warmed humidified O2; goal 0.5–1°C/hr; AVOID active external rewarming (warming pads, immersion) for moderate hypothermia — vasodilation → shock + cold acidic blood return → worsens cardiac function and acidosis
opts.rat: CORRECT — proper rewarming hierarchy. Severe hypothermia <28°C with cardiac instability may need active internal rewarming (peritoneal/pleural lavage, ECMO). The patient's vasoconstrictive defense against hypothermia is maintaining cardiac filling; rapid vasodilation through aggressive rewarming dumps blood to periphery and crashes the BP.

opts.text: HYPONATREMIA in myxedema coma is multifactorial: reduced free water excretion + reduced GFR + SIADH-like effect; SEVERE SYMPTOMATIC (Na <120 with coma/seizure) requires HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NS) 100 mL bolus over 10 min; GOAL Na RISE 4–6 mEq/L in first 6 hr to relieve symptoms; AVOID OVERCORRECTION >10–12 mEq/L in 24 hr → OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (formerly central pontine myelinolysis — PERMANENT neurologic injury)
