opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield hyponatremia management. Symptomatic severe = hypertonic saline; relieve symptoms first then slow correction. Overcorrection is the feared complication — irreversible. Recheck Na q2h initially during active correction.

opts.text: AVOID SEDATIVES (opioids, benzodiazepines) in myxedema coma — they worsen hypoventilation + central respiratory drive depression + AMS; if sedation needed for intubation, use short-acting (low-dose propofol) with close monitoring
opts.rat: CORRECT — important medication safety. Hypothyroidism reduces drug metabolism; standard sedative doses can be profoundly excessive in myxedema patients. Even routine antiemetics (promethazine) can over-sedate. Intubation often required.

opts.text: AUTOIMMUNE POLYENDOCRINE SYNDROME TYPE II commonly pairs HASHIMOTO'S hypothyroidism + ADDISON'S disease + type 1 DM (other components: vitiligo, pernicious anemia, hypogonadism); workup with ACTH stimulation test + anti-21-hydroxylase antibodies + screening for type 1 DM (HbA1c, fasting glucose) once stable; consider lifelong dual hormone replacement
opts.rat: CORRECT — endocrinology workup. Identifying APS-II changes long-term management (hydrocortisone replacement + thyroid hormone + DM management if developed). The patient's medication non-adherence concern + UTI infection (immunocompromised by adrenal insufficiency) fit pattern.

opts.text: Give LEVOTHYROXINE 400 mcg IV first to rapidly correct thyroid hormone deficiency, then add hydrocortisone if adrenal insufficiency develops later
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. T4 increases metabolic demand on already-failing adrenals → precipitates ADRENAL CRISIS. ALWAYS hydrocortisone FIRST (after cortisol level drawn), then levothyroxine. The most-tested principle of myxedema coma.

opts.text: Aggressive ACTIVE EXTERNAL REWARMING with warming blankets + warm water immersion to rapidly reverse hypothermia
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG for moderate hypothermia (28–32°C). Active external rewarming causes vasodilation → shock + cold acidic blood from periphery returning to core → worsens cardiac function and acidosis. Use PASSIVE rewarming for moderate; active internal for severe (<28°C) with cardiac instability.

opts.text: Lorazepam 2 mg IV for agitation; morphine 4 mg IV for pain control
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — sedatives worsen hypoventilation + AMS in myxedema. Hypothyroid patients have markedly reduced drug metabolism; standard doses can be profoundly excessive. Use short-acting agents at lowest possible doses if absolutely needed; close monitoring.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Adrenal Crisis r/t possible undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency (autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome with Hashimoto's) and T4 replacement increasing metabolic demand
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The hydrocortisone-before-T4 rule prevents this. If adrenal insufficiency is unrecognized and T4 is given first, adrenal crisis = cardiovascular collapse. Cortisol drawn + empiric hydrocortisone before T4 is the standard sequence.

opts.text: Severe Hypothyroidism / Multi-Organ Failure r/t myxedema coma with severe hypothermia, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. T4 + hydrocortisone replacement + passive rewarming + hypertonic saline for hyponatremia + D50/D10 for hypoglycemia + intubation for respiratory failure + treat precipitant. Multi-system bundle care.

opts.text: Risk for Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome r/t overcorrection of severe hyponatremia (Na 116) — permanent neurologic injury if Na rises >10–12 mEq/L in 24 hr
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Hypertonic saline for symptomatic correction; recheck Na q2h initially; goal 4–6 mEq/L rise in first 6 hr to relieve symptoms; cap at 8–10 mEq/L in 24 hr to avoid ODS. The most feared iatrogenic complication of hyponatremia management.

opts.text: Active UTI as precipitant r/t E. coli; needs concurrent antibiotic treatment alongside myxedema management
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Treating the precipitant is part of myxedema coma management. Ceftriaxone for complicated UTI in elderly; narrow per sensitivities; 7–14 day course.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit / Medication Non-Adherence r/t stopped levothyroxine 6 weeks ago because 'felt fine'; future prevention requires medication adherence intervention + family advocacy + possibly assisted living reassessment
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Discharge teaching: never stop levothyroxine without medical guidance, pill box, family medication oversight, recognize early decompensation signs, living situation reassessment, medical alert ID. Daughter as advocate.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: STAT cortisol level drawn; HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV BEFORE levothyroxine; continue 50–100 mg IV q6–8h pending cortisol result
opts.rat: THE GOLDEN RULE of myxedema coma treatment. Empiric hydrocortisone covers possible undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency + prevents adrenal crisis precipitated by T4 increasing metabolic demand. Most-tested principle.

opts.text: LEVOTHYROXINE (T4) 400 mcg IV LOADING DOSE AFTER hydrocortisone, then 100 mcg IV daily maintenance; cardiac monitoring during T4 administration; lower starting dose (200 mcg) acceptable in cardiac patients
opts.rat: Hormone replacement. Loading dose replenishes stores; maintenance daily. IV needed in coma (oral not effective). Cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia/MI risk in elderly with cardiac disease. Some centers add T3 for faster effect.

opts.text: Give LEVOTHYROXINE 400 mcg IV first then add hydrocortisone later if adrenal symptoms develop
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. T4 increases metabolic demand → precipitates adrenal crisis if undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency. ALWAYS hydrocortisone FIRST. The most-tested principle.

opts.text: PASSIVE REWARMING (warm blankets, warm environment, warmed IV fluids, warmed humidified O2); goal slow 0.5–1°C/hr; AVOID active external rewarming for moderate hypothermia 28–32°C
opts.rat: Proper rewarming for moderate hypothermia. Active external (warming pads, immersion) causes vasodilation → shock + cold acidic blood return. Active internal reserved for severe <28°C with cardiac instability.

opts.text: Aggressive ACTIVE EXTERNAL REWARMING with warm water immersion to rapidly raise core temperature
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG for moderate hypothermia. Vasodilation → shock + cold acidic blood return → worsens cardiac function and acidosis. Passive rewarming preferred; active internal for severe (<28°C) only.

opts.text: HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NS) 100 mL bolus over 10 min for severe symptomatic hyponatremia (Na 116 with coma); goal Na rise 4–6 mEq/L in first 6 hr; AVOID overcorrection >10–12 mEq/L in 24 hr (osmotic demyelination syndrome)
opts.rat: Symptomatic severe hyponatremia management. Hypertonic saline to relieve symptoms; controlled correction. Recheck Na q2h initially. ODS is the irreversible complication of overcorrection.

opts.text: D50W 1 amp IV bolus for hypoglycemia BG 56; D10W maintenance at 75 mL/hr; BG q1h initially
opts.rat: Standard hypoglycemia management. Concurrent adrenal insufficiency contributes; hydrocortisone replacement also helps glucose. Continue monitoring.

opts.text: Empiric CEFTRIAXONE 1 g IV daily for UTI precipitant; narrow per sensitivities; 7–14 day course for complicated UTI in elderly
opts.rat: Treat the precipitant. UTI is most common myxedema coma precipitant. Ceftriaxone broad coverage; narrow once sensitivities back. Avoid nitrofurantoin if eGFR <30.

opts.text: LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV PRN for agitation; MORPHINE 4 mg IV PRN for pain
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — sedatives worsen hypoventilation + AMS in myxedema. Hypothyroidism reduces drug metabolism; standard doses can be profoundly excessive. AVOID benzos + opioids when possible; use short-acting at lowest doses if necessary; close monitoring.

opts.text: Intubation for airway protection + respiratory failure (severe AMS, hypoventilation, CO2 retention, hypoxia); RSI with KETAMINE 1–2 mg/kg IV + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg IV (AVOID succinylcholine due to hypothermia + AKI hyperK; AVOID etomidate due to adrenal suppression)
opts.rat: Standard RSI for myxedema coma intubation. Ketamine preferred over etomidate (adrenal suppression). Rocuronium preferred over succinylcholine (hyperK risk + can be ineffective in profound hypothermia). Cuffed ETT; confirm with capnography + CXR.

opts.text: Hemodynamic support: DOPAMINE 5–10 mcg/kg/min IV gtt for bradycardia + hypotension (inotropic + chronotropic effect); NOREPINEPHRINE alternative if pure vasoconstrictor needed
opts.rat: Dopamine preferred when both inotropic AND chronotropic needed (bradycardia + hypotension typical of myxedema). Norepinephrine for pure vasoconstrictor needs (warm shock). Goal MAP ≥65.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP + ABG; central venous access; SpO2; foley for accurate UOP; q15-min vitals during active treatment then q1h; trend labs q4h
opts.rat: Standard ICU monitoring. Multi-system involvement requires close tracking. Trend TFTs after 24 hr (too soon before); trend electrolytes q2–4h initially for hyponatremia management.

opts.text: Discharge planning with case management: medication adherence intervention (weekly pill box, family medication oversight, education on chronic disease consequences); living situation reassessment with daughter (POA) input; home safety; close endocrine follow-up within 1 week
opts.rat: Comprehensive discharge planning. Medication adherence is the modifiable factor for prevention. Family advocacy + simplified regimens + education + close follow-up reduces recurrence risk.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: STAT cortisol level drawn; HYDROCORTISONE 100 mg IV given immediately (BEFORE levothyroxine); empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic (CEFTRIAXONE) for UTI precipitant
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. THE GOLDEN RULE: hydrocortisone before T4. Cortisol drawn first to assess adrenal status. Antibiotics for precipitant. All in the first 30 minutes.

opts.text: LEVOTHYROXINE 400 mcg IV LOADING DOSE (after hydrocortisone), then 100 mcg IV daily maintenance; cardiac monitoring during administration
opts.rat: #2 — Hormone replacement after adrenal coverage. Loading dose replenishes stores. Cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia/MI risk. Continue daily maintenance until oral feasible.

opts.text: PASSIVE REWARMING (warm blankets, warm environment, warmed IV fluids); HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NS) 100 mL bolus over 10 min for severe symptomatic hyponatremia; D50W 1 amp IV for hypoglycemia; D10W maintenance
opts.rat: #3 — Supportive bundle. Passive rewarming for moderate hypothermia; hypertonic saline for symptomatic hyponatremia; dextrose for hypoglycemia. All concurrent.

opts.text: Intubation for airway protection + respiratory failure (KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM RSI, AVOID succinylcholine + etomidate); arterial line; central venous access; dopamine 5 mcg/kg/min for hemodynamic support
opts.rat: #4 — Higher-level monitoring + support. Intubation for severe AMS + hypoventilation. Avoid succinylcholine (hyperK in hypothermia + AKI) and etomidate (adrenal suppression in already-compromised axis). Dopamine for bradycardia + hypotension.

opts.text: Family meeting with daughter (POA) + son to review trajectory (mortality 30–60% even treated); discharge planning (medication adherence, living situation, family monitoring, close follow-up); long-term endocrine + PCP coordination
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care + family + prevention. Long-term medication adherence is the modifiable factor. Daughter as informed POA, engaged. Discharge planning starts day of admission. APS-II workup once stable.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 2 (24 hr into treatment): T 36.6°C (passive rewarming successful); HR 78; BP 124/74 (off dopamine); RR 16 (still intubated); Na 124 (controlled correction); BG 110; mental status improving (responding to voice, following simple commands)
opts.rat: Excellent response to myxedema coma treatment bundle. Continue hydrocortisone + levothyroxine + supportive care. Plan extubation when fully alert and protecting airway. Continue hyponatremia correction at controlled rate (avoid overcorrection).

opts.text: Day 7: extubated, eating regular diet, ambulating with PT, TSH 38 (improving), Free T4 1.4 (in range), Na 138, BG normal, off vasopressors, no infection signs (UTI resolved), discharge planning to skilled nursing facility for ongoing rehab + medication management initiated
opts.rat: Successful recovery. SNF for ongoing rehab + medication management is appropriate given the non-adherence history. Long-term endocrine follow-up; APS-II workup arranged; living situation assessment with daughter input. Comprehensive discharge structure.

opts.text: 1-month outpatient endocrine visit: at home with daughter (moved to assisted living); on levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily with weekly pill box managed by daughter; TSH 6.2 (continuing to improve); cognitive function back at baseline; APS-II workup pending; future medication non-adherence prevention plan in place
opts.rat: Best-case long-term outcome. Living situation change + family medication management + close follow-up prevent recurrence. Daughter empowered as advocate. APS-II workup informs lifelong care planning.

opts.text: Hour 2: BP drops to 76/40, HR 38, lactate climbing to 6.8; T4 had been given 30 min before hydrocortisone (sequence error) — ADRENAL CRISIS precipitated
opts.rat: Iatrogenic adrenal crisis from giving T4 before hydrocortisone (sequence error). Emergent hydrocortisone 100 mg IV STAT; vasopressor support; fluid resuscitation; intensive monitoring. The most preventable complication if the rule is followed. RCA for the sequence error; education for team.

opts.text: Hour 12: Na corrected too rapidly from 116 to 132 (16 mEq rise in 12 hr) — OVERCORRECTION; risk for osmotic demyelination syndrome over next days
opts.rat: Hyponatremia OVERCORRECTION. Reverse the correction: stop hypertonic saline; give DDAVP + free water (D5W) to relower Na back to 4–6 mEq/L rise from baseline; recheck q2h. Osmotic demyelination syndrome (formerly central pontine myelinolysis) is irreversible; symptoms develop days later (locked-in syndrome, dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis).

opts.text: Day 3: develops new fever 38.6°C, increasing leukocytosis, new RLL infiltrate on CXR — ventilator-associated pneumonia
opts.rat: VAP complication of prolonged ventilation. Broaden antibiotics per HAP/VAP coverage (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem); sputum culture; reassess ventilator bundle compliance (head of bed elevation, oral care, sedation interruption when possible).

opts.text: Cortisol level returned LOW (<3 mcg/dL) confirming concurrent ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (Addison's disease — autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type II); will need LIFELONG glucocorticoid + mineralocorticoid replacement in addition to thyroid
opts.rat: Diagnostic confirmation that warranted empiric hydrocortisone. Continue hydrocortisone (will transition to PO hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone for chronic management); lifelong replacement; medical alert ID; stress dose steroids for any future illness/surgery. Plan ACTH stimulation test once off hydrocortisone (challenging — coordinate with endocrinology).

opts.text: After hydrocortisone + T4 + UTI antibiotics, mental status improved enough for extubation Day 4; family verbalizes understanding of medication non-adherence consequences; daughter offers to manage daily medication via pill box; discusses moving mother to assisted living
opts.rat: Comprehensive recovery + family engagement. Daughter as informed advocate. Assisted living addresses underlying medication non-adherence and cold exposure risks. Family-centered discharge planning.

opts.text: Persistent mild edema + dry skin + fatigue at 3-month follow-up despite TSH normalized; cholesterol elevated still
opts.rat: Expected slow resolution of chronic hypothyroidism features (skin, hair, weight, lipids) even after biochemical correction. Takes weeks-to-months. Continue treatment; reassure patient and family; lipid management (statin) if cholesterol persists elevated after thyroid corrected.

opts.text: Persistent need for hydrocortisone replacement on outpatient (Addison's confirmed) — patient + family educated on stress dose steroids, medical alert ID, never stopping the medication, sick-day rules
opts.rat: Expected lifelong management for confirmed Addison's. Stress dose steroids (double or triple usual hydrocortisone for any illness, surgery, dental procedure); medical alert ID; injectable hydrocortisone kit at home for emergencies (vomiting/inability to take PO); never stop the medication; endocrine follow-up every 3–6 mo.

title: Cardiogenic Shock from Anterior NSTEMI with Impella 2.5 Mechanical Circulatory Support
subtitle: SCAI Shock Stage D, Inotrope-Resistant Hypotension → Impella as Bridge to Recovery; Hourly Limb-Perfusion Checks, Heparin Gtt to ACT 160–180, Hemolysis Watch (LDH/Plasma Free Hgb), Purple-Plug Position Confirmation

patient.name: P. Donovan
patient.admitDate: Day 2 of CCU (presented yesterday with chest pain, NSTEMI; tonight evolved to cardiogenic shock; Impella 2.5 placed 4 hr ago via right femoral artery)
patient.room: CCU Bed 3

vitals.time: 0200 tonight (cardiogenic shock pre-Impella — on max inotropes)
vitals.bp: 78/52 (MAP 61) — on norepinephrine 0.4 mcg/kg/min + dobutamine 10 mcg/kg/min + epinephrine 0.05 mcg/kg/min

vitals.time: 0600 (CURRENT — 4 hr post Impella 2.5 placement; wean of inotropes in progress)

labs.v: Troponin I peak 28 (massive elevation); ECG: 2 mm ST depression V3–V6 + T-wave inversion (anterolateral ischemia); cath at 0900 yesterday: 95% proximal LAD lesion (culprit), 70% mid-circumflex, 60% RCA (3-vessel disease); PCI to LAD with DES placed yesterday; remaining vessels planned for staged intervention vs CABG
labs.r: ANTERIOR LAD lesion = high mortality territory (LAD supplies large portion of LV); 3-vessel disease frequently needs CABG vs staged PCI per heart team. Despite successful PCI, infarct damage causes cardiogenic shock in ~5–10% of MI patients (typically anterior with large territory at risk)

labs.v: SCAI Stage D (deteriorating): SBP <90 despite adequate volume + inotropes/vasopressors; CI <2.2 L/min/m² (this patient CI 1.4 on PA cath); SVR elevated 1880 (high — compensatory); PCWP 28 (elevated — pulmonary congestion); lactate 5.8 (elevated despite resuscitation); oliguria UOP 0.2 mL/kg/hr; cold clammy extremities; altered mental status pre-intubation
labs.r: SCAI SHOCK CLASSIFICATION: A (at risk), B (beginning shock), C (classic), D (deteriorating despite first-line therapy), E (extremis — refractory). Stage D triggers MECHANICAL CIRCULATORY SUPPORT (IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO). Hemodynamic profile of cardiogenic shock: LOW CI + HIGH PCWP + HIGH SVR (wet and cold)

labs.v: Impella 2.5 placed via R FEMORAL ARTERY at 0200; positioned with INFLOW in left ventricle + OUTFLOW in ascending aorta (axial flow pump pulls blood from LV → aorta); delivers up to 2.5 L/min of forward flow; UNLOADS LV (reduces wall tension + oxygen demand + ventricular work) AND ADDS forward flow (improves CO + organ perfusion); also available: Impella CP (3.5–4 L/min), Impella 5.0/5.5 (axillary surgical placement, 5+ L/min)
labs.r: IMPELLA = axial flow pump (catheter-based mechanical assist) placed via femoral artery (or axillary for larger devices); INFLOW in LV pulls blood, OUTFLOW above aortic valve delivers; sits across aortic valve. Improves systemic perfusion + UNLOADS LV. Contrasts IABP (counterpulsation in aorta — augments coronary perfusion + reduces afterload but NO active flow), VA-ECMO (full cardiopulmonary support via venous drainage + arterial return — bypasses heart + lungs but doesn't unload LV well — may need LV vent)

labs.v: PURPLE PLUG marker visible at femoral entry site (~10 cm of catheter inserted); device console shows 'P-LEVEL' = P8 (maximum support); 'PLACEMENT SIGNAL' = aortic (correct position — pump signal differentiates ventricular vs aortic vs septal); 'MOTOR CURRENT' waveform pulsatile (suggests correct position across aortic valve); echocardiogram confirms inflow ~3.5 cm below aortic valve in LV midcavity
labs.r: IMPELLA POSITION CONFIRMATION: (1) PURPLE PLUG visible at insertion site at expected depth; (2) PLACEMENT SIGNAL on console (aortic vs ventricular vs septal pattern); (3) MOTOR CURRENT waveform (pulsatile when correctly across aortic valve, dampened if migrated into LV or aorta); (4) ECHOCARDIOGRAM (gold standard) confirms inflow position. Migration is the most common position problem — requires repositioning. Document position q4h and after any patient movement

labs.v: Pre-Impella: CI 1.4, PCWP 28, SVR 1880, MAP 61 on max inotropes (cardiogenic shock); CURRENT (4 hr post-Impella): CI 2.6, PCWP 18 (decreased — LV unloading), SVR 1340 (decreased — partly from Impella forward flow allowing inotrope wean), MAP 80 on norepi 0.2 mcg/kg/min + dobutamine 5 mcg/kg/min (weaning); SvO2 68% (improved from 52%); lactate 3.4 (down from 5.8)
labs.r: HEMODYNAMIC IMPROVEMENT post-Impella expected pattern: CI increases, PCWP decreases (LV unloading), MAP supported, inotropes can be weaned, SvO2 improves (organ perfusion), lactate clears. Trend q1h initially. PA cath provides comprehensive hemodynamic data; alternative is bedside echo + clinical assessment if PA cath not in place

labs.v: HEPARIN GTT running per institutional protocol; target ACT 160–180 (some protocols use aPTT 60–80 instead); checked q4h via ACT cartridge at bedside; in addition, Impella PURGE SOLUTION (heparinized D5W 50 units/mL OR sodium bicarbonate-based if heparin contraindicated) runs continuously through device to prevent thrombus formation in pump
labs.r: IMPELLA ANTICOAGULATION: heparin gtt to ACT 160–180 (lower than full anticoagulation 200+ to balance bleeding risk + device thrombosis); ALSO Impella PURGE SOLUTION (heparinized D5W) runs continuously through pump (this purge alone is NOT systemic anticoagulation — need separate heparin gtt). Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is a concern with prolonged heparin — monitor platelets daily; if HIT develops, switch to argatroban or bivalirudin (see case-064)

labs.v: LDH 480 (mildly elevated — baseline 180 day of admission); plasma free Hgb 24 mg/dL (mildly elevated; normal <5); haptoglobin 22 (low — consumed by hemolysis); urine grossly clear (no visible hemoglobinuria yet); UOP adequate at 1.2 mL/kg/hr
labs.r: IMPELLA HEMOLYSIS: from RBC shear stress in pump; manifests as elevated LDH, elevated plasma free Hgb, low haptoglobin, hemoglobinuria (cola-colored urine), AKI. Monitor q12h. If severe (plasma free Hgb >40, hemoglobinuria, AKI), consider repositioning device, reducing P-level, or removal. Some hemolysis is expected; severe is device complication

labs.n: LIMB perfusion monitoring (vascular complication risk at femoral access)
labs.v: Q1H ASSESSMENT of RIGHT (Impella side) and LEFT lower extremities: COLOR (pink R = good; L pink), TEMPERATURE (warm R = good; warm L), SENSATION (intact bilateral), MOTOR (intact bilateral), CAPILLARY REFILL (<2 sec bilateral), DOPPLER PULSES (DP and PT palpable + Doppler R; bounding L); marked Impella side at insertion; reassess q1h documenting all components
labs.r: LIMB ISCHEMIA is a major Impella complication — large-bore femoral cannula can occlude or embolize. Hourly 5-P assessment: PAIN, PALLOR, PARESTHESIA, PARALYSIS, PULSELESSNESS (plus temperature, color, cap refill, motor strength). If signs of ischemia: notify physician STAT; vascular surgery consultation; may need ipsilateral distal perfusion cannula (DPC) or device removal

labs.v: Pre-Impella: norepinephrine 0.4 mcg/kg/min + dobutamine 10 mcg/kg/min + epinephrine 0.05 mcg/kg/min (high doses, deteriorating); CURRENT (4 hr post-Impella): WEANED to norepi 0.2 mcg/kg/min + dobutamine 5 mcg/kg/min; epinephrine OFF; plan continued wean as Impella supports + native cardiac function recovers
labs.r: INOTROPE/VASOPRESSOR hierarchy in cardiogenic shock: DOBUTAMINE (beta-1 inotropic + mild beta-2 vasodilation) + NOREPINEPHRINE (alpha-1 vasoconstrictor for BP); MILRINONE (PDE3 inhibitor; inotropic + pulmonary vasodilation; preferred in pulm HTN; longer half-life, more hypotension); EPINEPHRINE (beta-1 + alpha; reserve for refractory); DOPAMINE less favored due to arrhythmia risk. MCS allows weaning of inotropes which then improves outcomes (inotropes are mortal in prolonged use)

labs.v: Hgb 9.2 (mildly low — chronic + dilutional + mild hemolysis), Plt 168 (slightly low — Impella shear + heparin watch for HIT), INR 1.2 (heparin gtt running); BMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 102, HCO3 18 (mild acidosis from shock now improving), BUN 38, Cr 1.6 (AKI from shock + contrast nephropathy from PCI; baseline 1.0); LFTs mildly elevated (shock liver, recovering); lactate 3.4 (clearing from 5.8)
labs.r: Labs consistent with recovering shock + mild Impella-related hemolysis. Trend q4h. Cr likely from cardiogenic shock + contrast-induced nephropathy post-PCI; should recover with restored perfusion. K may need replacement during furosemide use (volume management)

labs.v: Intubated at 0140 (pre-Impella) for impending respiratory failure + cardiac arrest concern; KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM RSI (avoided etomidate due to adrenal suppression concern, avoided succinylcholine due to hyperK risk); lung-protective settings (TV 6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP 8, FiO2 0.4, plateau 22); plan to wean as patient improves
labs.r: Intubation often needed in cardiogenic shock for: airway protection (AMS), tachypnea + respiratory muscle fatigue, pulmonary edema, procedural sedation for line placement. Lung-protective ventilation standard. PEEP can REDUCE preload (helpful in pulmonary edema) but may reduce CI (cautious in pure cardiogenic shock — balance)

labs.v: Type 2 DM (HbA1c 7.8% — moderate control), HTN, hyperlipidemia, former smoker (quit 5 yr ago, 30 pack-year), HFrEF EF 30% chronic (echo 6 mo ago — now likely 15% after this MI); on chronic ASA, atorvastatin, lisinopril, metoprolol; no prior MI/CABG; BMI 28; lives with wife, retired
labs.r: PMH defines high cardiovascular risk profile. Chronic HFrEF + new large anterior MI = catastrophic LV decompensation. Continue GDMT (ACE-I/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2-inhibitor) once stable post-MI. Long-term: AICD candidate if EF remains <35% after 40 days; cardiac rehab; advanced HF specialist consultation

labs.v: Heart Team consultation initiated for: (a) decision on remaining vessels (staged PCI vs CABG), (b) advanced HF therapies if recovery incomplete (transplant evaluation, LVAD candidacy), (c) AICD planning for primary prevention if EF remains <35% after 40 days post-MI, (d) MCS escalation if Impella inadequate (VA-ECMO consideration); cardiac surgery on standby for vascular complications or CABG
labs.r: HEART TEAM = multidisciplinary cardiology + cardiac surgery + interventionalists + advanced HF + critical care; standard of care for complex CAD + cardiogenic shock + advanced HF decisions. ESCALATION pathway: Impella → VA-ECMO → LVAD/transplant. Cardiac surgery on standby for vascular complications (limb ischemia requiring repair, hemorrhage) or transition to surgical MCS

labs.n: Wife at bedside / goals of care
labs.v: WIFE (POA) at bedside, deeply engaged; advance directive 'full code with reassessment if poor prognosis'; family meeting planned with cardiology + cardiac surgery + advanced HF + palliative for next steps decision-making; parallel pathway: maximize recovery via Impella support + treat residual coronary disease; if not recovering, consider advanced HF therapies (LVAD bridge to transplant or destination)
labs.r: Family-centered care + goals-of-care discussions. Cardiogenic shock has 30-50% in-hospital mortality even with MCS; transparency with family. Parallel-pathway approach: aggressive recovery support AND palliative integration. Wife as informed advocate for decision-making points (escalation criteria, advanced HF therapies)

nursesNote: 0600 — 64 y/o male CCU Day 2 admitted yesterday with anterior NSTEMI (troponin peak 28, 2 mm ST depression V3–V6) requiring PCI to 95% proximal LAD lesion with DES; 3-vessel disease (LAD treated, mid-circumflex + RCA remaining for staged intervention vs CABG per Heart Team). Course complicated by progressive cardiogenic shock overnight: CI dropped to 1.4 on PA cath, PCWP 28, SVR 1880, MAP 61 despite escalating inotropes (norepinephrine 0.4 mcg/kg/min + dobutamine 10 mcg/kg/min + epinephrine 0.05 mcg/kg/min) — SCAI SHOCK STAGE D (deteriorating despite first-line therapy). Intubated at 0140 for impending respiratory failure (KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM RSI, avoided etomidate adrenal suppression + succinylcholine hyperK). IMPELLA 2.5 placed via R FEMORAL ARTERY at 0200 by interventional cardiology in cath lab — positioned with inflow in LV midcavity + outflow above aortic valve (axial flow pump delivers up to 2.5 L/min of forward flow + unloads LV); P-level P8 (maximum support); placement confirmed by (1) PURPLE PLUG marker at femoral entry, (2) PLACEMENT SIGNAL aortic on console, (3) MOTOR CURRENT pulsatile waveform, (4) bedside ECHO inflow ~3.5 cm below aortic valve in LV midcavity. CURRENT (4 hr post-Impella): hemodynamics dramatically improved — CI 2.6 (up from 1.4), PCWP 18 (down from 28 — LV unloading), SVR 1340 (down from 1880), MAP 80 on norepi 0.2 mcg/kg/min + dobutamine 5 mcg/kg/min (weaning from max doses; epi OFF), SvO2 68% (up from 52%), lactate 3.4 (down from 5.8) — excellent response pattern. ANTICOAGULATION: heparin gtt running, target ACT 160–180 (lower than full anticoagulation to balance bleeding vs device thrombosis); ACT q4h via bedside cartridge; PURGE SOLUTION (heparinized D5W 50 units/mL) running continuously through Impella to prevent pump thrombus (this is NOT systemic anticoagulation — separate from heparin gtt). HEMOLYSIS monitoring (Impella complication from RBC shear in pump): LDH 480 (mildly elevated from baseline 180), plasma free Hgb 24 (normal <5; elevated), haptoglobin 22 (low — consumed), urine grossly clear (no visible hemoglobinuria); mild hemolysis acceptable; severe (plasma free Hgb >40, hemoglobinuria, AKI worsening) would prompt repositioning device, reducing P-level, or removal. LIMB PERFUSION q1h assessment (vascular complication risk at large-bore femoral cannula): R lower extremity (Impella side) — pink, warm, sensation intact, motor intact, cap refill <2 sec, DP + PT pulses palpable + Doppler-confirmed; L lower extremity bounding pulses, no signs of ischemia or embolic event. 5-P CHECK (Pain, Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness) documented q1h; vascular surgery on standby for any signs of ischemia. PA cath continuing to guide hemodynamic management. Foley to gravity for accurate UOP (1.2 mL/kg/hr now, up from 0.2). Mechanical ventilation lung-protective (TV 6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP 8, FiO2 0.4, plateau 22); plan to wean as patient improves. Wife (POA) at bedside, engaged, asking detailed questions about Impella and recovery trajectory; chaplain available. HEART TEAM consultation initiated for: (a) timing of remaining vessel intervention (staged PCI vs CABG once stable), (b) advanced HF therapies if recovery incomplete (transplant evaluation, LVAD candidacy), (c) AICD planning for primary prevention if EF remains <35% after 40 days post-MI, (d) MCS escalation if Impella inadequate (VA-ECMO if further deterioration). PALLIATIVE CARE consultation early for parallel-pathway approach (aggressive recovery support + integrated palliative + family communication). ANTICIPATED COURSE: Impella support typically 1–7 days as bridge to recovery; weaning criteria include improving native cardiac function (CI >2.2 off support trial), lactate clearing, off inotropes, improving end-organ function; if recovery incomplete despite optimization, transition to advanced HF therapies (LVAD as bridge to transplant or destination; transplant if candidate). Long-term: GDMT optimization (ACE-I/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2-inhibitor), cardiac rehab, AICD if persistent low EF, advanced HF specialist follow-up. Mortality 30–50% in-hospital for cardiogenic shock + MCS; aggressive optimization + early recognition + Heart Team coordination improve outcomes.
providerOrders: ADMIT to CCU; cardiology primary; interventional cardiology for Impella management; cardiac surgery on standby for vascular complications or CABG; advanced HF specialist; palliative care for parallel pathway
IMPELLA 2.5 management:
P-LEVEL P8 (maximum support); adjust per cardiology based on hemodynamics + native cardiac function recovery
POSITION CONFIRMATION q4h: PURPLE PLUG at femoral entry, PLACEMENT SIGNAL aortic on console, MOTOR CURRENT pulsatile waveform; bedside ECHO at least daily and after any patient movement
PURGE SOLUTION (heparinized D5W 50 units/mL) continuous through Impella to prevent pump thrombus; ensure pressure 300–1100 mmHg; change purge q24h

HEPARIN GTT for systemic anticoagulation; TARGET ACT 160–180 (per institutional protocol; some use aPTT 60–80); check ACT q4h via bedside cartridge; some protocols allow ACT 140–160 if bleeding concerns
Monitor for HEPARIN-INDUCED THROMBOCYTOPENIA (HIT) with daily platelets; if HIT (see case-064 reference) → switch to ARGATROBAN or BIVALIRUDIN; do NOT continue heparin

HEMOLYSIS monitoring q12h: LDH, plasma free Hgb, haptoglobin; visual urine (hemoglobinuria); UOP trend; if severe hemolysis (plasma free Hgb >40, gross hemoglobinuria, AKI worsening), consider repositioning, reducing P-level, or device removal
Suction events (red alarm on console; pump pulling on LV wall from low preload) — give fluid bolus 250 mL crystalloid OR colloid; reduce P-level if persistent; reposition if needed
LIMB PERFUSION assessment q1h on Impella access side (right lower extremity) + contralateral for comparison:

COLOR (pink vs pale/dusky); TEMPERATURE (warm vs cool); SENSATION (intact vs paresthesia); MOTOR (intact vs weak); CAPILLARY REFILL (<2 sec vs prolonged); DOPPLER PULSES (DP + PT — palpable preferred, Doppler-confirmed if not palpable); 5-P assessment (Pain, Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness)

If signs of limb ischemia: notify physician STAT, vascular surgery consult, may need ipsilateral distal perfusion cannula (DPC) added below the femoral cannula, or Impella relocation, or device removal

Inotrope/vasopressor wean as Impella supports + native cardiac function recovers: target lowest effective doses; current norepi 0.2 mcg/kg/min + dobutamine 5 mcg/kg/min; wean per hemodynamic parameters (CI, MAP, SvO2, lactate); reassess q1h initially
PA cath hemodynamic monitoring: CI, PCWP, SVR, RA, RV pressures, SvO2 q1h; trend with treatment changes; goal CI >2.2, PCWP <18, SvO2 >65, lactate clearing
Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line; central venous access; SpO2; q15-min vitals during active intervention then q1h
Foley to gravity for accurate UOP (goal ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; >1 mL/kg/hr ideal); daily weights once stable
Lung-protective mechanical ventilation: TV 6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP 8, FiO2 titrated to SpO2 ≥92%, plateau <30, driving pressure <15; wean as patient improves
Trend labs q4h initially: BMP, lactate, ABG, troponin, BNP; daily LFTs (shock liver), CBC + plt (HIT watch), coags (heparin titration), LDH/plasma free Hgb/haptoglobin (hemolysis)

GDMT (Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy) for HFrEF post-MI — hold during shock, resume once stable: ACE-I/ARB or ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan), evidence-based beta-blocker (metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, or bisoprolol), MRA (spironolactone or eplerenone), SGLT2-inhibitor (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin); continue ASA + statin; clopidogrel/ticagrelor per PCI protocol (DES requires 6–12 mo DAPT)

Cardiology + interventional cardiology + cardiac surgery + advanced HF + palliative HEART TEAM consultation for: (a) remaining vessel intervention timing + modality, (b) advanced HF therapies (LVAD, transplant) if recovery incomplete, (c) AICD primary prevention if EF <35% at 40 days post-MI, (d) MCS escalation (VA-ECMO) if Impella inadequate

WEANING from Impella criteria: improving native cardiac function (CI >2.2 with reduced P-level support), lactate clearing, off or low-dose inotropes, improving end-organ function (UOP, mental status off sedation, renal function); weaning trial = reduce P-level gradually (P8 → P6 → P4 → P2) over hours, monitor hemodynamics, if tolerated proceed to removal; if not tolerated, return to higher P-level + continue support; removal in cath lab or ICU with vascular surgery available for hemostasis

ESCALATION to VA-ECMO if Impella inadequate + ongoing shock: full cardiopulmonary support; consider LV vent or 'EC-pella' (Impella + ECMO) to unload LV which ECMO alone doesn't do; cardiac surgery + interventional cardiology coordination
DVT prophylaxis: SCDs bilateral (mechanical only — heparin gtt provides systemic anticoagulation already)
Stress ulcer prophylaxis: pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily
Nutrition: NPO until extubated and bowel function intact; nutrition consult; advance diet as tolerated

Family meeting with wife (POA) + Heart Team + palliative: review trajectory expectations (mortality 30–50% cardiogenic shock + MCS), escalation criteria, advanced HF therapy candidacy if recovery incomplete, goals of care alignment; transparent prognosis + parallel pathway approach

Discharge planning early: cardiology + advanced HF specialist follow-up within 1 week, cardiac rehab referral, GDMT optimization plan, AICD plan if indicated, lifestyle modification (smoking cessation maintenance, diet, weight, exercise), medication adherence intervention, social work for support resources

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: SCAI Shock Stage D progression (cardiogenic shock deteriorating despite max inotropes pre-Impella): CI 1.4, PCWP 28, SVR 1880, MAP 61, lactate 5.8 — triggered MCS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the indication for MCS. SCAI Stage D (deteriorating despite first-line) triggers escalation to mechanical circulatory support (Impella, IABP, or VA-ECMO). The hemodynamic profile (low CI + high PCWP + high SVR + elevated lactate + oliguria) defines cardiogenic shock.

opts.text: Impella 2.5 placed via R femoral with PURPLE PLUG marker at insertion site + PLACEMENT SIGNAL aortic + MOTOR CURRENT pulsatile + ECHO-confirmed inflow position — correct placement confirmed by 4 indicators
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — position confirmation. All 4 indicators required for confidence: purple plug visible at expected depth, console placement signal aortic (vs ventricular/septal), motor current pulsatile (vs dampened), echo inflow ~3 cm below aortic valve in LV midcavity. Document q4h and after any patient movement (migration is most common position problem).

opts.text: Dramatic hemodynamic improvement post-Impella: CI 1.4 → 2.6, PCWP 28 → 18 (LV unloading), MAP 80 (sustained off epinephrine), SvO2 68% (up from 52%), lactate clearing 5.8 → 3.4
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — response to MCS. Expected pattern of improvement post-Impella: CI up, PCWP down, MAP supported, inotropes weaning, SvO2 improving, lactate clearing. Trend hourly. Inotrope wean is key — prolonged inotrope use is independently associated with mortality.

opts.text: ANTICOAGULATION: heparin gtt to target ACT 160–180 + Impella PURGE SOLUTION (heparinized D5W) continuous through pump
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — device-specific anticoagulation. ACT 160–180 lower than full anticoagulation (200+) to balance bleeding vs device thrombosis. Purge solution is NOT systemic anticoagulation (separate from heparin gtt). Monitor for HIT with daily platelets.

opts.text: Hemolysis monitoring: LDH 480 (elevated from 180 baseline), plasma free Hgb 24 (normal <5), haptoglobin 22 (low — consumed); urine grossly clear (no hemoglobinuria yet); mild hemolysis acceptable, severe would prompt intervention
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Impella complication. From RBC shear stress in pump. Mild hemolysis is expected and acceptable. Severe (plasma free Hgb >40, hemoglobinuria, AKI worsening) prompts repositioning device, reducing P-level, or removal. Monitor q12h.

opts.text: LIMB perfusion check q1h: R lower extremity (Impella access) — pink, warm, sensation/motor intact, cap refill <2 sec, DP + PT palpable + Doppler-confirmed; 5-P assessment (Pain, Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — major vascular complication risk. Large-bore femoral cannula can occlude, embolize, or cause limb ischemia. Hourly 5-P check + Doppler if pulses not palpable. Signs of ischemia: STAT physician + vascular surgery consultation; may need distal perfusion cannula (DPC), repositioning, or device removal.

opts.text: Inotrope/vasopressor wean as Impella supports + native cardiac function recovers; target lowest effective doses
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — inotrope wean improves outcomes. Prolonged high-dose inotropes increase mortality. MCS allows weaning. Reassess q1h; titrate based on CI, MAP, SvO2, lactate; prefer dobutamine (inotropic) + norepi (vasoconstrictor) for cardiogenic shock; milrinone alternative; epinephrine reserve for refractory.

opts.text: AKI Cr 1.6 (baseline 1.0) — shock + contrast-induced nephropathy post-PCI; hemolysis can worsen
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multi-organ involvement. Shock causes AKI; contrast adds insult; hemolysis can worsen further. Trend Cr; avoid nephrotoxins; renally dose meds; consider CRRT if severe/refractory. AKI typically resolves with restored perfusion + treating cause.

opts.text: Heart Team consultation for remaining vessel intervention + advanced HF therapy planning + escalation pathway (VA-ECMO) + AICD planning
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multidisciplinary care. Heart Team (cardiology + cardiac surgery + interventionalists + advanced HF + critical care) coordinates complex decisions. Standard of care for cardiogenic shock + MCS. Parallel pathway with palliative care for goals of care.

opts.text: Wife (POA) at bedside engaged in decision-making; advance directive 'full code with reassessment if poor prognosis'; family meeting planned
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family-centered care + goals of care. Cardiogenic shock + MCS has 30–50% mortality; transparent communication essential. Wife as informed advocate. Palliative care integration alongside aggressive recovery support.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 98.2°F
opts.rat: Within normal — rules out infection as confounder. Continue to monitor.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / MCS principles.

opts.text: CARDIOGENIC SHOCK definition: SBP <90 + tissue hypoperfusion (cool extremities, oliguria, AMS, lactate >2) despite adequate volume; hemodynamic profile: LOW CI <2.2 + HIGH PCWP >18 (wet) + HIGH SVR (cold compensatory); SCAI classification A–E (A at risk, B beginning, C classic, D deteriorating, E extremis); Stage C+ triggers vasopressor/inotrope; Stage D triggers MCS
opts.rat: CORRECT — clinical syndrome definition. SCAI staging guides escalation. The 'wet and cold' profile distinguishes cardiogenic shock from septic shock (warm and vasodilated; low SVR). 'Wet and warm' = distributive cardiogenic (sepsis-cardiac overlap). 'Dry and cold' = hypovolemic-cardiac overlap. PA cath helps differentiate.

opts.text: IMPELLA 2.5: axial flow pump catheter-based MCS, placed via femoral artery; INFLOW in LV midcavity, OUTFLOW in ascending aorta; delivers up to 2.5 L/min forward flow; UNLOADS LV (reduces wall tension + O2 demand + work) + ADDS forward flow (improves CO + organ perfusion); larger options: Impella CP (3.5–4 L/min), Impella 5.0/5.5 (axillary surgical placement, 5+ L/min)
opts.rat: CORRECT — Impella mechanics. Continuous axial flow pump (no pulsatility from device, though native cardiac pulsation transmits to MCS-augmented circulation). LV unloading is the unique benefit vs IABP (counterpulsation) and VA-ECMO (which doesn't unload LV — may need separate vent or 'EC-pella'). Position critical for function.

opts.text: IABP (intra-aortic balloon pump) DIFFERENT from Impella: IABP is COUNTERPULSATION (inflates in DIASTOLE → augments coronary perfusion; deflates in early SYSTOLE → reduces afterload); does NOT add active forward flow; modest hemodynamic benefit; useful for ACS + mild-moderate shock + post-CABG; trialed first historically but Impella increasingly preferred for more severe shock
opts.rat: CORRECT — IABP vs Impella distinction. Both reduce LV work, but Impella adds active forward flow (more support). IABP requires intact aortic valve, native cardiac rhythm with predictable timing; Impella works regardless of rhythm. Choice depends on shock severity, available expertise, anticipated duration.

opts.text: VA-ECMO: venoarterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation; full cardiopulmonary support (drains venous blood, oxygenates, returns to artery); used for severe biventricular failure or cardiac arrest; does NOT unload LV well (can WORSEN LV afterload) — may need LV vent (Impella) or surgical vent; longer setup; higher complication rate; preferred for refractory shock or cardiac arrest
opts.rat: CORRECT — VA-ECMO mechanics. Provides full cardiopulmonary support but increases LV afterload from arterial return — can cause LV distension + worsening pulmonary edema. 'EC-pella' = ECMO + Impella concurrent for full support + LV unloading. Escalation pathway: Impella → VA-ECMO → LVAD/transplant.

opts.text: IMPELLA POSITION CONFIRMATION (4 indicators): (1) PURPLE PLUG visible at femoral entry at expected depth; (2) PLACEMENT SIGNAL on console (aortic vs ventricular vs septal pattern); (3) MOTOR CURRENT waveform (pulsatile when correct, dampened if migrated); (4) ECHOCARDIOGRAM gold standard confirms inflow ~3.5 cm below aortic valve in LV midcavity
opts.rat: CORRECT — position confirmation hierarchy. Migration is most common position problem (catheter pulled into LV or pushed into aorta with patient movement). Reposition under fluoroscopy if needed. Document q4h.

opts.text: ANTICOAGULATION for Impella: HEPARIN GTT target ACT 160–180 (lower than full anticoagulation 200+ to balance bleeding vs device thrombosis); ALSO Impella PURGE SOLUTION (heparinized D5W 50 units/mL OR sodium bicarb-based if heparin contraindicated) runs continuously THROUGH pump — purge is NOT systemic; need separate heparin gtt; monitor for HIT (daily platelets); HIT → switch to ARGATROBAN or BIVALIRUDIN
opts.rat: CORRECT — device-specific anticoagulation strategy. Two distinct anticoagulation channels: systemic (heparin gtt) + device (purge solution). Both required. HIT monitoring critical given prolonged heparin exposure; if HIT develops, switch entire systemic anticoagulation to non-heparin agent.

opts.text: IMPELLA HEMOLYSIS from RBC shear stress in axial pump: monitor LDH, plasma free Hgb, haptoglobin, urine color (hemoglobinuria), UOP; mild hemolysis acceptable; severe (plasma free Hgb >40, hemoglobinuria, worsening AKI) prompts repositioning, reducing P-level, or device removal
opts.rat: CORRECT — common Impella complication. Some shear stress is inherent to the pump; mild hemolysis is part of normal Impella support. Severe hemolysis can cause AKI from myoglobinuric-like tubular injury + transfusion need + device complications. Monitor q12h.

opts.text: Limb ischemia is a major Impella complication — large-bore femoral cannula can occlude or embolize distally; HOURLY 5-P assessment (Pain, Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness) + temperature + color + cap refill + Doppler pulses; if ischemia: vascular surgery consult, may need ipsilateral distal perfusion cannula (DPC) or device repositioning/removal
opts.rat: CORRECT — vascular complication monitoring. The femoral cannula occupies large vessel lumen and can compromise distal perfusion. DPC is small cannula below the Impella cannula that delivers oxygenated blood to the limb. Vascular surgery on standby.

opts.text: Continue all four inotropes/vasopressors at maximum doses while Impella is in place — Impella plus inotropes provides redundancy and improves outcomes
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Prolonged high-dose inotropes increase mortality (arrhythmia, ischemia, metabolic cost). The whole point of MCS is to ALLOW WEANING of inotropes. Reassess q1h; titrate down per hemodynamics (CI, MAP, SvO2, lactate). Goal is lowest effective dose while MCS supports native function recovery.

opts.text: Hemolysis is rare with Impella and does not require routine monitoring
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Hemolysis is COMMON with Impella (some shear stress inherent to axial pump). Routine monitoring q12h with LDH, plasma free Hgb, haptoglobin, urine color, UOP. Severe hemolysis requires intervention.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Cardiogenic Shock Decompensation r/t inadequate Impella support OR device malfunction OR migration; need for continued hemodynamic monitoring + escalation criteria
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Underlying disease + device dependency. Hourly hemodynamic assessment, position confirmation, device function indicators, weaning when appropriate. Escalation pathway (VA-ECMO if Impella inadequate) defined. Cardiogenic shock + MCS mortality 30–50%.

opts.text: Risk for Limb Ischemia r/t large-bore femoral Impella cannula; need for hourly 5-P checks + vascular surgery on standby
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Limb ischemia is a major Impella vascular complication. Hourly 5-P + Doppler pulses + temperature + color + cap refill. STAT vascular surgery if signs of ischemia. May need DPC, device repositioning, or removal.

opts.text: Risk for Device Complications (hemolysis, suction events, position migration, thrombosis); need for routine monitoring + appropriate response
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Hemolysis monitoring q12h. Suction events (red alarm) — give fluid bolus, reduce P-level. Position q4h confirmation. Thrombosis prevention via heparin gtt to ACT 160–180 + purge solution. Each complication has specific response.

opts.text: Risk for Bleeding r/t therapeutic-dose heparin anticoagulation + multiple vascular access sites + AKI affecting platelet function
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Heparin titrated to ACT 160–180 (lower than full anticoagulation to balance bleeding risk). Monitor access sites + GI + GU + neuro for bleeding signs. Daily plt for HIT watch. Have antidote (protamine) available.

opts.text: Family Distress / Need for Goals-of-Care Alignment r/t cardiogenic shock with MCS, advanced HF therapy decisions, mortality 30–50%
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Wife as informed POA, engaged. Heart Team consultation + parallel palliative pathway. Goals-of-care alignment for escalation/de-escalation decision points. Transparent communication.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Impella position confirmation q4h: PURPLE PLUG visible at femoral entry, PLACEMENT SIGNAL aortic on console, MOTOR CURRENT pulsatile, bedside ECHO at least daily and after any patient movement
opts.rat: Standard Impella monitoring. Migration is most common position problem; early detection prevents inadequate support or LV apex injury. Document all 4 indicators.

opts.text: Heparin gtt titrated to ACT 160–180 per institutional protocol; ACT q4h via bedside cartridge; daily platelet count for HIT monitoring; Impella PURGE SOLUTION continuous
opts.rat: Standard Impella anticoagulation. ACT 160–180 balances bleeding vs device thrombosis. Purge solution is separate from systemic anticoagulation. HIT monitoring critical given prolonged heparin (switch to argatroban or bivalirudin if HIT).

opts.text: Hourly LIMB PERFUSION assessment of R lower extremity (Impella access) + L for comparison: COLOR + TEMPERATURE + SENSATION + MOTOR + CAPILLARY REFILL + DOPPLER PULSES; 5-P assessment
opts.rat: Standard vascular complication monitoring. Hourly is the protocol. If signs of ischemia: STAT physician + vascular surgery; may need DPC, device repositioning, or removal.

opts.text: Continue all inotropes (norepi, dobutamine, epinephrine) at MAXIMUM doses while Impella is in place to provide redundancy
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Prolonged high-dose inotropes increase mortality. The point of MCS is to ALLOW WEANING. Reassess q1h; titrate down per hemodynamics (CI, MAP, SvO2, lactate). Goal lowest effective dose.

opts.text: Hemolysis monitoring q12h: LDH, plasma free Hgb, haptoglobin, urine color, UOP; severe hemolysis (plasma free Hgb >40, hemoglobinuria, AKI worsening) prompts repositioning, reducing P-level, or device removal
opts.rat: Standard Impella complication monitoring. Mild hemolysis is expected; severe requires intervention. Document trend.

opts.text: PA cath hemodynamic monitoring: CI, PCWP, SVR, SvO2 q1h; trend with treatment changes; goal CI >2.2, PCWP <18, SvO2 >65, lactate clearing
opts.rat: Standard PA cath monitoring guides MCS + inotrope management. Bedside echo alternative if PA cath not available. Trends inform weaning decisions + escalation criteria.

opts.text: Heart Team consultation for remaining vessel intervention + advanced HF therapy candidacy + escalation pathway (VA-ECMO) + AICD planning
opts.rat: Standard of care for complex CAD + cardiogenic shock + advanced HF decisions. Multidisciplinary input essential. Parallel palliative pathway for goals of care.

opts.text: Weaning trial from Impella: reduce P-level gradually (P8 → P6 → P4 → P2) when criteria met (improving native CI, lactate clearing, off/low-dose inotropes, improved end-organ function); proceed to removal if tolerated
opts.rat: Standard Impella weaning approach. Allows native cardiac function to demonstrate recovery before removal. If not tolerated, return to higher support level; continue MCS until ready.

opts.text: Escalation to VA-ECMO if Impella inadequate + ongoing shock; consider 'EC-pella' (Impella + ECMO concurrent) for full support + LV unloading
opts.rat: Escalation pathway when Impella alone inadequate. VA-ECMO provides full cardiopulmonary support but doesn't unload LV — concurrent Impella addresses both. Cardiac surgery + interventional cardiology coordination.

opts.text: GDMT (Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy) for HFrEF post-MI — HOLD during acute shock; resume once stable: ACE-I/ARB/ARNI, evidence-based beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2-inhibitor; continue ASA + statin; DAPT per PCI protocol
opts.rat: Long-term HFrEF management. GDMT improves outcomes substantially. Hold during shock (hypotension); resume once stable + tolerating. AICD if EF remains <35% at 40 days post-MI.

opts.text: Bed rest with HOB <30° to prevent Impella catheter kinking
opts.rat: Standard Impella positioning. HOB <30° (some protocols allow 30°) to prevent catheter kinking at femoral artery insertion. Keep affected leg straight (no flexion at hip); minimize movement; pressure injury prevention bundle.

opts.text: Family meeting with wife (POA) + Heart Team + palliative care: review trajectory expectations (30–50% mortality), escalation criteria, advanced HF therapy candidacy, goals of care alignment
opts.rat: Family-centered care + goals-of-care alignment. Transparent prognosis communication. Parallel pathway: aggressive recovery + integrated palliative. Wife as informed POA + advocate.

opts.text: Ambulate the patient with PT 3 times daily to prevent deconditioning while Impella is in place
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Patient on Impella via femoral access requires bed rest with HOB <30° and straight leg position to prevent catheter migration and access site complications. PT-assisted ROM in bed is appropriate; ambulation only after device removal (or with axillary Impella 5.0/5.5 which allows mobility).

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Hourly hemodynamic assessment (CI, PCWP, SVR, MAP, SvO2, lactate); Impella POSITION confirmation q4h (purple plug + placement signal + motor current + echo); LIMB PERFUSION q1h (5-P + Doppler); inotrope wean per parameters
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Continuous device + patient monitoring. Position confirmation prevents migration consequences. Limb perfusion prevents ischemia consequences. Hemodynamics guide weaning. All occur simultaneously.

opts.text: Heparin gtt titration to ACT 160–180 q4h; ensure Impella PURGE SOLUTION (heparinized D5W) continuous with appropriate pressure; daily platelet count for HIT monitoring; hemolysis monitoring q12h (LDH + plasma free Hgb + haptoglobin + urine color)
opts.rat: #2 — Device-specific anticoagulation + hemolysis monitoring. Both systemic (heparin gtt) + device (purge) anticoagulation required. HIT vigilance with daily platelets. Hemolysis trends inform device adjustments.

opts.text: Bed rest HOB <30°, affected leg straight; pressure injury prevention bundle; suction events (red alarm) → fluid bolus 250 mL crystalloid + reduce P-level if persistent; cardiac monitoring + arterial line; trend labs q4h
opts.rat: #3 — Patient positioning + device complication response. Bed rest essential for femoral Impella. Suction events have specific protocol (fluid + P-level adjustment). Continuous monitoring.

opts.text: Heart Team coordination: remaining vessel intervention timing + advanced HF therapy candidacy + escalation criteria (VA-ECMO if Impella inadequate); cardiology + cardiac surgery + interventional + advanced HF + palliative; weaning criteria reassessed daily
opts.rat: #4 — Multidisciplinary care coordination. Heart Team essential for complex decisions. Parallel palliative integration. Weaning criteria reassessed daily — improving native function allows P-level reduction trial.

opts.text: Family meeting with wife (POA) + Heart Team + palliative care: review trajectory expectations (30–50% mortality), escalation criteria, advanced HF therapy candidacy, goals of care alignment; transparent communication
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained family-centered care + goals-of-care alignment. Family meeting integrates medical plan with family understanding. Transparent prognosis. Advanced HF therapy decisions require informed consent + family input.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 3 (48 hr post-Impella): CI 3.2 at reduced P-level P4 (Impella weaning), PCWP 14, MAP 92 off norepinephrine, dobutamine at 3 mcg/kg/min (weaning); SvO2 72%, lactate 1.4; mental status alert (extubated POD 2); limb perfusion stable; mild hemolysis stable
opts.rat: Excellent response to MCS + recovery of native cardiac function. Continue weaning P-level (P4 → P2 → off support trial) over coming hours-days. Plan Impella removal once tolerating P2 with stable hemodynamics off inotropes.

opts.text: Day 5: Impella removed in cath lab; access site hemostasis with manual pressure + closure device; native cardiac function CI 2.8 with EF 25% (improved from 15% post-MI); on GDMT (sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, spironolactone, dapagliflozin), DAPT, statin; cardiac rehab referral
opts.rat: Successful MCS weaning and recovery. GDMT initiated for HFrEF. Plan AICD evaluation at 40 days post-MI if EF remains <35%. Cardiac rehab + lifestyle modification + follow-up structure. Long-term outpatient care with advanced HF specialist.

opts.text: Discharge day 8: stable on GDMT, ambulating with PT, discharge to home with home health + cardiac rehab + cardiology follow-up within 1 week + advanced HF specialist within 2 weeks + AICD evaluation at 40 days; wife as informed advocate; advance directive updated
opts.rat: Comprehensive successful discharge. GDMT, AICD planning, cardiac rehab, multidisciplinary outpatient follow-up. Wife as informed advocate. Long-term recovery focus.

opts.text: Hour 8: R lower extremity becomes dusky, cap refill prolonged to 4 sec, weak DP/PT pulses by Doppler — LIMB ISCHEMIA from Impella access
opts.rat: Vascular complication. STAT physician + vascular surgery consultation. Options: distal perfusion cannula (DPC) added below Impella, Impella repositioning, or device removal. May require fasciotomy if compartment syndrome. Time-sensitive — limb loss possible if not addressed promptly.

opts.text: Hour 12: Impella POSITION SIGNAL changes to ventricular (suggesting migration into LV); MOTOR CURRENT becomes dampened; hemodynamic parameters drop (CI 1.8, lactate climbing 4.0); echo confirms inflow displaced into apex
opts.rat: Device migration into LV. Reposition under fluoroscopy in cath lab; verify post-repositioning with all 4 indicators. Risk of LV apex injury (perforation) if not addressed. Continuous position monitoring essential.

opts.text: Day 2: develops plasma free Hgb 68 (severe), gross hemoglobinuria, Cr climbs from 1.6 to 2.4 — SEVERE HEMOLYSIS with worsening AKI
opts.rat: Severe Impella hemolysis. Options: REPOSITION device (most common cause of severe hemolysis is malposition with pump partially against ventricular wall), REDUCE P-level (lower flow = less shear), or REMOVE device if alternatives fail. Consider transfusion for symptomatic anemia + continued AKI management.

opts.text: Day 4: despite optimization, CI remains 1.6 with rising lactate; transitioning to VA-ECMO + Impella ('EC-pella') for full support + LV unloading; advanced HF specialist evaluating for LVAD candidacy
opts.rat: Escalation to higher-level MCS. EC-pella provides full cardiopulmonary support + LV unloading. Advanced HF evaluation for LVAD as bridge to transplant or destination therapy. Family meeting to discuss escalation + advanced therapies + parallel palliative pathway.

opts.text: Day 3: develops new fever 38.9°C, leukocytosis, positive blood cultures growing Staph aureus — concern for Impella-related infection
opts.rat: Device-related infection. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin for MRSA coverage); ID consultation; consider device removal if infection severe + cannot be controlled with antibiotics + line in place; concurrent endocarditis evaluation given S. aureus bacteremia (case-065 reference); typically requires prolonged antibiotic course.

opts.text: Family meeting completed: wife verbalizes understanding of trajectory, agrees to escalation to VA-ECMO if needed, asks for clarification on LVAD candidacy criteria, requests palliative care presence at next decision point
opts.rat: Successful family engagement. Wife as informed POA participates in escalation decisions. Palliative care integration appropriately requested. Plan family meeting at next decision point. Document goals of care.

opts.text: Daily platelet count drops from 168 → 110 → 78 over 3 days — suspect HIT; switch from heparin to ARGATROBAN gtt; send HIT antibody testing; continue Impella support uninterrupted
opts.rat: HIT complication (see case-064 reference). Stop all heparin (including line flushes); switch to argatroban for systemic anticoagulation. Impella PURGE SOLUTION can also be switched to non-heparin alternative (sodium bicarbonate-based) in suspected/confirmed HIT. Send PF4 ELISA + SRA; act on 4Ts ≥4 without waiting for labs. Document HIT permanently.

title: Acute Subdural Hematoma from Ground-Level Fall on Apixaban in 79-Year-Old
subtitle: DOAC Doesn't Show on INR (Send Anti-Xa Level), Reversal with ANDEXANET ALFA or 4F-PCC, Neurosurgery Within Hour, HOB 30° + Avoid BP Spikes — and the Root Cause Was Sertraline-Apixaban Plus Carpet Edge

patient.name: M. Tanaka (Mariko, 79 F)
patient.code: Full Code (advance directive: 'full code if reversible; comfort-focused if neurologic devastation' — daughter is POA)
patient.admitDate: Today, 1450 (ED via daughter who found mom on floor; daughter does daily check-ins; estimated 'down time' 1–4 hours)
patient.room: Neuro ICU Bed 2

vitals.time: 1620 (CURRENT — post andexanet alfa + nicardipine + intubated; awaiting OR)

labs.v: Non-contrast head CT at 1510: ACUTE SUBDURAL HEMATOMA right frontoparietal region, MAXIMUM THICKNESS 14 mm, with 8 mm of MIDLINE SHIFT to the left; effacement of right lateral ventricle; early uncal herniation signs (concerning); no skull fracture; no other intracranial blood; cervical spine cleared on CT
labs.r: ACUTE SDH = collection of blood between dura and arachnoid (vs epidural = between skull and dura, arterial source); typically venous bridging vein source in elderly with brain atrophy (more vein stretch = easier tear with minor trauma). MIDLINE SHIFT >5 mm or thickness >10 mm = surgical evacuation indication (neurosurgical decision). Uncal herniation signs (CN III palsy, contralateral hemiparesis, Cushing reflex) = imminent brainstem compression

labs.v: APIXABAN (Eliquis) 5 mg PO BID for AFib (started 3 years ago; reliable adherence per daughter and pill counter); last dose THIS MORNING at 0700 (~8 hr ago); apixaban half-life ~12 hr; ANTI-Xa LEVEL (calibrated to apixaban) drawn at 1500 = 248 ng/mL (therapeutic 30–230, supratherapeutic >300; THIS LEVEL CONFIRMS therapeutic + active drug); INR 1.4 (mildly elevated but UNRELIABLE for DOAC monitoring — DO NOT use INR for DOAC management)
labs.r: DOAC PHARMACOLOGY CRITICAL FOR REVERSAL: APIXABAN, RIVAROXABAN, EDOXABAN = factor Xa inhibitors; DABIGATRAN = direct thrombin inhibitor. INR is UNRELIABLE for monitoring DOAC effect (mild/variable elevation). ANTI-Xa LEVEL CALIBRATED to specific drug is the relevant measure. REVERSAL: dabigatran → IDARUCIZUMAB (Praxbind, monoclonal antibody fragment); Xa inhibitors → ANDEXANET ALFA (Andexxa, factor Xa decoy) OR 4F-PCC (off-label but used)

labs.v: ANDEXANET ALFA (Andexxa) given at 1530 — 400 mg IV bolus over 30 min then 480 mg IV infusion over 120 min (LOW-DOSE regimen for apixaban <10 mg last dose >8 hr ago; HIGH-DOSE regimen 800 mg bolus + 960 mg infusion if >10 mg or <8 hr ago); plus TXA 1 g IV given empirically; held next apixaban dose; will reassess anti-Xa after andexanet completion
labs.r: ANDEXANET ALFA (Andexxa) = recombinant decoy factor Xa, binds and sequesters factor Xa inhibitors; FDA-approved 2018 for apixaban + rivaroxaban reversal. LOW-DOSE 400 mg bolus + 480 mg infusion for last dose <10 mg AND >8 hr ago; HIGH-DOSE 800 mg + 960 mg for >10 mg OR <8 hr ago. Reduces anti-Xa activity 80–90% within minutes; expensive (~$50,000); brief reversal then anti-Xa rebounds in hours. 4F-PCC (Kcentra) 50 units/kg IV is an alternative if andexanet unavailable

labs.n: WARFARIN REVERSAL contrast (for teaching reference — not applicable here)
labs.v: If patient had been on WARFARIN with elevated INR + bleeding: VITAMIN K 10 mg IV slow + 4F-PCC (Kcentra) 25–50 units/kg IV (faster + more reliable than FFP); FFP if PCC unavailable; recheck INR q1h; goal INR <1.4 for surgical intervention. Vitamin K alone takes 6–12 hr to work — both PCC + vit K needed for emergency reversal (PCC for immediate, vit K for sustained correction). DABIGATRAN: IDARUCIZUMAB (Praxbind) 5 g IV — monoclonal antibody fragment, near-complete reversal within minutes
labs.r: WARFARIN vs DOAC reversal is high-yield. WARFARIN: vit K (slow but sustained) + PCC (fast but transient) — use BOTH for emergency reversal. DABIGATRAN: idarucizumab (fastest, most reliable); HD also removes dabigatran. Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban): andexanet alfa preferred; 4F-PCC alternative. Know which reversal goes with which agent — frequently tested

labs.v: Neurosurgery at bedside 1525; ICP not yet measured (will place ICP monitor in OR); plan: EMERGENT CRANIOTOMY for SDH evacuation if anti-Xa reversed adequately (target ≤30 ng/mL post-andexanet); if rapid further neurologic deterioration, may proceed to OR before full reversal with intraop products available; OR ready by 1700
labs.r: SDH MANAGEMENT thresholds: thickness >10 mm OR midline shift >5 mm OR neurologic deterioration → surgical evacuation. Anticoagulation reversal essential before craniotomy (severe intraoperative bleeding risk). Neurosurgery decision drives timing — wait for reversal vs proceed urgent. ICP monitor placed at surgery or separately if not surgical candidate

labs.v: Pre-fall (per daughter): oriented x3, ambulatory with cane, independent ADLs, mild cognitive impairment (MMSE 26 last year); ED initial: GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5; opens eyes to voice, confused, localizes pain); CURRENT (1.5 hr later): GCS 7 (E1 V2 M4; deep painful only, incomprehensible sounds, withdraws); LEFT pupil 4 mm reactive, RIGHT pupil 5 mm SLUGGISH (early uncal herniation sign — CN III compression); LEFT hemiparesis emerging (contralateral to R hemisphere SDH); intubated at 1610 for airway protection + impending herniation
labs.r: GCS DETERIORATION from 12 → 7 over 1.5 hr = rapid deterioration requiring emergent surgical intervention. UNCAL HERNIATION TRIAD: (1) ipsilateral pupil dilation (CN III compression, then sluggish then fixed/dilated 'blown pupil'), (2) contralateral hemiparesis (compression of cerebral peduncle), (3) altered mental status. CUSHING REFLEX (late): HTN + bradycardia + irregular respirations — sign of severe ICP and impending death

labs.v: Initial BP 188/102 — high, concerning for Cushing reflex + ongoing intracranial bleeding; NICARDIPINE gtt started 1530 at 5 mg/hr titrated to GOAL SBP 140–160 (NOT to normal — over-correction reduces cerebral perfusion pressure CPP); BP 148/84 post-nicardipine; arterial line placed for beat-to-beat monitoring
labs.r: BP MANAGEMENT in acute intracranial hemorrhage: target SBP 140–160 (NOT to normal); over-correction reduces CPP (= MAP − ICP); under-correction allows continued bleeding. NICARDIPINE (CCB) preferred — predictable titration, fast onset/offset, no reflex tachycardia; CLEVIDIPINE alternative; LABETALOL OK but less easily titrated. AVOID nitroprusside (cyanide + cerebral vasodilator) and nitroglycerin (vasodilation may raise ICP)

labs.n: ROOT CAUSE: medications + environment (the modifiable factors)
labs.v: Patient on APIXABAN 5 mg BID for AFib (necessary anticoagulation); SERTRALINE 50 mg daily for depression — SSRIs increase BLEEDING RISK with concurrent anticoagulant (~50% increased bleeding); ENVIRONMENTAL: tripped on edge of throw rug in living room per daughter's reconstruction; vision check 2 yr ago (cataracts not severe enough for surgery yet); home not modified (no grab bars in bathroom, throw rugs throughout, dim hallway lighting); fall risk score elevated but no formal fall prevention intervention
labs.r: FALL-ON-ANTICOAGULANT cascade in elderly = catastrophic intracranial hemorrhage risk. SSRIs + anticoagulant = additive bleeding risk (often unrecognized interaction). ENVIRONMENTAL fall prevention: remove throw rugs, grab bars in bathroom, adequate lighting, non-slip surfaces, walker/cane assessment, vision optimization. MEDICATION review: simplest regimen, address SSRI risk-benefit, consider switching from apixaban to lower-stroke-risk strategy if recurrent falls (controversial — the AFib stroke risk often outweighs anticoagulant bleeding risk even with falls)

labs.v: Daughter found patient on floor; estimated down time 1–4 hours (mother had been on phone call at 1100, daughter arrived 1430); no obvious hypothermia (T 97.4 — within normal); CK 412 (mildly elevated — consistent with prolonged immobilization on floor); Cr 1.2 (mildly elevated, baseline 0.9 — mild AKI from dehydration + rhabdo); skin checked — small abrasions on R elbow + L hip, no major pressure injury
labs.r: Down time after falls in elderly: prolonged immobilization → rhabdomyolysis, hypothermia, pressure injury, dehydration, AKI; estimating down time helps anticipate complications. Daily check-ins or fall detection devices (medical alert with auto-detection) reduce down time. Some patients found after 12–24 hr have severe rhabdo + AKI + hypothermia — treated as multi-organ injury

labs.v: CBC: WBC 11.4, Hgb 11.8, Plt 188 (normal — no anti-platelet contribution); BMP: Na 138, K 4.0, BUN 28, glucose 142; LFTs WNL; ABG pH 7.36, pCO2 38, pO2 142 (post-intubation, on FiO2 0.40); lactate 1.8 (normal — no major hypoperfusion); coags: INR 1.4 (uninformative for DOAC), aPTT 32 normal, fibrinogen 320 normal
labs.r: Routine labs help rule out concurrent issues. Coags don't reflect DOAC effect — anti-Xa level (calibrated to apixaban) is the relevant test. Hgb 11.8 baseline; trend post-andexanet + post-surgery. Type and crossmatch 4 units PRBC for OR

labs.v: AFib paroxysmal x 5 yr (CHA2DS2-VASc 4 — high stroke risk, anticoagulation indicated; HAS-BLED 3 — also elevated bleeding risk but stroke risk outweighs); HTN on lisinopril + amlodipine; depression on sertraline (started 2 yr ago); osteoporosis on alendronate; T2DM on metformin (HbA1c 7.2%); HFpEF EF 55%; remote stroke 4 yr ago (TIA with no residual deficit — driving force for anticoagulation); BMI 24
labs.r: CHA2DS2-VASc 4 in this patient (CHF 1 — though HFpEF, HTN 1, Age ≥75 = 2, DM 1, Stroke/TIA = 2, Vascular 0, Sex F = 1; equals 5, not 4) — substantial stroke risk on anticoagulation; lifetime anticoagulation indicated. Decision to anticoagulate elderly fallers is complex: stroke risk (without anticoagulant) often substantially exceeds bleeding risk (with anticoagulant) even with falls; modifiable: fall prevention + anticoagulant choice + interactions

labs.v: DAUGHTER (POA) at bedside, very distraught; advance directive on file: 'full code if reversible; comfort-focused care if devastating neurologic outcome'; patient was independent + cognitively intact pre-fall — strong desire to return to baseline; if surgery + recovery does not yield meaningful recovery, comfort-focused care per advance directive; family meeting planned with neurosurgery + neuro-ICU + palliative care
labs.r: Goals of care alignment is essential in elderly with TBI + anticoagulation. Mortality from elderly anticoagulant-associated SDH is high (~30–50%); functional recovery in survivors variable. Family meeting BEFORE surgery if possible (informed consent + reasonable expectations + escalation/de-escalation alignment). Palliative care integration parallel to neurosurgical evaluation

labs.v: No spinal cord injury (cervical spine cleared on CT, no other fractures); no significant chest trauma; no abdominal injury; no other intracranial hemorrhage besides SDH; no skull fracture; no signs of stroke (the deficit pattern is consistent with SDH compression, not embolic stroke despite AFib history); UDS negative; not on antiplatelet (would compound bleeding); BG 142 (not contributing to AMS)
labs.r: Rules out concurrent injuries and alternative explanations. CT-cleared spine + no other intracranial blood + no skull fracture simplifies management. Antiplatelet would compound bleeding (frequent NSAID use in elderly also adds — confirm patient is not on aspirin, NSAIDs, clopidogrel)

nursesNote: 1620 — 79 y/o female with paroxysmal AFib on chronic APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID found on living-room floor by daughter at 1430 (estimated down time 1–4 hr) after ground-level fall over throw rug edge. Brought to ED at 1450 with GCS 12 (oriented to self only, confused, localizing pain) which has DETERIORATED to GCS 7 by 1610 with new RIGHT pupil sluggish/dilated (4 → 5 mm) and emerging LEFT hemiparesis — early UNCAL HERNIATION SIGNS. Non-contrast head CT at 1510 revealed ACUTE RIGHT FRONTOPARIETAL SUBDURAL HEMATOMA 14 mm thickness with 8 mm MIDLINE SHIFT — surgical evacuation indication. APIXABAN ANTICOAGULATION is on board: last dose this morning 0700 (~8 hr ago); apixaban half-life ~12 hr; ANTI-Xa LEVEL (apixaban-calibrated) at 1500 = 248 ng/mL (within therapeutic range, supratherapeutic >300; confirms active drug). INR 1.4 is UNRELIABLE for DOAC monitoring (DO NOT use INR for DOAC management — common error). REVERSAL initiated 1530: ANDEXANET ALFA (Andexxa) low-dose regimen 400 mg IV bolus over 30 min then 480 mg IV infusion over 120 min (low-dose for apixaban dose <10 mg + >8 hr ago; high-dose 800 + 960 if >10 mg OR <8 hr ago); TXA 1 g IV given empirically; held next apixaban dose. Reassess anti-Xa post-andexanet. 4F-PCC (Kcentra) is alternative if andexanet unavailable but andexanet is the FDA-approved specific reversal for Xa inhibitors. NEUROSURGERY at bedside 1525, plan EMERGENT CRANIOTOMY for SDH evacuation once anti-Xa reversed adequately (target ≤30 ng/mL post-andexanet); OR ready by 1700; ICP monitor will be placed intraoperatively. BLOOD PRESSURE management: initial BP 188/102 concerning for Cushing reflex + ongoing bleeding; NICARDIPINE gtt started 1530 at 5 mg/hr to target SBP 140–160 (NOT to normal — over-correction reduces CPP = MAP − ICP); arterial line placed for beat-to-beat BP monitoring. INTUBATED at 1610 for airway protection + impending herniation; KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM RSI (avoided etomidate adrenal suppression + succinylcholine hyperK and ICP rise); mechanical ventilation lung-protective. HOB elevated to 30° to optimize venous return + ICP. Avoiding sedatives that obscure neuro exam (light propofol gtt + minimal fentanyl for ET tube tolerance). Pre-op preparation: type and crossmatch 4 units PRBC; CEFAZOLIN 2 g IV pre-op prophylaxis; foley placed for accurate UOP. ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS for prevention: (1) ENVIRONMENTAL — throw rug edge (will need removal + home safety assessment), (2) MEDICATION — APIXABAN essential for AFib stroke prevention (CHA2DS2-VASc 5; high stroke risk — anticoagulation continues post-recovery, though may need reassessment), SERTRALINE 50 mg adds bleeding risk (SSRIs increase bleeding ~50% with concurrent anticoagulants — discuss risk-benefit with psychiatry; tapering option), no antiplatelets/NSAIDs (would compound bleeding further), (3) FUNCTIONAL — mild cognitive impairment + cataracts + no home modifications + no formal fall prevention intervention. ANTICIPATED COURSE: emergent craniotomy if reversal adequate; ICP monitoring intra/post-op; neuro ICU x 5–10 days; mortality elderly anticoagulant-associated SDH 30–50%; functional recovery variable in survivors (~40% return to baseline, ~30% significant residual deficit, ~30% mortality at 6 months). Daughter (POA) at bedside, distraught; advance directive 'full code if reversible; comfort-focused if devastating neurologic outcome' — family meeting with neurosurgery + neuro-ICU + PALLIATIVE CARE planned for parallel-pathway approach (aggressive recovery support + palliative integration); chaplain at bedside; ethics consult available if needed. Discharge planning: comprehensive falls prevention (home modifications — remove throw rugs, install grab bars, improve lighting, walker reassessment), medication reconciliation (reassess sertraline vs alternative depression treatment, optimize fall-prevention regimen, maintain apixaban for stroke prevention barring further events), PT/OT for ongoing rehabilitation, follow-up with neurosurgery + cardiology + psychiatry + PCP, possible transition to assisted living based on functional recovery + daughter's input.
providerOrders: ADMIT to NEURO ICU; neurosurgery primary; neurology + cardiology + palliative care consultations; daughter (POA) as healthcare proxy
