EMERGENT REVERSAL of APIXABAN (factor Xa inhibitor): ANDEXANET ALFA (Andexxa) — LOW-DOSE regimen 400 mg IV bolus over 30 min then 480 mg IV infusion over 120 min (for apixaban last dose <10 mg AND >8 hr ago); HIGH-DOSE 800 mg bolus + 960 mg infusion if last dose >10 mg OR <8 hr ago
ALTERNATIVE if andexanet unavailable: 4F-PCC (Kcentra) 50 units/kg IV (off-label use for Xa inhibitor reversal but effective + less expensive)
TXA 1 g IV given empirically as adjunct (antifibrinolytic; reduces bleeding)
Hold apixaban; plan to restart only after stable + cleared by neurosurgery (typically 1–4 weeks post-bleed; risk-benefit reassessment given AFib stroke risk vs rebleed risk)

WARFARIN REVERSAL (FOR REFERENCE — NOT THIS PATIENT): would use VITAMIN K 10 mg IV slow + 4F-PCC (Kcentra) 25–50 units/kg IV (PCC faster + more reliable than FFP); FFP if PCC unavailable; goal INR <1.4 for surgical intervention; recheck INR q1h
DABIGATRAN REVERSAL (FOR REFERENCE): IDARUCIZUMAB (Praxbind) 5 g IV — monoclonal antibody fragment, near-complete reversal within minutes; HD also removes dabigatran

BLOOD PRESSURE management: NICARDIPINE gtt 5 mg/hr titrated to target SBP 140–160 (NOT to normal — over-correction reduces CPP); arterial line for beat-to-beat BP monitoring; q5-min BP during titration; CLEVIDIPINE alternative; LABETALOL OK; AVOID nitroprusside (cyanide + cerebral vasodilator) + nitroglycerin (vasodilation may raise ICP)

NEUROSURGERY at bedside; plan EMERGENT CRANIOTOMY for SDH evacuation when anti-Xa reversed (target ≤30 ng/mL post-andexanet); OR ready; ICP monitor placement intraoperatively; pre-op cefazolin 2 g IV; type and crossmatch 4 units PRBC; have 4F-PCC available intraop

ICP MANAGEMENT pre-OR: HOB elevation to 30° (optimize venous return); minimize stimulation; avoid neck flexion/rotation; minimize Valsalva (cough/strain); cluster nursing care; if signs of intracranial hypertension (Cushing reflex, pupil changes, posturing): EMERGENT MANNITOL 0.5–1 g/kg IV bolus OR hypertonic saline 3% 250 mL bolus; mild hyperventilation to pCO2 30 as TEMPORIZING bridge to surgery

INTUBATION criteria: GCS ≤8, airway protection, impending herniation; RSI with KETAMINE 1–2 mg/kg IV (or PROPOFOL 1–2 mg/kg in stable patients) + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg IV; AVOID succinylcholine (hyperK + transient ICP rise); AVOID etomidate (adrenal suppression — controversial in TBI specifically); maintain MAP for adequate CPP; pre-oxygenate

Mechanical ventilation per lung-protective standards; FiO2 to SpO2 ≥92%; AVOID hyperoxia (free radicals); permissive hypercapnia NOT acceptable in TBI (CO2 dilates cerebral vessels → raises ICP); target pCO2 35–40 (mild hyperventilation 30–32 only as temporizing for herniation)

Continuous neuro checks q15 min initially: GCS, pupil size + reactivity, motor strength, response to commands; q1h once stable post-op; document trend; immediately notify neurosurgery for any deterioration

Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line; central venous access via internal jugular (subclavian acceptable; AVOID femoral if patient may have IVC compression from prolonged supine + obesity); SpO2; q5-min vitals during active resuscitation then q1h
Foley to gravity for accurate UOP (goal ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr); strict I&O; daily weights once stable
STRICT NPO until extubated, alert, and cleared by SLP for swallow safety (cognitive impairment + recent intubation + TBI = aspiration risk)
Stress ulcer prophylaxis: pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily (mechanical ventilation + TBI = stress ulcer risk)

VTE prophylaxis: SCDs continuous (mechanical only — therapeutic anticoagulation contraindicated with active intracranial hemorrhage); restart pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH or heparin SubQ) only after neurosurgery clears (typically 48–72 hr post-bleed if stable, longer if surgical evacuation)
Seizure prophylaxis: LEVETIRACETAM (Keppra) 500–1000 mg IV q12h for 7 days post-TBI (reduces early post-traumatic seizures); benefit for late seizures less established
Trend labs q4h initially: CBC + plt (heparin watch in long-term), BMP, magnesium, phosphate, ABG, lactate, coags (anti-Xa for apixaban reversal effectiveness q4h post-andexanet); LFTs daily
NEUROSURGICAL post-op care: ICP monitor (EVD or parenchymal monitor) maintained for typically 5–7 days; target ICP <20–22 mmHg; target CPP 60–70 mmHg; multimodal monitoring per institutional protocol

Long-term anticoagulation decision (NOT immediate): apixaban will be held; restart decision in 1–4 weeks based on stability + neurosurgery clearance + reassessment of bleeding vs stroke risk; CHA2DS2-VASc 5 = high stroke risk, anticoagulation likely indicated long-term unless recurrent falls; consider LEFT ATRIAL APPENDAGE OCCLUSION (WATCHMAN device) as alternative for high-bleeding-risk patients with AFib

FALL PREVENTION at discharge: home safety assessment (REMOVE THROW RUGS, install grab bars in bathroom, improve lighting, non-slip surfaces, declutter pathways), walker/cane assessment, vision optimization (cataract evaluation), medical alert device with auto-fall-detection, daily check-in routine, PT/OT for strength + balance, vitamin D + calcium for bone health, address polypharmacy contributors (sertraline-apixaban bleeding interaction discussion with psychiatry), assisted living consideration based on functional recovery

Family meeting with daughter (POA) + neurosurgery + neuro-ICU + palliative care: review trajectory expectations (mortality 30–50% elderly anticoagulant-associated SDH; functional recovery variable in survivors), advance directive review ('full code if reversible; comfort-focused if devastating outcome'), parallel-pathway approach (aggressive recovery + palliative integration), escalation/de-escalation decision points if neurologic recovery does not occur

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Acute SDH on CT: 14 mm thickness + 8 mm midline shift + early uncal herniation signs — surgical evacuation indication; emergent neurosurgery
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — surgical emergency. SDH thickness >10 mm OR midline shift >5 mm OR neurologic deterioration = surgical evacuation per neurosurgical decision. Time-sensitive — every minute of intracranial pressure compromise causes injury.

opts.text: Rapid GCS deterioration 12 → 7 over 1.5 hr; new RIGHT pupil sluggish/dilated (CN III compression) + emerging LEFT hemiparesis = early UNCAL HERNIATION signs
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — impending brainstem compression. Uncal herniation triad: ipsilateral pupil dilation + contralateral hemiparesis + AMS. Cushing reflex (HTN + bradycardia + irregular respirations) is late sign of impending death. Emergent management: HOB 30°, mannitol/hypertonic saline, intubation + hyperventilation as TEMPORIZING bridge, EMERGENT surgery.

opts.text: APIXABAN on board — ANTI-Xa level 248 ng/mL (active drug); INR 1.4 is UNRELIABLE for DOAC; need andexanet alfa OR 4F-PCC for reversal
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — DOAC reversal decision. INR does not reflect DOAC effect — common error to use INR for DOAC management. Anti-Xa calibrated to specific drug (apixaban, rivaroxaban) is the test. Andexanet alfa is FDA-approved specific reversal; 4F-PCC is alternative.

opts.text: ANDEXANET ALFA reversal LOW-DOSE regimen 400 mg IV bolus + 480 mg IV infusion (apixaban last dose <10 mg + >8 hr ago); held next apixaban dose; reassess anti-Xa post-reversal
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — specific reversal protocol. Low vs high dose depends on apixaban dose + time. Reduces anti-Xa activity 80–90% within minutes; brief reversal then rebounds in hours. Expensive ($50,000); 4F-PCC alternative if andexanet unavailable.

opts.text: BP 188/102 concerning for Cushing reflex + ongoing intracranial bleeding; NICARDIPINE gtt to target SBP 140–160 (NOT normal — over-correction reduces CPP)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — BP management. Target NOT to normal but below severe threshold. CPP = MAP − ICP; over-correction crashes CPP. Nicardipine preferred (predictable + fast onset/offset); avoid nitroprusside (cyanide + cerebral vasodilator) and nitroglycerin (vasodilation raises ICP).

opts.text: Intubated for airway protection + impending herniation; KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM RSI (avoided succinylcholine for hyperK + ICP rise; avoided etomidate for adrenal suppression)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — airway management in TBI. RSI drug selection important: ketamine OR propofol for induction; rocuronium for paralysis. Avoid succinylcholine (fasciculations + ICP rise + hyperK risk in TBI/immobility) and etomidate (adrenal suppression in some settings). Pre-oxygenate; maintain MAP for CPP.

opts.text: Root cause: ENVIRONMENTAL (throw rug edge) + MEDICATION (apixaban necessary for AFib stroke prevention; SERTRALINE adds ~50% increased bleeding risk via SSRI interaction with anticoagulant) + FUNCTIONAL (mild cognitive impairment, no home modifications, no formal fall prevention)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — modifiable factors for prevention. Falls in elderly on anticoagulant = catastrophic intracranial bleeding risk. SSRI-anticoagulant interaction often unrecognized. Discharge planning includes home safety modifications, medication reconciliation, PT/OT, possible assisted living.

opts.text: HOB elevated to 30°; minimize stimulation; avoid neck flexion/rotation; minimize Valsalva; HYPERTONIC SALINE 3% OR mannitol available bedside for ICP spikes
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — ICP management bundle. HOB 30° optimizes venous return without affecting CPP. Hyperosmolar therapy (hypertonic saline OR mannitol) for ICP spikes. Mild hyperventilation pCO2 30 as TEMPORIZING bridge only — chronic hyperventilation reduces CPP.

opts.text: Goals of care: daughter (POA) at bedside; advance directive 'full code if reversible; comfort-focused if devastating outcome'; family meeting with neurosurgery + palliative care planned
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — goals-of-care alignment. Elderly anticoagulant-associated SDH mortality 30–50%; functional recovery variable. Advance directive guides escalation/de-escalation. Parallel palliative care + aggressive treatment. Daughter as informed POA.

opts.text: VTE prophylaxis: SCDs continuous (mechanical only — therapeutic anticoagulation contraindicated with active intracranial hemorrhage); restart pharmacologic only after neurosurgery clearance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — VTE prevention vs bleeding risk balance. Mechanical SCDs are safe in active intracranial bleed. Pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH or heparin SubQ) restarted only after neurosurgery clears (typically 48–72 hr post-bleed if stable, longer if surgical evacuation).

opts.text: Allergies: sulfa rash; NKDA otherwise
opts.rat: Reassuring — no sulfa antibiotics planned. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 97.4°F (no obvious hypothermia despite floor down-time)
opts.rat: Reassuring — rules out significant hypothermia from prolonged immobilization. Continue to monitor. Mild dehydration + AKI (Cr 1.2) suggest some down time effect but no major thermal injury.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / pharmacology.

opts.text: ACUTE SDH = collection of blood between dura and arachnoid; venous bridging vein source typically; ELDERLY have brain atrophy → bridging veins stretched + vulnerable to tearing with even minor trauma; presents with HEADACHE, AMS, focal neuro deficit; ICP rises as hematoma expands → compression → herniation → death
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Distinguishes from EPIDURAL hematoma (arterial, middle meningeal artery, lucid interval, biconvex on CT). SDH crescent-shaped on CT (crosses suture lines). SDH chronic in elderly often presents as gradual AMS over weeks; acute is rapidly progressive after trauma.

opts.text: UNCAL HERNIATION TRIAD: (1) ipsilateral pupil dilation (CN III compression → 'blown pupil'), (2) contralateral hemiparesis (cerebral peduncle compression), (3) altered mental status; LATE sign = CUSHING REFLEX (HTN + bradycardia + irregular respirations) = impending brainstem compression and death
opts.rat: CORRECT — clinical recognition. Pupil changes progress sluggish → dilated → fixed/dilated. Cushing reflex is preterminal. Emergent management: EMERGENT MANNITOL 0.5–1 g/kg IV OR hypertonic saline 3% bolus + mild hyperventilation pCO2 30 (TEMPORIZING ONLY) + EMERGENT SURGERY.

opts.text: DOAC PHARMACOLOGY: APIXABAN, RIVAROXABAN, EDOXABAN = factor Xa inhibitors; DABIGATRAN = direct thrombin inhibitor. INR is UNRELIABLE for monitoring DOAC effect (mild/variable elevation). ANTI-Xa LEVEL calibrated to specific drug is the relevant measure. Plasma half-life apixaban ~12 hr (drug effect resolves over 24–48 hr without reversal)
opts.rat: CORRECT — DOAC pharmacology critical for management. Common error: using INR for DOAC. Anti-Xa (apixaban or rivaroxaban calibrated) is the test. Dabigatran has different monitoring (thrombin time, ecarin clotting time). Half-lives matter for spontaneous resolution timing.

opts.text: DOAC REVERSAL: DABIGATRAN → IDARUCIZUMAB (Praxbind) 5 g IV — monoclonal antibody fragment, near-complete reversal within minutes; XA INHIBITORS (apixaban, rivaroxaban) → ANDEXANET ALFA (Andexxa) — recombinant decoy factor Xa, low-dose vs high-dose regimen based on drug + dose + timing; 4F-PCC (Kcentra) is alternative for Xa inhibitors (off-label but used)
opts.rat: CORRECT — specific reversal agent matching. Know which goes with which: idarucizumab for dabigatran (because direct thrombin inhibitor; antibody binds drug directly); andexanet alfa for Xa inhibitors (decoy factor Xa). Cost: andexanet ~$50,000; idarucizumab ~$3500. PCC alternatives less expensive.

opts.text: WARFARIN REVERSAL (for comparison): VITAMIN K 10 mg IV slow + 4F-PCC (Kcentra) 25–50 units/kg IV; PCC faster + more reliable than FFP for emergency reversal; vit K alone takes 6–12 hr to work; need BOTH for emergency reversal (PCC for immediate, vit K for sustained correction); goal INR <1.4 for surgical intervention
opts.rat: CORRECT — warfarin reversal specifics. PCC contains factors II, VII, IX, X (the vitamin-K-dependent factors). FFP contains all coagulation factors but slower, requires thawing, larger volume, less reliable INR correction. Recheck INR q1h post-reversal.

opts.text: BP MANAGEMENT in acute intracranial hemorrhage: target SBP 140–160 (NOT to normal); CPP = MAP − ICP; over-correction reduces CPP; under-correction allows continued bleeding; NICARDIPINE preferred (predictable + fast onset/offset + no reflex tachycardia); CLEVIDIPINE alternative; LABETALOL OK; AVOID nitroprusside (cyanide + cerebral vasodilator) + nitroglycerin (vasodilation raises ICP)
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield principle. Target is NOT normal BP — the brain needs CPP, and CPP depends on MAP. Over-correction crashes perfusion. Specific drug choices matter (cerebral vasodilation effects).

opts.text: FALL-ON-ANTICOAGULANT CASCADE in elderly: SSRIs add ~50% increased bleeding risk to concurrent anticoagulants (often unrecognized interaction); ENVIRONMENTAL fall prevention (rugs, lighting, grab bars), MEDICATION review (simplest regimen, address SSRI risk-benefit), VISION/HEARING optimization, FUNCTIONAL assessment + PT/OT, possible assisted living for high-risk individuals
opts.rat: CORRECT — modifiable factors. The decision to anticoagulate elderly fallers is complex: stroke risk (without anticoagulant) often substantially exceeds bleeding risk (with anticoagulant) even with falls. WATCHMAN device (left atrial appendage occlusion) is alternative for high-bleeding-risk AFib patients.

opts.text: INR 1.4 means apixaban anticoagulation is mild and can be safely ignored; no reversal needed for this 'mild' anticoagulation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. INR is UNRELIABLE for DOAC monitoring — does not reflect anti-Xa activity. The anti-Xa level (248) shows therapeutic apixaban effect. Reversal needed for major bleeding (intracranial hemorrhage) regardless of INR. Common error to use INR for DOAC management.

opts.text: Target SBP to normal (<120) to minimize ongoing bleeding from the SDH
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Target SBP 140–160 (NOT to normal). Over-correction reduces CPP (= MAP − ICP) and worsens cerebral perfusion. Nicardipine titrated to maintain this range. Under-correction allows continued bleeding.

opts.text: Restart apixaban at home dose immediately after surgical evacuation to prevent AFib-related stroke
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Apixaban held for 1–4 weeks post-bleed; restart decision based on stability + neurosurgery clearance + reassessment of bleeding vs stroke risk; restarting too early = rebleed catastrophic. CHA2DS2-VASc high suggests long-term anticoagulation indicated but timing requires careful balance.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Brain Herniation / Death r/t acute expanding SDH with ongoing anticoagulation; rapid GCS deterioration + uncal signs
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Imminent threat. Emergent reversal (andexanet alfa) + ICP management (HOB 30°, mannitol/hypertonic saline, hyperventilation TEMPORIZING) + EMERGENT SURGERY. Time-sensitive — minutes matter.

opts.text: Active Anticoagulation Bleeding r/t therapeutic apixaban (anti-Xa 248) with acute intracranial hemorrhage requiring surgical intervention
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. DOAC reversal is essential before surgery. Andexanet alfa specific Xa inhibitor reversal; 4F-PCC alternative. Anti-Xa recheck post-reversal; target ≤30 ng/mL for surgery. Hold apixaban; restart decision in 1–4 weeks based on risk reassessment.

opts.text: Severe Hypertension r/t Cushing reflex + acute intracranial bleeding; need controlled BP reduction to target SBP 140–160 (NOT normal)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Nicardipine gtt to controlled target. Beat-to-beat arterial monitoring. Avoid over-correction (CPP crash) + under-correction (continued bleeding). Avoid cerebral vasodilators (nitroprusside, nitroglycerin).

opts.text: Risk for Future Falls / Recurrent Bleeding r/t modifiable factors (throw rug, SSRI-anticoagulant interaction, no home modifications, mild cognitive impairment, vision issues, no formal fall prevention)
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Discharge planning includes comprehensive falls prevention. Home safety modifications, medication reconciliation (reassess sertraline), PT/OT, vision optimization, possible assisted living, fall detection device. Without addressing, recurrent fall with anticoagulant = catastrophic.

opts.text: Family Distress / Goals-of-Care Alignment r/t advance directive 'full code if reversible; comfort-focused if devastating'; mortality 30–50%; need for family meeting + parallel palliative care
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Daughter (POA) engaged. Family meeting with neurosurgery + neuro-ICU + palliative care for parallel pathway approach. Escalation/de-escalation decision points if recovery does not occur.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: ANDEXANET ALFA (Andexxa) low-dose regimen 400 mg IV bolus over 30 min + 480 mg IV infusion over 120 min for apixaban reversal (last dose <10 mg + >8 hr ago)
opts.rat: FDA-approved specific reversal for Xa inhibitors. Dose regimen depends on apixaban dose + time since last dose. Recheck anti-Xa post-reversal. Brief reversal then rebounds — coordinate timing with surgery.

opts.text: FFP 2 units IV + Vitamin K 10 mg IV for INR 1.4 elevation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. INR is unreliable for DOAC; FFP + vit K are for WARFARIN reversal. Apixaban is a factor Xa inhibitor — needs andexanet alfa (specific) or 4F-PCC (alternative). Using FFP + vit K for DOAC reversal is a common error.

opts.text: 4F-PCC (Kcentra) 50 units/kg IV as alternative if andexanet alfa unavailable for apixaban reversal
opts.rat: Alternative reversal for Xa inhibitors when andexanet alfa unavailable. Off-label use but effective + less expensive. Some institutions use 4F-PCC as first choice based on cost considerations.

opts.text: NICARDIPINE gtt 5 mg/hr titrated to SBP 140–160 (NOT to normal); arterial line for beat-to-beat BP monitoring
opts.rat: Standard BP management in acute intracranial hemorrhage. Target NOT to normal — preserves CPP. Nicardipine predictable + fast titration. Beat-to-beat monitoring.

opts.text: Sodium nitroprusside gtt to rapidly lower BP for the severe hypertension
opts.rat: ⚠️ AVOID in TBI/intracranial hemorrhage. Nitroprusside causes cerebral vasodilation → raises ICP; also cyanide toxicity in prolonged use. Nitroglycerin similar concern (cerebral vasodilation). Use nicardipine, clevidipine, or labetalol instead.

opts.text: EMERGENT CRANIOTOMY for SDH evacuation when anti-Xa reversed (target ≤30 ng/mL post-andexanet); pre-op cefazolin 2 g IV; type and crossmatch 4 units PRBC; have 4F-PCC available intraop
opts.rat: Definitive surgical intervention. Thickness >10 mm + midline shift >5 mm + neurologic deterioration = surgical indication. Anticoagulation reversal essential before craniotomy. Intraop products available for bleeding.

opts.text: Intubation for impending herniation; RSI with KETAMINE 1–2 mg/kg IV + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg IV (AVOID succinylcholine for hyperK + ICP rise; AVOID etomidate for adrenal suppression in TBI specifically)
opts.rat: Standard RSI for TBI intubation. Ketamine OR propofol for induction. Rocuronium preferred over succinylcholine. Pre-oxygenate; maintain MAP for adequate CPP.

opts.text: HOB elevation to 30°; minimize stimulation; avoid neck flexion/rotation; avoid Valsalva; hypertonic saline 3% OR mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV bolus for ICP spikes
opts.rat: Standard ICP management. HOB 30° optimizes venous return without affecting CPP. Hyperosmolar therapy for ICP spikes. Avoid factors that raise ICP (head-down, neck compression, coughing/straining).

opts.text: Restart apixaban at home dose immediately after andexanet completion to prevent AFib stroke
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Apixaban held for 1–4 weeks post-bleed; restart only after stable + neurosurgery clearance + reassessment of bleeding vs stroke risk. Restarting too early = rebleed catastrophic.

opts.text: VTE prophylaxis: SCDs continuous bilateral; mechanical only (therapeutic anticoagulation contraindicated with active intracranial bleed); restart pharmacologic prophylaxis only after neurosurgery clearance (typically 48–72 hr post-bleed if stable)
opts.rat: Bleeding-vs-VTE risk balance. SCDs safe in active intracranial bleed. Pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH or heparin SubQ low-dose) timing per neurosurgery.

opts.text: Seizure prophylaxis: LEVETIRACETAM (Keppra) 500–1000 mg IV q12h for 7 days post-TBI
opts.rat: Reduces early post-traumatic seizures (within 7 days). Benefit for late seizures (after 7 days) less established. Continue 7 days; reassess; phenytoin alternative (more drug interactions + monitoring).

opts.text: Family meeting with daughter (POA) + neurosurgery + neuro-ICU + palliative care to review trajectory, advance directive, parallel-pathway approach, escalation/de-escalation decision points
opts.rat: Goals-of-care alignment. Elderly anticoagulant-associated SDH mortality 30–50%; functional recovery variable. Advance directive guides decisions. Parallel palliative care + aggressive treatment. Transparent prognosis.

opts.text: Comprehensive falls prevention at discharge: home safety modifications (remove throw rugs, install grab bars, improve lighting), walker reassessment, vision optimization, medical alert device with auto-fall-detection, daily check-ins, PT/OT for strength + balance, address medication contributors, possible assisted living
opts.rat: Modifiable factors for recurrent fall prevention. Without addressing, recurrent fall with anticoagulant = catastrophic. Multidisciplinary discharge planning. Family + patient education + advocacy.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Send STAT anti-Xa level (apixaban-calibrated); start ANDEXANET ALFA 400 mg IV bolus over 30 min then 480 mg IV infusion over 120 min; HOLD next apixaban dose; coordinate with neurosurgery + OR
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Anticoagulation reversal is essential before surgery. Anti-Xa level confirms drug effect + guides post-reversal assessment. Andexanet alfa specific Xa inhibitor reversal. OR coordination simultaneously.

opts.text: NICARDIPINE gtt 5 mg/hr titrated to SBP 140–160 (NOT to normal); arterial line for beat-to-beat BP monitoring; q5-min BP during titration
opts.rat: #2 — BP control while reversal works. Target preserves CPP + prevents continued bleeding. Predictable titration with nicardipine. Beat-to-beat monitoring.

opts.text: Intubation for impending herniation (KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM RSI, AVOID succinylcholine + etomidate); HOB 30°; minimize stimulation; hypertonic saline 3% bolus 250 mL OR mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV bolus available bedside for ICP spikes; continuous neuro checks q15 min
opts.rat: #3 — Airway + ICP management. Pre-OR stabilization. Hyperosmolar therapy for ICP spikes. Continuous neuro checks identify deterioration.

opts.text: Pre-op preparation: cefazolin 2 g IV pre-op prophylaxis; type and crossmatch 4 units PRBC; have 4F-PCC available intraop; foley placed; consent obtained (verbal in true emergency, document); coordinate OR by 1700
opts.rat: #4 — Surgical readiness. Surgical prophylaxis + blood products available + foley for accurate UOP intraop + monitoring. OR coordination with neurosurgery.

opts.text: Family meeting with daughter (POA) + neurosurgery + neuro-ICU + palliative care: review trajectory expectations (30–50% mortality), advance directive ('full code if reversible; comfort-focused if devastating'), parallel-pathway approach, escalation/de-escalation decisions; comprehensive falls prevention at discharge planning
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care + family + prevention. Family meeting integrates medical plan + family understanding. Parallel palliative + aggressive treatment. Discharge planning starts now with falls prevention as the modifiable factor.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 4: post-andexanet anti-Xa 18 ng/mL (reversed); OR completed at 1700 (SDH evacuated, ICP monitor placed); post-op CT shows reduced SDH + reduced midline shift to 2 mm; ICP 14 mmHg (target <20); patient sedated for ventilation
opts.rat: Excellent surgical + reversal response. Anti-Xa reversed; SDH evacuated; ICP controlled. Continue ICU monitoring; serial neuro checks; ICP monitor for 5–7 days; plan extubation when fully alert + protecting airway.

opts.text: Day 5: extubated, GCS 14 (oriented x3, mild attention deficit), L hemiparesis improving with PT, ICP monitor removed, plans to transition to step-down then acute rehab unit; daughter relieved; cardiology decision on apixaban restart in 2 weeks
opts.rat: Successful recovery trajectory. Acute rehab for functional recovery. Apixaban restart decision balanced (CHA2DS2-VASc 5 = high stroke risk vs recent SDH risk); typically restart 2–4 weeks post-bleed; consider LAA occlusion (WATCHMAN) as alternative for high-bleeding-risk.

opts.text: Discharge day 14 to acute rehab: GCS 15, mild residual L weakness improving, oriented, beginning ambulation with walker + assist; comprehensive falls prevention plan in place (home safety modifications, medical alert device with auto-fall-detection, daily daughter visits, vision optimization scheduled, sertraline tapered + replaced with non-SSRI bupropion); apixaban restarted at day 21 after follow-up CT cleared; cardiology + neurosurgery + PCP follow-up scheduled
opts.rat: Comprehensive successful discharge. Multidisciplinary care plan. Modifiable factors addressed (home safety, medication interactions, monitoring devices). Apixaban restart based on follow-up CT + neurosurgery clearance. Long-term recovery focus.

opts.text: Hour 2 (during andexanet infusion): GCS drops further from 7 to 4, fixed dilated R pupil now non-reactive, decerebrate posturing — HERNIATION progressing despite reversal
opts.rat: Catastrophic herniation despite reversal. EMERGENT mannitol 1 g/kg IV + hyperventilation pCO2 25 (rescue therapy); EMERGENT OR (proceed with reversal not yet complete + intraop products); discuss with family + advance directive — devastating outcome may not be reversible. Palliative integration intensified.

opts.text: Day 3 post-op: persistent unresponsive, GCS 5 off sedation, fixed dilated pupils, posturing — DEVASTATING NEUROLOGIC OUTCOME; family meeting; per advance directive, transitioning to COMFORT-FOCUSED CARE with extubation when family ready
opts.rat: Per advance directive 'comfort-focused if devastating neurologic outcome.' Family meeting with neurosurgery + neuro-ICU + palliative care + chaplain. Transition to comfort: extubation when family ready, no ICU escalation, pain/dyspnea management, family at bedside, organ donation discussion if appropriate.

opts.text: Day 4: develops VAP (ventilator-associated pneumonia) — new fever, infiltrate on CXR, sputum positive; broaden antibiotics (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam pending cultures); continue current management
opts.rat: Hospital-acquired infection complication. Broaden antibiotics per HAP/VAP coverage; reassess ventilator bundle compliance (HOB elevation maintained for ICP + VAP prevention, oral care, sedation interruption when possible). Common complication of prolonged ventilation.

opts.text: Hour 8: anti-Xa rebounds from 18 to 84 ng/mL (andexanet effect brief, drug rebounding from tissue stores); discussion with neurosurgery for additional reversal vs proceeding with planned surgery; coordinated decision to give 4F-PCC 25 units/kg + proceed with OR
opts.rat: Expected andexanet rebound. The reversal is BRIEF (drug rebounds from tissue redistribution); coordinate timing with surgery. 4F-PCC as adjunct or alternative. Document carefully; multidisciplinary decision-making.

opts.text: Family meeting completed: daughter understands trajectory; agrees with aggressive surgical intervention + reversal; reaffirms advance directive 'comfort-focused if devastating outcome' applies if recovery does not occur; chaplain provides support; document
opts.rat: Family-centered care success. Daughter understands + agrees with plan. Advance directive reaffirmed. Parallel pathway clear. Document conversation.

opts.text: 1-month follow-up: at acute rehab, MMSE 24 (back to baseline pre-fall), ambulating with walker, ADL independence improving; apixaban restarted with cardiology coordination; vision optimization with cataract surgery scheduled; comprehensive home safety modifications complete; family discussing assisted living for additional safety
opts.rat: Excellent long-term outcome. Functional recovery achieved. Anticoagulation balance reassessed and restarted. Comprehensive prevention. Family + patient empowered. Continue multidisciplinary follow-up + long-term anticoagulation monitoring.

opts.text: Persistent mild L hand weakness at 3-month follow-up despite PT (residual from SDH); otherwise functional; on apixaban with WATCHMAN device discussion underway as additional bleeding-reduction strategy
opts.rat: Expected residual deficit common after SDH; mild persisting weakness usually does not severely impair function. WATCHMAN device (left atrial appendage occlusion) provides AFib stroke prevention without ongoing anticoagulation — appropriate consideration for high-bleeding-risk patients. Continue rehab; cardiology evaluation for WATCHMAN candidacy.

title: Amitriptyline (TCA) Overdose with QRS Widening from Sodium Channel Blockade
subtitle: Sodium Bicarbonate IS the Antidote (Loads Na + Alkalinizes to Overcome the Channel Blockade), QRS >100 ms = Treat / >160 ms = Imminent VT/VF, AVOID Procainamide + Flecainide + Phenytoin (All Na Channel Blockers — Worsen Toxicity)

patient.name: J. Beaumont
patient.admitDate: Today, 1640 (ED via EMS — roommate found her drowsy with empty pill bottle ~2 hr after estimated ingestion)
patient.room: ED Resus Bay 1 → MICU pending bed

vitals.time: Baseline (PCP visit 1 month ago, on amitriptyline 50 mg HS for chronic pain + depression)
1810 (CURRENT — after bicarbonate bolus + drip + intubation + norepi)

labs.v: AMITRIPTYLINE (Elavil) ~50 tablets × 25 mg = ~1250 mg total ingested (~17 mg/kg for 73 kg patient — LETHAL range >10 mg/kg in adults); ~2 hours ago per roommate's reconstruction of timing (between empty bottle and previous full bottle); no co-ingestants reported but UDS sent; rescue note found stating suicide intent
labs.r: TCA TOXIC DOSES: >10 mg/kg in adults = potentially LETHAL; >20 mg/kg = SEVERE toxicity (cardiac arrest, status epilepticus). Even therapeutic doses (75–150 mg/day for depression; lower for chronic pain like neuropathy or migraine prophylaxis) can be toxic in small children. Onset within 1–2 hr post-ingestion; peak toxicity at 6 hr; can deteriorate rapidly

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia rate 138; QRS 142 ms (widened — normal <100; 100–160 moderate; >160 severe); RIGHT AXIS DEVIATION; tall terminal R wave in aVR (>3 mm — TCA-specific marker); QTc 488 (prolonged); no ST changes; no VF/VT currently
labs.r: TCA EKG MARKERS: (1) QRS WIDENING >100 ms = treat with sodium bicarbonate; >160 ms = imminent VT/VF risk; (2) RIGHT AXIS DEVIATION (terminal R in aVR >3 mm and S in lead I — Boehnert + Lovejoy criteria); (3) prolonged QTc from K channel effects; (4) sinus tachycardia from anticholinergic; (5) progression to wide-complex bradycardia → asystole if severe

labs.v: AMITRIPTYLINE blocks: (1) SODIUM CHANNELS in cardiac myocytes → QRS widening + arrhythmia (PRIMARY toxic mechanism); (2) muscarinic receptors → ANTICHOLINERGIC toxidrome (dry, hot, red, blind, mad); (3) alpha-1 receptors → HYPOTENSION; (4) GABA + Na channels in CNS → SEIZURES; (5) histamine receptors → SEDATION; (6) serotonin/norepi reuptake → therapeutic antidepressant effect at low doses
labs.r: MULTI-RECEPTOR effects explain toxidrome. Sodium channel blockade is the CARDIAC KILLER. Anticholinergic explains hyperthermia (impaired sweating), dry mucosa, mydriasis, ileus, urinary retention, agitation/delirium. Alpha-1 blockade explains orthostatic + persistent hypotension. CNS effects: sedation → coma + seizures

labs.v: 1 mEq/kg = 73 mEq IV bolus (≈ 1.5 amps of 50 mEq/50 mL ampule) given at 1700 over 5 min — for QRS >100 ms; QRS narrowed from 142 to 118 ms after first bolus; continuous BICARBONATE INFUSION started 1715: 150 mEq (3 amps) in 1 L D5W at 250 mL/hr; target ARTERIAL pH 7.45–7.55 (mild alkalemia) AND QRS <120 ms; ABG q1h; recheck Na (avoid hypernatremia >155)
labs.r: BICARBONATE MECHANISM in TCA toxicity: (1) SODIUM LOAD overcomes sodium channel blockade (more Na to compete at the blocked channel); (2) ALKALINIZATION reduces TCA binding to sodium channels (pH-dependent binding) + shifts TCA from active to inactive form. Goal pH 7.45–7.55 AND QRS narrowing. Bolus for any QRS >100 ms; drip for sustained effect. Recheck Na (hypernatremia risk), K (alkalemia drops K), ABG q1h

labs.v: Initial Na 138, K 3.6, Cl 102, HCO3 22; CURRENT (post bicarb): Na 144, K 3.0 (LOW — alkalemia shifts K intracellularly), HCO3 28; will replace K with KCl 20 mEq IV; monitor Na + K q2h during bicarbonate infusion
labs.r: BICARBONATE side effects: HYPERNATREMIA (Na load from bicarb); HYPOKALEMIA (alkalemia shifts K into cells); fluid overload (volume of D5W vehicle); alkalemia (rebound metabolic alkalosis); paradoxical CSF acidosis with rapid CO2 rise. Monitor Na, K, ABG q1h; replace K to maintain 4.0; cap Na <155

labs.n: ANTICHOLINERGIC toxidrome (one of the three TCA effects)
labs.v: Findings: dry mouth + dry skin (no diaphoresis despite hyperthermia 38.4°C); mydriasis bilateral 5 mm; absent bowel sounds (ileus); urinary retention (bladder scan 380 mL; foley placed); flushed skin; tachycardia; mild agitation pre-intubation
labs.r: ANTICHOLINERGIC TOXIDROME mnemonic: DRY AS A BONE (impaired sweating + dry mucosa), HOT AS A HARE (hyperthermia from impaired thermoregulation), RED AS A BEET (cutaneous vasodilation), BLIND AS A BAT (mydriasis + cycloplegia), MAD AS A HATTER (agitation, delirium, hallucinations). Helps differentiate from cholinergic toxidrome (SLUDGE — salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, GI, emesis)

labs.v: Distinguishing TCA overdose from other similar presentations: (a) SEROTONIN SYNDROME (case-060) — would have hyperreflexia + clonus + serotonergic trigger; this patient has bradyreflexia + dry skin + Na channel blockade pattern on EKG (NOT SS); (b) NMS (case-068) — would have lead-pipe rigidity + dopamine antagonist trigger + gradual onset; (c) PHENOTHIAZINE / ANTIPSYCHOTIC overdose — can have QT prolongation but less QRS widening; (d) COCAINE / sympathomimetic — would have HTN + diaphoresis; (e) ANTICHOLINERGIC alone (diphenhydramine, atropine, jimson weed) — anticholinergic toxidrome without cardiac sodium channel effects
labs.r: Differential narrowed by: HISTORY (TCA in home, empty bottle, intent); EKG (QRS widening + terminal R in aVR is characteristic); anticholinergic features. UDS may detect TCA (some assays cross-react with diphenhydramine, carbamazepine — confirm with TCA-specific test if available). Serum TCA levels available at some institutions but don't change immediate management (treat clinically)

labs.v: On arrival: drowsy, lethargic, responding only to voice (GCS 12); pupils 5 mm sluggish (anticholinergic); no focal deficit; no seizure activity but at high risk given QRS widening + toxic dose; intubated at 1730 for airway protection + impending respiratory failure + procedural sedation; sedation post-intubation with light propofol + minimal fentanyl
labs.r: TCA AMS spectrum: lethargy → coma. SEIZURE risk is high in moderate-severe TCA toxicity (sodium channel + GABA effects); BENZODIAZEPINES are first-line (LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV); AVOID PHENYTOIN (also Na channel blocker — worsens cardiac toxicity!). If status epilepticus despite benzo: levetiracetam or propofol; ICU-level monitoring

labs.v: BP 88/52 on arrival; NS 1 L IV bolus given at 1645; BP improved to 96/58 transiently then dropped to 92/56; NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05 mcg/kg/min IV gtt started at 1720 via central line, titrated to 0.1 mcg/kg/min; target MAP ≥65; BP now 112/68
labs.r: TCA HYPOTENSION from alpha-1 blockade — NS bolus first; if persistent, NOREPINEPHRINE (direct alpha-1 agonist) preferred over DOPAMINE (indirect — works less well in setting of catecholamine depletion from TCA effects on reuptake). PHENYLEPHRINE (pure alpha-1) is alternative. Avoid epinephrine alone (beta-2 vasodilation may worsen)

labs.v: ACTIVATED CHARCOAL 1 g/kg via OG tube given at 1730 AFTER intubation (airway protected); time-since-ingestion ~3 hr — within useful window (typically <2 hr most beneficial but TCAs have ANTICHOLINERGIC EFFECT slowing gut motility so still useful at 3–4 hr); no whole bowel irrigation (not indicated for TCA); no gastric lavage (no longer routinely recommended)
labs.r: ACTIVATED CHARCOAL: 1 g/kg PO/NG/OG; most effective <1–2 hr post-ingestion; less effective beyond but TCA-anticholinergic-induced ileus extends window; CONTRAINDICATED in unprotected airway (aspiration risk) + caustic ingestion + non-toxic ingestion; ALWAYS intubate first if mental status compromised. WHOLE BOWEL IRRIGATION reserved for sustained-release or body packers. GASTRIC LAVAGE largely abandoned

labs.v: Intubated at 1730 for airway protection (GCS 12 dropping) + impending respiratory failure (RR 10, CO2 retention on ABG) + procedural sedation for OG charcoal placement; RSI with KETAMINE 2 mg/kg + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg (avoided etomidate due to potential adrenal effects in critically ill; avoided succinylcholine due to potential anticholinergic effects on metabolism though typically OK in TCA); confirmed by capnography + CXR
labs.r: TCA RSI considerations: maintain MAP for cerebral perfusion + cardiac output; etomidate vs ketamine choice less critical than in NMS; AVOID succinylcholine RELATIVE — usually fine but some recommend non-depolarizing in critically ill. Pre-oxygenate. Confirm tube placement. Lung-protective ventilation post-intubation; maintain pCO2 35–40 (alkalemia from bicarbonate + hyperventilation has additive alkalinization effect on TCA channels)

labs.n: PMH / psychiatric history / medications at home
labs.v: Major depressive disorder dx age 22; chronic pain (fibromyalgia) on amitriptyline 50 mg HS (low-dose for pain + sleep; standard 75–150 mg for depression); also on sertraline 100 mg daily for depression; lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN anxiety (occasional use); 2 prior ED visits for suicidal ideation (last 6 mo ago, discharged with outpatient follow-up); recent relationship breakup; lives with roommate; family across country
labs.r: Multiple psychiatric risk factors. The amitriptyline was prescribed for chronic pain (low dose) but accumulated quantity allowed lethal-range overdose. Post-recovery: PSYCHIATRY admission likely, intensive outpatient mental health program, mandatory hold (5150 in CA or equivalent) pending psychiatric clearance, safety planning, removal of access to means (don't prescribe TCA + opioid + benzo in suicidal patient if alternatives available), addiction medicine if substance use, family + roommate education on warning signs

labs.v: BMP: glucose 142, BUN 18, Cr 0.9 (normal); CK 380 (mildly elevated from immobility + hyperthermia); LFTs: AST 64, ALT 78 (mildly elevated — early hepatic stress); UDS: tricyclic antidepressant POSITIVE (qualitative); APAP + salicylate NEGATIVE (no co-ingestion); ETOH negative; pregnancy test negative; lactate 2.4 (mild elevation from hypoperfusion improving)
labs.r: Routine labs help rule out co-ingestants (acetaminophen, salicylate, ethanol very common in intentional overdose). Pregnancy test mandatory in reproductive-age females. UDS qualitative for TCA — confirms but doesn't quantify (TCA levels less useful clinically than EKG + clinical assessment). Trend labs q4h

labs.v: Roommate at bedside, distressed but supportive; parents flying in from out of state; suicide attempt — MANDATORY HOLD initiated (CA 5150 or equivalent depending on state) pending psychiatric evaluation when mental status clear; suicide precautions throughout hospitalization (1:1 observation, remove access to means, search belongings, suicide-safe environment per institutional protocol)
labs.r: Mandatory psychiatric holds vary by state (CA 5150 = 72 hours involuntary; other states have similar provisions). Psychiatric evaluation when mental status allows (typically after toxicity resolves, often 24–48 hr). Intensive outpatient mental health program post-medical discharge; safety planning; removal of access to means; mental health resources for family + roommate; postpartum mental health screening N/A but mental health screening for the family + roommate (compassion fatigue, grief, anxiety)

labs.v: No co-ingestants confirmed (UDS — APAP/salicylate/ETOH neg); no nuchal rigidity; no focal neuro deficit; no trauma signs; no history of seizure disorder; not pregnant; not breastfeeding; no chronic medical conditions besides depression/fibromyalgia; no QTc prolongation history
labs.r: Rules out concurrent conditions. UDS confirms TCA + rules out common co-ingestants. The QRS widening is acute from TCA, not chronic conduction issue

nursesNote: 1810 — 26 y/o female with MDD + fibromyalgia presented to ED at 1640 via EMS after roommate found her drowsy with empty amitriptyline bottle; reconstructed ingestion of ~50 tablets × 25 mg = ~1250 mg (~17 mg/kg for 73 kg patient — LETHAL range >10 mg/kg in adults) approximately 2 hours pre-arrival; suicide note found at scene confirming intent. RECOGNIZED AS TCA OVERDOSE with classic clinical + EKG presentation. Initial vitals: HR 138 (sinus tach — anticholinergic), BP 88/52 (alpha-1 blockade hypotension), RR 10 (CNS depression), SpO2 92% RA, T 38.4°C (anticholinergic-impaired thermoregulation — 'hot as a hare'), GCS 12 dropping. EKG: QRS 142 ms (widened from sodium channel blockade — primary cardiac toxicity), RIGHT AXIS DEVIATION, terminal R in aVR >3 mm (TCA-specific marker), QTc 488 (prolonged); no VT/VF currently. Anticholinergic toxidrome features: dry mouth + dry skin (impaired sweating despite hyperthermia), mydriasis 5 mm, absent bowel sounds (ileus), urinary retention (bladder scan 380 mL → foley placed). Treatment per TCA overdose protocol: (1) SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg = 73 mEq IV bolus given at 1700 over 5 min for QRS >100 ms (THE ANTIDOTE — works by Na load overcoming Na channel blockade + alkalinization reducing TCA binding to channels); QRS narrowed from 142 to 118 ms post-bolus; continuous BICARBONATE INFUSION started 1715: 150 mEq (3 amps) in 1 L D5W at 250 mL/hr; target arterial pH 7.45–7.55 AND QRS <120 ms; ABG q1h; recheck Na (hypernatremia >155 cap) and K (alkalemia drops K — already dropped from 3.6 → 3.0, replacing with KCl 20 mEq IV); (2) HYPOTENSION management: NS 1 L IV bolus first; NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05 → 0.1 mcg/kg/min IV gtt for persistent hypotension (alpha-1 direct agonist preferred over DOPAMINE in TCA — TCA depletes catecholamine reuptake so indirect agents less effective); target MAP ≥65; BP now 112/68; (3) AVOID PHENYTOIN (also Na channel blocker — would worsen cardiac toxicity); AVOID procainamide, flecainide, other class IA/IC antiarrhythmics for the same reason; if seizures, use BENZODIAZEPINES (LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV first-line); refractory seizures → levetiracetam or propofol; (4) ACTIVATED CHARCOAL 1 g/kg via OG given at 1730 after airway secured (anticholinergic-induced ileus extends window beyond usual <2 hr); intubated at 1730 with KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM RSI; (5) Cooling for hyperthermia (passive — cool environment, exposed skin, fan); (6) LIPID EMULSION (intralipid 20%) available bedside for refractory cardiac arrest / hemodynamic collapse — 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus then infusion (limited evidence but considered for severe refractory TCA toxicity). MANDATORY PSYCHIATRIC HOLD initiated (CA 5150-equivalent) — suicide attempt; psychiatric evaluation deferred until mental status clears (24–48 hr typical); 1:1 observation throughout hospitalization; suicide precautions (remove access to means, search belongings, suicide-safe environment); intensive outpatient mental health program post-medical discharge; safety planning before discharge. Roommate at bedside, supportive; parents flying in from across country; chaplain available; social work for family support + medical alert ID consideration + medication-safe-storage education. Differential narrowed: TCA overdose confirmed (history + EKG pattern + anticholinergic features + UDS+ for TCA); rules out serotonin syndrome (no hyperreflexia/clonus), NMS (no rigidity, no dopamine antagonist, acute onset), other toxidromes. ANTICIPATED COURSE: TCA toxicity peaks 6–12 hr post-ingestion; sodium bicarbonate continued until QRS sustained <100 ms × 12+ hr; vasopressor wean as alpha-1 blockade clears; intubation likely 24–72 hr; PICU monitoring 48–72 hr (mortality from severe TCA overdose 2–3% with treatment, higher untreated); long-term TCA half-life 10–25 hr (slow elimination — extended monitoring window). POST-RECOVERY: medical clearance for psychiatric admission; intensive psychiatric care; medication regimen reassessment (consider safer alternatives — SSRI like sertraline she's already on, plus gabapentin or duloxetine for fibromyalgia pain instead of TCA); safety planning + access to means restriction + family/roommate education + 24/7 crisis resources; mandatory follow-up structure.
providerOrders: ADMIT to MICU; psychiatry consultation deferred until mental status clear; mandatory psychiatric hold (5150 in CA or equivalent state law) — patient cannot leave AMA until psychiatric clearance

SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg IV BOLUS (= 73 mEq for 73 kg) over 5 min for QRS >100 ms; may repeat q5 min if QRS still widened OR pH still acidemic OR hypotension persists; CONTINUOUS BICARBONATE INFUSION 150 mEq (3 amps of 50 mEq) in 1 L D5W at 100–250 mL/hr; target ARTERIAL pH 7.45–7.55 AND QRS <120 ms; ABG q1h; recheck Na + K + Cl q2h

Bicarbonate side effects to monitor: HYPERNATREMIA (cap Na <155), HYPOKALEMIA (alkalemia shifts K intracellular — replace KCl 10–20 mEq IV q2–4h to maintain K 4.0–4.5), fluid overload (D5W vehicle), alkalemia (target ceiling pH 7.55)

AVOID class IA antiarrhythmics (procainamide, quinidine) and class IC antiarrhythmics (flecainide, propafenone) for any arrhythmia — they BLOCK sodium channels and WORSEN TCA toxicity; AVOID phenytoin for seizures (also Na channel blocker)

ACCEPTABLE antiarrhythmics for TCA-toxicity wide-complex tachycardia: continue sodium bicarbonate (first-line); LIDOCAINE is a Na channel blocker but BINDS at different state — can be used cautiously for VT/VF (controversial); MAGNESIUM 2 g IV for torsades or QT prolongation

FLUID resuscitation + VASOPRESSOR: NS 1 L IV bolus initially for hypotension; if persistent, NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min IV gtt via central access (alpha-1 direct agonist preferred over DOPAMINE — TCA depletes catecholamine stores so indirect agents less effective); PHENYLEPHRINE (pure alpha-1) alternative; AVOID epinephrine alone (beta-2 vasodilation may worsen hypotension)

SEIZURE management: LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV q5–10 min PRN seizure (first-line); LEVETIRACETAM 60 mg/kg IV (1 g typically) for ongoing seizures; PROPOFOL gtt for status epilepticus; ⚠️ AVOID PHENYTOIN (Na channel blocker — worsens cardiac TCA toxicity)

ACTIVATED CHARCOAL 1 g/kg PO/NG/OG x 1 dose if within 1–4 hr of ingestion AND airway protected (intubated OR alert + protecting airway); CONTRAINDICATED with unprotected airway (aspiration); single dose typical (multi-dose charcoal controversial for TCA)

INTUBATION criteria: airway protection (GCS ≤8, vomiting, secretions), impending respiratory failure (CO2 retention, hypoxia), procedural sedation for charcoal administration; RSI: KETAMINE 1–2 mg/kg IV (hemodynamic stability) + ROCURONIUM 1.2 mg/kg IV (succinylcholine usually OK in TCA but rocuronium preferred in critically ill); cuffed ETT; confirm with capnography + CXR

MECHANICAL VENTILATION: lung-protective TV 6 mL/kg IBW; maintain pCO2 35–40 (mild hyperventilation can ADD to alkalinization for TCA channel reversal — coordinate with bicarbonate); FiO2 to SpO2 ≥94%; PEEP 5 initially
Continuous cardiac monitoring; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP + ABG q1h; central venous access for vasopressors + reliable access; SpO2; q5-min vitals during active treatment then q1h
Foley to gravity for accurate UOP (target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr); strict I&O; daily weights once stable

Cooling for hyperthermia: passive (cool environment, exposed skin, fan, cool IV fluids); active external cooling for T >40°C (life-threatening); AVOID antipyretics (acetaminophen — does not work for anticholinergic-impaired thermoregulation; NSAIDs add AKI risk)

LIPID EMULSION (Intralipid 20%) AVAILABLE BEDSIDE for refractory cardiac arrest / refractory hypotension despite standard therapy: 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus over 1 min, repeat q5 min up to 3 boluses, then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion; max 10 mL/kg in 30 min; limited evidence but considered for severe TCA toxicity; cardiology + toxicology coordination
POISON CONTROL CONSULTATION (1-800-222-1222 in US): for severe TCA toxicity, ongoing management questions, lipid emulsion guidance, recovery monitoring duration; document recommendations

ECMO (VA-ECMO) consultation for refractory cardiac arrest / cardiogenic shock not responding to bicarbonate + vasopressors + lipid emulsion; cardiac surgery + interventional cardiology coordination; bridge to drug elimination + recovery

Trend labs q2h initially: ABG (target pH 7.45–7.55), BMP (Na cap <155, K replace to 4.0–4.5), serum bicarbonate, lactate; CK + CMP daily; LFTs q12h; serum TCA level (if institution offers; not required for management)

SUICIDE PRECAUTIONS: 1:1 observation throughout hospitalization; remove access to means; search belongings; suicide-safe environment per institutional protocol; secure all medications + sharp objects + access to harmful items; psychiatry consultation when mental status clear (typically 24–48 hr after toxicity resolves)

POST-RECOVERY PSYCHIATRIC management: medical clearance for psychiatric admission once toxicity resolved + mental status clear + medically stable; intensive psychiatric admission (involuntary hold initially) → intensive outpatient mental health program; safety planning before discharge; medication regimen reassessment (consider SAFER ALTERNATIVES — SSRI (already on sertraline) plus gabapentin or duloxetine for fibromyalgia pain instead of TCA; topical agents for pain; avoid combination of multiple psychotropics + access to lethal-quantity medications in suicidal patients)

FAMILY + ROOMMATE support: parents flying in (will arrive); chaplain available; social work for psychosocial support + crisis resources + grief if applicable + family education on warning signs; medication-safe-storage education (lock box; limit refill quantities)

Discharge planning: medical clearance + psychiatric clearance + safety plan + outpatient mental health (intensive outpatient program; PCP coordination; psychiatry weekly initially; therapy weekly initially; crisis line + medical alert if applicable); medication reconciliation with safer alternatives; family + roommate involved in safety planning; restricted access to means; 24/7 crisis resources

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: AMITRIPTYLINE ingestion ~1250 mg (17 mg/kg) ~2 hr ago — TCA toxicity in LETHAL range (>10 mg/kg in adults); empty bottle + suicide note confirm intent
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — toxicity recognition. TCA >10 mg/kg = potentially lethal; >20 mg/kg = severe. Onset within 1–2 hr; peak at 6 hr; can deteriorate rapidly. Confirmation by history + UDS + EKG markers.

opts.text: EKG: QRS 142 ms WIDENED (sodium channel blockade); RIGHT AXIS DEVIATION; terminal R in aVR >3 mm (TCA-specific marker); QTc 488 prolonged
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — TCA EKG markers. QRS >100 ms = treat with bicarbonate; >160 ms = imminent VT/VF risk. Terminal R in aVR + right axis are TCA-specific (Boehnert-Lovejoy criteria). Continuous cardiac monitoring; serial EKGs.

opts.text: SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg IV BOLUS (= 73 mEq) for QRS >100 ms; followed by continuous infusion; target pH 7.45–7.55 + QRS <120; the ANTIDOTE works by Na load + alkalinization to overcome the channel blockade
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — antidote mechanism. Bicarbonate is the SPECIFIC antidote for TCA cardiac toxicity. Bolus for QRS >100; drip for sustained; ABG q1h; monitor Na, K, alkalemia. QRS narrowing confirms response.

opts.text: AVOID phenytoin (Na channel blocker — worsens TCA toxicity!); AVOID procainamide, flecainide (class IA/IC Na channel blockers); LORAZEPAM for seizures; LIDOCAINE cautiously for arrhythmia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-yield medication avoidance. Adding more Na channel blockade WORSENS the TCA's effect. Common error in seizure management is reaching for phenytoin — DON'T in TCA toxicity. Benzodiazepines first-line for seizures.

opts.text: Hypotension BP 88/52 from alpha-1 blockade; NS bolus first; NOREPINEPHRINE (direct alpha-1) preferred over DOPAMINE (indirect — TCA depletes catecholamine reuptake so indirect agents less effective)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — vasopressor selection rationale. Direct alpha-1 agonists (norepinephrine, phenylephrine) work better than indirect (dopamine, ephedrine) in TCA toxicity. Mechanism-based choice. Central access for safety.

opts.text: ANTICHOLINERGIC TOXIDROME: dry mouth + dry skin (impaired sweating), mydriasis, ileus, urinary retention, flushed, tachycardic — 'dry as a bone, hot as a hare, red as a beet, blind as a bat, mad as a hatter'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — toxidrome recognition. Helps differentiate from cholinergic (SLUDGE) and serotonin syndrome (diaphoretic, hyperreflexia, clonus). Anticholinergic-impaired thermoregulation causes hyperthermia (treat with passive cooling, NOT antipyretics).

opts.text: Intubation for airway protection + impending respiratory failure + procedural sedation for OG charcoal placement; activated charcoal 1 g/kg via OG given (anticholinergic ileus extends usual <2 hr window)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — airway + decontamination. Intubate BEFORE giving charcoal in compromised mental status (aspiration risk). Charcoal binds remaining drug in gut; anticholinergic ileus extends window beyond usual 1–2 hr. Single dose typical.

opts.text: Hypokalemia K 3.0 (from alkalemia shifting K intracellular); replace with KCl 20 mEq IV to maintain K 4.0–4.5; recheck K q2h during bicarbonate infusion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — bicarbonate side effect. Alkalemia drops K. Hypokalemia worsens cardiac arrhythmia risk on top of TCA effects. Replace aggressively to maintain K 4.0+. Monitor closely during bicarbonate infusion.

opts.text: SUICIDE attempt — mandatory psychiatric hold (5150-equivalent); 1:1 observation; suicide precautions; intensive psychiatric care post-medical clearance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychiatric + legal framework. Suicide attempt triggers mandatory hold per state law. Suicide precautions throughout hospitalization. Post-recovery: intensive psychiatric admission → outpatient program → safety planning + access to means restriction.

opts.text: Medication regimen reassessment post-recovery: amitriptyline contributed to suicide attempt by quantity available; consider safer alternatives (continue sertraline, add gabapentin or duloxetine for fibromyalgia pain, avoid lethal-quantity medications in suicidal patients)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — prevention. Medication safety in suicidal patients includes choosing agents with lower lethal-dose risk, smaller quantity prescriptions, lock box at home, family involvement. TCA prescriptions should be reconsidered in patients with active SI.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: UDS POSITIVE for TCA + NEGATIVE for APAP, salicylate, ETOH — rules out common co-ingestants
opts.rat: Important workup completion but not driving immediate action. Helpful — co-ingestants (acetaminophen common in intentional overdose) would change management. Document and proceed with TCA-focused treatment.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pharmacology / pathophysiology.

opts.text: TCA TOXIC MECHANISMS: (1) SODIUM CHANNEL BLOCKADE in cardiac myocytes → QRS widening + arrhythmia (PRIMARY CARDIAC TOXICITY); (2) ANTICHOLINERGIC (muscarinic blockade) → dry/hot/red/blind/mad toxidrome; (3) ALPHA-1 BLOCKADE → hypotension; (4) GABA + Na channel effects in CNS → seizures; (5) histamine receptor → sedation
opts.rat: CORRECT — multi-receptor mechanism. Each effect explains a clinical feature. Sodium channel blockade is the CARDIAC KILLER (drives QRS widening, VT/VF). Anticholinergic explains many features. Alpha-1 explains hypotension. Treatment targets each: bicarb for Na channel, supportive for anticholinergic, alpha-1 agonist for hypotension.

opts.text: SODIUM BICARBONATE MECHANISM in TCA toxicity: (1) SODIUM LOAD overcomes Na channel blockade (more Na to compete at blocked channel); (2) ALKALINIZATION reduces TCA binding to Na channels (pH-dependent binding); goal pH 7.45–7.55 + QRS <120; bolus 1 mEq/kg for QRS >100 ms; continuous infusion for sustained effect
opts.rat: CORRECT — mechanism explains why bicarbonate works. Both Na load AND alkalinization contribute. Mild ARTERIAL alkalemia (pH 7.45–7.55) is the target — not just normal pH. QRS narrowing on EKG confirms response.

opts.text: EKG MARKERS for TCA toxicity (Boehnert-Lovejoy criteria): (a) QRS >100 ms (>100 = treat; >160 = imminent VT/VF); (b) RIGHT AXIS DEVIATION; (c) tall terminal R wave in aVR (>3 mm); (d) S wave in lead I >3 mm; sinus tachycardia early; can progress to wide-complex bradycardia → asystole
opts.rat: CORRECT — specific EKG markers. Terminal R in aVR is the most specific TCA finding. QRS >100 ms is the treatment threshold for bicarbonate. Serial EKGs guide treatment response.

opts.text: ⚠️ AVOID class IA antiarrhythmics (procainamide, quinidine, disopyramide), class IC antiarrhythmics (flecainide, propafenone), and PHENYTOIN (also Na channel blocker) for ANY arrhythmia OR seizure in TCA toxicity — they ADD to the Na channel blockade and WORSEN cardiac toxicity
opts.rat: CORRECT — critical medication avoidance. Adding more Na channel blockade to a TCA-toxic patient = worsening cardiac toxicity. Phenytoin for seizures is a common error — use BENZODIAZEPINES instead. LIDOCAINE (class IB) is acceptable but cautious.

opts.text: TCA HYPOTENSION from alpha-1 blockade — NS bolus first; if persistent, NOREPINEPHRINE (direct alpha-1 agonist) preferred over DOPAMINE (indirect — works less well in setting of catecholamine depletion from TCA effects on reuptake); PHENYLEPHRINE alternative; AVOID epinephrine alone (beta-2 vasodilation may worsen)
opts.rat: CORRECT — mechanism-based vasopressor selection. TCA depletes presynaptic catecholamine stores (effect on reuptake inhibition); indirect agents (dopamine, ephedrine) less effective. Direct alpha-1 agonists work at the blocked receptor.

opts.text: ANTICHOLINERGIC TOXIDROME mnemonic: DRY AS A BONE (impaired sweating + dry mucosa), HOT AS A HARE (hyperthermia from impaired thermoregulation), RED AS A BEET (cutaneous vasodilation flush), BLIND AS A BAT (mydriasis + cycloplegia), MAD AS A HATTER (agitation, delirium, hallucinations); distinguishes from cholinergic toxidrome (SLUDGE/SLUDGEM)
opts.rat: CORRECT — toxidrome recognition. Five-finger pneumonic. Helps differentiate from cholinergic (SLUDGE: salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, GI, emesis) and from sympathomimetic (HTN + diaphoresis), serotonin syndrome (diaphoretic + hyperreflexia + clonus), opioid (miosis + respiratory depression).

opts.text: ACTIVATED CHARCOAL 1 g/kg PO/NG/OG within 1–4 hr post-ingestion + airway protected; CONTRAINDICATED in unprotected airway (aspiration); anticholinergic-induced ileus EXTENDS the window beyond usual 1–2 hr; single dose typical (multi-dose for sustained-release or salicylate)
opts.rat: CORRECT — decontamination principles. Airway protection FIRST. Charcoal binds remaining drug in gut. Anticholinergic ileus from TCA slows transit (charcoal more useful longer). Gastric lavage largely abandoned for routine use; whole bowel irrigation for body packers + sustained-release agents.

opts.text: LIPID EMULSION (Intralipid 20%) for REFRACTORY cardiac arrest / hemodynamic collapse despite standard therapy: 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion; mechanism = lipid sink (sequesters lipophilic drugs); limited evidence but considered for severe TCA toxicity (also used for local anesthetic systemic toxicity 'LAST')
opts.rat: CORRECT — salvage therapy. Lipid emulsion was developed for local anesthetic toxicity (lidocaine, bupivacaine) and extended to other lipophilic drugs (TCA, beta-blockers, CCBs). Bedside availability standard in OR + ED + ICU. Coordinated with toxicology + cardiology + ICU.

opts.text: BICARBONATE side effects to monitor: HYPERNATREMIA (cap Na <155), HYPOKALEMIA (alkalemia shifts K intracellular — replace KCl to maintain K 4.0–4.5), FLUID OVERLOAD (D5W vehicle), ALKALEMIA (target ceiling pH 7.55); recheck Na + K + ABG q1–2h during infusion
opts.rat: CORRECT — predictable side effects from sodium bicarbonate infusion. Hypokalemia is particularly concerning in TCA toxicity because it worsens cardiac arrhythmia risk. Aggressive K replacement essential.

opts.text: Give PHENYTOIN (Dilantin) 20 mg/kg IV for any TCA-related seizures
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Phenytoin is a sodium channel blocker — adds to TCA cardiac toxicity. Use BENZODIAZEPINES (lorazepam 2 mg IV) first-line for TCA seizures; if refractory, levetiracetam or propofol. Phenytoin is a common error in seizure management for TCA toxicity.

opts.text: Give acetaminophen 1 g IV q6h for the hyperthermia
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG mechanism. TCA hyperthermia is from ANTICHOLINERGIC IMPAIRED SWEATING/THERMOREGULATION, not hypothalamic set-point reset. Antipyretics (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) DO NOT WORK. Treat with passive cooling (cool environment, exposed skin, fan); active cooling for severe (T >40°C).

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Lethal Arrhythmia (VT/VF/asystole) r/t TCA-induced sodium channel blockade with QRS widening 142 ms
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Wide QRS = imminent cardiac toxicity. SODIUM BICARBONATE bolus + drip is the antidote. Continuous cardiac monitoring, defibrillator at bedside, ACLS team aware. Serial EKGs guide titration.

opts.text: Hypotension / Inadequate Tissue Perfusion r/t TCA alpha-1 blockade; need for NS bolus + direct alpha-1 vasopressor (norepinephrine)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Direct alpha-1 agonist preferred over indirect (TCA depletes catecholamine stores). Target MAP ≥65. Continuous monitoring. Central access for safety.

opts.text: Risk for Seizure r/t TCA Na channel + GABA effects in CNS; benzodiazepines first-line; AVOID phenytoin (Na channel blocker — worsens cardiac toxicity)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Seizures common in moderate-severe TCA toxicity. LORAZEPAM first-line. PHENYTOIN AVOIDED — adds to Na channel blockade. Refractory: levetiracetam or propofol; continuous EEG if status epilepticus.

opts.text: Risk for Iatrogenic Worsening r/t medication choices: AVOID class IA/IC antiarrhythmics + phenytoin (all Na channel blockers); AVOID acetaminophen for hyperthermia (doesn't work — anticholinergic mechanism); use mechanism-appropriate alternatives
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Common errors in TCA management can worsen toxicity. Flag chart with 'NO phenytoin, NO procainamide, NO flecainide, NO antipyretics — anticholinergic-mediated hyperthermia.' Pharmacy verification.

opts.text: Suicide Attempt / Psychiatric Care Needs r/t intentional ingestion; mandatory psychiatric hold; 1:1 observation; intensive psychiatric care post-medical clearance
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Suicide precautions throughout hospitalization. Mandatory hold per state law. Psychiatric evaluation when mental status clear. Intensive outpatient mental health program; safety planning; medication regimen reassessment with safer alternatives.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg IV bolus (= 73 mEq) for QRS >100 ms; continuous infusion 150 mEq in 1 L D5W at 100–250 mL/hr; target pH 7.45–7.55 + QRS <120; ABG q1h; recheck Na + K q2h
opts.rat: Antidote — first-line for TCA cardiac toxicity. Bolus for QRS >100 ms (or hypotension or seizure); drip for sustained effect. Target mild alkalemia + QRS narrowing. Monitor side effects.

opts.text: PHENYTOIN 20 mg/kg IV loading dose for TCA-induced seizure
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Phenytoin is a sodium channel blocker — adds to TCA cardiac toxicity. Use BENZODIAZEPINES (lorazepam 2 mg IV) first-line; if refractory, levetiracetam or propofol. Common error to avoid.

opts.text: PROCAINAMIDE for wide-complex tachycardia from TCA toxicity
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Procainamide is class IA Na channel blocker — adds to TCA cardiac toxicity. AVOID class IA (procainamide, quinidine, disopyramide) and class IC (flecainide, propafenone) antiarrhythmics. Continue sodium bicarbonate; LIDOCAINE (class IB) acceptable cautiously.

opts.text: LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV q5–10 min for seizures; LEVETIRACETAM 60 mg/kg IV for refractory; propofol gtt for status epilepticus
opts.rat: Standard TCA seizure management. Benzodiazepines first-line. Levetiracetam (no Na channel effect, safe in TCA) for ongoing seizures. Propofol gtt for status.

opts.text: NS 1 L IV bolus for hypotension; if persistent, NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min IV gtt via central access (alpha-1 direct agonist preferred over dopamine in TCA — TCA depletes catecholamine stores)
opts.rat: Mechanism-based hypotension management. NS first; direct alpha-1 agonist if persistent. Norepinephrine or phenylephrine. AVOID dopamine (indirect, less effective); AVOID epinephrine alone (beta-2 vasodilation).

opts.text: ACTIVATED CHARCOAL 1 g/kg PO/NG/OG within 1–4 hr post-ingestion AFTER airway protected (intubated or alert + protecting airway)
opts.rat: Standard decontamination. Airway protection first (aspiration risk). Anticholinergic ileus from TCA extends usual <2 hr window. Single dose typical.

opts.text: Acetaminophen 1 g IV q6h for hyperthermia
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong mechanism. TCA hyperthermia is from anticholinergic-impaired thermoregulation, not hypothalamic set-point. Antipyretics don't work. Treat with passive cooling (cool environment, exposed skin, fan); active cooling for severe (T >40°C).

opts.text: Intubation for airway protection (GCS dropping) + impending respiratory failure (CO2 retention) + procedural sedation for OG charcoal placement; RSI with KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM
opts.rat: Standard intubation criteria. Ketamine + rocuronium RSI for TCA toxicity. Lung-protective ventilation post-intubation; maintain pCO2 35–40 (mild hyperventilation adds to alkalinization for TCA channel reversal — coordinate with bicarbonate).

opts.text: LIPID EMULSION (Intralipid 20%) bedside availability for refractory cardiac arrest / hemodynamic collapse: 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion
opts.rat: Salvage therapy for severe refractory TCA toxicity. Limited evidence but standard bedside availability. Mechanism = lipid sink (sequesters lipophilic drugs). Coordinated with toxicology + cardiology + ICU.

opts.text: Replace K with KCl 20 mEq IV to maintain K 4.0–4.5 (alkalemia from bicarbonate shifts K intracellular — hypokalemia worsens cardiac arrhythmia risk)
opts.rat: Predictable bicarbonate side effect. Hypokalemia in TCA toxicity is dangerous (worsens arrhythmia). Replace aggressively; recheck K q2h during bicarbonate infusion.

opts.text: Suicide precautions: 1:1 observation throughout hospitalization; remove access to means; search belongings; suicide-safe environment per institutional protocol; mandatory psychiatric hold (5150-equivalent)
opts.rat: Suicide attempt requires comprehensive precautions. Mandatory hold per state law. Patient cannot leave AMA until psychiatric clearance. 1:1 observation. Safe environment. Documentation.

opts.text: POISON CONTROL CONSULTATION (1-800-222-1222 in US) for severe TCA toxicity management questions, lipid emulsion guidance, recovery monitoring duration
opts.rat: Standard resource for complex toxicology cases. Document recommendations. 24/7 availability. Provides specialized toxicology expertise.

opts.text: Medication regimen reassessment post-recovery: continue sertraline (SSRI) for depression; substitute GABAPENTIN or DULOXETINE for fibromyalgia pain (instead of amitriptyline); avoid lethal-quantity medications in suicidal patients; lock box at home; smaller refill quantities
opts.rat: Prevention strategy. Choose medications with lower lethal-dose risk in suicidal patients. Gabapentin (anticonvulsant + neuropathic pain) or duloxetine (SNRI + neuropathic pain) safer alternatives for fibromyalgia. Family + roommate involvement in medication safety.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Establish 2 large-bore PIVs (≥18 g); continuous cardiac monitoring + serial 12-lead EKG; SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg IV bolus (= 73 mEq) for QRS 142 ms; defibrillator at bedside
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Access + monitoring + antidote. Bolus immediately for QRS >100 ms. Continuous cardiac monitoring detects arrhythmia. Defibrillator ready. Serial EKGs guide titration.

opts.text: Continuous BICARBONATE INFUSION 150 mEq in 1 L D5W at 100–250 mL/hr; ABG q1h; target pH 7.45–7.55 + QRS <120; recheck Na (cap <155) + K (replace KCl to maintain 4.0–4.5) q2h
opts.rat: #2 — Sustained antidote. Drip maintains alkalinization + Na load. Monitor side effects (hypernatremia, hypokalemia, alkalemia). Trend toward QRS narrowing as marker of response.

opts.text: NS 1 L IV bolus for hypotension; if persistent, NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05–0.1 mcg/kg/min IV gtt via central access (alpha-1 direct preferred over dopamine in TCA); target MAP ≥65; intubate for airway protection + impending respiratory failure (KETAMINE + ROCURONIUM RSI)
opts.rat: #3 — Hemodynamic + airway support. Direct alpha-1 agonist for TCA hypotension. Intubation for airway protection + procedural sedation. Ketamine + rocuronium RSI.

opts.text: ACTIVATED CHARCOAL 1 g/kg via OG (after airway secured); cooling (passive — cool environment, exposed skin, fan); benzodiazepine for any seizure (LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV; AVOID phenytoin); lipid emulsion bedside for refractory cardiac arrest
opts.rat: #4 — Adjunctive therapies. Charcoal after airway. Cooling for hyperthermia (no antipyretics). Benzo for seizures (AVOID phenytoin). Lipid emulsion as salvage.

opts.text: Suicide precautions: 1:1 observation throughout hospitalization; mandatory psychiatric hold (5150-equivalent); psychiatric evaluation when mental status clear (24–48 hr); intensive psychiatric care post-medical clearance; medication regimen reassessment with safer alternatives at discharge
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained psychiatric + safety care. Suicide attempt requires comprehensive ongoing care. Mandatory hold. Outpatient intensive mental health program. Family + roommate involvement in safety planning.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 4: QRS narrowed to 102 ms; HR 96; BP 122/76 (on norepi 0.05 mcg/kg/min, weaning); pH 7.48 (in target range); K 4.2 (replaced); lactate 1.4 (cleared); GCS 13 (improving on minimal sedation)
opts.rat: Excellent response to bicarbonate + supportive care. QRS narrowing confirms antidote effectiveness. Hemodynamic stabilization. Continue bicarbonate infusion until QRS sustained <100 ms × 12+ hr. Wean vasopressor. Monitor for delayed deterioration (TCA half-life long).

opts.text: Day 2: extubated, alert + oriented (medical clearance achieved); QRS 88 ms (normal); off vasopressor; bicarbonate infusion stopped at 36 hr; psychiatry evaluation initiated (mandatory 5150 hold); medication review with safer alternatives planned
opts.rat: Medical recovery. Transitioning to psychiatric care. Mandatory hold continues. Intensive psychiatric admission → outpatient mental health program. Medication regimen reassessment (substitute gabapentin or duloxetine for fibromyalgia; continue sertraline).

opts.text: Discharge day 7: transferred to inpatient psychiatry on 5150 hold; medical clearance documented; QRS normal; medication regimen reconciled (amitriptyline DISCONTINUED — substituted with gabapentin 100 mg PO TID for fibromyalgia + continued sertraline 100 mg daily for depression); safety planning with family + roommate; outpatient intensive mental health program scheduled
opts.rat: Comprehensive recovery + safety planning. Medication regimen safer (no TCA in suicidal patient). Outpatient mental health structure. Family + roommate education. Continued 1:1 observation in psychiatric setting until psychiatric clearance.

opts.text: Hour 2: cross-cover physician orders PHENYTOIN 20 mg/kg IV for new-onset seizure activity; nurse questions order citing TCA toxicity and Na channel blocker contraindication; pharmacy + toxicology consultation confirms LORAZEPAM is appropriate; order canceled; lorazepam given with seizure cessation
opts.rat: System safety win. Nurse appropriately questioned a contraindicated medication. Pharmacy + toxicology consultation. Education for cross-cover physician. Document near-miss. Phenytoin in TCA toxicity = adds Na channel blockade = worsens cardiac toxicity.

opts.text: Hour 3: develops sustained VT despite bicarbonate; cardiac arrest with PEA; full ACLS; LIPID EMULSION 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus + infusion; ROSC achieved; transferred to MICU for continued support; toxicology + cardiology + ECMO consultation for refractory cases
opts.rat: Refractory cardiac toxicity. Lipid emulsion as salvage therapy. Continue ACLS + bicarbonate boluses + hemodynamic support. ECMO consultation if refractory (rare but available for selected severe TCA toxicity). Toxicology + cardiology coordination.

opts.text: Day 2: develops aspiration pneumonia (new fever, infiltrate, leukocytosis); broaden antibiotics; continue current management; expected complication of acute intoxication + intubation
opts.rat: Hospital-acquired complication. Aspiration risk from depressed mental status + intubation. Broaden antibiotics per HAP coverage. Reassess ventilator bundle compliance. Continue underlying TCA management.

opts.text: Hour 8: K drops to 2.4 (severe hypokalemia from bicarbonate-induced alkalemia); ECG shows U waves + ventricular ectopy; KCl 40 mEq IV given over 4 hr; magnesium replaced; K trends back to 4.0
opts.rat: Severe hypokalemia from bicarbonate side effect. Recognize early; replace aggressively. Hypokalemia worsens cardiac arrhythmia risk on top of TCA effects. Reinforce K monitoring q2h during bicarbonate infusion.

opts.text: Day 1 evening: patient verbalizes regret + future-oriented thinking; engages with psychiatric evaluation; agrees to inpatient psychiatric care; safety planning initiated with family + roommate participation
opts.rat: Psychiatric trajectory. Future-oriented thinking + engagement = better prognosis. Family involvement strengthens safety planning. Continued mandatory hold + psychiatric care; outpatient intensive program post-discharge.

opts.text: Persistent QTc 458 at discharge (mildly prolonged); cardiology recommends avoiding QT-prolonging medications going forward; pharmacy alert in EHR; medical alert ID consideration
opts.rat: Expected residual finding. TCA can have prolonged effects on QT even after acute toxicity resolves. Document; avoid additive QT-prolonging medications (haloperidol, ondansetron high-dose, citalopram, methadone); cardiology follow-up; pharmacy alert; medical alert ID.

opts.text: 1-month outpatient psychiatry follow-up: engaged in intensive outpatient program; on sertraline + gabapentin (no TCA); family + roommate informed advocates; medication-safe storage at home (lock box); no SI/HI; PCP coordination; crisis resources documented + accessible
opts.rat: Best-case long-term outcome. Sustained engagement in mental health care. Safer medication regimen. Family + community support. Comprehensive prevention. Continued outpatient psychiatry, therapy, PCP coordination, crisis line accessibility.

title: ABO-Incompatible Blood Transfusion Reaction from Wrong-Patient Verification Failure
subtitle: STOP the Transfusion + Keep IV Open with NS via NEW Tubing (Don't Flush the Bad Blood); Send Bag + Tubing Back to Blood Bank; Draw Retype/Crossmatch/DAT from OTHER ARM; Sentinel Event RCA

patient.name: M. Brown (Mary, 58 F — room 612)
patient.admitDate: POD 2 from R total knee arthroplasty; ordered 1 unit PRBC for post-op Hgb 7.2
patient.room: Ortho post-op Unit, Bed A — RECIPIENT of WRONG UNIT (intended for a different M. Brown on the unit)
