Watch for refeeding-CHF: fluid retention from sodium/water shifts can cause volume overload in already-compromised hearts; cautious fluid management (avoid aggressive IV fluids; oral preferred when possible)

nursesNote: 0800 Day 2 — 19 y/o female with severe anorexia nervosa restricting type (diagnosed 2 yr ago, multiple prior outpatient/IOP attempts) admitted yesterday from outpatient eating disorder clinic for medical instability (BMI 13.8, HR 44, BP 84/52, hypothermia 35.8°C) + suspected refeeding syndrome risk. INVOLUNTARY MEDICAL/PSYCHIATRIC HOLD per state eating disorder commitment provisions; 1:1 sitter at bedside for safety + meal supervision. REFEEDING SYNDROME EMERGING on Day 2 (24 hr into refeeding at 800 kcal/day): Phos DROPPED from 2.8 pre-refeeding to 1.4 (CRITICAL — normal 2.5–4.5), K dropped from 3.6 to 2.8, Mg dropped from 1.8 to 1.4; new symptoms emerging — fatigue, paresthesias, weakness, headache; QTc prolonged to 478 (baseline 410) with occasional new PVCs. HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA is the cardinal feature + most dangerous (causes cardiac failure, respiratory failure, hemolysis, rhabdomyolysis, seizure, coma at severe <1.0 levels). REPLACEMENT INITIATED at 0700: SODIUM PHOSPHATE 15 mmol IV over 4 hr (with oral supplement) + KCl 40 mEq IV over 4 hr + magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 1 hr; recheck phos in 6 hr, K + Mg q4h; cardiac monitoring continuous (telemetry); 12-lead ECG every shift. THIAMINE 200 mg IV given AT ADMISSION yesterday (BEFORE any nutrition) and continuing 200 mg IV daily x 3 days then 100 mg PO daily x 30 days (CRITICAL RULE: thiamine BEFORE carbohydrates — carbohydrate metabolism consumes thiamine + can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy in deficient patient; never give D5W or any dextrose-containing fluid before thiamine in malnourished patient). Nutrition plan being HELD at 800–1000 kcal/day until labs stabilize (advancement plan was 200–300 kcal/day q2–3 days; goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain; standard 'start low + go slow' approach — start at 10–20 kcal/kg/day for high-risk OR 5–10 kcal/kg/day for very high-risk BMI <14 + weight loss >20%); meals supervised by 1:1 sitter (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising); 3 meals + 3 snacks; dietitian rounds daily. CARDIAC complications of anorexia present: bradycardia (HR 56 now, was 44), prolonged QTc (high mortality risk — torsades), low-voltage T waves, occasional PVCs (new from refeeding); echo planned to assess for cardiomyopathy of malnutrition, pericardial effusion, mitral valve prolapse (common in anorexia). SUDDEN CARDIAC DEATH is a major mortality cause in anorexia (~10% of deaths) — vigilant monitoring. SAFETY assessment: passive SI ('I wonder what would happen if I just disappeared') but no active plan, no access to means, no intent; 1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision (anorexia itself = slow-motion suicide for some patients; mortality 5–10% per decade of illness — one of the highest of any psych illness); routine SI screening q-shift; document. MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM coordinated: internal medicine for refeeding/electrolyte management; psychiatry for eating disorder + comorbid depression/anxiety + SI; registered dietitian for meal planning + advancement + supervision; psychotherapy with CBT (cognitive behavioral therapy) + DBT (dialectical behavior therapy); social work for family dynamics + insurance + discharge planning; FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT) — parents at bedside (mother had bulimia, recovered — relevant family dynamic), engaged in education + agreed to support meal completion at home in eventual transition. ANTICIPATED COURSE: medical stabilization 1–2 weeks (electrolyte management + weight restoration initiation); transition to specialized eating disorder unit or intensive outpatient (IOP) when medically stable; long-term recovery is months-years with multidisciplinary support; recurrence rate ~30%. Family meeting with parents + psychiatry + nutrition + therapist + social work scheduled today to align on treatment plan, FBT principles, discharge planning, expectations. Mental health continues; depressed mood + anxiety likely respond as weight restored + nutrition normalized; SSRI consideration (fluoxetine has best evidence in anorexia — limited benefit; bupropion CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders due to seizure risk; antipsychotics like olanzapine sometimes used for anxiety + small weight gain effect). Continue close monitoring of refeeding labs (PHOS + K + Mg + Ca + glucose + thiamine + LFTs) q12h; daily weights at consistent time (gown only, after voiding, before breakfast — standardize to detect true changes); cardiac monitoring continuous; respiratory function assessment (diaphragm weakness from hypophosphatemia is concerning); strict I&O.
providerOrders: ADMIT to Medical Behavioral Health Unit; INVOLUNTARY HOLD per state eating disorder commitment provisions; 1:1 sitter at bedside for safety + meal supervision

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM: internal medicine (refeeding/electrolyte management primary), psychiatry (eating disorder + comorbid mood/anxiety + SI), registered dietitian (meal plan + supervision), psychotherapy (CBT/DBT/FBT), social work (family + insurance + discharge planning)

THIAMINE supplementation BEFORE any nutrition or dextrose-containing fluid: THIAMINE 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days, then 100 mg PO daily x 30 days; CRITICAL RULE — thiamine PRECEDES carbohydrate administration (Wernicke prevention)
If hypoglycemia or fluid resuscitation needed before thiamine given: use NS or LR (NOT D5 or any dextrose-containing fluid)
REFEEDING NUTRITION PLAN — START LOW + GO SLOW:

Initial caloric target: 5–10 kcal/kg/day for VERY HIGH-RISK patients (BMI <14, weight loss >20%, prolonged starvation >10 days); 10–20 kcal/kg/day for HIGH-RISK patients (BMI 14–18, refeeding labs abnormal)
Advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days based on clinical + lab tolerance; goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for sustained weight gain in chronic phase
If electrolytes deteriorate, HOLD or REDUCE caloric advancement until stable
Composition: ~50% carbohydrate, ~30% protein, ~20% fat (initially lower carbohydrate to reduce insulin surge; advance carbs as electrolytes stabilize)
Oral diet preferred over enteral over parenteral (gut > tube > IV); 3 meals + 3 snacks supervised by 1:1 sitter (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising)
Target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week (slow + steady; rapid gain causes refeeding + fluid overload + cardiac strain)
ELECTROLYTE management for refeeding syndrome:

PHOSPHATE (hypophosphatemia most dangerous): oral phosphate supplement (Phos-NaK or sodium/potassium phosphate) if mild (>2.0); IV phosphate for severe (<2.0) — SODIUM PHOSPHATE or POTASSIUM PHOSPHATE 15–30 mmol IV over 4–6 hr (rate cap to avoid hypocalcemia from phosphate-calcium binding); recheck phos q6h until stable + adequate
POTASSIUM (hypokalemia): oral KCl 40 mEq PO BID + IV KCl 10 mEq/hr peripheral (up to 20 mEq/hr central in severe); recheck q4h; target 4.0–4.5
MAGNESIUM (hypomagnesemia): IV MgSO4 2 g over 1 hr (severe) OR oral Mg oxide 400 mg PO BID; recheck q6h; target >1.8; MUST replace Mg to retain replaced K (low Mg prevents K retention)
CALCIUM (may drop with phosphate replacement): monitor ionized Ca; replace IV if symptomatic or critically low
GLUCOSE: BG q6h (insulin-mediated hypoglycemia rare but possible during refeeding; carbohydrate intake may cause mild hyperglycemia early)

Lab monitoring schedule: phos + K + Mg + Ca + glucose + BMP q6–12h x first week (depending on stability); thiamine level baseline; daily LFTs (refeeding hepatitis); weekly DEXA scan not needed inpatient (osteoporosis chronic)

Cardiac monitoring: CONTINUOUS telemetry x first week of refeeding; 12-lead ECG every shift then daily once stable; ECHO at admission to assess for cardiomyopathy of malnutrition + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse; cardiology consultation if any concerning findings

CARDIAC complications to monitor: BRADYCARDIA (HR <40 = severe; do NOT over-correct with fluids — chronic adaptation), HYPOTENSION (do NOT over-resuscitate — risk of refeeding CHF), QT PROLONGATION (electrolyte + chronic; torsades risk if very prolonged or with QT-prolonging drugs), CARDIOMYOPATHY (myocardial wasting; improves with weight restoration), PERICARDIAL EFFUSION (rare), MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE (common), ARRHYTHMIA, SUDDEN CARDIAC DEATH (major mortality cause)

CAUTIOUS FLUID management: AVOID aggressive IV fluids (refeeding fluid retention + cardiomyopathy = CHF risk); oral fluids preferred; if IV needed, NS not D5W until thiamine given; reassess daily; daily weights at consistent time (gown only, after voiding, before breakfast — standardize)

SAFETY: 1:1 sitter at bedside (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising + safety + companionship); routine SI/HI assessment q-shift; remove access to means; suicide-safe environment; mandatory eating disorder hold per state provisions; psychotherapy continues

PSYCHOTHERAPY + MEDICATIONS: CBT + DBT + FBT (family-based therapy — especially effective in adolescents + young adults; parents take charge of meals + weight restoration in early phase); medications: FLUOXETINE (best evidence in anorexia among SSRIs — limited benefit; consider for comorbid depression/OCD); OLANZAPINE (low-dose; some evidence for anxiety + small weight gain effect — atypical antipsychotic, monitor metabolic effects); ⚠️ BUPROPION CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders (seizure risk in eating disorder patients with electrolyte derangement + low body weight)

FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT): parents at bedside; family meeting today with psychiatry + nutrition + therapist + social work to align on treatment plan, FBT principles (parents take charge of meals + weight restoration; gradual return of autonomy in later phases), discharge planning, expectations; mother had bulimia (recovered) — relevant family dynamic

Long-term care planning: medical stabilization 1–2 weeks → transition to specialized eating disorder unit (ED unit) or intensive outpatient program (IOP); long-term recovery months-years; recurrence ~30%; multidisciplinary follow-up: medical/PCP, psychiatry, dietitian, psychotherapy; medical alert ID with eating disorder history; family education; peer support resources

Discharge criteria: medical stability (electrolytes normal x 48 hr off IV repletion; HR ≥45 sustained; BP ≥90/60; weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week); psychiatric stability + outpatient plan; family engagement + agreement to FBT principles; close outpatient follow-up scheduled (within 1 week of discharge); plan for ongoing meal supervision at home (parental supervision per FBT)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA 1.4 mg/dL (CRITICAL — dropped from 2.8 pre-refeeding); new fatigue + paresthesias + weakness + prolonged QTc 478 + PVCs — refeeding syndrome with cardiac/neuromuscular manifestations
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — most dangerous refeeding manifestation. Severe hypophosphatemia (<1.0) causes cardiac failure, respiratory failure, hemolysis, rhabdo, seizures, coma. STAT IV phosphate replacement. Recheck q6h. Cardiac monitoring throughout.

opts.text: HYPOKALEMIA K 2.8 + HYPOMAGNESEMIA Mg 1.4 — completing refeeding electrolyte triad; cardiac arrhythmia + QT prolongation + torsades risk; replace IV (KCl + MgSO4) and oral
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — additional refeeding electrolyte shifts. K + Mg + Phos all drop in refeeding. Replace gradually. Must replace Mg to retain replaced K. Continuous cardiac monitoring.

opts.text: Severe anorexia BMI 13.8 (extreme per DSM-5); high refeeding risk (BMI <14 + weight loss 32% + prolonged restriction); medical hospitalization criteria met
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severity stratification. Severity drives intensity of monitoring + refeeding caution. Multiple criteria met for inpatient admission. Multidisciplinary team essential.

opts.text: THIAMINE 200 mg IV given BEFORE any nutrition or dextrose; continuing prophylaxis x 3–7 days; CRITICAL RULE prevents Wernicke encephalopathy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Wernicke prevention. Carbohydrate metabolism consumes thiamine; refeeding without thiamine = precipitates Wernicke (confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia) → Korsakoff (amnesia, confabulation) if untreated. Standard prophylaxis 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days.

opts.text: START LOW + GO SLOW refeeding approach: 5–10 kcal/kg/day for very high-risk (BMI <14, weight loss >20%) OR 10–20 kcal/kg/day for high-risk; advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days; HOLD or REDUCE if labs deteriorate
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — refeeding strategy. Rapid advancement causes refeeding syndrome. Start at low caloric target; advance slowly with lab monitoring. Hold advancement if electrolytes drop. Goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain in chronic phase.

opts.text: Cardiac monitoring: bradycardia HR 56 (was 44 admission), prolonged QTc 478, occasional PVCs (new); sudden cardiac death is major mortality cause in anorexia (~10% of deaths); echo planned
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — cardiac complications surveillance. Continuous telemetry x first week of refeeding. Echo for cardiomyopathy + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse (common in anorexia). Avoid QT-prolonging meds. Cardiology if concerning findings.

opts.text: Anorexia mortality 5–10% per decade — one of highest of any psychiatric illness; from medical complications + suicide; passive SI present (no active plan); 1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — mortality risk + safety. Continuous 1:1 for safety + meal supervision (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising). Routine SI screening. Anorexia itself is slow-motion suicide for some — extra vigilance.

opts.text: Multidisciplinary team: medical + psychiatry + dietitian + psychotherapy (CBT/DBT/FBT) + social work + family engagement (FBT particularly effective)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — standard of care. Each discipline essential. FBT (family-based therapy) especially effective in adolescents + young adults (parents take charge of meals + weight restoration). Long-term recovery requires sustained multidisciplinary support.

opts.text: CAUTIOUS fluid management: AVOID aggressive IV fluids (refeeding fluid retention + cardiomyopathy = CHF risk); oral preferred; NS not D5W if IV needed (before thiamine)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fluid management nuance. The adapted bradycardia + hypotension should NOT be aggressively corrected (chronic adaptation; over-correction → CHF in cardiomyopathy + fluid retention from sodium/water shifts). Oral over IV when possible.

opts.text: MEDICATION SAFETY: BUPROPION CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders (seizure risk in low body weight + electrolyte derangement); FLUOXETINE has best evidence (limited); OLANZAPINE low-dose for anxiety + small weight effect; AVOID QT-prolonging meds
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychotropic medication safety in eating disorders. Bupropion contraindicated. Fluoxetine for comorbid depression/OCD. Olanzapine low-dose option. Avoid QT-prolonging combinations (already prolonged from chronic + electrolyte derangement).

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 36.2°C (mild hypothermia — improved from 35.8°C on admission)
opts.rat: Mild residual hypothermia from chronic malnutrition; improving with refeeding + warming measures. Continue passive warming (warm blankets, room temperature). Not driving immediate action; monitor.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / refeeding principles.

opts.text: REFEEDING SYNDROME = electrolyte + fluid shifts when malnourished patient resumes nutrition: insulin surge from CARBOHYDRATE intake drives PHOSPHATE, POTASSIUM, MAGNESIUM intracellularly → severe deficiencies; thiamine consumed by carbohydrate metabolism (deficiency → Wernicke); fluid retention (sodium/water shifts) → cardiac strain
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Carbohydrate intake triggers insulin → intracellular shifts. The total body stores were depleted during starvation; the serum levels held up but now drop sharply with intracellular shift. Time course: typically days 1–4 of refeeding.

opts.text: HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA is the cardinal feature + most dangerous; SEVERE <1.0 mg/dL = cardiac failure (ATP depletion → decreased contractility), respiratory failure (diaphragm weakness), hemolysis (RBC ATP depletion), rhabdomyolysis (muscle ATP depletion), encephalopathy + seizures + coma + death
opts.rat: CORRECT — defines the danger. Phosphate is essential for ATP, 2,3-DPG, cellular membrane structure, neurologic function. Replacement: oral if mild; IV sodium/potassium phosphate for severe; cap rate (hypocalcemia, hyperphosphatemia, nephrocalcinosis risk).

opts.text: REFEEDING RISK STRATIFICATION (NICE 2006): HIGH risk if BMI <16 OR weight loss >15% in 3–6 mo OR little/no intake >10 days OR abnormal pre-feeding electrolytes; VERY HIGH risk if BMI <14 OR weight loss >20% OR prolonged starvation >15 days OR multiple risk factors
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk stratification drives refeeding strategy. Higher risk → more cautious initial caloric target (5–10 kcal/kg/day vs 10–20). This patient at BMI 13.8 + 32% weight loss + 6+ months severe restriction = very high risk.

opts.text: REFEEDING APPROACH: START LOW + GO SLOW; initial 5–10 kcal/kg/day (very high risk) or 10–20 kcal/kg/day (high risk); advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days based on tolerance; goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain; HOLD or REDUCE if electrolytes deteriorate
opts.rat: CORRECT — standardized approach. Slow advancement is the key to prevention. Lab monitoring guides advancement. Carbohydrate proportion initially lower (50%) advancing as electrolytes stabilize. Oral diet preferred (gut > enteral > parenteral). Target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week.

opts.text: THIAMINE 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days BEFORE any nutrition or dextrose; CRITICAL RULE — carbohydrate metabolism CONSUMES thiamine; refeeding without thiamine = precipitates WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY (confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia) → KORSAKOFF syndrome (amnesia, confabulation) if untreated
opts.rat: CORRECT — Wernicke prevention rule. Standard prophylaxis at admission for refeeding-risk patients. Continue oral thiamine 100 mg PO daily x 30 days. NEVER give D5W or any dextrose-containing fluid before thiamine in malnourished patient — use NS or LR if fluid needed.

opts.text: CARDIAC complications of anorexia: BRADYCARDIA + HYPOTENSION (chronic adaptation — do NOT over-correct with fluids), QT PROLONGATION (electrolyte + chronic; torsades risk), CARDIOMYOPATHY (myocardial wasting), PERICARDIAL EFFUSION (rare; protein loss), MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE (common — cardiac mass reduction), SUDDEN CARDIAC DEATH (~10% of mortality)
opts.rat: CORRECT — cardiac complications spectrum. Continuous telemetry during refeeding. Echo at admission for structural assessment. Avoid QT-prolonging medications. Cardiology consultation for concerning findings.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE for anorexia: MEDICAL (refeeding + electrolyte management), PSYCHIATRY (eating disorder + comorbid mood/anxiety + SI), REGISTERED DIETITIAN (meal plan + supervision + advancement), PSYCHOTHERAPY (CBT, DBT, FBT — family-based therapy especially effective in adolescents/young adults), SOCIAL WORK (family + insurance + discharge planning)
opts.rat: CORRECT — standard of care. Long-term recovery requires sustained multidisciplinary support. FBT (Maudsley model) particularly effective: parents take charge of meals + weight restoration in early phase; gradual return of autonomy in later phases. Family education + therapy.

opts.text: BUPROPION (Wellbutrin) is the first-line antidepressant for comorbid depression in anorexia
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — BUPROPION CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders due to SEIZURE RISK (low body weight + electrolyte derangement + bupropion lowers seizure threshold). FLUOXETINE (Prozac) has the best evidence among SSRIs in anorexia though benefit is modest. Olanzapine (atypical antipsychotic) sometimes used low-dose for anxiety + small weight gain effect; monitor metabolic effects.

opts.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with D5W to address the bradycardia and hypotension immediately
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG on multiple counts: (1) the bradycardia + hypotension are chronic adaptations — do NOT aggressively correct (risk of refeeding CHF from fluid retention + cardiomyopathy); (2) D5W BEFORE thiamine in malnourished patient = precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy (carbohydrate metabolism consumes thiamine — if any IV fluid needed before thiamine, use NS or LR).

opts.text: Advance caloric intake rapidly (500–1000 kcal/day per day) to achieve weight gain quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. RAPID advancement causes severe refeeding syndrome. Standard approach is START LOW + GO SLOW (5–10 or 10–20 kcal/kg/day initial; advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days). Target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week is appropriate. Faster gain = refeeding + fluid overload + cardiac strain.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Refeeding Syndrome with Electrolyte Derangement (phos 1.4, K 2.8, Mg 1.4) r/t insulin-driven intracellular shifts with carbohydrate refeeding; cardiac/respiratory/neuromuscular complication risk
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The refeeding syndrome is the imminent danger. Hypophosphatemia is the cardinal + most dangerous feature. Replace IV + oral; hold/reduce caloric advancement; recheck labs q6h; continuous cardiac monitoring.

opts.text: Risk for Cardiac Arrhythmia / Sudden Cardiac Death r/t anorexia cardiomyopathy + QT prolongation + electrolyte derangement
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Sudden cardiac death is major mortality cause in anorexia (~10%). Continuous telemetry x first week. Replace electrolytes. Avoid QT-prolonging medications. Echo for structural assessment. Cardiology for concerning findings.

opts.text: Risk for Wernicke Encephalopathy r/t carbohydrate refeeding without adequate thiamine; thiamine consumed by carbohydrate metabolism
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Wernicke prevention via thiamine 200–300 mg IV BEFORE any nutrition or dextrose. Continue 3–7 days. Watch for confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia. Korsakoff if untreated (amnesia, confabulation — often irreversible).

opts.text: Risk for Suicide / Continued Restrictive Eating r/t severe anorexia (mortality 5–10% per decade); passive SI; food hiding/disposal/exercising risk
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Anorexia is slow-motion suicide for some. 1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision. Routine SI screening. Psychiatric care + medications (fluoxetine, olanzapine — AVOID bupropion). Long-term recovery.

opts.text: Family Dynamics / Multidisciplinary Care Coordination r/t need for sustained team-based recovery support
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. FBT (family-based therapy) especially effective in young adults. Multidisciplinary care: medical + psychiatry + dietitian + therapy + social work. Long-term outpatient structure essential.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: STAT IV phosphate (15 mmol sodium phosphate over 4 hr) + IV KCl (40 mEq over 4 hr) + IV magnesium sulfate (2 g over 1 hr); oral supplements added; recheck phos q6h, K + Mg q4h
opts.rat: Refeeding syndrome electrolyte replacement. Phosphate is most dangerous + needs IV in severe (<2.0). K + Mg replaced with monitoring. Cardiac monitoring throughout (electrolytes + arrhythmia risk).

opts.text: THIAMINE 200 mg IV daily x 3–7 days (already started at admission BEFORE any nutrition); continuing prophylaxis to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
opts.rat: Wernicke prevention rule. Carbohydrate metabolism consumes thiamine; refeeding without thiamine = precipitates Wernicke. Standard prophylaxis 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days. Continue oral 100 mg daily x 30 days.

opts.text: HOLD or REDUCE caloric advancement at 800–1000 kcal/day until refeeding labs stabilize; resume advancement at 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days when stable
opts.rat: Standard refeeding approach. Labs guide advancement. Hold if deteriorating; resume gradually. The 'start low + go slow' principle. Target 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain in chronic phase.

opts.text: Advance caloric intake rapidly to 2000 kcal/day immediately to achieve faster weight gain
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Rapid advancement causes severe refeeding syndrome. Standard approach is start low + go slow. Target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week. Faster gain = refeeding + fluid overload + cardiac strain.

opts.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with D5W to address bradycardia and hypotension
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. (1) Adapted bradycardia + hypotension should NOT be aggressively corrected (chronic adaptation; CHF risk with refeeding fluid retention + cardiomyopathy). (2) D5W BEFORE thiamine = Wernicke encephalopathy precipitation. If IV fluid needed, use NS or LR; cautious volume.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring (telemetry); ECG every shift; echo to assess cardiomyopathy + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse; cardiology consultation if concerning
opts.rat: Standard anorexia cardiac monitoring. Sudden death major mortality cause. Echo for structural assessment. Continuous tele x first week of refeeding. Avoid QT-prolonging medications.

opts.text: 1:1 sitter at bedside for safety + meal supervision (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising + safety + companionship)
opts.rat: Standard safety + meal supervision. Anorexia patients may hide food, exercise covertly, induce vomiting. Sitter provides constant supervision + companionship + safety. 1:1 reduces during stabilization.

opts.text: Bupropion 150 mg PO daily for comorbid depression
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders due to SEIZURE RISK (low body weight + electrolyte derangement). FLUOXETINE preferred for comorbid depression/OCD in anorexia (limited benefit but best evidence). OLANZAPINE low-dose option for anxiety + small weight gain effect.

opts.text: Multidisciplinary team coordination: medical (refeeding management), psychiatry (eating disorder + comorbid mood/anxiety + SI), registered dietitian (meal plan + supervision), psychotherapy (CBT/DBT/FBT), social work (family + insurance + discharge planning)
opts.rat: Standard of care for anorexia. Each discipline essential. Long-term recovery requires sustained multidisciplinary support. FBT (family-based therapy) particularly effective in adolescents + young adults.

opts.text: FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT) involvement: parents at bedside; family meeting today with full team; education on FBT principles (parents take charge of meals + weight restoration early; gradual return of autonomy)
opts.rat: FBT (Maudsley model) is evidence-based + effective in young adults. Parents engaged in meal supervision continues outpatient. Family education + therapy. Mother's bulimia history relevant family dynamic to address.

opts.text: Daily weights at consistent time (gown only, after voiding, before breakfast — standardized to detect true changes vs fluid fluctuations)
opts.rat: Standard anorexia weight monitoring. Standardization prevents misleading fluctuations from clothes, fluid, food. Trend matters more than single value. Weight discussed with patient per institutional protocol (some recommend 'blind weights' for psychological reasons).

opts.text: Long-term care planning: transition to specialized eating disorder unit or intensive outpatient program (IOP) when medically stable; multidisciplinary follow-up; family engagement; recurrence prevention
opts.rat: Standard discharge planning. Medical stabilization first; psychiatric/eating disorder care continues at higher level (ED unit, IOP, then outpatient). Long-term recovery months-years; recurrence ~30%. Sustained multidisciplinary support.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: STAT IV phosphate 15 mmol sodium phosphate over 4 hr + IV KCl 40 mEq over 4 hr + IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 1 hr; continuous cardiac monitoring; recheck phos q6h, K + Mg q4h; oral supplements added
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Refeeding electrolytes are the imminent danger. IV replacement for severe deficiencies. Cardiac monitoring throughout. Lab trending guides further care.

opts.text: HOLD caloric advancement at 800–1000 kcal/day until labs stabilize; THIAMINE 200 mg IV daily continued (Wernicke prevention); strict 1:1 meal supervision continues (prevents food hiding/disposal)
opts.rat: #2 — Adjust refeeding + maintain prophylaxis + safety. Holding advancement prevents worsening refeeding. Thiamine continues. Meal supervision prevents covert restriction.

opts.text: 12-lead ECG q-shift; echo to assess cardiomyopathy + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse; cardiology consultation for concerning findings; AVOID QT-prolonging medications
opts.rat: #3 — Cardiac assessment. Sudden death is major mortality. Echo at admission; serial ECGs. Avoid QT-prolonging medication combinations (already prolonged from electrolyte + chronic).

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM coordination: psychiatry + dietitian + psychotherapy + social work + family-based treatment (FBT); 1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision; family meeting today with full team for FBT education + discharge planning + expectations
opts.rat: #4 — Comprehensive care + family. Multidisciplinary team is standard. FBT particularly effective in young adults. Family meeting essential for treatment plan alignment. Mother's bulimia history relevant family dynamic.

opts.text: Long-term care planning: transition to specialized eating disorder unit or IOP when medically stable; outpatient multidisciplinary follow-up; family engagement; medication consideration (fluoxetine for depression/OCD; AVOID bupropion); medical alert + recurrence prevention
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained recovery. Long-term recovery is months-years; recurrence common. Specialized eating disorder programs effective. Outpatient structure essential. Family + peer support resources. Lifelong vigilance for relapse.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 4: Phos 2.6, K 4.0, Mg 1.9 (all stable in normal range after IV + oral replacement); QTc 440 (improved from 478); no new PVCs; nutrition advanced to 1200 kcal/day; patient reports feeling 'less weak'; cardiac monitoring continued
opts.rat: Excellent response to refeeding management. Electrolytes stable; cardiac findings improving. Continue advancement 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days. Continue monitoring + thiamine + 1:1 supervision. Engaged in psychotherapy + FBT.

opts.text: Day 10: BMI 14.5 (gaining 0.5 kg/week); electrolytes stable; QTc normal; engaging in psychotherapy + DBT skills; family meetings ongoing; planning transition to specialized eating disorder unit when discharged from medical floor in 2–3 days
opts.rat: Successful medical stabilization + early recovery. Slow steady weight gain. Continued multidisciplinary care. Transition to higher level of care (ED unit or IOP) for ongoing eating disorder treatment. Family engagement essential.

opts.text: 3-month outpatient follow-up: BMI 17.2 (continued gradual gain); engaging in CBT weekly + dietitian biweekly + psychiatrist monthly; family FBT ongoing; mood improved; back in college part-time; supportive family + peer group; no recurrence to date
opts.rat: Long-term recovery trajectory. Continued multidisciplinary care + family + peer support. Gradual return to normal life with continued vigilance. Recurrence rate ~30% — long-term follow-up essential. Continued psychotherapy + nutrition optimization.

opts.text: Day 3 evening: develops new dyspnea, crackles bilateral bases, JVP elevated, pitting edema lower extremities — refeeding CARDIOMYOPATHY + fluid overload (CHF); diuretic given; nutrition advancement HELD
opts.rat: Refeeding CHF. Fluid retention from sodium/water shifts + reduced cardiac function from chronic malnutrition + carbohydrate-driven insulin shifts = volume overload + cardiac strain. Furosemide for diuresis; hold nutrition advancement; cardiology consultation; echo if new findings.

opts.text: Day 5: develops new confusion + nystagmus + ataxia despite thiamine prophylaxis — possible WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY; IV thiamine dose doubled to 500 mg q8h; neurology consultation
opts.rat: Wernicke encephalopathy can occur despite prophylaxis (suboptimal absorption, increased needs from refeeding metabolism). Higher-dose IV thiamine; neurology consultation. Treat early to prevent Korsakoff (largely irreversible). Document thiamine timing meticulously.

opts.text: Day 4: another nurse, unaware of refeeding protocol, increased nutrition to 1500 kcal overnight; Phos dropped to 1.0; new PVCs progressing to brief NSVT; emergent IV phosphate + KCl + magnesium + cardiology + hold nutrition
opts.rat: ⚠️ Iatrogenic refeeding crisis from rapid advancement. The 'start low + go slow' principle must be enforced. Education for all staff. Document near-miss. Hold nutrition + electrolyte replacement + cardiac monitoring. Reinforce protocol.

opts.text: Day 7: patient caught hiding food in napkin during meal; 1:1 sitter intervenes; intake monitoring intensified; therapy session focuses on resistance + ambivalence about recovery; family informed
opts.rat: Expected resistance in anorexia treatment. Food hiding common. 1:1 sitter prevents but doesn't 'cure' the underlying drive. Therapy addresses ambivalence + motivation. Family involvement in problem-solving. Document; continue care.

opts.text: Discharge day 14: transferred to specialized eating disorder residential program for continued treatment; medical stable; weight gain 1 kg over admission; psychotherapy + family + multidisciplinary structure established; outpatient follow-up plan
opts.rat: Successful transition. Medical stabilization complete; ongoing eating disorder treatment at specialized facility. Continued multidisciplinary care. Family engagement. Long-term recovery focus. Outpatient structure scheduled.

opts.text: Persistent amenorrhea + osteoporosis (DEXA T-score -2.4) at 6-month follow-up despite weight restoration to BMI 18; continued OB-GYN + endocrinology follow-up; consider OCPs after weight stabilized
opts.rat: Expected residual findings. Hypothalamic amenorrhea + osteoporosis can persist after weight restoration. OB-GYN for menstruation + fertility; endocrinology for osteoporosis. OCPs controversial (don't restore natural cycle but may help bone density); calcium + vit D + weight-bearing exercise; bisphosphonate generally avoided in young women.

opts.text: Family verbalize relief at multidisciplinary approach; mother shares her own bulimia history + recovery; family agrees to FBT principles + outpatient support; therapy includes family sessions; PCP coordinated
opts.rat: Family engagement essential. Mother's recovery experience can be both helpful (understanding) and challenging (family dynamics). Family therapy addresses both. FBT principles continue outpatient. Multigenerational eating disorder patterns sometimes present — address directly.

title: Necrotizing Fasciitis (Type II, Group A Streptococcus) of the Right Lower Extremity with Streptococcal Toxic Shock Syndrome
subtitle: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION + Rapid Spread + Systemic Toxicity = NECROTIZING FASCIITIS Until Proven Otherwise; SOURCE CONTROL (Emergent Surgical Debridement) Within 6 hr Beats Antibiotics; Broad-Spectrum Abx + CLINDAMYCIN (Toxin Suppression); Sepsis Bundle + Vasopressors; LRINEC Score Aids Suspicion but Does NOT Rule Out; Mortality 20–40% — Time-Critical

patient.name: R. Okafor
patient.code: Full Code (family at bedside; surgical consent obtained for emergent fasciotomy/debridement)
patient.admitDate: Today, 0210 via ED (transferred from urgent care after 18 hr of escalating R calf pain initially diagnosed as 'cellulitis'); now 0700 in ED resuscitation bay
patient.room: ED Resuscitation Bay 3 → pending OR for emergent debridement; ICU bed reserved postoperatively

vitals.time: Urgent care yesterday afternoon (~18 hr ago, R calf pain x 1 day after minor abrasion gardening 3 days ago)
ED arrival 0210 (pain markedly worse; given oral abx + sent home from urgent care; returned to ED)
0700 CURRENT (4.5 hr after ED arrival; post 30 mL/kg crystalloid; pre-OR; new hemorrhagic bullae + crepitus)

labs.n: WOUND EXAM + clinical findings (RLE — calf/posterior leg)
labs.v: R calf: 0200 ED arrival had 12×8 cm erythema, edema, marked tenderness, severe pain (10/10) DISPROPORTIONATE to visible findings (skin only mildly erythematous), warm, ill-defined borders advancing 1 cm/hr (marker outlines rapidly outgrown); 0700 NOW: 22×15 cm erythema with central DUSKY/PURPLE discoloration, NEW HEMORRHAGIC BULLAE (3 large blood-filled blisters), patchy SKIN NECROSIS centrally, CREPITUS palpable on exam, foul/'dishwater' serous-purulent drainage from spontaneous skin breakdown; sensation DECREASED in central area (nerve necrosis); pulses dopplerable distally but diminishing; compartments tense; advancing past knee toward thigh
labs.r: CLASSIC NECROTIZING FASCIITIS (NF) findings — though 'classic' presentation is often LATE. EARLY (first 24 hr) often subtle: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION TO EXAM is the cardinal early clue (~80% of cases). LATE (within 24–72 hr): hemorrhagic bullae, skin necrosis, crepitus (gas from anaerobic infection — though Type II GAS may have minimal gas), 'dishwater' drainage, anesthesia over the area (subcutaneous nerve destruction), rapid extension (1 cm/hr possible), systemic toxicity (sepsis/STSS). LATE findings mean LATE diagnosis = HIGHER MORTALITY. SUSPECT EARLY — initiate surgical consultation even if exam looks 'just cellulitis'

labs.v: Pain 10/10 since arrival; worsened despite IV morphine 8 mg total in ED; pain initially described as 'deep, burning, throbbing — like the leg is being torn apart from inside'; required IV hydromorphone 1 mg + ketamine 30 mg bolus for analgesia (multimodal); now patient reports decreased sensation centrally over necrotic area but severe pain at advancing margins; analgesia coordinated with surgery for OR timing
labs.r: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION TO EXAM is the EARLIEST + most reliable clinical clue (sensitivity ~80%, before visible findings). Severe pain unresponsive to typical analgesics in a patient with seemingly mild cellulitis = SUSPECT NECROTIZING FASCIITIS. ANESTHESIA over the affected area (from nerve necrosis) is a late sign — paradoxically pain may DECREASE as nerves die while infection spreads. Multimodal analgesia (opioid + ketamine + scheduled acetaminophen) appropriate; coordinate with surgical team for OR

labs.n: LRINEC SCORE (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis)
labs.v: LRINEC score components for this patient: CRP 312 mg/L (4 points; >150 = max), WBC 24.8 (2 points; 15–25), Hgb 10.2 g/dL (1 point; 11–13.5 → actually 1 point bracket, this patient hgb <11 = 2 points), Sodium 128 mEq/L (2 points; <135), Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (2 points; >1.6), Glucose 218 mg/dL (1 point; >180) = TOTAL 13 (>8 = HIGH RISK >75% probability NF); LRINEC ≥6 raises suspicion; ≥8 highly suspicious; does NOT rule out — clinical judgment + surgical consultation drive decision
labs.r: LRINEC SCORE is a LABORATORY ADJUNCT for risk stratification — does NOT replace clinical judgment. Score ≥6 raises suspicion (positive predictive value ~92% at ≥8); ≥8 highly suspicious for NF. CRITICAL CAVEAT: LRINEC has LIMITED SENSITIVITY (~60–70%) — a LOW score does NOT rule out NF; if clinical suspicion is high, proceed with surgical consultation + possible operative exploration regardless of LRINEC. The score is most useful when clinical picture is ambiguous. Imaging (CT, MRI) may show fascial thickening/gas/edema but should NOT delay surgical exploration in high-suspicion cases. The diagnostic gold standard is OPERATIVE EXPLORATION + finding necrotic fascia + 'finger test' (easy dissection along fascial planes with finger) + frozen section

labs.v: Meets SEPSIS criteria (qSOFA ≥2: hypotension SBP <100, altered mentation, RR >22); progressed to SEPTIC SHOCK with vasopressor requirement; STSS CRITERIA met (per CDC): (1) ISOLATION GROUP A STREP from normally sterile site (blood culture pending — Gram + cocci in chains on initial Gram stain of wound aspirate), PLUS clinical signs: (2) HYPOTENSION SBP <90 (✓), (3) two or more: renal impairment (Cr 2.4 ✓), coagulopathy (Plt 88, INR 1.6 ✓), liver involvement (AST 142, ALT 168 ✓), ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 ratio 198 = mild ARDS), generalized erythematous macular rash (faint over chest), soft-tissue necrosis (NF ✓); ARTERIAL LINE placed; central line placed for vasopressor + monitoring
labs.r: STREPTOCOCCAL TOXIC SHOCK SYNDROME (STSS) accompanies severe invasive Group A Strep infection in ~50% of NF cases. SUPERANTIGEN TOXINS (TSST-like exotoxins) cause massive cytokine release → vasodilatation, capillary leak, multi-organ failure. MORTALITY of STSS 30–50%. Sepsis bundle: lactate (initial + repeat), blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics, broad-spectrum abx within 1 hr, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension/lactate ≥4, vasopressors for MAP <65 after fluids. CLINDAMYCIN suppresses streptococcal toxin production (ribosomal mechanism — bacteriostatic at protein synthesis stage); always combine with bactericidal agent (penicillin/beta-lactam) for GAS

labs.n: ANTIBIOTICS — empiric coverage for necrotizing soft-tissue infection
labs.v: Broad-spectrum empiric coverage initiated within 30 min of ED arrival: VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading (covers MRSA) + PIPERACILLIN-TAZOBACTAM 4.5 g IV q6h (broad gram-pos/neg/anaerobes) + CLINDAMYCIN 900 mg IV q8h (TOXIN SUPPRESSION for GAS/Clostridium); alternative: MEROPENEM 1 g IV q8h + vanco + clinda; once GAS confirmed, can NARROW to penicillin G + clindamycin (penicillin is bactericidal for GAS; clinda for toxin suppression); IVIG 1 g/kg IV considered (single dose; some evidence for STSS — controversial); blood cultures x2 sent BEFORE antibiotics; wound culture/Gram stain from OR specimen
labs.r: EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTIC STRATEGY for NF (initial — before culture results): cover gram-positive (including MRSA), gram-negative, AND anaerobes. STANDARD: vancomycin (MRSA) + piperacillin-tazobactam (broad gram +/-/anaerobic) OR meropenem (broad) + clindamycin (TOXIN SUPPRESSION — critical for GAS/Clostridium NF). CLINDAMYCIN is critical because GAS produces exotoxins that drive STSS + tissue destruction; clinda inhibits bacterial protein synthesis → halts toxin production (even when bacteria still alive). Once GAS confirmed, can narrow to penicillin + clinda. IVIG (1 g/kg single dose) considered for STSS — neutralizes circulating superantigens; controversial evidence but commonly used in severe STSS. ANTIBIOTICS ALONE DO NOT CURE NF — source control by surgical debridement is the cornerstone

labs.v: Surgery (general/trauma + plastics + orthopedic) consulted from triage at 0215; OR booked at 0245 (delayed 4 hr due to OR availability + need for resuscitation + consent + family arrival); patient en route to OR NOW (0700); plan: emergent FASCIOTOMY + DEBRIDEMENT of all necrotic tissue (skin, subcutaneous, fascia, possibly muscle) RLE; intraoperative cultures + frozen section; anticipate REPEAT DEBRIDEMENTS q24–48h until no further necrosis (typically 2–4 trips back); potential AMPUTATION if disease extensive + uncontrollable (BKA or AKA); NPV (negative pressure wound therapy/VAC) postoperatively; eventual SKIN GRAFT for closure; ICU postoperatively
labs.r: SOURCE CONTROL via emergent surgical debridement is THE LIFE-SAVING intervention in NF — antibiotics alone do NOT cure NF. TIME TO OR IS THE MOST IMPORTANT MODIFIABLE PROGNOSTIC FACTOR: mortality climbs significantly after 12–24 hr delay (some data show every hour delay = 9% higher mortality). Goal: surgery within 6 hr of presentation. SURGICAL approach: extensive debridement of all necrotic tissue beyond visibly viable margins; the 'finger test' (gloved finger dissects fascia easily — non-bleeding, gray, foul) confirms NF intraoperatively; intraop frozen section for confirmation. REPEAT DEBRIDEMENTS typically every 24–48h until clean. AMPUTATION sometimes life-saving if disease extensive. POST-OP: ICU, vasopressor support, continued abx, wound care (VAC), eventual reconstruction (skin graft, flap). NEVER DELAY for imaging if clinical suspicion high

labs.v: Sepsis bundle (Hour-1): LACTATE 6.4 mmol/L initial (>4 = severe; recheck 4.2 post-resuscitation — improving but elevated); BLOOD CULTURES x2 sent BEFORE abx (within 5 min of ED arrival); broad-spectrum ABX within 30 min; 30 mL/kg crystalloid (LR) bolus 2.1 L given over 1 hr; if hypotension persists after fluids OR lactate ≥4, VASOPRESSORS — NOREPINEPHRINE started 0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrated to MAP ≥65 (currently 0.15 mcg/kg/min); arterial line placed for accurate BP; central line placed for vasopressor + central access; consider adding VASOPRESSIN 0.03 units/min if escalating norepi dose; HYDROCORTISONE 200 mg/day if vasopressor-refractory; STRESS-DOSE STEROIDS not routine but considered in refractory shock
labs.r: SEPSIS BUNDLE (Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 bundle): (1) LACTATE measurement (initial + repeat in 2–4 hr if elevated), (2) BLOOD CULTURES before antibiotics, (3) BROAD-SPECTRUM ANTIBIOTICS within 1 hr, (4) 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L, (5) VASOPRESSORS if hypotension persists after fluid resuscitation to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg. NOREPINEPHRINE is FIRST-LINE vasopressor for septic shock (alpha-1 vasoconstriction with mild beta-1 inotropy). VASOPRESSIN as second agent (norepi-sparing). DOBUTAMINE if cardiac dysfunction. HYDROCORTISONE 200 mg/day for vasopressor-refractory septic shock (no longer requires ACTH stim test). Goal MAP ≥65 mmHg, urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate clearance. Time-sensitive throughout — every hour of delay in abx + source control = higher mortality

labs.v: CBC: WBC 24.8 (HIGH — left shift, bands 18%), Hgb 10.2 (low — hemolysis pattern possible), Plt 88 (LOW — early DIC); BMP: Na 128 (LOW — hyponatremia of severe infection), K 4.8, Cl 100, HCO3 14 (LOW — metabolic acidosis), BUN 38 (high), Cr 2.4 (HIGH — AKI from sepsis); LFTs: AST 142, ALT 168 (HIGH — sepsis hepatitis), Alk Phos 198, T Bili 2.4 (HIGH); LACTATE 6.4 → 4.2 (improving but elevated); COAG: INR 1.6, PTT 48, Fibrinogen 142 (low), D-dimer 4800 (high) — early DIC pattern; CRP 312 (very high); Procalcitonin 28 ng/mL (very high — bacterial sepsis); GLUCOSE 218 (stress hyperglycemia)
labs.r: Multi-organ involvement in sepsis/STSS: leukocytosis with bandemia (left shift = severe infection), thrombocytopenia + coagulopathy (DIC), AKI (sepsis-induced; pre-renal from hypotension + intrinsic from inflammatory injury), metabolic acidosis (lactate + AKI), hepatitis (sepsis 'shock liver'), hyperglycemia (stress response). These lab abnormalities ALSO score points in LRINEC (Na <135, Cr >1.6, glucose >180, Hgb <11, WBC 15–25 or >25, CRP >150). Monitor trends: improving = recovery; worsening = uncontrolled infection or new complication (e.g., persistent shock, new organ failure)

labs.v: ABG: pH 7.24, PaCO2 28, PaO2 78 on 6 L NC, HCO3 14, BE -12 (mixed metabolic acidosis + partial respiratory compensation; A-a gradient elevated); PaO2/FiO2 ratio ~198 (mild ARDS criteria); RR 30 (compensating for acidosis); on 6 L NC — escalating to high-flow nasal cannula 40 L/min FiO2 0.5 if PaO2 not improving; intubation criteria reviewed (worsening hypoxia, fatigue, persistent shock) — likely intubated in OR for procedure regardless
labs.r: RESPIRATORY assessment in sepsis: tachypnea is compensation for metabolic acidosis + sepsis-driven respiratory drive; mild ARDS may develop (Berlin criteria: acute onset, bilateral infiltrates, PaO2/FiO2 ratio <300, not solely cardiac). Lung-protective ventilation (LPV) if intubated: tidal volume 6 mL/kg PBW, plateau pressure <30, permissive hypercapnia. Patient likely intubated in OR for extensive surgical procedure + post-op ICU. Monitor for ARDS escalation (prone positioning for severe ARDS PaO2/FiO2 <150)

labs.v: Wound aspirate Gram stain (0230 in ED): GRAM-POSITIVE COCCI IN CHAINS (consistent with Streptococcus — preliminary suggests Group A Strep / Streptococcus pyogenes); culture pending (24–48 hr for confirmation + sensitivities); blood cultures x2 from peripheral + central drawn before abx — pending; intraoperative tissue cultures + frozen section will be sent from OR; tissue Gram stain will be done intraop; PCR for GAS (rapid) available at this facility — sent
labs.r: MICROBIOLOGY of NF determines pathogen-directed therapy: TYPE I (polymicrobial — mixed aerobic/anaerobic; older diabetic/immunocompromised; trunk/perineum/diabetic foot — often includes Bacteroides, E. coli, anaerobes, sometimes Clostridium), TYPE II (monomicrobial GAS, occasionally MRSA; younger healthier hosts; extremities; this case), TYPE III (Vibrio vulnificus — saltwater exposure, oysters in liver-disease patients), TYPE IV (fungal — rare, immunocompromised, traumatic inoculation). GAS confirmed → can NARROW to penicillin G + clindamycin (penicillin bactericidal for GAS; clindamycin for toxin suppression). MRSA empiric coverage needed until ruled out (vancomycin or linezolid)

labs.v: PMH: Type 2 DM (HbA1c 8.4 — poorly controlled); HTN; obesity (BMI 33); minor R calf abrasion 3 days ago while gardening (possible entry portal — soil/skin flora); no IVDU; no recent surgery; no immunocompromise (on metformin + lisinopril + atorvastatin only); social: lives with wife + 2 adult children; non-smoker; occasional alcohol; works as accountant; recent stress (job change 2 months ago); no recent travel; no varicella/zoster
labs.r: RISK FACTORS for NF: DIABETES (immunosuppression; this patient ✓), peripheral vascular disease, immunosuppression (steroids, chemo, transplant, HIV), obesity, chronic kidney disease, IV drug use (skin breach + immunosuppression), recent surgery or trauma (entry portal), varicella infection (Group A Strep secondary infection — esp. children + adults with varicella), NSAID use (controversial — may mask early signs + impair immune response). Many NF cases occur in healthy hosts after minor trauma (this case: minor abrasion). PORTALS of entry: minor breach in skin (cuts, abrasions, insect bites, surgical incisions, IV sites), or sometimes no identifiable portal (hematogenous spread from pharyngitis in case of GAS)

labs.v: STANDARD PRECAUTIONS + CONTACT PRECAUTIONS for large draining wound; GOWN + GLOVES on entry; PRIVATE ROOM (ICU postop); dedicated equipment (BP cuff, stethoscope, thermometer); HAND HYGIENE strict (soap + water if visible soiling — alcohol-based hand rub effective for most flora but contact precautions favor washing); RESPIRATORY ETIQUETTE (not droplet — GAS skin infection not respiratory); CDC: NO ROUTINE prophylaxis for healthcare workers unless prolonged unprotected close contact with respiratory secretions of invasive GAS patient; chemoprophylaxis (penicillin or clindamycin) considered for CLOSE HOUSEHOLD CONTACTS of invasive GAS patient (definition: 24+ hours in same household in 7 days before illness or direct contact with oral/respiratory secretions) — family to be evaluated by health department / infection prevention
labs.r: INFECTION CONTROL for invasive Group A Strep: CONTACT precautions for the large draining wound (gown + gloves; private room; dedicated equipment). NO respiratory droplet precautions for cutaneous GAS infection (unlike pharyngitis where droplet is added). HEALTHCARE WORKER CHEMOPROPHYLAXIS not routinely needed unless prolonged unprotected close contact (e.g., mouth-to-mouth resuscitation, intubation without PPE). HOUSEHOLD CONTACTS: chemoprophylaxis (penicillin V 500 mg PO QID x 10 days OR clindamycin 600 mg/day) may be considered if severe invasive GAS to prevent secondary cases; involve infection prevention + health department

labs.v: No bowel/bladder incontinence (no perineal involvement — rules out Fournier gangrene); no oral ulcers or pharyngitis (no obvious GAS portal in throat); no rash classic for varicella; no IV drug use track marks; no signs of meningitis (no nuchal rigidity); no cardiac murmur on exam (endocarditis unlikely but blood cultures pending will help rule out); no obvious abscess on imaging suggesting drainable collection; no recent NSAID use beyond occasional ibuprofen (controversial risk factor); no recent surgery or hospitalization (community-acquired)
labs.r: Rules out alternative or concurrent diagnoses. Fournier gangrene (NF of perineum/scrotum) requires different surgical approach. Pharyngeal source ruled out by exam. Endocarditis screening with blood cultures + echo if indicated. NSAIDs may mask early NF signs + impair immune response — historically controversial association; clinically, ensure adequate pain control without relying on diagnosis-masking effects of NSAIDs alone

nursesNote: 0700 — 54 y/o male with PMH T2DM (HbA1c 8.4 — poorly controlled), HTN, obesity (BMI 33) presenting with NECROTIZING FASCIITIS R lower extremity + STREPTOCOCCAL TOXIC SHOCK SYNDROME (STSS). HISTORY: minor R calf abrasion 3 days ago while gardening (entry portal); 18 hr of escalating pain initially diagnosed as 'cellulitis' at urgent care + sent home on oral cephalexin; presented to ED at 0210 with rapidly progressing pain, fever 39.6, hypotension 98/62, tachycardia 128, RR 26. ED EXAM at 0210: R calf 12×8 cm erythema with PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION TO EXAM (10/10 unresponsive to morphine 8 mg; described as 'tearing from inside') — cardinal early sign of NF; advancing 1 cm/hr (marker outlines outgrown rapidly); surgery + infectious disease consulted from triage at 0215. By 0700 CURRENT: 22×15 cm with central DUSKY/PURPLE discoloration, NEW HEMORRHAGIC BULLAE (3 large blood-filled blisters), patchy skin necrosis centrally, CREPITUS palpable, foul 'dishwater' serous-purulent drainage, decreased sensation centrally (nerve necrosis), advancing toward thigh — CLASSIC LATE NF FINDINGS. LRINEC SCORE 13 (HIGH — >75% probability NF; ≥8 highly suspicious; this score does not replace clinical judgment but reinforces operative urgency): CRP 312 (4 pts), WBC 24.8 (2), Hgb 10.2 (2), Na 128 (2), Cr 2.4 (2), Glucose 218 (1). PROGRESSED to SEPTIC SHOCK with STSS: vasopressor-requiring hypotension (NOREPINEPHRINE 0.15 mcg/kg/min for MAP ≥65; central line + arterial line placed); multi-organ dysfunction (AKI Cr 2.4, hepatitis AST 142/ALT 168, coagulopathy INR 1.6 + Plt 88 + Fibrinogen 142 + D-dimer 4800 = early DIC, mild ARDS PaO2/FiO2 198, metabolic acidosis pH 7.24 lactate 6.4 → 4.2 post-resuscitation). STSS CRITERIA MET: GAS isolation (Gram stain shows Gram-positive cocci in chains — culture pending; PCR sent) + hypotension + ≥2 organ involvement. SEPSIS BUNDLE (Hour-1) COMPLETED: lactate (6.4 initial, 4.2 repeat — improving), blood cultures x2 BEFORE abx, broad-spectrum abx within 30 min, 30 mL/kg crystalloid (2.1 L LR), vasopressors for MAP <65 after fluids. EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTICS started 0235 (within 30 min): VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading (MRSA coverage) + PIPERACILLIN-TAZOBACTAM 4.5 g IV q6h (broad gram +/-/anaerobic) + CLINDAMYCIN 900 mg IV q8h (CRITICAL FOR TOXIN SUPPRESSION — inhibits GAS protein synthesis → halts exotoxin production driving STSS even when bacteria still alive); once GAS confirmed, can narrow to penicillin G + clindamycin; IVIG 1 g/kg IV considered for STSS (controversial but commonly used in severe STSS — neutralizes circulating superantigens; surgeon + ID discussing). SOURCE CONTROL: emergent OR for FASCIOTOMY + DEBRIDEMENT booked at 0245; en route to OR NOW (0700 — total time from ED arrival to OR = 4.75 hr; goal <6 hr; mortality climbs sharply with delay — some data show each hour of delay = 9% higher mortality); plan: extensive debridement of all necrotic tissue (skin + subcutaneous + fascia + possibly muscle); intraop 'finger test' (easy fascial dissection = NF confirmed) + frozen section + cultures; anticipate REPEAT DEBRIDEMENTS q24–48h until no further necrosis (typically 2–4 trips back to OR); potential AMPUTATION (BKA or AKA) if extensive + uncontrollable; VAC dressing postop; eventual skin graft for closure; ICU bed reserved postop. PAIN management multimodal: IV hydromorphone 1 mg + ketamine 30 mg bolus + scheduled acetaminophen + planned OR analgesia/anesthesia handoff; ANESTHESIOLOGY pre-op consult done — will intubate in OR (anticipated post-op ICU intubation given extensive procedure + shock state). INFECTION CONTROL: CONTACT precautions for large draining wound (gown + gloves on entry; private ICU room postop; dedicated equipment); no respiratory droplet precautions needed (cutaneous GAS, not pharyngitis); HEALTHCARE WORKER chemoprophylaxis NOT routinely needed unless prolonged unprotected close contact with respiratory secretions; HOUSEHOLD CONTACTS (wife + 2 adult children) referred to infection prevention + health department for chemoprophylaxis consideration (penicillin V or clindamycin); strict hand hygiene; visitor PPE education. FAMILY at bedside (wife + 2 adult children); surgical consent obtained; explained life-threatening nature, potential for amputation, ICU course, repeat debridements, prolonged recovery; chaplain offered; case manager + social work activated for prolonged hospital + rehab planning. POST-OP ICU plan: continued vasopressor support + sepsis bundle, hemodynamic monitoring (arterial + central lines), renal replacement therapy consideration if AKI progresses to anuria/severe acidosis/hyperkalemia, ventilator with lung-protective ventilation, repeat lactate q4h, repeat CBC + BMP + LFTs + coags q6–12h, repeat ECHO if cardiac dysfunction suspected, monitor for additional organ failure, repeat surgery q24–48h, glycemic control (insulin gtt — DKA prevention in T2DM with severe infection + stress hyperglycemia 218), DVT prophylaxis (mechanical until coagulopathy resolves, then heparin), stress ulcer prophylaxis (pantoprazole), nutritional support (enteral preferred when hemodynamically stable + bowel function intact), early mobility once stable, skin care + wound care + VAC management, family communication + multidisciplinary rounding. PROGNOSIS: NF mortality 20–40%; with STSS 30–50%; major modifiable factor is time to surgical source control + adequate antibiotic + organ support. Anticipated long ICU + hospital + rehab course (weeks to months); functional recovery depends on extent of debridement, possible amputation, post-op reconstruction, rehab engagement.
providerOrders: ADMIT to ICU postoperatively (after OR); ED resuscitation bay until transport to OR; full sepsis + STSS management

EMERGENT SURGICAL SOURCE CONTROL — booked OR at 0245; target time-to-OR <6 hr from presentation: extensive fasciotomy + DEBRIDEMENT of all necrotic tissue (skin, subcutaneous, fascia, +/- muscle) R lower extremity; intraop 'finger test' (easy fascial dissection confirms NF) + frozen section + tissue cultures + Gram stain; anticipate REPEAT DEBRIDEMENTS q24–48h until no further necrosis (typically 2–4 returns to OR); potential AMPUTATION (BKA or AKA) if extensive + uncontrollable infection
SEPSIS BUNDLE (Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1):
Lactate measurement (initial + repeat q4h while elevated)
Blood cultures x2 (peripheral + central if available) BEFORE antibiotics
Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hr (started within 30 min at 0235)
30 mL/kg crystalloid (lactated Ringer's preferred) for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L (2.1 L bolus given)
Vasopressors for persistent hypotension after fluid resuscitation: NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP ≥65 mmHg (currently 0.15 mcg/kg/min)
Add VASOPRESSIN 0.03 units/min if escalating norepi dose (norepi-sparing second agent)
Add HYDROCORTISONE 200 mg/day IV continuous infusion if vasopressor-refractory shock
EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTIC REGIMEN — broad coverage for necrotizing soft-tissue infection:
VANCOMYCIN 25 mg/kg IV loading (target trough 15–20; covers MRSA)
PIPERACILLIN-TAZOBACTAM 4.5 g IV q6h (broad gram-positive/negative/anaerobic)
Alternative: MEROPENEM 1 g IV q8h (if penicillin allergy or extended coverage needed)
CLINDAMYCIN 900 mg IV q8h (CRITICAL — TOXIN SUPPRESSION for GAS/Clostridium; inhibits bacterial protein synthesis → halts exotoxin production driving STSS even when bacteria still alive)
Once GAS confirmed (Gram + cocci in chains preliminary; PCR + culture pending): narrow to PENICILLIN G 4 million units IV q4h + clindamycin
IVIG 1 g/kg IV x 1 dose: consider for STSS (controversial but commonly used in severe STSS — neutralizes circulating superantigens); discuss with ID
INVASIVE MONITORING + ACCESS:
ARTERIAL LINE (radial or femoral) for accurate continuous BP monitoring on vasopressor
CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETER (subclavian or internal jugular) for vasopressor + central access + CVP if needed; large-bore peripheral IVs x2 also
FOLEY catheter for strict urine output monitoring (goal ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr)
Continuous telemetry + pulse oximetry + capnography if intubated

RESPIRATORY support: 6 L NC currently (SpO2 92%); escalate to HIGH-FLOW NASAL CANNULA 40 L/min FiO2 0.5 if not improving; INTUBATION criteria reviewed (worsening hypoxia, fatigue, persistent shock, mental status decline) — likely intubated in OR for extensive procedure regardless; if mechanically ventilated post-op, LUNG-PROTECTIVE VENTILATION (tidal volume 6 mL/kg PBW, plateau pressure <30, permissive hypercapnia)
INFECTION CONTROL:
STANDARD PRECAUTIONS + CONTACT PRECAUTIONS (large draining wound): gown + gloves on entry; private ICU room postop; dedicated equipment (BP cuff, stethoscope, thermometer)
NO routine droplet/respiratory precautions for cutaneous GAS (not pharyngitis)
HEALTHCARE WORKER chemoprophylaxis not routinely needed unless prolonged unprotected close contact with respiratory secretions
HOUSEHOLD CONTACTS (wife + 2 adult children) referred to infection prevention + health department for chemoprophylaxis consideration (penicillin V 500 mg PO QID x 10 days OR clindamycin 600 mg/day)
Hand hygiene: soap + water if visible soiling; alcohol-based hand rub otherwise; visitor PPE education

LABORATORY monitoring (post-op ICU): CBC + BMP + LFTs + coags + lactate q4–6h initially x first 24 hr then q12h; troponin if cardiac dysfunction suspected; ABG q6h; blood cultures repeat if persistent fevers despite source control + abx; procalcitonin trend to guide antibiotic duration; CK if rhabdomyolysis suspected

PAIN management: multimodal — IV hydromorphone 0.5–1 mg q1–2h PRN + ketamine infusion 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/hr (analgesic + opioid-sparing) + scheduled acetaminophen 1 g IV q6h; coordinate with anesthesia for OR analgesia handoff; AVOID NSAIDs (renal + bleeding + controversial NF association)

RENAL: monitor for AKI progression; CRRT (continuous renal replacement therapy) consideration if oliguria/anuria, severe acidosis, hyperkalemia refractory to medical management, or volume overload refractory to diuretics

GLYCEMIC CONTROL: T2DM + stress hyperglycemia (glucose 218); INSULIN INFUSION (target 140–180 mg/dL per critical care guidelines); finger-stick BG q1h on insulin gtt; transition to subcutaneous when stable + eating; HbA1c 8.4 baseline noted for outpatient DM management

DVT PROPHYLAXIS: mechanical (sequential compression devices) until coagulopathy resolves; transition to pharmacologic (heparin 5000 units SQ q8h or enoxaparin 40 mg SQ daily) once safe; HIT surveillance with platelet trend
STRESS ULCER PROPHYLAXIS: pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily

NUTRITIONAL support: NPO until hemodynamically stable + bowel function intact; then EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION via NGT/OGT within 24–48 hr (preferred over parenteral); high-protein (1.5–2 g/kg/day) for catabolic/wound-healing needs; dietitian consultation

WOUND CARE: post-debridement VAC (negative-pressure wound therapy) dressing changes per surgical plan (typically q48–72h); monitor wound + advancing margins q4h; document with measurements + photos; advance/de-escalate per surgeon

FAMILY support + communication: chaplain offered; case manager + social work activated for prolonged hospital + rehab planning + insurance; daily multidisciplinary rounding with family invited; explain life-threatening prognosis, potential for amputation, prolonged ICU + hospital + rehab course (weeks to months); shared decision-making for care goals + advance directives

DISCHARGE planning (long-term — initiate early): anticipate prolonged ICU course (1–4 weeks), step-down then floor, eventual rehab (LTACH or skilled nursing or acute rehab depending on functional status + extent of debridement/amputation); long-term wound care + reconstruction (plastic surgery); PT/OT throughout; psychological support (trauma + adjustment + body image); DM management; primary care + ID follow-up

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION to exam (10/10 unresponsive to morphine 8 mg; described as 'tearing from inside') with only mild visible erythema on initial presentation — cardinal early sign of NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — earliest + most reliable clinical clue for NF (~80% sensitivity, before visible findings). Severe pain unresponsive to typical analgesics in a patient with seemingly mild cellulitis = SUSPECT NECROTIZING FASCIITIS. Surgical consultation + operative exploration if suspicion high — do NOT wait for late findings (which signal HIGHER mortality).

opts.text: LATE FINDINGS now present: hemorrhagic bullae, central skin necrosis, crepitus, foul 'dishwater' drainage, decreased sensation centrally (nerve necrosis), advancing 1 cm/hr with rapid extension
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — classic LATE NF findings. Late findings signal late diagnosis = higher mortality. EMERGENT source control (surgical debridement) is the LIFE-SAVING intervention. Time to OR is the most important modifiable prognostic factor.

opts.text: LRINEC SCORE 13 (HIGH — >75% probability NF; ≥8 highly suspicious): CRP 312 (4 pts), WBC 24.8 (2), Hgb 10.2 (2), Na 128 (2), Cr 2.4 (2), Glucose 218 (1)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — laboratory risk stratification. Does NOT replace clinical judgment. ≥8 highly suspicious for NF. Limited sensitivity (~60–70%) — LOW score does NOT rule out NF. Most useful when clinical picture ambiguous. Imaging (CT/MRI) should NOT delay surgery if clinical suspicion high.

opts.text: SEPTIC SHOCK with vasopressor requirement (NOREPINEPHRINE 0.15 mcg/kg/min for MAP <65) + STREPTOCOCCAL TOXIC SHOCK SYNDROME (STSS) criteria met: GAS isolation + hypotension + ≥2 organ involvement (AKI Cr 2.4, hepatitis AST 142, coagulopathy INR 1.6 + Plt 88, mild ARDS PaO2/FiO2 198)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — septic shock + STSS recognition. STSS accompanies severe invasive GAS in ~50% of NF cases. Mortality 30–50%. Sepsis bundle + vasopressors + source control time-critical. ARTERIAL line + central line placed for monitoring + vasopressor delivery.

opts.text: TIME-CRITICAL surgical source control: emergent fasciotomy + debridement booked at 0245; target time-to-OR <6 hr; mortality climbs sharply with delay (~9% higher per hour); anticipate REPEAT debridements q24–48h until no further necrosis; potential amputation if extensive
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — surgical source control is THE life-saving intervention. Antibiotics alone do NOT cure NF. Time to OR is the most important modifiable prognostic factor. Repeat debridements typically needed (2–4 trips back). Coordinate OR + anesthesia + family + consent + ICU bed.

opts.text: ANTIBIOTIC strategy: VANCOMYCIN (MRSA) + PIPERACILLIN-TAZOBACTAM (broad gram +/-/anaerobic) + CLINDAMYCIN (CRITICAL — TOXIN SUPPRESSION for GAS); blood cultures BEFORE abx; once GAS confirmed, narrow to penicillin + clinda; IVIG considered for STSS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — empiric antibiotic regimen for necrotizing soft-tissue infection. CLINDAMYCIN is critical for GAS — inhibits protein synthesis → halts toxin production even when bacteria still alive. Penicillin G is bactericidal once GAS confirmed. IVIG (controversial) for STSS — neutralizes superantigens.

opts.text: SEPSIS BUNDLE (Hour-1) completed: LACTATE (6.4 → 4.2 — improving), BLOOD CULTURES x2 BEFORE abx, ABX within 30 min, 30 mL/kg crystalloid (2.1 L LR), VASOPRESSORS for MAP <65 after fluids; goal MAP ≥65, UOP ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate clearance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sepsis bundle execution. Time-sensitive throughout. Norepinephrine first-line. Vasopressin second agent. Hydrocortisone for vasopressor-refractory shock. Monitor lactate clearance + UOP + MAP. Every hour of delay = higher mortality.

opts.text: INFECTION CONTROL: CONTACT PRECAUTIONS for large draining wound (gown + gloves; private ICU room postop; dedicated equipment); NO droplet precautions for cutaneous GAS; HOUSEHOLD CONTACTS (wife + 2 adult children) referred for chemoprophylaxis consideration; healthcare worker prophylaxis not routinely needed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — invasive GAS infection control. Contact precautions for wound. NO respiratory precautions for cutaneous GAS (vs. pharyngitis where droplet is added). Household contacts may need chemoprophylaxis (penicillin V or clindamycin) per health department. Healthcare workers usually do not unless prolonged close unprotected respiratory contact.

opts.text: Multi-organ involvement: AKI (Cr 2.4 — RRT consideration), hepatitis (AST/ALT >140), early DIC (Plt 88, INR 1.6, Fibrinogen 142, D-dimer 4800), mild ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 198), metabolic acidosis (pH 7.24, lactate 4.2), stress hyperglycemia (218)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sepsis-induced multi-organ dysfunction. Each organ system needs targeted monitoring + support. CRRT for AKI if progresses; lung-protective ventilation if intubated; blood products if active bleeding from DIC; insulin gtt for glycemic control. Comprehensive ICU care.

opts.text: DIABETIC RISK FACTOR: poorly controlled T2DM (HbA1c 8.4) — immunosuppression contributed to severity + speed of progression; minor calf abrasion gardening 3 days ago was entry portal; glycemic control (insulin gtt 140–180 mg/dL target) post-op
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — risk factor + post-op management. DM is major risk for NF (immunosuppression + microvascular disease). Minor skin breach + poor glycemic control = catastrophic infection. Insulin infusion to achieve target glycemia per critical care guidelines. Long-term DM management addressed at discharge.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed with selected regimen. Penicillin/clindamycin/vancomycin all options.

opts.text: Patient denies recent IV drug use
opts.rat: Reassuring — IVDU is a risk factor for NF (skin breach + immunosuppression); absence here does not change immediate management; document. Risk factor in this case is DM + minor abrasion.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / management principles.

opts.text: NECROTIZING FASCIITIS (NF) = rapidly progressive soft-tissue infection involving deep fascia + subcutaneous tissue (sometimes muscle); spread along fascial planes; bacterial toxins + ischemia destroy tissue; PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION to exam is the earliest reliable clue; LATE findings (hemorrhagic bullae, skin necrosis, crepitus, 'dishwater' drainage, anesthesia) signal late diagnosis + higher mortality
opts.rat: CORRECT — central pathophysiology + clinical presentation. NF spreads along fascial planes (faster than visible surface findings progress); subcutaneous nerves + vessels destroyed → anesthesia + dusky discoloration; bacterial gas production (esp. Clostridium, some other anaerobes) → crepitus; tissue death + serous drainage = 'dishwater' appearance. Mortality 20–40%; SOURCE CONTROL via emergent surgical debridement is THE life-saving intervention.

opts.text: NF CLASSIFICATION: TYPE I (polymicrobial — mixed aerobic/anaerobic; older diabetic/immunocompromised; trunk/perineum/diabetic foot — includes Bacteroides, E. coli, anaerobes, sometimes Clostridium → Fournier gangrene); TYPE II (monomicrobial Group A Strep / occasionally MRSA; younger healthier hosts; extremities; this case); TYPE III (Vibrio vulnificus — saltwater/oysters in liver-disease patients); TYPE IV (fungal — rare, immunocompromised, traumatic inoculation)
opts.rat: CORRECT — classification drives empiric antibiotic strategy + risk factor recognition. This patient = Type II GAS (Gram + cocci in chains on aspirate) with classic presentation in extremity. Once organism confirmed, can narrow antibiotic therapy. Type-specific clinical clues help anticipate complications + recurrence risk.

opts.text: STREPTOCOCCAL TOXIC SHOCK SYNDROME (STSS) accompanies severe invasive GAS in ~50% of NF cases; SUPERANTIGEN TOXINS (TSST-like exotoxins) cause massive cytokine release → vasodilation + capillary leak + multi-organ failure; mortality 30–50%; CDC criteria: GAS isolation from normally sterile site + hypotension + ≥2 organ involvement (renal, coag, liver, ARDS, rash, soft-tissue necrosis)
opts.rat: CORRECT — STSS recognition + mortality. Superantigens bypass normal antigen presentation, activating massive T-cell response → cytokine storm. Multi-organ failure is the rule. Time-sensitive sepsis bundle + source control + IVIG (controversial; commonly used) + organ support. Mortality remains high even with optimal management.

opts.text: TIME-TO-SOURCE-CONTROL is the most important modifiable prognostic factor in NF; goal <6 hr from presentation; some data suggest each hour of delay = ~9% increase in mortality; antibiotics + organ support alone DO NOT CURE NF; surgical debridement extends beyond visibly viable margins; 'finger test' (easy fascial dissection) intraop confirms diagnosis
opts.rat: CORRECT — time-critical surgical principle. Antibiotics cannot penetrate necrotic/avascular tissue. Debridement removes the source. Repeat debridements typically needed (q24–48h until no further necrosis). Amputation may be life-saving if extensive. Never delay for imaging if clinical suspicion high.

opts.text: ANTIBIOTIC STRATEGY for NF: broad-spectrum empiric coverage including gram-positive (with MRSA), gram-negative, AND anaerobes — VANCOMYCIN + PIPERACILLIN-TAZOBACTAM (or MEROPENEM) + CLINDAMYCIN; CLINDAMYCIN is CRITICAL for GAS — inhibits bacterial protein synthesis → halts EXOTOXIN production driving STSS even when bacteria still alive; once GAS confirmed, narrow to PENICILLIN G (bactericidal) + clindamycin (toxin suppression)
opts.rat: CORRECT — empiric + targeted strategy. Clindamycin's mechanism (protein synthesis inhibition) is uniquely valuable in toxin-mediated streptococcal disease (also Clostridium). Penicillin remains the bactericidal agent of choice for GAS but in heavy bacterial burden has reduced effect ('Eagle effect') — clindamycin overcomes this. IVIG (1 g/kg single dose) considered for STSS — neutralizes circulating superantigens.

opts.text: LRINEC SCORE (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis) components: CRP, WBC, Hgb, Sodium, Creatinine, Glucose; ≥6 raises suspicion, ≥8 highly suspicious (PPV ~92% at ≥8); LIMITED SENSITIVITY (~60–70%) — a LOW SCORE DOES NOT RULE OUT NF; clinical judgment + surgical consultation drive the decision regardless of LRINEC; imaging (CT, MRI) may show fascial thickening/gas/edema but SHOULD NOT DELAY SURGICAL EXPLORATION if suspicion high
opts.rat: CORRECT — proper interpretation. LRINEC is a risk stratification adjunct, not a rule-out test. Low score in a high-suspicion patient does not justify delay. Surgical exploration is the gold standard. Imaging should never delay an operation; useful when atypical or for surgical planning if doable quickly.

opts.text: SEPSIS BUNDLE (Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1): (1) LACTATE measurement (initial + repeat q4h if elevated), (2) BLOOD CULTURES before antibiotics, (3) BROAD-SPECTRUM ABX within 1 hr, (4) 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4, (5) VASOPRESSORS if hypotension persists after fluids to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg; NOREPINEPHRINE first-line; VASOPRESSIN second; HYDROCORTISONE for vasopressor-refractory
opts.rat: CORRECT — sepsis bundle execution. Time-sensitive throughout. Norepinephrine first-line for septic shock (alpha-1 vasoconstriction + mild beta-1 inotropy). Vasopressin as second agent (norepi-sparing). Dobutamine if cardiac dysfunction. Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day for vasopressor-refractory septic shock (no longer requires ACTH stim test). Goal MAP ≥65, UOP ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate clearance.

opts.text: INVASIVE GAS INFECTION CONTROL: CONTACT precautions for large draining wound (gown + gloves; private room; dedicated equipment); NO routine respiratory droplet precautions for CUTANEOUS GAS (unlike pharyngitis where droplet is added); HEALTHCARE WORKER chemoprophylaxis NOT routinely needed unless prolonged unprotected close contact with respiratory secretions; HOUSEHOLD CONTACTS: chemoprophylaxis (penicillin V or clindamycin) may be considered for severe invasive GAS to prevent secondary cases — involve infection prevention + health department
opts.rat: CORRECT — invasive GAS precautions. The wound exudate is the infectious vector for cutaneous GAS — contact precautions appropriate. Respiratory precautions only if pharyngitis or pulmonary involvement. Household contact prophylaxis per CDC/health department guidance for invasive GAS to prevent secondary cases.

opts.text: Wait for CT scan and MRI to confirm diagnosis before consulting surgery — imaging is more reliable than clinical exam
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Imaging SHOULD NEVER DELAY SURGICAL EXPLORATION if clinical suspicion is high. CT may show fascial thickening/gas/edema (helpful when atypical or for surgical planning if quickly available) but is NEITHER required nor sufficient. The gold standard is OPERATIVE EXPLORATION. Time-to-source-control is the most important modifiable prognostic factor — every hour of delay = higher mortality.

opts.text: Antibiotics alone (without surgical debridement) can cure necrotizing fasciitis if the regimen is broad-spectrum and started early enough
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Antibiotics cannot penetrate necrotic/avascular tissue — they cannot reach the bacteria embedded in necrotic fascia. SURGICAL DEBRIDEMENT (source control) is THE life-saving intervention. Antibiotics are essential adjuncts (limit spread, treat bacteremia, suppress toxins via clindamycin for GAS) but cannot cure NF without surgical source control.

opts.text: Aggressively replete BP to a MAP target of ≥85 mmHg with norepinephrine and large-volume crystalloid bolus (60–80 mL/kg)
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Goal MAP for septic shock is ≥65 mmHg (per Surviving Sepsis guidelines); higher MAP targets (≥85) have NOT shown survival benefit and increase risk of arrhythmia + ischemia from high-dose vasopressors. Crystalloid resuscitation 30 mL/kg initial; further fluids guided by hemodynamic assessment (passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation, point-of-care US). Over-resuscitation causes pulmonary edema + tissue edema + worse outcomes.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).
