labs.v: INFLUENZA — annually (any season); INACTIVATED (IIV) for most adults; ages 65+ get high-dose or adjuvanted (Fluad/Fluzone HD); HIGHER PRIORITY in DM (increased flu morbidity + mortality); PNEUMOCOCCAL — adults 19-64 with DM: PCV15 followed 1 yr later by PPSV23, OR PCV20 alone (per ACIP 2021-2022 updates); adults ≥65 + no prior pneumococcal: PCV20 alone OR PCV15 then PPSV23; HEPATITIS B — adults 19-59 with DM + unvaccinated: complete 2 or 3-dose series (Heplisav-B 2-dose or Engerix-B/Recombivax 3-dose); ages ≥60: shared decision; reason: DM patients exposed via finger-stick BG monitoring + occasional shared equipment + higher Hep B prevalence; COVID-19 — current per CDC schedule; updated annually + as variants evolve; DM = higher COVID severity risk; Tdap/Td — Tdap once + Td booster every 10 years (or Tdap if pregnant or wound); ZOSTER (shingles) — Shingrix 2-dose series at age ≥50 (regardless of prior shingles or zoster vaccine); RSV — age ≥75 universally; ages 60-74 if at increased risk (DM may qualify per shared decision); HPV — through age 26 routinely; 27-45 shared decision; MMR + varicella — typically childhood-completed; MENINGOCOCCAL — by indication; document vaccine history + offer + administer per ACIP
labs.r: Vaccination is part of comprehensive DM care + significantly reduces vaccine-preventable disease morbidity in this immunocompromised population. ACIP recommendations updated annually. Adults with DM at increased risk for influenza complications, pneumococcal disease, hepatitis B (finger-stick exposure), severe COVID-19. RSV recommendation evolving for DM age 60-74. Engaging primary care + pharmacy + community vaccination programs increases coverage

labs.v: CULTURAL: Latino background — patient + wife primary Spanish + comfortable English (visit in English with check-ins for understanding; Spanish materials provided as supplement); engage WIFE (primary cook + family meal planner); discuss traditional foods + healthier preparations (corn tortillas over flour; beans + brown rice over white rice; grilled over fried; water/unsweetened tea over sodas/horchata); honor cultural meal patterns (large midday meal common); avoid demonizing traditional foods (small portions of culturally important foods okay); community resources (Latino community health center, Spanish-language DSMES classes, peer support groups); HEALTH LITERACY: assessed by teach-back + Newest Vital Sign or similar; tailor materials to literacy level + use visuals + simple language; HEALTH INSURANCE: patient employed + insured (employer-based PPO); medication cost discussion — metformin generic + cheap; ACE/ARB + statin generally cheap; co-pay assistance programs for newer agents (SGLT2i, GLP-1RA) if escalation needed later; FOOD INSECURITY screen (Hunger Vital Sign) — negative; SOCIAL DETERMINANTS: housing stable, transportation reliable, employed with paid sick time + insurance, supportive family + community; PSYCHOSOCIAL: PHQ-9 screening = 4 (minimal depression symptoms); patient acknowledges anxiety + grief about diagnosis ('I always thought it wouldn't happen to me even though my parents have it'); FAMILY HISTORY discussion: both parents T2DM (mother on metformin + sitagliptin; father on metformin + insulin); maternal grandmother amputation from DM complications; this family history motivates patient to avoid same trajectory; encourage first-degree relative screening (children + siblings); HEALTH BEHAVIORS already started: increased water intake, reduced soda, walking 15 min/day x past week + lost 1 kg
labs.r: Cultural humility + language access + family engagement + addressing social determinants of health are essential for sustained DM self-management. Latino populations have higher T2DM prevalence + earlier onset + higher complication rates — disparities driven by social determinants (access, income, education, language, immigration status, food environment, neighborhood resources, structural racism) more than genetics. Tailor education to literacy + culture + language + family system + community. Peer support + community health workers (promotores/promotoras) effective in Latino DM care. Acknowledge patient's existing positive behavior change

labs.v: MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING approach: explore patient's own reasons for change ('What worries you most about diabetes?' 'What would your life look like in 5 years if you stay healthy?'), readiness ruler (0-10 importance + 0-10 confidence), elicit change talk + reinforce + summarize; AVOID lecturing, scaring, or telling what to do — patient-driven; ADD specific concerns/barriers (time, energy, cost, family resistance, cultural factors); SMART GOALS (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) for this visit: (1) 'Walk 20 min after dinner 4 nights/week starting Monday, log on phone app' (started already with 15 min — incremental build); (2) 'Replace soda with unsweetened iced tea or water for next 4 weeks; allow 1 soda/week as special occasion' (already reduced — formalize); (3) 'Take metformin with breakfast + dinner; set 2 phone alarms; check off on calendar' (titrate next week); (4) 'Schedule ophthalmology appointment within 1 month' (clinic provides referrals + appointment lines); (5) 'Family meeting Sunday to discuss diagnosis + meal planning with wife + 3 children'; FOLLOW-UP: phone call from diabetes educator in 1 week (check tolerance + adherence + questions), clinic visit in 2 weeks (titrate metformin + recheck BP), 3-month follow-up with repeat HbA1c + lipid + UMACR + comprehensive review; HABIT-FORMING tips: link new behavior to existing routine (after dinner → walk; before brushing teeth at night → check off med calendar), environment design (no soda in house; walking shoes by door), social support (walk with wife or coworker), self-monitoring + tracking (app or notebook), celebrate small wins; if struggling — problem-solve barriers + revise goal rather than judgment + abandonment
labs.r: Behavior change is the most challenging aspect of chronic disease management. Motivational interviewing (Miller + Rollnick) is evidence-based + respects autonomy + elicits internal motivation. SMART goals are specific + actionable. Habit formation: link to existing routine, environment design, social support, self-monitoring, celebrate wins, problem-solve barriers without judgment. Setbacks are normal + part of process — reframe as data + adjust. Long-term durability requires sustained engagement + adapting as life evolves

labs.n: FAMILY HISTORY + first-degree relative screening recommendation
labs.v: Family history: BOTH PARENTS T2DM (mother age 72 on metformin + sitagliptin + statin + ACE inhibitor; father age 75 on metformin + insulin + statin + amlodipine; both with CAD; mother early diabetic retinopathy on PRP); MATERNAL GRANDMOTHER T2DM with below-knee amputation age 65 + died from MI age 72; PATERNAL UNCLE DM with CKD on dialysis; first-cousin T2DM age 35; CHILDREN: son 22 (BMI 28 — overweight), son 18 (normal weight), daughter 15 (normal weight + active in sports); WIFE: BMI 29 — overweight; no DM history but high family risk + age 46; FIRST-DEGREE RELATIVE SCREENING recommendations: age ≥35 routine screening (HbA1c or FPG) every 3 years if normal, every 1 year if prediabetes; earlier if overweight/obese + risk factors (family history is a major risk factor); recommend wife + adult son screening soon; recommend lifestyle modification for entire family as PRIMARY PREVENTION; family meal planning + activity together reinforces patient + protects children
labs.r: Family history is one of the strongest T2DM risk factors. First-degree relatives (parents, siblings, children) have 2-6x increased risk. Family-based intervention (lifestyle modification for entire family) protects children from developing DM + supports patient's adherence. Discuss inheritance pattern (polygenic + environmental; not single gene); some families benefit from genetic counseling if early-onset/multiple types/atypical features (rare). Encourage screening of relatives + family-based behavior change. Insurance covers diabetes screening per USPSTF recommendation

labs.v: No CURRENT symptoms of severe hyperglycemia (resolved with metformin start + reduced sugar intake — polyuria + thirst + weight loss decreased); no DKA features (alert, no fruity breath, no Kussmaul respirations, no severe abdominal pain, no vomiting, bicarbonate normal); no SEVERE hypoglycemia history (not on hypoglycemia-causing agents); no foot ulcer or wound; no vision changes (last eye exam 3 years ago — overdue but no current concerns); no chest pain or exertional symptoms; no current depression (PHQ-9 = 4); SUCCESSFULLY QUIT smoking 6 months ago (was 1 PPD x 20 years) — major modifiable risk factor addressed; no alcohol abuse; no drug use; stable employment + insurance + family support; high health literacy + motivation to learn; engaged wife at visit
labs.r: Reassuring + protective factors. The clinical baseline is good — early diagnosis (no microvascular complications evident yet), stable hemodynamics, no acute emergencies, motivated patient with strong social support + recent positive behavior change (smoking cessation, hydration, weight loss starting). This sets up excellent prognosis with sustained engagement

nursesNote: 0900 — 48 y/o Latino male R. Aguilar presenting to Family Medicine Clinic for first comprehensive PCP follow-up after ED diagnosis 2 weeks ago of new TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (HbA1c 9.2 + random glucose 312 with classic symptoms polyuria + polydipsia + weight loss; no DKA, bicarbonate normal). Discharged from ED on metformin 500 mg PO daily x 1 week with PCP follow-up + clinic call. Today's 60-min new T2DM education visit (clinic dietitian + diabetes educator + pharmacist embedded; wife present at patient's request). VITALS: BP 142/88 (uncontrolled), HR 88, RR 16, SpO2 98%, T 36.8°C, weight 97 kg (lost 1 kg since ED), BMI 31.7, waist 108 cm (above risk threshold 102 cm for men); foot exam intact pulses + sensation intact with 10-g monofilament 10/10 sites bilateral + no ulcers/lesions; visual inspection skin/oral healthy. DIAGNOSIS reinforced with teach-back: T2DM criteria reviewed (HbA1c ≥6.5 OR FPG ≥126 OR 2-hr OGTT ≥200 OR random ≥200 with symptoms; patient met multiple criteria; symptomatic so no 2nd test needed); explained chronic + progressive nature (beta-cell function declines; most patients eventually need additional medications over years); eAG from HbA1c 9.2 = ~217 (formula 28.7 × A1c − 46.7) — patient grasped concept. PHARMACOTHERAPY: continued metformin 500 mg BID this week (titrated up from daily), then 1000 mg BID with meals next week (slow titration reduces GI side effects most common nausea + diarrhea + metallic taste; usually transient); take WITH meals; contraindications reviewed (eGFR <30 avoid, hepatic dysfunction, decompensated HF, severe hypoxia, alcohol abuse — lactic acidosis risk rare but serious); hold prior to iodinated contrast if eGFR <60; baseline B12 ordered + annual (long-term metformin B12 deficiency risk); mechanism explained (decreases hepatic glucose production + increases peripheral insulin sensitivity; low hypoglycemia risk — does NOT cause hypoglycemia as monotherapy so daily finger-stick BG not required but recommended at diagnosis to learn pattern + after meals to see food impact); if A1c >7 at 3 months will add 2nd agent based on ASCVD/HF/CKD status (SGLT2i or GLP-1RA preferred if ASCVD/HF/CKD present). BP MANAGEMENT: target <130/80; current 142/88 uncontrolled; LISINOPRIL 10 mg PO daily started (ACE inhibitor first-line in DM + HTN — renoprotective + cardioprotective + especially with albuminuria; titrate up to 20-40 mg as tolerated); monitor K + Cr 1-2 weeks after initiation + with titration; lifestyle BP measures (DASH diet, sodium reduction <2,300 mg/day, weight loss, exercise, alcohol moderation). STATIN: HIGH-INTENSITY indicated for adults with DM age 40-75 + LDL ≥70 (or moderate-intensity if low risk + LDL <100); ATORVASTATIN 40 mg PO daily started (target LDL <70 in higher-risk); side effects reviewed (myalgia common usually mild; LFTs check if symptoms; new-onset DM paradox small risk outweighed by CV benefit); ASCVD 10-yr risk ~12% (intermediate-high) per Pooled Cohort Equations; aspirin not routinely for primary prevention; smoking cessation already accomplished 6 mo ago (was 1 PPD x 20 yr) — congratulated + reinforced. COMPLICATION SCREENING + REFERRALS: (1) DILATED EYE EXAM by ophthalmology referral within 1 month (annual thereafter); (2) FOOT EXAM today + at every visit (visual) + comprehensive annual (10-g monofilament, vibration, pulses, reflexes — today's exam normal; reduces amputation 40-50%); (3) URINE MICROALBUMIN/CR RATIO + eGFR (CMP) sent today + annually (microalbuminuria ≥30 mg/g = early diabetic nephropathy; ACE/ARB indicated for albuminuria or HTN); (4) LIPID PANEL sent + as guided by treatment (LDL 138, HDL 38, TG 220 today); (5) BP at every visit; (6) DENTAL referral q6 mo (DM + periodontal disease bidirectional); (7) PHQ-9 = 4 (minimal); (8) NUTRITION assessment by clinic dietitian today + 1-month follow-up; (9) SLEEP apnea screening (STOP-BANG = 4 — moderate risk) — sleep medicine referral discussed + accepted; (10) family history review + first-degree relative screening recommendation (wife + adult son screening soon). LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION (foundation): NUTRITION — Mediterranean diet pattern emphasized with cultural adaptation (corn tortillas over flour, beans + brown rice over white rice, grilled chicken/fish over fried, fresh fruit over juice, water/unsweetened tea over sodas/sweet horchata; engaged wife as primary cook in planning); plate method (½ non-starchy vegetables, ¼ lean protein, ¼ whole grain/starch); honor cultural meals while moderating portions; weight loss goal 5-10% (4.8-9.7 kg over 6 months); PHYSICAL ACTIVITY 150 min/week moderate-intensity (brisk walking — patient already started 15 min/day + lost 1 kg in 2 weeks) + build to 30 min 5x/week + add 2-3 sessions/week resistance training; avoid sedentary >30 min at a stretch; SLEEP 7-9 hr; ALCOHOL moderation (≤2 drinks/day men; less is better); SMOKING already quit — congratulate + relapse prevention; STRESS management; SOCIAL CONNECTION + family engagement. SELF-CARE skills taught with teach-back: BLOOD GLUCOSE monitoring (not required on metformin alone but recommended at diagnosis to learn pattern + post-meal impact; equipment + technique demonstrated; CGM discussed as option if interested + insurance covers); FOOT CARE daily (inspect with mirror, wash + dry between toes, moisturize except between toes, well-fitted shoes, no barefoot, cut nails straight or podiatry referral); ORAL CARE (brush 2x + floss + dental q6 mo); HYPOGLYCEMIA RECOGNITION + RULE OF 15 (not at risk on metformin alone but important to know — symptoms, 15 g fast-acting carb, wait 15 min, recheck, repeat if <70, eat protein + complex carb after recovery); SICK DAY RULES (hold metformin during severe illness/vomiting/dehydration/AKI — lactic acidosis risk; insulin generally continued + may need MORE; stay hydrated; easily digestible carbs; check ketones if T1DM or unwell + high glucose; call clinic if persistent vomiting, dehydration, severe hyperglycemia, DKA signs); WHEN TO CALL clinic (glucose persistently >250, vomiting >24 hr, dehydration, signs of DKA, severe hypoglycemia not resolving, foot ulcer, vision changes, chest pain, neuro symptoms). VACCINATIONS UPDATED today: influenza (last fall — current), pneumococcal PCV20 (eligible 19-64 with DM) — administered today, hepatitis B Heplisav-B 2-dose series started today (DM patients at higher Hep B risk from finger-stick monitoring), COVID-19 updated 4 months ago (current), Tdap 5 years ago (current — Td booster in 5 yr), Shingrix not eligible until 50 (deferred 2 years); RSV not yet indicated (age 48). SMART GOALS established with motivational interviewing approach (patient-driven): (1) Walk 20 min after dinner 4 nights/week starting Monday + log on phone app (building from 15 min/day already); (2) Replace soda with unsweetened iced tea or water for next 4 weeks + allow 1 soda/week as special occasion (already reduced — formalizing); (3) Take metformin with breakfast + dinner; set 2 phone alarms + check off on calendar; (4) Schedule ophthalmology + sleep medicine + dental appointments within 1 month (clinic provides referrals + appointment lines); (5) Family meeting Sunday to discuss diagnosis + meal planning with wife + 3 children. FAMILY HISTORY: both parents T2DM (mother on metformin + sitagliptin; father on metformin + insulin); maternal grandmother amputation from DM; first-degree relative screening recommended (wife age 46 BMI 29 + adult son age 22 BMI 28 + son 18 + daughter 15); family-based lifestyle modification as primary prevention. CULTURAL + LITERACY adapted teaching with Spanish materials as supplement + teach-back throughout; high engagement + motivation; psychosocial supportive (engaged wife, employed + insured, supportive family + community); food security okay; recent successful behavior change (smoking cessation 6 mo ago + weight loss started + soda reduction). FOLLOW-UP: diabetes educator phone call in 1 week (tolerance + adherence + questions); clinic visit in 2 weeks (titrate metformin + recheck BP + add 2nd antihypertensive if needed); 3-month follow-up with repeat HbA1c + lipid + UMACR + comprehensive review; ophthalmology + sleep medicine + dental referrals placed; nutrition follow-up 1 month; family meeting Sunday encouraged; clinic phone access provided for questions. Patient + wife engaged, asking informed questions, tearful at moments but receptive; took photos of teaching materials with phone; expressed gratitude for thorough visit + family involvement; verbalized commitment to changes; demonstrated teach-back on metformin dosing + sick day rules + hypoglycemia treatment + foot care + when to call clinic. Anticipate excellent prognosis with sustained engagement + family support + multidisciplinary team + early diagnosis (no microvascular complications evident yet) + motivated patient with strong social support + recent positive behavior change.
providerOrders: DIAGNOSIS confirmed: TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (T2DM) — HbA1c 9.2, random glucose 312 with classic symptoms (polyuria + polydipsia + weight loss); ICD-10 E11.65 (T2DM with hyperglycemia)
PHARMACOTHERAPY:

METFORMIN — continue 500 mg PO BID this week, then 1000 mg PO BID with meals next week (slow titration reduces GI side effects); take with meals; baseline + annual B12; hold prior to iodinated contrast if eGFR <60; sick day rules; lactic acidosis education

LISINOPRIL 10 mg PO daily started (ACE inhibitor first-line in DM + HTN — renoprotective + cardioprotective); titrate to 20-40 mg as tolerated for BP target <130/80; monitor K + Cr 1-2 weeks after initiation
ATORVASTATIN 40 mg PO daily started (high-intensity statin for DM age 40-75 + LDL ≥70 — target LDL <70 in higher-risk); side effects reviewed
ASPIRIN not started (not routine for primary prevention in DM)

BLOOD PRESSURE target <130/80 (current 142/88 — recheck end of visit 138/86); lifestyle BP measures (DASH diet + sodium reduction <2,300 mg/day + weight loss + exercise + alcohol moderation); titrate lisinopril + add 2nd agent (amlodipine or HCTZ) in 2 weeks if not at target
COMPLICATION SCREENING:
DILATED EYE EXAM by ophthalmology referral within 1 month; annual thereafter

FOOT EXAM today (10-g monofilament 10/10 sites bilateral + intact pulses + no ulcers) + at every visit (visual) + comprehensive annual; podiatry referral if neuropathy, deformities, poor vision, or unable to perform self-care
URINE MICROALBUMIN/CR RATIO + CMP (eGFR) sent today + annually
LIPID PANEL today (LDL 138, HDL 38, TG 220) + as clinically guided
BP at every visit
DENTAL referral q6 months (DM + periodontal disease bidirectional)
PHQ-9 today = 4 (minimal); annual depression screening; address if symptoms develop
SLEEP APNEA — STOP-BANG = 4 (moderate risk); sleep medicine referral placed
PHARMACY consult during visit for medication review + tolerance + drug interactions + cost discussion
VACCINATIONS UPDATED:
Influenza (current — last fall)
PNEUMOCOCCAL — PCV20 administered today (adults 19-64 with DM)
HEPATITIS B — Heplisav-B 2-dose series started today (adults 19-59 with DM + unvaccinated; 2nd dose in 1 month)
COVID-19 (current — updated 4 months ago)
Tdap (current — 5 years ago; Td booster in 5 years)
Shingrix — deferred until age 50 (2 years)
RSV — not yet indicated (age 48; reassess at 60-74 per shared decision; universal ≥75)
LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION (foundation of all DM care):

NUTRITION — Mediterranean diet pattern with cultural adaptation (Latino traditional foods + healthier preparations); plate method; portion control; engage wife as primary cook in planning; clinic dietitian consult today + 1-month follow-up; Spanish-language nutrition materials provided

PHYSICAL ACTIVITY — 150 min/week moderate-intensity (start with current 15 min/day + build to 30 min 5x/week brisk walking) + 2-3 sessions/week resistance training; avoid sedentary >30 min at a stretch
WEIGHT LOSS — 5-10% body weight over 6 months (4.8-9.7 kg goal; patient already lost 1 kg in 2 weeks)
SLEEP 7-9 hr/night; address insomnia + OSA workup
ALCOHOL moderation (≤2 drinks/day men; less is better)
SMOKING — already quit 6 months ago (was 1 PPD x 20 years) — congratulate + reinforce + relapse prevention

SELF-CARE skills taught with teach-back:

FOOT CARE — daily inspection with mirror + wash + dry between toes + moisturize except between toes + well-fitted shoes + no barefoot + cut nails straight or podiatry referral
ORAL CARE — brush 2x daily + floss daily + dental q6 months
HYPOGLYCEMIA RECOGNITION + RULE OF 15 — symptoms, 15 g fast-acting carb, wait 15 min, recheck, repeat if <70, eat protein + complex carb after recovery
SICK DAY RULES — hold metformin during severe illness/vomiting/dehydration/AKI; insulin generally continued + may need MORE; stay hydrated; easily digestible carbs

WHEN TO CALL clinic — glucose persistently >250, vomiting >24 hr, dehydration, signs of DKA (rapid breathing + fruity breath + abdominal pain), severe hypoglycemia not resolving, foot ulcer, vision changes, chest pain, neuro symptoms
SMART GOALS (patient-driven via motivational interviewing):
1. Walk 20 min after dinner 4 nights/week starting Monday + log on phone app
2. Replace soda with unsweetened iced tea or water for next 4 weeks + allow 1 soda/week as special occasion
3. Take metformin with breakfast + dinner; set 2 phone alarms + check off on calendar
4. Schedule ophthalmology + sleep medicine + dental appointments within 1 month
5. Family meeting Sunday to discuss diagnosis + meal planning with wife + 3 children
FAMILY HISTORY + first-degree relative screening recommendations:
Both parents T2DM (mother on metformin + sitagliptin; father on metformin + insulin); maternal grandmother amputation; family history major risk factor
Recommend screening for wife (age 46 BMI 29) + adult son (age 22 BMI 28) + younger children at age-appropriate intervals
Family-based lifestyle modification as primary prevention for entire family
FOLLOW-UP plan:
Phone call from diabetes educator in 1 week (tolerance + adherence + questions)
Clinic visit in 2 weeks (titrate metformin + recheck BP + add 2nd antihypertensive if not at target)
3-month follow-up with repeat HbA1c + lipid + UMACR + CMP + comprehensive review
Ophthalmology + sleep medicine + dental + dietitian (1 month) referrals placed
Clinic phone access provided 24/7 for questions

DOCUMENTATION today: teach-back demonstrated on metformin dosing + sick day rules + hypoglycemia treatment + foot care + when to call clinic; written materials provided in English + Spanish; photos of teaching materials taken by patient; smart goals documented; family + community resources discussed; patient + wife engagement + questions documented

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this new-DM teaching visit?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention + intervention at this teaching visit.

opts.text: NEW T2DM diagnosis (HbA1c 9.2 + random 312 with symptoms); CHRONIC + PROGRESSIVE disease requiring multidisciplinary management; ADA criteria met (HbA1c ≥6.5 OR FPG ≥126 OR 2-hr OGTT ≥200 OR random ≥200 with symptoms — symptomatic confirmation does not require 2nd test)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnosis confirmation + chronic disease framing. T2DM is chronic + progressive (beta-cell function declines over years; most patients eventually need additional medications). Early tight control reduces microvascular + (over decades) macrovascular complications (UKPDS legacy effect). Multidisciplinary care: PCP + endocrinology if needed + dietitian + diabetes educator + ophthalmology + podiatry + dental + behavioral health + pharmacist + family.

opts.text: METFORMIN first-line per ADA — slow titration (500 mg daily → BID → 1000 mg BID) with meals to reduce GI side effects; baseline + annual B12; contraindications (eGFR <30 avoid, hepatic dysfunction, decompensated HF, severe hypoxia, alcohol abuse — lactic acidosis); hold prior to iodinated contrast if eGFR <60; does NOT cause hypoglycemia as monotherapy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pharmacotherapy initiation + education. Metformin is 1st-line per ADA + AACE/ADCES + EASD. Slow titration improves tolerability. Common GI side effects usually transient. Critical contraindications + sick day rules + B12 monitoring. Patient education: take with meals, when to call (severe diarrhea/vomiting + signs of lactic acidosis — fatigue/muscle pain/dyspnea/abdominal pain).

opts.text: BP 142/88 uncontrolled (target <130/80 for most adults with DM); LISINOPRIL 10 mg PO daily started (ACE inhibitor first-line in DM + HTN — renoprotective + cardioprotective); monitor K + Cr 1-2 weeks after initiation + titration; lifestyle BP measures (DASH + sodium <2,300 mg/day + weight + exercise + alcohol moderation)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — BP management. DM + HTN target <130/80. ACE/ARB first-line for renoprotection + cardioprotection (especially with albuminuria). Monitor K + Cr after starting. Lifestyle BP measures complement medication. Titrate as tolerated; add 2nd agent if not at target.

opts.text: ASCVD risk ~12% (intermediate-high) per Pooled Cohort Equations; HIGH-INTENSITY STATIN indicated for adults with DM age 40-75 + LDL ≥70; ATORVASTATIN 40 mg PO daily started (target LDL <70 in higher-risk); side effects reviewed (myalgia common usually mild; rhabdomyolysis rare; LFTs check if symptoms); aspirin NOT routine for primary prevention in DM
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — ASCVD risk reduction. ACC/AHA + ADA: high-intensity statin for adults with DM age 40-75 unless contraindicated. Aspirin primary prevention controversial — individualized. ASCVD risk calculation guides discussion + treatment intensity. Smoking cessation single most important modifiable risk factor (patient already quit).

opts.text: COMPLICATION SCREENING: ANNUAL dilated eye exam, foot exam at every visit + comprehensive annual (10-g monofilament + vibration + pulses + reflexes), urine microalbumin/Cr + eGFR annually, lipid panel + BP + dental q6 mo + depression screening + sleep apnea screening + nutrition + pharmacy review
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — comprehensive screening reduces morbidity. Microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) + macrovascular (CAD, stroke, PAD). Early detection + treatment prevents progression. Multidisciplinary referrals essential. ADA Standards of Care updated annually.

opts.text: LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION (foundation): Mediterranean/DASH/low-carb diet with cultural adaptation (Latino traditional foods + healthier preparations + engage wife as primary cook); 150 min/week moderate-intensity activity (start where patient is + build); 5-10% weight loss (4.8-9.7 kg over 6 months); sleep 7-9 hr; alcohol moderation; SMOKING already quit; stress + social connection + family engagement
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — lifestyle modification is foundation of all DM care + reduces all complications. Effects dose-dependent — even modest improvements produce benefit. Cultural adaptation critical for sustained adherence. Engaging family (especially primary cook) amplifies impact. Acknowledge patient's existing positive behavior change (smoking cessation + weight loss starting).

opts.text: SELF-CARE skills with teach-back: blood glucose monitoring (not required on metformin alone but recommended at diagnosis), daily foot care (inspect + wash + dry between toes + moisturize except between toes + well-fitted shoes + no barefoot), oral care (brush 2x + floss + dental q6 mo), hypoglycemia recognition + RULE OF 15, sick day rules (hold metformin in severe illness/AKI; lactic acidosis), when to call clinic
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — self-care is foundational for chronic disease management. DSMES (diabetes self-management education + support) reduces complications + costs. Address all 7 AADE/ADCES self-care behaviors: healthy eating, being active, monitoring, taking medications, problem solving, healthy coping, reducing risk. Engage family. Provide written + visual materials in primary language. Verify understanding with teach-back.

opts.text: VACCINATIONS: Influenza annual + PCV20 (or PCV15→PPSV23) for DM 19-64 + Heplisav-B 2-dose for DM unvaccinated 19-59 + COVID-19 updated + Tdap + Shingrix at 50 + RSV per age/risk; documented + administered at this visit when due
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — vaccination is part of comprehensive DM care. DM patients at higher risk for influenza + pneumococcal + Hep B (finger-stick exposure) + severe COVID-19. ACIP recommendations updated annually. Engage primary care + pharmacy + community programs to increase coverage.

opts.text: FAMILY HISTORY: both parents T2DM + maternal grandmother amputation + paternal uncle CKD on dialysis; FIRST-DEGREE RELATIVE SCREENING recommendation (wife 46 BMI 29 + adult son 22 BMI 28 + younger children at age-appropriate intervals); FAMILY-BASED lifestyle modification as primary prevention
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family history is major T2DM risk factor (2-6x increased risk for first-degree relatives). Family-based intervention (lifestyle modification for entire family) protects children + supports patient adherence. Discuss inheritance + recommend screening. Insurance covers DM screening per USPSTF.

opts.text: SMART GOALS via motivational interviewing (patient-driven): walk 20 min after dinner 4x/week, replace soda with water/tea, take metformin with meals + alarm, schedule referrals within 1 month, family meeting Sunday; FOLLOW-UP: diabetes educator phone call in 1 week + clinic visit in 2 weeks + 3-month comprehensive follow-up
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — behavior change support. Motivational interviewing respects autonomy + elicits internal motivation. SMART goals are specific + actionable. Habit formation: link to existing routine, environment design, social support, self-monitoring, celebrate wins, problem-solve barriers without judgment. Sustained follow-up + adapting as life evolves.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed. No medication allergies to consider in current or future regimen selection.

opts.text: Patient brought 14-year-old daughter to translate during the visit instead of using the professional interpreter — family prefers it
opts.rat: ⚠️ Inappropriate (though not the situation in this case — patient comfortable in English with Spanish materials as supplement + wife present for support). General principle: never use family member (especially not child) for medical translation — compromises information accuracy, violates patient confidentiality, creates inappropriate role for child translator. Professional medical interpreter required for complex/sensitive content. Use Spanish materials + Spanish-speaking staff when available.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What teaching, screening, and management principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying DM management principles.

opts.text: T2DM is CHRONIC + PROGRESSIVE — beta-cell function declines over years; most patients eventually need additional medications; EARLY TIGHT CONTROL reduces microvascular + (over decades) macrovascular complications (UKPDS legacy effect); diagnosis criteria per ADA (HbA1c ≥6.5 OR FPG ≥126 OR 2-hr OGTT ≥200 OR random ≥200 with symptoms — symptomatic confirmation does not require 2nd test)
opts.rat: CORRECT — pathophysiology + diagnostic framework. Setting realistic expectations (chronic + progressive) prepares patient for long-term management + reduces guilt when medications escalate. Early tight control matters even if effects on CV outcomes emerge decades later.

opts.text: METFORMIN first-line per ADA — decreases hepatic glucose production + increases peripheral insulin sensitivity + modest weight loss + LOW HYPOGLYCEMIA RISK (does NOT cause hypoglycemia as monotherapy); slow titration with meals reduces GI side effects; contraindications (eGFR <30 + hepatic + decompensated HF + severe hypoxia + alcohol abuse — lactic acidosis); B12 monitoring; if A1c >7 at 3 mo add 2nd agent based on ASCVD/HF/CKD status (SGLT2i or GLP-1RA preferred if comorbidity)
opts.rat: CORRECT — metformin mechanism + monitoring + escalation. ADA preferred 1st-line. ASCVD/HF/CKD presence determines 2nd agent choice (SGLT2i or GLP-1RA preferred — CV + renal + weight benefits). Sulfonylureas + DPP-4 + insulin are options for specific situations. Patient education on common side effects + when to call.

opts.text: BP target <130/80 for most adults with DM (per ADA; some societies <140/90 or shared decision); ACE INHIBITOR or ARB FIRST-LINE in DM + HTN (renoprotective + cardioprotective; especially with albuminuria); monitor K + Cr 1-2 weeks after initiation + titration; lifestyle BP measures complement medication (DASH + sodium <2,300 mg/day + weight + exercise + alcohol moderation)
opts.rat: CORRECT — BP management framework. ACE/ARB first-line because of additional renoprotection + cardioprotection beyond BP control. Monitor for hyperkalemia + AKI. Add 2nd agent (calcium channel blocker like amlodipine; thiazide diuretic like chlorthalidone or HCTZ) if not at target. Lifestyle measures essential.

opts.text: HIGH-INTENSITY STATIN for adults with DM age 40-75 + LDL ≥70 (atorvastatin 40-80 OR rosuvastatin 20-40); ASCVD risk calculation (Pooled Cohort Equations) guides intensity in some cases; ASPIRIN NOT routine for primary prevention in DM (individualized); SMOKING CESSATION single most important modifiable risk factor (patient already quit — congratulate + reinforce)
opts.rat: CORRECT — ASCVD risk reduction framework. ACC/AHA + ADA guidelines. Statin myalgia common usually mild (rhabdomyolysis rare); statin-associated new-onset DM small risk outweighed by CV benefit. Aspirin primary prevention in DM is controversial — bleeding risk vs CV benefit. Smoking cessation is high-yield.

opts.text: COMPLICATION SCREENING (annual): dilated eye exam, comprehensive foot exam (10-g monofilament + vibration + pulses + reflexes; visual at every visit), urine microalbumin/Cr ratio + eGFR, lipid panel, dental q6 mo, depression screening, sleep apnea screening; early detection + treatment prevents progression (retinopathy → anti-VEGF/photocoagulation; nephropathy → ACE/ARB + tight glycemia + BP control; neuropathy → glycemia + symptom management; foot exam reduces amputation 40-50%)
opts.rat: CORRECT — complication screening + early treatment. ADA Standards of Care detail intervals + thresholds. Multidisciplinary referrals (ophthalmology, podiatry, dental, nephrology, behavioral health, sleep). Early detection allows interventions that preserve function + quality of life + reduce mortality.

opts.text: LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION is the FOUNDATION of all T2DM care: Mediterranean/DASH/low-carb diet with cultural adaptation; 150 min/week moderate-intensity activity + 2-3 sessions/week resistance training; 5-10% weight loss substantially improves glycemia + BP + lipids; sleep 7-9 hr; alcohol moderation; smoking cessation; stress + social engagement; effects DOSE-DEPENDENT — even modest improvements produce measurable benefits
opts.rat: CORRECT — lifestyle modification as foundation. Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP) showed 58% reduction in diabetes progression with lifestyle (vs 31% with metformin in prediabetes). For established T2DM, lifestyle remains foundational + complements pharmacotherapy. Cultural adaptation is critical for sustained adherence.

opts.text: VACCINATIONS for DM adults: INFLUENZA annual + PNEUMOCOCCAL (PCV20 alone OR PCV15→PPSV23 1 year later for 19-64 with DM) + HEPATITIS B (Heplisav-B 2-dose or Engerix-B 3-dose for 19-59 unvaccinated; shared decision ≥60 — DM patients at higher Hep B risk from finger-stick monitoring) + COVID-19 updated + Tdap + Shingrix at ≥50 + RSV per age (≥75 universal; 60-74 by risk)
opts.rat: CORRECT — vaccinations per CDC ACIP + ADA. DM increases vaccine-preventable disease morbidity. Hep B vaccination for DM patients reflects increased exposure from BG monitoring + occasional shared equipment + higher prevalence. ACIP updates annually.

opts.text: SELF-CARE skills: blood glucose monitoring as appropriate (not required on metformin alone), foot care daily + at every visit, oral care + dental q6 mo, hypoglycemia recognition + RULE OF 15, sick day rules (hold metformin in severe illness/AKI; lactic acidosis risk), when to call clinic — verify with TEACH-BACK + provide written + visual materials in primary language
opts.rat: CORRECT — self-care + teaching framework. DSMES reduces complications + costs. Address all 7 AADE/ADCES self-care behaviors. Engage family. Provide written + visual materials in primary language. Teach-back validates understanding (NOT just 'do you understand?' — ask patient to explain back).

opts.text: Tell the patient that he must lose at least 50 lb in 6 months and exercise 1 hour every day or his diabetes will get worse and he will end up like his grandmother (amputation)
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. (1) Goals must be SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) + realistic — 50 lb in 6 months is unrealistic + unsustainable; 5-10% body weight loss (4.8-9.7 kg) over 6 months is evidence-based + meaningful. (2) MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING (patient-driven) outperforms lecturing/scaring. (3) Fear-based teaching can backfire (avoidance, guilt, denial, treatment refusal). (4) Build self-efficacy through small successes + collaborative goal-setting.

opts.text: Recommend the patient eat NO carbohydrates whatsoever, eliminate all traditional Latino foods + family meals, and stop eating with his wife and children since their meals are not 'diabetic-friendly'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG on multiple counts. (1) Carbohydrate elimination is unnecessary + unsustainable + culturally inappropriate; emphasize quality (whole grains, vegetables, fruits) + portion control + plate method. (2) Demonizing traditional foods harms cultural identity + family engagement + adherence; instead, healthier PREPARATIONS of cultural foods. (3) Family meals are PROTECTIVE — engage wife + family in healthy meal planning together; isolating patient at meals harms social fabric + adherence. (4) Family-based lifestyle modification protects all members.

opts.text: Initiate a basal-bolus insulin regimen at this first follow-up visit (long-acting glargine + short-acting lispro at each meal) since A1c is 9.2
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach in this stable T2DM patient. (1) Metformin is 1st-line per ADA; insulin is typically reserved for severe symptomatic hyperglycemia (e.g., A1c >10 with symptoms), DKA, hospitalization, or after multiple oral agents fail to achieve target. (2) Insulin in T2DM adds complexity, hypoglycemia risk, weight gain, injection burden — should be considered when needed but not as 1st-line in a stable outpatient. (3) Patient's symptoms already improving on metformin + lifestyle. Reassess at 3 months — if A1c not at target, add 2nd agent (SGLT2i or GLP-1RA preferred if ASCVD/HF/CKD; insulin if profound hyperglycemia persists).

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing priorities at this teaching visit
steps.inst: Rank these nursing priorities from #1 (HIGHEST) to #5 (LOWEST) for this comprehensive teaching visit.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit: New T2DM Diagnosis r/t need for understanding of disease + medication (metformin titration + sick day rules) + lifestyle modification + self-care skills + complication screening — establishes foundation for all future care
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST at this visit. Comprehensive new-DM teaching is the central work of this 60-min visit. Teach-back validates understanding. Provide written + visual materials in primary language. Engage family. Build self-efficacy through manageable initial goals.

opts.text: Risk for Long-Term Diabetes Complications r/t hyperglycemia + uncontrolled BP + dyslipidemia + lifestyle factors — comprehensive screening + treatment (metformin + lisinopril + atorvastatin + lifestyle + vaccinations + ophthalmology + sleep + dental + dietitian referrals)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Coordinated treatment + screening prevents microvascular + macrovascular complications. Early tight control matters (UKPDS legacy effect). Multidisciplinary referrals essential. ADA Standards of Care guide intervals + thresholds.

opts.text: Behavior Change Support r/t need for sustained lifestyle modification (nutrition + activity + weight + sleep + alcohol moderation + smoking cessation maintenance + stress + family engagement) — motivational interviewing + SMART goals + habit formation + cultural adaptation
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Behavior change is most challenging aspect of chronic disease management + most impactful for outcomes. Motivational interviewing + SMART goals + cultural adaptation increase durability. Setbacks are normal; problem-solve without judgment.

opts.text: Family Engagement + First-Degree Relative Screening r/t strong family history of T2DM + amputation + CKD; family-based lifestyle modification protects children + supports patient; recommend wife + adult son screening; family meeting planned
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Family history is major risk factor (2-6x for first-degree relatives). Family-based intervention amplifies impact. Discuss inheritance + recommend screening. Engage wife as primary cook + family planner. Family meeting Sunday.

opts.text: Anxiety + Grief about Diagnosis r/t fear of complications + lifestyle change + family history witness (mother + father + maternal grandmother amputation) — emotional validation + reassurance + chaplain/behavioral health if needed
opts.rat: #5 — Important + addressed throughout visit. Patient + wife tearful at moments. Acknowledge emotions. Provide reassurance + hope (early diagnosis + good prognosis with engagement). Address depression risk (DM 2x depression risk). PHQ-9 = 4 (minimal); annual screening; address if symptoms develop.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this visit.

opts.text: Continue METFORMIN with slow titration (500 mg BID this week, then 1000 mg BID with meals); baseline + annual B12; contraindications + sick day rules + lactic acidosis education; teach-back demonstrated
opts.rat: ADA 1st-line for T2DM. Slow titration reduces GI side effects. Baseline + annual B12 (long-term metformin B12 deficiency risk). Contraindications + sick day rules + lactic acidosis recognition educated. Teach-back validates understanding.

opts.text: LISINOPRIL 10 mg PO daily started (titrate to 20-40 mg as tolerated; monitor K + Cr 1-2 weeks); ATORVASTATIN 40 mg PO daily started; BP target <130/80; LDL target <70 in higher-risk; lifestyle measures complement medication
opts.rat: ACE inhibitor first-line in DM + HTN (renoprotective + cardioprotective). High-intensity statin for DM age 40-75 + LDL ≥70. Monitor K + Cr after starting ACE. Lifestyle BP + lipid measures complement medication. Standard ADA + ACC/AHA recommendations.

opts.text: COMPLICATION SCREENING + REFERRALS: dilated eye exam within 1 month + annually; comprehensive foot exam today + at every visit; urine microalbumin/Cr + CMP sent today + annually; lipid panel today; dental q6 mo; PHQ-9; sleep apnea screening (STOP-BANG); nutrition + sleep medicine + dental + pharmacy referrals
opts.rat: Comprehensive screening + multidisciplinary referrals reduce morbidity. Early detection + treatment prevents complication progression. ADA Standards of Care detail intervals + thresholds. Referrals essential for specialty input.

opts.text: VACCINATIONS UPDATED: pneumococcal PCV20 administered today; Heplisav-B 2-dose series started today (2nd dose 1 month); influenza + COVID + Tdap current; Shingrix deferred until age 50; RSV not yet indicated
opts.rat: Vaccinations per CDC ACIP + ADA. DM patients at higher vaccine-preventable disease risk. Hep B for DM 19-59 + unvaccinated reflects finger-stick exposure. Document + administer when due.

opts.text: LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION (foundation): Mediterranean diet pattern with cultural adaptation (Latino traditional foods + healthier preparations + engage wife as primary cook); 150 min/week moderate-intensity activity (build from current 15 min/day + resistance training); 5-10% weight loss over 6 months; sleep 7-9 hr; alcohol moderation; smoking cessation maintained; stress + social + family engagement
opts.rat: Foundation of all T2DM care. Effects dose-dependent — even modest improvements produce benefit. Cultural adaptation critical for sustained adherence. Engaging family amplifies impact. Acknowledge patient's existing positive behavior change.

opts.text: SELF-CARE SKILLS taught with teach-back: blood glucose monitoring (not required on metformin alone but recommended at diagnosis); daily foot care (inspect + wash + dry between toes + moisturize except between toes + well-fitted shoes); oral care; hypoglycemia recognition + RULE OF 15; sick day rules (hold metformin in severe illness/AKI); when to call clinic
opts.rat: Self-care is foundational for chronic disease management. DSMES reduces complications + costs. Address all 7 AADE/ADCES self-care behaviors. Engage family. Provide written + visual materials in primary language. Teach-back validates understanding.

opts.text: Tell the patient he must lose 50 lb in 6 months + exercise 1 hour daily or he will end up like his grandmother (amputation); scare him into compliance
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. Goals must be SMART + realistic; 5-10% body weight loss over 6 months is evidence-based + meaningful (not 50 lb). Motivational interviewing (patient-driven) outperforms lecturing/scaring. Fear-based teaching can backfire (avoidance, guilt, denial, treatment refusal). Build self-efficacy through small successes + collaborative goal-setting.

opts.text: Recommend ELIMINATION of all carbohydrates + all traditional Latino foods + eating separately from family at meals because their meals are not 'diabetic-friendly'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG on multiple counts. Carbohydrate elimination unnecessary + unsustainable + culturally inappropriate. Emphasize quality + portion control + plate method + healthier preparations of cultural foods. Family meals are PROTECTIVE — engage family in healthy planning. Family-based lifestyle modification protects all members.

opts.text: SMART GOALS via motivational interviewing (patient-driven): walk 20 min after dinner 4x/week; replace soda with water/tea; metformin with meals + alarm; schedule referrals within 1 month; family meeting Sunday; FOLLOW-UP — diabetes educator phone in 1 week + clinic visit 2 weeks + 3-month comprehensive review
opts.rat: Behavior change support. Motivational interviewing respects autonomy + elicits internal motivation. SMART goals specific + actionable. Habit formation: link to existing routine, environment design, social support, self-monitoring, celebrate wins, problem-solve barriers without judgment. Sustained follow-up + adapting as life evolves.

opts.text: FAMILY HISTORY discussion + FIRST-DEGREE RELATIVE SCREENING recommendation (wife age 46 BMI 29 + adult son age 22 BMI 28 + younger children at age-appropriate intervals); family-based lifestyle modification as primary prevention for entire family
opts.rat: Family history is major T2DM risk factor (2-6x for first-degree relatives). Family-based intervention protects children + supports patient adherence. Insurance covers DM screening per USPSTF. Discuss inheritance pattern (polygenic + environmental).

opts.text: Initiate basal-bolus insulin regimen (long-acting glargine + short-acting lispro at each meal) at this first follow-up visit since A1c is 9.2
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG in this stable patient. Metformin is 1st-line per ADA. Insulin reserved for severe symptomatic hyperglycemia (A1c >10 with symptoms), DKA, hospitalization, or after multiple oral agents fail. Patient's symptoms improving on metformin + lifestyle. Reassess at 3 months — if not at target, add 2nd agent based on comorbidity (SGLT2i or GLP-1RA preferred if ASCVD/HF/CKD; insulin if profound hyperglycemia persists).

opts.text: CULTURAL + LITERACY + PSYCHOSOCIAL adaptation: Spanish materials as supplement (patient comfortable in English); engage wife as primary cook; acknowledge emotions about diagnosis + family history; PHQ-9 = 4 (minimal); food security + social determinants screening; community resources (Spanish-language DSMES + peer support + promotores)
opts.rat: Cultural humility + language access + family engagement + addressing social determinants are essential for sustained DM self-management. Latino populations have higher T2DM prevalence + earlier onset + higher complication rates driven by social determinants. Peer support + community health workers (promotores) effective. Acknowledge emotions + provide hope.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at this teaching visit
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions during the 60-min visit. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: EXPLAIN DIAGNOSIS + medication plan with teach-back: T2DM criteria + chronic + progressive framing + metformin titration + lisinopril + atorvastatin + sick day rules + hypoglycemia recognition + when to call clinic; written + visual materials in English + Spanish; wife present + engaged
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST in teaching sequence. Foundation for all subsequent care. Establish understanding of diagnosis + medications + emergency situations + when to call. Teach-back validates understanding. Engage family. Provide materials in primary language.

opts.text: LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION teaching with cultural adaptation: Mediterranean diet pattern + plate method + portion control + cultural preparations; 150 min/week activity (build from current); weight loss goal 5-10%; sleep + alcohol moderation; smoking maintained; engage wife + family meeting planned
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Lifestyle is foundation of all T2DM care + reduces all complications. Cultural adaptation + family engagement critical for sustained adherence. Build from patient's existing positive changes. Dietitian consult today + 1-month follow-up.

opts.text: SELF-CARE skills with teach-back: BG monitoring as appropriate; daily foot care + comprehensive foot exam today; oral care; sick day rules; vaccinations updated (PCV20 + Heplisav-B today); demonstrate equipment + technique; observe return demonstration where applicable
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Self-care + vaccination operationalize knowledge. DSMES reduces complications + costs. Address all 7 AADE/ADCES behaviors. Hands-on practice + return demonstration solidify learning. Vaccinations administered when due.

opts.text: COMPLICATION SCREENING + REFERRALS coordinated: dilated eye exam within 1 month; comprehensive foot exam today + at every visit; urine microalbumin/Cr + CMP + lipid panel sent today; sleep medicine + dental + dietitian + pharmacy referrals; family history discussion + first-degree relative screening recommendation
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Coordinated screening + multidisciplinary referrals reduce morbidity + provide specialty input. Early detection + treatment prevents complications. ADA Standards of Care guide. Family-based prevention amplifies impact.

opts.text: SMART GOALS + FOLLOW-UP plan: patient-driven goals via motivational interviewing (walk 20 min 4x/week + replace soda + metformin alarm + schedule referrals + family meeting); diabetes educator phone in 1 week + clinic visit 2 weeks + 3-month comprehensive review; clinic phone access 24/7 for questions
opts.rat: #5 — Last in sequence + ongoing. Behavior change support via motivational interviewing + SMART goals + sustained follow-up. Build self-efficacy through manageable initial steps. Adapt as life evolves. Setbacks are normal — problem-solve without judgment.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each follow-up finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 3-month follow-up: HbA1c 7.4 (down from 9.2); BP 132/82 (down from 142/88 on lisinopril 20 mg + lifestyle); LDL 88 (down from 138 on atorvastatin 40 mg); weight 92 kg (down 5 kg / 5% — at goal); walking 30 min 5x/week; tolerating metformin 1000 mg BID + lisinopril + statin; no soda; engaged wife + family doing meal planning together; ophthalmology + dental + sleep medicine appointments completed
opts.rat: Excellent response to coordinated multidisciplinary care. Glycemic + BP + lipid + weight + activity + dietary + medication adherence all improved. Family engagement deepening. Specialty appointments completed. Standard target met or approaching for most parameters. Continue current regimen + reinforce.

opts.text: 6-month follow-up: HbA1c 6.6 (at goal <7); weight 90 kg (down 7 kg / 7% — meeting goal); BP 128/78 (at goal); LDL 72 (target <70 in higher-risk); diabetic eye exam: no retinopathy; foot exam: intact sensation + no ulcers; urine microalbumin: <30 (normal); no medication side effects; thriving + engaged + advocating for family screening
opts.rat: Excellent long-term recovery. All major parameters at or near target. No microvascular complications evident. No medication side effects. Family + community engagement supports sustained adherence. Continue current regimen + annual screening + 6-month follow-up.

opts.text: 1-year follow-up: HbA1c 6.4 stable; BP + lipids + weight all at goal; no complications; wife enrolled in DSMES class herself + adult son's screening showed prediabetes — entire family adopting lifestyle modification; patient became peer mentor for newly diagnosed DM patients at clinic
opts.rat: Excellent durability + family-based prevention success. Family screening identified prediabetes in son — early intervention prevents progression. Patient peer mentorship is high-yield engagement (helps both patient + other patients). Long-term partnership with healthcare team.

opts.text: 3-month follow-up: HbA1c 9.0 (minimal improvement); patient discontinued metformin after 2 weeks due to diarrhea + did not call clinic to discuss; did not start lisinopril or atorvastatin (cost concerns + skepticism); resumed soda + sedentary; weight stable; missed ophthalmology + sleep medicine appointments; reports feeling overwhelmed
opts.rat: Disengagement + non-adherence — common in early chronic disease management. Address with NON-JUDGMENTAL CURIOSITY + problem-solving: explore barriers (cost, side effects, fear, beliefs, life stress, family resistance), provide solutions (extended-release metformin for GI tolerance, $4 generic programs, copay assistance, peer support, simplified regimen, addressing competing priorities), avoid blame, rebuild engagement, smaller more achievable initial goals, consider behavioral health if depression/anxiety; restart with smaller steps + closer follow-up.

opts.text: 6-month follow-up: develops symptoms of SEVERE HYPERGLYCEMIA + DKA (polyuria + polydipsia + nausea + abdominal pain + fruity breath + Kussmaul respirations) after stopping metformin during illness without sick day plan; A1c 11.2; ED admission for DKA workup + insulin; reassess type 1 vs 2 with autoantibodies (could be LADA — latent autoimmune diabetes of adulthood)
opts.rat: DKA in 'T2DM' raises differential. Possible LADA (latent autoimmune diabetes of adulthood — adult-onset T1DM-like with positive antibodies; insulin-requiring eventually). Sick day rules + medication continuation education critical (this is why we taught hold-metformin-during-severe-illness rules). Antibody testing (GAD, IA-2, ZnT8) + C-peptide to confirm. Insulin therapy + DKA management + endocrinology referral.

opts.text: 1-month diabetes educator phone call: patient tolerating metformin 1000 mg BID well after slow titration (mild initial diarrhea resolved); BP on lisinopril 132/82; LDL on atorvastatin (no labs yet — will check at 3 months); walking 25 min 4x/week + replaced soda with water/unsweetened tea + lost 1.5 kg additional; took daughter to her first eye exam; family meeting completed + wife scheduled her own DM screening; questions about CGM (covered by insurance + interested)
opts.rat: Excellent early response + engagement. Medication tolerance + adherence + lifestyle change + family engagement + self-advocacy + interest in CGM all positive. Continue current plan + arrange CGM if interested. 3-month comprehensive review on track. Patient setting himself up for long-term success.

opts.text: 3-month follow-up: HbA1c 7.2 (improving but not yet at goal of <7); add SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin 10 mg PO daily) given ASCVD risk + renal protection benefit; patient educated on UTI/genital mycotic + euglycemic DKA warning signs + sick day rules updated; lifestyle continued; close follow-up
opts.rat: Expected escalation per ADA — if A1c >7 on metformin monotherapy at 3 months, add 2nd agent based on comorbidity. SGLT2i preferred for ASCVD/HF/CKD risk reduction (also weight loss + BP benefit). Patient education on side effects (UTI + genital mycotic + euglycemic DKA risk) + sick day rules updated (hold SGLT2i in severe illness + dehydration + before surgery + 3 days; risk of euglycemic DKA). Close follow-up.

opts.text: Annual follow-up: dilated eye exam shows mild non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR) on left; referred to retinal specialist for monitoring; no treatment yet needed; reinforces importance of tight glycemic + BP control + annual eye exams + early detection
opts.rat: Early microvascular complication (NPDR — mild) — common over time even with reasonable control + reinforces value of annual screening. Mild NPDR typically monitored; progression to severe NPDR or proliferative retinopathy or macular edema triggers treatment (anti-VEGF intravitreal injections, photocoagulation, vitrectomy). Tight glycemic + BP control slows progression. Patient educated + arranged follow-up.

opts.text: 2-year follow-up: HbA1c 6.8 stable on metformin + SGLT2i + lisinopril + atorvastatin; weight 88 kg sustained (-9 kg / 9% from baseline); BP at goal; LDL at goal; eye exam stable mild NPDR (no progression); foot exam intact; UMACR negative; family screening: wife prediabetes + lifestyle program; son normal; patient enrolled in clinic peer mentor program + actively supports other newly diagnosed patients
opts.rat: Excellent sustained long-term outcomes. Glycemic + BP + lipid + weight all at goal. No complication progression. Family screening + prevention engagement. Peer mentor role is high-yield engagement. Long-term partnership with multidisciplinary team. Annual screening continues.

opts.text: Clinic team debriefing after first comprehensive new-DM teaching visit: reviewed what worked (60-min visit length, family present, embedded dietitian + educator + pharmacist, Spanish materials, motivational interviewing, SMART goals, follow-up cadence), opportunities (clinic CGM ordering workflow improvement, expand Latino DSMES classes, partner with local promotores program); patient satisfaction survey returned positive
opts.rat: Standard quality improvement practice. Team debriefing captures lessons learned + improves future visits. Patient satisfaction survey + clinical outcomes both inform process. Community partnerships (promotores) extend clinic reach. Continuous quality improvement is part of high-functioning primary care.

title: Inpatient Hospice Admission for End-Stage Metastatic Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma with Refractory Pain, Nausea, Terminal Delirium, and Terminal Secretions: ELNEC-Based Comfort Care, Caregiver Support, and Last Hours of Life
subtitle: HOSPICE ELIGIBILITY: Life Expectancy ≤6 mo If Disease Runs Natural Course (Medicare Hospice Benefit); SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT Tailored to MECHANISM (Opioid LADDER + ROTATION + Breakthrough; Mechanism-Based ANTIEMETICS — Vestibular/Mechanical/CTZ/ICP; HALOPERIDOL for Terminal DELIRIUM + Remove Contributors; GLYCOPYRROLATE/ATROPINE for TERMINAL SECRETIONS + Repositioning + Family Education; Low-Dose MORPHINE for DYSPNEA; LORAZEPAM for Anxiety; BOWEL REGIMEN with ALL Opioids); CAREGIVER FATIGUE → RESPITE + Bereavement; CULTURAL + SPIRITUAL + Family-Centered Communication; LAST HOURS Education (Mottling + Cheyne-Stokes + Apnea + Declining Responsiveness — Family Knows What to Expect); RN SCOPE at Death (Pronouncement Varies by State); 13-Month BEREAVEMENT Per Medicare Hospice Benefit

patient.name: S. Whitfield
patient.code: DNR/DNI documented + signed POLST in chart (Allow Natural Death); comfort-focused care; family present + aligned with goals of care
patient.admitDate: Admitted to inpatient hospice unit at 1400 today; transitioned from 3 months of home hospice care due to refractory pain + nausea + new terminal delirium + caregiver fatigue (wife age 60 + adult daughter providing 24/7 care; daughter took FMLA from work)
patient.room: Inpatient Hospice Unit Bed 4 (private room with family bed + recliner + quiet environment + spiritual care + bedside courtyard window); family present at admission (wife, adult daughter, adult son flying in tonight)

vitals.time: Last home hospice visit yesterday afternoon
vitals.temp: 37.6°C; weight 48 kg (down from 78 kg pre-diagnosis 8 months ago = cachexia; baseline BMI 26 → current BMI 16); jaundiced sclera + skin; abdominal distension; bilateral ankle edema

vitals.spo2: 91% on RA (declined nasal cannula — gentle fan + repositioning instead per his prior wishes; SpO2 monitoring de-emphasized per comfort-focused care)
vitals.temp: 38.0°C (terminal fever — common; treat for comfort with acetaminophen if patient appears uncomfortable; cooling measures gently); mottling beginning at knees + lower legs
38.2°C; mottling extending up thighs; minimally responsive to voice (responds to deep touch with grimace); family at bedside; pain managed; secretions managed

labs.v: HOSPICE ELIGIBILITY (Medicare Hospice Benefit): life expectancy ≤6 months if disease runs natural course (certified by hospice medical director + attending); patient enrolled 3 months ago after declining 2nd-line chemotherapy (failed first-line FOLFIRINOX with disease progression at 6 months; offered gem-nab-pac 2nd line; declined further chemotherapy after discussion of toxicity vs minimal expected benefit + preference for quality time); GOALS OF CARE clarified through serial family meetings: COMFORT-FOCUSED CARE (Allow Natural Death — AND), no resuscitation/intubation/ICU transfer, no artificial nutrition/hydration unless explicitly for symptom relief (not life-extension), no antibiotics unless for comfort (e.g., UTI causing discomfort), focus on PAIN + SYMPTOM RELIEF + DIGNITY + FAMILY PRESENCE + SPIRITUAL/EMOTIONAL SUPPORT; signed POLST in chart with full DNR/DNI + Allow Natural Death; HEALTHCARE PROXY (wife) activated; advance directives reviewed at every transition; PROGNOSIS at admission to inpatient hospice: hours to days based on functional decline + vital trajectory + symptom burden + multi-organ involvement
labs.r: MEDICARE HOSPICE BENEFIT eligibility: certified life expectancy ≤6 mo if disease runs natural course; recertification at 90-90-60-60-day intervals + every 60 days indefinitely if eligibility persists. SERVICES INCLUDED: nursing + aide + social work + chaplain + bereavement + medical director + medications + equipment + supplies + 24/7 access + respite (5 days inpatient q month) + inpatient (general inpatient for acute symptom management OR continuous home care for crisis) + routine home care. GOALS OF CARE conversations are ONGOING + evolve as patient + family + disease progress. POLST translates wishes into medical orders that follow the patient across settings

labs.n: DIAGNOSIS: metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (head of pancreas, locally advanced, multiple liver mets + peritoneal carcinomatosis)
labs.v: 62M with metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PDAC) diagnosed 8 months ago after weight loss + jaundice + abdominal pain → CT showed pancreatic head mass with liver metastases + peritoneal carcinomatosis + CA 19-9 markedly elevated; EUS-FNA biopsy confirmed adenocarcinoma; staged IV; ECOG 4 (in bed, fully dependent — was ECOG 1 at diagnosis); progressed through FOLFIRINOX (4 cycles, disease progression); declined 2nd-line; enrolled in home hospice 3 months ago; biliary obstruction managed with biliary stent 5 months ago; gastric outlet obstruction not present (no NG/PEG; able to take small sips/bites by mouth recently — now declining); WEIGHT LOSS 30 kg over 8 months (~38% of body weight — cancer cachexia); JAUNDICE (T bili likely >15 — not currently labs-driven in hospice care); ABDOMINAL DISTENSION from ascites + organomegaly (paracentesis offered for comfort if symptomatic); BILATERAL ANKLE EDEMA (hypoalbuminemia + venous return + cachexia)
labs.r: PANCREATIC ADENOCARCINOMA (PDAC) is one of the most aggressive solid tumors. Median overall survival for metastatic disease: 6-12 months with chemotherapy; longer in select subgroups (BRCA-mutated → PARP inhibitors); shorter without treatment. CACHEXIA (involuntary weight loss + muscle wasting + anorexia) is multifactorial (tumor cytokines + reduced intake + malabsorption + metabolic derangement); not reversible with nutrition alone. Patient on standard end-of-life trajectory. Symptoms throughout course: pain (visceral + neuropathic from celiac plexus/retroperitoneal invasion), nausea (GI obstruction, gastroparesis, opioid-related, ICP from brain mets if present), jaundice + pruritus, ascites, fatigue, weight loss + cachexia, anxiety + depression + spiritual/existential distress

labs.n: PAIN MANAGEMENT — refractory pain requiring inpatient escalation
labs.v: PAIN at admission: 7/10 visceral abdominal pain (upper abdomen + back — classic pancreatic + retroperitoneal/celiac plexus invasion); NEUROPATHIC component (burning, shooting, allodynia in epigastric region); SOMATIC + ISCHEMIC bone pain from rib mets (recently identified by clinical exam); HOME REGIMEN: morphine extended-release 60 mg PO q8h + morphine immediate-release 15 mg PO/SL q1h PRN breakthrough (limited tolerance with declining PO intake + nausea); subcutaneous PCA initiated 1 week ago by hospice (morphine basal 4 mg/hr + 2 mg q15 min PRN breakthrough) — pain remained 6-8/10 with frequent breakthrough use; ADJUVANTS: gabapentin 300 mg PO TID for neuropathic (held due to nausea + reduced PO); methadone considered but not started; OPIOID-INDUCED CONSTIPATION addressed with senna + polyethylene glycol + methylnaltrexone PRN (no bowel movement x 4 days at admission — concern); INPATIENT ESCALATION: switched to subcutaneous hydromorphone PCA (rotated from morphine for renal-induced metabolite accumulation + improved efficacy; conversion ~5:1 morphine:hydromorphone subcutaneous so 4 mg/hr morphine ~= 0.8 mg/hr hydromorphone — adjusted for incomplete cross-tolerance starting at 0.5 mg/hr basal + 0.2 mg q15 min PRN); KETAMINE infusion 0.1 mg/kg/hr low-dose subcutaneous adjuvant for refractory pain + opioid-sparing (avoiding sedation/dysphoria with low-dose start); INTRATHECAL pump considered but not feasible given prognosis hours-days; CELIAC PLEXUS BLOCK considered but not now (would require procedure + prognosis too short); ROUND-THE-CLOCK acetaminophen 650 mg PR q6h (around-the-clock for fever + opioid-sparing); DEXAMETHASONE 4 mg IV/SQ daily continued (multi-purpose: bone mets pain + anorexia + nausea + fatigue + general well-being in cancer)
labs.r: PAIN MANAGEMENT in end-stage cancer follows WHO ANALGESIC LADDER + individualized approach. Most cancer pain controllable with appropriate regimen. PRINCIPLES: regular round-the-clock dosing + as-needed breakthrough; mechanism-based selection (opioids + adjuvants for nociceptive; gabapentin/pregabalin/duloxetine/TCAs for neuropathic; corticosteroids for bone/inflammation; bisphosphonates/radiation for bone mets); OPIOID ROTATION when one opioid not effective (cross-tolerance is incomplete — switch + reduce dose 25-50% then titrate); SUBCUTANEOUS route when oral not feasible (rapid uptake; reliable absorption; less burdensome than IV); PCA empowers patient + reduces delay; KETAMINE low-dose adjuvant for refractory pain; ALL OPIOIDS need a BOWEL REGIMEN (senna + osmotic + methylnaltrexone for opioid-induced refractory); intrathecal pump + celiac block + radiation for selected patients with longer prognosis; ACETAMINOPHEN round-the-clock + dexamethasone often added for multipurpose benefit. UNDERTREATING PAIN is a common error — be aggressive within comfort goals

labs.n: NAUSEA MANAGEMENT — mechanism-based (multi-mechanism nausea in this patient)
labs.v: NAUSEA at admission moderate (3-5/10 by patient report when able; family + RN observation: grimacing + retching + dry heaving); MULTIPLE MECHANISMS contributing: (1) OPIOID-INDUCED (chemoreceptor trigger zone CTZ activation; first 1-2 weeks of new opioid, with dose escalation, or with rotation); (2) GASTROPARESIS / mechanical (pancreatic mass + ascites + delayed gastric emptying); (3) METABOLIC (hyperbilirubinemia, possible uremia, hypercalcemia from bone mets — not labs-driven in hospice); (4) ANTICIPATORY/anxiety component; (5) INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE excluded (no brain mets known); MECHANISM-BASED ANTIEMETICS: HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg PO/SQ q6h scheduled + q4h PRN (CTZ blockade — best for opioid + metabolic nausea); METOCLOPRAMIDE 10 mg PO/SQ q6h (PROKINETIC — best for gastroparesis/mechanical; caution: contraindicated in complete bowel obstruction + EPS/QT risk); ONDANSETRON 4-8 mg PO/SQ q8h PRN (5HT3 — useful for chemo/radiation; less for end-of-life nausea but used for refractory); OLANZAPINE 2.5-5 mg PO/SL daily (broad-spectrum antiemetic + appetite stimulation + mood + sleep — particularly useful in end-of-life multi-mechanism nausea); DEXAMETHASONE 4 mg daily (continued for cerebral + ICP + multi-purpose); LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL/SQ q4h PRN (anticipatory + anxiety + sedation); GINGER + acupressure + small frequent meals (when tolerated) + cool foods + low-stimulus environment; AVOID PROCHLORPERAZINE (more sedating + EPS risk in elderly); avoid PROMETHAZINE in elderly (sedation + delirium + EPS)
labs.r: NAUSEA MANAGEMENT at end of life follows MECHANISM-BASED approach (Brooks/Twycross). VESTIBULAR nausea (motion-related) → meclizine/cyclizine/scopolamine. MECHANICAL/gastric stasis nausea → metoclopramide (prokinetic; avoid if complete obstruction) or domperidone. CHEMORECEPTOR TRIGGER ZONE (opioid + metabolic) → haloperidol (gold standard) or olanzapine (broad-spectrum). INCREASED ICP → dexamethasone + 5HT3 antagonist. ANTICIPATORY/anxiety → lorazepam. Multi-mechanism nausea (this patient) → combination therapy. AVOID promethazine + prochlorperazine in elderly (sedation/EPS/delirium). Reassess + titrate frequently. Address contributing factors (constipation, dehydration in some cases, anxiety, food preferences). Olanzapine + dexamethasone + haloperidol combination often effective

labs.v: DELIRIUM features developing past 48 hr: acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganized thinking, altered consciousness; hyperactive components (restlessness, picking at sheets, agitation, calling out for deceased mother) + hypoactive components (somnolence, withdrawal); CONTRIBUTING FACTORS: terminal disease progression (cerebral hypoperfusion + metabolic derangement), opioid metabolite accumulation in renal decline (rotated from morphine to hydromorphone for this), hypercalcemia from bone mets (no labs in hospice — empiric consideration), hypoxia, dehydration, urinary retention, fecal impaction, hyperbilirubinemia, ICU/hospital environment (single quiet room helps), benzodiazepines (paradoxical agitation in elderly — minimize but lorazepam useful for anxiety + sedation in dying), opioids (titration but appropriate analgesia improves delirium); MANAGEMENT: REMOVE contributors when possible (opioid rotation done, ensure bowel emptying, urinary catheter for retention, gentle reorientation, family at bedside, quiet familiar environment, soft lighting, no overhead lights at night, minimize line/tube tethering, music/familiar voices, spiritual rituals); HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg PO/SQ q6h scheduled + q2h PRN for agitation (start low + titrate; preferred first-line for terminal delirium per palliative guidelines; less anticholinergic than typical antipsychotics; minimal sedation at low doses); CHLORPROMAZINE 12.5-25 mg PO/PR/SQ q4-6h PRN if more sedation needed; RISPERIDONE 0.5 mg PO BID or QUETIAPINE 25-50 mg PO/SL BID alternative; LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL/SQ q4-6h PRN for anxiety/agitation (use cautiously — paradoxical effect possible); AVOID anticholinergics (worsen delirium); FAMILY EDUCATION that terminal delirium is part of dying process — not pain or suffering per se; reassurance + presence + familiar voice; family may try to engage rationally but acknowledge limited cognitive engagement; meaningful presence + touch + music more impactful than verbal redirection
labs.r: TERMINAL DELIRIUM is common (~80% of patients in last days of life) + frequently distressing for family. MULTIFACTORIAL etiology. MANAGEMENT principles: remove contributors when feasible, treat reversible causes (urinary retention, fecal impaction, hypercalcemia in some cases, oversedation), pharmacologic management with haloperidol (gold standard) + benzodiazepine for breakthrough agitation; family education + reassurance + presence are central; quiet familiar environment + soft lighting + minimal stimulation; avoid restraints (not appropriate in EOL); spiritual + cultural rituals supported. FAMILY often more distressed than patient — explain what is happening (the patient is not in pain; this is part of dying; they may seem to be 'seeing' or 'speaking with' people who are not present — this is a common end-of-life experience). REASSURANCE + presence

labs.v: AUDIBLE GURGLING/RATTLING secretions emerging this afternoon (4-6 hr after admission); caused by accumulation of saliva + bronchial secretions in oropharynx + upper airway as patient loses cough + swallow ability + becomes less responsive; FAMILY DISTRESS HIGH (sounds 'like he's drowning' or 'suffocating') — but patient generally NOT distressed because diminished consciousness; MANAGEMENT: REPOSITION to side-lying or semi-prone with HOB elevated (drainage); GENTLE oral suctioning ONLY if accessible secretions (avoid deep suctioning — distressing + minimally effective + causes more secretions); ANTICHOLINERGIC AGENTS to reduce secretion production (NOT to dry existing secretions): GLYCOPYRROLATE 0.2-0.4 mg SQ/IV q4-6h PRN (preferred — does not cross BBB so less central anticholinergic effects + delirium); ATROPINE 0.4 mg SQ/SL q4-6h (alternative — crosses BBB so more potential for delirium; sublingual 1% ophthalmic drops also used); SCOPOLAMINE 1.5 mg transdermal patch q72h (slower onset but sustained); HYOSCYAMINE 0.125 mg PO/SL q4h PRN; OPTIMIZE EARLY — anticholinergics work best at PREVENTING accumulation rather than drying existing; FAMILY EDUCATION is the central intervention — explain what is happening (secretions are pooling because he no longer has the strength to clear them; this sounds distressing but is NOT distressing TO HIM because he is not aware of it; we are doing what we can but cannot completely eliminate the sound; this is a normal part of the last hours); REDUCE IV fluids if applicable (excessive fluid can worsen secretions in dying patient); LISTEN to family + acknowledge their distress; provide explanation + reassurance + presence
labs.r: TERMINAL SECRETIONS occur in 35-90% of dying patients in last hours/days. MANAGEMENT pearls: (1) family distress is often greater than patient distress, (2) anticholinergic agents work best when started EARLY before significant accumulation, (3) deep suctioning is counterproductive (stimulates more secretions + distresses patient), (4) repositioning + gentle oral care + family education are central, (5) glycopyrrolate preferred over atropine for less CNS effects + delirium risk, (6) reduce IV fluids if contributing, (7) FAMILY EDUCATION about what is happening + why we cannot eliminate it completely + that patient is not aware — this is the most important nursing intervention

labs.n: DYSPNEA + RESPIRATORY support at end of life
labs.v: DYSPNEA at admission moderate (SpO2 91% on RA but patient declined nasal cannula per prior expressed wishes); Cheyne-Stokes breathing pattern emerging (irregular crescendo-decrescendo with apneic pauses — common in last hours, indicates brain stem changes); MANAGEMENT: LOW-DOSE MORPHINE 2-5 mg PO/SL/SQ q4h PRN (and as part of PCA continuous) — gold standard for dyspnea at end of life; mechanism: reduces ventilatory drive + reduces sensation of dyspnea + reduces anxiety + reduces preload (with cardiogenic dyspnea); NO RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION at appropriate doses in opioid-tolerant patient with appropriate titration; LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL/SQ q4-6h PRN for anxiety component of dyspnea (synergistic with opioid); FAN at low setting blowing across face (vagal stimulation reduces sensation of dyspnea — evidence-based + non-pharmacologic); POSITIONING (semi-Fowler or upright if tolerated); minimize physical exertion (commode at bedside if mobile; bedpan; assist with all ADLs); supplemental O2 only if HYPOXIC + perceived to help patient comfort (not target SpO2 — comfort-focused); BiPAP/CPAP/HFNC not indicated in comfort-focused care unless explicitly helping symptoms (which is rare in terminal decline); INTUBATION not indicated (DNI documented + counter to goals); ANXIOLYTICS + ENVIRONMENTAL adjustments + REASSURANCE; OPIOID overdose is RARE when titrated for symptom; double-effect principle: aggressive symptom control even if foreseeable but unintended hastening of death is ethically + legally permissible when intent is comfort
labs.r: DYSPNEA at end of life is common + distressing. LOW-DOSE OPIOID is the gold standard (morphine evidence-based for dyspnea; mechanism not fully understood but multi-modal). Opioid 'fear' (respiratory depression) is largely unfounded at appropriate doses in opioid-tolerant patients with appropriate titration. ETHICAL principle of DOUBLE EFFECT: aggressive symptom management with appropriate intent (comfort) is permissible even if it foreseeably but unintentionally hastens death; this is supported by ethics + law + religious traditions. NON-PHARMACOLOGIC adjuncts (fan, positioning, environment, presence) are evidence-based + complementary. COMFORT-FOCUSED care does not target SpO2 — only patient comfort. Cheyne-Stokes is common in last hours + does not require intervention beyond family education

labs.n: OTHER SYMPTOM management (constipation, anxiety, anorexia/cachexia, oral care, dry mouth, fatigue, skin care, pruritus)
