opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — outdated. 4F-PCC (Kcentra) is PREFERRED over FFP per current ACC/AHA + ASRA + ACS guidelines: RAPID (within 30 min vs hours for FFP thaw), LOW VOLUME (no fluid overload — critical in elderly + cardiac), COMPLETE factor replacement (II, VII, IX, X — FFP has variable + lower factor concentrations), FASTER INR correction. FFP is reserved for when 4F-PCC unavailable or combined coagulopathy needing fibrinogen + multiple factors.

opts.text: IDARUCIZUMAB (Praxbind) is the appropriate reversal agent for warfarin-associated life-threatening bleed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — drug-specific. IDARUCIZUMAB (PRAXBIND) is for DABIGATRAN (direct thrombin inhibitor) reversal — NOT warfarin. Warfarin reversal uses 4F-PCC + IV vitamin K. ANDEXANET ALFA (ANDEXXA) is for FXa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban) — NOT warfarin. KNOW THE SPECIFIC ANTICOAGULANT + use the SPECIFIC reversal agent. Substituting is ineffective.

opts.text: Patient should AVOID ALL vitamin K-rich foods (leafy greens, broccoli, etc.) while on warfarin because they will decrease the anticoagulant effect and increase stroke risk
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. The correct principle is DIET CONSISTENCY — consume CONSISTENT amounts of vitamin K-rich foods, NOT avoidance. Total avoidance is impractical + unhealthy + leads to wide INR fluctuations when patient inevitably eats greens occasionally. Warfarin dose is titrated to the patient's habitual diet — consistency allows stable INR. Tell patient: 'eat what you normally eat, just keep it consistent — don't drastically change.' Encourage healthy balanced diet with consistent vit K + fiber + nutrients.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at this presentation
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Acute Hemorrhagic Shock + Anticoagulant-Associated Bleed r/t supratherapeutic INR 8.4 + active UGIB; immediate reversal with 4F-PCC + IV vitamin K + HOLD warfarin; resuscitation with PRBC + crystalloid + 2 large-bore IVs + permissive hypotension SBP 90–100 until source control; lethal triad prevention; MTP if shock continues
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Life-threatening hemorrhagic shock + anticoagulant emergency. Time-sensitive reversal + resuscitation. Foundation for all subsequent care. WITHOUT immediate intervention, patient deteriorates rapidly. RN role: ABCDE + IV access + reversal administration + transfusion + monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: Source Control of Bleeding r/t Forrest IIa duodenal ulcer; EMERGENT EGD with endoscopic hemostasis (epinephrine + thermal + clips); pantoprazole infusion × 72 hr; CLO+ for H. pylori → treatment 14 days starting; IR + surgery on standby if rebleeding; repeat EGD in 6–8 weeks; NSAID cessation + smoking cessation
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Source control is essential — reversal + transfusion are bridges. Endoscopic hemostasis within 4 hr. Forrest IIa = high risk needing intervention. PPI + H. pylori treatment + NSAID cessation for recurrence prevention. Without source control, ongoing bleeding undoes resuscitation.

opts.text: Drug-Drug Interaction Recognition + Medication Safety r/t TMP-SMX precipitated INR shift in patient on warfarin without monitoring; system + patient + provider safety gap identified; medication reconciliation + pharmacy MTM + patient + caregiver education; HIGH-ALERT medication culture (ISMP); any new med while on warfarin = INR check 3–5 days
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Pharmacology + medication safety is the central teach point of this case. Recognition of drug-drug interaction + system gap. Pharmacy MTM + patient education to prevent recurrence. HIGH-ALERT medication culture. Foundation for prevention going forward.

opts.text: Anticoagulation Restart + Long-Term Management r/t CHA2DS2-VASc 6 high stroke risk + UGIB just controlled (rebleed risk) balance; restart 1–2 weeks once stable + H. pylori treatment initiated + endoscopy clear; SWITCH to APIXABAN (most favorable bleed profile per ARISTOTLE) consideration; PPI long-term; bridging not typically needed for AFib without recent stroke
opts.rat: #4 — Significant + ongoing. Restart timing + agent selection balances thrombosis vs rebleeding. SWITCH to apixaban for AFib after warfarin-associated bleed often appropriate (ARISTOTLE evidence). Mechanical valve patients cannot use DOAC. Shared decision-making + cardiology + GI. PPI long-term.

opts.text: Patient + Caregiver Education + Discharge Planning r/t drug-drug interactions especially antibiotics + diet consistency for vit K + bleeding signs to recognize + fall prevention + pill organizer + medical alert bracelet + pre-procedure communication + follow-up schedule + teach-back; multidisciplinary discharge
opts.rat: #5 — Foundational throughout + at discharge. Patient + caregiver engagement is foundational to safe long-term anticoagulation. Comprehensive education + teach-back + follow-up coordination + multidisciplinary discharge. Sets up safe transition + recurrence prevention.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient at presentation.

opts.text: HOLD WARFARIN + 4F-PCC (Kcentra) 50 units/kg IV (INR >6 dose) + IV vitamin K 10 mg SLOW over 30 min (sustained reversal via new factor synthesis 6–12 hr; AVOID rapid push — anaphylactoid risk); preferred over FFP (RAPID + LOW VOLUME + COMPLETE)
opts.rat: Life-threatening warfarin-associated bleed = 4F-PCC + IV vit K as first-line per current guidelines. RAPID + LOW VOLUME + COMPLETE reversal of factors II, VII, IX, X. Vit K for sustained reversal because PCC half-life shorter than warfarin. AVOID rapid IV vit K push.

opts.text: 2 large-bore (16-gauge) peripheral IVs + central line (right IJ triple lumen by ED MD with US guidance) + type & crossmatch 4 units PRBC + 4 units FFP + 1 platelet pack; emergency-release O-neg PRBC + crystalloid (LR) bolus + permissive hypotension SBP 90–100 until source control (no head injury); warm IVF + blood + blankets
opts.rat: Hemorrhagic shock resuscitation. Large-bore IV access + central line for resuscitation + reversal. Type & crossmatch + emergency-release for immediate transfusion need. Permissive hypotension until source control. Lethal triad prevention via warming.

opts.text: EMERGENT EGD within 24 hr (within 4 hr in this case) by GI; pre-EGD IV erythromycin 250 mg for gastric motility; IV pantoprazole 80 mg bolus + 8 mg/hr infusion × 72 hr; endoscopic therapy combined modality (epinephrine + thermal + clips); biopsies for H. pylori; IR + surgery on standby; repeat EGD 6–8 weeks
opts.rat: EGD source control essential. Within 24 hr (within 12 hr if unstable). Erythromycin prokinetic + PPI infusion + combined endoscopic therapy (more effective than single modality). H. pylori test + treat for recurrence prevention. IR + surgery on standby for refractory.

opts.text: Massive transfusion protocol if shock continues: 1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets ratio + calcium gluconate 1 g IV per 4 units (citrate hypocalcemia); platelet target >50k for procedure; PRBC for Hgb <8 stable or <9 cardiac disease/active bleed
opts.rat: Ratio-based resuscitation mimics whole blood + prevents dilutional coagulopathy. Calcium for citrate. Platelet + Hgb thresholds per evidence. Lethal triad prevention through 1:1:1 + calcium + warming.

opts.text: PATIENT + CAREGIVER education: drug-drug interactions especially antibiotics (TMP-SMX, fluconazole, metronidazole, amiodarone, ciprofloxacin, SSRIs, NSAIDs, omeprazole — ANY new med = INR check 3–5 days); diet CONSISTENCY for vit K-rich foods (NOT avoidance); bleeding signs to watch + report; fall prevention; pill organizer + reminders + caregiver check-in + pharmacist MTM; medical alert bracelet; pre-procedure communication; follow-up schedule
opts.rat: Comprehensive patient education foundational to safe anticoagulation. Teach-back method for verification. Daughter as primary caregiver + healthcare advocate engaged. HIGH-ALERT medication culture. Foundation for prevention going forward.

opts.text: ANTICOAGULATION restart plan: 1–2 weeks once stable + H. pylori treatment initiated + endoscopy clear; SWITCH to APIXABAN (most favorable bleed profile per ARISTOTLE — lower intracranial bleed + GI bleed vs warfarin); 5 mg BID (or 2.5 mg BID if 2 of 3: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, Cr ≥1.5); PPI long-term; shared decision-making with patient + daughter + cardiology
opts.rat: Anticoagulation restart with switch to apixaban often appropriate after warfarin-associated bleed in AFib patients. ARISTOTLE evidence. PPI long-term while on any anticoagulant. Shared decision-making.

opts.text: FFP 4 units as FIRST-LINE reversal for life-threatening warfarin-associated bleed; 4F-PCC is reserved for when FFP is unavailable
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — outdated. Current evidence + guidelines (ACC/AHA + ASRA + ACS) favor 4F-PCC (Kcentra) over FFP for life-threatening warfarin-associated bleeding: RAPID (within 30 min vs hours for FFP thaw), LOW VOLUME (no fluid overload — critical in elderly + cardiac), COMPLETE factor replacement (II, VII, IX, X). FFP reserved for when 4F-PCC unavailable or combined coagulopathy needing fibrinogen.

opts.text: IDARUCIZUMAB (Praxbind) 5 g IV to reverse the warfarin-associated bleed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — drug-specific. Idarucizumab is for DABIGATRAN (direct thrombin inhibitor) reversal — NOT warfarin. Warfarin reversal uses 4F-PCC + IV vitamin K. Substituting reversal agents is INEFFECTIVE. Know the SPECIFIC anticoagulant + use the SPECIFIC reversal agent.

opts.text: Restart warfarin at home dose tomorrow morning since the INR will be falling toward target with the reversal agents
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. HOLD WARFARIN — restart timing is INDIVIDUALIZED + occurs 1–2 weeks after bleed control + H. pylori treatment initiated + endoscopy clear. Premature restart = risk of recurrent bleeding. SWITCH to apixaban often appropriate for AFib. Shared decision-making with cardiology + GI. Patient + daughter educated.

opts.text: Continue ibuprofen for arthritis pain because the patient has documented chronic pain and stopping it would compromise her quality of life; the antiplatelet effect is mild compared to warfarin
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — NSAIDs are MAJOR additive bleeding risk + drug-drug interaction with warfarin via platelet effect + GI mucosal damage + protein displacement. STOP ALL NSAIDs permanently in this patient with PUD + UGIB + anticoagulation. Use acetaminophen ≤3 g/day or topical therapy (capsaicin, diclofenac topical), physical therapy, weight management, joint injections, glucosamine/chondroitin (variable evidence), or non-NSAID alternatives (duloxetine for OA pain). Patient education + alternative pain management is essential.

opts.text: Counsel patient to AVOID ALL leafy greens + cruciferous vegetables permanently to prevent warfarin failure
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. The correct principle is DIET CONSISTENCY — consume CONSISTENT amounts of vitamin K-rich foods, NOT avoidance. Total avoidance is impractical + unhealthy + leads to wide INR fluctuations. Warfarin dose is titrated to the patient's habitual diet — consistency allows stable INR. 'Eat what you normally eat, just keep it consistent — don't drastically change.' Healthy balanced diet with consistent vit K + fiber + nutrients.

opts.text: H. pylori treatment with PPI + amoxicillin 1 g PO BID + clarithromycin 500 mg PO BID × 14 days (per institutional sensitivities); confirm eradication 4–8 weeks post-treatment via urea breath test or stool antigen
opts.rat: H. pylori positive on biopsy = standard treatment with triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin) × 14 days; alternative quadruple therapy if local resistance concern. Eradication confirmation 4–8 weeks post-treatment. Critical for ulcer recurrence prevention.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at ED presentation
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: ABCDE + 2 large-bore IVs (16-gauge) + type & crossmatch + emergency-release O-neg PRBC + crystalloid bolus (LR 1L) + supplemental O2 + vital signs continuous + sent labs (CBC + coags + BMP + lactate + LFT + crossmatch sample); permissive hypotension target SBP 90–100 until source control (no head injury); warm IVF + blood + blankets
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Hemorrhagic shock requires immediate ABCDE + IV access + emergency blood + crystalloid + monitoring. Foundation for all subsequent care. Time-sensitive — every minute matters. RN role: ABCDE + IV access + blood activation + labs + monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: ADMINISTER REVERSAL: HOLD warfarin; 4F-PCC (Kcentra) 50 units/kg IV (INR >6 dose = ~3100 units for 62 kg patient) over 5 min via central line; IV vitamin K 10 mg in 50 mL D5W SLOW over 30 min (sustained reversal; AVOID rapid push — anaphylactoid risk); reassess INR q6h × 24 hr (rebound risk)
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Reversal is time-sensitive + life-saving. 4F-PCC preferred over FFP. IV vit K for sustained reversal. Frequent INR reassessment for rebound. Coordinated with resuscitation + source control.

opts.text: ACTIVATE EMERGENT EGD by GI (within 24 hr; within 4 hr in this case); pre-EGD IV erythromycin 250 mg for motility + IV pantoprazole 80 mg bolus + 8 mg/hr infusion; NG tube placed for stomach decompression + assessment (GI fellow); platelet target >50k for procedure; reversal coagulopathy + Hgb adequate before EGD
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Source control essential. EGD within 4 hr in this case. Pre-EGD prep + endoscopic therapy with combined modality. Without source control, ongoing bleeding undoes resuscitation.

opts.text: MEDICATION RECONCILIATION + drug-drug interaction recognition: identify TMP-SMX as warfarin potentiator; document; system + patient + provider safety gap; pharmacy MTM consult; PCP communication; cardiology + GI consults; comprehensive review of all current medications + OTC + supplements for additional interactions
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Recognition + safety system response. Pharmacy MTM + multidisciplinary consults + PCP communication. Foundation for prevention. Identify gap + close it + educate patient + family.

opts.text: PATIENT + CAREGIVER EDUCATION + discharge planning: drug-drug interactions especially antibiotics; diet consistency for vit K; bleeding signs; fall prevention; pill organizer + reminders + caregiver check-in + pharmacist MTM; medical alert bracelet; pre-procedure communication; follow-up schedule; teach-back method; written materials in patient's preferred language; daughter as primary caregiver engaged
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing + at discharge. Patient + caregiver engagement foundational to safe long-term anticoagulation. Comprehensive education + teach-back + follow-up coordination + multidisciplinary discharge. Sets up safe transition + recurrence prevention.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Post-EGD ICU admission 2200: HR 88 NSR, BP 118/68 MAP 85, Hgb 9.6 post-transfusion (3 PRBC + 2 FFP given), INR 1.6 post-4F-PCC + vit K (target <1.5 trending — repeat in 6 hr for rebound), lactate 1.8 normalizing, UOP 60 mL/hr improving, alert + oriented, pain controlled; endoscopic hemostasis successful (Forrest IIa duodenal ulcer with epi + thermal + 2 clips); CLO+ for H. pylori
opts.rat: Excellent post-reversal + post-source-control trajectory. Hemodynamic stability + Hgb improvement + INR target approaching + lactate normalizing + UOP recovering + mentation preserved. Standard expected response to appropriate management. Foundation for ongoing recovery.

opts.text: Hospital Day 2 morning: HR 82 NSR (2 brief AFib runs overnight self-converted — paroxysmal pattern preserved); BP 126/72 MAP 90; SpO2 97% RA (weaned); Hgb stable 9.4; INR 1.4 at target maintained; melena resolved; tolerating sips clear liquids; cardiology consulted for AFib + anticoagulation restart with apixaban switch consideration; H. pylori treatment 14 days starting today
opts.rat: Excellent continued recovery. INR target maintained. Bleeding resolved. Tolerating PO. AFib paroxysmal pattern preserved (not new-onset). Cardiology engaged for anticoagulation restart + apixaban switch. H. pylori treatment initiated for recurrence prevention. Standard trajectory.

opts.text: Hospital Day 3: continues stable on telemetry floor; tolerating soft diet; INR 1.3; Hgb stable 9.4; daughter at bedside; PT consulted for fall prevention + balance training; pharmacy MTM consult completed with patient + daughter education on drug interactions + medical alert bracelet ordered + pill organizer set up; discharge planning for SNF rehab × 1 week vs home with home health
opts.rat: Standard discharge planning trajectory. PT + pharmacy MTM + caregiver education + practical tools (pill organizer + medical alert). Foundation for safe transition. SNF for short rehab vs home health based on functional assessment + daughter capacity to support.

opts.text: Hospital Day 1 evening 2000 (1 hr post-EGD): patient develops new hematemesis ~150 mL bright red + BP drops 118/68 → 92/54 + HR rises 88 → 110 + repeat Hgb 7.8 (from 9.6); REBLEEDING; GI activated + repeat emergent EGD + IR on standby; PPI infusion continued + additional PRBC + crystalloid + reassess platelet + coag status
opts.rat: REBLEEDING after endoscopic hemostasis (10–20% incidence within 72 hr — close monitoring is why ICU is appropriate). REPEAT EGD first-line; IR embolization if endoscopic fails; surgery if IR fails. RN role: recognize early + activate team + maintain resuscitation + escalate. Standard expected complication that requires escalation.

opts.text: Hospital Day 2 (12 hr post-reversal): INR 2.4 (rebound from 1.4 nadir — PCC half-life shorter than warfarin); additional IV vitamin K 5 mg given over 30 min; INR rechecked at 6 hr after dose = 1.6 (improved); continued q6h INR monitoring; warfarin restart deferred
opts.rat: REBOUND INR is the expected risk after 4F-PCC if vit K dose inadequate (PCC half-life ~12 hr; warfarin half-life ~40 hr → without vit K, anticoagulant effect returns). RECHECK INR q6h × 24 hr is standard. Additional vit K given. RN role: recognize rebound + administer additional vit K + escalate. Standard expected risk.

opts.text: Discharge Day 5: stable on telemetry floor; tolerating soft diet; INR 1.2; Hgb stable 9.6; cardiology meeting completed — patient + daughter elect to SWITCH to APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID instead of warfarin restart (lower bleed profile per ARISTOTLE + no INR monitoring + no dietary restriction); start apixaban 2 weeks post-discharge (today + 2 weeks) once H. pylori treatment complete + 4-week PPI established + outpatient follow-up confirmed; PPI continued long-term; written discharge instructions + caregiver teach-back + medical alert bracelet on; daughter primary caregiver
opts.rat: Excellent discharge trajectory. SHARED DECISION-MAKING with cardiology + patient + daughter resulting in apixaban switch (favorable per ARISTOTLE). PPI long-term. H. pylori treatment + 4-week PPI before apixaban start (recurrence prevention). Comprehensive caregiver education + teach-back + practical tools. Foundation for safe long-term management.

opts.text: Outpatient follow-up at 4 weeks: H. pylori treatment completed × 14 days; urea breath test negative (eradication confirmed); apixaban started 2 weeks ago without complications; PPI continued daily; no recurrent bleeding; INR no longer monitored (apixaban does not require monitoring); cardiology + GI + PCP follow-up all engaged; daughter reports patient is doing well + pill organizer + reminder system working + medical alert bracelet on
opts.rat: Excellent 4-week outcome. H. pylori eradication confirmed. Apixaban without complications. PPI continued. Multidisciplinary follow-up. Practical tools working. Daughter engaged. Standard expected positive trajectory.

opts.text: Hospital Day 1 evening: RN administers FFP 4 units instead of 4F-PCC because 'FFP is what we have always used and is more widely available'; patient develops fluid overload + pulmonary edema + hypoxia requiring high-flow NC + diuresis + careful management; INR correction slower than expected; bleeding ongoing requiring additional transfusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG choice. FFP is INFERIOR to 4F-PCC for warfarin reversal: SLOW (hours for thaw vs minutes for PCC), LARGE VOLUME (up to 1L for 4 units → fluid overload risk especially in elderly + cardiac), INCOMPLETE factor replacement (variable + lower factor concentrations), TRALI risk, ABO compatibility required. Current guidelines (ACC/AHA + ASRA + ACS) favor 4F-PCC. This is a system + provider knowledge gap. The patient's fluid overload + slower INR correction + ongoing bleeding are direct consequences. RN advocacy + institutional protocols + provider education needed.

opts.text: Hospital Day 2 morning: RN identifies that warfarin order has been re-entered on the chart as 5 mg daily starting today (without provider intent — order entered in error during reconciliation); RN catches the error before any administration + escalates to provider + medication safety system + ISMP-style HIGH-ALERT medication double-check protocol initiated; near-miss reported per safety culture
opts.rat: EXCELLENT RN advocacy + medication safety culture. HIGH-ALERT medication double-check + barcode scanning + RN catch + escalation + near-miss reporting are critical safety practices. ANTICOAGULANTS are among ISMP highest-risk medications. RN role in safety + advocacy + institutional protocols. This is the protective trajectory enabled by safety culture.

opts.text: 6-month follow-up: patient doing well on apixaban + PPI + daughter primary caregiver; no recurrent bleeding; H. pylori eradication confirmed; AFib stable on metoprolol (no new strokes per cardiology); pill organizer + daughter check-in + pharmacist MTM + medical alert + pre-procedure communication all working; cardiology + GI + PCP follow-up at scheduled intervals; patient cognitively stable; quality of life preserved
opts.rat: Excellent long-term outcome. Demonstrates value of: appropriate acute management + DOAC switch + H. pylori treatment + PPI + multidisciplinary follow-up + caregiver engagement + practical safety tools + patient education. No recurrent stroke (anticoagulation effective) + no recurrent bleed (DOAC + PPI + H. pylori treated + NSAIDs stopped). Standard ideal trajectory.

title: Smear-Positive Pulmonary Tuberculosis Unmasked in 54-Year-Old Homeless Shelter Resident Initially Admitted as Community-Acquired Pneumonia: Late Recognition with Airborne Exposure of 11 Staff + 3 Roommates + ED Contacts, N95 Fit-Test + Negative-Pressure Isolation, 4-Drug RIPE Therapy with DOT Planning, Hepatotoxicity Surveillance, Public-Health Reporting, and Contact Tracing per CDC/CDPH
subtitle: RISK-FACTOR RECOGNITION (homeless shelter residence + chronic cough >3 wk + weight loss + night sweats + hemoptysis + HIV testing offered) — these cues should trigger AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS on ADMISSION not after CXR/sputum results; UPPER-LOBE CAVITARY infiltrate on CXR is the classic but NOT mandatory finding; SPUTUM AFB SMEAR × 3 separate specimens 8 hr apart with at least 1 EARLY MORNING + NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test — MTB/RIF Xpert) for rapid diagnosis + rifampin-resistance detection; INDUCED sputum if not productive; AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS = N95 respirator (FIT-TESTED + seal-checked at every donning) + negative-pressure airborne infection isolation room (AIIR) with ≥12 air changes/hr + HEPA filtration + door CLOSED; surgical mask on PATIENT when leaving room for procedures (NEVER N95 on patient — N95 protects WEARER); patient must be ESCORTED + radiology/procedural area notified ahead; ALL VISITORS in N95 + minimized; EXPOSURE INVESTIGATION (staff TST baseline + at 8–10 weeks; treat LTBI if conversion — INH 9 mo or rifapentine + INH 3 mo 3HP regimen; symptom screening); 4-DRUG RIPE INITIAL THERAPY (RIFAMPIN + ISONIAZID + PYRAZINAMIDE + ETHAMBUTOL) × 2 months → INH + RIF × 4 more months (drug-susceptible TB total 6 months); PYRIDOXINE (vitamin B6) 25–50 mg daily with INH to prevent neuropathy (especially in alcohol use + diabetes + pregnancy + HIV + malnutrition); HEPATOTOXICITY surveillance (baseline LFTs + symptoms — anorexia + nausea + jaundice + RUQ pain; AST/ALT ≥3× ULN with symptoms OR ≥5× ULN without = stop hepatotoxic meds + workup; restart sequentially after recovery); OPHTHALMOLOGIC baseline + monthly visual acuity + color vision for ETHAMBUTOL (optic neuritis); URIC ACID + joint exam for PYRAZINAMIDE; ORANGE secretions warning for RIFAMPIN (urine, tears, sweat, contact lenses); RIFAMPIN drug-drug interactions (potent CYP3A4 inducer — REDUCES warfarin/OCs/methadone/protease inhibitors/seizure meds — STERLING list); PUBLIC HEALTH REPORTING (TB is reportable disease — CDPH form within 24 hr in CA; CDPH coordinates contact tracing + DOT + housing); DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) by health department to ensure adherence + prevent MDR-TB emergence (skipped doses = resistance); HOUSING + social determinants — homeless TB management requires CDPH housing voucher + shelter coordination + isolation-capable shelter; ISOLATION DURATION (3 consecutive negative AFB smears 8 hr apart while on effective therapy minimum 2 weeks + clinical improvement); HIV CO-INFECTION testing OFFERED on admission (TB-HIV syndemic; HIV positive accelerates TB + alters presentation + treatment); LATENT TB INFECTION (LTBI) treatment differs (INH 9 mo OR 3HP rifapentine + INH OR 4 mo rifampin — single-agent because lower bacillary burden — no resistance risk)

patient.name: R. Owens
patient.code: Full code; no advance directives on file; alert + decisional capacity present for healthcare decisions; CDPH involvement for housing + DOT will be coordinated post-discharge
patient.admitDate: Admitted from ED 0245 as community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) with cough + fever + weight loss → started on IV ceftriaxone + azithromycin per CAP pathway → roomed in semi-private room 412 with 1 roommate (Mr. K, 68 y/o post-op day 1 colectomy) on standard precautions; Day 2 morning CXR formal read = UPPER-LOBE CAVITARY infiltrate raising TB suspicion → ID + pulmonary consult activated → AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS + negative-pressure isolation room (AIIR) Bed 7 activated immediately + sputum AFB smear × 3 + NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) sent → first AFB smear POSITIVE (3+) at 2200 Day 2 → second smear POSITIVE (3+) Day 3 morning → NAAT POSITIVE for M. tuberculosis with RIFAMPIN-SUSCEPTIBLE result → 4-drug RIPE therapy initiated → exposure investigation activated by Infection Prevention + Employee Health + CDPH
patient.room: Negative-pressure Airborne Infection Isolation Room (AIIR) Bed 7 (≥12 air changes/hr + HEPA filtration + door closed + signage + anteroom for PPE donning/doffing; transferred from semi-private Bed 412 at 0930 Day 2 immediately upon TB suspicion from radiology read; original semi-private Bed 412 + roommate area terminal-cleaned + roommate Mr. K moved to private room + monitored as TB contact)

vitals.temp: 38.6°C; weight 58 kg (BMI 17.9 — underweight per patient stated 'lost ~15 kg over past 6 months'); pale + diaphoretic + thin + appears chronically ill; productive cough with scant blood-tinged sputum × past 2 weeks per patient; reports night sweats requiring change of shirt × past 6 weeks; fatigue + decreased appetite; lives at homeless shelter × 14 months; previously did seasonal agricultural work; sober from alcohol × 8 months

vitals.time: Admission to medicine Bed 412 (semi-private) at 0245 Day 0
vitals.temp: 38.2°C (post-acetaminophen); STANDARD precautions only (admitted as CAP); IV ceftriaxone 1 g + azithromycin 500 mg given per CAP pathway; sputum cultures sent (routine bacterial + fungal); CXR ordered + initial wet read = 'right upper lobe infiltrate consistent with pneumonia'; ROOMMATE Mr. K in adjacent bed (post-op day 1 colectomy on enhanced recovery pathway); 4 RNs + 2 RTs + 1 phlebotomist + 1 CNA + 1 housekeeper + transport + 2 PT/OT + radiology tech in close contact during admission process

vitals.time: Day 2 morning 0700 — formal radiology read (TB suspicion identified)
vitals.temp: 37.8°C; formal CXR read by attending radiologist = 'UPPER-LOBE CAVITARY infiltrate with possible miliary pattern in right upper + middle lobes; differential includes tuberculosis, atypical pneumonia, fungal infection, malignancy — recommend AFB sputum + NAAT + airborne precautions pending evaluation'; ID + pulmonary consult activated immediately; AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS initiated at 0830 Day 2 with TRANSFER to negative-pressure AIIR Bed 7 at 0930 Day 2; sputum AFB smear × 3 + NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) sent; INFECTION PREVENTION + EMPLOYEE HEALTH + CDPH notified at 0915 Day 2; HIV testing offered + accepted (results pending — non-reactive at 0 + 4 weeks per protocol)

vitals.time: Day 2 evening 2200 — first AFB smear POSITIVE (3+)
vitals.temp: 37.4°C; AFB smear POSITIVE 3+ (≥10 bacilli per HPF); second smear pending; NAAT result pending; PATIENT in AIIR with airborne precautions; 4-drug RIPE therapy prepared but starting following morning per ID protocol (avoid hepatotoxicity with rapid initiation in acutely ill patient + obtain baseline LFTs + uric acid + vision + weight + creatinine first; HIV ruled out via 4th-generation EIA non-reactive); ROOMMATE Mr. K transferred to private room + monitored as TB contact with baseline TST + symptom screen + chest X-ray; CDPH coordinated; EXPOSURE INVESTIGATION expanding (ED contacts + admission shift staff + roommate + transport + radiology + housekeeping + visitors)

vitals.time: Day 3 morning 0700 — NAAT result + 4-drug RIPE initiated
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; second AFB smear POSITIVE 3+; NAAT POSITIVE for M. tuberculosis complex with RIFAMPIN-SUSCEPTIBLE result (rules out MDR-TB at rifampin level — final susceptibilities pending culture growth × 6 weeks); baseline LFTs (AST 28, ALT 32, alk phos 110, T bili 0.8 — normal), uric acid 4.8, ophthalmology consult for ETHAMBUTOL baseline (visual acuity + color vision baseline normal), creatinine 0.9; 4-DRUG RIPE THERAPY initiated 0900 Day 3: ISONIAZID 300 mg PO daily + RIFAMPIN 600 mg PO daily + PYRAZINAMIDE 25 mg/kg = 1500 mg PO daily + ETHAMBUTOL 15 mg/kg = 900 mg PO daily; PYRIDOXINE (vit B6) 50 mg PO daily to prevent INH neuropathy; PATIENT receptive to therapy + understands DOT + duration

vitals.time: Day 7 (1 week into therapy, hospital day 8) — improving
vitals.temp: afebrile × 36 hr; weight 59 kg (gained 1 kg); cough decreased; sputum AFB smear at Day 7 still POSITIVE 2+ (early — expected to take 2+ weeks of effective therapy + 3 consecutive negative smears to confirm de-isolation); patient tolerating RIPE without hepatotoxicity (LFTs Day 7: AST 35, ALT 42, alk phos 115, T bili 0.9 — no clinically significant change); no visual changes; orange urine + tears noted as expected with rifampin (patient educated previously); CDPH housing assistance + DOT plan in progress for post-discharge transition to isolation-capable shelter with daily DOT visits by public health nurse

labs.n: PRESENTATION + risk-factor recognition (the missed initial cues)
labs.v: 54 y/o male R. Owens admitted ED 0145 Day 0 with 2 weeks productive cough with scant blood-tinged sputum + 6 weeks night sweats requiring shirt change + 6 months ~15 kg weight loss + decreased appetite + chronic fatigue. PMH: prior heavy alcohol use disorder (sober × 8 months — completed inpatient + sober living), former IDU (clean × 4 years), no chronic medical conditions per patient; no PCP × 5+ years; last medical contact = ED visit 3 years ago for trauma; no childhood records available; place of birth = US (Texas, raised on agricultural farm; per CDC TB epidemiology US-born cases are minority but homeless + alcohol use disorder + IDU + agricultural work = recognized risk factors). HOMELESS × 14 months (currently at large urban homeless shelter — multi-occupancy dorm style); SOCIAL DETERMINANTS — food insecurity, transportation limited, no PCP, episodic ED care, intermittent housing stability; sober × 8 months with AA + sober living completed; supportive case manager at shelter; no health insurance currently (Medi-Cal application in progress; emergency Medi-Cal eligible). MISSED INITIAL CUES (case learning): chronic cough >3 wk + weight loss + night sweats + hemoptysis + HOMELESS SHELTER residence + history of substance use + lack of regular medical care = ALL CLASSIC TB RISK FACTORS that SHOULD HAVE TRIGGERED AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS ON ADMISSION pending CXR/sputum results — not after radiology read 30 hr later. EXAM at admission: thin + chronically ill-appearing; mild RUQ + RUL crackles + decreased breath sounds; no other localizing signs; oriented x3; appropriate mood; no focal neurological deficits; mild hepatomegaly without jaundice (later workup ruled out hepatitis); peripheral lymphadenopathy not enlarged. IMAGING: ED CXR wet read 0200 = 'right upper lobe infiltrate consistent with pneumonia' (initial read missed cavitation); formal read Day 2 morning 0700 by attending radiologist = 'UPPER-LOBE CAVITARY infiltrate with possible miliary pattern; differential includes tuberculosis, atypical pneumonia, fungal infection, malignancy — recommend AFB sputum + NAAT + airborne precautions pending evaluation.' INITIAL DIAGNOSIS = community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) → IV ceftriaxone + azithromycin + standard precautions + semi-private room 412 with roommate Mr. K (post-op colectomy). EXPOSURE WINDOW: from ED triage 0145 Day 0 → AIIR transfer 0930 Day 2 = approximately 32 hours of ongoing infectious aerosol exposure in standard precautions setting; AT LEAST 11 staff (4 RNs + 2 RTs + 1 phlebotomist + 1 CNA + 1 housekeeper + transport + 2 PT/OT + radiology tech) + 3 roommates (Mr. K + 2 ED bay neighbors) + intermittent visitors had close contact without N95.
labs.r: MISSED RECOGNITION on admission is the central teach point — chronic cough + weight loss + night sweats + hemoptysis + homeless + substance use + no PCP = high-risk constellation for TB warranting AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS pending CXR/sputum even without confirmed diagnosis. CDC + CDPH guidelines recommend EMPIRIC AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS for any patient with suspicion for active pulmonary TB until ruled out (3 negative AFB smears + clinical resolution). The 32-hour exposure window in standard precautions setting = significant institutional safety event triggering full exposure investigation + employee + roommate contact tracing + lessons-learned + protocol review. RN role: recognize high-risk cues + ADVOCATE for airborne precautions even before confirmed diagnosis.

labs.n: DIAGNOSIS — AFB smear + NAAT + culture + susceptibility
labs.v: DIAGNOSTIC pathway for suspected pulmonary TB: (1) THREE sputum specimens collected ≥8 hr apart with at least 1 EARLY MORNING specimen — sent for AFB smear + NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) + culture + drug susceptibility; (2) INDUCED SPUTUM with hypertonic saline if patient not productive (RT-led, in negative-pressure room, full PPE — generates aerosols → highest exposure risk); (3) BRONCHOSCOPY with BAL if induced sputum unavailable/non-diagnostic (also aerosol-generating); (4) GASTRIC ASPIRATE in young children who cannot produce sputum (3 consecutive early-morning specimens via NG). AFB SMEAR (acid-fast bacilli microscopy with Ziehl-Neelsen or auramine-rhodamine stain) — RAPID (results 1–24 hr) + LOW sensitivity (≥10,000 bacilli/mL needed to be positive) + LOW specificity (cannot distinguish MTB from NTM — non-tuberculous mycobacteria). GRADING: 0 (none), 1+ (rare), 2+ (few), 3+ (numerous), 4+ (innumerable) bacilli per HPF. NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test — XPERT MTB/RIF) — RAPID (2 hr) + HIGH sensitivity (~99% in smear-positive; ~75% in smear-negative culture-positive) + HIGH specificity for MTB + simultaneous detection of RIFAMPIN resistance via rpoB gene mutation (proxy for MDR-TB) — STANDARD of CARE for initial diagnosis per CDC + WHO since 2010+. AFB CULTURE — GOLD STANDARD (60–90% sensitivity) + slow (2–8 weeks growth on LJ medium or 1–3 weeks on liquid MGIT) + provides DRUG SUSCEPTIBILITY (resistance to INH, RIF, PZA, EMB + second-line drugs). LINE PROBE ASSAY (LPA) — molecular susceptibility testing for INH + RIF resistance (rapid 1–2 days). THIS PATIENT: first AFB smear 3+ at 2200 Day 2; second AFB smear 3+ Day 3 AM; NAAT POSITIVE for M. tuberculosis complex with RIFAMPIN-SUSCEPTIBLE result (rules out MDR-TB at rifampin level — final phenotypic susceptibilities pending culture growth × 6 weeks); culture pending; LPA pending. SMEAR-POSITIVE 3+ = HIGHLY INFECTIOUS (highest infectiousness corresponds to AFB smear burden + cavitary disease + cough); de-isolation requires 3 consecutive NEGATIVE AFB smears 8 hr apart while on effective therapy ≥2 weeks + clinical improvement.
labs.r: DIAGNOSTIC pathway for suspected TB: AFB smear × 3 + NAAT for rapid diagnosis + culture for susceptibility. NAAT is current standard for initial rapid diagnosis. AFB smear grade correlates with infectiousness. Smear-positive = HIGHLY INFECTIOUS. De-isolation requires 3 negative AFB smears + effective therapy + clinical improvement. AEROSOL-GENERATING procedures (induced sputum, bronchoscopy) require negative-pressure room + N95.

labs.v: AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS for suspected/confirmed pulmonary or laryngeal TB: (1) NEGATIVE-PRESSURE AIRBORNE INFECTION ISOLATION ROOM (AIIR) — engineering control with ≥12 air changes per hour (existing facilities ≥6 acceptable but ≥12 preferred) + DIRECTED AIRFLOW (negative pressure relative to corridor) + HEPA-FILTERED exhaust or external exhaust + DOOR KEPT CLOSED + anteroom for PPE donning/doffing if available + private room (single occupancy); (2) N95 RESPIRATOR for all HCW + visitors entering room — FIT-TESTED + SEAL-CHECKED at every donning (positive + negative pressure check); PAPR (powered air-purifying respirator) for those who cannot fit-test or beard/facial hair interferes with N95 seal; SURGICAL MASK is NOT adequate for TB (does not filter respirable particles); (3) MINIMIZE entries — coordinate care + batch interventions when possible; (4) PATIENT wears SURGICAL MASK when LEAVING room for unavoidable procedures (radiology, OR — coordinated transport with destination ready + door closed during transit; N95 is NEVER on the patient — N95 protects the wearer from inhaled aerosols, patient with surgical mask contains source); (5) LIMIT VISITORS + screen for symptoms + provide N95 + fit-test + brief education; (6) SIGNAGE on door indicating airborne precautions + door closed; (7) STAFF EDUCATION + competency on PPE donning/doffing; (8) NO COHORTING with non-TB patients; (9) HOUSEKEEPING use full PPE for terminal cleaning post-discharge — environmental disinfection per institutional protocol; UV-C disinfection adjunct in some facilities; (10) AEROSOL-GENERATING PROCEDURES (induced sputum, bronchoscopy, intubation, suctioning, CPR, nebulizer treatments, BiPAP, sputum collection) — performed in AIIR with N95 + minimal personnel + room cleared post-procedure for at least 99% airflow exchange (~30+ min at 12 ACH). PPE DONNING SEQUENCE: hand hygiene → gown (if contact + airborne combined) → respirator (N95 with seal check) → eye protection (goggles or face shield) → gloves; PPE DOFFING SEQUENCE: gloves → gown → hand hygiene → eye protection → respirator (REMOVE OUTSIDE the room AFTER door closed — IMPORTANT) → hand hygiene. SURGICAL MASK vs N95: SURGICAL MASK protects others from wearer (source control — patient with TB wears surgical mask when leaving room); N95 protects WEARER from inhaled aerosols (HCW/visitor wears N95 when entering AIIR). REUSE per institutional policy. SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS: bearded HCW must use PAPR; pregnant HCW per institutional risk-stratification; HCW with immunosuppression should not enter if alternative staffing available.
labs.r: AIRBORNE precautions = AIIR + N95 + door closed + minimize entries + patient surgical mask if leaving room. NEVER place N95 on patient — wrong protection direction. NEVER use surgical mask alone for HCW entering AIIR. PPE donning/doffing competency essential. Aerosol-generating procedures require coordinated minimal-personnel + AIIR + post-procedure clearance time.

labs.n: 4-DRUG RIPE therapy — initial intensive phase + continuation
labs.v: STANDARD 6-MONTH REGIMEN for drug-susceptible pulmonary TB (per CDC/ATS/IDSA): (1) INTENSIVE PHASE × 2 months = 4 drugs RIPE — RIFAMPIN (R) + ISONIAZID (I) + PYRAZINAMIDE (P) + ETHAMBUTOL (E); (2) CONTINUATION PHASE × 4 months = 2 drugs HR — ISONIAZID + RIFAMPIN. TOTAL = 6 months for drug-susceptible TB; LONGER (9–12 months or more) for cavitary disease with positive 2-month culture (predicts relapse risk), CNS TB (9–12 mo), bone/joint TB (6–9 mo), miliary TB (9–12 mo), HIV-associated TB (6–9 mo + ART). DOSING (adults, drug-susceptible): INH 5 mg/kg daily (max 300 mg) or 15 mg/kg 3× weekly (max 900 mg); RIF 10 mg/kg daily (max 600 mg) or 10 mg/kg 3× weekly (max 600 mg); PZA 25 mg/kg daily (max 2000 mg) or 50 mg/kg 3× weekly (max 4000 mg); EMB 15 mg/kg daily (max 1600 mg) or 30 mg/kg 3× weekly (max 2400 mg). PYRIDOXINE (vitamin B6) 25–50 mg PO daily WITH INH to prevent peripheral neuropathy (essential in alcohol use disorder, diabetes, pregnancy, HIV, malnutrition, age >50; recommended for all patients in some protocols). DRUG-SPECIFIC ADVERSE EFFECTS + monitoring: (1) ISONIAZID — peripheral neuropathy (preventable with pyridoxine), HEPATOTOXICITY (most concerning — monitor LFTs + symptoms), HYPERSENSITIVITY (rash, fever), CNS effects (psychosis rare); (2) RIFAMPIN — HEPATOTOXICITY, hypersensitivity (flu-like syndrome), thrombocytopenia rare, RED-ORANGE secretions (urine + tears + sweat + permanently stains contact lenses — patient EDUCATION REQUIRED before first dose), POTENT CYP3A4/2C9/1A2 INDUCER (reduces effect of warfarin, oral contraceptives, methadone, protease inhibitors, NNRTIs, hormonal therapies, seizure meds, immunosuppressants — STERLING reminder list); (3) PYRAZINAMIDE — HEPATOTOXICITY (highest risk among RIPE), HYPERURICEMIA + arthralgia/gout (uric acid checked + monitored — usually not requiring treatment unless symptomatic), photosensitivity, rash; (4) ETHAMBUTOL — OPTIC NEURITIS (decreased visual acuity + color vision red-green deficits + visual field defects — REVERSIBLE if caught early + drug stopped; ophthalmology baseline + monthly visual acuity + color vision; AVOID in young children who cannot report visual changes). HEPATOTOXICITY surveillance: baseline LFTs (AST + ALT + alk phos + T bili) + symptom screen at every visit (anorexia, nausea, vomiting, RUQ pain, jaundice, dark urine); REPEAT LFTs monthly OR sooner if symptoms; HOLD hepatotoxic drugs (INH + RIF + PZA) if AST/ALT ≥3× ULN WITH SYMPTOMS OR ≥5× ULN WITHOUT symptoms — workup for cause + sequential reintroduction after resolution. DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) — observation of each dose by trained healthcare worker (public health nurse, community DOT worker) — MANDATORY for selected patient populations (homeless, substance use, prior non-adherence, MDR-TB risk, pediatric); STRONGLY RECOMMENDED for all TB patients per CDC; ensures adherence + prevents resistance emergence; missed doses = single most important driver of MDR-TB development. VIDEO-DOT (vDOT) — emerging alternative with smartphone-based observation.
labs.r: 4-DRUG RIPE INTENSIVE × 2 mo + 2-DRUG HR CONTINUATION × 4 mo = 6-month standard for drug-susceptible TB. PYRIDOXINE with INH. Each drug has specific adverse effects requiring surveillance. HEPATOTOXICITY = most clinically significant + requires LFT monitoring + symptom screening. DOT is essential — missed doses drive MDR-TB. RN role: medication administration + adverse effect surveillance + patient education + DOT coordination with public health.

labs.n: PUBLIC HEALTH REPORTING + contact tracing + CDPH coordination
labs.v: TB is a MANDATORY REPORTABLE DISEASE per California Code of Regulations Title 17 §2500 — REPORT WITHIN 1 WORKING DAY (by phone or fax) for suspected or confirmed TB; WRITTEN report (CDPH 8554 or VRBIS electronic) within 7 days. Reports go to LOCAL HEALTH DEPARTMENT (LHD) → CDPH TB Control Branch → CDC National TB Surveillance System. WHAT TO REPORT: positive AFB smear or NAAT or culture + clinical TB diagnosis + suspected TB pending workup. CDPH coordinates: (1) CONTACT INVESTIGATION (high-priority contacts within 7 days) — household + close contacts assessed via TST or IGRA + symptom screen + chest X-ray; LTBI treatment offered for converters; active TB worked up for symptomatic contacts; (2) DOT — public health nurse or community DOT worker visits patient daily for medication observation; (3) HOUSING + SOCIAL DETERMINANTS — TB Control Program in some jurisdictions has emergency housing vouchers + isolation-capable shelter beds + food assistance + transportation; (4) CASE MANAGEMENT — coordinated by public health TB nurse with hospital ID + medical home; (5) TREATMENT COMPLETION verification; (6) MDR-TB consultation if drug resistance identified; (7) MEDICAL HOLD + court-ordered treatment authority in extreme cases of non-adherence (legal authority of LHD under §121360). EXPOSURE INVESTIGATION for this case: (a) IDENTIFY all contacts during infectious period (typically from 3 months prior to symptom onset until ≥2 weeks of effective therapy with clinical improvement); for this patient = 32 hours of hospital exposure pre-isolation + shelter contacts × 14 months; (b) CONCENTRIC RING approach — closest contacts first (roommate Mr. K + admission shift staff with direct close contact) → expanding rings (other shift staff + transport + radiology + visitors); (c) BASELINE TST or IGRA + symptom screen + chest X-ray for contacts; (d) REPEAT TST at 8–10 weeks (window period for conversion); (e) LTBI TREATMENT for converters — INH 9 months OR rifapentine + INH 3HP (12 weekly doses) OR rifampin 4 months — typically single-agent because of lower bacillary burden in LTBI = no resistance risk; (f) ACTIVE TB workup + treatment for symptomatic or smear-positive contacts; (g) PRIORITY for high-risk contacts (immunocompromised + pediatric + recent converters). HOSPITAL EXPOSURE: at least 11 staff + 3 roommates + intermittent visitors + ED triage exposure investigated by EMPLOYEE HEALTH + INFECTION PREVENTION + CDPH coordinated. SHELTER EXPOSURE: 14-month residency at homeless shelter — CDPH contact tracing at shelter (other residents + staff + visitors); shelter-based testing campaign coordinated.
labs.r: TB REPORTING is MANDATORY within 1 working day per state public health law. CONTACT INVESTIGATION led by CDPH/LHD. CONCENTRIC RING approach. BASELINE + 8–10 week repeat TST/IGRA for conversion. LTBI treatment for converters. DOT coordinated by CDPH. HOUSING + social support coordinated by TB Control. RN role: report promptly + facilitate contact identification + coordinate with public health + advocate for patient + housing/social support.

labs.v: HIV TESTING OFFERED to ALL patients with active TB (CDC + WHO recommendation) — TB-HIV are SYNDEMIC: HIV is the strongest known risk factor for active TB (10× lifetime risk LTBI→active in HIV vs 5–10% lifetime risk in non-HIV; 10% PER YEAR vs 10% LIFETIME); HIV accelerates TB progression + alters presentation (more often disseminated, lower-lobe, atypical CXR, more often smear-negative due to lower bacillary burden, more often extrapulmonary); TB is leading cause of death in HIV worldwide. HIV TESTING with patient consent (opt-out vs opt-in per institutional + state policy — CA has opt-out for ED testing; for TB patients HIV testing strongly encouraged but consent required). THIS PATIENT: 4th-generation EIA antigen/antibody test sent on admission; result NON-REACTIVE at 0 + repeated at 4 weeks for window period per protocol; HIV negative. IF HIV POSITIVE: (1) ART initiation timing — start TB therapy first then ART within 2 weeks for CD4 <50 or 8 weeks for CD4 ≥50 (per WHO + IDSA — earlier ART improves survival but delayed reduces IRIS risk); (2) DRUG-DRUG INTERACTIONS — RIFAMPIN is potent CYP3A4 inducer + REDUCES levels of protease inhibitors + NNRTIs + integrase inhibitors → use RIFABUTIN instead (less induction) or adjust ART regimen; consult ID + HIV specialist; (3) IMMUNE RECONSTITUTION INFLAMMATORY SYNDROME (IRIS) — paradoxical worsening of TB symptoms 1–6 weeks after ART initiation due to immune recovery; manage with continued TB therapy + corticosteroids if severe; (4) PROPHYLAXIS for opportunistic infections (PCP, MAC) per CD4 count; (5) LONGER TB treatment duration (9 months for HIV co-infection per some guidelines); (6) HIGHER risk of treatment failure + relapse + MDR-TB; (7) CD4 count + viral load monitoring. PATIENT EDUCATION + counseling + linkage to HIV care if positive; PARTNER notification + testing.
labs.r: HIV TESTING is offered to all TB patients — TB-HIV syndemic. HIV accelerates TB + alters presentation + treatment. If HIV+ = ART + TB therapy coordinated with attention to drug interactions (rifampin → rifabutin or ART adjustment) + IRIS risk + OI prophylaxis + longer TB treatment + intensified monitoring. RN role: facilitate testing + counseling + linkage to HIV care + adherence support + emotional support + confidentiality.

labs.v: DE-ISOLATION from airborne precautions in hospitalized smear-positive pulmonary TB: ALL THREE criteria required: (1) RECEIVING EFFECTIVE ANTI-TB therapy ≥2 weeks (some sources ≥3 weeks); (2) CLINICAL IMPROVEMENT — afebrile + decreased cough + improved appetite + weight stable/increasing; (3) THREE consecutive NEGATIVE AFB smears collected ≥8 hr apart with at least 1 EARLY MORNING specimen. DRUG-RESISTANT TB has stricter criteria (multi-drug-resistant = MDR; extensively drug-resistant = XDR) — typically requires NEGATIVE CULTURES (not just smear) + longer treatment duration. PATIENTS WITH LARYNGEAL TB or extensive cavitary disease may require longer isolation. DISCHARGE PLANNING for homeless TB patient: (1) HOUSING — CDPH TB housing voucher to ISOLATION-CAPABLE shelter (single room with door + no shared bathroom if possible + N95 supply + symptom monitoring) — coordinated by TB Control Program; alternative: TB-specific transitional housing; (2) DOT arrangement — public health nurse OR community DOT worker visits daily for observed dose; vDOT for select patients; (3) FOLLOW-UP — TB clinic appointments at 2 weeks + monthly + at completion; sputum AFB smears + cultures monthly until consistently negative; CXR at completion; LFTs monthly + symptom screen; vision + color vision monthly for EMB; (4) NUTRITION + food security — food bank referrals + SNAP application + medical food package; (5) TRANSPORTATION — bus passes + transportation vouchers; (6) PCP + HEALTHCARE HOME — connection to FQHC or community health center for primary care after TB treatment + ongoing health needs; (7) MEDI-CAL/insurance enrollment for ongoing healthcare; (8) MENTAL HEALTH + substance use disorder support — continued AA + sober living + counseling; (9) PATIENT EDUCATION + plain-language teach-back — medication purposes + adverse effects + when to seek help + DOT + duration + side effects + drug-food interactions; (10) MEDICAL ALERT card listing TB medications. NON-ADHERENCE risk + intervention: ESCALATING engagement (peer support → DOT → enhanced DOT → court-ordered treatment under §121360 in extreme cases — legal authority of LHD); MDR-TB risk from non-adherence is the greatest treatment failure mode. PATIENT ENGAGEMENT + dignity-preserving + culturally responsive care + trauma-informed approach (many homeless patients have trauma histories); social work + chaplain + peer support central.
labs.r: DE-ISOLATION = effective therapy ≥2 wk + clinical improvement + 3 negative smears. DISCHARGE PLANNING for homeless TB patient is complex + requires multi-agency coordination (CDPH + DOT + housing + nutrition + transportation + PCP + insurance + behavioral health). DOT is essential for adherence + MDR-TB prevention. PATIENT ENGAGEMENT + dignity + cultural responsiveness + trauma-informed care.

labs.n: LATENT TB INFECTION (LTBI) vs ACTIVE TB DISEASE — for contact converters
labs.v: DISTINCTION between LTBI and ACTIVE TB DISEASE is fundamental for contact investigation: LTBI = positive TST/IGRA + asymptomatic + normal CXR + no clinical disease (bacilli contained by immune system in granulomas — non-infectious + reactivation risk lifetime ~5–10%, higher with immunosuppression, HIV, diabetes, anti-TNF therapy, ESRD, recent infection); ACTIVE TB = positive TST/IGRA + symptomatic (cough, fever, weight loss, night sweats, hemoptysis) + abnormal CXR + AFB smear/NAAT/culture positive (infectious + requires multidrug therapy + isolation). TST (Mantoux PPD) — intradermal 5 TU PPD → read induration (NOT erythema) at 48–72 hr; cut-offs: ≥5 mm in HIV+/immunocompromised/recent close contact/CXR fibrosis; ≥10 mm in high-risk (homeless, IDU, healthcare workers, recent foreign-born, prisoners, diabetes, ESRD, gastrectomy, malignancy, children <4); ≥15 mm in low-risk; INTERPRETATION caveats — BCG vaccine can cause false-positive (less likely if remote); anergy in severe immunosuppression can cause false-negative; boosting effect (two-step PPD for HCW baseline). IGRA (interferon-gamma release assay — QuantiFERON-TB Gold + T-SPOT) — blood test detecting IFN-γ release upon MTB antigen stimulation; HIGH specificity (not affected by BCG) + similar sensitivity to TST; PREFERRED for BCG-vaccinated and patients unlikely to return for TST reading; results 24–48 hr. CXR for any positive TST/IGRA. LTBI TREATMENT options (after active disease ruled out): (1) ISONIAZID 9 months (300 mg daily; with pyridoxine 25–50 mg daily) — gold-standard but long duration → adherence challenges; (2) 3HP REGIMEN = rifapentine 900 mg + isoniazid 900 mg WEEKLY × 12 doses (3 months) — by DOT — shorter + better adherence than 9H; (3) 4R = rifampin 4 months (good for INH-resistant or intolerance); (4) 3HR = isoniazid + rifampin 3 months daily. SINGLE-AGENT therapy is appropriate for LTBI because lower bacillary burden = no resistance risk; ACTIVE TB requires multidrug therapy because high bacillary burden = resistance emergence risk if single-agent. CONTACT investigation for THIS PATIENT: BASELINE TST/IGRA + symptom screen + CXR for 11 staff + 3 roommates + visitors + shelter contacts; REPEAT at 8–10 weeks (window for conversion); CONVERTERS to active or LTBI = treat appropriately + CDPH coordinated.
labs.r: LTBI vs ACTIVE TB distinction is fundamental. LTBI = positive TST/IGRA + no symptoms + normal CXR + no infectious + treat with single-agent regimen. ACTIVE TB = symptoms + abnormal CXR + AFB/NAAT/culture positive + infectious + isolate + treat with multidrug. CONTACT investigation = baseline + 8–10 week repeat. LTBI treatment regimens shorter (3HP 12 weeks by DOT preferred for adherence).

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: HIV non-reactive on 4th-generation EIA + 4-week confirm; hepatitis B + C negative on serologies; rapid plasma reagin negative (syphilis); other STI testing negative; no diabetes (HbA1c 5.4); LFTs baseline normal (no underlying liver disease); creatinine 0.9 (normal renal function); no MDR-TB on rapid NAAT (rifampin-susceptible result rules out MDR at rifampin level; final susceptibilities pending); no extrapulmonary involvement on initial workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis pending); no CNS symptoms (no LP indicated); no bone/joint symptoms; no genitourinary symptoms (urine AFB sent + pending); no peritoneal/pericardial involvement; sober × 8 months with strong recovery program; patient receptive to therapy + DOT + understands duration + side effects + agreed to public health partnership; sober living capable shelter housing being coordinated; emergency Medi-Cal in progress; supportive case manager at shelter; no current substance use; no acute distress; tolerating PO; ambulatory (cleared from bed rest after Day 2 stabilization); no immediate life-threatening complications. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: rifampin-susceptible TB (rules out MDR at rifampin level — most concerning drug resistance); patient engagement + receptive to DOT + housing partnership; recovery support + sober × 8 months; CDPH responsive + resources available; hospital + ID team experienced + coordinated; no HIV co-infection; baseline LFTs normal; nutrition workup + supplementation in progress.
labs.r: Despite significant initial recognition gap + exposure event, multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS support good outcome: rifampin-susceptible (not MDR), engaged patient, social support, CDPH responsive, no HIV, normal baseline LFTs. Standard expected trajectory with appropriate management. Lessons learned from initial recognition gap will inform institutional protocol improvements.

nursesNote: Day 2 (hospital day 3) 0930 — TRANSFER from semi-private Bed 412 to negative-pressure Airborne Infection Isolation Room (AIIR) Bed 7 completed after Day 2 0700 formal radiology read by attending raised TB suspicion (upper-lobe cavitary infiltrate). PATIENT: 54 y/o male R. Owens admitted 0145 Day 0 with cough × 2 wk + scant blood-tinged sputum + 6-wk night sweats + 6-mo ~15 kg weight loss + fatigue → treated as CAP with IV ceftriaxone + azithromycin × 30 hr on STANDARD precautions in semi-private room with roommate Mr. K (POD1 colectomy). MISSED INITIAL CUES (institutional learning opportunity): homeless shelter × 14 months + chronic cough >3 wk + weight loss + night sweats + hemoptysis + no PCP × 5+ years + prior alcohol use disorder + former IDU = HIGH-RISK constellation for active pulmonary TB warranting empiric AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS pending CXR/sputum results per CDC + CDPH guidance — NOT initiated on admission. EXPOSURE WINDOW = approximately 32 hr in standard precautions setting; AT LEAST 11 staff (4 RNs + 2 RTs + 1 phlebotomist + 1 CNA + 1 housekeeper + transport + 2 PT/OT + radiology tech) + 3 roommates (Mr. K + 2 ED bay neighbors) + intermittent visitors had close contact without N95 — full exposure investigation activated. CURRENT room AIIR Bed 7 = ≥12 air changes/hr + HEPA + negative pressure + door CLOSED + signage + anteroom for PPE donning/doffing; PPE protocol = N95 respirator FIT-TESTED + seal-checked at every donning (positive + negative pressure check) + gown + gloves + eye protection; PAPR available for bearded HCW or those who cannot fit-test; SURGICAL MASK on PATIENT when leaving room for procedures (NEVER N95 on patient — N95 protects wearer; surgical mask is source control); entries MINIMIZED + care batched. DIAGNOSTIC pathway: 3 sputum specimens collected ≥8 hr apart with at least 1 EARLY MORNING — sent for AFB smear + NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) + culture + susceptibility; first AFB smear POSITIVE 3+ at 2200 Day 2 (≥10 bacilli/HPF = HIGHLY INFECTIOUS); second AFB smear POSITIVE 3+ Day 3 AM; NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) POSITIVE for M. tuberculosis complex with RIFAMPIN-SUSCEPTIBLE result (rules out MDR-TB at rifampin level — most concerning drug resistance; final phenotypic susceptibilities pending culture growth × 6 weeks); culture pending; LPA pending. 4-DRUG RIPE THERAPY INITIATED Day 3 0900 (after baseline LFTs + uric acid + vision + creatinine + HIV non-reactive confirmed): ISONIAZID 300 mg PO daily + RIFAMPIN 600 mg PO daily + PYRAZINAMIDE 1500 mg PO daily (25 mg/kg × 60 kg) + ETHAMBUTOL 900 mg PO daily (15 mg/kg) + PYRIDOXINE (vit B6) 50 mg PO daily to prevent INH peripheral neuropathy (essential given prior alcohol use + malnutrition — recommended for all patients per institutional protocol). PATIENT EDUCATION at first dose by RN with pharmacy: (1) RED-ORANGE secretions from RIFAMPIN expected — urine + tears + sweat + WILL PERMANENTLY STAIN CONTACT LENSES (patient does not wear contacts but informed for future awareness); (2) RIFAMPIN is potent CYP3A4 inducer — REDUCES warfarin/OCs/methadone/protease inhibitors/seizure meds — communicate with any prescriber; (3) ETHAMBUTOL can cause optic neuritis — REPORT vision changes immediately (decreased acuity, red-green color confusion, visual field changes — REVERSIBLE if caught early); (4) HEPATOTOXICITY symptoms — anorexia + nausea + vomiting + RUQ pain + jaundice + dark urine — STOP medications + call immediately; baseline LFTs normal (AST 28, ALT 32, alk phos 110, T bili 0.8); monthly LFTs + symptom screen; (5) PYRAZINAMIDE can cause hyperuricemia + arthralgia — usually not requiring treatment unless symptomatic; (6) DOT is essential to prevent MDR-TB — missed doses cause resistance; CDPH public health nurse will observe each dose post-discharge; (7) duration = 6 months total for drug-susceptible TB (2 mo RIPE + 4 mo HR); (8) communication: medical alert card with TB meds + dx; share with any new provider; (9) drug-food interactions — RIF on empty stomach for absorption (1 hr before meal); INH on empty stomach (avoid antacids); EMB with food acceptable. PUBLIC HEALTH REPORTING completed Day 2 0915 — local health department phone notification within 1 working day of suspicion per CA Title 17 §2500; written CDPH 8554 within 7 days; CDPH TB Control Branch coordinating: contact investigation (concentric ring — closest contacts roommate Mr. K + admission shift staff first → expanding to other shift staff + radiology + housekeeping + ED contacts + visitors); BASELINE TST or IGRA + symptom screen + chest X-ray for all contacts; REPEAT TST at 8–10 weeks (window for conversion); LTBI treatment for converters (typically 3HP rifapentine + INH × 12 weekly doses preferred for adherence over 9H INH); ROOMMATE Mr. K transferred to private room + monitored as TB contact with baseline TST + symptom screen + chest X-ray (initial all negative; will repeat at 8–10 weeks per protocol). HIV TESTING offered + accepted — 4th-generation EIA non-reactive at 0 + repeat 4 weeks per window-period protocol; counseling provided; partner notification not applicable (patient not currently sexually active per his report; will revisit when stable); hepatitis B + C + RPR negative; STI panel negative; no diabetes (HbA1c 5.4). NUTRITION: BMI 17.9 (underweight); 15 kg weight loss / 6 months; nutrition consult activated; high-protein + caloric supplementation + multivitamin + thiamine 100 mg IV daily × 5 days then PO 100 mg daily (alcohol use disorder history); refeeding syndrome risk monitored but mild (not severe malnutrition like case-076 anorexia or case-082/084); food security education + SNAP application + medical food package via TB Control; ensure 3×/day. SOCIAL WORK: CDPH TB housing voucher coordinated for isolation-capable shelter (single room + N95 supply + symptom monitoring); emergency Medi-Cal application; bus passes; FQHC connection for ongoing PCP; AA + sober living continued; trauma-informed engagement (homeless patients often have trauma histories); peer support; chaplain visit (patient identifies as Methodist). DAY 7 update (1 week into therapy): tolerating RIPE without hepatotoxicity (LFTs AST 35, ALT 42, alk phos 115, T bili 0.9 — no clinically significant change); no visual changes per Snellen + Ishihara; orange urine + tears noted as expected (patient educated previously); cough decreased + appetite improving + weight gained 1 kg (59 kg); afebrile × 36 hr; sputum AFB at Day 7 still POSITIVE 2+ (early — expected to take 2+ weeks effective therapy + 3 consecutive negative smears for de-isolation); planning for SNF or isolation-capable shelter transition at ~2-3 weeks once de-isolation criteria met (effective therapy ≥2 wk + clinical improvement + 3 consecutive negative AFB smears 8 hr apart with at least 1 early morning). PLAN: continue 4-drug RIPE × 2 months → 2-drug HR × 4 months (total 6 months for drug-susceptible TB; longer if cavitary disease with positive 2-month culture or other risk factors); pyridoxine throughout; LFT + visual acuity + color vision + uric acid + symptom screen monthly + with any concern; sputum AFB smears + cultures monthly until consistently negative; CXR at completion; DOT by CDPH public health nurse post-discharge; FQHC connection for ongoing PCP; isolation-capable housing via CDPH TB Control; AA + sober living continued; nutrition + food security supported; medical alert card; emergency Medi-Cal active; multidisciplinary follow-up; institutional lessons-learned + protocol review for TB risk-factor recognition on admission (admission risk-factor screening tool + nursing empiric airborne precautions authority); EMPLOYEE HEALTH + INFECTION PREVENTION ongoing exposure investigation + follow-up + LTBI treatment for converters; CDPH ongoing contact tracing + shelter-based testing campaign + DOT coordination.
providerOrders: ADMIT to negative-pressure Airborne Infection Isolation Room (AIIR) Bed 7 (≥12 ACH + HEPA + negative pressure + door closed + anteroom + signage)

DIAGNOSIS: smear-positive pulmonary tuberculosis (AFB 3+ × 2 specimens; NAAT positive for M. tuberculosis complex; rifampin-susceptible; final susceptibilities pending culture growth × 6 weeks); 32-hr exposure event under standard precautions pre-isolation = institutional safety event
CODE STATUS: FULL CODE; no advance directives on file; alert + decisional capacity present
AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS:
Negative-pressure AIIR (≥12 ACH + HEPA + door closed + anteroom + signage)
N95 RESPIRATOR for all HCW + visitors entering room — FIT-TESTED + seal-checked at every donning
PAPR available for bearded HCW or those who cannot fit-test
Patient wears SURGICAL MASK when leaving room for unavoidable procedures (N95 NEVER on patient)
Limit visitors + screen for symptoms + provide N95 + brief education
Minimize entries + batch care interventions
PPE donning sequence: hand hygiene → gown → respirator + seal check → eye protection → gloves
PPE doffing sequence: gloves → gown → hand hygiene → eye protection → respirator (REMOVE OUTSIDE room after door closed) → hand hygiene

Aerosol-generating procedures (induced sputum, bronchoscopy, intubation, suctioning, CPR, nebulizer, BiPAP, sputum collection) in AIIR with N95 + minimal personnel + room cleared ≥30 min post-procedure (99% air exchange at 12 ACH)
Terminal cleaning per institutional protocol post-discharge
DIAGNOSTIC workup:
Sputum AFB smear × 3 ≥8 hr apart with at least 1 early morning specimen (DONE; both positive 3+; third pending)
NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) (DONE; positive MTB complex with rifampin-susceptible)
AFB culture + drug susceptibility (pending; expected 2–8 weeks)
Line probe assay (LPA) for INH + RIF resistance (pending)
HIV 4th-generation EIA antigen/antibody (non-reactive at 0; repeat 4 weeks per window-period protocol)
Hepatitis B + C serologies + RPR (negative)
STI panel (negative)
HbA1c (5.4 — no diabetes)
CBC + BMP + LFTs + uric acid + creatinine baseline + monthly
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for extrapulmonary workup
CXR baseline + monthly until completion
Ophthalmology baseline visual acuity + color vision (Ishihara) + monthly for ETHAMBUTOL (optic neuritis surveillance)
4-DRUG RIPE INITIAL PHASE × 2 months:
ISONIAZID (INH) 300 mg PO daily (5 mg/kg, max 300)
RIFAMPIN (RIF) 600 mg PO daily (10 mg/kg, max 600)
PYRAZINAMIDE (PZA) 1500 mg PO daily (25 mg/kg × 60 kg)
ETHAMBUTOL (EMB) 900 mg PO daily (15 mg/kg)
PYRIDOXINE (vit B6) 50 mg PO daily with INH (peripheral neuropathy prevention)
RIF on empty stomach 1 hr before meal for absorption; INH avoid antacids; EMB with food acceptable
Administration via DOT-compatible schedule with documentation
CONTINUATION PHASE × 4 months (after intensive phase):
ISONIAZID 300 mg PO daily + RIFAMPIN 600 mg PO daily + PYRIDOXINE 50 mg PO daily
Total duration 6 months for drug-susceptible TB (extended to 9–12 mo for cavitary + positive 2-month culture + CNS + bone + miliary + HIV co-infection)
ADVERSE EFFECT surveillance:
Monthly LFTs (AST + ALT + alk phos + T bili) + symptom screen (anorexia, nausea, RUQ pain, jaundice, dark urine, fatigue)
HOLD INH + RIF + PZA if AST/ALT ≥3× ULN WITH symptoms OR ≥5× ULN without symptoms — workup + sequential reintroduction after resolution
Monthly visual acuity (Snellen) + color vision (Ishihara) for ETHAMBUTOL
Monthly uric acid + joint assessment for PYRAZINAMIDE
Peripheral neuropathy assessment (pyridoxine prevents)
Rash + hypersensitivity (any drug)
RED-ORANGE secretions from rifampin (educate patient — expected + harmless; warn about contact lens staining)
Drug-drug interactions with RIFAMPIN — review all current + new medications (CYP3A4/2C9/1A2 induction reduces warfarin/OCs/methadone/protease inhibitors/seizure meds)
DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT):
CDPH public health nurse coordinated for daily DOT post-discharge
vDOT alternative if appropriate
Missed doses = MDR-TB risk — engage social work + peer support + escalating engagement strategies for non-adherence
PUBLIC HEALTH REPORTING:
TB is mandatory reportable per CA Title 17 §2500
PHONE report to local health department within 1 working day of suspicion (DONE Day 2 0915)
WRITTEN report CDPH 8554 within 7 days (in progress)
CDPH TB Control Branch coordinating contact investigation + DOT + housing + case management
CONTACT investigation:

Concentric ring approach — closest contacts first (roommate Mr. K + admission shift staff with direct close contact) → expanding rings (other shift staff + transport + radiology + housekeeping + visitors + ED contacts + shelter contacts)
BASELINE TST or IGRA + symptom screen + CXR for all identified contacts
REPEAT TST at 8–10 weeks for conversion (window period)

Active TB workup + treatment for symptomatic or smear-positive contacts
EMPLOYEE HEALTH coordinating hospital staff investigation

Receiving effective anti-TB therapy ≥2 weeks

THREE consecutive NEGATIVE AFB smears ≥8 hr apart with at least 1 early morning specimen

DISCHARGE planning:

Emergency Medi-Cal enrollment + insurance for ongoing healthcare
FQHC + PCP connection for ongoing primary care

Bus passes + transportation support

MULTIDISCIPLINARY:
Infectious Disease + Pulmonary (TB management)
Infection Prevention (isolation + exposure investigation)

INSTITUTIONAL safety review (lessons learned):
TB risk-factor screening tool at admission (chronic cough >3 wk + hemoptysis + weight loss + night sweats + fever + homeless + IDU + immigration from high-burden country + prior TB exposure + HIV)
Nursing empiric airborne precautions authority pending CXR/sputum when risk factors present
Radiology rapid TB-suspicion alert protocol

Staff education + competency on TB risk factors + airborne precautions + N95 fit testing
Protocol revision + dissemination + audit
MONITORING (RN bedside):
Vital signs q4h
Continuous SpO2 (acceptable to titrate off O2 as tolerated)
Daily weights
Strict I/O
